column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I’m in love with photography. I love its indexical link to the world, and delight in the ways it shows me things that I can’t see in the real world. I make photographs just about every day and have been immersed in it daily for most of my adult life, as I taught photography over the past 30+ years. I really think of myself as a “photographer” but a 20or so years ago I began making drawings of photographs, partly as a response to having a young child (I found it more difficult to spend long hours in a darkroom). Drawing has become a significant and meaningful extension of my art practice. In my renderings of photographs, I find that I’m able draw attention to aspects of found images that I’m interested in, similar to how the act of photographing is about choosing - “I’ve decided that this thing in the world is worth paying attention to, I want to show it to you.” Another very important theme in my work has to do with the veracity of photographs. My practice of re-depicting images or subject matter highlights the fact that a photograph is indeed an interpretation, and not necessarily ‘the truth’, but sometimes can reveal hidden truths. Some of my strategies in this regard have been - drawing (Snapfolio, Home and Away), re-presenting public images (Google Amy Russell, Street[view] Photography) , repetition (Arlington), and of course editing. Just as the process and evolution of my artwork has been highly motivated by aspects of my personal life, the content of my work has always been pretty personal too. I “draw” from that which is close at hand – from pictures taken in my workplace, images in my family’s photographic archives, and in my home or around my neighborhood. My recently completed project, Pandemic Surveillance, came from trying to maintain a tactile, analogue photographic practice to teach my high school photography students remotely. Aware that my students would be sitting at a computer most of the time for all of their classes, I wanted to find something that would pull them out from their screens to make things by hand, so we made these pinhole cameras that would make an exposure for days or weeks or more. It became clear as I was learning this technique that the weeks long exposures, that would fail to record any human activity, said a lot about the experience of living (shut up in our homes) in a pandemic, so I started placing pinhole cameras around the outside of my house looking out at my still neighborhood. The project was consistent with what has interested me for a long time – ideas around home, family and community and also the nature of photographs and how they can echo conditions in the culture. Another recent project Home and Away came out of the loss of my parents pretty close together (in 2016 and 2017). After they died I was spending a lot of time in my mother’s home and with my father’s things and found this amazing set of Polaroids of my grandparents RV. I also saw, starkly, how my mother’s neighborhood in Venice had changed since my childhood there. Home and Away is a suite of drawings that look at loss and presence, home, family connections, but they’re about time and process too. Each drawing takes me between 20 to 40 hours and there was something very comforting and loving about spending that kind of time with my lost loved ones. I also like to bring a little humor into my work -sometimes this is brought out by the title or the content or both. The lack of photographs of my father as an adult in his parents photo archive, juxtaposed with the 8 polaroids they had of their RV, while sad and telling, I find quite humorous. Another example of this is Google Amy Russell, as someone with an extraordinarily common name I thought it would be funny to make a “self-portrait” of all the other Amy Russells - my tongue in cheek “self-portrait” for the digital age (made by hand). An exciting project I’m concurrently embarking on is tentatively calling Natural History. I’m honored to have been awarded a Smithsonian Artist’s Research Fellowship (SARF) to explore out-takes from the ethnographic film Single Parent by Hubert Smith that was made about my family in 1972. I am at the beginning of studying Single Parent and am excited to see where that material takes me. I’m concurrently working on a project depicting various (similar) tools discovered at my feminist environmentalist mother’s home after she died. I’ve found around 20 scrapers and putty knives. Her legacy also includes other things that ‘just might be useful one day’ - thread, fabric, candy wrappers, scraps of wood and tile, tape, newspaper clippings, ink, watercolors, glue and chisels and wire; I am using these materials to depict the found tools. Home, family, adding, subtracting (loss), and most of all the legacy of my mother and other feminists of her generation are what compels me in this work. Back in my 20s, I didn’t think my mother was a real feminist; not the learned kind who read books and attended lectures. As I have grown older I’ve figured out that she and her generation of women who set out on their own with their children in the 70s, were the real deal. They may not have had the academic language, but they were feminists in their actions and the way they raised us. My mother’s constant drive to fix, create and re-use, to do everything herself and also delight in the wonder and usefulness of everyday things is reflected in what she left behind, and also in me.

column25: Amy Russell is a second generation Angelino. After studying graphic design and photography at Pratt Institute in New York she returned to Los Angeles and completed a graduate degree in photography at CalArts and stayed to live in LA for good. She began teaching art in middle and high school in 1991, and retired in 2021. Amy Russell has been in many group exhibitions since the 90s, with a bit of a lull in the early 2000’s when she had small children. She has shown at Paul Morris Gallery and Silvershed in NY, Four Walls in San Francisco and The High Dessert test site in Wonder Valley. In 2000, her solo exhibit Snapfolio at ACME Gallery in Los Angeles was ‘Pick of the Week’ in The L.A. Weekly. Although Amy works with different media, she always comes back to certain themes - the nature of photography and perception, notions of family and home, and always present is the poignancy and beauty of the ordinary and every day.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
It is the ability to always think new, learn new, and push myself to navigate into the unknowns. Honestly, I become an artist not because being an artist has the best advantage over being any other form. Being an artist merely means being myself. Slow looking has been my innate and instinctive ability. I am able to see things others do not see, feel and sense things that others cannot feel or sense; thus, my creation visualizes the invisible and transmit my feelings and senses to the public. I grew up painting and drawing in all media, including 2D digital illustration. Then I felt it wasn't enough. I wanted to encompass more mediums, so I got an architectural training and am now a visual worldbuilder. I like to create a world with its cinematic vibe and its own rules and customs, where characters organically interweave into artwork without contrivances. Over the years, I loved novel expressions to reference something deeper than the contemporary, to reference ancestral visions of life and mythology, but always with a novel methodology. For me that is to navigate the unknowns.

column25: Artist Statement by Yiou Wang Becoming-Other: Animal, Animacy, and the Animate “Art is the opening up of the universe to becoming-other.” – Elizabeth Grosz In the fields of digital media, video, and game, my works often provoke how we engage with otherness - from nonhuman cohabitants, transitional and transformational bodies, to what displaced human beings from the physical and virtual mono-narrative - in a co-imagination between mythology and technology. Over the years, I draw inspirations of animal-human metamorphosis and otherness from a variety of myths and Asian animist traditions, coupling with technologies of motion capture, CGI, and virtual reality, explored through the lens of machine as avatars’ topographical morphing (on the body) as both an interaction with the environment and an externalization of the visceral changes (inside the body). I am profoundly drawn to the ancestral beliefs of humanity, when plants and animals, rivers and mountains were alive and when us humans were shifters. A language of techno-animism permeates my thoughts and creations - the fusion of ancient animism and technology. Influenced by my Chinese roots and a penchant for Asian surrealist cinema and literature, I suppose physical and virtual more-than-human entities hold intrinsic spirit and agency, which influence how we, as humans, and I, as an empathic observer, interact with them. My works tend to aestheticize the “other” in anthropocentrism or in a disenchanted society. I routinely engage in contemplations on what makes one an alien, what alien-ness means to us, spontaneous or responsive to other stimuli. Everyday objects, no matter how familiar, when viewed in a different perspective between physical and virtual realities and scales, often appear alien. My personal life journey makes me tick the “alien” box in almost every legal document. In many cultures, there are rituals in which men dress like women, and people dress like animals; gods ride sedan carriages from village to village. Transitional hybridity has been and is always an inherent part of us, but modern societies deny it and dismiss it. Of my whole adulthood being an Asian in the USA, I closely live with visceral sensations of an alien, which connect me to techno-animism - finding the eureka moments in the underestimated convergences between new media art’s methodology and mythology. Every day many ideas emerge in my head like small tributaries, some of them would last over a long period and gradually become rivers, and my thoughts and research will start before these rivers become actual projects. CGI R&D (research and development), meditation and writing consist of an important part of my research methods, in which I flesh out and visualize ideas with R&D, and make the obscure clear and structured with meditation and writing, including essays and letter exchange with friends. I am navigating the unknown by creating.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a first generation U.S. Mexican, the tension between manual and intellectual labor drives my artistic inquiry. My work shifts between analyzations of familial, community, historical, spirituality and cultural trauma that affects the identities I live with: in brownness, in queerness, in being low-income in America and raised a traditional Catholic Mexican home. The Xicanx identity echoes the chant of “ni de aquí, ni de allá” (neither from here, nor there), which I use to challenge the complexities of a cultural in-between of Latinx diaspora, systematic racism and classism, and gender dysphoria. The work I have been making is a take on magical realism, using the based-on-truth stories of my late, undocumented aunt and turning them into personal mythologies. Analyzing the psychological implications of a very specific fright, I am expanding on what is it to live with this fright that was left by my aunt and possibly inherited by me. In Spanish, one of the words for fright is Susto. Across Latin America, Susto connotation is most associated with 'soul loss after a traumatic event'. Susto is viewed as an illness, with no definite cure. One of my 'myths' I am utilizing is the story of my aunt killing a hawk with a rock, after the hawk's spree of terror on the small animals in my family's yard, including my pet rabbit who was discovered as just a head with no trace of a body. While a great David and Goliath-like story of frightlessness, my aunt felt a sort of discomfort from the retelling of the story after finding out that hawks are highly protected in North America by the Migratory Bird Treaty Act. Finding out that a hawk had more lawful protection in this continent than herself, she feared the repercussions of her murder of this bird of prey as an undocumented person. My work is expanding on this narrative and others as I navigate through her life, her death, and my perpetual Susto.

column25: Dulce Soledad Ibarra (they/them/theirs) is a multidisciplinary artist, designer, educator, and curator with investments in community and identity-emphasized arts and opportunity. As a practicing artist, Ibarra discusses issues and narration of generational guilt, identity, class, labor, displacement, and injustice in paintings, drawings, sculptures, videos, installations, performances, and participatory work. Looking through queer Xicanx perspective, the work is fueled by emotional labor, personal and cultural research, and a strong interest in place-making and narrative-building. Much of Ibarra's work centers around the aesthetics and resilience of the Piñata/Party Supply District of Downtown Los Angeles, engaging in the means of sustaining as a community of businesses and as a place of cultural familiarities and commodities. Ibarra has exhibited, screened, performed, and programmed at venues across Southern California and beyond, including Angels Gate Cultural Center, Charlie James Gallery, Consulado General de México en Los Ángeles, Craft Contemporary, Echo Park Film Center, Guggenheim Gallery at Chapman University, Human Resources Los Angeles, ONE Gallery in West Hollywood, and Pieter Performance Space, among others. Ibarra holds an MFA from the University of Southern California and earned a BFA in Sculpture from California State University, Long Beach.

column25: My work dares to question: How far we can take 2 dimensional elements by collaging them 3 dimensionally? Can collage be a fluid, interactive new media art form instead of immobile in a still image in a frame? Can collage break the 4th wall and offer a deeper personal experience by viewer and piece becoming one? And with a wider lens: Can contemporary new media inherently challenge our perceptions of reality or must art be physical to be respected and enjoyed as to what is real? I focus on 3D collage motion works and animations inspired by decades working with analog and digital collages, sculptural collages, and life-sized walk through art installations. I choose collage -cutting and pasting disparate elements, materials, processes, and formats together- because it provokes chance and flexibility, which lends itself well to the experimental path contemporary new media art is on course with. Although my current work is shown as short cautionary tales via collaged videos, they are originally created as 3D sculpted environments, and the 2D videos became the result. My process is creating 3 dimensionally and positioning the camera movements to guide the viewer through the story as it unfolds. Producing the works as glb formatted files provide options for guests to experience self guided walk throughs of the stories with the use of AR/VR. This medium can evolve further and incorporate narratives to subvert dominant discourses and offer meaningful subject which spark social relevance, so when the viewer does enter and become one with the piece they’re emotionally and intellectually engaged.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Finding fulfilment in being my own boss and being creative every single day. Having spent time in various careers before establishing myself as a full-time working artist, I find myself working harder than ever before, but am fulfilled and passionate about what I'm doing. My previous career, first as a physicist in academic and later as a management consultant, I've never shied away from hard work or the long hours, but as an artist, there is a longevity that I had never before realised. I'm excited about being in a profession that I will never retire from!

column25: I paint expressive flowers against vivid abstract backgrounds with line-drawn architectural details from across Europe, Asia and America. Having lived exclusively in large metropolitan cities, pockets of vibrant flowers were always found against the backdrop of buildings. The same way that noticing the fusion and contrasts of flowers in urban environments brought me moments of joy, calmness and reflection, my hope is that I can help others feel the same. In this series, I invite audiences to pause and see the beauty in the world around them as they are transported to places around the world. My process begins by selecting a building or city view and the type of flower to paint. The contrast of my natural and manmade subjects are mirrored in the contrast of technique, scale and colour employed in this collection. On canvas, a bold, abstract background is built up using a variety of tools to apply and spread paint and in many layers. Using white acrylic paint pens, architectural details are drawn in free-hand. The final stages involve painting in bold, oversized flowers to tie the piece together. Lost in the city. Lost in Nature. Lost in Colour.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Overtime, I have developed my own techniques and practice through ideas and experimentation. My passion for animals, nature and conservation drives my creative inspiration. My pieces are close to life-size and sometimes actually life-size. I like to create my main subjects of these portraits by pyrography so that all of the attention and detail is on them. In the backgrounds I do different things, such as, stain the wood with colored stains, or natural stains (ex: puréed blackberries, wine, tea, beet juice, etc.), and acrylic paints. There are also times where I may add color to the animal using acrylic paint and even colored pencils for finer details. I strive to capture expressions in wildlife and the connectedness we, as humans, have with nature. The emotions and personalities of animals are similar to our own and I want to spark that connection through those shared feelings.

column25: Margaret Henry is a self-taught, multidisciplinary artist, primarily working in pyrography. Her creative inspiration has been driven by her love and passion for animals and the outdoors since she was a child. This passion and connection grew with her time living in nature at it’s best and worst as a wildland firefighter (hotshot) for the US Forest service. When Margaret stumbled upon pyrography, a pencil became a burning tool and she knew she had found her medium. Pyrography is a vessel for expressing her emotional connection to nature. Through experimentation and combinations of mediums (paint, stain and colored pencils), her work has evolved into having a tactile aspect where a 2D piece feels 3D. Margaret is an emerging artist in Gig Harbor, WA. She has mainly donated art to raise money for charities through auctions and works towards wildlife conservation.

column25: My work is a sculptural realization of lived experience, thus responsible for its’ chaotic path. I have times working in chocolate, cotton candy, or found plastic and everything in between. Work for me is about the process, the manipulation of a given material, and treating these materials as temporary guests in my hands. The drive for me has always been the sacred found object, an object with its own history given to me to mold into another narrative. Eventually, we realize that our own body and self is also found object, given briefly so we too can write our own story.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is the ability to interpret the world around you and respond to it through a visual language. Art grants the ability to explore complex ideas and conceptualize them into materiality. Within my own practice which combines performance, print, sculpture, and video, I create a multi-faceted experience for the viewer and bring tangibility to an otherwise fictitious persona. To create an immersive experience for the viewer that perhaps does not read in the traditional white wall gallery sense. If I can create something that captures the imagination of the viewer, while revealing some personal vulnerabilities of my own through the guise of the character I will have succeeded, especially if the viewer can take a souvenir home. It feels selfish at times, to create work through my lens, to rationalize the value of art versus that of doctors or those looking to make the world a better place. Some societal contribution of significance versus the art that I create. When the work is well received, better yet understood, it’s elating. To create work that is vulnerable and stems from my own experience and then received or touched by others. Once at a printmaking conference, a young person came up to me, we had never met before, they held my hand and looked into my eyes to tell me how important my work was to them. It was moving to have a stranger feel an incredible connection to my work. That my work mattered. As a printmaker, the value of the multiple allows for my work to be accessible and distributed to a wider audience. To make work that can be held and exist in its multiplicity. My primary medium is lithography, so to carry on the tradition of printmaking methods and combining it with new techniques and approaches invigorates my practice and allows for me to share my passion for printmaking. Printmaking is one of the few “democratic” mediums, it gives agency to creators to distribute their ideas and is heavily community oriented. When I was going from community college to my undergraduate studies I found comfort and support in the printshop. Print has a special place in my practice due to the beautiful nature of human connection.

column25: My work is an exploration of beauty, femininity, and fallibility through an interdisciplinary practice ranging from print, sculpture, and video. My recent body of work utilizes the manifestation of a hyper-femme persona known as Buck Wilde to create source imagery and myth. Buck Wilde is a performance persona and vehicle for storytelling that creates the basis for my artistic practice as well as an exploration of autofiction and authenticity through humor and performance. A former rodeo clown, milk poster girl, and first place winner of a Dolly Parton lookalike contest, Buck Wilde is a manifestation of insecurity, desire, and commerce, her likeness documented through photo and transmuted into the multiple nature of print. The work is a response to this newfound era of “bimbofication”, exploring my own gender expression and desire into a liberated form, while embracing beauty in femininity. While the stories are based in truth, the performance of Buck Wilde allows me to blur the lines between truth and fiction and embellish parts of her to create a mythic aura. Growing up in a Vietnamese-American matriarchal family I spent my childhood in the back of my grandmother’s nail salon, and my mother’s hair salon. Being privy to these intimate spaces dedicated to beautification continues to influence my work through a push and pull of material and theory. Part truth, part fiction, Buck Wilde weaves a tale of beauty, fallibility, and disappointment. Framed as a quasi-museum/gift shop, my thesis exhibition, The Buck Wilde Attraction, featured photographs of Buck Wilde’s adventures, prints, video documentation, and various ephemera, including postcards, air fresheners, and bumper stickers for guests to take. The exhibition was meant to bring validity and tangibility to an imagined persona through storytelling objects.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Andrea Coleman’s work makes use of found family images, memorabilia, and videos as a basis to produce a series of digital paintings. Combined with expressive mark-making and coloration, each piece addresses elements of nostalgia and a distorted form of memory. Coleman digitally manipulates images found in photo albums, or VHS recordings to challenge ideas of authenticity and re-appropriate the aesthetic of aura and memory. Drawing inspiration from her own, familial relationships and oral histories, the work investigates the intuitive interconnectedness and multidimensional relationship between snapshot and portraiture.

column25: Andrea Coleman is an visual artist, writer, and art administrator based in Chicago who currently utilizes the mediums of paint, fabric and personal archive throughout her practice. Heavily inspired by her suburban upbringing, animation art and mural concepts, her work currently explores how aura and narrative work together to interpret acts of reminiscing and honoring. Coleman earned her Bachelor of Fine Arts from Columbia College Chicago. She is an Idea Award recipient of the Art and Art Activism organization, a recipient of the A+T Seed Grant and an awardee of the Make A Wave Award of 2023 by 3arts. Coleman was a previous artist-in-residence of Chicago Artist Coalition and is currently named as one of the Breakout Artists for Chicago’s Next Generation of Image Makers of 2021. Andrea is also a Co-member of CBIM (Concerned Black Image Makers): a collective of Black artists, thinkers, and curators that prioritize shared experiences and concerns by lens based artists of the Black diaspora.

column25: My enthusiasm towards decolonizing consumption and art creation is intertwined with remembering the lessons that the earth has to teach us. I work with natural plant materials like corn husks in conjunction with manipulated art supplies to consider identity, mixedness, and resistance. The corn husks represent the skin of the figures, recalling Mesoamerican beliefs that our very beings are created from maíz. This material use works to dismantle folk and fine art hierarchies. I call these “mestizx media” works, reclaiming the “mestizo” colonial caste label. I define mestizx media as when materials originate from the region(s) of the artist’s ancestors. Accepting mixedness is also about embracing queerness and the fluid nature of identities that reject constructed binaries. My public artwork has included themes of celebrating contemporary artists and activists, histories of the land, native plants and animals, and concepts of love and solidarity. My work serves to work through my own intersections and to strive for intercultural conversations in my community. This, I hope, will open doors to compassion and healing in this world of destruction.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I strive to create art that is so engaging and unique that it cannot be compared to anything else. Sometimes I think I have accomplished that to a certain degree. I do not limit myself to one approach or certain materials. My current disciplines range from acrylics to yakisugi wood burning. I find myself influenced by love and awareness, heartache and pain, comedy, emptiness and universal wonder. Most of my pieces are created on a large scale, because I feel that they have a greater impact. Ultimately, I want the impact of my art to be fun and ignite meaningful conversations. I have experienced and understand the difficulties and hurdles we go through to find the confidence to create. The main goal with my art is to encourage others to fearlessly walk their own creative path.

column25: Bryan Cridlebaugh is a multidisciplinary artist, whose only formal training was in Junior High and High School. In 2014, after years of feeling like he missed his calling as an artist, he decided to get serious about his art and hasn’t stopped since. Bryan’s inspiration spans a wide spectrum with very few limitations. Many of his works are acrylic on canvas or wood platforms. Aside from painting, he practices a unique form of yakisugi, a Japanese wood burning technique. Bryan is driven to develop innovative art in his Pacific Northwest studio, where he has created over 300 pieces. Due to his love of creating, he has not been able to show as much of his work as he would like.

column25: Twenty-six years ago, my parents immigrated to the United States. As a child, I would imagine my parents’ odyssey through rudimentary terms: walking, loving, and bold. Through the years, however, I’ve managed to contextualize the reality of our situation and now use words such as treacherous, lonely, and fearful. When asked, my father replays the moment he and his group laid flat on their stomachs, hiding from an oblivious ICE officer who sat nearby. My mother recalls trekking through knee-high mud in an Arizona desert, thousands of miles away from her home in Acapulco, Guerrero. My sister, on the other hand, can’t remember much. She was only five years old at the time. Upon arriving in the United States, my family endured domestic and economically restricting policies that shaped their path moving forward. Working low-paying restaurant jobs, living in the same home, and witnessing family and friend’s deportation characterize my family’s American story. And through it all, society holds its gaze, judging and debating over them. In order to disrupt these American eyes, I obscure faces, objects, and stories. I provide details of this complicated history because I also hope to highlight how this experience isn’t wholly unique to my family. Furthermore, these photographs take place from the point of view of our home because of its function as a place of refuge for undocumented immigrants across the nation.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My art has always been a way for me to work among my anxieties and existential thoughts through storytelling or narrative. While drawing is at the foundation of my practice, I’ve gathered skills from a variety of avenues that led me to my current work. My personal interest in process lends to detailed, thoughtful composition and utilizes themes of nature, consciousness, and altered states of reality. I see my drawings as stills of a larger story unfolding... I’ve struggled with depression and a strong sense of existentialism a lot of my life, and I find communicating these things through art could help me and other people feel less isolated. Art making gives me the opportunity to research many different routes of life, and I've merged my love of art with my personal interests of ritual, symbolism, religion, philosophy, psychology, and mystery. There will always be unknowns to explore and expand upon-- in our relationships, our societies, our external world, and ourselves.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I use ink and paper to create richly textured landscape drawings. Clusters of lines, dots and patterns create forms that one starts recognizing as trees, houses, streets, cars, electrical posts, hills, rivers and the horizon. Familiar sights emerge and disappear as the viewer drifts in and out between appreciating the drawing as a particular landscape and also a collection of purely formal elements. This approach stems from my interest in how ideas are shaped and the pictorial brevity needed to translate the real sights into an experience of the mind. The work samples (each around 11x14in) consist of drawings resulting from walking around small towns bordering the Hudson river. Tight arrangements of trees, houses, streets and cars set against the vast river makes for classically attractive compositions. This, in turn, gives a framework for creating images from basic elements of form. Strokes of the pen - their size, direction and energy - are as vital as what they are trying to show. The resulting work is a visual dance where the classical landscape is as important as pictorial devices used to create it.

During the pandemic, Patryk and his wife worked on a series of children books (she wrote, he illustrated). He started dividing his time between working as a filmmaker and an artist. He is married and has two small sons.

column25: My work explores kinetic, tactile ceramic objects imbued with everyday participatory and mechanical systems. Juxtaposing this is their suggestion of ancient, weathered artifacts, presenting a device of dubious utility and provenance. They seek to entice your physical engagement through memory, familiarity, and the intuition of touch. Developing this body of work has led me to consideration of everyday objects: tools, doorknobs—the ergonomically suggestive, whose purpose through our engagement are comprehensible. The idea of tactile games, Montessori objects, and toys, similarly informs my work for presenting a proposition of participation. The nature of games/play necessitates a competing player to discover the rules and mechanics of gameplay and completion of objective. Drawing from these everyday instances of touch and familiarity, the works encourages viewers' performance, to turn handcranks, assemble bricks, and de/constructing parts via fasteners. The absurd question, 'How do I get folks to touch my ceramics?' was the catalyst to this unconventional approach. Absurd, as I see humor in the challenge of achieving this through the ceramic material alone, one considered fragile and inappropriate to ask to perform a precise, mechanical function. Additionally, I prioritize the ceramic objects themselves to entice physical interaction, forgoing explicit museum text. My curiosity is centered on instincts of engagement with familiar objects and, conversely, how with unfamiliar items, we still begin to generate an understanding of engagement, object's function, and use. This exploration aims to challenge and play with the known and unknown, prompting reflective consideration on the interactive nature of encountered objects.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My process holds as its center the cosmology of the Yoruba spiritual beliefs and practices that are translated and adopted by queer BIPOC people in the United States and throughout the diaspora. Connecting spirituality to Orishas for people who have lost religious, familial, or other community to find a belief system that is expansive enough to include any identity. Within the practice of these spiritual ideals, queer BIPOC people are able to choose self, family, and community, among other desires. Commune forms as an act of liberation, connecting desire with destiny, inverting the pyramid of what matters, and releasing distractions to allow fluidity in all directions. My sculptures and paintings embody a language of their own that echoes heightened energy. Each object stores a bit of memory that continues like a sentence representing a prayer spoken with a close, soft breath, rhythmic and repetitive.

column25: Valincy-Jean Patelli is a self-taught Black Non-binary artist working and living in Brooklyn, New York. Patelli experiments with three-dimensional textures to create mixed-media works and sculptures. This nonlinear process allows them to blend stories from their ancestors with theoretical and cultural references from the African diaspora. Through environmental and historical allusion, Valincy's work explores the marginalization of Queer and Black American culture. They create multi-layered textures by transforming common materials into Artifacts and Heirlooms. Patelli places particular importance on their choices of materials, such as gold, plaster, wood, burlap, canvas, and other non-traditional mixed media; they intend to invoke radical and provocative stories that challenge this intersectional narrative of Queer Blackness. Patelli’s career spans national and international venues, including Patio Taller Residency in Puerto Rico (2014), a self-directed residency in Berlin, Germany (2015-2016), A.B.M residency in Phoenix, Arizona ( 2023). Exhibitions of note include I Will Always Love: The Whitney Houston Biennial, Chashama, NYC (2016), Site Specific: Kunsthof, Berlin, Germany (2016), The Sheen Center: Little Rock 9: 1957, NYC (2018), UNRPED Group Show, Los Angeles (2020-2022), Por um Teto Todo Nosso (For a Roof of Our Own) Uncool Artist Collective Centro de Cultural Laura Alvin, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (2022-2023) and NOoSPHERE Arts’ Repopulations: New Horizons (2022) NYC. Solo exhibition, I Will Tell You the Future, The Grand, Los Angeles (2023). Upcoming exhibitions, Woman’s Sculptor Group Exhibition, Elijah Wheat Showroom, Newburg, NY (2024) and I Will Always Love You Biennial, LaMama Gallery, NYC (2024). Patelli’s work is held in numerous North American and European private collections.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I sigh with relief and gratitude at this question. I sigh with relief and gratitude knowing that I am an artist. I think the best thing about being an artist is the ability to engage with its great multiplicity. Art can be simply a lifestyle, or the act of exploring your internal world, a form of activism, a form of therapy, creating catharsis, forming community, sharing unique perspectives, recording stories, discovering worlds, and so more. Fortunately, I have the autonomy to decide how I want to engage with art on a daily basis. Most days, being an artist for me looks like starting my day with 30 minutes of improvised dancing (art), then coming back to stillness through a visual meditation (art), starting my workday with some admin work (this can definitely be an art form, I just haven’t found it yet), cooking a delicious meal full of various colors and textures (art), making progress on a photo based project (art), facilitating a playful movement class (art), and spending some time writing about my day/dreams (art). As an artist I can't help but approach my life/style with a similar integrity that I would approach any creation, which includes playfulness, patience, curiosity, and discipline. That is the best thing.

column25: My current artistic expression is painting. The mediums I am using include acrylic, watercolor, latex paint, ink on paper, wood, and found objects. In 2015, during a period of emotional turbulence, art became my sanctuary for releasing pent-up feelings. Working as a Jewelry illustrator at the time, where precision and minuteness dominated, my professional drawings mirrored my sense of feeling small and unseen. In stark contrast, my artwork provided a breathing space, evolving towards a looser and more fluid direction. Embracing sketchier lines and allowing colors and paint to blend freely, my art became a liberating exploration. The shift in my artistic approach also coincided with the freedom to pursue and experiment with themes that intrigued me, such as lips, eyes, and fashion. This process became a journey of self-discovery and empowerment, enabling me to find my artistic voice and liberate myself from preconceived notions of how things should be done. When the opportunity presented itself for me to create a mural, I revisited the screen-printing style I had loved and utilized in high school. This style incorporated nature, abstract elements, and simpler shapes, all brought to life with vibrant colors. The use of these simplified shapes not only facilitated the evolution of my artistic practice but also made the mural creation an inclusive process, allowing people of all ages to actively participate in painting and contributing to the artwork, transforming it into a shared creation that becomes a part of their narrative.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
California based artist, Kalleb Arefaine “The Rainbow Prince” has been honing his skills in “psychedelic pop art” for the past ten years, and videography since he was twelve years old. Originally a graffiti artist, Kalleb blended his psychedelic experiences on canvases and clothes creating perfect hallucinogenic artwork. Kalleb’s work is highly focused on bright colors, and happiness. His work is very fun, interactive and easy going. In an interview for Left Lion magazine in the UK, he says “I want my pieces to be relatable to teenage me, and teenagers today. Colors and other fun things slowly disappear from our clothes as we get older. I understand there’s levels of professionalism that come with having a job or a certain level of success, but we shouldn’t let our profession or age affect the freedom we have to express ourselves.” You can see that he is on a mission to redefine psychedelic imagery, take it away from the world's concept of psychedelics being a bad “drug”, and more in to a space of art therapy, consciousness and a colorful life. You will be transported to another time. If you like 60s culture, experimental sounds of house music and colorful imagery, then you will love his work. A magical experience of illusion and consciousness for the whole world. The kids will be fascinated and the adults will be enthralled.

column25: Over the last ten years, I've devoted myself to mastering my artistry as 'The Rainbow Prince.' Throughout my career, I've been commissioned by pop culture enthusiasts, was featured in a prominent UK magazine and have been able to support my lifestyle with art. My exhibition, 'Safe Sex Is Cool,' was a personal tribute inspired by a close friend's HIV experience. My art was also showcased in a Rolling Stones article about 'Occupy Oakland,' and I printed thousands of free posters for the movement. Furthermore, my artistic expression expanded internationally, as I painted murals for two consecutive summers at one of Ibiza, Spain's prominent clubs 'Paradise, DC-10'. My artistic journey is a reflection of my life—a colorful reflection I hope to continue sharing with the world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In high school I had a college counselor who asked me one day, while preparing to write me a letter of recommendation, ‘how did you get here?’. I realize that I've been trying to answer that question my whole life and the medium I chose to do so is through art. My art is a reflection of this journey. My work on the built environment explores the impacts physical structures have on Black and Latino communities. My Public Housing series focuses on how the system of housing projects still redlines, displaces, and erases people. Why is There a Liquor Store on Almost Every Corner? addresses issues of food desserts, discriminatory land use and zoning practices, nuisance businesses all while documenting and mapping liquor stores. In my recent work Esta es pa´ mi Viejo (working title), using photographs I collected after my father got sick from cancer and after laying him to rest in Mexico, I chronicle my father’s migration story of coming to the U.S. from Mexico through a series of intimate figurative paintings. These works explore familial histories, intersections of race, identity, and migration. In large, my work focuses on both the external and internal influences that shape my identity as an Afro Latino (Belizean and Mexican) man from South Central LA.

column25: Terrick Gutierrez (b. 1994) is an interdisciplinary artist, curator, creative technologist, and storyteller based in Los Angeles. He explores the intricacies of urban life in LA, with a keen focus on the built environment. In both of his mixed media on paper series about liquor stores and public housing projects, Gutierrez isolates each subject matter as a way to hyper visualize and focus on them. Growing up in South Central LA, he remembers liquor stores and projects anchoring his community. For him, this shines light on how laws and practices, such as land uses and zoning, continue to shape urban landscapes, influencing the way we live and impacting our behaviors, interactions, physical navigation, and overall well-being. Through this work, Gutierrez addresses critical social issues such as food insecurity, cultural erasure, and displacement. Gutierrez’ work also delves into questions of identity and personal narratives, drawing from his experiences as a biracial individual. As a child of Belizean and Mexican immigrants, his recent artistic focus centers on assimilating his parents' migration stories into his own work. Through this approach, he aims to shed light on the broader themes of race, identity, and migration. Gutierrez earned his Bachelor of Arts in psychology with a minor in art from Morehouse College in 2017 and furthered his education by obtaining a Master of Professional Studies in interactive telecommunications from New York University in 2019. He has exhibited across the country, including cities such as Atlanta, New York, and Los Angeles. His work finds homes in private collections on both the East and West Coast.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The deepest joy of being an artist lies in being an artist the sheer freedom to express myself without boundaries, and to push the limits of what art can be – that's the magic of being an artist. My ability to delve into unexplored mediums and techniques. Having a playful dance with ideas, where I can make edits to the overall vision and allow the next steps to be a foundation for contemporary works. My art isn't just about self-expression; it's about sparking conversations, challenging perspectives, and thought-provoking installations that raise social awareness, or a colorful mural that brightens a community, knowing that my art can transform perspectives. There is also this feeling of losing myself in the process, to learn and unlearn while growing in the fluidity of the artwork. This journey is not a solitary one. I find threads of inspiration from the vibrant community of artists, mentors, and muses who push me further, challenge me deeper, and celebrate my every step. The laughter shared in studios, the hushed contemplation during exhibitions, the spirited debates over technique and meaning of my artistic world.

column25: Born in New York City. Raised in East Harlem – El Barrio, my artistic journey is entwined with the vibrant tapestry of my Caribbean roots and living in a bustling city. Although vibrant, at times there has been a profound sense of absence in art and community engagement, particularly those reflecting the affluent Afro-Latino narrative. My passion is to weave art, culture, and land stewardship into our communities. Speaking to our stories through painting still life objects of our homes, recollections of forgotten cultures. In forming Sunroom Sala, and working to bring these visions to life through immersive experiences rooted in art, food, and land stewardship. My practice thrives on multidisciplinary exploration, embracing various techniques and mediums like acrylics, watercolors, and found objects, and using public spaces to connect to host art workshops dedicated to the growth of reclaiming spaces for ourselves. In 2021 I began a series 'Siembras: Sowing Seeds' which exemplifies the philosophy of connecting to my roots. Using heirloom items, and still-life objects to capture the essence of Afro-Caribbean culture and inviting viewers into my culture. This transformed into workshops where I began facilitating painting sessions of still-life objects, tropical plants, and fruits or seeds. Setting the tone by playing Afro-Caribbean music and conga drums. Participants discover traditional ties to the earth, using art and food as their artistic compass. A multi-sensory journey, providing a reminder that nourishment extends beyond the plate. Siembras: Sowing Seeds, is an invitation to learn, grow, and explore art within African & Latino culture.

column25: Creating collage is a mystical adventure. Handling paper and tools and adhesives suspends time and transports me elsewhere. It’s a kind of alchemy, as well. An initial inspiration may change direction or content or intention on the way to completion. I welcome this unpredictability and find this practice invigorating and deeply satisfying. Those familiar with my collage art know that my work addresses the consequences of institutionalized, policy-driven, legally sanctioned violence. My goal is to find ways to speak to the guilty bystander in all of us and challenge our systemically induced complicity with a way of life that dehumanizes everyone. As a poet, I often have trouble finding ways to express these feelings. Collage is my go-to when words fail. Whether writing a poem, creating a collage, designing a zine, assembling a handmade book, stitching a pañuelo, or beading and gilding a canvas, it is my desire to create arresting and compelling images, both aesthetically and emotionally. I want those who see my art to see themselves and their lives in the images I create: to recognition a reflection of self and 'the other,' to discover new ways of seeing and understanding the world, to learn a visual vocabulary that may have been there all along and now seems brand new, to surrender to a moment that is deeply felt and for which there may be no words, or to embrace a reason to have a lively discussion about what they've just experienced. My communities include unwitting guilty bystanders, those seeking beauty in the world, and those who don’t yet know how moving and life affirming art can be.

column25: Symbol and Meaning I use palm trees and power lines as contemporary cultural symbols for Southern California. This region, once inhabited by the indigenous tribes of Tongva, Cahuilla, Kumeyaay, and Serrano, with their rich cultural heritage and history, is now mythologized for its non-native plants and a littered skyline. My photographs are not about the location of a specific palm tree or power line but that they are everywhere, ruling the California horizon from Malibu to Joshua Tree, Lancaster to San Diego, Chula Vista to Imperial Valley, and back to Victorville. The palm tree is the mascot for Sunny So Cal. Their softly waving tropical fronds send a message to the world, “Come to California and become a star.” But like Hollywood, they are a myth. The only palm native to California is the fan palm (Washingtonia filifera). The scores of other palms are transplants, including the non-native Mexican Fan Palm (Washingtonia robusta), now the region’s icon and, ironically, considered invasive in some areas. Power lines, of course, are not native anywhere. Civil engineers plant them to expand the electric and information grid to power our daily lives. The ubiquity of palms and power lines is why I photograph them. They are at the center of our altered and irreversible landscape. They are the myth of nature, not native flora and fauna, but Disneyfied tropes from Hollywood’s lens. They are a facade against which I pick at with my camera. I sew/suture these subjects together as a critique of photography. Photography and landscapes have been intertwined since the medium’s inception, seemingly disparate at first but intrinsically linked. It can be argued that nature was photography’s first subject, as it certainly was for William Henry Fox Talbot’s “Pencil of Nature.” The tradition of recording one’s natural surroundings with a lens and its reaction to light represents a rich lineage in photography. I feel a kinship to mundane images of the New Topographics. At the same time, many other landscape photographers climbed the branches of Carleton Watkins and William Henry Jackson, who portrayed nature with grand romanticism, beginning the tradition of sublime vistas and wondrous creations. Even when the view is awe-inspiring, creating pictures so deeply tainted by romanticism is disingenuous because the means used actively ruin the land. Hands and Machines The association between hands and machines is a recurring theme in my photographs. Hands appear as shadows on the print, through cutting the paper and the sewn marks. In the printing phase, I will hold my hand over the paper for an extended period to leave a mark. In the next stage, I cut off pieces of the print that I didn’t wish to keep, creating unusual paper shapes. Next, I machine sew and hand embroider elements to draw, add color, and emphasize specific sections. Finally, I spot-bleach the print in areas I wish to bleed or obliterate. I emphasize the correlation between hand-made and machine-made because hands create and wield tools. Machines do not autonomously mine the planet, build and connect power lines, or document the landscape without the intention of the human user. Just as I am responsible for my photographs, we, too, are responsible for destroying our environment through irresponsible environmental interference and misuse of science and chemicals. Toxic Photos I feel most connected to the pictorialist because I’m not interested in documenting reality with “straight photography.” I aim to use development metaphorically by bringing photo chemical processing methods to the foreground rather than masking them behind the notion that they are somehow separate from the image. Photographic chemicals are poisonous. It is a well-known rule in the darkroom not to dump the fix down the drain because it is toxic to the environment. I am keenly aware of chemical waste management and regularly seek ways to reuse my materials. We should examine this relationship, reframe the idea that a camera is an impartial witness to nature, and recognize that the camera and photographic processes are tied to the environment.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Dinofy represents a unique artistic journey where light, science, and art converge to highlight the beauty and urgency of wildlife conservation. Inspired by the grandeur of dinosaurs and my deep commitment to endangered species, Dinofy offers an immersive experience that blends imagination with reality. The genesis of Dinofy came from a fusion of traditional and innovative art methods, sparked by my lifelong fascination with prehistoric life and nurtured through experiences at the American Museum of Natural History. Utilizing a mix of acrylics, fluorescent pigments, and glow-in-the-dark paint, my technique captures the dynamic essence of these majestic creatures, reminiscent of bioluminescence. This project is more than an artistic endeavor; it's an educational and inspirational platform. It invites families to explore and understand the significance of biodiversity. Through various lighting environments, including darkness and black light, Dinofy immerses visitors in a world where art and education merge, empowering them to become advocates for our planet's wildlife. Dinofy is not just about viewing art; it's about experiencing a story of survival, legacy, and hope. It's a call to action, encouraging us to preserve the natural world for future generations.

column25: René Ugarte Bio Visual Artist Conservation Advocate Dinosaur Enthusiast. From Caracas, Venezuela / NYC for 14 years / Inspired on the AMNH In a world where the rhythms of nature take precedence, and life is an intricate dance with the environment, I found my calling as an artist, conservationist, and messenger of hope. I am René Ugarte, a visual artist originally hailing from the lush landscapes of Caracas, Venezuela, and I've been on a lifelong journey to express my deep connection with the natural world and the animal kingdom. From a very young age, my heart was drawn to the wonders of the natural world. I remember even protecting the ants that crossed my path, realizing that I couldn't save every individual, but I could contribute to the larger ecosystem. This strong connection with nature was instilled by my upbringing, led by my parents who established a conservation non-profit named 'Plantar' back in Venezuela. Together, we planted seeds while expressing my dad's art and these seeds would one day grow into towering trees, and this experience filled me with an unwavering sense of purpose. My fascination with the majesty of ancient creatures was sparked by the iconic film 'Jurassic Park.' As I moved to the bustling metropolis of New York City, living in proximity to the American Museum of Natural History, I found myself drawn to these prehistoric giants that were so close! even when just fossilized. I began visiting the museum to draw inspiration from their incredible exhibits, especially since it was an activity I could do for a really low cost. A decade later, I decided to embark on a new chapter in my artistic journey. It was time to define my artistic style, one that would be a culmination of my lifelong passion for conservation, my fascination with dinosaurs, and my experiences in the visual fields, having worked in film, television, theatre, media, design, and marketing. I was determined to channel all of my skills and creativity into my art, not only as a form of self-expression but also as a means to convey a message of hope. My artistic journey isn't just about capturing the beauty of endangered animals and ancient creatures. It's about inspiring hope in the hearts of all, even in the darkest of times. My personal experiences, including the challenges in love and periods of unemployment, have taught me that in the face of adversity, we must find our own light in the darkness. For me, working on my passion is not only a form of self-expression but a testament to the fact that there's always a way to find hope, even when it seems elusive. Through my art, I aim to inspire others to discover that same spark of hope within themselves, help others, especially those who are almost fully lost, to recognize the importance of conservation, and to appreciate the magnificence of the natural world. Join me on this journey of art, conservation, and hope as we work together to illuminate the world, one canvas at a time.

column25: I am Jess Hurley Scott, an artist whose craft is a meticulous dance between precision and illusion. My canvases are not traditional; they are layers of Lucite panel painted in reverse by hand. This proprietary technique transforms the boundaries between two-dimensional painting and sculptural art. Through this intricate layering within custom acrylic framing, I seek to explore the realms of illusion, perception, and the profound emotional connection we have with the sea and the natural world. My art is a journey through the looking glass, where reality meets interpretation. I begin with my own photographic references, capturing the raw essence of nature, particularly the sea, which has always held a special place in my heart. But my work is not about mere replication; it's about deconstruction and transformation. I dissect these compositions into layers, each painted with meticulous care, utilizing translucency and depth to craft my own interpretation of reality. The sea, with its ever-shifting moods and timeless beauty, is a recurring theme that resonates deeply within my work. It serves as a powerful metaphor for the ebb and flow of life, and the profound emotions it stirs within us. My art seeks to capture not just the visual beauty of these landscapes but also the underlying emotions they evoke. The scale of my work varies, from near miniature vignettes that draw viewers in with intimate details to dramatic, sweeping seascapes that engulf them in the vastness of the ocean's grandeur. Each piece is a carefully orchestrated symphony of technique and emotion, inviting viewers to question their perception and immerse themselves in the layers of meaning I have woven. Through my art, I hope to challenge conventional notions of reality, inviting viewers to explore the intricate dance between the seen and the felt. My goal is to create a space where the boundaries between the tangible and the imagined blur, where the sea becomes not just a subject but a mirror to our own emotions and aspirations.

column25: I am a visual artist based in Minneapolis, Minnesota. My art process includes painting, printmaking, collage and installation art. Having majored as a painter in University, I use painting as a means of experimentation, exploring ways to capture the fluidity and energy of paint. I like to play with the ideas of vast spaces typically using landscape and seascapes as inspiration and a starting point. I am not looking for a formula to reproduce but rather a constant curiosity with a sense of discovery. My method consists of researching all kinds of techniques on paper; stenciled, stamped, dyed and resist, creating a unique palette of color and textures. I build up multiple layers of paint, paper and cuts allowing for a spontaneous burst of hues vibrating to the surface. Continuing to explore and experiment with surface ideas as a quality researching new painting techniques and textures found in the architecture and provocative landscapes from memory. I´ m interested in recording the current moment by documenting a material record and it´ s mark making, then transforming the after effect. Memorable textures and surfaces play an important role to my palette and expression.Contemplating the beauty and ethereal quality of nature of places i once called home, I use various painting materials and methods to evoke a sense of space. Surrounded by different cultures, I use my life experiences as a way of seeing. My work is transformative by nature rearranging compositions and fragments through surface abstraction. Making an effort to integrate visual surface with a physical space. I use painting as a means of experimentation, exploring ways to capture the fluidity and energy of paint. column25: I see light as more than just a tool for visibility. Light is both paint and brush. It tells us time and place, rhythm and energy and movement, it directs attention and elicits emotion, and it also provides color. My current works use light to paint color on abstract geometric sculptures that can be photographed without any need for digital manipulation. They are examinations of color applied through light and serve as an entry point for the viewer to think more deeply about the ways light can be used. Prior to creating 'Fine Art', I created theater as a lighting designer and moving light programmer. My work now is a direct reaction to many shows that I have seen or took part in creating that felt they must have a meaning or message to be called art. I believe that art can exist for the sake of beauty and wonder and as such I strive to use light as the catalyst for sharing those beliefs.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a sculptural fiber artist, redefining my use of portraiture through wool to create soft sculptures that speak to geographical histories, womanhood, spirituality and community, in relation to the body. My work exists figuratively, in full or often fragmented forms, and is offered in the form of traditional sculpture, installation, performance, video and collaboration. Inspired by West African masks and Yoruba ritual practices, these needle felted and fiber based portraits morph into a form of storytelling that re-interpret who can be worshiped, challenge archived histories and narrate Black stories, experiences and memories. My work has, and will always involve a visual coded language that translates to, and is inspired by African diasporic communities- pulling from the culture and history of specific spaces where we exist/existed.

column25: Nastassja Swift is a multi-disciplinary artist holding a BFA from Virginia Commonwealth University. In 2022, she was a Distinguished Fellow at the Penland School of Craft, and was recently commissioned by the SCAD Museum of Art to perform “Turning Seeds” a collaborative and site-specific masked performance. In 2019, her short film, and collaborative performance, “Remembering Her Homecoming,” premiered at the Afrikana Independent Film Festival, and screened at the Virginia Film Festival in Charlottesville. Nastassja has been selected for a Public Arts Commission in Richmond, VA, her first large scale public art project. She is the recent recipient of the Center for Craft: Craft Research Fund Artist Fellowship, a VMFA Fellowship, a Dr. Doris Derby Award, an Art Matters Fellowship Award, the inaugural Black Box Press Foundation Art as Activism Grant, and a Virginia Commission of the Arts Fellowship. Her work has been acquired into the Grace Linton Battle Memorial Fund for the Arts Collection, as well as Quirk Hotel in Charlottesville. Nastassja has been included in the Berlin publication - SomeMagazine, RVA Magazine, RHome Magazine and the Stranger, a Seattle publication. She has participated in several national and international residencies and exhibitions, including her solo exhibit in Doha, Qatar in 2016, NADA Miami, The Urban Institute of Contemporary Art in Michigan, and fellowships at the Vermont Studio Center, SPACES, and MASS MoCA. Nastassja is currently living and working in Virginia.

column25: My work focuses on examples of the fraught nature of the Western art-historical canon and its relation to the present. By mashing up ingredients such as 3D scans from museum collections, academic figure drawing techniques, and modernist compositional and material tropes I create seductive monstrosities in order to examine art history, and my place in it, through a critical, revisionist lens. I use a process that unifies interests in drawing, painting, sculpture, and photography. 3D software provides a space where all of those forms of making can exist at once. The presence of my hand in the work is displaced and slippery. I constantly shift back and forth between digital and analog: making sculptures that I only interact with using a stylus on a flat screen, then lighting and photographing them within that same digital space. By rendering them by hand with paint, ink, and carbon I bring the digital process back to being the subject, rather than object, of the final work.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In recent years, my research has evolved. It has expanded to incorporate journalistic techniques with contemporary art practices. I have utilized my digital collage technique with a variety of communities in the hopes of highlighting their concerns, pleasures and history. While these construction methods have remained constant, each project has ended with a variety of results ranging from sculpture, to digital photography and video. An example of one of my community-based projects would be one I’ve continuously worked on in rural Vermont, where I have been photographing dairy farmers affected by the dairy pricing crisis. Many of the farmers’ families worked these plots for generations, and are now losing their land due to historically low milk prices. This has resulted in many families leaving their farms and has even increased the suicide rate. After visiting and photographing over the years, I created a body of work, Why buy the cow. Right when I was preparing this series for another exhibit, I was offered a residency in eastern Maine, where I was to execute a similar project with their local farmers and fisherman. The pandemic shut this down, though they offered me the opportunity to do it virtually from home. I did my photographic series about Kentucky farmers and held online events with the Maine Farmland Trust. I started a project where I visited over 12 different KY counties, 14 farms and businesses that worked with them. The project focused on the issues that each county had to deal with and show the variety of obstacles they tackle. While making the series, my Vermont Dairy farm series caught the attention of my College President, who visited the show and is now helping me publish it through the University and we are now planning on exhibiting it around the state. I have always wanted my work to help the communities that have volunteered or allowed me to document them, but it wasn’t until the past few years where I feel I may be doing just that. With the Vermont dairy series, I split all proceeds with a VT non-profit that supports small scale farmers. With the Kentuckian Project, it now continues into its third year with the support of the Lexington Arts Foundation and the University of Kentucky Special Collections Archives. All images are shared for free with the volunteer subjects and are included in the image archive. Though projects like these have been amazing and helped me better connect to my home state, it has been my experiences with artist residencies that has truly given me the best opportunities to network and experiment. Artist retreats and residencies have different mission statements, though three common threads I find they often share are networking with fellow artists from an international community, providing extended time to work away from distractions and to creating work in collaboration or in reflection of a specific community. I have utilized the 12 residencies I have attended for different projects. Some were used to finish a project in the seclusion of their studios, while some were to collaborate with a specific community located nearby. I have found that residencies are an amazing opportunity to add to your creative practice and experiences. Aside from sharing my artwork with others, I also want my community to see, produce and bring outside art to my city or even state. I have attempted to do this in a few different ways. One being attending public forums and being an active participant; speaking on panels, lectures, radio or tv interviews and art groups on the academic and civic level. Secondly, I am always trying to make connections between national and international artists and organizations with my community. I am often bringing artists and curators to events at my university or to the public. And I am eager to aid a regional artist on how to get funding, exhibitions, studio visit or even lecture at distant art hubs. I believe that your art career is not only going to benefit from your own successes, but from the growth and vibrancy of the community you surround yourself with. By the time I put my camera down and retire, I hope I have followed my life rule of “always leave a place better than you found it”.

column25: My background in visual art, music, community organizing, and social science informs the hybrid nature of my artistic research. Working predominately across new media and social practice, a recurring, formal characteristic of my work is the subversion of technology—often cast as a distancing agent—to facilitate intimate understandings and responses. I understand technology, or techné, in its ancient sense, as inclusive of both social technics and the ethical frameworks through which technologies come into being. In light of this, my works in games, video, sound, print media, photography, text, software, and installation environments take the social conditions they produce into equal account as their spatial configurations of images and objects. Recently, I have become inspired by new research that frames the utopian imaginary not as a fixed end state, but rather as a methodology that can be democratically cultivated. As such, my current work is rooted in developing rehearsals for this process. To this end, I embrace collaboration and collective authorship reflective of the degree to which cultural production is always already grounded in a web of interrelations and mutualities of being. Each piece begins as a research project that explores solidarity economics, liberatory technologies, (more-than)human ethics, and other social ecological territories. I then deploy various devices—absurdity, nostalgia, pop culture, ambient sound, sublime imagery—to disarm viewers and allow them to approach the more serious subject matter embedded within. Through this syncretic and discursive approach, I (re)organize sensory information to empower people to see and feel in new ways.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My research focuses on urgent issues we face today, especially the global climate crisis and its impact on our future. I aim to use an interdisciplinary approach to connect human experiences with environmental data, promoting empathy and a deeper connection with other species and our planet. My research-creation is grounded in principles of collaboration and co-creation. In my research-creation, I collaborate with scientists, engineers, artists, and designers to visualize environmental phenomena, like air pollution and soil contamination, and incorporate this environmental data into videos, sculptures, and immersive experiences and installations. Presently, I am concluding a PhD in Digital Media in the Computational Arts Department at York University where I am a Sensorium Centre for Digital Arts & Technology Graduate Research Associate and Graduate Fellow of Academic Distinction. My trajectory of artworks that aim to hold space for reflection regarding human agency enacted through the constructed world and are generative of imagined alternative planetary futures. Questions I am asking with these projects include whether environmental sensing and visualization artworks act as an empirical interface for grasping our complex, interwoven, beyond-human ecologies and inspire new ways of thinking about the planet? Furthermore, might they lead to novel methods for response and environmental engagement with the ongoing event of climate change?

New Orleans, USA) is a computational media artist whose research questions center around ecosystemic human and plant relationships in relation to the present global climate crisis and speculative futures. ARTIST STATEMENT:
The repetition of the circle also serves as a reminder of the cyclical nature of life, and the idea that everything is connected. The negative space within and around the circles represents the unseen and unknown, a reminder of the vastness of what we cannot see or understand. My goal is to create pieces that inspire contemplation and provoke a deeper understanding of our place in the world. I want my work to serve as a reminder of the beauty and complexity of the universe and to encourage the viewer to see the connections and possibilities within their own lives The collection of the ink drawings explore the depths of life from its most basic manifestations. It is a journey through the microscopic worlds of the primitive circle and the macroscopic tropes; from cell, mandala, seeds, trypophobia, laundry or a planet. I sort, stack, copy, and otherwise manipulate these simple forms until they become a system suggesting a pattern, an interaction, a narrative even. This system forms a larger union that anchors the drawing: an ordering of complexity.
