column25: techn010ffspring.com/ !!! technoloffspring !!! = animated films and interactive games of absurdist technological beings birthed from “IntertwinedHerStories” originating from the cross-section of *-gender-*-technology-*-lens-based-media-*-the human body-*-caregiving-*. These animations and games propose a reimagining of what “technology” might mean in the context of an absurdist caregiving society and originate from an “entangled constellation of caregiving” as subjects of care and caregivers themselves. They are digital assemblages of altered and combined imagery, found and developed, that assembles linkages between creative (technological and scientific) work of women from the present and past. !!! techn010ffspring !!! are complex machines that weave together an “Entangled Constellation of Connections” between the labor of women as caregivers and as the inventors of a wide array of technology, including step-ladders, ironing boards, hair brushes, syringes, typewriters, beds, pockets, clotheslines, washing machines, dough mixers, adding machines, pessaries, to name a few. The !!! techn010ffspring !!! are digital-machine-assemblages, contraptions built from historic and contemporary building blocks—both found and generated imagery related to women and technology. Found imagery includes patents by women, old picture books of women working with domestic technology, historic photographs of women working with industrial, manufacturing, textile, and communication technologies, AI generated imagery, and images of contemporary technology referenced in media articles. From these found elements, the artist develops 3D-CAD (computer-aided-design) style components with textile-like fabric coverings. Initially designed as 3D machines, these !!! techn010ffspring !!! are animated to show their movement. Drawing upon her training in mechanical engineering, Sarah Buckius then creates “tapestry-engineering drawings” with annotations and details that are layered over textile-like patterns. Velvet fabrics with original patterns made from disassembled and collaged elements of patents by women frame the digitally printed canvases. These tapestry-engineering drawings connect the monumental technologies of both textiles and the computer, calling attention to the fact that textile technologies laid the foundation for contemporary computing technologies. Weaving together the two technologies in the work references Buckius’ work with 3D-CAD design as an engineer, digital media and sewing as an artist, and caregiving work as a mother that involves countless textiles. All of the references hold personal and political meanings for Buckius. As the mother of three small children, two of whom were five-weeks premature and in the NICU for nine weeks, and as a recent survivor of breast and uterine cancer, Buckius has experienced intense caregiving for others and medical care for herself. These emotionally and physically laborious experiences interface with a variety of technologies: brushing the hair of premature babies’ scalps (hair brushes), washing blankets and sheets after twins’ constant vomiting (sheet and blanket textiles, washing machines), special bottles and nipples to feed babies with feeding issues (baby bottles and nipples), undergoing robotic-assisted laparoscopic full hysterectomy (da Vinci™ Surgical Robotic System), stitches for lumpectomy (medical sutures), beds (baby cribs and places of healing for herself), to name a few. Trained as an engineer and an artist, Buckius’ machines are intentionally complex, layered, and illogical or absurdly logical. In the nature of women’s caregiving, they teeter between order and chaos. Her “digital tinkerings” tell epic tales of motherhood, technology, female bodies, and commerce—both personal and externalized through women’s inventions and early forays that bridged caregiving and commerce. Buckius' work proposes improvisation as a form of absurdist resistance to, and alternative to, patriarchal, capitalist, production-based, and seemingly rational, useful, logical systems. Plushies are all the rage with kids these days; the artist’s kids love them. No wonder! They are squishy, soft, playful, cuddly, and huggable, so important in inventive children’s play. Doll-like artifacts have been found dating back to 3000–2000 BC as an imagination-inspiring source of social and emotional learning, creativity, and the play that is so necessary for healthy human development. AND, someone had to make the dolls! Dolls have been made by children and adults. Doll making and manufacturing connects to the work of women, historically and presently. For these Patent Plushies with Pockets, the artist used imagery of patents by women as a starting point, and then the geometric/mechanical-like shapes were translated to 2D shapes that were cut out of fabric (originally designed by the artist) and then sewn into pillow-like plushy forms. Improvising with these forms yielded biomorphic forms. Adding mechanical, metal components found in historic photographs of women working with manufacturing and building technologies brings an unexpected hardness to the otherwise soft, squishy forms. Each has a pocket that holds important items, referencing the politics of pockets related to women’s clothing. The plushies are presented on doll stands like children’s playthings. The artist muses that her kids “can make dolls out of anything” and that they find these forms to be “cute” and deserving of empathy and care and want to play with them and their hair like “my little pony”. For the artist, making them has been akin to caregiving, a process that is a sort of metaphor for her caring for her children and caring for herself when ill. Caregiving is work that is almost 99.9% improvisation as every minute requires flexibility, adaptability, and fluid inventiveness. This process is mimicked in the process of making these plushies. Adding to and removing elements from them, disentangling their parts from hers (hair from her fingers and their other poky parts), trying to devise ways to attach different parts whose shapes do or don’t fit together, using fasteners and connectors, sewing, stitching, containing, letting loose, wrangling and letting go, fixing and knowing when to not fix, holding things in pockets, letting things stick out, openings, closings, combining, cutting, layering, fraying, mending, ripping, stitching. In order to make them, it requires lots of mending, improvised sutures, hair braiding, sharp pokes with needles and metal, lots of fine motor hand-work (a particular challenge for the artist as her fine-motor skills have been negatively impacted by the medication she takes to keep her cancer from recurring). They are fragile and sharp, protected and vulnerable, organic and mechanical, soft fabric and hard metal, curvy and poky, they fasten and connect, while letting parts stick out and unhinge.

column25: Artist M (MHB), is a traveler creating art in correspondence with her life-affirming thoughts. M exposes her audience to her queries about life and makes art accessible to the everyday person by curating art experiences. Themes emerging from M’s work open dialogue about an appreciation of beauty, worldly events, and the hardships of the mind. For M, life is always in transit and changing. Her artwork technique is experimental and playful with an evolving style grounded in sharing visuals to true stories and experiences often untold or mistold in African and Black culture. M conceptualized the exhibition, “Orientation: The Voice of the Youth (OTVY)”, a public art exhibition scheduled to display in Stapleton, Staten Island. The exhibition is an exploration and excavation of the thoughts, and feelings of Staten Island students and M on the world around them. OTVY will officially launch BLKSHE’s philanthropic arm, the Kamara Foundation, thereby establishing the foundation's youth arts education program addressing the arts education deserts in Black and Brown communities. In 2022, M founded ART NABE, a Black-owned and minority-operated creative community curating pop-up events spotlighting local talent with an emphasis on underrepresented entrepreneurs and creatives as vendors and performing artists. M is a Teaching Artist for the Kamara Foundation and conducts art-related workshops for intimate groups to bond through artistic expression. M has a B.A. in Art & Media Studies and Communication from Temple University and served as a member of the Manchester Arts Commission in Manchester, New Hampshire from 2020 - 2022. Click here to view M’s artwork.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Using textiles and fiber as a way to explore self-portraiture, my work investigates gender identity and body politics to gain a sense of belonging. Reflecting on the discourse of Rest and Care continuing to enter the conversation of contemporary museum practices, I consider fondly the exhibition ‘Black Power Naps’. Created by Afro-Latinx artists Navild Acosta and Fannie Sosas’, the work was an attempt at curating a safe space for people of color to recuperate on rest we’re so commonly known to lose (especially as a result of sleep deprivation, and the corporal punishment experienced by the enslaved). I question what “safe spaces” and uncensored emotions can look or feel like (particularly for black autistic and neurodivergent femmes/enbys), as well as the deep-rooted intersection between museology and the arts. Inspiration for this work are the books ‘Mouths of Rain: An Anthology of Black Lesbian Thought’ edited by Briona Simone Jones, ‘Black Aliveness, or A Poetics of Being’ by Kevin Quashie, and the song ‘Cobra’ by Megan Thee Stallion. For many creatives, the production of a work is its final stage of making. As both an archivist and multidisciplinary artist, I choose to explore the structured yet malleable quality of archival theory in my sculptures. They serve as a means of preservation; found within the ember-like quality of objects dipped in hot beeswax, and the archival nature of encapsulating time through silkscreened poems.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As “formerly” colonial-subjects, I think that it is more important than ever to start reclaiming the role of telling our own stories. Having always had an interest in the film and creative industries but often not seeing authentic representation of voices like my own; it has become my life’s mission to excavate and amplify other traditionally-underrepresented experiences and stories. I believe that this is one of the best ways we can not only self-empower ourselves, but also self-determine the rest of our own futures (despite having to accept the resultant influences of colonialism). In order to do this to the best of my ability, I have worked across multiple exploratory mediums and with other African-Futuristic artists to explore world-building an Afro-Utopian future that we can all authentically exist in. Rather than continue trying to fit into some narrative that places me further away from ‘the Other,’ I have now decided to lean completely into this Otherness, and to dedicate my practice to world-building the futures of Afro-Utopia. In this, we choose to build our own worlds rather than continue responding to white hegemony; and this is how I believe that we reclaim the role of telling our own stories authentically as members of the Diaspora.

column25: AMKMQ is a multi-disciplinary artist, writer and “world-builder.” Painting and drawing since she was about three years old, AMKMQ has molded worlds, beings and ideologies for herself through the use of a diverse array of mediums – ranging from painting and sculpture to videography, poetry and digital design. Her work often ends up a blended concoction of physical and digital art (blurring the lines between the two). Taking roots in Afro-Futuristic Feminism, AMKMQ’s craft seeks to redress her 21st-century, post-colonial hybridized scars – through the processes of introspection; post- and pre-colonial analysis; and creating her own lineage, fossils and ‘histories’ out of these almost-sickly, vibrant landscapes and ‘worlds.’ A philosopher at heart, her work centers around the following themes: Afro-Futurism, -Absurdism and and -Spirituality; Metaphysics; Black Female Mental Health; Feminism and the Black Female Utopia; Pre- and Post-Colonial Analysis; Color Psychology; the Politics of Remembering in Post-Colonial States; as well as African-Mythocracy and personal introspection. With all earnestness, she refers to herself as an ‘Apocalyptic Afro-Futuristic Traveler’ – describing her ‘journeys’ through the worlds she goes to in her minds and within her spirit as “traversing through colorful lands, places and beings in which [she] gets to exist exactly as she is, and destroy the worlds forced upon her in this reality.” Currently in her final years at the Brown University|Rhode Island School of Design Dual-Degree Program, AMKMQ studies International & Public Affairs and Painting while balancing a full-time professional art career.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I often refer to myself as a psychedelic artist. This style resonates through all of my art. My paintings are also heavily influenced by geometric patterns and the use of symmetry. A good majority of my paintings are what I call ‘totem poles’- the way I lay out the patterns results in a series of stacked faces or characters, not every viewer sees the same ones either, All of my paintings can be turned on all sides to reveal a new image. This is largely influenced by my own Pareidolia, the tendency to see faces and other images in random visual patterns. True to the psychedelic style, all of my work is UV reactive or glows. Other viewer have commented my work is like the The game “I Spy”. My paintings are conversation starters and can be very interactive as onlookers are pointing out and identifying various subjects that they see.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is to invite people into other ways of being in the world, and create forms of belonging that are agreed upon by people rather than by hierarchy. As I take up my ancestral connections to craft, I create costumes and performances that address my ongoing need for belonging as a queer, trans, disabled, rural and “unnatural” being. Art is also incredible because it provides a material space in which I can attend to the types of harm that my ancestors and I have caused through colonization – a process which relies on the internal violence of giving up our languages and ceremonies and forgetting our styles of dress. Part of my commitment to being an artist is that making garments is what my body most strongly wants when I slow down and listen – and what my mind tells me is useless. Whiteness has trained me to think that pursuits like engineering and science are the only “useful” ways of showing up in the world. Yet it was my Master’s thesis research at Princeton on modeling wind and solar portfolios for achieving net zero carbon in the American West that made me realize I did not want to be an engineer. I saw that we have all the technology we need for change, but none of the political will. I started asking myself what types of processes a body would have to go through in order to change the business-as-usual assumptions of what constitutes an “energy need,” and saw that refusing to look at our energy desires is an important bottleneck to change. I decided I wanted to work with the individual and collective processes of preparing a body to become available to change. If, for instance, a hip hop class is in part made possible by the norms that structure what people wear into the dance studio, what other garments could we wear to prepare ourselves for other forms of organizing our desires? My art asks questions about what becomes possible from intentional practices of collectively dressing ourselves. I want to run a company that makes costumes as ritual objects through which dancers and everyday people can plan for, embody, initiate, and feel change.

column25: I am a textile and performance artist, professional costume designer, amateur hide tanner, and aspiring shepherd born and based in Tiwaland (Taos). Through my artistic practices, I take up the call of Sylvia Wynter to inhabit a form of humanness that takes our hybrid nature as bio-mythos seriously: human being as embodied fleshly biology and as story, as spell. My work is about practicing other myths for being human beyond the ones that racial capitalism asserts as obvious and inevitable. I experiment with materials, gestures, movements, gatherings, and framings that allow these practices to unfold in the physical/storytelling realms. I think about and make containers, dissolve them, and repeat. What does it take to look over the edge of what we know ourselves to be, and leap? I come from a lineage of people who worked with knife and bone and tongue and dream to shepherd people through portals of death and birth. To attend to the pain and pleasures of these thresholds. Alongside my ancestors, I make altars and other rituals; connect with the materials of my surround; construct costumes; mutate and wear materials; devise choreographies; participate in unknowable movements; and write and think about these processes to honor what has been and imagine/remember what might be. I draw on the craft legacies of my ancestors who were reindeer herders (and later roadkill scavengers and poachers) on my mother’s Sámi side, as well as on the goat-herding practices of my father’s Appalachian mountain lineage. These ancestors teach me that resisting enclosure through embodied connections with four-legged creatures is a life commitment and a politics. By using scrap wool and the whole fleece when I felt and weave, by scavenging for roadkill to eat and to tan, by using discarded butcher’s hides, and by learning death rituals for animals, I seek to honor the kinships that make me human.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I have spent most of my life either studying theology, wondering why it is I'm alive, or making art. in all of these modes of being, big questions have always sought me out, fed me, bled me dry. “why are you here? what are you made for? what will make life matter?” in years spent interrogating both life and myself, a possible answer that I am exploring is that the only meaning that matters is the one you decide makes you full and alive. in my work, I explore existentialism and meaning-making through extemporaneous clay building. the body impulses and reacts while the mind forms connections and creates a meaningful story out of “nothing.” my process of creating simulates the act of being alive: making guesses at what is right, not truly knowing, yet out of wet clay and story emerges a significant life.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is the freedom to explore and express yourself authentically. As an artist, you have the power to channel your emotions, thoughts, and perspectives into tangible creations that can inspire, evoke emotions, and connect with people in profound ways. It's the liberty to pour emotions onto a canvas, sculpt thoughts into tangible forms, and compose symphonies from the depth of one's soul. The ability to create something new, to push boundaries, and to offer your unique perspective to the world is incredibly fulfilling. The creative process itself, with its challenges and triumphs, allows for constant growth, learning, and the discovery of new possibilities. Ultimately, it's the opportunity to share your inner world with others and leave a lasting impact through your art that makes being an artist truly rewarding.

column25: Cho's current artistic exploration delves into the phenomenology of visibility and invisibility within their paintings. Focused on presenting raw materials in compositions without creating illusions, Cho integrates natural light, space, surface, and shapes as integral aspects of their work. Light assumes a crucial role in interacting with the canvas fabric, influencing the created shadows. Traditionally a representational painter, Cho has recently challenged themselves to refine their understanding of color values, delving into monochrome and color-field paintings through minimal, conceptual thinking. The ongoing exploration of materials in both painting and sculptural works excites Cho, who aims to share this reality with the art world. Cho's artistic journey involves a study of philosophical hermeneutics, exploring the art of understanding by integrating specific elements into a coherent whole. The focus lies on the uncanny, seeking a minimalist expression by utilizing non-traditional duck canvas and juxtaposing shapes while emphasizing paint as a substance rather than just color. The search revolves around the interplay between the 'whole' and 'part of,' aiming to grasp the 'in-between' within their practice. Central to Cho's exploration is defining what minimalism signifies for their art and understanding how it relates to their methodology. Currently fascinated with integrating paintings onto more dimensional surfaces rather than solely hanging them on walls, this approach allows for a more immersive engagement with the surrounding space, transcending the conventional viewing experience.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Born and raised in L.A., I’m a first-generation Salvadoran American from a family of refugees, migrants, and exiles. My interdisciplinary work stems from my roles as a documentarian, educator, activist, and former community organizer. It is rooted in visual narrative being a critical tool for social justice and driven by the legacies of resistance within my communities and family in and outside of El Salvador. Using my own photographic imagery, other historic photos, collected objects, and appropriated pop-culture iconography, I’m invested in aesthetic politics, collective memory, and creating tactical interventions within social, cultural, and political contexts. I produce both short and long-term research-based projects that come in a range of forms, including multimedia installations, public interventions, web-based projects, performances, and community-based practices. My work has been used for educational purposes, journalism, legal evidence, human rights advocacy, protest, and documentary films.

column25: Neil “Clavo” Rivas, aka Tony Rivas, is a multidisciplinary artist whose work stems from his roles as a documentarian, educator, activist, and former community organizer. Rivas’ documentary work has covered numerous social movements and political events over the past 18 years, such as Black Lives Matter, Justice for Trayvon Martin Movement, Occupy movement, 2009 Salvadoran presidential race, 2009 Honduran military coup, La Vela de las Intrépidas in Oaxaca, South Central Farm, California farmworkers’ movement, School of the Americas Watch, and 2004 California State University statewide walkouts. From 2006-2016, Rivas produced Las Marchas y Intervenciones, a body of work consisting of photographs of rallies and other actions that he participated in within the U.S. immigrant and refugee rights movement, with a series of interdisciplinary projects through which those images were revisited, tactically transformed, and re-contextualized. The culminating project, Las Gran Marchas Revisited – is multifaceted and commemorates the ten-year anniversary of the two largest immigrant rights demonstrations in U.S. history, the March 25th & May 1st marches of 2006 in Los Angeles, in collaboration with main organizers and youth who participated. The collection of projects also includes Remember Me Part II: Phantom Sightings at MacArthur Park, a site-specific public intervention featuring life-size cardboard cut-outs that revisited the 2007 police invasion of famous park in L.A. during a rally. The intervention was followed by a multimedia installation about the invasion and one-year anniversary intervention. B The original photographs used to create the intervention were also used for a community-based newspaper and as evidence for police brutality victims during the invasion in a legal case that was won. Additionally, included is a project called Por Que Occupy – comprised of a billboard, sticker campaign, and participatory web-based platform that intersected the immigrant rights movement with the Occupy and Indignados (Spain) movements. In 2011, he created Clavo’s School for Young Superheroes (SYS) – a traveling school inspired by the institute where the X-Men are educated that creates and implements project-based learning curricula centered around developing aesthetic sensibilities, critical thinking, and civic duty. In 2012, inspired by SYS, he co-created 20/20 FOTO – a cross-border art program between El Paso, Texas, and Juárez, Mexico, in partnership with Nuestras Hijas de Regreso a Casa and the CCA Center for Art and Public Life. The same year, he created the U.S. Department of Illegal Superheroes (ICE DISH) – the largest investigative agency in the U.S. dedicated to the apprehension and removal of “illegal superheroes.” ICE DISH is still active. Rivas' work has been presented in universities, public spaces, galleries, and museums, such as the Crystal Bridges Museum of American Art, San Antonio Museum of Art, Museum of Latin American Art, Addison Gallery of American Art, National Palace of Culture of Guatemala, Museum of Art of El Salvador, Torrance Art Museum, and Cartoon Art Museum. His work has been published and written about throughout the Americas, Europe, Australia, and China by the likes of CNN Español, The Guardian (UK), NBC, Univision, The Huffington Post, The New York Times blog, The Washington Post, Democracy Now!, MTV, NPR, Lado B (Mexico), Prensa Contrapunto (El Salvador), ArtInfo International, Glasstire, Art Practical, Daily Serving, KQED, Colorlines Magazine, SF Weekly, San Antonio Current, Arkansas Times, Upworthy, and others. His work has been acquired by Michigan State University Libraries, Kala Art Institute, along with private collections in the U.S., Australia, and El Salvador. He has been the recipient of several awards, grants, and fellowships, most recently from the Center for Cultural Innovation, California Community Foundation, National Association of Latino Arts and Cultures, and SEIU–United Service Workers West. He has done artist residencies throughout California and Guatemala, at sites such as Angel Island State Park, Kala Art Institute, Richmond Art Center, Intersection for the Arts, Children’s Discovery Museum of San Jose, Galería de la Raza, CFCE Antigua, and El Nahual. Rivas earned his MFA in Fine Arts at California College of the Arts and his BFA in Art Photography at California State University, Long Beach.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My projects investigate not only how we curate art in a room, but as well, work to deeply understand the diversity of experience for all individuals. Rather than privileging the experience of having access to all of the senses (especially sight and hearing), I explore work that strays from a single type of medium or engagement. I am interested in creating work that destabilizes primary language, prioritized audio, or single-perspective visuals— resulting in immersive spaces, tactile works, and often multi-faceted interactive/activated spaces. I am currently exploring memorials as part of the intersection of art and experience. The ongoing focus of my work is to continue to explore the curation of these complex spaces with the attention to womxnhood; Black, brown, indigenous history; queer narratives ; bodies with ranges of ability; and the gap into which the experiences of under -privileged/-resourced communities; displaced / misplaced / mistreated humans (-incarcerated and -immigrated), and so many others tend to disappear.

column25: I use my art making to explore and expand on the hand that life has dealt me. I try to live by the saying “Jack of all trades, master of some”. I'm always wanting to learn new mediums and combine what I’ve known for years. Even though I’m a painter through and through, if I see another medium I wanna learn I figure out how I can create in that form until I am satisfied with the result. I am also a big believer in art as therapy, so much so that I named a show I had in 2020 “Art is Therapy”. A show that's all about my last body of work I did in 2018 while reflecting on a previous manic state. I tried to dissect why I created what I did and how I felt while creating the new works in response. The show sadly wasn’t made public due to the lockdown being instated, but it was filmed and posted on YouTube a month later. All this to say art has been and always will be my way I process and heal.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artwork, I create virtual dreamscapes, portraits of mystic figures, and surreal vignettes that explore being neither here nor there. To this end I am particularly interested in themes of otherness, ambiguity, transformation, and fluidity informed by multiracial, neurodiverse, and queer identity intersecting in a digital world. My work is often eclectic and experimental, not married to a singular style or modality. In my art a tension arises both visually and of process, between loose improvised elements and precise organization. This oscillation between chaos and tight control is expressed, emphasized, and challenged in my work as an analogue to my experience with ADHD. My practice continues to grow more introspective as I grow older. Memories and dreams are subjects informed by imagined possibilities, inherited trauma, and fractured histories. With awareness of those histories, I am interested in topics or erasure, passing, and how the politics of identity change through time and different contexts. Through visual plasticity and transformation, I celebrate the joys of mixture, the unknown, and possibility.

column25: Warren Armando Pope (b.1990 Tacoma, WA) is an artist, public artist, and architectural designer based in Seattle, WA. He creates eclectic, experimental paintings and digital art that explore being “neither here nor there:” themes of otherness, ambiguity, transformation, and fluidity informed by multiracial, neurodiverse, and queer identity intersecting in a digital world. Topics of representation, passing, fractional history, self-conflict, and erasure are frequent themes. Warren has shown work at the Vashon Center for the Arts, Museum of Museums, and Northwest African American Museum. In addition to studio art, Warren leads public art projects supported by Rohleder Borges Architecture. Since joining RBA, he has been a finalist for 4 projects and completed two: ‘Together, Ascend’ in Homer, AK and ‘Swoon’ in University Place, WA. He frequently brings his public art experience to the classroom as a guest speaker, reviewer, and occasional teacher at the UW Summer Youth Program introductory architecture studio.

I'm a lover at best and a fighter to the core - these are my two extremes. Whether I am creating a music mix about sexual pleasure, writing a poem about self-acceptance, or digitally painting a crowd of protestors demanding a “ceasefire”, I want my statement of solidarity to be clear and never misconstrued: I am a systemic abolitionist and activist who will never dilute the truth. I am a believer in the power of the people always. Pleasure and healing are not luxuries, but a birthright.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
I’m a mixed-media (oil, acrylic, digital) painter who has been classically trained at California State University, Fullerton. My focus and primary interest pertains to the human figure and its form. The play of color can be detected in my work as a reflection of my own moods and emotions throughout the years. It is an homage to the female form; it also portrays my own internal struggle with depression and anxiety and broken relationships. While the figure is the focal point of my paintings, the backgrounds also reveal a state of mind. In contrast to contemporary figurative works of fragmented, decaying body parts, I reminiscent on the tradition of Romanticism--the body as a beautifully agonizing expression of emotions--in the footsteps of W. H. Waterhouse and John Singer Sargent. I suppose I’m a hopeless Romantic.

column25: I am an Iranian artist who moved to the United States in 2012. In Iran, in the first decade of my path as an Artist, I mostly made my photographs in interior spaces (usually my parents' house), and I worked primarily with objects. I was drawn to work in places with significant personal histories. After moving to the United States, I lost that fortress of indoor spaces that contained such soul and history for me. Here, I became a tourist and found that I could not pass a certain threshold of wonder in looking and could not trust my photos enough to consider them something beyond the surface. I stopped taking pictures at the beginning of my residence in the United States. Since 2013, I explored my family's photographs, public photos, and documents of Iran's tumultuous political history. By 2014, this exploration evolved into a project where I embroidered traditional Persian motifs onto my family's damaged negatives and significant historical photographs from Iran and Iraq's war period and the Islamic Revolution of 1979. This embroidery was an act of restoration, an expression of my outrage, and a political statement against the oppressive regime. Through this work, I sought to reclaim and recontextualize personal and collective memories, stitching my narrative over the images of the past. In 2015, a trip to Iran led me to start an archive of family photos, revealing painful gaps. My parents said to me that they burnt many photographs after the 1979 Islamic revolution, a drastic act mirroring their tumultuous relationship with photographs. The loss ignited a lasting emotional scar, leaving behind fear and numbness. Obsessed with the surviving photos and those lost, I collected many old family photographs from Iran, extending beyond my own family. Listening to others' post-revolution photo stories highlighted three poignant acts: Burning, Tearing, and Burying. These became the conceptual and visual bedrock for my 'It's Hard to Kill' trilogy. A trilogy that delves into the complex themes of identity, historical recollection, and constructed memories. Over the past eight years, I have embarked on a journey through the photographic histories of Persian families, seeking to preserve their narratives by reproducing and transforming their old family photos. This endeavor led to the creating of the first part of the 'It's Hard to Kill' series trilogy in 2017. The series began with reimagined moments of photograph burning, encapsulating the first act of obliteration. My current focus is on the subsequent acts—tearing and burying. The act of tearing, often explored through studio visits and collaboration, is nearing completion for public presentation. Meanwhile, the burying act started to take shape recently as I experimented with embedding photographs within layers of wooden panels, creating a self-made landscape to unearth these censored histories. Residing in Los Angeles from 2018 to 2023, I was immersed in the stories of the Persian diaspora, who fled Iran after the 1979 Islamic revolution. Their experiences and the less-censored photographs they preserved inspired a deeper examination of the censorship tactics of the Iranian Islamic regime, particularly against religious minorities. This project integrates the implicit self-censorship within Iranian photo albums I saw in Iran with the stories of the Persian community in the diaspora. In May 2023, I curated a photo album featuring partial tears and deliberate arrangements to expand this work in volumes. Also, I consider my old negatives/photos as an archive I can work with now differently. It's how I made 'Objectivity and Subjectivity' in 2019. Those are photos that I took in my 20s to encapsulate my childhood home, objects, and memories. And after ten years, in my 30s, I questioned those moments/memories by manipulating those negatives. My projects are either photographs or heavily reliant on photographic elements. However, my creative expression also extends to performance art and installations. For instance, in the summer of 2018, I presented an installation and a performance piece at the National Hispanic Cultural Center in Albuquerque. This work delved into the complex themes of borders and immigration. My ongoing exploration of art involves delving into issues of identity and nationality and their interactions within societies. My personal history profoundly influences my creative output.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My focus on slow drawing is deeply intertwined with my adolescent experiences of falling behind and requiring intensive hospital treatment due to a debilitating overthinking and inability to complete tasks without endless attempts to fix and improve. In a world structured with deadlines, visual arts grant me the time to submit to my urges for repetition, fixation, and reworking. My practice involves intensive mark making, erasing, rubbing, and layering to force into my chosen surface a memory of 'past lives' - the faint lines, scratches, and blemishes of prior sketches and ideas that could not be fully erased. With these processes, I create figurative drawings, paintings, and sculptures with subtle surrealist undertones, depicting young solitary figures transfixed in vacant dreamscapes. I explore the perpetual limbo that is induced by traumatic states of obsessive and repetitive behavior - fixation, self mutilation- in relation to stillness from shock. From a gender context, my portraiture also aims to speak to young girls, women, and queer individuals disturbed by their physical and mental form. My work speaks to the ashamed, the restless, and obsessive; trapped in visceral cycles of alteration and completion, frustration and release, life and death. I hope my static drawings shatter their stillness and move them to make peace with change.

column25: I come from two worlds, but I belong to neither one. I am a first-generation Lebanese-American attempting to navigate the hyphen between two disparate identities. I was born in Ohio, spent my childhood in California, and my adolescence in Beirut, a city freshly wounded by a 15-year civil war. In 2002, I returned to the U.S., and have since lived in multiple cities in search of a place to call home. When I return to Beirut, I make photographs. When I return, I take the images apart and force them into a state of disintegration, opening an investigation into the plurality of identity and the instability of memory. I demolish the city’s architecture, its interiors, my body, and the bodies of others. I reconstruct the broken images, sculpting them into new landscapes and rhythms, using repetition to mimic healing processes, to make trauma visible. What results are artificial, psychic landscapes that translate the sense of fragmentation, rupture and non-place.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my overall creative practice, I am committed to integrated approaches to honesty and structural social change. Through my multimedia, project-based work, I seek to raise consciousness around the stories we tell about ourselves as individuals and our multiple communities, and to unearth (dysfunctional as well as sustainable) structural interdependencies among these stories. I believe deep, holistic awareness of our presently-lived stories (knowing where and who we are now) is foundational to structural social change (knowing where we want to go and who we want to be). In my current art practice, I am gathering data and narratives from Southern California-based adult self-identifying Asians (of different generations, income levels, educational backgrounds and walks of life in general) toward building a community-based “narrative ecosystem.” This ecosystem integrates facets of an individual’s identities and life experiences with each other, as well as with the multifaceted narratives of others in their communities. The ecosystem will synthesize one-on-one video and audio interviews, group activities and online surveys. My mixed media sculpture work also centers on holistic reflection and integration; my recent body of work explores my Korean American experience through the energies of the “erotic,” “Neutral” and “generic.” Audre Lorde’s erotic is soul-generated desire or intuition. Roland Barthes’ Neutral comprises idiosyncratic modes of aliveness (outside binary and oppositional paradigms) that do not settle into fixed concepts. The generic, achieved through tacit societal consensus, is a bland stronghold that anchors normative reality. Each of these energies offers its own version of comfort, belonging or freedom; as an immigrant growing up in different regions of the US, I (unconsciously) experienced these energies within me as (paradoxically) overlapping or competing. World-generating dynamics of entanglement and truthful paradox recur as practices, tools or puzzles I explore in all my work, through my and others’ lived stories.

She received her MFA from California Institute of the Arts in Art and Creative Writing in 2022. Her artwork has been internationally exhibited through galleries, film festivals, fairs, publications and community and office spaces, including in 2023 at SVA Theatre (New York, NY), Treehouse NDSM (Amsterdam, Netherlands), Pence Gallery (Davis, CA), Brand Library & Art Center (Glendale, CA), THE REEF (Los Angeles, CA), Irvine Fine Art Center (Irvine, CA), MOAH:CEDAR (Lancaster, CA), and online through Stoneboat Literary Journal and Shoebox Projects. In 2024, her work will be included in group shows at Crear Studio (Santa Ana, CA), The Artists Gallery (Los Angeles, CA) and Studio Channel Islands (Camarillo, CA). Her awards received for sculpture and video work include a MOZAIK Future Art Award in 2020 and 2021, the City of Lancaster Mayor's Award and Assistant City Manager's Award through MOAH:CEDAR in 2022, and Third Place through Pence Gallery, First Place through Shoebox Projects and an LA Art Documents Award through MOAH:CEDAR in 2023. She has written for Artillery and other arts publications and received a 2021 MOZAIK Future Art Writers Award. Through the nonprofit arts advocacy organization Arts for LA, she co-organized resource-sharing public events for Los Angeles-area artists as a 2023 Emerging Arts Leader, and advocated for legislative policies supporting the California arts economy as a 2022 Delegate. She was also a 2020 CalArts Research and Practice Fellow with ACLU Southern California's inaugural Artist in Residence, Audrey Chan. All of her curatorial projects have featured unedited, personally meaningful works by intersectionally diverse independent artists with varying levels of formal arts training. In 2023, she received a Vibrant Cities Arts Grant and California Arts Council Emerging Artist Fellowship. As a 2023 - 2024 California Arts Council Creative Corps artist grantee through Arts Council for Long Beach, she is developing a long-term community-engaged narratives integration project with self-identifying Asian adults based in Southern California.

column25: A significant starting point in my work is the reclaimed, post-consumer materials that I am determined to give new meaning and value to. In centering secondhand garments as canvas, as worthy of effort and care and circularity, I feel reconnected to the history of tailoring & mending and am encouraged to consider the environmental impact of textiles and clothing. This practice was born out of a discomfort with shirts that are too long for my torso and the burning realization that mass-produced apparel does not account for the infinite variations of bodily proportion/fit. While improvements in the fashion industry require a large-scale change to our current capitalist mode of production through a focus on fair trade and the environment, the promotion of reuse and repair allows for personal control over consumption habits while bringing awareness about larger global topics. What surprises people most is the moment they see the garment as both a piece of clothing and art. They see in, sometimes the extremes of, my work, the potentiality of their wardrobe — sometimes they will think of a specific item they own, maybe something that doesn’t fit right, that has a stain or hole, or just isn’t eye-catching enough to pull from their closet and consider the next life of those pieces. My pieces display a range of simple upcycling techniques such as bleach, embroidery, and mending to more complex transformations of garments into self-expressive art. Although this work strays from traditional art in its use of clothing as canvas, the formal aspects of art that I incorporate add visual interest and can work to highlight the construction of the garment. I tend to focus on pattern, color, line, and value, while the principles of harmony (or opposite of that, chaos), emphasis, and variety determine the placements of the embellishments on a dynamic, 3-D surface.

column25: My work as a filmmaker is motivated by magic—I believe it exists everywhere if you know where to look, which inspires me most. This stems from the fact I grew up within two very different cultures, Belize, Central America, and rural Mississippi. While these worlds are vastly different, they both share a deep tradition of folklore and storytelling—a love for which I latched onto and never let go. My goal as a filmmaker is to tell genre stories and shine a light on minorities who are often forgotten and neglected in fantastical worlds. Origins of Us explores the birth of the universe through a femme, magical realist lens to provide an inclusive alternative history. The story unfolds over six chapters as an original poem guides us through this new world. Actress Jamie Neumann of The Duece, Lovecraft County, will narrate the voice-over poem. Through this visual and audio spectacle, the audience journeys through a reimagined beginning of humanity. I envision shooting the film component on super 8mm film with my cinematographer, Natalie Kingston, a 2024 Emmy Nominee for the Apple series Black Bird. She also shot the 2019 Grammy-nominated documentary Two Trains Runnin’. Natalie and I have worked on many short films, and the first feature film I directed was Shapeless, which premiered at Tribeca. I will submit the film to experimental film festivals and create an installation exhibit in my two creative communities, Los Angeles and New Orleans. I imagine playing the film through projections, soundscapes, and lighting installations that audiences can walk through as if traveling back to a time that doesn’t exist.

column25: Born and raised amidst the architectural haven of Chicago, IL, I was captivated by the city's seamless blend of contemporary, modern, and turn-of-the-century styles. This harmonious juxtaposition of old and new has profoundly influenced my artistic sensibilities, inspiring me to seek a similar balance in my creative endeavors. Enrolling at the Art Institute of Chicago, I yearned to delve into the depths of artistic expression. However, an innate yearning for personal discovery propelled me to embark on a solitary journey of esoteric and metaphysical exploration. For a decade, I immersed myself in the world of self-discovery, absorbing as much knowledge as possible about human consciousness and the mysteries of existence. Emerging from this transformative odyssey, I returned to my artistic roots, armed with a newfound understanding of human experience and a compelling desire to fuse art and metaphysics. My sculptures, cast in bronze, embody this fusion, exploring themes of reality, perception, and the enduring quest for self-actualization. Each piece serves as a testament to the transformative power of introspection and the profound beauty inherent in the interplay between the tangible and the intangible. Through my sculptures, I aspire to inspire and empower individuals to embrace their authentic selves, actively shape their realities, and embark on their own unique journeys of self-discovery.

column25: James Russell May Artist's Statement I am intrigued by how the subject matter and themes of traditional Western art can seem at once familiar and alien to the eyes of the contemporary viewer. This has become a primary element of my paintings. In my work I attempt to form a bridge between the present and the past, as well as the material and ethereal. I paint my figures in a heavily detailed, mannered, and realistic method requiring layers of painstaking work. It is a style that has grown from a fundamental fascination with the very act of painting and is based upon both an observation of life and a studied awareness of how the human form (and other subjects) has been portrayed in the art of the past. The figures produced in that mindset are then placed in an environment of abstracted painting and textures. The intended result is balance between two competing personal aesthetics. In my recent work I have sought to move away from ostentation and focus on distilled representations of the human form. Working with oil colors on metal, the reflective surface serves as a blank canvas for contemplation. The sometimes sparse compositions and refined gestures invite viewers to immerse themselves in the scene, by isolating and emphasizing specific body parts, I aim to evoke a sense of universality and empathy. In the absence of elaborate context, viewers are prompted to project their own experiences, emotions, and narratives onto the artwork.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist I have been fascinated with mass consumerism, waste, health, nature and women’s issues. I view my activity as an anthropological study or environmental ethnography that explores our cultural values, behavior and norms. My work is rooted in an exploration of cycles, circles, connection and disconnection. In addressing these issues, my current studio practice involves the use of discarded materials, including outdated electronic equipment, toys, medical supplies and shipping waste. Working in the tradition of “assemblage” and “collage”, I use found materials in concert with natural materials to create sculptural forms meant to examine our relationship with nature in this man made world. Recently I have been increasing the scale of my work moving towards large interactive installations and away from singular discrete objects. I am interested in both experiential and cognitive exploration of constructs within our society. Creating work that invites sensory experience addresses my desire to produce enhanced physical sensations in the viewer. Using iconic or nostalgic objects brings a framing of thought and emotion which is both personal and societal. I strive to highlight the consumerist quality of our culture and its disregard for the environment, social issues surrounding our health, and perspectives on women. This disconnection is contrasted with a connection to the natural elements in the work.

She gained a Master’s Degree in Expressive Therapies from the University of Louisville in 1996. Working full time as an Art Therapist for 15 years informed her work to incorporate community involved projects and interactive installations. Her experience in set building for Actors Theater and Bunbury Theater highlight her love of creating environments. She is the current Chair of the Enid Generations of Women Sculptors and the Louisville TimeBank. Her recent residency at the BigCi Artist residency in Bilpin, Australia and deep seeded wander lust has inspired her to seek more art-travel opportunities in the future.

There is something so magical and mystical about creating something tangible with just an idea and the labor of our hands. I never tire of creating something new, and even if something turns out great, I don't want to make another like it, I want to create something entirely new! It's both exciting and challenging to have an idea in my mind and then work with materials to see that become a reality. And then sometimes, I just start the process of creating and let the materials take the lead, working with the medium to create something never before seen. As an artist I also enjoy the challenge of working with different mediums and gaining skills in their use. Gilding is my current fascination. I began with simple mixed-metal foil that needed to be sealed, but once I felt confident in that process, I began gilding with genuine gold foils. What a fun challenge. I am always excited to start gilding and am just as happy when I have it completed. It is a wonderful challenge. I have other ideas about materials, new paints and processes to use and am looking forward to trying them.

column25: After providing shelter to a living creature, time, wind, waves, sun, sand and other sea creatures all exert their force upon a discarded shell, to transform each one into something completely unique. There is a fascinating beauty to every shell that is weathered, broken, bleached and sometimes dyed by the ocean floor. This wear on the shells is evidence of hundreds, possibly thousands of years of natures’ power on the shells. I aim to work with the results of this process to accentuate the new beauty created in each shell. I am drawn to the art of Kintsugi* and the message that value is found in embracing both the beautiful and broken places. The beauty in the imperfect is highlighted rather than hidden in shame. Repairing an object reveals the deep love the owner has for the creation; it is too precious to throw away, regardless of the damage, presenting the wisdom that there is value to be found in embracing the whole journey. Formed first by nature, transformed by the powers of the environment, each art shell becomes a truly one-of-a-kind, unrepeatable creation in KintsugiGrace® *Kintsugi – The Japanese Art of repairing broken things with gold. The brokenness is not hidden or glossed over, but rather embraced and highlighted.

column25: Faraz Ghorbanpour’s works tell the story of how one navigates through a repeating history of colonialism and capitalism towards personal independence. Throughout his life, he existed in predominantly white and exclusionary spaces. Upon experiencing the realities of social structures in Western and American cultures, art became the escape from the socially enforced normalities in daily life. Values of perfection within individualism and religious ideation permeates throughout his global understanding. He sees these as weapons used to harm those unable and unwilling to conform. As social structures are used to reject those that cannot adjust, Faraz uses his art to portray an escape from the world built around him. Having a history of sculpting to cut traditionally European motifs into wood and juxtaposing them with human figures, Faraz depicts scenes of people separating from and reinterpreting traditional systems the world was created around. From discussing social hierarchies, to uneven distribution of power and wealth, his main medium is wood, as it has a sense of temporality and an impermanence that warps over time. Since 2020, he has been sharing his work in shows and online. From larger sculptures, stop-motion animations, and relief sculptures, his work breaks down aspects of his life to compare with our current state of society. As we see the effects of capitalism and colonial ownership, his work aims to depict a hopeful resistance against those measures. Challenging social structures, creating an “other”, artificial scarcities, and the hyperfocus of stability, the artist confronts the norms we find ourselves in to become our most free self.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Each year, I feel it may be easier than the last. But I find myself yearning to be understood, while also not wanting to be consumed. The juxtaposition of these two notions haunts me while I navigate through this lifetime as a Black woman. A queer femme just now sprouting their idiosyncratic wings. The sound of the click clacking my super-long nails against the sides of my paint cups, rattling against the keyboard, are all pieces of the work. Endless voicenotes, tons of backspaces, the exasperated sighs - all make up the work. The process of the work is also the work. If I am bound to be gazed upon, I might as well take control of what it is that will be exhibited. Autonomy is one thing that I know that I’ve been striving to achieve - especially in my older years. Using a color palette of bright, vivid colors; as well as drawing upon the intricate colors that make up the undertones of Black skin - I feel that I am able to create my own gaze of self and the gaze of Blackness.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am curious about corpo-realities. About indigenous technologies that reside within the human body. About our habits of perception that often prevent us from seeing each other, even as we are looking directly at each other. Over recent years, I have been drawn to re-visioning representations of black bodies, bodies of color, queer bodies, and indigenous bodies. I unearth and gesture toward the vastness of the interior worlds of each person. I contemplate the precarity of the vulnerable pride that remains porous to the world. Shape, texture, color, and collective memories redressed: these are but “gadgets of thought.” This art is a way of thinking out loud about what it means to be a person among persons in an increasingly complex world. As a performance artist and embodied scholar, I am also interested in how rhythm, breath, and sensation factor in visual art. How the somasensory experience of the visual has a significant influence over perceptual inertia.

column25: I am Tawanda Chabikwa—a Zimbabwean-born, interdisciplinary, artist-scholar exploring ideas surrounding practice-led research and indigenous research methodologies. I investigate contemporary black Africana aesthetics and the conceptual imperatives that undergird their work. The work centers the concept of “selfcraft” through aesthetic practices in Afrikan lifeworlds. I hold a B.A. in Human Ecology from the College of the Atlantic, an M.F.A. in Dance from Southern Methodist University, and a doctorate in Africana Studies from the Department of African American and African Studies at The Ohio State University. Currently, performance, visual art, and research writing comprise my praxis.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am an artist who makes work exploring environmental issues connected to human behaviors using fiber, public engagement, research, and data-driven algorithms. I use weaving, knitting, and joining techniques to establish foundational environments that are added onto by building up narratives related to human activity. My work demonstrates the link to researched information through titles and project statements. Additionally, titles often reference intimate emotional transfers one might exchange while involved in a co-dependent relationship. I employ color, hand-dyed yarn, and painting to set the tone of the subject matter or location representation. I rely on materials with minimized environmental impacts, like hemp and Tencel, while sometimes incorporating otherwise throw-away items like single-use plastics. I utilize compositional methods that challenge the viewer to investigate further and to reflect inwardly. I integrate varied textures, material placement, and spacing rhythms to guide perspectives. For weavings, in particular, I use open and compacted material spacing to invite conversation and to provide obstacles. Both methods invite compromise and consideration of the landscape and subject. I also subvert traditional methodologies to challenge learned generational behaviors. Examples can be seen where fringe is added to weaving that wouldn't usually have it in a particular area or at all, when paint is added to already dyed fiber, and when yarn is used in open spaces to illustrate the basics of the underlined structure.

column25: Dawn Ertl is an artist born in the San Fernando Valley in Los Angeles, CA but has lived in many different parts of the state. Her experience of constantly moving while growing up between rural areas and cities affected how she interacted with others. She feels connected and disconnected simultaneously, allowing her to step back and view relationships and environments from a distance, even while being part of it. She works hard to form and sustain long-lasting relationships to cultivate and protect her curated communities. At the same time, she is comfortable being alone. In solitude, she finds other ways to connect through research and planning. The same perspectives that have drawn her to art have pulled her closer to social practices and fiber-based techniques, especially weaving, establishing a foundational structure while continuously supporting exploration and providing safety. Both genres rely on physical and mental interconnections to make them stronger and long-lasting. Dawn has exhibited her work throughout the United States at various galleries, including Four Fourteen, William Blizard Gallery, Seaver Gallery, Tiger Strikes Asteroid, Los Angeles (TSA LA), South Bay Contemporary, The Arcade, The Manhattan Beach Art Center, El Camino Community College, Los Angeles Municipal Art Gallery, Boston Court, as well as the Cerritos College Art Gallery.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is an exploration of the materials around me. Using traditional fiber arts techniques applied to materials that may not be thought of as art materials, I am interested in creating opportunities for the viewer to reconsider the things we leave behind or consider useless. Working with recycled, forgotten, and discarded materials presents a challenge as well as a continued source of excitement- I am constantly surprised with and constrained by what I find to work with. These materials are used to create new yet familiar spaces to play, relax, and feel together. Each work is the physical translation of my feelings and experiences into something tangible. Familiar materials from colorful cable housing, aux cords, and old headphones tucked in the back of that closet. Worn out tee shirts and fabric from the thrift store. Abandoned combs and weed-whip string, punctured innertubes, bubble wrap, and zip-ties come together to mirror the interplay between the effects of human relationships with a place and with each other.

column25: Amanda Banks is a self-taught mixed-media fiber artist currently residing and practicing in North Georgia. Born in Illinois, Amanda relocated to the South as a child, where the rural landscapes provide a source of energy and inspiration. Their work depends on and responds to the environments they inhabit. Amanda uses their surroundings as a source of materials- trash picked up from the street, second-hand yarn and fabric, boxes of forgotten things, or treasures foraged in the woods. The practice of intentional gathering and holding guides the timing and process of her work and allows the materials to act as a catalyst for creativity and exploration. Amanda has taught workshops privately and in collaboration with educational institutions. They have most recently exhibited at Swan Coach House Gallery, Atlanta Contemporary Museum, East Tennessee State University, Slocumb Gallery, and in Rome, GA, where she curated and exhibited in Protest & Power (Feburary 2023) and We Been Here (February 2022) in celebration Black artists from her region. Amanda is an alumni resident at Stove Works Gallery in Chattanooga, TN, Stay Home Gallery in Paris, TN, and an upcoming resident at Arrowmont Pentaculum in Gatlinburg, TN (January 2024). You can see her work in the Blue Heron Nature Preserve gallery from November 10th -December 16th, as well as the Red Dot exhibition at Stoveworks Gallery December 2023- January 2024. She is currently facilitating a conversation between metals and fibers, ideating immersive installations, and planning collaborative community-based projects. And applying for things…always applying. You can find them online at www.amandaworld.love and Substack.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Photography and writing serve the same need for me. I have to get it out of my head and put it into someone else’s brain. I work primarily in black and white and low-light, but some stories demand color. I believe that great animal portraits and great human portraits have the same qualities. A photo of any living being, should draw you into their world and make you want to know more. Every creature has a backstory, and a unique view of this life – a view and lessons that may surprise you, if you take the time to ask and listen. I have had a deep connection with animals since before I could talk and have been active in rescue for over a decade. Cats, especially feral cats, are commonly treated as throw-away animals, in my local area and nationwide. My photography is part of my work to penetrate the pervasive barrier of human exceptionalism and inspire a deeper appreciation of them for the amazing individuals that they truly are. When shooting outdoors, I prefer to do it at night, when it’s rainy or snowing and everything is amplified, when no one else wants to be out. The place I love is disappearing. I’m trying to capture what’s beautiful before it’s gone.

column25: Sandra Dalton is a fine art photographer focusing on portraiture and pictures that tell a story. She is best known for cat portraits, featuring feral and rescue cats, and her night photography. Growing up near metropolitan areas, she has chosen to live in the mountains for most of her adult life. Sandra started out as a writer. Raised by a photographer, she spent her early years in front of the lens, and only began to delve into photography in her 30’s. She has always had a stronger connection with animals than humans. Her work reveals her love of storytelling and her love of animals. Initially preparing for vet school, she quickly learned that a career in veterinary medicine was not the only way to help or work with animals and that her passions, and budget, did not align with the business side of being a vet. Diving into music and writing made more sense. The music side didn’t pan out in the beginning, but writing took an unexpected turn that stuck. While earning her living as a freelance writer, she started doing TNR and taming feral cats. And the cats quickly became her best photography models. She began showing my work in galleries and exhibitions in 2014. She eventually started volunteering for her local radio station and then the small animal rescue group that was just getting started in her area, while continuing to develop her art and slowly building her photography business. In 2020 the focus changed, what was needed of her changed. She knew it was time to delve fully into the things she loved most and could do the most good with and stepped away from 20 years of freelance writing to transition to pursuing photography fulltime. She joined the board of directors for Sangres Art Guild in 2021.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For years I have seen myself primarily as a writer, and have written thousands of pages in an ultimately futile attempt to express things that, it turns out, I’m unable to put into words. But what I struggle to get out on reams of paper I can often express in a single visual image. When writing to convince, an artist must be gentle. No reader will accept being told what to think. The effective writer’s task is to gently build a case but ultimately stand back and allow the reader to form the conclusion. The writer must never confront or accuse, only allow the possibility. I now create visual art that doesn’t stand back. I make art that confronts, art that accuses. Free from the limitations of the written word, my creative practice is wholly focused on distilling complex societal issues and collective policy choices into singular, emotionally resonant images drawn from my subjective reality. I focus on myself as the primary subject not because I find myself especially fascinating, but because my experience is representative of that of millions of disabled, chronically ill and medically marginalized people whose voices are ignored every day. By showing myself in the many unflattering, dehumanizing or invasive moments that are part of a disabled person’s life, especially when interacting with our medical system, I spare a sick comrade the indignity of having to do so.

column25: Tanja Hester is a multidisciplinary activist, artist and author based in North Lake Tahoe, California. In her visual art, she creates large-scale, color-shifted works in oil, acrylic, pastel and mixed media that address broad societal injustices, often through a personal lens. Inspired by the immunodeficiency she was born with and the degenerative joint disease that has become a disability, Tanja’s work gives viewers a glimpse into a world isolated by necessity, where reality and the fantastical often merge. Her recent work exposes aspects of disability and chronic illness that many viewers are unlikely to have considered, explores her distorted reality that grew out of COVID-19 era isolation, and depicts scenes of beauty within the horror of climate change. For example, in her “Unfinished” series, Tanja references typical unfinished portraits, but rather than finish the head and little else, as art viewers are accustomed to seeing, her self-portraits are complete only in the areas she can reach while laying or sitting on the floor, reflecting her limited energy and painting ability during periods of high pain and fatigue. The series is a conceptual look at what we collectively miss out on when we do not support disabled people and their desire to contribute meaningfully to society and culture. In addition to her studio practice, Tanja also writes extensively about social and environmental justice, and is the author of two books, most recently Wallet Activism: How to Use Every Dollar You Spend, Earn and Save as a Force for Change (2021, BenBella Books), winner of the Nautilus Awards gold medal for best social change and social justice book. For her dedication to activism, in 2023 Tanja was named alongside luminaries like Lil Nas X and US Senator Elizabeth Warren as a Changemaker by Money Magazine, one of fifty people changing the global financial system to make it more just and equitable. In her past life, Tanja was a consultant to Democratic politics. She earned a BA in English literature from the University of California, Berkeley, and cobbled together a DIY studio art education from a range of California community colleges.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Relationships to identity and the intersections of community heavily inform my art-making process. I find purpose in documenting lived experiences of myself and my community, and I see it as extremely valuable to record the nuances of trans lives and history through art-making. My work functions in the same way that the melting pot of the communities that I place myself within do, separate entities living in unity. Most of my work deals with transness, the body, community, and relationships. My works employ digital drawing, tattooing, and textile work made up of cyanotype and relief printmaking. Fabric held together haphazardly by loose threads, yarn, staples, and sutures mimic medical waste also included in these works. These materials and processes come together into a cohesive whole for a shared common interest: forming mine, and others personal experiences, thoughts, joys, and struggles as marginalized people into something very public, raw, and honest. fabric, text, and body imagery in conjunction with each other. My textile works emphasize the relationship of fabric to the body through their shared vulnerability and ability to be cut and stitched, transformed, and molded into something new, much like the ways that I, and other trans people have changed through our transitions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Why paint? Because a photograph does not tell the whole story. What I seek to express is emotion, mood, or “spirit” that a camera cannot capture. When I am watching a white egret start its preening ritual in its evening roost, and the setting sun casts a glow around its form, the silhouette and the predictable series of movements are like a dance that I just want to join, and share. It represents a moment of unguarded self-care, introspection and tranquility. I am particularly in love with large wading birds. I would say this is because I have a background in biology, but it is likely the other way around. My passion for the natural world, as well as my desire to understand it are what drove me to my studies. A chance experiment with paint led me to this career, and I find it to be more impactful than research or even teaching. My recent series features larger-than-life birds on canvas, in motion, flying and preening. Simple backgrounds allow the bird to be the focus and enhance the tranquility of the images. I often employ 23 karat gold similar to historic iconography to elevate the status of the subject and to play with the representation of glowing light. I currently paint oil on canvas but I am always exploring new media.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Its either a blessing or a curse, but it seems I enjoy finding a second use for old items. It is a good thing I work in metal mainly, otherwise I may have stores of paper, wood, and plastic too. Then I may just cross the line from artist to hoarder. I have always been frugal. In college I paid for gas by picking up beer cans on my way home from classes. Today I make sculptures from found metal objects and sheets of metal that other people have already cut their parts from. I like to say I am buying their holes to make my art. I spend most of my life recreating outside, so it is no surprise that I mainly make animals. I get a kick out of what I make and I hope the humor shows through to others. For example, a penguin sculpture in Eastern Washington is not a big seller, but have it carry a lunchbox, have a fishing pole on its shoulder, and title it “Hi Ho Hi Ho Off to Work I Go” and it sells. I start my process with a theme or a particular found object. I love sitting down with pen and graph paper and brainstorming ideas. I will produce 20 or more sketches of all of the ideas that could work. The tough part is deciding which works the best. I make an armature of rebar, bend metal “skin” to create the animal, and then go through my “boneyard” to find the perfect base and accessories. The following is a description of some of the cool parts I have in my “boneyard”. It is for a sculpture of a salmon leaping to catch a star. Under the salmon is a steaming cup of coffee and a base. I enjoy this sculpture because I know the provenance of most of the pieces. The base is a hopper that stored roasted coffee beans, from the Blue Star Coffee Roasters remodeling project. Blue Star had purchased the roaster from an old Italian coffee roaster in San Francisco. The coffee mug is from a propane tank made in 1948. The tank once belonged to friends who lived off the grid for 30 years, raising 2 children. They even brought in all their water. The handle of the mug is from a trampoline a teacher friend gave me when his youngest went off to college.

column25: I am a Newark, NJ native and a multifaceted African American artist weaving together design, quilting, history, and fashion anthropology. Rooted in the African American church culture of my youth, my earliest exposure ignited my passion for design. Having apprenticed with Fidencia's Dressmaking in Costa Rica, I later founded 'Brown Dezign,' specializing in alteration design. My journey continued in Marin, California, collaborating with 'Lea Contour' on custom-made clothing. Before attending City College of San Francisco for fashion design, I earned a BA in Cultural Anthropology, exploring the modern cultural implications of slavery in American society. Fieldwork in urban cities led to insightful interviews, showcased in the podcast 'New Era.' In 2016, I co-founded Transcending Arts LLC, inspired by The Jaimeo Brown Transcendence band's cultural concepts. Quilting became a central focus, drawing from Gees Bend Alabama's rich cultural symbols. The synergy with Jaimeo's music birthed a visual mosaic, encompassing wall quilts, quilted clothing, necklaces, and various quilted products. I see garment design as a powerful storytelling medium, expressing one's identity. My work is a bridge between a colorful past and a vibrant future, echoing my profound passion for African American art, history, and technology.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a first-generation Vietnamese immigrant artist, I draw from concepts of translation, memory, cultural symbols, affect theory, materiality, and research on Vietnamese histories to convey frictions at the intersection of cultural identity, colonial fragments, and transnationalism. My interdisciplinary practice employs experimentation and material transformation to combine my personal perspectives and my research on postcolonial transnationalism. My background in painting makes me attuned to the color scheme in my work, both as phenomenology and as a signifier of meaning. I am interested in popular culture, subversive translation, and the global circulation of artworks and images. I aim to make my work legible to multiple audiences (Western and non-Western) and offer an open-ended complexity within its postcolonial narrative. My work acknowledges oil painting as the point of colonial contact between Vietnam and the French in the 1920s. After the French’s introduction of oil painting to Vietnam, Vietnamese oil painting underwent further changes following the Vietnam War (1955-1975) and the subsequent influx of postwar Western tourism. The use of oil painting in my work questions the role of oil painting in Vietnam as well as what it means to be a contemporary Vietnamese artist. This work accompanies my research into Vietnamese art and cultural archives. My ethnographic approach goes against the grain of the Western art historical canon to embrace these fragments of archives.
