ARTIST STATEMENT:
Kaoru Domoto-Taylor aka k.d. : Artist Statement I am not just a doll maker, I'm a weaver of worlds. Each k.d. Doll is a portal to a universe where every child, regardless of their skin, eyes, or story, can step inside and find themselves reflected, celebrated, and loved. My journey began in the heart of Harlem, where I witnessed firsthand the magic of diverse communities and the power of play. Watching children from every corner of the globe laugh, learn, and grow together, I saw the void left by toys that didn't mirror their vibrant tapestry. It ignited a spark in my soul, a yearning to create dolls that wouldn't just entertain, but empower. k.d. Dolls are born from that spark. Each one is a meticulously crafted masterpiece, stitched with love and infused with the vibrant spirit of inclusion. The moment you meet one, you can't help but smile. Their eyes twinkle with possibility, their clothes whisper stories of faraway lands and boundless dreams. They draw you in, beckoning you to touch their lifelike hair, feel the warmth of their smiles, and embark on a journey of self-discovery. These are not mass-produced playthings, but handcrafted companions, each one unique like the children they inspire. They travel from my design board, where they first take shape as whispers of imagination, to the display table, where they blossom into tangible dreams. Every brushstroke, every stitch is a love letter to the diversity that makes our world sing. You'll find k.d. Dolls at bustling street fairs, joyful community celebrations, and cozy corners of the internet. They're everywhere children gather, weaving threads of connection and understanding. A single doll can be a beacon of hope for a child yearning to see themselves reflected in the world. A diversity set sparks a vibrant classroom discussion, igniting curiosity and empathy. Each stitch is a story whispered, a bridge built between cultures, hearts, and imaginations. More than just toys, k.d. Dolls are tools for empathy, seeds of self-love, and passports to a world where every child, regardless of their background, can see themselves as the hero of their own story. They are mirrors that reflect beauty in all its forms, windows that open onto worlds of possibility, and hands outstretched in friendship. So, when you choose a k.d. Doll, you're not just buying a toy, you're joining a movement. You're saying yes to a world where every child has a place to belong, a voice to be heard, and a heart that beats with the rhythm of acceptance. You're weaving a future where difference is celebrated, and imagination knows no bounds. I welcome new friends to a world where every child, and adults, too, can find their own reflection in the kaleidoscope of wonder. I decided to create dolls that everyone could enjoy and love together, no matter who they were or where they came from. The dolls, like the children and adults who love them, are all unique.

column25: Kaoru Domoto-Taylor: Artist Bio Kaoru's journey with k.d. Dolls began with a spark ignited in Harlem, USA. A young Kaoru, fresh from art school in Osaka Japan, found herself amidst a vibrant community brimming with stories waiting to be told. The girls she met, their laughter echoing in the Harlem YMCA halls, often gravitated towards dolls with lighter skin tones, a reflection of societal biases that tugged at her artist's heart. As a wife and mother in a blended family, Kaoru's world became a canvas of rich hues. Her husband, African American, and their son, a blend of African American and Dominican heritage, painted her life with vibrant shades of love and acceptance. This tapestry of experiences became the soul of k.d. Dolls. Each k.d. Doll is more than just a plaything; it's a celebration of diversity, a mirror reflecting the beauty of every skin tone. Crafted with love from Kaoru's own hands, these handmade rag dolls whisper stories of self-love and acceptance, empowering children to embrace their unique identities. From bustling community pop-up shops to online platforms, k.d. Dolls have found homes in hearts and playrooms throughout the city, around the country, and across the globe. Despite the challenges of a one-woman show, Kaoru's passion burns bright. The pandemic's shadow may have dimmed the lights, but her spirit remains undaunted. With a recent boost from a Columbia University Business School program, Kaoru's vision for k.d. Dolls took flight. Dreams of collaboration paint the future: a skilled dollmaker friend crafting bodies, a young intern lending a hand at pop-up shops, and most importantly, workshops where tiny hands weave their own dolls, celebrating the kaleidoscope of colors that make us human. The ultimate dream is reaching classrooms, placing k.d. Dolls in the hands of early learners, nurturing young minds with the message of diversity, equity, and inclusion. One school at a time, Kaoru hopes to weave a world where every child sees themselves reflected in playful eyes and stitched smiles. For Kaoru, k.d. Dolls are more than just dolls, they're threads of love, woven into stories of acceptance, waiting to be cherished by every child.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Combining elements of neo-expressionism, graffiti, and African art, I create mixed-media abstract figurative images, sculptures, and collages of the Black diaspora to celebrate, uplift, and communicate complex emotions of Blackness. My practice is rooted in expressing communal and transformative experiences of Black Americans with an emphasis on ancestral practices and lineages. Using a variety of mediums such as photography, clay, spray paint, wood, digital art, and acrylics, I redefine people, objects, scenery, feelings, and statements, allowing room for multiple perspectives. I use patterns, lines, shapes, and symbols throughout my pieces to pay homage to my ancestors, reproducing familiar visual and aural signs and arranging them in new conceptually layered installations. Created from a home studio, my work navigates and encourages Black liberation through visual depictions and influences of Black culture, community, sustainability, joy, and expression. Serving as a reminder of the many ways that activism, culture, and joy can coexist, I create art that is bold in color and complex in shape and style to stand out as an extension of the community members it reflects. My work encourages reflection and inspires change.

column25: PERIPHERY IS CENTRAL 'No Provenance' is my volume of faux ekphrastic poems written from the point of view of the relationships to art is a personal dialectical perspective that includes history, intention, absence, and random coincidence. Most people reflect on the circumstances when they saw a work of art more than the elements of the piece itself. Personal attractions to art are deep and not infrequently---dumbfounded---to appropriate a phrase---because the ability to speak about the chance attraction to a work created for another purpose generally leaves the viewer speechless. A part of who we are is brought forward transferred to thing that exists in a spiritual/commercial realm. In this volume Art exists as spiritual, temporal, voice but not pedantic. I began this project working on forms to express the intimacy of forms of art mostly freed of the intention of the maker and becoming more the articulation of the possessor of its content---creating a secondary, peripheral dialog of meaning that interacts and changes contexts. I explored the idea of the importance of the fictive in artifice in my prior poetry. 'Vanishes', my previous volume was fixated on believing illusion. In 'No Provenance' I am endeavoring to explore imaginary engagements of personal subtexts with an object. What we believe we see, isn't exactly accurate in this poetic exploration. The truth of these individual poetic texts should create an otherness, a misappropriation that is the essence of these convoluted relations. What I as a poet/artist I am creating are scenarios of misconstruance that hold both fictive veracities, a level of factual reality and error, and prevarication. What becomes the essential form in this work is the interactive expression of an unintended idea. The submitted extended poem 'Dummy Crown' uses a formal sonnet crown to interlink random experiences of interpretation that were unintentional, but express personal voices. The art acts as a medium of permission for cognitive dissonance to find personal expression. Decades ago, I was overcome by Giovanni di Paolo's 'St. Clare Saves a Child Being Mauled by a Wolf' on a casual stroll through the Houston MFA. I returned frequently to view it, although I have some personal and academic connection to Quattrocento Italian Art, what I found was an attraction and nearly obsessive fixity to a specific object which by and large held no relational meaning to my life. Investigating it I found another artist, Gael Stack, also had undertaken an interpretive study of the same work. Her reinterpretation was a tryptic, the center piece purchased by Houston MFA cataloged, but not displayed. The remaining two have never sold. It is that dialog I wanted to explore in a form that was coincidentally apt for the period of the original. It didn't seem possible to ignore coincidence---since apart from creating or commissioning, encounters with art are coincidental experiences. The crown links fourteen works ranging from a talking ventriloquist dummy, the pop record, “Uyimbube”, an imaginary VHS of a marionette opera, the transfiguration of a painting by J Fred Muggs, Michelangelo's unfinished pieta in a Milanese prison, a racist tea towel, a Dynell wig, She na gigs and a former exotic dancer, Django Rheinhardt's paintings considered by his wife, a steel worker museum exhibit, a stolen Maplethorpe negative offered for sale to poet in a restroom, a literary Zoom meeting. Vignettes linked by a theme but refusing narrative meaning---as if telling the story of telling the story were the purpose or the initial creation, but engage the reader in a different piece of art than originally presented. The second piece submitted, 'Piranesi's 'Racked Man' as a Portrait of My Father'. Reflect a personalized historical interpolation of the fantastical obsession of the Piranesi etchings with their prophetic possibility in depicting the demise of the industry and life in my family and home in the Rust Belt. Here the proportionality of imaginary imprisoned space enters a different historical narrative with an equally tragic ironic dimensionality. Neither intending to romanticize steel mills, nor engage in the victimization of events. The poem wants to present that moment of balance between the art and an event that now infuses it with meaning, but a meaning that was unforeseen by the artist. My father never having seen a Piranesi, and Piranesi creating his work centuries prior. That non sequitur is both invented by the poem and at simultaneously accurate. The final submitted poem 'Altered Polaroids 1991' investigates the notion of altering images at a pretentiously important moments in art history revisioning the gentrifying domestication of Soho as a portrait of an immutable man standing on the corner in front of an 'art scene' that manifests and vaporizes indifferent to his existence. He one of the multitudes who 'were there', but the direct engagement of his life is extraneous to his position. He becomes the supernumerary central to the picture. His actual existence makes him a violation in the ecstatic marketplace of art. What I am attempting is to provide a perspective beyond ekphrasis or art criticism that presents a momentary alignment of art, context and personality into forms that extend meaning. In a series of sonnets not submitted I consider the life of Dorothy Gale after she returned from Oz, Dorothy Wordsworth's view of the village of Grasmere told by a fairy, the saga of the Dali Lama's shoes becoming gallery art, a lover's quarrel over a conceptual Twombly, American music during wartime and duel between Houdini and Proust, a dream visit from Frank O'Hara. I represent these fictions not as events-in-themselves but re-presentations of art contagious with personality. What I am creating is ekphrasis of art that is NOT REAL ART, and in another sense a burlesque of some the portentous absurdity of the art world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
'Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent' When portraying a subject, the constraints of the medium, light and shadow, two dimensions, a single moment static in time, are limiting considerations an artist must shape into a compelling work. I believe that by redefining those boundaries allows more complexities of character and narrative to reveal themselves. All we are ever doing as Artists is giving the viewer a small segmented trice of a subject moving through space and time. In this project, I explore presenting a subject outside of traditional means. Though we are limited in our perception, there’s a naturally occurring beauty spontaneously, constantly happening. In order to reflect the complexity of each subject, deliberate choices are made in consideration of time, perspective, and motif to convey a particular mood. These compositions are, in process, a way to show a more complex narrative, and reflect a way we actually experience both the present and memories. Informed by my experience as both a applied artist and as a photojournalist, I wish to unify the tangibility of the material with the ephemeral of the sublime

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work originates in the soil with materials I grow or forage. I use various media, including the cyanotype and pastels, but primarily mushroom spores, the powdery substance that resides in the gills and pores of mushrooms. When released in nature, mushroom spores land on nearby soil, leaves, or rocks, always with the intention of survival. In my process, these tiny reproductive particles fly or float, then alight - my medium for works on paper. Thus, the spore print, a mushroom identification tool, is adopted in form and medium to create works on paper. Spore images contain organic abstractions and reference the ancient collaboration in mycorrhiza to conjure a natural world both strange and deeply familiar.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I utilize fantastical imagery, inspired by a range of influences like Renaissance, Mannerist, and modern video game art, as a method of processing and transforming childhood trauma. I grew up in the US foster care system which often relies on conservative, Christian-oriented homes and institutions to raise abandoned and abused children. As a queer youth dealing with the loss of the family I was born into, this system, although it did house me and provide for me, did so while reinforcing a psychologically traumatic doctrine. I see my work as a way of navigating both my need to feel empowered and my need to express pain as a queer soul in an imaginative world of vibrant, unseen forces. Drawing on the history of religious and mythological artwork, as well as my own likeness, I create a character that acts as an avatar for me to enact intimate dramas and moments of personal reflection inspired by my lived experiences and emotional states. My figures are composites of Biblical angels and demons, among other mythological creatures. Their chimeric forms I design in an act of defiance against the label of “abomination” that was applied to queerness in my childhood. With their intense glances, they divulge hidden secrets, their pensive claws express withheld anxieties, and their feathered wings protect themselves and each other, forging new identities as divine. Color is another way I celebrate queerness in my work, rendering forms through a rainbow of gradients and jewel tones that recall the magical, blacklight neons of club scenes that, before the tragedy of Pulse Night Club, once felt like utopic safe spaces for queer communion.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am working on my memories of feelings toward my vulnerability. Since I have such weird skin, I have lots of wounds and scars on my body because I get hurt easily, and it is difficult to heal. My profound discomfort with my skin comes in part because it lays bare my own vulnerability. I weave and mend. These are my ways of representing my complicated feelings toward my skin as a physical organ but also my process as a metaphor. Weaving and mending is an act of connection: Bringing broken pieces of fabric together to heal. I use soft, fluffy material to weave and mend. Most of the colors are very neutral. It looks very soft and warm, so people never recognize that it comes from such ugly marks. Even though scars and wounds are viewed as very ugly and negative, I reimagine these marks in my artwork as symbolic of patience and healing. Viewing how these marks affect skin texture informs the way I create weaving and manipulate texture in my pieces. Finally the outcome conveys overcoming negative emotions. I recognize empathy in the emotions tied to vulnerability—feeling weak, incomplete, and sinking into depression when one realizes a lack of strength. I would like to support people in healing their anxiousness, weakness and distress through my work.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artistic exploration, I use the imagery of deer and other wildlife found in various stages of death and decay to convey the intricate relationships surrounding dating, rejection, malice, challenging partnerships and family dynamics. The deer serves as a personal metaphor for the feminine and emotional toll of difficult interactions. My goal is to create a bridge between the inherent morbidity of the theme and beauty of material. I employ textiles, hides, sinew, and beads in my paintings to add a tactile dimension and also evoke historical significance of traditional gender roles. The intentional use of repurposed hides and hand made sinew echoes feminine sustainability and a connection to the natural world. Through this fusion, I aim to evoke empathy, contemplating the complexities of relationships while celebrating the strength it takes to transform a somber subject into a reflection on the resilience and beauty of working through interpersonal dynamics.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My experience of metamorphosis as a transgender person permeates my practice, and so my sculptures exist as a tangible story of transformation. I engage in processes associated with “women’s work” to stitch together my queer identities primarily with textiles and beadwork to create self-portraits, wearables, and interactive works that reclaim my individual history. I utilize a method of “freestyle stitching” as a resistance to the use of patterns, which frees me from adhering to a formulaic approach in my work. This technique directly correlates to queerness; by defying predetermined societal expectations, I liberate myself by redefining and reconciling my relationship with gender and sexuality. By reimagining some of my favorite childhood memorabilia, I utilize a playful nostalgia while chronicling my lived experiences. I continue to build a novel mythology for my hybridized creatures and self-commemorative work to foster compassion towards a former self that I had felt imprisoned within. I invite viewers to witness the tension between my body, psyche, and desires through experiencing my physical representations of personal vulnerability.

He was selected by juror Patrick Ela to show in “Contemporary Art: Folklore, Fakelore, Personal Myths Narratives” at the Bonita Museum and Cultural Center, San Diego, CA. Weiss has participated as an artist in residence at GlogauAIR, Berlin, Germany from October 2022 through March 2023 and exhibited in two Open Studios during his stay. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For me, art has always held an element of connection, either with oneself or with others. From sketching as a child to playing music as a teenager to photo editing as an adult, different artistic mediums have always been a way for me to connect with and make sense of the world around me. Through sculpting, I have discovered what feels like the purest way for me to express myself, as an artist and individual. I always experience a moment of bone-deep joy when another person takes an interest in my work and connects with it in their own way. My pieces are inspired by a love of nature and fantasy, blending the two into 3D designs that invite the viewer to look closer and challenge their imagination. Through sculpting, I can bring into existence something that previously only existed in my mind and give it physical form. I love the ability to make the unreal, real.

Another valuable aspect to being an artist is I rarely feel lonely because while my art is part of my own identity, my art itself is also a constant companion. column25: The imagery of the mid-20th century is some of the most iconic and memorable visual representations in history. I seek to draw the viewer in with playfully marrying the smiling idealized people from vintage advertisements with whimsical surrealism. Color plays a crucial role in my pieces, as I often start with a color story and build from that. Lately a personal goal I have made for myself has been to push pieces further and manipulate the material more than I normally would. I find when I do so, I produce more visually dynamic and successful pieces. While some of the combinations in my pieces are bizarre for the fun of it, I often explore more heavy subject matter like social issues and stigmas, mental health, gender stereotypes, sexuality and feminism, parenthood and grief. I enjoy eliciting a response in the viewer with eye-catching, outlandish imagery; whether it be a smile or a laugh, or getting one to think and introspect. I find people connect with my work when they find a relatable aspect within it, so I often aim to just let a concept develop organically rather than force it. I also enjoy when the viewer is able to extract their own personal narrative from the combination of imagery I present. Additionally, I find enthusiasm is invoked in people who were growing up in the eras of the imagery I use, when they recognize a product or an advertisement they remember. I incorporate nostalgia into my work, whether it is first-hand for older generations, or nostalgia for a time we haven't experienced; a visual and conceptual time travel perhaps. I am very inspired by the mid-20th century and images it produced; as I collect more material, I am more eager to get into my studio and create. I love giving the materials a 'second life' when they have been stored away and seemingly forgotten for so long. While the recognition and positive response from viewers is gratifying, ultimately I create because I feel it is what I was put on earth to do, and my passion to create is so strong I don't dare ignore it!

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I thought I would include first my artist statement for my previous feature 'Out of Breath'.... In May 2020, along with the rest of the world, I watched in horror as George Floyd was murdered. In the aftermath of Floyd’s death, I spent June and July marching through the streets of NYC. I have been to many protests throughout my life, but the ones last summer were truly cathartic, perhaps in part because most of us were coming out of months of lockdown in small apartments. I decided I wanted to capture this unique and surreal moment in NYC in a film. An idea came to me to use plot points from Jean-Luc Godard’s 1960 film 'Breathless' as a starting point to tell a story about NYC amidst the pandemic and protests. I was interested in the idea of a Black man being both the hero and anti-hero and through his morally dubious action of shooting a white cop (ala Breathless) creating a dialogue that was not binary, in which morals were not black or white, right or wrong. I was also interested in the character of Audrey, a photographer who badly wants to be an 'ally', but in the end, finds the limits of her commitment, which seems to be the story of many white activists' relationship with the BLM movement. With these thoughts in my head, I quickly wrote the first draft, then worked on subsequent revisions with my friend, the amazing Black fashion designer, and producer, Tiia Richardson, as well as with another friend, the actor, Mario C Brown who plays Jean-Michel in the film. My wife Christie O’Loughlin also came on board as a producer and, after writing the script in June and July, we were able to go into production in early September of 2020 when it still looked and felt like summertime in NYC. With the city nearly deserted and many things still shut down, we made the film that you are about to see. I hope you enjoy it. I thought I would also include my artist statement for my current project 'Broken Beak'.... Ever since reading “Drive Your Plow Over the Bones of the Dead” by Olga Tokarczuk, I have been interested in creating a film that seamlessly blends a philosophical idea with a narrative structure. As such, when the opportunity arose that Christie and I would be able to use the Devonport Power Station as a primary shoot location for a feature film, I began thinking about how I might try and make a narrative feature like this. The idea came to me to write an ecological horror film in the guise of a murder mystery - the true heart of the film being about an Indigenous woman’s metamorphosis into a mythical monster that is a protector of natural resources. The film would be a synthesis of popcorn horror entertainment laced with a thought-provoking ecological fable. I, like many of us, struggle with the fact that we are confronting planetary ecological collapse that has primarily been brought about by Western imperialistic thought and systemic capitalistic greed. This destruction has coincided with the erasure of indigenous ways that were more in harmony with the natural world and planet. As an Indigenous filmmaker (I am part Mississippi Choctaw and a member of the Choctaw Nation), and as the film would be set in New Zealand, I wanted the lead character to be indigenous. I have always had an affinity for the horror genre and its ability to weave in piercing social commentary in pulpy ways. As I began thinking about Ogress, the original 1992 Candyman film by Bernard Rose resonated with me. Rose used a Boogeyman-type character in that film to capture a deeper truth about the horrors and history of racism in a Chicago slum. Thus, I began thinking about a story that might create a natural Boogeyman-type character. Ogress opens with the following quote: “What I really wanted to say was that a monster is not such a terrible thing to be. From the Latin root monstrum, a divine messenger of catastrophe, then adapted by the Old French to mean an animal of myriad origins: centaur, griffin, satyr. To be a monster is to be a hybrid signal: both shelter and warning at once.” - Ocean Vuong, On Earth We Are Briefly Gorgeous Telling a story in which the lead character eventually becomes the mythical monster herself communicates the message that we need these old monsters of folk stories and mythology, these ogres, to keep mankind in awe of the natural world and respect Mother Nature.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am interested in forms and animated gestures that teeter on the cusp of familiarity; ambiguous signifiers where meaning is open to playful negotiation. My personal typology borrows from existing visual languages such as architecture, cartoons, science-fiction, and biology. I aim to scrape away enough recognizable meat from these source images so as to leave only foggy contours with which to link to other vaguely ascribable forms - creating thrilling new amalgams of emergent meaning. This project of reduction and distillation is inspired by cartoons as a process of non-verbal language development in which the averaging of personal mark-making mechanics and economic constraint helped to define a now universally recognized cache of visual abbreviations. Earlier work of mine was primarily preoccupied with abbreviated expressions of corporeality. I saw my sculptures as tactile cartoons of bodily anxiety. They were sensational objects rife with unsettling contradictions: simultaneous attraction and repulsion; humor and horror. I began stretching organic details over rectilinear scaffolding to create hybrid forms of body and architecture. This corporeal preoccupation had now become more ontological in nature, and I wanted to explore how audiences engaged with muddied symbolic definitions of self and not-self. Most recently my exploration of this boundary-less realm has manifested in animated sculpture loops and wall-hangings where two distinct materials meet to create frozen instances of collision. Coming from a dense urban center like Los Angeles means bearing witness to a lot of collisions, as well as material reminders of the Sisyphean project to maintain those distinctions of autonomy: Razor-ribbon, wrought-iron bars, etc. I use paired-down exclusionary symbols like walls, spikes, and other signifiers of hostile architecture as totemic stand-ins for the systems of control that depend upon this binary conception of reality. Breaches of this concept - the puncturing of one autonomous membrane by another - can be violent and frightening, but there is also always a glimmer of liberation from a philosophy that pits oneself against the rest of creation. Little pieces of our day-to-day world show up repeatedly in my work (i.e. home improvement materials like stucco and pivotal plot involvement from the USPS in my film Hot Dog Hands), but these quotidian details primarily serve to establish a sturdy platform from which to launch into more fantastical realms. Central to my construction of speculative realities is a diligent attention to “World Building,” a term borrowed from genre literature but a philosophy I think benefits the conception of all art. By creating work that demonstrates an alien - though COHERENT - origin, I invite my audience to participate within their own mind in the expansion of that imagined world far beyond the boundaries of the fragment with which I've presented them. It is a welcome collaboration.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Worlds Within Worlds David Aughenbaugh Exponential functions in math can make your brain hurt, but in art they can be transformative. I am interested in human consciousness and I create animations that use exponential growth to have a particular effect on the viewer’s mental state. The animations themselves are abstract, colorful and feature an infinite zoom experience, with a seemingly impossible amount of detail. The resulting effect puts the viewer into a relaxed flow state of quiet focus and complete immersion. I create these animations digitally, using years of practical experience as a visual effects artist in the film industry. My interest in human consciousness and trance states led me to study hypnotherapy. Combining these interests my work focuses on how visuals and sound can be used intentionally to affect the viewer’s mental state. There are few artists working directly in this space. Brian Eno is a pioneer and a continuing inspiration. Laurie Anderson is another. The best film makers utilize this function intentionally, with Stanley Kubrick and Christopher Nolan being good examples. It is also found in music, especially in live performances. The Surrealists said that art functions automatically on a subconscious level. A piece like Scraptangle Super Zoom is an experiment in how to maximize this effect. It avoids narrative and literal “meaning” but instead uses evocative imagery and a flow experience to stimulate the creative imagination in the viewer. I am exploring how variations might function in the larger context of serving various interests such as VR and ASMR, and where trade-offs might exist between quiet-mind flow states and engagement through interactivity. I am currently exploring ways to create immersive experiences and large-scale public art projects. These involve projection to temporarily transform a public space, allowing many people to discover the work spontaneously. This kind of project also generates curiosity well beyond the traditional art world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My mother was a painter and my father was a thinker. As an interdisciplinary artist working in diverse disciplines from music, fashion, and visual art to film and video, my work explores the nuances of the Black experience through the lens of global Hip-Hop culture. Whether I’m painting a public mural or organizing an event, community work is at the forefront of my practice. I enjoy being an active community facilitator and connector – working to forge creative relationships to benefit my community with my work. I believe artists are the storytellers of their communities. My music and art are more than just a personal diary of what I’ve seen and experienced. My entire practice is dedicated to bearing witness to and documenting the rich brilliance, creative spirit, and joy that thrives within my community.. I start a new artwork by trusting my intuition and instincts. I embrace the element of surprise and let it lead me to the information and imagery I need. From there, it's a process of taking imagery apart and finding a dynamic way to put it back together again. Rich, bold colors and textures are a defining signature of my work. And although I’ve always been attracted to the work of artists who use bold color, my color and design sense have been most influenced by two things- the immediacy of graffiti art that jumps off the wall and demands your attention, and more importantly, by the music, clothing and art, my parents collected and my grandmother’s entrepreneur spirit, It’s all there in my work waiting to be discovered and explored.

column25: After exploring the woven form, I have mastered the art of diagonal twill and mad weave, with which I create forms and structures not normally found in the basketry world. My medium for this unique work is watercolor paper, which I paint and cut into very narrow uniform strips to achieve the precision I seek. Approaching my work as a puzzle drives me to discover new shapes and weaving innovations. I often think, “How will it work out if I try this, or how can I get this shape or pattern combination? What if I use these colors in this combination in this order? What if…” I am intrigued by the potential outcome of any new design. The evolution of my body of work is built on taking risks, and avoiding the “known”. The risks offer challenges, which often lead to new directions. This is the excitement that keeps me working in a repetitive medium: it is an on-going meditation on improvisation, a continual experiment through which my work can progress and develop.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being aware that influence doesn’t always betoken homologous heuristics; my influences include the works of JRR Tolkien, Frank Herbert, Jean Michel Basquiat, Salvador Dali, Adolf Gottlieb, Anthony Braxton, Roscoe Mitchell, Wadada Leo Smith, Thomas Bruckner, Meredith Monk, George Crumb, Krzysztof Penderecki, Stephen Sondheim, ecological systems dating back to the origins of life, climate change, habitat destruction, racism, bigotry, greed, jealousy, compassion, empathy, love, displacement of original inhabitants, classism, but above all else my experiences with my children, my peers and in the natural world. I believe art represents a reflection of our experiences as they relate on intrapersonal, interpersonal, and environmental levels. My work regardless of medium explores themes of gender and cultural identity, classism, racism, fear, displacement of original inhabitants, wild animal and plant habitat destruction, genocide, compassion, empathy, endurance, perseverance, objectivity and perspective as it relates to the group and individual, power of belief, honesty, loyalty, addiction, power roles, political extremism, morality, mortality, function of binary, climate change, and their gradients of duality.

column25: Majid Araim is an Iraqi-American multi-instrumentalist, composer, educator, curator, visual artist, researcher and a fixture of the Atlanta improvisation and new music communities. Majid's prolific work as a composer focuses on resonance and dynamics, experimental approaches, and is oriented towards the natural world and fantasy realms. Since 2019, Majid has been increasingly focusing their sights on theatrical interpretation of expression documented in premiers of Cardinal Queen 2019, Regarding Bullfrogs 2019, Excerpts of an Opera 2020, Fragments From Otherwhere 2021, and Giants Moot 2022: and received an individual artist grant from Fulton County in 2023. Majid has given hundreds of performances across the United States and Europe as soloist and in the duo Whispers of Night, as well as experimental music groups such as Atlanta Improvisers Orchestra, BASrelief, The Convergence, Chamber Cartel, Bent Frequency, sunknameless, and Small Peoples Music Ensemble. Majid is a recent recipient of PREMIER Artist Residency at Georgia Tech School of Music for Whispers of Night’s work with geofons, specialized contact mics for use in ground mediums, exploring communication through filtration of our natural environment. Majid holds degrees in Music Composition and Jazz Studies from Georgia Southern University, and since 2013 has presented over 100 concerts as curator of the Magic Lantern series. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I was looking for a creative outlet during Covid and was fortunate to discover preserved moss art. Preserved moss art allows you to bring the beauty of the external natural environment indoors without the commitment of any maintenance -- no watering, sunlight, or regular upkeep is necessary. This makes it an ideal choice for indoor spaces where maintaining live plants might be challenging. Moss art uses real moss which has been harvested and has undergone a special preservation process to retain its texture and soft, plushy quality. The moss is then dyed or colorized with paints. My creations are made by combining contrasting colors and textures of reindeer moss, Spanish moss, lichens, and dried flowers in order to create striking multi-dimensional art that is sure to bring a natural component to any room. It has allowed me to incorporate my love of plants and the outdoors into this unique and environmentally friendly form of artistic expression. My hope is that my pieces will reconnect people with their innate desire to be closer to nature when being outside is not possible, and that its organic textures and vibrant colors will evoke a sense of tranquility and serenity on the viewer.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Cathleen Luo is a sculptor and art educator based in Harlem. Inspired by the likes of Wangechi Mutu and Christina Quarles, Luo employs surrealist imagery and techniques to delve into their personal journey as a queer and disabled Asian American individual. Their artistic focus centers on exploring androgynous forms that have been stretched and warped into unreality. Their figurative sculptures draw from Chinese Buddhist and Taoist imagery from their upbringing, alongside influences from contemporary indie film aesthetics. Their figures have smooth, metallic skin, flaunted in unearthly colors because their low vision is most drawn to these surfaces. Luo's work delves into the reclamation of queer bodies of color as alien and beautiful. They challenge the notion that the body is secondary to an architectural home, aiming to evoke a sense of uncanniness and distortion experienced by the figures in their creations due to displacement, immigration, and environmental isolation. Investigating the legal definitions of 'alien' and 'alienation' as per the United States, their art seeks to validate and recontextualize these diverse experiences. Given Luo's visual impairment, their art is built to be experienced through touch. Playing with contrasts in smoothness and texture, as well as unusual elongation and shortening of body parts, Luo subverts conventional notions of femme beauty.

Luo has exhibited at Useless Art Society, The Kota Alliance, Macaulay Honors College, and other art organizations based in New York City. Their work has been published in Ratrock Magazine and The Columbia Review. The core of Luo's artistic exploration revolves around the queer femme body and the concept of 'alien,' delving into both its science-fiction connotations and its political implications as a foreigner in the United States. Through painting and sculpting bodies in unconventional forms, they challenge conventional notions of the body as a home, emphasizing its potential to feel unnatural within its surroundings. Luo's work delves into the profound psychology of alienation in various contexts, whether in the isolating landscapes of suburbia or as an Asian/American femme navigating white America.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a sculptor, designer, and socially engaged artist deconstructing the interconnections between power, economics, labor, and race. I seek to illuminate these systems’ foundational impacts on personal schemas and interpersonal relationships. The concepts, imagery, and materials of my work are informed by infrastructure, which encompasses the act of supporting, the undergirding for creation, and the workforce maintaining our unsustainable global practices. The objects I make embody the reality that systemic forces are driven by economic and social incentives in power structures, which play greater than acknowledged roles in our individual material, cognitive and emotional conditions. The intersecting histories and consequences of omnipresent apparatuses—from taxation to electrical grids to the maintenance of “racecraft”*—are foundational to the tapestry of human existence. I employ sewing and textiles to interweave the effects of entrenched systems on the body, the delicate, the intimate. * Fields, Barbara J. and Fields, Karen E., Racecraft: The Soul of Inequality in American Life, Verso, 2014

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My art is a testament to the power and beauty of the divine feminine, a celebration of magic interwoven with social justice, and an exploration of the transformative potential in cultural healing and parallel universes. Through my creations, I aim to evoke a sense of reverence for the inherent strength and wisdom throughout history and across cultures. symbolizes both the nurturing and fierce aspects of womanhood, reminding us of our innate abilities to create, nurture, and empower. Magic has always been an integral part of human existence, an enchanting force that fuels our imagination and enables us to transcend conventional limitations. In my work, I seek to capture the essence of this ethereal energy, inviting viewers into a realm where infinite possibilities materialize. Through vivid colors, intricate patterns, and surreal imagery, I aim to transport observers into a world where dreams and reality intertwine. With a profound belief in social justice, my art serves as a platform for marginalized voices to be heard and amplified. I strive to, discrimination, and injustice through visual storytelling. Each brushstroke is imbued with a desire to spark conversations, provoke intros inspire collective action towards a more equitable and inclusive world. Cultural healing is another cornerstone of. The recognition and appreciation of diverse cultures are vital for fostering empathy, understanding, and unity. I draw inspiration from ancient traditions, indigenous wisdom, and local rituals, infusing my art with a tapestry of cultural references. By honoring and preserving these rich customs, I endeavor to promote healing and connection between communities, that were once dividing forces. Parallel universes offer an extraordinary alternate realities and expanding our perceptions. In my work, I invite viewers to venture beyond the confines of the familiar, allowing their minds to wander in realms where anything is possible. Through this exploration, we can challenge the limitations imposed by our own perceptions and embrace the transformative potential that lies within us. In conclusion, my art is a symphony of vibrant colors, intricate details, and thought-provoking narratives. It is a testament to the divine feminine, an embodiment of magic, a call for social justice, a catalyst for cultural healing, and an exploration of parallel universes. By immersing creations, I hope to ignite your imagination, awaken your consciousness, and inspire you to embrace the beauty, power, and infinite possibilities that exist within and around us all.

column25: “No Femmes. No Fats.” When I started making profiles on gay dating/hookup apps as a teenager, this is a phrase I came across frequently, defining me and my body as unwelcome. As a queer person with a non-normative body and an invisible disability, the constant rejection from gay communities set in motion a battle between my queerness and the body I inhabit. This series, titled Unaffixed, began with self-portraiture—an action that felt aggressive towards myself due to my aversion to it as a practice, perhaps through years of conditioning to despise my appearance. However, it allowed me to begin a visual exploration into non-normative queerness through the utilization of elements such as absurdity, humor, and the uncanny. I eventually moved on from just self-portraiture and began photographing with other queer people in a collaborative process which allowed other narratives and experiences to influence my work. This collaborative process became an integral part of my work, with me often appearing in the photograph with my subjects, where we experiment and explore together through the images we create. These photographs not only establish a space of queer collaboration and play, but also offer a counter narrative to the normative sexualized gaze of contemporary queer photography. While I am based in New York City, my work pushes against metronormativity—the idea that queer people must live in large cities and urban areas in order to be themselves. Metronormativity looks down on rural and non-urban communities as toxic and damaging to queer people when these individuals may not have the means nor the desire to leave. Furthermore, rural and non-urban areas are often homes to thriving tight-knit queer communities that are often overlooked. By traveling to create this work, I am able to show appreciation and offer representation for these ignored and under appreciated queer communities. My photographs neither describe nor ascribe. They do not offer complete or concrete narratives of identity. Rather, they present uncertain and fragmented narratives that exist in a space between reality and fantasy, reflecting the transitional space(s) in which queerness exists. Queerness is embodied through the unaffixed elements of fluidity, playfulness, and ambiguity. When I take photographs, the process is spontaneous and collaborative—not unlike the practice of free-association—and echos the fluidity and dynamism of non-normative queer identities.

column25: I was raised by mom who immigrated from Serbia and my dad who is from the United States. When I was young, my parents heavily considered raising me in Serbia. The bombing of Serbia in the late 90’s made this choice for them; I would be raised in the US. Had the bombing not happened my life could have been extremely different. I could have had a different home, friends, traditions etc. I became a Serbian citizen at a young age; this officially made Serbia a second home and eventually, the conceptual foundation for my photography. I use photography to explore the themes of home, change and romanticization. I seek to have a deeper understanding of what it means to exist in our transient world. Where is home when it could be anywhere? My curiosity to explore my surroundings causes internal disruption as my body rejects change. My reaction does not stop me because I believe it is an unfortunate result of making progress. To me, home is not a structure but a feeling of safety. Safety in understanding my surroundings and being with people whom I love. I don’t understand why I feel the need to find a new home. When I look back on my life, it is through photo albums and the ‘highlights’. Potentially, I am searching for a home, which allows for more to look back and think how lucky I was to live this life My method of photographing involves my manual analog medium format cameras. I prefer this to digital because there is no instant feedback. I must photograph with my technical knowledge and instincts. Once the film has been exposed, I am once again looking at the landscape instead of judging myself and the image. I thoroughly enjoy having control at each step along the way. I cannot wait for the final image but I equally look forward to the process. My portfolio represents my current state of mind. Each image was created in the past year of my life, after leaving behind my first home. It is a diary of my life. I took chances, followed my instincts and created something I am proud of. Moving forward, I know my perspectives will change. With age it is inevitable and I cannot wait to discover what is to come.

column25: My work involves making images derived from natural processes observed in a boreal forest watershed. It is highly personal in that the territory also serves as my home range of nearly 50 years. I'm primarily occupied with illuminating ice, water, and the glyphs formed in their passage. The work is initially discovered in the field, a liminal environment, where change is more evident than meets the casual eye. Digital processing shapes and refines the resulting images. Natural phenomena in this place - the quirky, strange, and sublime – compels a creative response. Abstraction raises nature-based images above the bounds of reference and interpretation. Free of expectations, the work’s ambiguity touches numinous and transcendent chords. It invites viewers to look below the surface. Whether it’s active erosion or deposition, melting or freezing, the images originate in transition zones. In the wake of gravity, time, and temperature, they form, dissolve, and form again. Geomorphology at this scale is a perpetual dance, witnessed by few. However, captured as images, its manifold dimensions and ephemeral qualities challenge, delight, and inform the curious eye. Emphasizing close detail and using unconventional orientation, the work pivots on trading immediate recognition for greater complexity. Color, line, and granularity echo a sub-arctic presence. Its narrative context resolves amid relationships connected to season, location, and issues related to climate warming. The images border on obscure and strange, yet the work is aligned with aesthetic traditions from realism to minimalism. In the balance, the project’s images are sources of beauty and contemplation.

column25: I am someone who prides herself in creating authentic work that comes from my heart—it's always work that's motivated by what I want to do and create. And I want to create work that has intention and meaning behind it—pieces that people will treasure and not objects that just wind up in a landfill. I like to incorporate the wildlife, the natural world around me, my cultural heritage, and my own personal folklore—all the things and experiences that make me who I am into my work so any piece that you see out in the wild simply screams, 'That's a LIONINTHETREES piece'. My beginnings as a working artist started in the DIY movement back in the early aughts. And I later found some success by creating my own designed manufactured goods such as enamel pins, merchandise, a plushie, and more. But I felt called to return to my DIY handmade roots and started shifting my focus back to printmaking. I ended up taking a pottery class back in 2022 and it's as though the medium was made for me—it feels I've found my soulmate as an artist. I've been able to marry my love for making things by hand and printmaking through pottery and the sgraffito technique, and it's truly changed the direction of my art for the better at a time where I felt like I was stagnating and floundering.

column24:

One of the most compelling aspects of being an artist lies in the ability to connect with people on a deeper level by expressing authenticity through imagery. Artists have the privilege of conveying their thoughts and voices genuinely, and even when exploring similar themes, the varied approaches to conceptualizing and visualizing those ideas result in diverse artistic expressions. What makes being an artist truly rewarding is the understanding that, through their work, audiences can resonate with the artist's perspective or craft entirely different narratives. The process of creating art allows for a genuine exploration of one's thoughts and emotions, transcending linguistic and cultural barriers. The artist's unique vision and interpretation manifest in the visual language they employ, providing a rich tapestry of interpretations for viewers. It's a dynamic exchange where spectators, upon witnessing the artwork, have the freedom to engage with the artist's narrative or craft entirely new stories of their own. In essence, the beauty of being an artist lies in the power to foster connections, provoke thought, and inspire a multitude of interpretations, creating a rich tapestry of shared human experiences through the language of visual expression.

column25: The figures in my oil paintings, their facial expressions are hidden; in some paintings, their mouths and eyes are missing, only noses and ears are shown. It’s as if this is a way of keeping their secrets, or as if they are asleep with closed eyes. Their huge bodies look heavy enough to make a lot of noise when moving around, but, paradoxically, they also seem to float, as in dreams or memories, implying the suspension of time in memory. But they also live in that moment, filling the void with their gentle gestures. Therefore all the moments become another time for them. The self-imposed solitude and the desire for connection are incompatible, but they build a new communication way through their cautious body language. Tenderness, to quote Roland Barthes, is an “infinite metonymy” for love. The figures’ gentle gestures are a metonym of their desire to be loved by others. Even in a small space, they keep a careful distance from each other. This distance expresses waiting for affection from others not trying to seize it right away. This is how they protect their hearts, but also a way of giving love as an active practice. Even if their love needs more time to be noticed, it is always there. Their relationship is symbiotic based on Buddhist belief, Dependent Arising, Yeongi-sull, which denies the reality of the self, but offers proof of one’s existence through interacting with others and surrounding objects. In other words, I exist because of you and you exist because of me. The figures, are here, in this place, to find and grasp their identity and existence in a state of denial of the reality of the self. The will to identify their existence shows the need and desire to relate to others and to mundane things, even if that thing is just a bean or a salt shaker. This shows they are in a state of absence of the substance of the self and that their gender does not matter anymore to them in the eternal cycles of rebirth. The figures’ gender is concealed by covering their arms and legs or turning their bodies away from the viewers. There are dramatic differences in the saturated hues of the lights and the shadows. These vivid colors spread out from the figures to the voids between them and vice versa. Sometimes the figures collide or push away from each other due to the forceful juxtaposition of complementary colors. These pigments grow strong in silence as if they are the only way to express these overflowing complicated feelings. Their silent shouting becomes visually extreme and palpable with the combination of textures, drips and spatters. This synesthesia of color and emotion, coming from the misfit color combines into a singular color ambiance, matching the desire of the figures’ trials to cope with their indescribable feelings in order to empathize with the world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
If creation is divinity, then we're all a bit divine, I want to help others see the divinity in me, and in doing so, recognize the divinity in themselves. For example, I started doing tattoos and welding, learning new processes and materials about which I was curious. Turns out a lot of people like getting tattoos, and some people also want things like outdoor grills and handrails. Learning and becoming proficient in a procedure, nurturing that ability, and sharing it to the benefit of others is one of the things that makes life worth living. I experience pain intensely, as most individuals do, and want to celebrate hurt as it defines relief and joy, to recognize the importance of angst and frustration as without them we would not know purpose, meaning, belonging. The best thing about being an artist is being able to navigate all these themes in a way that impacts others and contributes to a collective ethos.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
To me, the best part of being an artist is the opportunity for public engagement. I enjoy making work very much and spend a lot of time researching and building my symbology, sampling, and weaving. This part of my practice is very fulfilling, but when I have the chance to bring my art into the world I am always surprised by how many deep and powerful connections are made. There is something magical about cloth. It is so commonplace, so familiar to everyone, that seeing it as an artwork, just a little removed from it's usual domestic place, that invites others in. I have spoken with strangers about the crafts of their great aunties and children, of special heirlooms lost to time, or of favorite sweaters knit in favorite colors. Everyone has a connection to cloth, to weaving, and I love that my work offers people a safe and friendly place to share these connections.

column25: Through the use of digital and handmade processes, I make installations, works on paper, and textiles with a focus on digital jacquard tapestries. To me, the loom acts as a mediator between traditional and computerized technologies, offering a unique way to combine online and offline experiences into images in cotton and wool. I consider myself to be a collector of technologies and threads. In the studio, I weave textiles on a mid-century Bergman floor loom, a passed-down marudai, and a knitting machine from the 1960s that I restored piece by piece. Every process tells a story of a different code system. Punch cards and graph paper are as vital to my process as the fluid watercolors I paint with when constructing my base imagery. This passion for systems, for breakable rules, stems from a lifelong curiosity of reason and rule-bending. From 2015 to 2019, I lived in Oslo, Norway. Here I had the opportunity to learn digital jacquard tapestry, a mechanized process based on pixel binary. Through this medium, I can merge her digital and physical life into soft, familiar material. This experience has led to factory-scale projects, working with fabricators and industrial machines, as well as hand-driven weaving projects in Iceland and Scandinavia. The work seen in my Work Samples was created while on residency at the Praxis Fiber Workshop's Digital Lab (2022), at the Icelandic Textile Center (2019), and while studying for my MFA at Kunsthegkolen i Oslo. This process with its computerized, pneumatically-driven loom is at the forefront of textile innovation and very costly to own and operate. I am currently in the process of finding my next digital weaving opportunity, focusing on other 'analog' weaving processes in the meantime. I am always researching, sampling, and exploring the possibilities of textile production, and finding ways to make great work in either technique.

column24:

My focus lies on re-evaluating cultural and environmental histories in a contemporary context as a form of meta-ecological research that is depicted through a variety of media, serving both as a referential document and a celebration of chaos in the natural world. Subjects have ranged from the American Southwest desert to the counter-cultural threads of Tangier, Morocco. In my voluminous explorations of ecological histories and landscapes, I showcase the implicit yet inextricable ties between human culture and nature, often through the lens of climate change and colonialism. I aim to incite personal reflection and meditative contemplation on our relationships, experiences, and responsibilities to our surrounding environment.

column25: Hello, I’m Jordyn Ruiz, a traditional and digital artist based in San Antonio, Texas. My work serves as a window into my inner world, where I express my emotions and share my heart through art. I create pieces that navigate through the depths of my feelings, offering a safe space for these emotions and stories to be seen, felt, and transformed, contributing to personal growth. My art acts as a diary, capturing significant moments in my life when words alone couldn't fully convey what I felt. I believe that art bridges the gap between the unspoken and the often inexplicable nature of our human experience. Inspiration for my creations comes from a myriad of sources – relationships, song lyrics, books – as I continually blend my diverse experiences into unique representations of the emotions and moments I aim to convey in a new and personalized way. Art, for me, is a foundational emotional outlet. Sharing my creations brings me immense joy, providing an opportunity to connect with others through shared emotions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I owe my lifelong love of painting to my ojichan and obaachan, who were both professional artists. Growing up biracial and second-generation American, I often felt caught in the in-between, sometimes feeling “too American to be Japanese” and other times “too Japanese to be American.” This push-and-pull shows up in my work: nostalgia, a search for belonging, and the comfort of retuning home are recurring themes in my paintings. My paintings also reflect my love for traditional Japanese crafts, and the mindfulness they evoke. I love capturing the quiet intimacy of everyday moments and holding on to the soft expressions in an old family photo.

The best part of being an artist is sharing artistic experiences with others. My installations are immersive and generous, giving people the opportunity to safely stop and talk to one another about who they are and what is important to them. I relish the opportunity to get to know one another better, to share in the best of our humanness, and to discover our rich distinctions. column25: We go through life, looking for hope and love, finding it in expected and unexpected places. My artwork strives to be a physical manifestation of the cherished yet unseen parts of our relationships: hopes, dreams, wishes, loss, and love. It fosters relationships between people who may or may not otherwise meet, allowing them to spend time making memories and building friendships in specific places of one’s life. My work examines the joy of unexpected yet beautiful spaces and places that may be discovered as one explores the land and structures within it. My installations feature places based upon landscapes: rivers, caves, geodes, stars, and the moon. Currently a lot of my work focuses on clouds, as a metaphor of the storms and hopes of life. The sky is a physical space we all share, even during social-distancing. Space/place are crucial: how do our bodies relate to and interact with small objects? Larger objects shift and become more architectural; they shift from things related to our bodies to spaces we inhabit. How do our bodies within spaces and in proximity to sculpture define who we are? What is our community is like within these spaces/places? The landscape inspired spaces are comprised of rich, luscious, inviting materials, such as fabric, glitter, and twinkle lights. These inviting environments allow viewers to explore the land and get to know one another in a deeper, more profound way. They provide a space for renewal and perseverance. My artwork embraces radical optimism (everyone can find joy and hope) and indiscriminant hospitality (all are welcome to create and experience joy through sharing). Viewers will realize that people are like landscapes: we make assumptions about initial appearances, but through knowledge and experience, pleasant surprises abound as we gain a deeper knowledge of each other while in the land and through art. My art practice is rooted in experimentation and exploration: experiencing place and spending time with people to discover experiences which bring joy and reflection. Sewing is central to my practice, lines drawn in thread. I sew not only fabrics but plastics, papers, and odd materials. Fabric is a material that invites touch and interaction, a reminder that we inhabit and embody real bodies that experience objects in space. Magic happens through textures and touch. My structures are hopefully safe spaces that are inviting, hopefully healing some of the trauma from the pandemic, social injustice, and polarization. Each sculpture and structure are created from experimentation, of taking similar structures apart to figure out how they work, or of trying things that seem like they’d work in theory. Some structures break the system, creating anomalies that undulate. Drawing is key to my process: maquettes are sketched, digitally drawn, and/or built out of paper, wire, and chenille stems. People give me fabrics and, by trial and error, I figure out which materials are best for various uses. As a child, I didn’t have toys, but my meteorologist father brought home weather satellite images, which I drew all over. Often my drawings ended up in my neighbors’ doors or mailboxes. My mom had lots of fabric and a sewing machine. Sewing toys began my lifelong process of making things out of textiles. I exploded every day. To quote Ray Bradbury, “You remain invested in your inner child by exploding every day. You don’t worry about the future, you don’t worry about the past — you just explode.” Academia gives me the framework to understand and articulate my art within the art world, history, and contemporary culture. Open heart, open mind, open practice, and joy in making and sharing remain instrumental for how I conduct my research, my exploding art every day.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a consequence of my interest in interdisciplinary sciences I have amassed knowledge which has manifested itself in the form of art. I have no professional training in art, only conviction. Most of my work is an appeal to my audience to think more critically and have more awareness about the society in which they live. A lot of my esoteric knowledge is not the kind of thing that can be discussed openly. The things I have come to understand about the world through my education and the process of illumination is not everyday common knowledge. This knowledge can be extremely isolating. During my process of revelations, I found comfort in artwork. It made me feel less insane and brought me solace to see artworks by others. The work of these artists suggested they understood the same strange things that I was made aware of. I began my artistic journey as a way to express my new understanding of the world. I was determined to warn others the dangers of technology, propaganda, control and the dystopia of a world run by a handful of elites. I worked tirelessly with the thought that maybe someday, someone else would see this work and be thankful they were not alone in their acute awareness. I wanted to pay it forward to the next generation of those chosen to 'see.' I have an appreciation for artwork that elucidates unusual realities that typically go unnoticed by others. I also have the spirit of an activist. I have a strong moral compass and use this to guide my work. I make pieces to highlight racial disparities, endorse feminist ideologies, and to satirize greed, obsession with wealth and heartless elitist privileges. I feel my work conveys very important messages that I hope will reach a wide audience. Now more than ever we need to understand the value of human kindness. We must exercise our empathy. We must rise to the occasion and be humanitarians.

column25: Katherine Kane is a Costa Mesa based artist who uses her love for people and social equality to create her art. Her journey began as a physiology student at Michigan State University. After completing neuroscience internships at Rockefeller University and MIT, she took a job in HIV research at Harvard. She moved to NYC and began a graduate program at NYU studying social psychology and learned big picture mechanisms of how groups of people are controlled in society. This gave her the awareness to begin creating artwork based on her cumulative knowledge in various fields of study. In the meantime, her interest in perfumery was sparked by a chance encounter with a perfumer that landed in a position in a creative fragrance lab. After facing discrimination in the fragrance industry, she began to regularly participate in social activism in the workplace and use art as a form of therapy. She realized she could blend her love for social justice with her burgeoning interest in art. She was able to channel her experiences as a woman of color into subversive art that challenges the status quo. In addition to her esoteric work that focuses on revelatory work and ethical dilemmas faced by participation in a system ruled by elites, she began to blend agitation propaganda and protest art into her repetoire. She is not a formally trained artist, and uses whatever resources she can to visually express her concepts. Her work catalyzes a spark in her audience's moral compass. She inspires more awareness and analysis of life's inequalities through her work. She urges viewers to break out of indifferent attitudes and feel empathy. She is open to any medium of expression, but currently utilizes acrylics, mixed media collages, sculptural works, and digital platforms to bring her visions to life. When she is not producing art, she is formulating flavors for applications within the cannabis industry. She continues to develop fragrance formulations out of her love for the raw materials and the process; not for profit or agenda, but the pure joy of it. She knows creating beautiful smells can influence the atmosphere of a space and can induce powerful emotional reactions. Soon, she hopes to blend her perfume expertise as a means of expression in concert with her art. She believes the combination will initiate symbioses and promote understanding. She believes curating the olfactory atmosphere will enable the audience to embrace her artistic message when viewing her exhibitions. She is pleased with her relocation from NYC to Southern California where she enjoys time with her partner and cat, tennis matches and raising awareness for racial injustice and feminist activism. A series of Katherine’s art is currently on display in Downtown LA at the Dove Biscuit Studios.

column25: Bio My name is Vashti! I am a native of Colorado! Art has always been a big part of my life. However, it wasn’t until the pandemic that I picked up my paint brush and started to use my gift to inspire others. Growing up I didn’t see a lot of POC representation in the art space. Let alone mental health issues. I decided to be the change and try to make others feel represented! Through sharing my art, I was able to successfully gain traction and be featured in the Gazette and the Denver State Capitol. The goal was to shed more light on the underrated African American artist in the community. This opportunity has led me to believe that change can only happen if we make it, and that’s my mission as an artist. To spread awareness about mental health in the black community. Next year I will be exhibiting at The Colorado Springs Pioneer Museum in hopes to reach a wider audience. This message is very close to my heart and it brings me joy to share it with whoever will listen.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In this segment of my work, I explore the profound sense of loneliness and disconnection that that is pervasive today. We all seek connection but barriers are put up by a fast-paced, physically challenging and increasingly divisive world. Social media purports to connect us but has become a battleground. COVID locked out the rest of the world which we saw through a Zoom glass darkly. Politicians, influencers and the news have learned that fear and accusations collect the most eyeballs. We are surrounded by a sea of faces yet feel utterly alone. The expressions and body language of the people depicted in these artworks show despair, confusion and disillusionment. In some cases, the people become faceless, no longer worthy of identification. My medium is watercolor. It is fluid and tough to tame. It is a metaphor for our limited control over the world. Sometimes the best we can do is to react well to the circumstances that spill before us. Through my art, I invite viewers to confront their own experiences of loneliness and disconnection, fostering empathy for the shared human condition. If you can look beyond the surface and find humanity in these images, perhaps that will lead to greater understanding and recognition of the struggles of the lonely and disconnected (as we all are at times).

column25: My relationship with objects is connected to how I interpret the body, the emotional self, and human consciousness. I aim to provoke the viewer to consider their own relationship between the objects and themselves. I make work to connect with others, and to create an intimate experience between the viewer and the work; something similar to the intimacy formed between two people. Vulnerability is one way in which I choose to connect. I open myself up to the audience, through the work. I confide in the work and the work whispers subtlety to viewer. Visually, I am drawn to organic and vaguely figurative forms…body as an object that has a relationship with the other bodies and objects around them. This begins my practice, often starting with process oriented craft techniques. I then fuse these products with experimental materials and forms that seem to have an emotive quality that I relate to. I trust intuition and improvisation, and rely on spontaneity to challenge my own aesthetic and emotional sense. Materials I choose may start as formal decisions, but also must have meaning, concept, and/or inherent cultural signifiers. The pieces are thus imbued with their own unique appeal…vulnerable yet unafraid. Mundane objects now become important amongst the crafted material as metaphor for the creative meeting the banal. By turning the dials just so, my work asks the viewer how we relate to each other; what can we do to see our interconnectedness with each other and the objects around us; and how does this piece relate to us physically, emotionally, and intellectually. Inviting the viewer to interact and bond with my work is quite intimate and exhilarating. I find that this notion…this fantasy…of engaging intimately with my viewers, their bodies and minds, drives my work in the studio effortlessly.

In 2021, she experienced a transformative encounter that reignited her desire to rediscover her true essence - freedom and artistic expression. Her art is heavily influenced by her love for flowers, vibrant colors, spirituality, community, love, and the courage to start anew. Eugina's 'A Dozen Accolades' art series celebrates the recognition and appreciation we receive, even during our most vulnerable moments. Each painting exudes joy and acceptance, leaving a lasting impression on the viewer. Eugina considers herself a perpetual student, always open to learning and willing to share her knowledge as a teacher. Her story reminds us of the beauty in self-discovery and the importance of valuing the lessons that come with personal growth.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a multimedia artist, I find myself pinging back and forth between different mediums, and applying those to a variety of subjects. While I do enjoy surrounding myself with a facade of decorative aesthetics, and find focusing on beauty in all its forms to be a worthy practice, I also appreciate discovering subtle ways to make deeper explorations into the complexities of the world we live in and the challenges it presents. If we have all become accustomed to 'sugarcoating the medicine' , we might as well accept that as modern practitioners and consumers of 'Art', even truth has become associated with certain decorative aesthetics. I would like to think that I am willing to travel past the need to ignore reality, even through the difficult landscapes of irony, cynicism and morbidity, to find delicate nuanced opening into some acceptance of abstract truths that are beyond political affiliation, labels and words. And from there, I am also willing to return, with new complex and exciting ideas in hand, to the place where life and beauty is simply celebrated, and to share in that spirit.

column25: The beginning of the quarantine found me at the Château d'Orquevaux in France, taking part in a month-long artist residency, creating small studies of nudes forced to fit into a box as we often do in American culture. I have traveled to the UK previously where I showed and lectured about my works dealing with loss of a loved one, called Brushstrokes of Emotion at the Skeletons: Stories and Social Bodies Exhibit, in 2018. As a homegrown American, not white, not black, I struggle with finding out where I fit in. “Brownish” is the new buzzword I’ve heard. As a brown artist I am pigeon holed into making a certain kind of art and excluded from places that show black or brown art, because my work doesn’t “look” like black or brown art. I'm exploring different forms of my own identity through that lens, as well as through my explorations in art. I try to blend the mundane with organic magick. I always hope my work will inject some inspiration and emotion to the viewer. The culture zeitgeist in my work explores modern day themes such as body shaming, and fear of death. Follow me now as I create my own tarot deck. Tarot is used as a form of cartomancy in which practitioners draw cards to gain insight around a situation. The 78 cards in the deck each represent a different energy. I'm exploring the visual representation of those energies in my current painting series, The Phantasmagoria.
