column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am an abstract painter using materials that call me back to the places in which I grew up, the places that live deep within my soul. My childhood was spent exploring the wilds of the Pacific Northwest Coast and the lush, quiet undergrowth of the Cascade Mountains. Time there allowed me to feel peaceful and understood, like I was a known part of something magical and unbelievably vast. Even amidst the ocean’s relentless crashing waves, storm debris and brackish scent or the rushing waters of a glacial stream, my spirit was calmed. These landscapes are chaotic and messy and in every way perfect and beautiful. I paint what these experiences left within me. Often, I begin with bold colors and expressive mark making, yet at other times I feel a pull toward something more earthy and contemplative. Both give me something to respond to as I begin to add layers of oil, charcoal, pastels and other media. Building a story that reflects the yearning of the soul to be known and understood.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In the realm of artistic expression, technique and discipline have long been revered as the pillars of mastery. The traditional atelier training, with its rigorous focus on technical precision and adherence to established rules, has shaped generations of artists. However, as an artist seeking to challenge the status quo, I find myself drawn to dismantling the rigid structures of the atelier and exploring the liberating possibilities of a childlike, silly, and disruptive creative process. Through my artistic journey, I have come to understand the immense value of technique and the discipline instilled by atelier training. The meticulous brushstrokes, the harmonious play of light and shadow, and the mastery of form are undeniably powerful tools in the artist's arsenal. Yet, as I delved deeper into this structured world, I felt a sense of creative suffocation, a longing for spontaneity, and a desire to break free from the shackles of conventional wisdom. Inspired by the boundless imagination of children, I began to explore the realm of silly and disruptive creativity. I sought to unleash the inner child within me, who had not yet been tamed by rules and expectations. Through this process, I discovered a renewed sense of wonder, a vibrant connection to my instincts, and an unbridled joy in the act of creation.

column25: Isabella Mebarak was born in Barranquilla, Colombia with Lebanese descent. At six years old she moved with her family to Miami, Florida. After elementary school, she auditioned for an arts middle school. Since accepted, she continued in magnet art schools until college. After one semester as a presidential scholar at Otis College of Arts and Design, Isabella dropped out and moved to Spain to pursue a more traditional atelier style training. Two years later she moved to Italy, to continue learning from the masters and learn Italian. Unfortunately, the pandemic struck and she was forced to move back to Miami. She tried to integrate herself into the miami arts scene, but feeling uninspired and unchallenged, she moved to Brooklyn, NY. Isabella currently lives and works between NYC and Miami, where she is working on larger paintings, sculpture and community based work.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artistic exploration I delve into major themes inspired by both personal life experiences and significant global events. One such example is my response to the 2022 Europe war between Ukraine and Russia—a historic moment that compelled me to express its impact through my art. The human body, is another profound subject for me, that becomes a recurring theme in my works, encompassing portraits, nudes, and depictions of social events that capture the essence of human existence. Employing the technique of impressionism in these paintings adds a nuanced layer, creating an emotional resonance. While my earlier years were marked by intricate surreal paintings adorned with fine details, my artistic journey led me to a transitional phase, embracing the captivating beauty and emotive depth of impressionist styles. Today, I find joy in experimenting with a fusion of these approaches, allowing the nature of each painting to guide my choices. The interplay between graphic surrealism, laden with intricate details, and the emotive strokes of impressionism forms a dynamic balance in my current body of work. Driven by an insatiable curiosity and a genuine love for the artistic process, I have explored many painting techniques and diverse themes over time. This ongoing exploration lead me to constantly seek new avenues, pushing the boundaries of the known into the uncharted realms of artistic expression. Each canvas becomes an opportunity to paint the previously unpainted, an exciting venture into the unknown that fuels my passion for the boundless possibilities within the world of art.

column25: Alex Sandulescu is an oil painter living in Los Angeles, California. He moved here with his parents, when he was little from Romania. Alex Sandulescu discovered his passion for art early in life, leading him on a self-taught artistic journey. He pursued higher education, earning a Master's in Interior Architecture abroad. Alex eventually turned his focus to the world of oil painting. For him, oil painting represents the pinnacle of the creative process, allowing him to blend his architectural expertise with a touch of soul to craft captivating works of art. He is a self taught oil painter. He loves to experiment with several types of painting techniques and styles from impressionist to surreal works. In his works Alex also explores a variety of different themes. Since he studied architecture, in many of his paintings Alex also includes buildings. He believes having a complex environment enhances the paintings. He finds inspiration in almost any painter, from Salvador Dali to Caravaggio to Claude Monet, Pissaro, Andrew Weith, John Singer and many others. Alex believes there is something to learn from different artists. While he studied interior architecture in his home country , in Romania, he used his creative talent to win several architectural competitions. An important competition where he won First prize, was a national contest in 2013, organised by the international design brand Elle Decoration, where Alex had to design the reception area for a modern building. Another contest worth mentioning was an Honorable Mention he received at WT Smart City Awards in Milan, Italy, during design week, for the Avis Magica Vertical Nature project he designed for MIAMI, a project which was featured on several architectural journals and websites all over the internet. Alex started to paint only a few years ago but he already exhibited some of his work in the US and in Europe too. He is currently working on several oil paintings inspired by his life in the US , but also a traveling theme which Alex approaches in many of his paintings where he tries to capture the soul of different countries which he visits. To see more of Alex's work please visit his website: www.alexsandulescu.com or his Instagram: @ArtistAlexSandulescu

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For nearly twenty years, I have been working on a sculptural series titled I am Here. Each piece begins the same way: observing a particular location and documenting this “living subject” through photography. The captured images are then translated into reliefs, rendered convincingly using a unique technique of folding and sewing layers of industrial plastic mesh. The resulting works tell community stories. They depict instances of people coming together in space and time and celebrate the diversity that exists all around us. Deeply interested in the human experience, it is my hope that these works can help to nurture a sense of pride of place and an appreciation for others among all who are involved with, and view, them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My collage art has evolved a lot since its start in my undergraduate studies. Much of the original though process outside of my academic studies involved finding images that simply 'fit together' in context of theme, scale, or color. It was a superficial way of creating collage art as I feel it lacked any meaning or message. In 2020, I had a dramatic change in lifestyle due to a multitude of factors and I had to slow down the work I was making and it was during this time that I changed my process, I would take the time to create a collage zine with a fully realized message, planned composition, and specific imagery that I wanted to convey a message. It was after completing this work that I would find immense satisfaction in creating art and would be an extremely important process to include to all of my artwork thereafter. My artwork now focuses on ideas that come from within, I must begin every project with 'What do I want to say' and followed by 'How do I want to say it?' My art takes images out of context and reconstructed to create its own narrative, using work that already exists to create a message, to express an emotion, a viewpoint or an exercise into technique. Collage and photography capture ideas and questions coming from within my soul, they are used to answer questions about my life and resolve my feelings that I can't address any other way. It is through this process of creation that I hope I can convey that feeling to an audience and if not, I hope an audience can recontexualize my messages in their own way as it would be appropriate to my process.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
With reverence for fantasy, I direct choreographies from a queer Black Brown Trinidadian femme perspective. My creative work harnesses dance, experimental theater, sound, and visual design to frame narratives of eroticism, power, and compassion. I am constantly slowing my mind and unwinding and opening my body to be swayed by everyday, mystical experiences. A good love song makes me cry. I obsessively track and analyze my dreams. Rigor is in my blood. I journal and write short stories and draw and sing. The pull of engaging with other bodies and beings feels undeniable. I am always dancing. My work is audacious, generous, and sometimes difficult to stomach. The work dances Caribbean research on gender and sexuality in an unflinching manner. The choreography is not afraid to say what it has to say, as loudly as necessary. In my work, theoretical considerations are transposed into deeply human emotion, provoking audiences toward self-examination and into closer consideration of the worlds around them. Performers move with visceral virtuosity and generate highly charged, palpable energy that translates across mediums. Characters unravel, wind up, sort out entanglements, pursue curiosities, revel in sweetness, all in real time. Performances are nuanced, focused, and relentless.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Rotten Fruit is a research artist project that peels back the layers to reveal the United Fruit Company's growth into Central and South America; the work addresses the complicated colonial history of those regions. Giselle Mira-Diaz refers to the discernible realities of conceptual thought surrounding archived materials that disappear between Western and European colonized narratives. Her purpose was to obtain exclusive access to documents, photographs, and oral histories that are specifically housed in archives located in South and Central America. By interrogating these materials, Giselle produces a contemporary response through photography, video, performance, and other mixed-media interventions. The work aims to reveal forgotten historical narratives that are connected to the social frameworks and imperialistic hegemony of American corporations, which have historically and presently maintained authority over land, economic output, and labor in Central and South America. These pieces cover the loss of life sanctioned by The United Fruit Company (now known as Chiquita Banana), resulting in the murders and disappearances of Banana Plantain workers and their families, and the destruction of the land and environment through the harmful malpractice of Banana monocultures.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
There is perhaps nothing more frightening to a human than the knowledge that one day they will die. Yet, armed with this knowledge, many humans walk through life already dead; content to waste their life either on fruitless pursuits, or no pursuit whatsoever. In the Middle Ages and Renaissance, the Dance of Death served as a reminder of life's brevity. Today, those reminders are few and people seem to deny that death even happens. Death is a great mystery; you can experience it only once, religions are based on it, and it is promised to every one. A large part of my work focuses on my fascination with the life—death struggle. Through my explorations I seek to not only enlighten myself about the mechanism of death, but to also send a wake up call to the viewer: “Do something with your life!'

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I'm working on a photo documentary project about the women involved in the singer/songwriter scene of the 1970s and 80s to showcase just how important it is to have those voices be prominent. Growing up gay and recently embracing a more nonbinary identity, I often found a connection with the voices of women, rather than the men I heard frequently on the radio. The music of these women was often a voice that felt like a guiding light. I was primarily raised by my grandmother, who died in December of 2020. I've been spending the last few years archiving her life's writing and photographs, but have struggled to make any of my own work regarding the loss. I wasn’t sure how to turn it into a project but I knew I needed to create something. I put out books twice a year with my writing and my photos, the first of which was a posthumous collab featuring my grandmother's poetry, but I wanted to do something bigger. I remembered all the times she and I planned photo shoots that didn’t happen, how I always thought I’d do it the next time I saw her. And then she died. On a whim I started cold emailing the women musicians of the 1970s I’d grown up listening to. Maybe if I could photograph these other women who had such a hand in raising me in their own ways, I could make peace with what I lost. Lots of emails went out. Very very few were returned, and when they did it was a “that’s nice, but not now”. In September of this year during a particularly manic moment I thought I would email Terre Roche, 1/3 of the iconic sister band The Roches. There was no chance she’d answer. She was a level of under the radar cult cool that felt truly unattainable. I was wrong. Terre answered my email with love, and she invited me into her cozy Manhattan apartment for tea. We spoke for nearly four hours. Getting to meet her, and speak with her for so long was so special, and set the tone for this documentary project that I realized had sat in my heart for so long. The women of the 1970s and 80s folk and singer/songwriter scene carried the era on their backs. Unless you were incredibly lucky to continue to find mainstream success, you were lauded for your moment and then sent to be tucked away into your home to wither and die, to be eulogized with photos of you at 19; defined by such a fraction of your life and your craft. All the while their male counterparts continued to tour, continued to amass new fans, continued to gain sex appeal. I've been sending out emails to other artists still, and while many do not respond, I've gotten the official confirmation from Judy Collins and Ronee Blakley. Pairing these two with Terre will hopefully open more doors to more performers, whose voices are so crucial to this project. At my core I'm a storyteller, and my photos have always been an active document and reflection of my life and the people around me. This project is different in that I feel with it's success, I could give back to the people who've been forgotten. Ultimately everything is about grief. Grief for a death, grief for a loss, grief for a career and artistry that should have been taken so much more seriously. I work freelance as much as possible, but also work managing a coffee shop. I run a very tight budget, and get by on very small margins every month. That often makes it difficult for me to pursue the projects I dream of. This grant would allow it to breathe in a way it couldn't before.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
To live life as an artist is to continually challenge one’s perception of the world. Even something as simple as perspective—both artistic and social definitions—illustrates how intimately life is intertwined with art making art. In that way, I am able to bring certain realizations from my art practice to my life more generally, and feel compelled to do the opposite. For example, the near constant news of disasters our world faces informs my art making in a fundamental way: my work reflects my own process of reflection, assimilation, rejection of what I see in the world. Then, as I create work in that state of mind, I am able to return to the larger world a more balanced, thoughtful, and integral person and citizen.

column25: The most recent works in my portfolio are from my ongoing project, Lacrime, which explores contemporary Weltschmerz and how being exposed to the pain of our world can affect individual and collective states of being human. I see Weltschmerz—melancholia associated with the worlds’ weariness—as a defining characteristic of our time; while witnessing widespread injustices perpetuated by ubiquitous media it becomes almost impossible to not empathize with people and places which are culturally, politically, or geographically distant. The paintings and sculptures in Lacrime serve as a meditation on the importance of mourning, and more specifically crying, as a means to connect with the world at large. Portrayed in muted colors, the depicted still figures are cemented in perpetual silence; I think of them as carrying an otherworldly quality that stems from an admiration of magical realism in both literature and music. To enhance this quality, I am playing with depth perception by including textured glass elements that reflect, refract, and bend light to captivate the viewer and offer a still moment in a turbulent time. Lacrime illustrates that making art allows me to reflect on the aforementioned spiritual melancholy and provides a refuge in knowing that the weight of the world does not rest on any one's shoulders. With recent increases in political tensions, social cataclysms, and environmental disasters worldwide, I see the phenomenon of Weltschmerz as omnipresent, which creates a sense of urgency and motivates me to continue exploring a subject that has never been more relevant.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is focused on exploring the symbolic aspects of the plant kingdom. I am fascinated by the mythology behind plants and how they can be viewed beyond food and medicine. What are the stories behind a plant's name? How can one flower like the Hellebore represent fire and Mars in one culture and conversely be a symbol of holiness for another culture? The tales about plants by people around the world carry valuable lessons on the art of careful observation and adopting alternative perspectives. Through my artwork, I aspire to encourage viewers to delve into this unique outlook on nature, inviting them to take a closer, more contemplative gaze.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is an exploration of the cultural origins of and influences on my gay identity. I use toys, games, teaching materials, and pop culture icons of my formative years to create fun but provocative autobiographical works of social commentary. The work includes painting, sculpture, and assemblage. The images and text I use have existed already to portray one narrative – I reassign, adjust, and re-contextualize these images and text to create another narrative that also existed, but was invisible, as was I. Through this process, I open a pathway into both worlds to create a sense of place and belonging. Much of the work is comprised of multiple pieces; referencing assimilation through repetition; a teaching method used in my elementary school academic and social experience. Images are culled from past pop culture sources, including toys, games, books, and family photographs. I bring these dated images into the present by making subtle changes and adjustments, and by so doing, the work reflects my true self. The work is infused with a sense of humor that embraces the audience. Recognizable icons, materials, and objects draw viewers into what appears to be a world of the familiar, however upon closer inspection, this assumption is radically challenged and at times, a disquieting response becomes palpable. It is at this moment my work becomes a bridge to a world that existed in parallel to the familiar but was invisible. My own experiences have been revisited and revised to include and acknowledge my own human existence and ongoing search for a sense of belonging and place.

column25: I’m presently working on creating a trilogy of large-scale experiential installations, which explore the intersection of art and healing. My goal in making these kinds of immersive environments is to create spaces that resonate with beauty and healing energy, foundational aspects of my artistic practice. I want to create immersive spaces that are not only visually compelling but create a living field of energy that can be felt. I choose materials specifically with this in mind, particularly materials that have been used by healers and metaphysicians for thousands of years. The idea is to use these materials as a vehicle for a subtle alchemy. The Salon of Inner Beauty, the first of these pieces to be completed, utilized 150,000 acupuncture needles to construct an immersive artwork that is visually beautiful while generating a vibratory field as the viewer stands among the healing energy of thousands of tiny healing objects. The installation becomes a sanctuary to be experienced from within the form. People who engaged with this work were actively feeling the space and taking in the energy of the work. This work was exhibited in Los Angeles in July 2023. The second and third phases of this trilogy of works are in the development phase. In the second work of the trilogy, I address the omnipresent electromagnetic frequencies in our contemporary environment with the creation of The Faraday Cave. Handwoven copper wire structures, reminiscent of a crystalline cave, serve not just as visually intriguing pieces but as haven charged with the healing frequency of pure copper. The choice of copper, with its historical use in medicine and metaphysics, underscores the holistic approach to healing and reinforces the interconnectedness of art and well-being. The final work of the trilogy is The Orgonite Silo. Orgonite is a composite material originating from the theories of Wilhelm Reich, an Austrian psychoanalyst and inventor in the mid-20th century. This unique substance is crafted by combining organic and inorganic elements, typically resin, metal shavings, and crystals. The concept of orgonite is grounded in Reich's idea of 'orgone energy,' a universal life force akin to concepts like qi or prana in ancient spiritual and healing practices. People believe that orgonite can harness and manipulate this energy for various purposes. It is often used to promote well-being, reduce electromagnetic radiation, and enhance spiritual practices. The combination of resin, which acts as an organic matrix, metal shavings for conductivity, and crystals for their energetic properties, is thought to create a harmonious and balanced energy field. Orgonite devices, such as pyramids or sculptures, are created with the intention of positively influencing the surrounding environment and the individuals within it. Despite the controversial nature of Reich's theories, orgonite has gained popularity among those interested in alternative healing and metaphysical practices. The Orgonite Silo, crafted from hand cast orgonite bricks serves as an accumulator of positive energy, symbolizing harvest, nourishment, and storage, offering a space of the transformative potential.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist, I can forge my own pathway and constantly evaluate (and reevaluate) what feels like a good fit. Each decision is mine, which means that I am responsible for the way that I move through the world and interact with others. Art-making and process-based explorations show me how to do so. Having only worked as a part-time professional independent creative for three years, I feel more alive now than in any other job I've held in the past. In a way, those jobs that didn’t work out primed me for a self-directed, entrepreneurial future. Especially while I was recovering from spinal surgery during 2020-2022, art-making led the way. Due to the combination of the pandemic and my health situation, I couldn't work for about a year, and while waiting for my bones to fuse, I took to drawing. Somehow, the coping skills that I used as a kid came back to me. Over so many months, art-making became a way for me to rehabilitate my nervous system, relearn strategies to enliven a body that seemed to have failed me, and regain a sense of self. At the same time, I was selling work and garnering commissioned projects. Art-making allowed for me to take control over an extended healing process that seemed out of my hands. Though it is still an uphill climb to make it work as an artist, this nontraditional pathway feels healthy to me, and whatever community that comes from the journey seems meant to be. Everyone's speed and pace is varied, and I love how the creative community finds synergy together because of these differences and individual strengths. As artists, through the examination of our craft, I think that we can work to resolve inner turmoil or shift how we interpret external conflicts by forging a sense of personal peace that can be communicated with others, even if only through what comes from resounding visuals and vibrations emitted on the outside.

column25: Collectively, my artistry presents a visual language that considers varying aspects of an introspective healing process, navigating through layers of paint and media to arrive at a cohesive course for the viewer, offering different entry-points for the eye. Through precise linework and rich bursts of color, I showcase a blend of patterned reactions through an assortment of twists and turns that merge with, and therefore shape, whatever situation comes next. Originally developed in tandem with my recovery from a serious spinal cord injury, my abstract style offers the idea of art as an outlet through which emotions may be processed and channeled in nonviolent ways. Once able to see my creative output hit a material surface, I can digest the various thoughts and sensations that occur within myself and better understand how a composition morphs into a finished artwork supported by a solidified state of mind. The throughline of my work is rooted in the discovery and development of creative strategies as a problem-solving tool. All in all, I look to translate my experiences into an approachable framework that exists within a shared state of growth, both in and out of the studio. For the past two years, I have structured coping strategies into workshops for a local mental health community center. Referencing similar abstract techniques, my next body of work will continue to function as a visible form of peer support but more specifically cater to my own individual narrative as a way to show personal progress, offer examples of catharsis, stimulate dialogue, and integrate conversation. As a teaching tool, my work brings abstraction into focus, delivers historical context, and bolsters active social practice.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Embark on a dynamic artistic journey with me, Robbi Firestone—a fearless explorer weaving passion, innovation, and insatiable curiosity into the fabric of my artistic expression. As a renowned NFT Artist, Speaker, and Multidisciplinary Artist, my creative endeavors span a wide spectrum, encompassing traditional art supplies, found objects, medical supplies, and even the unexpected—like Cheetos. Having presented on international stages and being recognized as a Maker's Place Featured Artist, my artistic voice resonates on a global scale, featured in prominent media outlets such as The New York Times, Los Angeles Travel, Huffington Post, Worth Magazine, etc. Have fun Stepping into the daring realm of CrunchTrump, a groundbreaking project that pushes the boundaries of political pop, satire and creativity. Crafting a 4-foot tall portrait of Donald Trump entirely from Cheetos, with 10,000 accompanying NFTs, this venture pledges 8% of profits to planetary conservation and inspiring women to run for political office—an audacious statement within the realm of contemporary art. My innovative NFT Exhibitions in AR and VR, including NFT NYC, Art Nasel Miami, MakersPlace O(v.)erturned, and IllustSpace, redefine immersive realities. Venturing boldly into Virtual Reality, I've utilized Tilt-brush to create Metaverse fine art, breaking barriers and orchestrating Metaverse art encounters at events like meow Wolf, NM tech Summit, Museum Circles, SearchlightNFT: NYC-NFT and @TheClubFomo, where I proudly serve as the Founding Creative Director & NFT Artist. My role on the Board of Advisors for SearchLight NFT solidifies my commitment to pioneering new frontiers in the ever-evolving world of digital art. Sharing insights on platforms like Twitter Spaces NFT-licious and creating Web3 onboarding content for diverse audiences, I am dedicated to democratizing access to this revolutionary form of artistic expression. Collaborations with Grammy Winners like MercyMe, KebMo, and Mayhem Lane showcase art's ability to transcend mediums, capturing musical brilliance visually. 'Crunch Trump' isn't just art; it's a satirical spotlight transcending political boundaries. Blurring the lines between sculpture and political commentary, it's an innovative exploration that invites viewers to appreciate the humor in the intersection of politics and pop culture. Beyond the confines of traditional art spaces, the project extends its influence through exclusive merchandise inspired by 'Crunch Trump' on our website. A portion of the sales contributes to organizations supporting women running for political office—a tangible way in which art becomes a catalyst for positive change in the world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I collect and appropriate from vintage vernacular photographs, yearbooks, and magazines dated before the Gay Liberation Movement of 1969. I frequently visit antique stores for materials and turn to eBay by searching terms such as 'gay interest', 'male affection', or 'vintage gay photograph', and purchase these images from anonymous eBay sellers as an act of preservation for artifacts of unforgettable love and the space in between a photograph's meaning. By working with found materials, I believe the act of archiving and collecting photographs is both a source of absence and comfort in exploring a lost visual history prior to the Gay Liberation Movement. What constitutes as a queer photograph in memory of life in a pre-Stonewall America? Through forms of installation, I create objects that memorialize these photographic materials through modes of assemblage, collage, sculpture, and printmaking in honor of this lost visual history. My work nods to the writing of David Deitcher and has directly referenced Hal Fischer, Felix Gonzalez-Torres, and organizations such as The Mattachine Society and ACT UP. Within my assemblages are printed matter, polaroids, gay newspapers, matchbooks, vintage photographs, t-shirts, a red tie, and my childhood best friend's locket of ashes. It is with appropriation, collage, and assemblage where potential sits to discover new meanings for lost visual histories. My search through photography considers what has not had space for being visible other than what is held behind the curtain of a photo booth, between age-stained pages of a yearbook, or kept secret in lost canisters of film.

column25: Beating Hearts are part of my wider Arroz con Popote series, which utilizes rice and straws as a queer medium, an innovation I introduced into the art world. In Mexican slang, the phrase 'arroz con popote' is associated with male homosexuality, referring to the act of eating rice pudding through a straw, with or without offense: Andy Warhol likes arroz con popote. This body of work pays homage to my Mexican roots, while addressing contemporary themes through a queer-cultural lens, in the form of installation, performance, and digital art. The work aims to promote understanding and acceptance, contributing to the creation of a more inclusive society. The Beating Hearts collection further recognizes the humanity, dignity, and existence of queer people everywhere. This is emphasized through the use of flag colors and the naming of each piece. I further draw upon my Catholic upbringing to reinterpret the Sacred Heart of Jesus, which is traditionally depicted as a heart encircled by thorns. In doing so, I infuse a centuries-old symbol with personal and cultural significance, creating an icon symbolic of the queer community's endurance.

I reference pop and suburban culture to inform characters that are memorable, familiar, and satisfyingly weird. I have been drawing digitally for 12 years going forward, and my work relies heavily on fluid lineart with varying weight, dramatic semi-realistic lighting, and exaggerated “toony” features to communicate narrative. Magical realism is the overarching genre that defines my aesthetics. The themes most prevalent in my work include Fate vs Free Will, Hubris, Religious Trauma, and Identity. My characters are queer POC weirdos and exist in different universes that you can witness in comic, concept art, and editorial illustration.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is expression, hands down. Art is a holistic expression. Expression is the part of being a human that shows how you feel. expression shows what we need(lack), how we see the world and it's how we operate(act) within the world. EXPRESSION is key to communicating and taking action or taking inaction for that matter. As humans, we all have these feelers, (more than just fingers and the five senses), intuition, emotions, magnetic resonance, and the perception of it all...being human is feeling the world and then reacting or responding to it ... For better or for worse, its all a holographic reflection of the whole wave and should be respected as such, not marginalized or shunned. subsequently, my viewpoint of art falls into lockstep, the art that a person presents as their expression is how they see, receive, and conceive the world. Any person that CREATES music, visual art, machines, or even LAWS, is simply telling A story of A lived experience. Synonymous with the person saying, 'I am a human that feels, thinks, and lives in this way'... I feel alive when I'm able to make things aesthetically pleasing while telling a story of the pain experienced. I feel alive when I'm able to create things that I need to be seen, felt, heard, or experienced, because I have seen felt, and heard these things. I show my art to speak of trauma I perceive daily that hurts so deeply, that it has burned a patterned fracture into my consciousness which holographically reflects this world. I know that threw art I can reimagine and alchemize my pain into beauty(though it does not disappear), this makes me feel alive(able to live). It may sound creepy and confusing but, the pain dies and is reborn as a strength that somehow I have always had unconsciously. When I write a song that shares the energy of the exact emotion I felt at the time, people are miraculously able to relate to the words and the way that I'm singing(expressing) it automatically. I have always been self-aware of my expression because I have always known this world is threatened by my presence before I even arrive. Expressing art helps me feel alive and accounted for.

column25: I enjoy picking out unorthodox and oversized materials and mediums to serve as my canvas. When the canvas is vast and unconventional people will understand, that this is not art that can be contained by an 8x11 paper or a 16x20 canvas, this is art about real life on a real-life object. Objects you interact with every day, a door, a desk, a wall, a cabinet, or even a window is perfect! This practice is a commentary on the weighty subject matter being highlighted within any given piece. I never know what I'm creating until I begin creating it, the first thing I start with is a feeling. Upon completion, this particular feeling will be the essential energy I want to reify within the observer, introspectively(within the self, only for the benefit of the self). A key aspect found within every one of my works is that I am always revealing 'the secret'. My genre of art is modern, contemporary, street art, collage, sculpture, installation & mixed media art. There is always an amalgamation of eclectic inspirations such as classical art, anime, science, graffiti, sacred geometry, philosophy, and spirituality. I believe for a person to fully receive and understand my art they should be wishing to free themselves and be truthful about what they are witnessing within their social environments. I intend to express my art form in an environment of tranquility with modern Jazz, pop/R&B, and hip-hop, great food & drinks, and even GREATER LIGHTING!!! I start my work with a pencil, then I will outline in either charcoal or paint pen, and then it's onto painting, acrylic, oil, or watercolor. Next, I do a touch-up with markers, then I color correct with charcoal and pastels. I use sculpting foam, wood, string, burlap, wires, and metal hardware if that texture is required. I sculpt, draw, carve, sew, weave, collage and power drill it all into place. For 8 years now I have worked out of my home Art Studio/SRO, which is tight, but I make it work. All of my installations however are to be experienced as fully immersive, 3D interactive, and otherworldly sculptures that invoke intrigue. when I create wall art it will always be a central statement piece of the decor simply because each piece is a world within itself. My art reflects my work, my work is my life, and my life has been a lesson in interdisciplinary & intersecting experiences.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am interested in facilitating moments of sensation, connection, and communication through the visceral and vicarious elements of performance and their afterlives as photographs, videos, sculptures, and installations: all traces of my physical self. Their materiality and texture encourage the simple act of feeling. My works make the remnants of colonial, capitalist, and patriarchal violence visible and tactile. Believing that the toll of such violence resides down beneath the skin and flesh, I begin the process of developing new works by extracting somatic knowledge and memories from my own body. Familial, community, and national histories and archives also serve as inspirations for my works, which offer audiences specific vignettes of my experiences of moving through this world as a Black queer femme. My scripted evening-length pieces and durational performances combine movement, sound, and the live fabrication of costumes, sculptures, and structures that are distinctly corporeal. Reflecting my interest in analyzing femininity, the props I use in my performances are ready-made, domestic objects that can be acquired, joined, and disassembled with ease. My body’s (in)ability to accommodate the shape and size of excessive amounts of these objects leaves it exposed and vulnerable to burdensome forces. My repetitive attempts to support these objects mirror the choreography required to negotiate the limitations of the structures that extend from and beyond the home. Recently, I have become interested in creating performances that can only be experienced by a small, intimate audience. These performances disrupt the typical power structures that separate artists from spectators. Given my belief that art is participatory, I resist traditional notions of theatre-making that place audience members in a passive role in which they can only receive performances or consume works in an extractive way. I want to involve audiences in the performances they witness, so they recognize that they are complicit in the action that takes place. Each time I perform, I offer up my body so that it may be a vessel that reveals the audience’s true nature, intentions, wants, and needs.

For me the best thing about being an artist is working with my hands and expressing myself in ways that my community, family, friends, and strangers from other cities to other continents can understand even if they do not have formal training in the arts. The rumors are true - being an artist is financially difficult, often scary, and always challenging - and still I wouldn't trade it for anything else because I live every day growing more in tune with myself and others. Art is a way to engage our deepest selves, the people around us, our ancestors, and those that will come after us. Further, it is a way to deal with the often complex and even dangerous circumstances we find ourselves in as individuals and collectives. Art offers a path to liberation through personal reflection and the experience of shared humanity in community. I feel that being an artist is a great privilege.

column25: Community work, historical inquiry, lived experience, ancestral memory, and premonition are the primary sources of my practice. I want everyone to be free. I have been an artist all my life though I spent years believing I hadn’t inherited the wealth or bloodline. I questioned art’s place amid violence and loss before learning that there it belongs most. In college I studied history and global dynamics of ethno-racial order, though protest, community organizing, justice work, and eventually my practice proved more significant than theory. I’ve been tear-gassed, beaten by police. I’ve walked prison yards and dorm halls cracking jokes and listening to stories of abuse whispered with fear. Conversations with those living on the street have been a joy of my life on both coasts and in-between, where I’ve given as much as I’ve been given. I spent 2021 on the road from NYC to LA, traveling down the east coast, through the south, all over Texas, up to Wyoming and down to California. Snow-capped pines to backroad gators to jazz and beignets to no one around for 30 miles. Ropes and bridge leaps into the creek. Mountaintop sleep, the fox in the rain with thunder rolling in. Streets that are the same everywhere. Million-year-old rocks and shapes, sounds, textures, temperatures that remind us we’re on a planet. The loss of my grandfather. Love’s early flowers. Through my craft I tell my story and learn to be vulnerable. I got out of the mud in Utah’s salt flats to save myself – I make things to achieve the same. My family had to survive. I am thankful for the pressure I feel to self-actualize. As a child I saw and experienced domestic violence, witnessed racism and sexism in New York courts and then in streets, schools, and prisons through my daily life, education, and work. I find strength in a familial and cultural history of resistance, creating freedom – gentleness in the opposite’s face. Now I make maps of experience and time. I paint them on large frames that I build myself in a process that is sacred to me. And I remember my grandfather working on his house in the south. It is a dance. My maps are often colorful and dense. There are twists, hiding spots, and secrets buried under additive layers of irreversible cosmic sedimentation like the million-year-old rocks in West Texas and California. Okay – sometimes just layers of oil paint whose ochres are sourced from those rocks. I write poems in the margins and include the beasts that duck and bob across the water. Maps are a portrait of the time, no? I trace the zeitgeist against the topography. Words, shapes, emotional ruptures, diagrammatic reference material, deep-seated feelings from the past, and visions of the future are bases of my work. I have painted figures and places months before encountering them, only then understanding where they came from. Maybe we all share generational trauma by virtue of sharing the spaces that have seen it all through millennia – intimacy and violence, incarceration and liberation side-by-side. I know ‘it’ is in the air because I feel it. I know you do too. The goal of my work is to expose these layers in time and sculpt a new moment that is as gently encouraging as it is forceful – a mirror. Look! Look at what we have built, how it affects every one of us. Look at how staring it in the face makes you feel, beautiful human being with the power to change it all. May my work leave my audience more empathetic, understanding that when we respect ourselves we cannot go out of our way to harm others. Themes in my practice include personal and collective identity formation, the way history is told and remembered/disremembered, surveillance and control, the ongoing commodification of human experience, and loss of self/degradation of self-image through social media. I am eager to join a community of ambitious creators in order to learn from others and to challenge myself on materiality and process. I want to maximize the potential of every opportunity afforded to me, that I am the first in my family to even explore. More than anything I want to expand my practice as the instrument of world-altering change that art can be.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Since childhood I’ve leaned into my affinity for visual communication through trying to make meaning of vivid dreams and art making. Seeing and knowing through dreaming led me to a curiosity regarding signs and symbols. Art making gave me agency to build my own pictorial language. This combination helped me to arrive at my creative practice. My visual language manifests through a lexicon of repeating shapes, symbols, figures, inversions and forms. These elements all serve their own meaning and purpose and are connected to my personal story, liminal spaces, spirit and inter relational dynamics. I have been creating a pictorial alphabet. Rainbows, hearts, flowers, clouds, and other symbols are omnipresent in my work. All are symbols that are recognized and have assigned meaning across various cultures while also having personal meaning. Appropriating them and assigning expanded understanding to them is crucial to my language building and visual storytelling. It is my visual form of writing. Through semi-representational and abstract images, I portray the context, the meaning of the symbols in my work and their emotional range/capacity. Highlighting adult concepts with a childlike vibe and aesthetic allows me to access joy while reimagining and challenging representations of trauma and emotional space. My work allows for the re-envisioning of reality and expansion of what is communicated in the ways we deal with ourselves, others, the earth and the universe. Through my work I visualize real and imaginative spaces. By mixing in remnants of the figure within abstracted environments, I also convey a space in which the body and the spirit can communicate, find alignment and reconnect.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Having recently incorporated the use of Artificial Intelligence in one of my projects, I find it intriguing to observe the growing interest in the production of art using AI tools. This trend sparks a debate about the influence of emerging technologies within the art sector, whether displayed in galleries or found in the commercial realms of mainstream media. In the commercial art sphere, the near-instantaneous image production capability of AI tools such as DALL-E and Midjourney can be a point of concern in the valuation of art by consumers and the public. Historically, the introduction of technological automation has often led to a decreased appreciation for human labor and goods. As AI technology advances towards a successful imitation of human creativity -or at least producing recognizable visual aesthetics indistinguishable from human-made designs-it is conceivable that we may face the same bleak outcome. In the realm of fine art, the definition of art has evolved beyond the production and imitations of images. Contemporary art values individual aesthetics and the cultural, social, and historical dimensions that an artwork reflects. Our appreciation of art stems from emotional resonance, aesthetic innovation, and conceptual complexity. It is through these aspects that we choose to believe in the inherent value that an artist assigns to the work, which in turn makes it meaningful. Art, in its essence, is humanizing, rooted in, and crafted by human nature. While the future of AI and the degree to which it may truly emulate human thought and creativity remains unpredictable, we currently stand at a crossroads where technology acts not as a breach of human creativity but offers a rare opportunity to reassess and redefine what creativity means in isolation. Yet, it is difficult to argue that many people share the same consensus on how the integration of technology will affect our perception of art, as there is a tendency to undervalue AI-produced artwork. This response is natural and can stem from a multitude of reasons, whether it be the lack of clarity we have in understanding the role of technology within the artistic process or the ‘burnout’ caused by the sheer volume of AI-generated images on the internet. The saturation of ‘AI art’ has resulted in a renewed appreciation for analog methodologies that can convey an authentic human touch. Because of this, it becomes important for the artist to blend human-evocative aesthetics into technology, to advocate for genuine, personal thoughts and emotions that can supersede the ‘AI-ness’ of the artwork. And by conceptually, and visually manipulating the artistic language of the work, it becomes more of a meaningful collaboration between machine and human, potentially reshaping the way we perceive this new form of art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I ask a lot of questions about where our internal selves touch the outer world. These questions are regularly about fears, traumas, melancholy, and sentimentality. From those questions I get curious and do deep dive research into word history; the origins and meanings of words I am rolling around. From that research I start to form a visual of these experiences that are inspired from conversations I have with others, historical reference, dreams, and research I am both doing in the moment and previous research from the past. The accumulation of these questions, manifestations, conversations, and research is where I begin to create. These questions are not solitary, and I do not believe them to be empty. I cultivate them over long periods of time; sometimes so long I forget I asked them in the first place. You can’t line them up in neat little rows like one would a garden. Each question is a part of an evolutionary line that I can trace back to a source; but the direct descendent may be so unrecognizable from its ancestor as to promote more questions. Because I’m not always interested in capturing emotions and experiences like artifacts in a cabinet of curiosity or pining them down like in a butterfly collection. They make more sense to me when they are being lived and experienced. Sometimes the organic nature is routed through elaborate rules and rituals. I talk in codes and roads, wanting to move others and be moved myself. These threads of thought sometimes become tapestries, and sometimes they are wrapped back into balls to help guide me through a different labyrinth. We touch and they touch, and our hands brush.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As I have worked through struggles with embodiment; being adopted into a family of a different race than my own, of struggling with the Beauty Myth that permeates our culture, and now, of hitting middle age and feeling the loss of youth and visibiltiy, I have am acutely aware of the role fashion has played on this stage. In the past I have used my clothing as rebellion and also as a desperate attempt to claim worthiness and to belong. I have wondered what our world would be like if our clothing wasn't about fitting someone else's ideal of beauty, or appropriateness, or trendiness. I imagine a scenario in which inner world matched our outer one and our apparel was not the attempt to fit in or rebel (as it was in my case) but rather an expression of the issues that unite us as people. To this end, I find my art has evolved into headpieces that are expressions of issues I grapple with daily. I show them in fashion shows, special events, and in photography. In doing so, I ask models, known to me to have overcome much on their own journeys, to wear my work. The combination of a sculptural piece that I create out of recycled material on the head of a person with their own story to tell is powerful to me. I create headpieces, then invite others to wear them. Combined, they tell a new story, one that reminds me that I am not the only one struggling with and meeting life's challenges. I look at my work on someone else's head and wonder how this headpiece reflects the model's inner world. It amazes me that a headpiece brings out wildly different emotions depending on who wears it and creates something new each time it is worn. If we dig deep enough, however, the stories they begin to sound alike and mirror each other. Closely aligned with embodiment is the concept of being enough (with thanks to Brene Brown for making this idea come alive). I began working in recycled materials somewhat out of necessity - I was broke and needed work that provided flexibilty to care for aging parents. I began making hats out of paper bags, asking friends and neighbors to save their grocery bags and scraps of fabric for me. I used left-over paint to paint the bags, roll them into hats, and then embellished them with scraps of fabric and other scrounged items. I've been delighted to find that using recycled materials is a constant reminder that I have all I need and that I am enough. It helps me stay off the wheel of mass-consumerism, and even more importantly, provides freedom from comparing myself to others. To begin with trash as a material means I have to innovate ways of working with it. It means the work is fresh and closely aligned with my work process and perspective.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
For as long as I can remember, I have been drawing to connect my inner self with the outer world. I did not trust my voice, but the pencil reached out for me. My work is about creating the movement of organic forms and patterns inspired by nature and found materials. I transcribe and reveal the beauty of nature and urban waste through intuitive calligraphy. As an artist of two cultural backgrounds, my work naturally thrives in the marriage of juxtaposed influences. The calligraphic gestures of traditional painting are my brush strokes, even when using bean pods, collecting coffee lids, painting murals or weaving plastics. The beautiful facades of my work hide my underlying anxiety for the impact we have on the environment. In my attempt to become a zero-waste artist, I collect things like junk-mail, yogurt tops, tea wrappers, and bits of broken glass to layer into my work. I cut them, shred them and make them mine. The trash bits from cutting them into shapes end up driving my next project.

column25: Sophia Lee is a Taiwanese-American multimedia artist. She creates paintings, drawings, light installations, and public artwork, finding her voice in modernizing the tradition of calligraphy and a positive re-contextualization of urban waste. Her long-flowing stroke technique celebrates the graphic quality of calligraphy in deconstructed patterns and non-recognizable forms. Creating images that thrive on the edge of the familiar, revealing grace and beauty through abstraction and repetition. She builds her artwork in layers through embroidery, collage, stitching, weaving, and different painting techniques. Crafting dreamlike, abstract textures out of reclaimed seed pods, expired cosmetics, junk mail, and discarded plastic. Sophia was born in Taiwan and immigrated to the United States when she was nine years old. At the time, her only understanding of English was the Alphabet. Her childhood was a combined education in both Mandarin (summers in Taiwan) and English. She grew up in a generation of immigrant children that was dubbed as 'a parachute kid.' After studying Fashion at FIDM in Los Angeles, she continued on to study Fine Arts at the Art Center College of Design in Pasadena. Depressed by the amount of waste created and the promotion of relentless consumerism working in the fashion industry, she left it for a career in publishing designing book covers in New York City. When 911 happened in 2001, Sophia quit her life in the city to become semi-nomadic. She rediscovered Taiwan as an adult and lived in different cities in Europe and Asia. She was an early adopter of digital nomad. While traveling, she was inspired to return to drawing. When she settled in Taipei in 2005, she began her first series of work, holding a solo exhibit, l’Arabesque, in 2007. She became a full-time artist when she moved to San Francisco in 2015. Sophia has lived in Los Angeles, Newport Beach, New York, Portland, San Francisco, and Taipei, participating in solo and group shows. In recent years, she began creating murals and installations. As an artist, she seeks to engage directly with the public, exploring the existence of art in the realm of the everyday and its positive impact on the life of local communities. Since the pandemic she lives and works between San Francisco and Newport Beach.

column25: My artistic journey is a fusion of vibrant abstraction and ancestral essence. It all began with a simple yet intriguing desire: to bring together the vivid abstract images I had previously crafted and breathe new life into them. The idea was to harness these visual marvels as a wellspring of colors that would fuel a novel semi-autonomous brush, giving life to strokes driven by the power of human gestures. Captivated by this concept, I turned to my trusty moleskin notebook, a realm where ideas take shape before they transcend the boundaries of imagination. In these pages, I penned down a list of ingredients and recipes, a metaphorical DNA and RNA, waiting to form the genetic code of my artistic venture. My vision was to create a paintbrush that would actively chase user gestures. This pursuit was realized through the intricate mechanics of an invisible spring-loaded mechanism, which intricately linked the bristles of the brush. These bristle tips, each meticulously designed, collectively took on forms that resonated with deeper meanings, turning strokes into a vivid embodiment of thoughts and heritage. The journey from these sketches in my notebook to the tangible world of code was a metamorphosis in itself. Sculpted from my code, Squirrel, ColorPicker, ImagePaletteRemapper, and Ribbons emerged as the applications and architects of my digital realm, each playing a crucial role in shaping my artistic vision. Squirrel, ColorPicker, and ImagePaletteRemapper became the virtuoso composers of a custom color palette that infused life into my canvas. Ribbons, on the other hand, became the medium through which my artistic expressions could find their voice. In the symphony of creation, these components orchestrated a harmonious melody, giving rise to semi-autonomous brushes that stood as sentinels, watching and imitating the dance of human hands. But beyond the mechanics and codes, my work is a tale of transformation, where ideas are not confined to the realm of thought but metamorphose into tangible, interactive forms. It's a dialogue between ancestry and innovation, an exploration of the tactile and the digital. The vibrant hues that dance upon my canvas, carefully sampled from the palette crafted by Squirrel, are infused with the vitality of the human touch, becoming brushstrokes that capture the very essence of existence. My inspiration is drawn from two distinct yet intimately connected sources: the rich tapestry of cultural heritage and the intricate patterns of abstraction. These elements converge in my artwork, forming a bridge that invites viewers to traverse realms where whispers of the past harmonize with the cadence of digital innovation. As you delve into this statement, envision my work as a visual narrative, a conversation between history and the present moment. Brushstrokes of yesteryears intermingle with the dynamic gestures of today, giving rise to a timeless dialogue on the canvas of perception.

column25: Ciguapas, sirenas, y santos serve as my muses and inspirations. In drawing parallels to ballroom culture with African marronage and covert Santería circles, I seek to revive queer ancestral bloodlines traced back to the Yoruba people brought to the island of Haiti and the Dominican Republic in addition to the Taíno people who migrated there long before Columbus. A step towards this vision was my altar installation for THEY WATCH YOU THRIVE where Asyra was able to return as a ciguapa alongside Chanel Matsunami’s fabric sculptures. Light and sound serve as my protagonists and stage. Photography and videography are mythopoeic tapestries. Lavish fabrics become manifestations of the deities and saints I honor within my work. Together, they each form a space where I can embody my highest being. In turn, this embodiment becomes a love letter from my highest self to the world, and especially to those deeply wounded by marginalization and alienation. This sense of community and sisterhood may be seen in my three-part portrait series, FUENTE SIN FIN, GEMELAS DEL SOL, and RIO DE ORO, where I asked friends to join me in this inner-goddess exploration. I hope to create a new visual portal series for THEY WATCH YOU THRIVE as well. Museums divorce the spiritual force that resides within my ancestors’ crafts. My installations seek to manifest the spiritual energies inherent within culturally specific creations. To emulate a deity while reclaiming monstrosity is my path of transformation and resistance. There is a revolution to be had, and it starts with facing myth and propaganda for what they are.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Since 2016, my journey with abstract glass paintings has evolved, incorporating rear-illumination over the last year to deepen the interplay of light and texture. My fascination with this medium lies in its multifaceted nature—it is an art of perspectives, engaging the viewer in a dialogue that is as much about the variances in their perception as it is about the art itself. Communication, in all its forms, is a transaction of ideas and interpretations. My creations are a testament to this exchange—each glass canvas is a conversation starter, offering different narratives from the displayed side (front/back) to the intricacies of the light behind it. These elements coalesce to shape the viewer's experience, reminding us that art is a living entity, reshaped by the perspectives of the beholder, the context of the moment, and the ever-shifting tides of culture and time. The process of my art-making considers addition and subtraction. I lay down layers of paint, augment them with various materials, and remove parts at different stages of drying. This juxtaposition of presence and absence, texture and smoothness, opacity and transparency, culminates in the final piece, which can continue to evolve based on its presentation. It is a manifestation of the unspoken—the power of what is omitted—which underscores the significance of the latent messages that resonate beyond the visible. I extend an invitation to the viewer: to engage with my work, to perceive the emotions and stories that these paintings stir within. And as time marches on, to revisit them, to explore how the passage of time and the evolution of circumstance color these messages in new hues. This is the beauty of abstract art—it is never static but a living dialogue that evolves with us.

column25: Katharine Torgersen is a visual artist and writer based in Rochester, NY. Drawing on her personal experiences, Katharine's artwork delves into themes of healing from complex trauma, loss, addiction, and mental health. She draws inspiration from her time abroad, specifically in Southeast Asia and the Sacred Valley, where she absorbed diverse cultural influences that enrich her art. She works in a number of mediums, primarily acrylic on glass, the display of which is backlit. Additionally, she works in photography, collage, and mixed media. Through her art, she weaves together threads of resilience, vulnerability, and transformation. By giving visual form to her emotions and experiences, she invites viewers to join her on a journey of introspection and healing, fostering empathy and understanding. Katharine's work has been showcased in public art spaces, including Gallery 5 in Richmond, Virginia and The Yards in Rochester, New York. She's organized sales where 100% of proceeds go toward LGBTQ+ organizations, such as Diversity Richmond and Orlando Youth Alliance. As a staunch advocate for the healing and organizing power of art, she is deeply committed to promoting accessibility and inclusivity within the creative community. In an increasingly fragmented world, Katharine's art serves as a bridge, connecting individuals from different walks of life through shared human experiences. Her art embraces the complexities of the human condition; she seeks to break down barriers of shame and stigma, and to inspire viewers to embark on their own journeys of self-discovery and healing.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
It is a great privilege to have the opportunity to create art. For me, it's a meditative practice in that it contributes to an emptying of the self into the work and a subsequent empty mind, which feels absolutely luscious. I feel that I work directly with the powers of the universe, allowing 'chance' to impact the work along with the application of my own instincts. It is a partnership and a method for me to tune in to forces outside of myself, which allows me to step outside of the material experience of being human. Before my mind empties, I experience profoundly abstracted cognitions about the nature of the universe. I consider the possibilities of parallel or overlapping universes, unseen associations between objects in the universe, disruptions on the time/space continuum, and the infinitude of creative possibility. Occupying this mind space reduces the sense of self and gives a weightlessness to my person. The power of imagination lies in its ability to be unbounded. As an artist, I get to play all day. Beyond my own relationship to the work, I experience immense joy in sharing it with others. I love to hear what people see in my abstractions and the emotions the my works evoke in people. Imagination manifested into art is a valuable and necessary form of communication between people.

column25: As humans, we have both physical evidence of that which we experience in the exterior world and the infiniteness of internal imagination. I am interested in the ways in which I can visually hold these two truths at the same time, despite the improbability of their realization. The body of work altogether combines elements of both existing and imaginary worlds as metaphysical investigations into the nature of the universe, including the concept of parallel universes. Playing with the dimensions of materiality, I investigate possibilities through spatiality and formal relationships within the works. I make paintings, lithographs, assemblages, collages and drawings using a heavily saturated color palette of oil, acrylic and watercolor paints as well as colored pencil, marker, ink and oil pastels. Through three specific modes, the work occupies differing places on a spectrum between abstraction and representation. The first mode is pure abstraction as an investigation into the internal. Pure form, color, shape, balance, saturation and negative space are the tools through which I communicate emotions or spaces existing in an imaginary universe. I source this instinctually by allowing myself to naturally make marks without attempting to influence the outcome. My second work mode is a liminal space, bridging the gap between abstract and representational. Often, these works take the form of abstracted landscapes or urban settings existing in a space between the recognizable dimensions in which we live and imaginary landscapes. These employ the use of a consistent shape and color-driven visual language with a combination of abstracted and representational semiotics. These works are curations of collected observed material both from my own abstract work and the material/external world. Materials are collected, deconstructed from both their visual and symbolic contexts, and then reconstructed into finished works that challenge the boundaries of tradition. Source materials include sketchbooks, paintings, photographs, and print material. The third and final mode is representational work based on photography, and my homage to the material world. Depicting a sense of place is foremost in this practice. While both subject and content are the most recognizable and referential to the external, shared world, the works are intentionally informed by their materiality to create specific moods and evoke emotions in the viewer. As my practice evolves, the modes remain distinct from one another; yet they have the potential to exist together in new ways. I am currently working on new pieces that combine these heretofore separate methods of creating. It seems an entirely new universe is unfolding before me.

My work is a multimedia exploration of processing experienced trauma and the accompanying desire to facilitate understanding and healing. My current focus surrounds a journey of grief. It is a holistic survey involving creative process, multidisciplinary academic research, education, and practice. I bring aspects of meditative rituals to the creation of series and multiples through repetitive constructive actions. I’m interested in art and healing through personal engagement and how it exists globally as a clinical and spiritual practice. I'm curious to know what grief does to a person from a biological and psychological standpoint. I engage in process based work, creating multiples and series. I operate with senses of curiosity and whimsy. As an avid scholar with a background in a variety of coursework, I take a holistic approach to creating. My works involve repetitive and meditative continuing motions in construction and mark making. I follow my intuition throughout, trusting the process. I find the acts of inception and creation to be as important as a completed piece. The making is the exploration and a point of catharsis in the trauma and healing cycle.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
There is freedom in being an artist. Growing up in a culture that valued rules and technical skill, I was taught there was a right way to do things, a right way to express myself. Whether I was writing an English paper or performing South-Indian classical music, I often felt that my creative voice came less from me and more from others’ prescriptive expectations of me. Unlearning these internal constraints can happen in any medium; for me it’s through visual art. Art feels limitless. There are endless materials, mediums, subjects, and feelings to create with. There is no rubric for me to follow, and the challenge of this new and infinite universe is both terrifying and liberating. Art has given me permission to explore and develop my own voice.

column25: When I dissociate, I experience my body as a collection of shapeless, ephemeral fragments. When I wake up from these episodes, they feel like distant dreams that I can’t remember or process. In my paintings, I visually reconstruct these fragmented states to clarify and understand them, exploring the nature of fragmentation, somatic memory, and transformation. My portraits superimpose three iterations of a person’s face to create a single strange humanoid head. As I paint, I distort, transpose, merge, and duplicate various facial features to combine them. I work primarily in oil paint with an acrylic under-painting that shows through the cracks, suggesting disintegration and transparency of the figure. My guiding principle is to follow my instincts in the moment so that my subconscious reveals itself. This process and subject matter are influenced by integrative psychotherapy models such as Internal Family Systems and Somatic Experiencing. My work is predicated on the transformative power of curiosity about the self. In clarifying my past dissociative experiences, emotions that were once elusive and frozen in time become alive and dynamic in the present. Visualizing these pieces of myself in paint allows me to sit with them, appreciate them, and ultimately reintegrate them into a new whole.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
The submitted work is part of an ever-evolving installation comprised of ornamental mixed-media works that unearth the silenced, concealed, and erased narratives of the Black diaspora, my personal experiences as a biracial Black woman, and the foundational systems of oppression that continue to influence contemporary culture. This project delves into themes surrounding respectability politics, their interconnectedness with White Supremacy and colonization, and the dual role they play in both advancing and dismantling communities. Inspired by W.E.B. Du Bois' essay 'The Talented Tenth' and rooted in hidden American histories such as The Pearl Incident, the project draws from scholarly insights provided by figures like E. Franklin Frazier, Sabrina Strings, and Margot Jefferson. In this project, I employ the metaphor of a house to represent the social systems and constructs in which we live, often complacently without questioning their foundations. Drawing from the real estate term 'bad bones,' which describes a house's foundation and its ability to withstand the test of time, I extend this term to characterize the societal structures we inherit and perpetuate generationally or those we adopt to contextualize our present lives. Ornamental assemblages throughout the installation create layers of codes and constructs specifically felt within the Black community. These pieces reconfigure each time they are installed, retaining remnants from past iterations, thereby creating a living history within the artwork itself.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Many artists today address and incorporate a variety of scientific disciplines and technologies. Computer, video, and sound-generated work are prevalent in most major contemporary art museums, and biological themes are ubiquitous. Nevertheless, chemistry, whose many processes can be inherently aesthetic and sensually compelling - often eliciting an 'oh wow!' response - is seldom directly considered despite its historic role in art-making. Paints, pastels and other rendering media are chemical formulations that have been traditionally employed by artists. As an artist, I have been one of the many grateful users of paints, dyes, binders, and various mediums that have been developed and perfected by chemists. I would like to acknowledge the vast and historical body of artwork made possible through chemistry. Chemists have created media of every conceivable hue and viscosity and made these materials more light fast, quicker drying, more permanent, and less toxic. However, chemistry's contribution to image making extends beyond paint media. My images are rendered without the use of any paint. Instead, dynamic chemical reactions on sheet metal form the basis of my process. Changing color through reaction, crystallization, fusing, and solidification are a few of the ways chemistry informs this work. The colors develop when part of the light striking the surface reflects and part passes through an oxide film before reflecting off the metal below. When the delayed light reappears and combines with the surface light waves, they may either reinforce or cancel each other, generating a specific hue. The thickness of the oxide film dictates the color. Light's movement on the metal, as well as the viewer's motion, are the kinesthetic forces that alter our perception. It is really cool to watch the color change as the day progresses and the angle of the sunlight shifts. When lit obliquely, the color appears saturated, majestic, and even reverential. Chemistry is sometimes the subject of my work, often its inspiration, and always the method or means of its creation. Chemical reactions are highly charged visual stimuli that have influenced my work for years. My art is not created to explain chemistry, but to harness chemistry to create art. These works are not simulations or illustrations but, rather, art produced by chemical processes. Not art about chemistry, but art by chemistry.
