column25: I create mixed-media collages based on deteriorating photographs and collective memories of my personal and familial history as a refugee in Vietnamese internment camps. Inspired by storytelling, crafting, and myth-making, my mixed-media base is a nod to my immigrant experience. My collage patterning and layering use craft culture as a metaphor for constructing identity. However, my work is imbued with tension in its materiality and the source of its composition. The craft store jewels sparkle as if real, emphasizing the scene's idyllic nature and belying the traumas of exile. I weave my own and my family's experiences as refugees from Vietnam. Working from images found in family albums, digital photographs, and fashion and decoration magazines, I explore an identity formed amongst and from the memories of others. For the last 12 years, I have been making mixed-media art on paper, canvas, and wood panels. My works have always been hand-drawn using stencils and painted afterward. My current work is an opportunity to incorporate craft technology (such as vinyl cutters) to create patterns. This work is the second time I am attempting to do a large-scale paper installation with mixed media. 2008 I started making my mixed media painting using just one hexagon and kept rotating until a new pattern emerged. The practices grew organically from the background of my mixed-media painting and interacted with the figures in the foreground. Fast forward to 2016, I began incorporating some of my printmaking skills by using silk screening to generate my background patterns. The patterns slowly remind me of barbwires used in the refugee camp where my family and I stayed. Like the barbwire that kept us in, the patterns kept us from today's American culture. I am increasingly interested in craft culture (mainly scrapbooking culture) and how they construct identity because I see a similar parallel to my construction. Therefore, I want to use vinyl cutters to design and collage patterns. As I research and read up on the Pattern and Decoration movement (1975-1985) related to my art, I plan to take my works to an installation format. Rather than hand-draw each pattern as I have previously done, a cutter (or pattern printer) will print it for me. I have always been interested in using digital technology in my handmade art. These paintings and installations served as fragmented memories that allowed me to create new narratives as I collaged them using scrapbooking and mixed-media materials. I transform everyday family photo albums into colorful abstractions using a gold gelly roll pen, sequins, acrylic stickers, and acrylic crystals. I layer together fragmented images through mixed-media collages to create a new historical narrative reflecting my identity's tension. I want to create a space so that my family and I can exist, and it will also allow us to grow organically through the embellishment and patterns on and around our bodies.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a composer/ producer of marks and sounds. Through paintings, sound, and projections, I ignite the liminal space of intuition through an interdisciplinary vocabulary of feeling. Sound and color tantalize our subconscious, tickle our embodied knowledge, and stimulate our intuition before recognizability and consciousness interpret our experience. I work in intervals and conversations; in relationships of mark to mark, note to note, tone to texture, breath to brush, and color to sound. My works revel in the moment before thought, when a whisper heightens our senses, blood rushes, and we are swoon with sensation. Weaving through abstraction and representation sonically and visually, my work aims at a direct communication of embodiment, with a nod to legacies of color field painting, figuration, installation, and abstract expressionism. My practice is an ongoing translation of internal liberation through sound to visual forms, and back again, as interdisciplinary as the strategies for cultivating freedom. The interiority of oppression is often a story of incredible resourcefulness, strategy, technique, attention, beauty, community, liberation, and love. Celebrating the vitality of this interior space that exists beyond socially imposed limitations of Black living is at the core of my family history and my practice. Leaning into language commonly used to describe both watercolors and Black living, words such as “difficult” and “uncontrollable”, I position the fluid nature in my work as an expression of full internal liberation. The central use of movement in my practice pushes against the desire to control and instead positions us to appreciate the sensations of liberation- the swell, the pour; the beat, the splash; the hit, the mark; the bass, the wash; the delay, the glaze; the tone, the texture- even if they are just for us, and for just a moment.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Rooted in sociopolitical and historical research, I create work around issues of migration, labor, and technology. I am currently researching and creating work around large-scale infrastructure, specifically, systems of physical and digital transportation. My first project is a graphic novel about fiber optic undersea cables – the network that enables 99% of the world’s internet – and how it connects disparate communities around the world. My second project is a site-specific, time-based community painting. Slated for installation in Fall 2024 at the Evergreen Conservancy in Indiana, Pennsylvania in the United States, this project looks at Pennsylvania’s coal and oil industry and its lasting impact on the working class. In both these projects, I question the role of capitalism in spurring physical and digital migration, the latter being the movement of computer work to regions such as India, where menial tasks (such as correcting facial recognition software) can be done cheaply. In my practice, I imagine a world where capital does not determine the value and relevance of people, which further stresses a struggling lower class. My research into transportation – of humans, intellectual property, and materials – has also sparked greater conscientiousness over the carbon footprint of my artistic practice. As an artist who works in tactile materials, such as oil paint on canvas, I am mindful of the ways in which the production of artist-grade materials and the transportation of artworks to international exhibitions harms our dying planet. In 2023, I stopped purchasing synthetic artist-grade materials; limited my purchase of paper and canvas; and transitioned to making my own paints and painting surfaces out of discarded or natural materials. Re-evaluating where and how I source materials has enabled me to nurture an art practice that supports my local ecology and connect with organizations that are healing our planet and buttressing local communities. This change also expanded my teaching practice. Working primarily in underfunded public schools in New York City and Philadelphia, I am committed to accessibility as a teaching artist. This change in my practice allowed me to model for my students that one does not need artist-grade materials to create art.

Once I had this realization, I couldn’t help but rejoice in how grateful I was that I am an artist, and thus, I am able to appreciate the art of life. There are so many people who go through life not realizing that everything we do is art, and I feel lucky to not only have had this realization, but be equipped with the tools I need to live my life of art to the fullest. The realization hat life is art has encouraged me to romanticize everything I do in my life, taking simple everyday tasks and finding the beauty and grace in them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create Black narratives in a Japanese medium, aiming to enrich manga with diversity and depth. Rather than imitating established styles, I embrace the theory and praxis of the form, maintaining its core while infusing it with my unique voice. My work explores how artmaking in new contexts encourages knowledge production. In the burgeoning community of international creators producing manga and Japanese-styled art, my linguistic and cultural background in Japanese sets me apart. Recognizing my position as an American working in a Japanese cultural form, I value the cultural mixing that underpins contemporary manga: Comics came to Japan via England and modern manga techniques were developed through the study of American cinema. Through my work, I intend to create a unique contribution that respects tradition while aspiring to contribute to the evolving landscape of international manga. Portfolio In making my latest project, Radius, I have worked closely with theoretical physicist Dr. Nicoles Yunes on the scientific accuracy of the story, adding an educational element to the art. In the futuristic world of 'Radius,' a technologically advanced humanity has become reliant on robots for labor and entertainment. At the heart of this narrative is Samir, a boy robot endowed with the rare ability to dream. Set against the backdrop of a society fueled by the discovery of antimatter and a revolutionary process of energy production through particle annihilation, the story explores the consequences of progress and delves into the intricate relationship between humans and machines. Czech playwright Karel Čapek’s 1920 play Rossum’s Universal Robots introduced “robot” into the English language, first coming from the Czech word “Robota” meaning forced work or slavery. Drawing inspiration from my experiences as a professional boxer, Radius underscores the commodification and exploitation of the body within the realms of capitalist sports and media, using humans as a metaphor for the complexities of class, notably embodied by the concept of Whiteness.

My work lies at the intersection of Japan's efforts to globalize via popular culture and the growing relevance of comics in academia as a lens for exploring socio-cultural phenomena. column25: As a multidisciplinary artist, my work delves into the complexities of being a Black woman in contemporary society. Through the use of technology, I strive to create thought-provoking pieces that explore themes such as colorism, cultural pride, and mental health. My artwork is deeply rooted in my Esan & Igbo culture, and it is important to me to represent the narratives and lived experiences of Black women. Throughout my artistic journey, I have been driven by the need to address the historical marginalization of Black women. It is my aim to showcase the resilience and beauty of Black women, while challenging the societal norms and restrictions that have been placed on us. Through my artwork, I seek to empower Black women to define our own standards of beauty and demand the rights and lives we deserve. In recent years, I have experimented with blockchain technology and have begun minting my artwork as non-fungible tokens. I believe this to be a way for myself and my culture to receive proper credit and compensation in the future. My most recent projects have explored the intersection of fashion and graphic design, using vintage and retro hairstyles and makeup as a starting point. In these works, I celebrate Black beauty and also pay homage to women in my family. As I continue my artistic journey, I plan to delve deeper into the expression of emotions such as anxiety, depression, and grief. Through various mediums such as creative coding and installation art, I hope to bring to light the reality that Black women, like everyone else, face issues with mental health. By being vulnerable and exploring these darker themes, I hope to open up conversations around mental health and create a space where Black women can feel seen and heard. I believe that this exploration of darker themes through my art will also be therapeutic for me. It will allow me to process and make sense of my own emotions, while also providing a creative outlet to express myself. I hope that by sharing my journey through my artwork, I can help others who may be experiencing similar challenges.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I struggle with depression. I guess the best thing about being an artist is the way my creative work pulls me out of that psychological state. I can slip into being highly intellectually cerebral and I'm regularly in danger of becoming unproductively attached to daily politics, social injustices, cultural irrationalism, etc. Art making is a break from those trains of thought; making things stimulates a different part of my mind and brain. Not that I don't struggle emotionally and psychologically with art, perpetually questioning whether or not an individual piece, or the work as a whole, is 'good,' or is conveying what I want it to. The question of whether or not people see what I see in it is the usual source of doubt, asking in my head, 'am I kidding myself?'. So, sometimes the artistic process feels like a struggle. But with age I'm learning how to avoid the self-torture part, experiencing the challenges instead as stimulating and energizing. Even when making art feels very difficult, it serves as an antidepressant for me, which is a true blessing. I believe that art really does save lives.

column25: On the most recent occasion of showing my work, several people in various ways associated my work with fossils. To me this reaction was gratifying, even more so when folks said the pieces *reminded* them of fossils, as opposed to saying they *looked like* fossils. Fossils are mysterious products of nature that materially connect us to a time otherwise unavailable to us. Ideally, the objects and images I make won’t appear to have been created by a person but, like fossils, appear to be the products of natural forces, forces with which we may not be presently familiar. Granted, I can't say I’m there yet, but that is the guiding principle. I have, from childhood and throughout my life and work, always held a deep reverence for nature, including a profound respect for the significance of natural time, that is, of the earth’s timeframe, which I can't say that I fully understand or can even conceive. Even so, I find comfort in the mysterious, unpredictable, evolutionary potential that time has always rewarded and may still yet. So, artistically, I have never been attracted to a Warholian Factory mode of production, nor compelled by Hirst-style spectacle. I have always been most comfortable with gradually “growing” an object or an image and, ultimately, I became interested in creating images that appeared to have grown onto – or out of – the canvas, panel, or paper itself. When I started in this “force of nature” creative direction, I initially began with all-over paintings, the surface quality of which are not entirely unlike Jackson Pollock’s Action Paintings (see my artist bio!): marks, lines, and pigments being consistently and evenly distributed, though heavily layered and never as a repeating pattern. Unlike expressionism, my all-over paintings employ a highly-detailed (alt-minimalist?), organic visual vocabulary that is deliberately and mysteriously un-painterly. (I know I have succeeded in creating an element of mystery, if nothing else. For the single thing I most consistently hear from people looking at my all-over paintings is a question: “what am I looking at?”) The drawings in the “Loess” series submitted with this grant application started with photocopies of fragments of these all-over paintings. I use a photocopy transfer process that effectively embeds the printer ink into a thin layer of acrylic medium applied to bristol paper. The photocopy has essentially been glued face down to the bristol with acrylic medium; the photocopy paper is then moistened and peeled off, and all pulpy remnants are rubbed away, exposing only the ink from the print. From the transferred image I bring out discrete objects by scraping away surrounding ink, elaborating and embellishing the emerging objects with drawing and additional mark making. (Using carbon paper transferred with a sharp tool creates crisp, dark, ink-like lines and marks that are consistent with the printlike sensibility of the photocopy transfer). If I can return to the “fossil” association (which I didn’t intentionally seek, but I do appreciate): My work is essentially a response to the climate catastrophe that we as humans have created and are perpetuating, the pieces being speculative meditations on the *nature* of the future. As I wrote in my description of the submitted images, the larger body of work – started 20 years ago – from which the drawings emerge, represents a series I titled “Symbioscene” to describe an imagined epoch following the Anthropocene. When viewers would suggest the association of fossils, I would respond that the pieces are perhaps fossils of the future. Though I hope my work also communicates a sense of teaming post-human life, rather than only a forensic exploration of the all-to-soon-to-be dead, it is inevitable that the profound feelings of solastalgia (climate anxiety) at the root of my work will manifest in forms evoking darkness, loss, and death. I see the “Loess” suite of drawings, in fact, as new life emerging from the fossil-like paintings. As I explain in the submission page of this grant application, 'loess' is an ecological term describing a generative element of rich, living soil. The Loess drawings are meant to signify the sheer tenacity of nature’s will to life. The next step for me with this drawing series (I have scores of them) is to create hand-bound books with the drawings serving as pages. Thus rather than existing as a number of individual images on paper that demand to be framed and under glass, they become a collection of leaves bound together into unique new objects: A Library of Loess or Handbooks for the Symbiocene.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My creative practice stimulates the ground to investigate new forms of “media interventions.” These are deliberate acts of interrupting existing media platforms, narratives, or systems that subvert traditional modes of communication and engage audiences in critical thinking or reflection. By integrating new media technologies with traditional conceptual strategies, my practice synthesizes ideas into messages that are tailored for our contemporary society. Perhaps, that’s why I call myself a new-media artist. However, the term /new/ could be easily replaced by /re/ because every system I create is a /re/mediation of existing media. Consider, for instance, the undulating wave-like sculpture that orchestrates syncopated rhythms of light and sound, or the meticulously choreographed dance performed by thousands of artificial pigeons within a machine-generated imagery of changing seasons. What do these disparate creations have in common? Both leverage existing communication systems to deliver immersive experiences that revolve around their own unique subjects. These subjects permeate libraries, academic discourses, contemporary film and television, and may even hold personal significance to me due to my cultural background and upbringing. In fact, these subjects captivate my attention for weeks, months, or even years, until they fully materialize as digital-physical assemblages known as 'art.' As an artist, I function as a conduit through which the subject manifests into a /new/ and visceral form, in and of this world. What does this manifestation look and feel like? Envisioned primarily through the ingenuity of machines and custom software, these works transcend static boundaries. Time serves as their medium, sometimes necessitating an external stimulus through an interface or an electromechanical device to initiate a change in behavior. The internet becomes their fabric, intertwined with technologies such as artificial intelligence, virtual reality, and potentially physical computing, breathing life into their existence. The fusion of moving images within tangible contraptions often aids in visualization. Consequently, the subject metamorphosizes into a narrative through cutting-edge technology, expressed with a presentation style rooted in classical sculptural arrangement and installation format. Furthermore, my deep interest in the history of media arts and its relatively nascent subterranean discipline has driven me to engage in writing, curation, and teaching within this field. These experiences expose me to new historical works, techniques, subjects, and stories, thereby perpetuating a self-sustaining cycle of influence that shapes my creative practice. In conclusion, my creative endeavors are fueled by a genuine and modest aspiration to invent innovative media interventions that immerse audiences in powerful narratives, challenging our perceptions of the ever-changing world around us. I eagerly anticipate what lies ahead and what manifestations will emerge through my ongoing practice.

column25: Amay Kataria is a new media artist known for his innovative and thought-provoking media interventions. With a background in computer engineering and a Master of Fine Arts from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago, Kataria is dedicated to creating cutting-edge immersive experiences that combine art, design, and technology. He uses custom industrial software to transform existing media infrastructures into interactive installations and performances that captivate audiences. His work draws inspiration from Robert Rauschenberg's 'Open Score,' a performative piece presented during 9 Evenings: Theater & Engineering in 1966, which involved Frank Stella playing tennis with wired rackets that generated a score and controlled the lights around the Armory. Kataria also incorporates elements of movement and sound culture, blending them with advanced technology to create systems that evoke warmth, empathy, and joy for the audience. His work has been exhibited at major festivals and exhibitions worldwide, including Vector Festival, Hyde Park Art Center, Ars Electronica, Electromuseum, and others. He has also been invited to prestigious residencies at institutions such as Art Center Nabi, AIR Sandnes, and Mana Contemporary, where he pushes the boundaries of new media art. As an artist, Kataria continues to contribute to and expand the horizons of new media art. His work exemplifies the power of technology to create meaningful and transformative experiences. He remains committed to exploring the full potential of this exciting and rapidly evolving field, captivating and inspiring audiences with his artistry.

column25: Born in St. Petersburg during the harsh times of the Perestroika and growing up in different languages, cultures and religious believes (Catholicism, Judaism and Russian Orthodox) has sharpen Polina Schneider’s instincts towards the way people interact with each other. In 1997, she migrated to Germany where she faced another cultural perspective. Polina is a true long-term student: She studied Computer Science first and continued with Chinese as a Foreign language later, proving that languages and IT and women do not have to be a contradiction. After graduating with two Bachelor degrees in 2018, she recently came back to the university world and is now enrolled into the Photojournalism and Documentary Photography program in Hannover. In the same year, she joined the socially critical collective “Tuerspion” (English: door viewer). Therefore, in her photography, Polina contrasts and processes leitmotifs of her own search for identity, environmental topics and cultural and political issues. She participated in the International Environmental Photography Lab by the Native Agency and was chosen as an emerging artist by Women Art Prize/X-Treme with an exhibition in Berlin. She promotes the issues on which the association is based, namely: drawing attention to social wrongs, shaping public discourse and ensuring that the perspectives of women are perceived by the public. In October 2022, „L.A. photo curator“ chose Polina’s documentary work about a seal center in the Netherlands as their best series. Since then, Polina participated in various photo exhibitions in Europe and USA: in Berlin, Rome, Hannover, Hamburg, Paris, Pittsburg, Sofia and Klagenfurt. The gallery space „Geste Paris“ and the American art magazine „Dek Unu“ both published Polina’s recent „I, Superhero“ project, which also won the 2023 debut award of Phodar Biennale in Bulgaria, Sofia and was exhibited in several times in Germany and the Netherlands. At Pasadena Photography Arts, Polina presented the entire series in their open show. Starting from February 2024 and continuing for half a year, Polina will have her first solo exhibition at the city hall in Berlin Reinickendorf. Moreover, Polina is part of the self-founded photo collective C21, where six female photographers from all around the globe are devoting themselves towards environmental photography and alternative, inclusive storytelling. Their first, subtle introduction to the public happened at Zingst Festival 2023.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Khaila Batts’ work is influenced by the fluidity of memories, she blends digital collage, acetate, and painted surfaces. She creates surrealistic and chaos scenes through color manipulation. She explores how perception pieces together incomplete and altered images. Color and emotion inform her artistic practice; together, they enable her paintings to examine the relational nature of color. Using tiny organic brushstrokes and cool shades of blue, she recontextualizes violence scenes as calming and comforting as ocean waves. Batts’s large-scale paintings are ambiguous representation of her conflicting feelings, struggles and relationships between her and her surroundings. Batts’ work is created as a reflection of mundane, everyday life. Batts draws inspiration from familiarity; she often incorporates photographs of family and her neighborhood into her work, serving as vessels for connection and a recollection of the past.

column25: Khaila Batts is a artist hailing from New York, NY. Her academic pursuits led her to earn an M.F.A. in Studio Arts from The City College of New York in 2023, building upon a strong foundation laid during her B.A. in Interdisciplinary Studies, Arts, and Humanities in 2020, at the same institution. As a Teaching Assistant during the Spring of 2023, Khaila showcased her commitment to fostering artistic growth while assisting Professor Diana Naccarto in two-dimensional art. Khaila's artistic prowess has found expression in a series of solo and group exhibitions. In 2023, her work gained recognition with a website publication in the White Columns Curated Artist Registry. Notable exhibitions include 'Transplants,' a juried showcase at Amos Eno Gallery in Brooklyn, and 'An Exhibit in the Garden,' a group show at Maggie's Garden on 564 W 149th St. Her contributions were also featured in 'Zine, (In)Visible Guides: Convening' at Abrons Art Center in New York. The year 2023 saw her participation in the group exhibition 'Continuations: A Tribute to Colin Chase' and her MFA Solo Thesis show, 'Aquamarine,' both held at the Compton-Goethals Art Gallery. In 2022, Khaila contributed to the MFA 2nd-year group show titled 'What We Did Last Summer' at City College. In 2022, she received the Connor Merit scholarship in the fall and spring semester. Khaila's artistic journey took an enriching turn with her participation in the Monson Arts Residency from October 16 to November 9, 2023. Currently, Khaila anticipates her upcoming participation in the MASS MoCA Residency scheduled for March 2024, further solidifying her commitment to continuous growth and exploration in the field of fine arts.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Storytelling is always the cornerstone of my work, independent of the medium. The story tells us what the art should look like, sound like, feel like. In creating my recent autobiographical body of work, Tales From The Old Mill, I was compelled to tell my story of returning home to the Bay Area after moving away to New York, and to explore the idea of 'home' as a physical place, a memory, and a desire. The stories told across the project all pertain to the search for home, whether in a place, another person, or blood-related family, and address topics of identity, lineage, substance abuse, heartbreak, and isolation. The interconnectivity of the different mediums serve to illustrate different aspects of the narrative, and in the context of this particular story, to holistically express the psychedelia and surrealism that ensues from bad memories, trauma, nostalgia, and anxiety. The concept of location and a physical place’s identity constantly inspires me, and for Tales I sought to capture the essence and monuments of my quiet woodland hometown through illustrated iconography that utilized a visual style inspired by the children's storybooks I grew up reading. Sonically I chose to pair the naturalness of acoustic instruments with the nocturnal wash of filtered synthesizers and pianos, along with a scattering of field recordings, incorporating the sounds of fog, crickets, birds, and footsteps. Each song is paired with an illustration, in hopes that experiencing the two mediums together elicits a deeper meaning and a greater feeling. The companion film, Winterlude, tied all these elements together by incorporating animated versions of the illustrated iconography, the music of the album, the literal monuments that inspired the project with filming done in both the Bay Area and New York City, and a narrative inspired by the themes of Tales, investigating the trepidation and unease I felt prior to returning home. In working across multiple mediums it's my hope and goal to create pieces that strengthen one another when experienced in tandem, but that also stand on their own as individual works of art, music, and film.

column25: Max Schieble AKA Elbows is a Bay Area-born, San Francisco-based artist creating interdisciplinary narrative works across visual art, music, and film. In October 2021 he released the first part of Tales From The Old Mill, an expansive audio-visual project comprised of 59-minute album, an 18-minute short film, and a series of illustrations. The full project sees Schieble fill the roles of artist, composer, producer, director, illustrator, and designer in an effort to chronicle his return home to the Bay Area through tales of missing parents, debauchery in the upper middle class, and a fateful train trip up north. The album portion of Tales From The Old Mill was released by EveryDejaVu Records, and the accompanying short film, Winterlude, premiered in 2022 at New York’s Snow Town Film Festival, later airing on WPBS. Schieble has appeared live with Grammy Award winner Natalia Lafourcade, Nick Hakim, BENEE, L’Rain, and others, and has performed throughout North America. In 2016 Schieble opened his first solo exhibit, At The Bottom of the Well, at Brooklyn’s IMAGE Gallery. The two-month installation featured large-scale canvases, screen prints, and original drawings, and was accompanied by an original score that played throughout the exhibit. His work has also appeared in Chelsea's Lyons Wier gallery, and sites such as NPR, The FADER, Pigeons & Planes, and Bandcamp. As a commercial visual artist Schieble has worked with Ogilvy, Tribeca Film Festival, Complex Media, PINTRILL, and a number of independent musicians and record labels. He graduated from NYU’s Gallatin School with a degree in cross-media narrative studies, a concentration that focused on the interconnectivity of storytelling between visual arts, writing, and music. Elbows' next project, Wither w/o Petunia, is an album and video series made in collaboration with Brooklyn-born emcee SKRD, due out in 2024.

column25: Based in Queens NY, I am an artist and archivist who emerges from the disciplines of experimental performance and oral history. My work is guided by a curiosity in, and pursuit of, ethical community engagement, as well as, a belief that art has the potential to shape the historical record. I facilitate multi-outcome documentary arts projects – projects that use documentation and participation as a way of researching and producing material for social change, as well as, reimagining and reframing preexisting archival collections for a new purpose. This work takes the form of multi-channel video installation, staged photographs, digital collage, sound and object archives, public listening events, and experimental film. I direct collaborative choreographic work that complicates expected relationship, incorporates multi-generational casts, exposes interior logics, and prioritizes process. Motivated by relationality and sociality, I bring groups together to create temporary communities and make material out of the social dynamics in the room, including harnessing conflict as a generative tool. I augment and reinterpret film, audio and still image material from these rehearsals to compose my work, seeking to walk the line between the found and the fiction, the performative and the documentary. Ten years of work in the field of oral history has driven my interest in the improvisational and performative possibilities of individual memory. Both in the archive and in rehearsal, the unending spark is in the negotiating of the individual and community relationships across shared experience and lines of difference. The questions raised by my work in libraries leads to a desire to activate materials which traditional archival practice often obfuscates thus working to orchestrate a lively conversation between the present and the past.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I have an education in art, music as well as mathematics. I research at the truly cutting-edge vanguard of electronic arts, music and performance. The areas of my activity covering media arts, AI art, immersive art, storytelling and performance with a critical consideration of social problems. My work deals with more general topics such as 'machine ontology' and 'aesthetics of artificial behaviour' in a perspective based on the visual aspect of machines and programming their reactions in the environment. I am a pioneer of art based on Machine Learning. My projects deal with human-machine interaction and multi-agents development process. I am focused on social, economic and political realities as well as the complexity of contemporary society, tangible tension between volatility and stagnation, collapse and transformation experienced by people who live in these unstable conditions. I am particularly interested in communication with 'others', languages that may arise from internet or environmental data or artificial languages arose when AI-s exchange information each-other. I am convinced that generating new languages incomprehensible to people is very important, it broadens the field, exceeds the metaphysical limits embedded in our understanding of time-space and at the same time opens us to 'other'. I deal also with spatial sound and video synthesis. I examine dynamic relation between randomness and order, entropy and concentration of energy.

column25: Robert B. Lisek is an artist, mathematician and composer who focuses on systems, networks and processes (computational, biological, social). He is involved in a number of projects focused on media art, creative storytelling and interactive art. Drawing upon post-conceptual art, software art and meta-media, his work intentionally defies categorization. Lisek is a pioneer of art based on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning. Lisek is also a composer of contemporary music, author of many projects and scores on the intersection of spectral, stochastic, concret music, musica futurista and noise. Lisek is also a scientist who conducts research in the area of foundations of science (mathematics and computer science). His research interests are category theory and high-order algebra in relation to artificial general intelligence. Lisek is a founder of Fundamental Research Lab and ACCESS Art Symposium. He is the author of 300 exhibitions and presentations, among others: SIBYL - ZKM Karlsruhe; SIBYL II - IRCAM Center Pompidou; QUANTUM ENIGMA - Harvestworks Center New York and STEIM Amsterdam; TERROR ENGINES - WORM Center Rotterdam, Secure Insecurity - ISEA Istanbul; DEMONS - Venice Biennale (accompanying events); Manifesto vs. Manifesto - Ujazdowski Castel of Contemporary Art, Warsaw; NEST - ARCO Art Fair, Madrid; Float - Lower Manhattan Cultural Council, NYC; WWAI - Siggraph, Los Angeles.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Throughout my artistic career I have used my practice to express ideas that are at times political, highly personal, or simply beautiful. I am as fascinated by the natural world as I am by the interpersonal struggles that drive our human relationships. While photography is my primary form of expression, I also create works in video, mixed-media, and highly manipulated digital forms. My influences are diverse in range. From photographers Francesca Woodman and Jerry Uelsmann, painters Rene Magritte and Ida Applebroog, to quilt artist Faith Ringgold among others. My most recent work 'Woman Who Once Had Wings' is, as mentioned in my description, about the vulnerability of being a woman. Like many, I have experienced the joy and power of my body, but have also experienced harmful sexual advances, harassment, and abuse. Now in my 50s, my invisible, aging body carries both the scars and desires of my youth. Throughout this series I use self-portraiture to unapologetically own the gaze upon my body. Other motifs such as feathers, open sky, and domestic interiors are used to represent the desire to break free from emotional and psychological bondage. While some images are singular, I have paired others as diptychs. The formal and conceptual connections that happen when combining images allow me to express a more layered reading, suggesting burden, resilience, desire, and freedom. I cannot presume that my physical body, it’s size, shape, skin color, or apparent age is universally relatable. I do intend however, for these concepts to be understood on a larger, archetypal level. Whatever one’s background, there are common experiences amongst all women.

column25: It is beautiful and humbling to speak through my paintbrush. It smears brilliant hues, leaving behind soothing warmth, and it tiptoes through pathways of intricate details with hand beaded pieces. I find joy in the tightness of controlled designs, and in the absolute freedom of big and bold shapes. At one point early on in my career, I grew gray in boredom and fatigue until I realized that I do not have to stay within “the box” or just on the canvas. So, I began experimenting with mixed media, using fabric, synthetic hair, and other materials. I have hundreds of art thoughts flitting and dancing through my mind daily, and I love the ability to be diverse in my work from representational to abstract. I need to keep it moving, keep it changing, starting somewhere different, making it up along the way. This keeps me feeling fresh and always looking forward to the next new thing. Being able to see myself in my art as a Black woman is food to my soul, it is therapy. I especially want youth and individuals in my community to be fed in the same way. Through my art, I am telling them how much I value them and how much I value myself. I am sharing with my son, the black and brown students that I teach, and my sisters and brothers of this community that we are wondrous, and that our faces will be seen, and our voices heard. “Revolutionary” is the word that has made itself a mosaic in my life. Through my art I have learned to give only the parts of me that I choose to share, I have learned that my work is my form of activism. As artists we are always shouting to the world through our canvas, our photography, our dance, our song. Shouting to be heard, and when we finally see our beautiful brown faces illuminated from the tv screen, a billboard, behind a podium, and we hear our voices strong and rich, it is life changing. It is powerful. I deal with colorism and texturism, forms of racism where those with lighter skin, European features, and more loosely curled hair are given preference over those with darker skin and tighter curls. My art is a poem to my own brown skin and curls ever-twirling up to the sky.

column25: My work studies the nature of the Self and the pressure of context. I appropriate mass-produced images from my own formative years, with the objective of reorienting their meaning, while at the same time preserving whatever quality the original image might possess. I impose my own narrative on the one originally designed for another, sometimes opposite, purpose: for example, in Albedo, the girl is more 'threatening' than the sleeping wolf. I pursue a symbiotic effect that is achieved with poetry and imagery. I experiment with ways of combining elements; and by using stenciled letters in a grid, I am able to design the shape of the composition as I work it out on graph paper. The letters are hand cut and collaged, arranged with no leading between the lines, producing a text that looks at once ancient and modern. My work then seeks to relate my own experience of discovery-- my poetry is autobiographical-- and place it in a context where light is thrown on our preconceptions allowing for reconsideration of who we might be.

column25: My current work is an evolving idea based on concepts and techniques put forth by the impressionists of the early 1900s. The main focus of my subject is related to emotion and feeling accompanied by color placement. My work deals with identity, portraiture, figures, and psychology. No color used is ever 'straight out of the tube'. Adding Raw pigments and pouring mediums, basic acrylic paint becomes something so much more. Each painting has a balance between vulnerability and emotion, juxtaposed with the inviting warmth of these colors. Color ties our emotions to reality, we see a color and have a visceral reaction 'I love it' or 'I hate it'. Colors can make us feel sad or happy. They can remind us of moments we've had. Without color, art means nothing. It is the most critical choice in every painting, your living room, and the clothes you wear. Color is important. My work involves the use of color at the forefront of every piece. Skin tones, light reflection, and large color blocks for the background give the viewer a chance to be engulfed in the emotions of the subject and to immerse themselves in its feelings. Only a few colors are selected to be represented in each work so every color and emotion can be appreciated in its own space. Each piece expands on the subject's identity, psychology, and feelings within the confines of the work

column25: Yan Wei was born in 1981 in the northeast of China. Later, with the reversal of the policy affecting educated youth returning to their hometowns, Yan Wei resettled in Beijing and enrolled at the Academy of Art and Design at Tsinghua University, earning a bachelor's degree. After graduation, Yan Wei worked as an illustrator at an advertising company, creating posters and TV commercial storyboard. Eventually, She left the advertising industry and transitioned into the art field, sustaining economic stability through illustration. Collaborating with renowned brands such as Levi's, Adidas, Fiat, Ford, Nivea, Tmall, and others, Gradually she gained attention from galleries, participating in solo and group exhibitions. By the end of 2022, after three years of experiencing China's 'zero-COVID' policy, she and their family moved to the United States. Her life experiences in China have been marked by transformation, from poverty to the era of reform and opening up, changes in the political landscape, to the recent rise of conservatism and nationalism. These experiences have seamlessly integrated into her personal journey, becoming potential influences shaping their evolving perspectives.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My image choices talk about isolation, the anonymity of the crowd, ambiguity, the space between action and reaction, and the ignored places between the deliberate and the staged. Derived from the motion pictures of the 1930s through the 1950s, I'm particularly interested in this era of film and how it aligns with the concept of artifice via opulent and escapist themes. As subjects of my paintings are about the fleeting, the missing, and the unattainable, as paintings they become the very antithesis of the ephemeral. This contradictory concept is also contained in the way we may connect to these early images through the platforms of modern technology. This new access to 'old' materials has created a contemporary anachronism where anything recorded may be accessed. These images and sounds are part of our contemporary consciousness along with all of the other things that make up our world. As a contemporary artist, I am responding to the 'new' subject of these old movies. In painting these images, I have internalized every detail of the source image. Every brushstroke is a result of my looking at each part of the image with equal attention and focus. My paintings are a product of my absorption of the images that I choose and of my processing those images through the externalization of the action of painting.

column25: Born in Honolulu, Hawaii, Kristen Thiele lives and works in Miami, FL. Thiele received her BFA from the School of the Art Institute of Chicago in 1995 and her MFA from Miami International University in 2012. During her years in school and after, she sang and played bass in the band Motorhome. They released 2 CDs and 3 singles on New York labels Zero Hour and Dirt Records and played with bands like Everclear, Alanis Morrisette, Missing Persons, Th' Faith Healers, etc. After graduating from SAIC, Thiele apprenticed with Chicago screen printer Steve Walters of Screwball Press and learned the art of silkscreen printing, primarily printing posters for local and national bands. Upon her return to Miami, she became a member of the Art Center South Florida, (now Oolite Arts) on Lincoln Road in South Beach, where she held a studio until her move in 2010 to Bridge Red Studios in North Miami. Thiele currently co-runs Bridge Red Studios/Project Space, alongside her father Robert Thiele, exhibiting longstanding South Florida artists in an alternative environment. Thiele's posters were published in the definitive rock poster book, 'The Art of Modern Rock' and her paintings in, 'Miami Contemporary Artists' and '100+ Degrees in the Shade.'

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The Pearly Gates Archive is a body of work through which I’ve channeled a decade of existential discombobulation. Its images are digitally assembled, by hand, merging irony, nostalgia, and pieces and parts of scanned twentieth century photographs into the idiosyncratic language of analog vernacular photography. Gabriel García Márquez once optimistically wrote that “Life is the best thing ever invented.” The Pearly Gates Archive, circling this claim like water around an open drain, is a counterfactual examination of this idea. Instead of facts, its analysis offers up evidence of absurdities having been committed, one after another, serial observations of glitches in the matrix, so brief that we can’t quite put our finger on exactly what they mean — or if they’re actually real. Perhaps, if nothing else in the end, only revealing a fragile relationship between fiction and authenticity, between truth and belief, and between competing instincts to both value “the best thing ever invented” while, at the same time, doing everything we can to squander it. Everyone has their own opinion on such things, of course. In the past, apologists have rationalized our penchant for hazarding into the existential weeds by focusing on our successes at having eventually found our way out of them (albeit having suffered periodic reductions in force along the way). Next up, loom the pearly gates of the Anthropocene, on the other side of which, so we, none the wiser yet riding hell bent for leather, have been told: the streets there will be paved with gold.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Inherently, we are interested in observing others as a way of understanding ourselves. My work is inspired by this curiosity and allows the viewer to participate. These works are individualistic narratives which explore personage through self-presentation, facial expressions, and gesture. They often inspect the under-revered, and appreciate the subject’s presence and dignity, giving pause to honor the person. The aesthetic is assertive and the color is divorced from naturalism. Skin color is therefore eliminated which for me this is metaphorical for the hope that society aspires to, and achieves equality among races and other kinds of human differences. The work is meant to honor the entirety of humanity—not just one section of the population or kind of person. With these works, I've been experimenting with texture and the physical quality of the surface through using alternative recycled materials such as pieced-together bubble mailers and paper bags. I've found them to be exciting and sturdy surfaces and believe these media add a uniquely interesting and environmentally friendly component to the work. Using and combining color and pattern is euphoric to me and has always come very naturally and instinctively. I have a strong visceral, and nearly synesthesia-like reaction to it in that I can almost taste it and hear it. It’s as if one sense gets overloaded and spills into another. Because my work at its core is about humankind’s understanding of itself, studying human nature, and its relationship to storytelling, I tend to envelop my portraits with color and pattern because the work venerates the individual with the ultimate sentiment that we try to live in a more socially and environmentally just world, encouraging people to appreciate and love one another, and to in turn understand the importance of social justice and respect for our earth and all its creatures.

column25: Heidi Brueckner is a Professor of Art at West Valley College in Saratoga, CA where she has taught painting, drawing, and design for over 23 years. A native Californian, Brueckner studied at the University of Heidelberg and The Goethe Institute in Germany in the late 1980s. During this pivotal year, she visited the major museums of Europe and found herself heavily influenced artistically by 20th century German art. Brueckner received a BA in Fine Art and a BA in Art History from University of California, Santa Cruz in 1991. She received an MFA in Painting from University of Kansas in 1997. Professor Brueckner’s work has been shown at museums, galleries, colleges, and in publications nationally and internationally. She has received many awards and scholarships for her work. In 2018, she published the book “Monsterbet”, based on a series of 26 satirical oil, acrylic, and mixed media paintings spoofing the format of a children’s alphabet book. In recent years, Brueckner has had 16 solo exhibitions, participated in over 100 juried group shows, and won 13 first place awards, which included the Italian International Prisma Art Prize and the Faber Birren Color Award. She was also a finalist for the 2022 7th Edition Boynes Emerging Artist Award. Her work has been written about in publications and online art sources such as Art Seen Magazine, All She Makes Magazine, Visionary Art Collective, Create! Magazine, The Huts Magazine, ILikeYourWorkPodcast.com, GearBoxGallery.com, 48 Hills News, NotRealArt.com, Suboart Magazine, The Pacific Sun, and The Vassar Review. Recent 2022-23 solo shows include Buckham Gallery in Flint, MI; Abington Art Center in Jenkintown, PA; Gearbox Gallery in Oakland, CA; East Central College Art Gallery in Union, MO; O’Hanlon Center for the Arts, Mill Valley, CA (4 person); Women United Art Movement, Online; Crossing Arts Alliance in Brainerd, MN; Kirkland Art Center in Clinton, NY; and Delaplaine Arts Center in Frederick, MD. Upcoming solo exhibitions in 2023‑25 include; Crary Art Gallery in Warren, PA; Grants Pass Museum of Art in Grants Pass, OR; Dalton Gallery in Rock Hill, SC; and Thelma Sadoff Center for the Arts in Fond du Lac, WI. In August 2023, she attended the Wildacres Artists Residency in Little Switzerland, North Carolina. She currently lives and works in Oakland, California.

column25: As a collage and mixed media artist, my work is centered around the exploration of fantasy narratives, surreal worlds, the esoteric, and the practice of play. My pieces often feature elements of collage, where found imagery and textures are fused together to create a new world of possibility. My inspiration comes from a wide variety of sources, including mythology, fairy tales, and personal experiences. I enjoy playing with the symbolism and archetypes present in these stories, as well as the emotional resonance they hold for so many people. Through my art, I seek to explore these themes in a way that is both personal and universal. At the heart of my practice is the idea of play. I believe that when we engage with our imagination with a playful and experimental spirit, we are able to access new realms of creativity and insight. Ultimately, my goal as an artist is to create works that are both visually striking and thought-provoking. I hope to engage the viewer in a dialogue about the nature of storytelling, the power of the imagination, and the ways in which we construct our own realities.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My artwork celebrates social, spiritual and cultural differences. Often referencing the profound importance of self-discovery within these constructs, my work offers moments of contemplation. It explores the varying relationships between these physical and metaphysical worlds in hopes the viewer might expand their understanding of self in this liminal space. Concentrating on abstract form, the use of shadow projection represents this identity in a metaphysical sense while movement, objects and color represent this identity in a physical sense. Travel is an essential part of this process. I utilize this time to not only experience nature and its inspirational offerings but to meet people along the way. When I find someone who I connect with, I record and conduct an interview posing a series of life based questions before painting their portrait. This gives me a unique perspective of what it means to be human. The constant embrace of surrounding color and texture while experiencing the stillness within ‘self’, prompt me towards those extended moments of contemplation resulting in a new series of works. By using repeated layers of hand-made mulberry paper and paste layered with acrylic, my process becomes sculptural-like giving the work a three dimensional quality. My studio process begins with soaking the mulberry bark until it becomes fibrous. It is then ground down and mixed with adhesives, powders and compounds to create a buildable texture. Some of the fibers are also then pressed to create paper. They are later painted on and deconstructed for a second time to contribute to the layering process. This is a representation of the cycles we move through as individuals understanding our character and the idea of self. Paintings can carry anywhere between 20-100 layers of paper and paste and take about 1-3 months to complete.

column25: I’m interested in creating contexts in which personal narratives intersect with greater sociopolitical issues around Queerness, Disability and their sensibilities. This means exploring the ways in which I feel like objects, materials, spaces and gestures can acquire attitude. The attitude in my work emerges from a critical perspective about the culture in which we live which is wasteful, reckless about its future and constantly prioritizing economics over the well-being of people. The way certain materials refer back to bodies compels the me most in my creative practice. Bodies are the sites upon which politics play out and are felt most immediately. It’s through processes of crafting, collecting, decorating or living with/in materials or objects that my work emerges in the space between sculpture, performance and installation.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I spent more than thirty years as a practicing attorney, but after my children grew up and finished college I was compelled to go back to my first love as an artist, Growing up my family assumed that I would pursue a career as an artist and my parents encouraged mem to do just that. Although for much of my youth, I believed a career as an artist was in my future, I lost my confidence when I spent a summer at The Chautauqua Institute as a fellow to study art there. What I saw was so many gifted and wonderful artists that I thought there was no way my work could measure up to what I saw all around me. Returning to the arts, I now have the luxury to create and to do the things I love. That is the reason I returned to school at the age of seventy-one years old to earn a Master in Fine Arts from the Maryland Institute College of Art. I went to art school not to learn how to make art (I think that is innate and inherent in every creative being), but to learn how to be an artist. For me, lawyering was rigid and often lacked the ability to express myself as an individual. In other words, the law is the law and offers little room for self expression, especially when trying to take a novel approach in representing my clients. So for me, I had scrape away that legal veneer and to recontextulize my world view and to understand that as an artist there is no right or wrong way to go about especially in the mediums I choose to work with. As a multi-media artist, I often work with found and unconventional materials to make my art. Although I think I'm fairly skilled as a painter, there are rules one has to follow, but working with the materials I often use I realize there are no rules and the potential for expression is without limitations. My goal or mission is to create a body of work that will be my legacy for my children and grandchildren and one that will endure long after I leave this earth and the physical realm of my existence. To that end, I have begun working on a project that reflects the rich history of my family, tracing back to when my first ancestors were kidnapped and brought here from the Gold Coast of west Africa. Unlike, many African Americans, who have no knowledge or historical reference to their ancestral links to slavery, my family has well documented the past and I intend to reflect that in my work. In some ways, my story will be provocative and personal but I also believe it will have global appeal was many people today struggle with a sense of self and where we come from. With this objective in mind I am turning towards expressing my work in new media and I have several works in progress that engage elements of painting, textiles and quilt making. I am so excited about the potential of this work and I look forward to sharing it someday soon.

column25: Walking through my community inspires me to imagine multidimensional landscapes created from everyday objects. These unconventional artifacts reflect the world we live in, consume, and fill with things. Printed material and imagery document our existence. Discarded objects reveal what (and who) our society values, and what gets thrown away. Through drawing, sculpture, and installation assembled around these materials, I discover their stories, their beauty, and endless adaptation. They create worlds that seem familiar and surreal. My work imagines the places and spaces where the natural environment, the spiritual world, and manmade materials collide. I am drawn to the patterns of growth and renewal I find in nature, through drawing and collage, images and textures, and the stuff of our day-to-day life. Sometimes a drawing is layered upon a collage of images, paper, and cork; other times, I assemble or forge new landscapes from melted plastics and synthetics generated by our past and present. Stretching the boundaries of the form and materials until something – or someplace – new emerges. Afrofuturism inspires who and what you see as the installations are portals to an immersive experience of Black life past, present, and future. I conjure and celebrate infinite Black existence, taking up space in reimagined and reconstructed territories. We must see ourselves in boundless multidimensional landscapes. It is part of who we are. It is liberation. Pattern, repetition, and constant movement form the foundation of my work. By twisting, tearing, melting, and re-shaping, my hands are tools to reinvent the natural and human-made elements. As we increasingly lean toward artificial materials and intelligence, my practice honors something slow, meditative, and intentional. The layered and textured landscapes invite you to look closely, to slow down and discover the layers of materials, drawing and imagery. My intentions are to unite real human labor and real materials to motivate human connections.

column25: I create paintings and relief prints. I am inspired by design and composition, and on a deeper level how collage and composition can reflect the world we live in. Bold, graphic color and collage are two things I am constantly thinking about and employing in my work. Constructing an image through color, texture, and image provides a way for me to remix clues from the real world into a flat, designed space. I see my works as catalogues of images, feelings, experiences, and vibes that may be abstract or surreal but reference actual parts of life. I use ambiguity as a tool to invoke emotion and uncertainty when a viewer engages with my artwork. The color, texture, and pattern in my work references surfaces in the urban and social spaces we inhabit. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my recent paintings, I craft intricate compositions that weave personal narratives, reflecting my experience as a Queer individual coming of age in the 1980s amidst pervasive homophobia and the silencing impact of AIDS. Lacking other role models, I was attracted to the anarchy encoded in flamboyant, gender-defying characters and supervillains from my Saturday morning cartoons. These figures are a recurring element in the language of my artwork. I also draw inspiration from images and materials of the Queer Underground in the period preceding the 1969 Stonewall riots. During that era, personal effects, photographs, journals, newsletters, and magazines were systematically discarded, leaving behind a fragmented and contested history. Through my work, I seek to forge a connection with and illuminate the lives of my queer ancestors, emphasizing the nuances of their daily experiences lived in constant fear of persecution and prosecution. My creative process involves obsessive and intricate brushwork. For my paintings, I primarily use indelible ink on paper. Ink, like memory, leaves a lasting impression, while the use of paper directly connects my work to the materials unearthed through my research. A pivotal exhibition profoundly shaping my artistic approach was the Prinzhorn Collection at the Drawing Center in New York City in 2000. This exhibit showcased artworks crafted by individuals with mental illness at the turn of the 19th century. The collection presented emotionally charged and intricately composed pieces, woven with text, complex patterns, and surreal imagery. It served as a poignant reminder that, even in the most challenging circumstances, art remains a deeply personal form of self-expression. This sentiment has become a guiding mantra in my daily artistic practice, where I construct a visual reality allowing my past, present, and Queer history to connect and converse.

column25: BOBBY ABATE (Brooklyn, NY) is a Queer artist, filmmaker and editor. Bobby’s work has been featured in exhibitions and screenings at prestigious venues such as the Museum of Modern Art in New York, the New York Film Festival, the Guggenheim in Bilbao, the Chicago Museum of Contemporary Art, the San Francisco Cinematheque, and the ICA in London, among others. Bobby is currently an artist-in-residence at the Lower Manhattan Cultural Council’s Art Space on Governor’s Island in New York City. In 2023, Bobby was awarded the DVAA artist fellowship. Bobby holds an MFA degree from Bard College. As an editor, Bobby worked on season 3 of the Queer docuseries We're Here on HBO, which recently won Peabody and Television Academy awards, in addition to creating animations for the renowned drag performer Sasha Velour. Bobby is currently working on a series of works that delve into Queer underground culture before the seminal Stonewall riots of 1969. In 2021, Bobby self-published The Outsider Tarot featuring 80 original artworks that reimagine the traditional Tarot in a modern, queer context. This deck and 200-page guidebook were presented at the Whitney Museum, Participant INC., and Miami MoCA, sold at Artbook in PS1 MoMA Queens, and are part of the permanent collection at the Fine Arts Library of Harvard University.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice uses weaving, embroidery, and textile sculpture to explore femme sexuality, queer disidentifications, non-linear narratives, and coming-of-age stories in an attempt to rewrite my closeted past. Using memoirs, the ACT-UP movement, and Munoz’s writing on worldbuilding as the site of my research, this work explores how queer women are portrayed and often consumed within mainstream culture. This work complicates the traditions of weaving, queers abstraction with a femme touch, and considers the role of non-linear narratives and what it means for marginalized communities. Materials that are bright, glittery, contrasting, and of a low-fi craft vibe are often found in the work. Haphazard ways of making belie the actual labor that goes into making these objects because they are urgently made and that urgency needs to feel palpable. My work involves three areas of interest: my embroidered banner project, “Dykes on the Dancefloor,” my weavings that focus on structural investigations that speak to emotional and physical wear and tear, and my recent project, “3 Dykes Walk into a Bar…” that involves tapestry weavings and enlarged, sculptural textiles. My embroidered, sequined work is focused on queering abstraction and denying the viewer an easily consumable image of queer women on the dancefloor experiencing transcendence (or heartbreak or boredom). My weavings borrow queer-coded song lyrics to hint to a knowing viewer that if you know, you know. These works are interested in how I can make weavings do things they shouldn’t. How can I structure a hole or a tear and still have a cloth that survives? Woven cloth is stronger than we realize. The tapestries and enlarged sculptural textiles are building a non-linear narrative where I get to act out a queer youth I didn’t have because I was closeted until my early 30s. This work is irreverent, immature, and full of TMI moments. Text is taken from my diaries, or song lyrics are juxtaposed against images that are lifted from movies with a queer lead actress or a film about harrowing addiction. In the way that coming of age feels cinematic and mundane simultaneously, these works are melodramatic and relatable. While the pieces often feel frenetic and slapdash, they are made slowly and thoughtfully. The energetic throughline expresses the intensity of the feeling and emotion behind this work. Weaving, sewing, embroidery, and applique ensure that my hand is required in the making and how the finished piece looks. The materials range from cheap and readily accessible to mid-range so that it reflects my socioeconomic background and means. Textiles are a site of regret and redemption for me. The physical, time-intensive processes are where I examine my regrets, and the works that emerge are an act of reimagining and redemption.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artistic practice, I employ an automatic, experimental and intuitive process that allows me to delve into the depths of my creativity. Each brushstroke, collage element, or mixed media technique becomes a conduit for self-expression. My artwork serves as a reflection of the intricate interplay between my internal emotional landscapes and the world around me. Color plays an essential role in my art and I don't shy away from chromatic color. I often like to push the boundary of what may be considered appropriate and lead the viewer out of their comfort zones. I find pleasure in the dynamic interaction between contrasting elements, and the infusion of color amplifies the emotional depth and expressive path that the artwork naturally follows. Besides using acrylic paint as the predominant medium, I am also called to include other mixed media elements. I incorporate collage using old book pages and painted papers, as well as, crayon, pastels, and graphite. Another technique that I use often involves scraping into and through the surface of the work in order to achieve a drawing like quality and reveal what may have been hidden below the layers. I am interested in exploring the kinetic, energetic and visceral experience and reflecting that back to the viewer. There is an interplay between the spiritual and internal pursuits of myself as the artist and a desire to open to the primal power that moves through us all. Whether it be juxtaposing colors, the way I rip through a layer of paper to reveal a hidden element, or the way I decide to erratically draw over the surface, the work challenges me to move inward and outward in all directions, to use my body and trust myself to articulate what wants to move through me. By daring to leave it all on the surface my own vulnerability allows the viewer to be vulnerable themselves and delve into a deep introspection. Overall, through my paintings I strive to create immersive experiences that invite the viewer to explore the depths of their own emotions and perceptions. My work becomes a mirror that reflects the complexity of human experience, provoking a renewed sense of connection. It is my intention to create a safe and transformative space where solace can be found, allowing the viewer to confront their fears and find resonance in the depths of their own being.

column25: Natalie Dadamio is a fervent mixed media abstract expressionist, whose artistic journey has been nothing of the ordinary. With a Bachelors degree, in experimental film from Binghamton University as well as a heightened love for all things odd, eccentric and experimental, Natalie now draws on her past history and experiences and expertise into creating colorful, raw and expressive mixed media artworks. Her artistic exploration thrives on intuition and automatic painting, by navigating the realms of abstract expressionism with a fearless and daring devotion to unveiling the unseen and embracing the unconventional. Her work is an enchanting amalgamation of vibrant colors, intricate textures, and ethereal forms, inviting viewers into a world where the esoteric meets the everyday. She is the recipient of two noteworthy residencies, including one at AIR Studios in Santa Fe, New Mexico, operated by the Jen Tough Gallery, in July 2023. Additionally, Natalie has been honored with a month-long artist residency from the Broome County Arts Council scheduled for July 2024. An active member of the Broome County Arts Council and a member of the Cooperative Gallery 213, Natalie channels her passion for art not only into her personal creations but also into fostering a vibrant and inclusive artistic community. Her participation in these organizations reflects her belief in the power of collaboration, sharing knowledge, and uplifting fellow artists. Additionally, Natalie extends her passion for art beyond her personal practice. She's embraced teaching art and coaching as avenues to guide individuals through their personal struggles. With a deep belief in the therapeutic power of artistic expression, Natalie helps others navigate their emotions and challenges through creative exploration. As an art instructor, Natalie brings her expertise and empathetic approach to empower aspiring artists, encouraging them to discover their unique creative voices. Her coaching endeavors focus not only on technical skills but also on fostering confidence and resilience through art, aiding individuals in overcoming personal obstacles. Through her teaching and coaching efforts, Natalie seeks to create a nurturing space where individuals can harness the healing potential of art, using creativity as a tool for self-discovery and personal growth.

Danquis Johnson Danquis’ work consists of a fusion of painting, film, nature and sound. Danquis is most fascinated with the exploration of experimentation through both physical and digital media. What will noise sound like with various tools? What will the paint allow me to create and how will it flow? How am I viewed metaphysically outside of myself during my anecdotal happening? Through the exploration of these unpredictable equations he has to envision the metaphysics turning into the physical as he is separate from reality. This is where film and direction ties my work. Original studio practices such as drawing and painting feel like new forms of art while recording. The art itself is in control. My body sways the paint, fixes the structure of sound and breaks it. Isocrhonous is the metaphysical and the physical. Currently Danquis is exploring himself as the subject through acting performance via film. His goal is to marry the worlds of acting aligned with aesthetic paint through experimental film.

column25: I began my career in sculpture with an interest in architectural design. I soon moved beyond that into minimalism, and a focus in functional work. I became hyper-focused on creating artwork that broke out of the shell of the pedestal and into the realm of usefulness. I combined my knowledge of engineering and woodwork with my creativity to create objects that invoked emotional responses. From there, I grew an interest in moving back into my musical roots that I established before I became an artist. Again, combining my engineering and woodworking backgrounds with my musical background to push forward minimalist abstractions of normal instruments. Then I began doing performances and interactive pieces with the instruments I made, these projects were meant to create spaces that evoke specific emotions and experiences.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Criminals are the one social group in America we have permission to hate. They are entitled to disrespect and little moral concern. When criminals are viewed as the social cancer infecting our communal health, safety, and thriving, we cease to see and affirm their humanity. Often, those who have been incarcerated, those who have had family members who have been incarcerated, and those who have survived crime have experiences that overlap significantly. Few see the complex gray area which exists in between the black and white. Faces of Mass Incarceration seeks to shed light on how the criminal justice system impacts individuals in a plethora of ways. Each portrait, painted from life, depicts a person who has been formerly incarcerated, system impacted or impacted by the system in some way. Though each story was different, all included trauma, neglect, death, abuse or loneliness. As I painted and conversed with each person, I found friendship, kindness and a heart of empathy. These portraits were painted to honor the dignity in each individual.

column25: Since a very early age Dale was always dedicated to the pursuit of art. Whether it be simple crayons on scrap paper when he was still a kid or his studies of pastels, charcoal, watercolors and eventually oils in his later teenage years, his passion for the arts has never waned. Always in pursuit of bettering his skills he has taking on many different mediums over the years and is always challenging himself to master various techniques in each of them. In more recent years he began teaching himself digital art and has even dabbled in 3d art. Always exploring new methods of expressing his artistic nature, he felt this new medium would open up new opportunities that weren't possible through traditional means. That doesn't mean, however, that he has stopped his pursuits in traditional art. It's just giving him new tools and knowledge to enhance what he's already gained through years of practice with traditional mediums. So although his methods of creating art has changed many times over the years his goal still remains the same. To help spread a little joy and beauty through his work and in the process make the world a little bit better of a place than when he started.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My current work focuses on anthropomorphized animals in our culture, particularly rabbits. Our treatment of rabbits is contradictory; attitudes range from adoration of their cuteness to contempt. Rabbits are animals that everyone has encountered, starting with Peter Rabbit and Bugs Bunny. They evolve from childhood toys to pampered pets, garden pests, science experiments, dinner and clothing. Because rabbits are viewed in such contradictory ways, they became a blank canvas for me to work through. I play with peoples’ expectations and emotions; peel away the preconceptions and expose the unease of our relationship with rabbits and how we interact with them. The animals in my sculpture are an unnervingly accurate but uncanny version of the natural animal, creating an initial shock followed by two different beings trying to understand and connect. The animals are their own entities, curious about the viewer but not overtly welcoming. Fragile and vulnerable, the sculptures become inverted anatomies showing their interior organ structures on the body’s surface. In revealing their vulnerable interiors, the sculptures create a tension between the reality of the animal and our cultural concepts; the wild and the tame, the beautiful and grotesque. Many people assume my sculptures are created from taxidermy. They are not. I make everything by hand, starting with painted sketches and sculpted maquettes. I embroider samples to figure out the linen and fur colors, floss colors and stitch directions. I then sculpt the body, make the fabric pattern and sew the linen and fur together. I hand embroider the organ systems onto the fabric. The head and paws are then sculpted in polymer clay, baked and the fur carefully glued on. All the parts assembled and permanently attached. The work is very labor intensive. The dedication, reflection, and time imbues the animals in my work with a presence that is missing when I try to speed up or automate the process. I will mull an idea over for several months, making sketches, models and testing various construction methods before spending a year creating my work. The magic seems to occur in the minute decisions that come with each tiny stitch or touch to the clay. The finished object is important to me; like the stuffed toys that are the first objects we treasure, the sculptures become beings completely contained within themselves.
