column25: My work focuses on ideas of healing and growing from trauma. Creating art has been a way for me to cope with my trauma, as well as a way for me to handle the tough situations that I have faced and heal from them. During my journey of healing, I have felt like I was too focused on the bad things that have happened to me instead of the good things that were actively happening in my life; so I decided to create a body of work that not only focused on these bad things, but also celebrate the difficult situations I have had to overcome in the past few years, as well as what I have accomplished. These pieces are all from my recent solo exhibition, “It’s My Trauma and I’ll Cry if I Want To.” The centerpiece of the gallery in my exhibition was the party scene installation of the same title, which is a celebration of myself and my accomplishments after going through some of the worst experiences of my life. I did not want this installation to be a “pity party”, so my banner proudly states “I SURVIVED.” Every great party needs a cake, so I created a fake cake for my table, which plays into the phrase, “you can't have your cake and eat it too.' I was inspired by Judy Chicago’s “The Dinner Party,” and her use of different mediums, like needlework, which inspired me to learn how to sew to create different parts of my installation. Judy Chicago’s installation was dedicated to 1,038 women from history, so I decided to dedicate my piece not only to myself, but more importantly, to others who are survivors of sexual assault and abuse. During my healing process, I have had to revisit trauma from my childhood as well, which inspired my ceramic set 'Childhood Relics,' which is a recreation of my childhood dollhouse and toys that I used as a form of escapism as a child. There is something that is just so therapeutic about sculpting, especially with clay. Creating something out of a blob of clay I think tells a story on its own. When I start a ceramic sculpture, I have to decide how big I want to make it. I really enjoy making things life size, so I try to figure out what the scale is and prepare my clay. When working on a larger scale piece, it requires more time, precision, and resources. My sculptures are usually very heavy due to their size, which is risky, because, in my experience, it is more likely to have problems when it comes to drying and firing. Working with clay has been a challenge because it is not so forgiving. One wrong move and you can completely destroy a piece, and the kiln can break a piece at any stage of the process. It has been a true test of my patience and ability to let go. In printmaking, I prefer to make multi-color linoleum cut prints. These come with their own set of challenges. My process is that I usually cut a piece of linoleum to the size of the print I want to create, and then I decide what I am going to put on it. This process can be pretty intimidating, but it's a fun challenge for myself as I come up with ideas and a game plan. Before I start a print, I have to map out how I am going to cut each layer and what color goes where. I also have to experiment with my ink colors to see how they will pair with each other and layer on top of one another. When I carve each layer, I feel like I am working in reverse because of how I have to build the colors and subject matter up. The way I am simultaneously taking away from the linoleum, but adding onto the print itself is exhilarating. The way I have to carve the linoleum sheet, roll ink on it for each print, and then roll it through the press makes me physically engaged with my art. It feels so intentional and requires precision on my part. When I have a successful print series, it feels so rewarding because of all the planning, carving, prepping ink, inking, rolling, and clean up that goes into it. It’s so exciting to see how it changes each time it goes through the press. By my last color, I will usually only have a little bit of linoleum on the sheet, but to my print, it is usually the most crucial part, being the little details that make it just pop. The carve marks left on the piece from removing previous layers don’t bother me as much as they did when I first started printing, as I think they tell their own story and add character to the piece. My art is such an important part of my life. It is how I get to tell my story and connect with others. Creating art gave me a voice when I felt like I didn’t have one. It gave me the power back in my life that I felt like I had taken from me for so long. I have been on a journey of self-discovery throughout the making of these particular pieces. Figuring out who I am and not letting myself be defined by my trauma has been very difficult, and I want my audience to go on that journey with me as they look at my art. I hope that my art can help other people realize that there is hope for the future and that things do get better even when you feel like you have lost all hope.

column25: as an artist and healer, i am guided by the voices of my ancestors and the transformative power of plant medicines. my name is b dukes, and i am a multi-hyphenate trans, non-binary artist who hails from the backwoods of the deep south. i am big ma's baby, carrying the wisdom and teachings of my foremothers in every aspect of my work. Over the past 15+ years, I have honed my craft and deepened my intentions while grounded in the roots of my practice. I approach my writing, sonic creations, DJ mixes, visual art, filmmaking, and ceremonies with the healing and liberation of my Black and Brown queer kin in mind. My art is not just a means of expression, but a tool for transformation and healing. I believe in the sacred art of playing with nature, inquiry, pleasure, and rest. I am currently exploring the creation of sacred spaces, interactive art, films, and visual art that liberates, heals, and grounds. My work is inspired by the earth, and I seek to create art that is in harmony with the natural world. Additionally my current research is deeply inspired by the works and wisdom of Dr. Monnica Williams, bell hooks, Luisah Teish, Akwaeke Emezi, Audre Lorde and Carlos Castaneda. I am currently a psychedelic therapy student of AWE based in South America, seeking to expand my knowledge and incorporate new modalities into my healing offerings. Most recently, my sonic explorations are guiding me to produce experimental and ritual infused improvisational sounds baths. Interpreting the modality by dancing with archaic, electronic and indigenous instruments and languages. Chants ricochet off the walls while synths, drums, shakers and scents of the Gods fill the space — these are temples for rest, restoration and deepened connection. At the intersection of this point of inquiry and plant medicine, my first performance piece “To Be A Witness”, is currently being born. To Be A Witness (2023) is a multi-year artistic and spiritual commitment - a multidimensional ritual - a performance piece rooted in the exploration of witnessing and being witnessed at the core of collective transformative healing, existing and traveling within the planet medicine sphere as a Black, trans, non-binary spirit. Incorporating the years of shadow work, individual research, exploration and healing support via fungi, plus current tools being provided through the AWE training program. Guided by the ancestral matriarchs and medicine of the earth, I am currently occupying Ohlone territory. I am committed to using my art to promote healing, liberation, and social justice. Through my work, I seek to honor my ancestors, connect with the natural world, and inspire others to embrace their own transformative power.

column24:

I explore and celebrate the intricacies of Black identity through personal narratives and ideologies. My artistic expression involves portraying cinematic scenes from my past and present reality. Growing up in a predominantly Black neighborhood, I delve into my memories, seeking to recreate moments that evoke a profound sense of nostalgia for viewers. Innovation is a constant pursuit for me, always searching for fresh ways to breathe life into my ideas. Lately, I have been experimenting with perspective, capturing scenes from different angles from above, beneath, and through the lens of our common vision. Additionally, I have been deconstructing sneakers, integrating them into my artwork to allow viewers to step into my shoes, both literally and metaphorically. My work invites viewers to not only observe these experiences but also to feel what it's like to be part of my broader community, offering glimpses into the lived reality of being Black in America.

column25: Jonah Elijah, a native of Houston's Third Ward, Texas, his work transcends time, drawing upon his personal memories and upbringing. Within his narratives, he delves deep into identity, through mediums of portraiture, language, and mass media. Elijah engages with the past, employing found objects charged with meaning, such as sneakers, televisions, and artifacts, as symbolic metaphors for the dynamic experiences they represent. Elijah received his BA in Studio Art from the University of Texas at San Antonio in 2017. Since receiving his MFA from Claremont Graduate University in 2020, his studio practice has progressed through residencies such as Virginia Center for Creative Arts, Virginia where he created his Virginia Series, responding to the center’s historical land in the foothills of Blue-ridge Mountains in Amherst & VCCA, France where he created his reflexion series taking multiple hand held mirrors & painting individual portraits of family & friends from his day to day. Further inviting viewers into his personal narrative, Elijah continues to explore memory and identity. Through this, he highlights the joy within Black culture.

column25: Representation is at the core of my artistic practice. I remember keenly being a child and not seeing authentic representations of girls who looked like me in the media. Though I didn't realize it then, it had a profound impact on my self-esteem and self-image. Black kids deserve to see themselves in art too. In both my static and moving work, I intentionally center underrepresented people and voices. In my illustration work, I often create pieces that highlight the beauty of Blackness and Black women. In my film work, I like to tell stories from unique perspectives, like my current animated short film which is told from the perspective of a Nigerian-American teenage girl. Ultimately, my goal is to curate a body of work that vibrantly captures the diversity and versatility of Blackness while also championing diverse narratives that often go unseen and unheard.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Not limiting materials is so important in my work. I use whatever best conveys the subject or story that I am addressing. I recently have been working on pieces dealing with renewal, fleeting moments, human intervention of natural cycles in a variety of medium. Almost all my work has to do with nature, our natural environment, climate change and all organic things. I want the work to convey a sense of beauty, but also confront the challenges we are faced with our environment. I want people to look at things in our world that sometimes are ignored or just not thought about. I what people to look at my work and think about how important our world really is. “We can live in harmony with nature and not dominate it. We can live within it as part of the cycle of our world”. My sculpture pieces are metal and assemblages using found materials, natural materials and various paint mediums to tell stories that are personal or contemporary to our society. The intent is to create a feel of something of the past or a relic but that has a message for our current society. I have been working on four main series of work: Tree Series, Shattered Glass Series, Reliquary Series and Tree/Fire Series. Each is related but have their own distinct quality and theme. This artwork many times has layered meaning both personal or in a boarder context with issues of our society in the present. I have been a painter at heart over the years literally for as long as I can remember. I primarily have painted in oils but have also used other media. I also have incorporated painting and drawing in my 3D work. Most of my painting is about the movement of paint and how it feels. It is expressive and impressionistic in style. I have painted plein air, from photos and memory. I love the feel quality of oil paint and prisma colored pencils. I am also using my painting techniques with my sculptural forms. Photography is another area I have been experimenting. Time moves so fast that we rarely take time to notice a single beautiful moment. I have been achieving my photo artwork by using my cell phone camera under certain types of light and contrasts. I then bring out the true feeling and meaning through photoshop. I want to preserve the pure glimpse of time on the move. I have also been incorporating my photos in my reliquary sculpture work. One of my favorite quotes is from Leonardo Da Vince “Art is never finished it is only abandoned”.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being an artist, for me, means becoming more aware of how you perceive the world around you by learning to interpret what you see, hear, touch, and feel in different ways. With my work, I draw attention to the rituals and traditions of daily life and collect fragments of our cultural heritage to revive memories and share our culture with others. I meet people who I listen to, interact with, and collaborate with to create a visual language that tells their stories. I am very grateful for the freedom to learn and experiment every day and to be able to focus entirely on my work. I love the process of making and using traditional techniques that are very labor-intensive. It allows me to connect with the long line of makers and experience through my body their toil and labor that makes up my work.

column25: Brigitta Varadi is a multimedia artist who delves into tradition, craft, and the everyday rituals of working life. She investigates themes of sustainability and cultural heritage through a combination of research, material experimentation, labor-intensive processes, and community engagement. Interweaving notions of fine art and craft, labor, and heritage, Brigitta focuses attention on the importance of traditions in an age of mass production and the global economy. Brigitta examines the disappearing traditions and daily activities of small, secluded communities around the world: her grandmother mopping up her kitchen floor several times a day, the “liberty” tea made by inhabitants of New York State, the marking system of the dwindling community of shepherds in Ireland and the breuccio, a piece of red silk sewn into a sachet to hold coarse salt, shared between women to protect from evil spirits, in rural Italy.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My process has been a life long effort to prove legitimacy to myself, as an artist. Because I have been more focused on an interior path, I did not start interacting with the outside world with my art efforts in a professional sense until relatively late in life. I wanted to be sure of myself, to trust the value of my expression, before soliciting attention to it. I chose to move to a remote rural location in the Sierra Nevada mountains (from New York City) about 20 years ago, to focus on my personal art development. Presenting my inner expression publicly has been a particular challenge for me, but also exhilarating (as feedback can be generative). As it turned out, my inner expression required massive doses of outer interaction to fully develop and manifest physically. This inner/outer process happened especially through the development of my Burn Pile / Andromeda Mirage project over the past 9 years, which I describe here: Disparate expressions of truth flow together into a communal truth essence — scrambled, released from sense. Might this confluence of differences conjure a shimmer of unity from eddies of unmeaning? Truth donors were invited to speak truth inside a private booth. Their statements were immediately leached of meaning by being scrambled with truth statements from previous donors, by way of a purpose-made code. Donors then had the option to release these garbled statements to be variously commemorated by me, or to delete them. This project aspires to link people in heart-felt incoherence — imbuing meaning by leaching out meaning — inviting participants to toss their truths into a flow of change. I offered the booth at various locations throughout the Central Valley of California — shopping malls, thrift stores, flea markets and public parks. After many deployments, many pages of scrambled statements have been produced. I have commemorated these scrambled, donated truth statements by crafting papier maché plates, text on paper signage, flowing text on LED signs, the construction of a paper Cabinet, an audible papier maché Conch, piñatas, text on flow-patterned paper lumber, facsimiles of the undersides of dining tables, and many other objects — all for large installations suggestive of collapsed domestic spaces. By making objects to commemorate the scrambled confluence of these many donors’ sincere expressions, my intention has been to demonstrate respect for an ambient sincerity beyond specificity, a sincerity perhaps also released through a collapse of meaning.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Our adornments are intertwined with their materials. As a jewelry artist, I merge recycled metals, repurposed wood, and responsibly harvested plants with fabrication and casting. By combining carefully considered materials and traditional jewelry techniques, I create adornments that speak to how intertwined we are with our surroundings. Connection brings awe and wonder into my life and leads me to develop creative designs that deepen my relationship with place. This interrelatedness is a central value in my work, seen in the use of cast organic materials and endemic wood elements. Adornment is an act of conversation with ourselves and an expression of ourselves to the world around us. If our jewelry is inseparable from its materials and intertwines us with our surroundings, what do the materials of our jewelry say about how we relate to the world around us?

column25: Laura Lau Klein has been creating art and exploring adornment for as long as she can remember. Her early memories of making beaded jewelry and wearing seven skirts at once paint how early this all started. Lau found herself painting her way through high school and dreaming of traveling the world, leading her to study Studio Art, Art History, and Anthropology at the University of Delaware. After school, she explored the world and its many avenues of adventure and supporting herself, from living in the woods to serving tables. Her time spent backpacking and gardening in the mountains of Western North Carolina nurtured her connection with the natural world, spurring her towards a deep conversation with herself about what materials she uses to create and why. At that time, Lau began working with natural dyes and pigment making, media she continues to explore in her two-dimensional work. Simultaneously, Lau was pulled toward metals, excited by this medium's sheer possibilities. Lau now lives and makes in Asheville, North Carolina. She continues to explore her techniques, materials, and the stories within handmade objects while creating beautiful adornments to enrich people's daily lives.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Through painting and sculpture, Los Angeles based world builder Emily Veldman captures the narratives of her expansive fictional land. This new realm serves as a sanctuary for Veldman, a place where she can address and heal from the complexities of her mental health. Each canvas represents a blank slate, where Veldman blocks out life’s responsibilities and anxieties, replacing them with vibrant and captivating fantasies. Patterns, bright colors, and composition form the stage transporting viewers to an alternative universe, one where personified flowers and animals reside. The true magic unfolds as she incorporates materials such as felt, yarn, and embroidery floss. These elements add texture to the canvas and introduce fresh perspectives. Crocheted yarn morphs into a rug on a wooden floor, while fabric and string transform into laundry hanging on a clothesline. Veldman is inspired by the power of art to transport others to different worlds and to help them process their emotions. Her hope is that her work will provide viewers with a safe space to heal and to find joy in the midst of their struggles.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a painter focused on portraying people and their inner lives. My work engages themes of racial embodiment and subject formation, because I am interested in how embodiments like race can be described through illustrations of one’s phenomenal experiences of the qualitative world. This body of work grows out of a need to find a rigorous aesthetic vocabulary to articulate points of passage in racial identity—intimacy and alienation, pride and shame, assimilation and expulsion: to account for the social and private realities of raced personhood. I am particularly concerned with describing subject formation, for people living within a social consciousness that values that which they can never be, as conditioned by a complicated and continuous negotiation with trauma, fantasy, and performance. These paintings explore the complexity of this negotiation through use of visual tension and narrative ambiguity.

It's the ability to be able to create something with your own two hands, but also not having much control over what is made; allowing for the work to make itself in a way. You get the chance to step out of the world around you and into whatever it is right in front of you. Each stroke of the paint brush guides itself, as I am just the vessel allowing the paint, emotion, and energy to freely move through me, and onto the canvas. We speak another language than others. A visual language that evolves with every project. As an artist I think I have the ability to read everything/everyone, including myself in greater detail than others. And if I don't know much about myself, my art informs me; as each piece is a direct extension of my soul.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My recent work uses satellite photos to create landscapes of factories, warehouses, and residential construction sites, all seen from above. These aerial landscapes are overlaid with enlarged and carefully camouflaged hammers, hatchets and shovels, yielding figure-ground compositions that are initially difficult to see. Whereas figure-ground compositions normally emphasize sharp contrasts, their delineation in my work is subtle and delicate instead. Depending on how they are viewed, the tools either emerge from or disappear into the aerial landscapes below. Cycling from one layer to another, the eye alternates between vast urban worksites and tools once used to build them. The longer we look, the more the imagery morphs, the boundaries blur, and our familiar orientation slips away. This experience is elaborated in a series in which enlarged “quick squares” (a multi-purpose roof builder's tool) are layered over large factory buildings and empty parking lots. Camouflaged to resemble the factory roofs below, the oversized quick squares generate a complex medley of triangles, overlapping rectangles, cutout shapes, and shadows. These prints can be displayed individually or presented as a two- or three-part series. (Only two of the prints are included in this submission.) Deliberately disorienting, these multi-layered visual puzzles invite viewers to decipher the different levels and distinguish between what is visible and what is concealed. Subjectively, these images evoke enormous monuments floating above separate and detached pedestals below.

column25: I resumed making art in 2016 after working two decades as a clinical psychologist. I began by exploring alternative forms of landscape art, mostly viewed from an aerial perspective. In the USGS Map Project, I adapted pen-and-ink topographical maps of geological landscapes in Utah and Arizona formed over millennia. Presented mostly in vertical panels, these maps read more as abstract forms – some recognizable, others defying description. A second body of work explores satellite photos of residential neighborhoods, some already established and others still under construction. Also conceptualized as alternative landscapes, these prints provide unsentimental overhead views of America in the early 21st century. Once printed on paper, the images are folded into evenly distributed zig-zag ridges and mounted so that they appear to float on the wall. (Both sets of work are available for viewing on my Instagram account.) My current project, entitled Overlooked, is described in the artist statement in this application. Since 2019 my work has been in 35 juried group exhibitions in 13 states, including New York, Massachusetts, California, and Oregon.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My favorite part about being an artist is the ideating and making process. Creating and bringing life to a crazy idea that had been racing through my mind is such an exciting and fulfilling experience. From the first spontaneous sketches, I often feel exhilarated at the prospect of new work. Then getting dirty with the mediums and really making the work, wrestling with ideas and the physical, connecting the dots between the intentional and my subconscious, the whole process is like a chaotic puzzle. It’s here that I’m able to take a closer look at what I value and really find myself. For me, this process couples both the extreme and the pensive, and leaves room for some of the most joyful, critical, reflective, and rewarding moments of my practice.

column25: My practice explores ideas of intimacy, separation, solitude, grief, and memory through ceramic sculpture. In this work, I examine fundamental transformations that my relationships have undergone over time and flawed memories of periods of my life I consider significant. In this way I attempt to better understand myself - my biases, fears, ambitions and apprehensions - and how my experience with the human condition can be reflective of the experiences of others. I create these manifestations of my hard feelings to personally search for closure or healing, but also to establish connections with the viewer through the sharing of experiences. A part of establishing these connections is freely giving work to viewers, which has become central to my practice. This interaction subverts capitalistic structures that prevent meaningful dialogue between artwork, viewer, and artist, but also speaks to the complex and intense materialist urge to own, collect, and possess, as well as the sad reality of transactional relationships.

column25: In creating my work, I am analyzing and exploring my identities through culture, location, gender, and home. I challenge perceptions around mental illness and explore the dualities of acceptance and confusion, traditional and modern, body and soul, beauty and pain. My work is authentic because of its imperfections, vulnerability, and the balance of chaos with beauty. My work is a celebration of the flawed human. Humans are beautiful because of their flaws, resilience, and complexities. The materials of paper, fabric, and clay mimic the characteristics of being strong and fragile, simple and complex, common and unique. Furthermore, the textures bring these materials to life; the fibers of the paper, the crinkles and wrinkles, the finger impressions on clay, and little nuances of the human hand. Perfection is only interesting for so long before humans are unable to relate. That is why I embrace the little marks of the evidence of a long process, and find ways to honor them. In doing so, I hope the viewer sees the cracks, the rips, the uneven surfaces, the tensions and the softness, and sees themself, equally faulty and equally beautiful because all those inconsistent characteristics come together to create something real and true. Each piece has provided healing by reflecting on my own depressive episodes. It is from these deep investigations that I am able to understand and accept myself for all that I am. I hope the viewer is able to share this experience of acceptance of the work and of themselves.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am constantly sifting for truth through the sea of outer stimulus of technology and media; assessing the validity. I am attempting to move past the white noise and static. Through my art, I am refocusing on the natural world and opening the portal of my ever-expanding compassion for humanity to access visions of the grandest possibilities for the future. As I dissect and explore my most authentic self, my love & hope for humanity grows exponentially. I categorize my work as fantastic realism. I often work in the mischtechnik style, using many layers and glazes to achieve an ethereal feeling and depth. I paint between the lines, often making the unseen visible and palpable. I aim to capture the liminal spaces between what we know and what we feel. My imagery embodies humanity, animal and spirit beings, and nature in themes of regeneration, sustainability and visions for the future of mankind. In my public art, I strive to engage community and empower others, helping to tell their stories and amplify their voices, planting seeds of possibilities. My recent focus has been on trying to hone my imagery and compositions to articulate stories. Sometimes that story is archetypal and mythic; other times it is as basic as self-love or forgiveness. I am delving into the great mystery of life. My process is very mindful. It is my spiritual practice and it highlights the magic that I see in the world.

column25: Shara has a BFA from School of Visual Arts in NYC. She has been a practicing pagan for over 30 years. She did a formal, 7-year spiritual and artistic apprenticeship with Bill Thorpe, a pagan priest, bard and artist: studying pagan spirituality, mythic imagination and folklore, as well as fine art. She has spent the last 15 years actively studying myth and archetypes, while taking classes in mischtechnik, permaculture, sacred geometry and visionary art to learn the cultural stories of the past and how to keep them alive through her artwork. Shara utilizes themes of compassion, sustainability, nature and animism in her work. She approaches artwork as a spiritual practice, with meditation and prayer. Shara has exhibited her work in many galleries in the USA and abroad. For the past 4 years, she has been a leader/conductor of a group called the Vision Train & their non-stop global art jam, with renowned artist, Amanda Sage. She has been painting, sharing and teaching in this 'Vision Train' virtual studio with hundreds of visionary artists from around the world. They hold many classes and collaborative experiences for artists who work with spiritual imagery and visionary art techniques. Shara studied with Daniel Mirante & Laurence Caruana at the Visionary Arts Academy Summer Seminar in Torri Superiore, Italy, and with Amanda Sage. All of these master painters studied with Ernst Fuchs & Brigid Marlin: following a direct lineage of the Mischtechnik. Shara brings all of this experience to her public art & to teaching and mentoring in Fitchburg & as a resident artist at Main Street Studios. Paganism has taught her the power of “ARTivism” and involvement in her community, both locally and globally. By making public art and coordinating community events in the local region, and by being an Art Steward and leader in Fitchburg, Shara is teaching, sharing, and practicing the ideas of co-creative, collective, community engagement and empowerment. “We are building sustainable practices to keep our community strong and vital. By creating public art, we send messages and plant seeds for future generations to learn sustainable practices for global healing”, says Shara. She has created 5 murals in her hometown of Fitchburg in the past 3 years. They share stories of heart opening, resilience, reaching towards change and represent many diverse types of artists and ethnicities within the population. Shara organized the Lowe Park Chalk Art Festival; a 1-day event where professional artists, community groups & children make art with chalk in designated spaces on a basketball court in a local park. There is music & food. The event connects the people, while giving them ownership of the art being created in their own space. It serves a marginalized population in a low-income neighborhood, provides resources & builds pride. Shara’s path is to guide others to be their best selves, providing tools for their own empowerment & success. Shara balances her personal painting practice with her civic engagement and public art projects. She works to continue making an impact in the world, locally and globally.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The short answer is 'being able to express myself,' but the full response is the freedom and the ability to imagine and create. A lot of times when showing my work, there would be people asking me how I came up with the idea. Initially, I did not understand why they would ask this question since 'think' would be my answer to their question. But gradually, I realized being able to think wild, build out of frame, and create from nothing is not easy for everyone. Not every individual can 'make up' something they have never seen before. Now being an artist, I see art as the medium that I use to communicate with people and our society. Instead of speaking languages, I talk about social issues through my art, and I want to invite people to see them from a different perspective. The reason why I do not speak in my performances in general is that I expect my work can break through language barriers and definitions structured by words. Giving the audience a space to interpret the empty and silent part motivates people to think from none. It is not about what I think is 'right' or 'wrong' but raising awareness of a problem and asking people to reevaluate the topic. I would be happy if my art could help inspire viewers and participants to be possible to imagine as well. And I believe this makes our world even more diverse and flourish.

column25: I am a performance artist from Taiwan with a bachelor’s degree in psychology. I moved to New York City in 2020 after receiving my MFA in Community Arts from Maryland Institute College of Art, Maryland. Social issues stimulate me to create, especially when it comes to relationships between human beings. I look into the word “relationship,” observing and analyzing teamwork and conflicts between cultures, generations, countries, and even between humans and the environment. Then I create art reflecting on these problems to raise awareness of what they cause and how we can improve. Combining psychology and art, I apply psychological theories to my work, utilizing interactive elements and the ability of hospitality in my performances to invite people not only to view as spectators but join the process and experience as participants. By erasing the boundary between the artist and the audience, I aim to bring people together to rethink social issues and start conversations with each other. I expect my art to potentially motivate people to take action and make changes. In each performance project, there is an assigned topic waiting to be addressed. I create a performance based on body movements and interactions with an installation, props, and costume design to fulfill the whole idea. Being recognized as an immigrant artist, I self-identify as Taiwanese and a part of the BIPOC community. I ask for peace, no wars with other people, in my hometown and anywhere else through my art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My evolution as an artist began with embroidering my own photographs, which morphed into embroidering vintage snapshots and has grown into the analog collage work that I create today. I chose analog collage for the satisfying tactile sensation of manually cutting, placing and pasting paper and other objects. I find the process comforting and therapeutic. My collage pieces are the sum of what I have explored, experimented with and learned in the past few years. I’m constantly curious about new techniques and themes and have seen my work become more narrative as I go along. Some themes I explore are feminism, mysticism, surrealism and absurdism. My work is mostly playful and whimsical in nature, sometimes with darker undercurrents. I collect used materials almost weekly to add to what I affectionately call “my paper hoard”. The more I practice, the more possibilities I see in random items: a discarded shopping list, plastic bread tags, a cardboard cereal box. There are few limits to what I will include in one of my collages. Sustainability, recycling and repurposing are the backbone of my artistic practice.

column25: Jeannie Worley is a collage artist residing in Evansville, Indiana, USA. She was born in Japan, the daughter of a Vietnam Veteran. She spent her childhood traveling around the world for her father’s Navy career. In adulthood, she started her own wedding and portrait photography business and worked for 25 years. She specializes in embroidered photographs and mixed media analog paper collage. Her work is almost entirely composed of secondhand items: orphaned photos, books, old letters, junk mail, food packaging, maps and postcards, etc. Jeannie’s work has been exhibited in virtual and local exhibitions and art galleries. Her solo collage show, Pieces of Yesterday, was exhibited in July at The Rumjahn Gallery in Evansville, IN. She also regular participant in Yart, a local biannual art fair. Her collage work has been published in Photo Trouvee Magazine’s Echoes of Yesterday, The Mini Collage Collective’s 2023 Look Book and Piece by Piece: Cutting, Swapping and Creating curated by Kelly Schaub. Jeannie lives in Evansville with her partner, the artist Gary Logan Hobdy and their two spoiled cats.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a multidisciplinary artist based in Brooklyn with roots stemming from Japan, I draw profound inspiration from organic materials and the exquisite allure of nature. My art works finds its genesis in the idyllic landscapes of rural Japan, where majestic mountains, green tea fields, and the vast expanse of the Pacific Ocean nurtured the depths of my imagination. In the midst of amphibians, fishes, and a myriad of small creatures like frogs, snakes, lizards, moss and insects, I discovered solace in capturing their very essence through meticulously sketched lines and shapes. These intricate details, along with the rhythmic flow of wind, the vitality of plants, and the vastness of the ocean, inspired my abstract landscapes, brought to life with the vibrant strokes of oil or acrylic on canvas. My experience of working of public art, it grants me the opportunity to create art on a grand scale for the enjoyment of the community. For instance, I was immensely honored to have my painting selected for “The Flag Project, 2022,” a prestigious collaboration between Rockefeller Center, the Climate Museum, and the United Nations Environment Programme. One of my large-scape flags, measuring 8’x5’, proudly adorned the skies adorned the skies above the renowned Rockefeller Center Skate rink. Moreover, I was privileged to be chosen as a lead artist for the “Hospital-Based Community Murals Project” in 2019, part of the NYC Health + Hospitals’ Arts in Medicine program. Through these endeavors I not only contribute to the aesthetic beauty of public spaces but also engage with diverse communities, immersing myself in the myriad of human emotions and perspectives spanning different generations. My artistic endeavors delve into the intricate tapestry of human life, society, and nature, endeavoring to evoke emotions that uplift the hearts and minds of viewers. By interweaving my observations of the natural world with the complexities of human existence, I aim to ignite a deep connection with the profound essence of our shared experiences. Through my art, I aspire to transcend boundaries, prompt introspection, and foster a sense of wonder and awe in those who encounter my work. Through my art, I aspire to ignite conversations, raise awareness, and cultivate a deeper appreciation for the delicate balance between humanity and the natural world. By inviting viewers to immerse themselves in multisensory encounters and thought-provoking narratives, I strive to foster a sense of connections and inspire positive change in our collective consciousness.

column25: Sophia Chizuco is a multidisciplinary artist, art educator, and curator based in Brooklyn, originally from Japan. She earned her B.A. in Art and Education from Tokyo Gakugei University. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I design paintings, drawings and murals by interpreting nature through an urban lens. Nature is always my inspiration yet I have always been drawn to living in cities for the diversity of people and complexity of daily life. Spectacles like dandelions pushing through concrete, wild parrots taking over cities or rain and draught cause landslides are reminders of nature's agency while all around civilization claims to control it. My experience as a woman has felt like this, and now Roe vs Wade has been overturned but we continue to find ways to be as free and equal as our male counterparts. I began making art as a child, and professionally by drawing. Drawing satisfies my need for immediate intimate output of mark making that looks and feels human. The intimacy of it follows my subjects which I draw and study in detail to reveal their secrets that can easily be missed, especially in our fast paced, short attention span modern life. Nature, thank goddess asks us to take our time. In that it has therapeutic powers, and I hope those and a feeling of hope translate in my work, which is often grappling with deeper psychological ideas. For that matter, art making itself is therapeutic. Reflecting on the quietness of my small, detailed drawings, I decided, shortly after graduating from Otis, to speak louder, in the way Abstract Impressionists did, by making mural size work. This transition was absolutely practical because, during my four years in undergrad I had been working with a decorative painter painting commissioned murals for private clients. I knew how to design for a specific site, how to prepare a surface, choose my materials, enlarge a design, work at height, and painting technique at a large scale. All I had to do was inject my ideas and passion I had for my art practice into the process of creating a mural. Since then I have painted over 10 large scale murals, some public and some private. Public practice is a place I continue to grow in because this involves collaboration. When designing a mural for a public site, the first consideration is who it is for. Who lives and works or travels there, who used to be there and who will come? How can I accurately represent them through my practice? Can I collaborate with other artists? These questions have allowed my voice to be amplified by millions of others' voices evolving the ideas in my work in important and relevant ways, and for exciting and unseen paths for personal growth as an artist and contributing member of society.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work explores time, memory, and impermanence. I am interested in who gets remembered, how we are remembered, how memory changes, and why certain things are forgotten. With a family history of dementia, I seek to understand the brains lucidity, memory triggers, how much a person’s memory can be removed before they are changed, and the endurance it takes to try to hang onto one’s mind when dealing with a cognitive disease. I try to show the personal experience of losing one’s memory and doubting one’s mind. Using a broad range of fugitive materials and techniques that break down over time – like water to dissolve, fragmentation, and optical lenses and tricks – my work shows the fragility of time and the fleeting nature of one’s mind. I choose materials that connect to the human body and mirror processes of age and the brain. The use of the photographic medium is at the heart of my practice, as I am fascinated by what remains and lives on after one is lost. I am compelled to explore how we impact those around us and our environment and the inevitable absence that will one day occur.

column25: Kaitlin Santoro is an interdisciplinary artist and educator. Working across photography, glass, and printmaking, her work explores time, impermanence, loss, and memory. She creates work that slowly breaks down and shifts, documenting the process and the aftermath to illustrate the fragility of memory over time. Santoro pushes the traditional boundaries of lens based, print, and 3D techniques by using incompatible materials, creating and using distortion lenses, or working in non-archival methods like printing with household solvents. Santoro’s work has been exhibited internationally and was most recently included in exhibitions at the International Center of Photography, UrbanGlass, and the Print Center of New York. She has been awarded residencies at the Manhattan Graphics Center, Pilchuck Glass School, DaVinci Art Alliance, the Sculpture Space, and the Oxbow School of Art. Santoro has presented at the Glass Art Society annual conference, Pilchuck Glass School, the Tyler School of Art, and Queens College. She received her BFA from the University of Connecticut, and her MFA in Photography from the Tyler School of Art and Architecture. She currently teaches at the Tyler School of Art and Architecture in Philadelphia and for the City University of New York.

column25: I’m an artist and writer who creates cross-genre in poetry, theater and visual art. These inspire and inform each other in my work. I’ve made huge novel-sized installations and produced haiku-like miniatures no bigger than a seed. I relish an art historical reference and I’m a sucker for a visual pun. I prefer to see the mistake-making hand present and often work in media we’ve abandoned since childhood, like tracing and coloring. I enjoy humble materials and have an affinity with the Arte Povera, found art and land art movements. My projects unfold over time. Because I work in so many different media I am often thinking in oeuvre, and relational themes. I have an intellectual, analytic background and training from my education, experience and personality. I’ve always been interested in scholarly topics. I rarely stop thinking about the implications of our age of mechanical reproduction. I also get stirred up by the immediate and topical. I’ll take as much pleasure in voting on who wore it better as I do contemplating work by Richter, Rothko, Smithson, Bourgeois. Well, not really. But I’ve always enjoyed that straddle between pop culture and high culture because, as far as I’m concerned, they are all culture. I love museums but maintain an on-going practice of photographing art guards as we both watch the people watching each other watching the art. Every day, ordinary interactions are, to me, the most spiritually profound. Keats said truth is beauty and I agree that beauty is also, deeply, truth. I like how the whole matter is addressed to a practical piece of sculpture that can hold ashes, water, soup and art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My name is Justin Patton. I am a creator. I bring my thoughts to life through paint, clay and computers. The creation of my work has gone through many evolutions of the years. Staring with my love for cubism paintings, figurative drawing and geometric design, I crafted for many years portraits of people and landscapes using straight hard lines and chaotic placements of shapes. After constantly chasing the desire for “Atmosphere “ in my work, I have introduced a softer technique that has given me the ability to create images and sculptures that have a more ethereal feel. Something akin to Beksinski or Giger in has always been a goal of mine. I find myself searching constantly for that “perfect” work that sings to me. Something I have been obsessed with my whole life. Until then, I treat each of my creations as a practice of art. Each one teaches me something new and I build upon the skills acquired from the previous one. Art is my passion and I will continue creating till my end.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
The. best. thing. about being an artist is the freedom and engagement that comes from making something that is 100% my own out of thin air. This is the only area in life where one gets to make literally ALL of the choices. You don't have to compromise creative or thematic vision when making your own work as you do with client work. Though I enjoy selling and showing my work, no one can dictate what it is or how I make it, and if my choices result in fewer sales or shows, they are still my choices and always my best effort. Every time I leave the studio I take a picture of my work in progress and refer to it often throughout the evening. Seeing the progression of a painting is endlessly entertaining! It keeps me focused on a positive, creative challenge that is sometimes frustrating but incredibly gratifying to see through to completion. In a sentence: Being an artist keeps me engaged while putting something positive into an increasingly uncertain world.

column25: My current painting series BIOMETRIX combines humor and visual curiosity to produce a skeptical inquiry into the history of portraiture and our contemporary weakness for technology. Painted on shaped-canvas and based on my own photographs, greatly enlarged images of human irises and fingertips touch upon themes of power, identity, preciousness and psychedelic sexiness while mixing the tropes and techniques of the centuries-old genre with the tools and ideologies of digital culture. Rendered in painterly marks and vivid color, these fragments of human anatomy appear to me like vast, otherworldly landscapes. In reality, they are collections of biometric data: bits and bites of biology freely provided to faceless corporations ostensibly for the convenience of quicker access to sensitive personal information. The fussy gold frames on these works nod to the signifiers of status and value of both the typical subjects of portraiture (clergy and gentry), and the objects themselves (oil paintings). Such works are ubiquitous in museum collections and I include this element to underscore a parallel between the value placed upon personal data and fine art as commodities, as well as the value bestowed upon or withheld from certain groups or individuals in contemporary culture. In late 2019 I took a break from the night-scape paintings I'd been showing for the past few years and turned my focus inward, returning to work that indulged my long-standing fascination with sensual experience. As with my earlier paintings of mouths and skin, these new 'sense' paintings of irises and fingertips were initially all self-portraits - a fact that proved to be both practical and meaningful early on in the pandemic as my external life contracted to that which I could watch, rather than experience, and isolation ruled the day. Suddenly the world outside my studio was reduced to a succession of images and scary headlines on progressively smaller screens (TV, Laptop, phone). Once the vaccines rolled out, with cautious relief I invited others into the studio to participate, all but one are women-a blunt pun referencing the female gaze, but also a deliberate expression of power reclaimed.

column25: An ineffable nuance, my work is a representation of being both Black and a Woman. Unpacking the phenomenon that is Blackness and operating from multiple worldviews, my work reflects and compares my experiences in different realms. The paintings themselves are seductive and inciting, unapologetically grabbing the viewer's attention through bold, attractive colors and shiny substrates. With this grip, the viewers are confronted by Blackness in relation to themselves. Embedded within the work is Caribbean culture, specifically Jamaica. I have found myself constantly questioning how Blackness is understood in predominantly white spaces upon entering American society. This is how the work is understood. The environment in which my work lives, culminates an unnerving feeling of something being off. The presentation of the body along with expressive abstract moments causes a type of speculative fiction to emerge. These paintings are held in a time and space that allows them to stand in their own dimension; existing simultaneously but separate to ours. I am able to do this by depicting environments I create out of need; making room for dense emotions, stark confrontations, and simply a space for blackness to exist. The burning desire to create a new way of thinking about reality carries my work forward. How systematic oppression and microaggressions reinforce this inclination for liberation from implications added to the Black body. My work is not this or that, but an “and”, bringing forth themes of religion, identity, and colonialism. Furthermore, the work refers to subjects such as protection and liberation, as well as emotions such as compassion, otherness, and love.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Throughout my entire life, I faced many challenges that 'typical' kids do not. It took me a long time to speak as a child, and communication is still difficult. Because of my autism, I struggle with verbal communication. Talking with people and writing my thoughts are difficult because it is hard to find the right words for me to say. Through my art, I feel free. I think autism has given me a unique perspective, and I hope it comes through in my art. My understanding of the world may differ as my senses are always on high alert. For example, I see pictures in my head when I hear music. Or, when I think of a movie, I can see it and listen to it playing in my mind. Sometimes, these constant bombardments are unwelcome and may overwhelm me, but this allows me to boost my visual communication to let my works convey an intended message in a manner that I may not be able to verbalize.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The most gratifying aspect of being an artist is the ability to use my craft as a medium for expressing and navigating the complexities of my memories and life experiences. Growing up in a small town with limited resources, I found solace and a creative outlet in working with unconventional materials. These early experiences shaped my ambition and laid the foundation for my unique design philosophy. As a child, crafting clothing from found objects in my parents' basement provided a distraction from the mundane aspects of my life and the challenges within my family. Turning to hot glue, a needle, and thread allowed me to transform these ordinary materials into extraordinary garments, showcasing my innate need for self-expression. The first charity fashion show I organized at the age of 12 marked the beginning of a journey that not only introduced me to the world of design but also served as a coping mechanism for the complexities of my upbringing. When I reinterpret those childhood prototypes into new work, it feels like conserving a small piece of time or bottling a memory. It's a way of connecting with my past, understanding the evolution of my creative process, and finding solace in the continuity of my work over the years. Designing clothes has become more than a profession; it is a therapeutic means to work through my emotions and navigate the challenges of life. Looking back at my body of work allows me to trace the dots that connect various stages of my life, serving as a visual and emotional autobiography. Each garment tells a story, encapsulating moments, emotions, and personal growth. Moreover, every new creation becomes a seed planted for the future, offering a reservoir of experiences and emotions to draw from in my ongoing artistic journey. In essence, the best thing about being an artist is the profound connection between my creative process and personal narrative. It provides a way to express, conserve, and revisit the ever-evolving mosaic of memories and experiences, creating a tangible and meaningful thread that weaves through the tapestry of my life.

column25: My work in fashion is rooted in deep nostalgia, relation to objects, and compulsive making. The garments I make are an ever-blooming mix of unique and precise coherency mingled with repetitive object assembly and the niche social culture of growing up queer in Generation Z. I navigate the space between past and present, inspired by a sense of nostalgia and reinterpreting memory through creative reuse. I am drawn to things that are absurd, ridiculous or surreal, yet enticing. Material exploration is at the core of my process, and I approach a new design with childlike curiosity, shedding any preconceived notion of a textile or object’s intended purpose. Treasure hunting for found material continues to be central to my process, and preserving the integrity of an object is key. The main goal of my artistic practice is to promote sustainable design practices and methods of fashion production. There are massive amounts of new clothing being produced globally and it’s alarming for the environment, garment workers, and consumers alike. I envision my work to be part of a new wave of intentional and meaningful design, making use of pre-existing materials with entire histories ingrained and woven into them. I look at objects not for what they are, but what they represent, and what they could be. A collage of memory, a visual scrapbook, an autobiography. I am interested in the evolution of a life, in what makes people who they are, what makes me who I am. My garments challenge traditional gender binaries by embracing a fluid and inclusive approach to fashion, transcending stereotypical notions of masculinity and femininity. Through innovative design and material choices, I intentionally blur the lines between conventional gendered aesthetics, creating pieces that invite individuals to express their identity authentically. My work serves as a powerful tool for empowerment by rejecting societal norms and celebrating the diversity of self-expression. By utilizing unconventional materials and reinterpreting nostalgic elements, my designs encourage wearers to resist the status quo, fostering a sense of individuality and freedom in the realm of fashion regardless of one’s gender identity.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Zelda Jane Thorn is a painter and writer who performs drag on the canvas. Making diaristic self-portraits in the tradition of Frida Kahlo, Cindy Sherman, and Sasha Gordon, Thorn's work is steeped in science-fiction and fantasy, often making works that reference, critique, and relate specific works both to her own experience and to broader social mores, including Dune, Dr. Who, and Dungeons & Dragons. Working in a realist mode reminiscent of John Singer Sargent or Jenna Gribbon, Thorn is concerned with light and composition, often staging elaborate lightscapes for the reference photos for her paintings. Many of her paintings are accompanied by short snippets of story - no more than necessary to set the scene - intended to expand and elaborate on the themes of the image, which typically include social norms, gender, narrative, transformation, and death.

column25: Anastasia Egeli is a third-generation artist, carrying on the tradition of a pre-eminent family in American portrait painting. Her grandfather, Bjorn Egeli, emigrated from Norway as a teenager and went on to establish a successful portrait practice. His prestigious commissions include two presidents, Eisenhower and Nixon, prominent historical figures such as Gen. Douglas Mac Arthur, and several Supreme Court justices. All of Bjorn’s children, including Cedric, Anastasia’s father, became artists themselves. Cedric, in particular, went on to establish an important portrait and teaching practice and is also represented by Portraits, Inc. Successful artists often attribute their advance to important student-teacher relationships — the latter being the means to which they became heirs to a significant school of practice. Cedric’s artistic lineage is noteworthy. He studied with Henry Hensche, who in turn studied with Charles Hawthorne. Hawthorne was an early pioneer in American Impressionism and the founder of the Cape Code School of Art. Hawthorne, and his school’s subsequent student generations, developed a realist painting method informed by a colorist sensibility that continues to appeal to a wide array of patrons. As heir to this lineage, Cedric’s portrait work has earned a prestigious following, with pieces in the collections of the Pentagon, John’s Hopkins and Duke University. Likewise, having studied both with her father Cedric, his teacher Henry Hensche, and America’s preeminent portraitist Nelson Shanks (also a Hensche student), Anastasia is now heir to the New England School of Painting. As such, she infuses her portraits with luminous color and lively brushwork. Her spirited sitters glow with life—as if basking in the brilliant light and clean fresh air of a Cape Cod summer. Currently, Anastasia is completing commission work in her studio in New York City. She offers portrait workshops at the Egeli studio in Maryland and teaches color theory during the summer at the Cape School in Provincetown, MA.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being an artist is akin to queerness: it allows you to create your own version of who you are and what you do without constraints from the outside world, outside perspectives, or mainstream thinking. It’s one of the very few spaces where a person is able to define themself. Being able to see inspiration everywhere, to interpret ideas and take in the world, and to manifest something new made with my own hands is freedom. This freedom also lies in creative communities, where shared ideas, excitement for material, and passion for process are valuable currency. Being an artist allows us the freedom to make change with our creative voices. The other greatest thing about being an artist is that it provides a place to hold my feelings. I feel this imperative to make something every day. And this making offers freedom to let sadness, anger, joy, and passion leave my body and take up space in the world. It offers a way to communicate when words don’t make sense. This is the privilege of being an artist, the privilege of taking things out of my brain and showing them to other people. Honestly, it feels like the only way I can really show myself to other people.

column25: When all hope is lost, I turn to the earth. My work is based in process, in examining and embracing the steps and stages involved in making. My personal practice has shifted to include geological and botanical materials, and place itself has become a grounding element in my art and my life. What is a place? Typically, we answer this question through the framework of a human life. A place is a collection of memories in a house, of city blocks, of trees planted by a previous generation. But humanity’s role in any place is microscopic compared to the natural processes that made a place over the millennia. By using natural elements offered by a specific place, by learning of its natural history, we gain a wider and truer perspective of time. Something as solid as a sandstone cliff is actually a shifting and always-changing structure. It only seems to stand still because, as people, we are here for such a short time. My current practice explores the intersection of geology and fiber-based work. Though the materiality of rock and textiles appear to be opposites, portraying geologic formations through sewing and knitting is a compelling way to explore the intricacies of natural structures, their nuanced beauty, and their expansive history. Land Sandwiches and Conglomorate are both new works that explore the phenomenon offered by geology below our feet. Utilizing malleable materials to portray rock hard structures also demonstrates that softness in not antithetical to strength.

There is no evidence of miracle in the original Delacroix’s painting, and I am not a practicing believer, but the repeated act of painting this story of terror, anguish, and hope feels like a meaningful plea.

column25: Amy Massey is a visual artist living in Asheville, NC. She grew up in Pittsburgh, PA and received a BFA from Columbus College of Art & Design. Initially, her studies were focused on photography and its ability to intimately capture the essence of a person or place. After college, Amy moved to Oregon and her body of work, Dimensions of Intimacy, developed as a reflection of her travels intermingled with her explorations of the emotional landscape that can be gleaned within a sense of space. column25: I could be described as a “Type-A creative.” Following a two-decade corporate career, I thrive on precision and strategy. But as an artist, I know that the fun begins when I relinquish control. Music has always been my passion, and it’s the inspiration and fuel for everything I do, especially painting. In this vibrant intersection of color and sound, my musical-themed paintings dance the delicate balance between structure and improvisation - such as incorporating the relaxed curves of an instrument juxtaposed with its technical features, or capturing a musician’s palpable emotions during a performance. I find myself not just a painter but a storyteller inspired by the music that has accompanied me through life. Each piece is a visual narrative of the emotions and stories I’ve collected from the more than 1,000 concerts that I’ve attended. With my paintings, I invite viewers to embark on their own journies of interpretation and reflection, as music is a universal language that transcends boundaries. I am known for my signature style of dynamic interplay of colors and textures, with each layer responding to the layers that came before, revealing the painting’s personality with depth and complexity. column25: A Jazz-Infused Exploration in Abstraction For over three decades, I sculpted light and shadow like a master conductor, shaping visions within the polished confines of advertising and fine art photography. But beneath the veneer of glossy perfection, a different rhythm resonated. The improvisational spirit of jazz, a love that simmered since my teens, beckoned me toward a new artistic exploration. Retiring wasn't a creative finale; it was an artistic reboot, an opportunity to trade the staged tableau for the unfurling canvas of abstraction. Each piece becomes a natural echo of my inner feelings, ignited by the mystery of improvised melodies and raw emotions that pulse through jazz. It's like stepping into a kaleidoscope of sound, where every note refracts into a dance of light and form. Pixel by pixel, my digital brushes illustrate the syncopated rhythms of a guitar's mournful cry, the playful staccato of a trumpet's riffs, and a double bass's deep, pulsing heartbeat. My tools are an orchestra of technology, but Edward Weston's words resonate: 'It's an illusion that art is created by the tools we use. Art is created with our eyes, hearts, and minds.' While whispers of other artists' influence might linger in the margins, my voice blares through the vibrant chaos, a testament to the unique landscape born from my soul and the soul of jazz. This series isn't just an artistic exploration; it's a visual code waiting to be deciphered. Melodies morph into swirling expanses of color, harmonies pulse in the interplay of light and shadow, and emotions weave intricate tapestries of texture and form. It's a dance of contention and cohesion, mirroring the very essence of jazz where chaos yields to a breathtaking, unexpected symmetry. I have a condition called Dyslexia, once a stumbling block in the realm of words, has become a springboard for visual articulation. It grants me a unique lens, an obscured superpower that translates the torrent of emotions within me into vibrant geometries and spontaneous rhythms. Just as a bluesy bass line paints sounds that blend and sharp, my art combines emotions onto the digital canvas. When you encounter these visual improvisations, I hope a similar vision ignites within you. May your eyes see the echo of a saxophone's wail in a twisting line, your heart feel the bass's mournful hum in a deep shade of color, and your mind dance to the unspoken language that bridges my vision and yours. Come, lose yourself in the kaleidoscope of my mind, and let's rediscover the shared heartbeat of art and improvisation, where the glossy fades and the groovy takes flight.

Always a multifaceted artist, Nico experimented with many different art forms from a young age. Having multiple passions can be difficult due to time and resources, but when one truly has a vision and love for creating it is only a matter of time before goals are accomplished. Over the years Ocin’s work spans from drawing and painting to cooking and clothing, but the underlying love and originality through everything remains the same.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In all of my artistic practices I examine and investigate the nature of time and space, which I think is the essence of being in the world. My media include: dance films, paintings, drawings, photographs, costumes and sculptural objects - all of which reference time and space in some way. Some of these works layer images from different times and spaces to suggest simultaneity, some are direct responses to the natural world. I am very influenced by Greek mythology, Roman history, twentieth-century philosophy, speculative fiction, and the novels of Murakami. While the movement in film and video are more natural extensions of my origins as a dancer, in all of my processes, I arrive as a dancer, so making art objects is very much a physical act for me, much the same as dancing a piece of choreography is. I started drawing as a child, and continued the practice even though I went on to work primarily in dance, video, and photography. As I realized that my photographs had increasingly resembled drawings, I decided that it was time to return to making drawings. My drawings are made from layers of chalk, charcoal and pencil, and range in size. After working almost exclusively with chalk, charcoal and pencil on paper for a few years, I decided to return to painting. I was inspired to play with household materials and found objects, which helped me to find the physical quality that I enjoy in the process of making artworks.

column25: Christy Walsh started her performance career as a child, singing, acting and dancing with her grandmother, Alice Wamsley, in Wamsley’s musical theater company, Norfolk Musical Theater. Walsh went on to study with Gene Hammett, Glenn White, Susan Boree and others at the Tidewater Ballet in Norfolk, Virginia, where she also graduated from the Governor’s Magnet School. This led to an itinerant career with regional ballet companies: Virginia Ballet Theater, Pennsylvania Ballet, Richmond Ballet, and others; independent choreographers: Elbert Watson, Beverly Cordova Duane, and others, and even event performances at Heartbreak Cafe in Virginia Beach - all while concurrently studying photography at Tidewater Community College and Rochester Institute of Technology, finally graduating from Virginia Commonwealth University with a BFA in Communication Arts and Design. She continued to perform for her grandmother whenever she could and started choreographing under the mentorship of Theresa and Anna Maria Martinez at Virginia Beach Ballet Academy. It was in Richmond that Walsh first presented choreography independently and made her first dance films. While she would continue to dance in other choreographers’ work, her focus shifted to making her own art. Part of that art-making involves two-dimensional work on paper and canvas, as well as three-dimensional work that takes the shape of sculpture and costume. She developed these skills in college, and it was during this time that she first began to show photographs, drawings and collages in group shows and galleries in the Hampton Roads area. The development of this work fed into her dance video projects, which were featured in festivals. After university, Walsh participated in a “Dance for Camera” residency at MASS Moca, the CUNY Dance Initiative, and a number of other artist residencies in New York, Virginia and Texas. She settled in New York and in 2001 started stringdance+media, under the banner of which she created a large repertoire of dances and produced many dance videos. Walsh’s works for the theater include evening length pieces: “Stories of the Sun God”, at Green Space in Queens; “Luminitza” at multiple venues in San Antonio, TX; Για σε το Ροδο, Ροδο μου at Katrominas Theodorakis Theater in Chios, Greece. She has also produced site-specific works like “As Much As Things Change” for FIGMENT, “The Friend and the Enemy Are In the Mirror” for Chashama, in addition to collaborative efforts such as “Carving the Curve” at the Sculptors’ Dominion in San Antonio, TX. Many shorter works have been presented in a wide range of venues, from choreographic showcases to arts festivals in the US, Greece and Korea. Her video art and photographs have been shown in group shows in New Jersey, New York, Texas, and Virginia as well as in Greece and Spain. Walsh’s award-winning films and videos have been screened in festivals in an expanding list of countries that includes: China, Columbia, Cyprus, Denmark, France, Greece, India, Indonesia, Israel, Italy, Paraguay, Portugal, South Africa, Spain, Sweden, Romania, Russia and the US. “Schemes Ripening In The Midday Sun”, an exhibition of drawings and video, was accompanied by a short performance at Scarsdale Library, in Scarsdale, NY, in April 2023. Walsh received a grant from Arts Westchester for the show. Her multimedia show “The Lost Colony of the Argonauts” is in development and is the recipient of a 2024 NYSCA grant.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work unveils the erasure of existence, generational pain, and trauma endured by BIPOC, trans, and gender non-conforming individuals through storytelling in my visual art practice. This reimagination explored a departure from works tailored to the Western audience and harnessed the power of portraiture to envision a world where immigrants, BIPOC, and trans and queer communities were the central audience of art. This depiction offers a kaleidoscopic view of what the community could look like today. Central to my work is an exploration of historical injustices, with a focus on the cleansing of trans and gender non-conforming bodies through colonization and when imperialism prevailed. It explores vital themes such as access to resources, trans bodies in ancient mythology, spirituality, social activism, Filipino and indigenous folklore, and performances captured outside of time. As an artist who has endured homelessness, workplace bullying, and the pain of being separated from my family, I closely work with New Alternatives, a homeless shelter for LGBTQIA+ youth in Manhattan. I mentor some of the queer youth, and allocate a portion of my work's sales proceeds to the organization.

column25: As an emerging transgender artist from the Philippines, my creative journey delves into themes of gender, identity, belonging, and migration. My quest for safety and access to medical transition in America ignited my artistic vision, offering an escape from my reality and a glimpse into a more inclusive future. I channel my expressions through drawings, paintings, digital art, photography, sculpture and film, often integrating my body into performances. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For me, the best part of being an artist is the incredible freedom to turn my thoughts, emotions, and perceptions into visual creations that can connect with people from all walks of life. What truly excites me about being an artist is the ability to shape the intangible. Whether I'm working with paint, drawings, or animation, I love the power to transform abstract ideas and emotions into tangible, visual representations. It's like unlocking a universal language that allows me to explore the complexities of the human experience beyond words and cultural barriers. The process of creating art is therapeutic and cathartic for me. Working with different mediums becomes a meditative journey, allowing me to delve into my thoughts, emotions, and subconscious. Whether it's a physical canvas or a virtual space, it becomes a sanctuary where I can confront and process the complexities of life, finding solace and insight through the act of creation. Being an artist keeps me in a constant state of curiosity and exploration. The journey is marked by an ongoing quest for inspiration, pushing my artistic boundaries, and experimenting with new techniques and mediums. This curiosity not only fuels my artistic growth but also enriches my perspective on the world, leading to a deeper understanding of both myself and my surroundings. The collaborative potential of art adds another layer of richness to my experience. Engaging in dialogue with fellow creators, collaborating across disciplines, and contributing to a collective pool of creativity are aspects I truly enjoy. Exhibitions, installations, and community projects become platforms for me to connect with diverse audiences, fostering a sense of shared humanity through the universal language of visual expression. I love being an artist with the ability to bring my imagination to life, creating a visual symphony that resonates with the souls of people from all walks of life. It's about the freedom to explore, the power to communicate beyond words, and the joy of continuous self-discovery. Through my work, I contribute to the cultural tapestry of humanity, leaving a lasting legacy that speaks to the beauty and complexity of the human experience, accessible to all.
