column25: IAs an artist, I find my creative spirit weaving through diverse mediums—painting, puppetry, stained glass design, filmmaking, screenwriting, and animation. My artistic journey draws inspiration from a rich tapestry of influences, ranging from the ancient petroglyphs of Indigenous Peoples to the vibrant and thought-provoking realms of Pop and Surrealist art. Additionally, my work bears the imprints of Irish archaeology and folkways, creating a unique fusion of elements that transcends traditional boundaries. Rooted in the vivid memories of my childhood, I channel the dynamic energy of the Colorform cutouts that once captivated my imagination. The vibrating palette of those formative years continues to resonate in my artistic expression, infusing my creations with a sense of nostalgia and playfulness. It is within this eclectic blend of influences that I discover the raw materials for my artistic alchemy. Central to my creative vision is the incorporation of images that reflect not only my personal narrative but also the collective history we all share. My canvases are populated with portraits of loved ones and figures from both my intimate circle and the broader scope of our shared human history. Through each stroke of paint, every intricately crafted puppet, and the luminous hues of stained glass, I seek to capture the essence of human connection and the profound beauty of our shared existence. In essence, my art becomes a vessel through which I aim to stitch my personal story into the larger fabric of our collective history. It is a conscious effort to intertwine the threads of my experiences with the broader narrative of humanity, creating a visual narrative that resonates on both personal and universal levels. The petroglyphs serve as a bridge between the ancient and the contemporary, connecting me to the timeless expressions of those who came before while allowing me to contribute to the evolving artistic dialogue of today. My exploration of Irish archaeology and folkways adds a cultural depth to my work, providing layers of meaning that invite viewers to delve into the complexities of identity and heritage. Through the lens of my creative lens, I aim to reanimate and reinterpret elements of the past, fostering a dialogue that transcends temporal and cultural boundaries. As a filmmaker, screenwriter, and animator, I extend my artistic language into the realm of moving images. Each frame is a canvas, and every narrative arc is an opportunity to explore the intricacies of the human experience. Whether through the silent storytelling of puppetry or the dynamic visual language of film, I aspire to evoke emotions, provoke thoughts, and ultimately, leave an indelible impression on the hearts and minds of those who encounter my work. In this multifaceted exploration of artistic expression, I invite viewers to embark on a journey—a journey through the landscapes of memory, the corridors of history, and the boundless realms of imagination. With each creation, I endeavor to contribute not only to the art of today but also to the timeless dialogue that connects us all. Through my art, I seek not only to capture moments but to transcend them, offering a glimpse into the interconnected threads that weave the fabric of our shared human experience.

My work also explores themes of mental wellness particularly in communities of color. Each piece or collection of pieces gives way to freedom and fluidity with abstract gestures and sculptural nuances in an effort to express various emotions and encourage “out of the box” internal thought and processing. My works are linked not only by subject matter, but also by my use of materials. I primarily work with ink and acrylic on canvas or paper, but am looking forward to growing deeper in my sculpture and weaving practice. I hope to continue exploring different mediums as new ideas arise and call for a shift in material use. I use historical and experiential research to inform my use of color and improvisational gestures. My work is grounded in my identity and intuitive thought processes.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work represents an inventory of artifacts - slivers of the past whose origins have long been obscured. I reconstruct rituals, folktales, and superstitions in a continuation that builds on all previous iterations, inciting the viewer to investigate our collective history. I want to affirm the need to navigate an uncertain and unpredictable future world by creating a narrative that constantly considers the past while foretelling an ongoing relationship with history. I render these themes in large scale wood-cut wall sculptures, utilizing a bright palette that employs the visual language of global folk art traditions. These sculptures provide a new context for ideas that have often been relegated to peripheral spaces or overlooked as simple tchotchke. With shape and form that fluctuates between painting and sculpture, I examine mythology as a manifestation of humanity’s ability to anthropomorphize even the smallest facets of the natural world. Recent work examines rituals resulting from the need to look outward, studying methods of divination; oracles that have disappeared from practice and those that have existed into the present day.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
What is the possibility that objects can reveal healing evidence? The sculptural objects I create depict fragmented spiritual acts of loss and transformation. For me, these acts include gardening, restoration, and acceptance. At the intersection of grief and joy, the work acknowledges the passing of time through a personal narrative permeated with everyday objects. Healing evidence is proof of acceptance. It acknowledges pain through story and metaphor and makes use of what’s within reach (i.e. memory, disposables). If it were a color, it would be green. Healing evidence may leave traces of superstition in terms of belief or symbology. Appreciating ordinary moments from the kitchen, backyard, and nearby, I am investigating what it means to recycle both memory and material as a way to identify a pattern and distill an environment that exists beyond the studio. For instance, the temporal qualities of food, plants, and dirt coalesce with memory and it’s decomposition. By primarily focusing on imagery from the outdoors that exist in small home gardens, I’m exploring the humor and playful qualities of creating a backyard sanctuary which will not protect me from the outside world but it may have the power to temporarily. Superstition exists here through reframing and cultivating hope. (If I build a birdhouse, perhaps my loved ones can revisit me in bird form. If they can’t, I will still welcome the warbler, the chickadee and the sparrow.)

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Carla Keaton was born on August 14, 1969, in Chicago Illinois. She was born the daughter of James Keaton, a Mississippi sharecropper, and to Betty Keaton, product of a strict southern Baptist upbringing. Carla was raised in Minneapolis, after her parents, along with her two other siblings moved to Minnesota in 1972. She recalls growing up in an environment where very little was said, and feelings were rarely expressed. Work was her parent’s sole companion. They worked long, and they worked hard. Bills had to be paid, and children had to be fed. Quality time was not affordable. Carla, the youngest of the three, spent most of her time in her room reading and drawing. She looked forward to the late nights, early mornings, when her mother and father returned home from work. She stayed up long past her bedtime, just to steal a glimpse of them wearily climbing the staircase towards their bedroom. They were tired and worn, and too tired to notice. Carla watched and read the drain on their faces, the anguish in their eyes, and the exhaustion of their bodies. It was all very clear. No words needed. No questions asked. Although Carla wasn’t aware of it early on, as her art evolved and she gained life experience, it became apparent, that giving voice to the silent, the weary, the misunderstood, the misused, the mistreated, and the underrepresented, had been the premise of her work and the premise of her work ever since. Inspiration for my paintings come from a need to capture, define, record and communicate the histories, stories and moments in time, experienced by human beings globally. These recorded “moments” serve as a portal, offering the viewer access to the lives and souls of those captured on canvas, and the lives and souls of us all. “The eyes are the windows of the soul” English Proverb

While in college she began experimenting with combining different media, pulling from memories of growing up in rural Florida and a love of mythology to create her own visual language. She’s continued training throughout her career, studying under artists Jerry Salinas, Mary Qian, and others. She works in a loose and vibrant style using acrylics, oils and other media to add expressive layers of marks and colors. The artist draws on both memories and mythologies as inspiration for the symbols, metaphors and subjects in her work. Flowers and mountains are two subjects where she uses form and color to explore the complexity of emotions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
When is the representation of the thing more important than the thing itself? Dipping in and out of senses, I seek to paint a portrait of a time and place breaking from the canonical representation of a landscape. Trash and treasure alike, intentional looking becomes political in the epoch of hasty consumption. Embedding personal narratives, preferences, and perspectives, (for what can I make without the infusion of my own eye – I…) I am creating a place for myself out of objects and symbols, similar to picking out clouds and naming their shapes. Even if the inception is secret, I share that which brings me joy! From play there is room left for play! I’m interested in the slippery line between image and object! I'm thinking of my artistic lineage! Call it an ode to influence! I seek to make paintings that carry on the torch of all of those who paved the way for my feet. I am situating myself in the decisive moment of now. There is neither good nor bad only those who trumpet such; no thought is original, only my combinations can be different. I am not just searching, but finding. And however the weather has gotten me here (either inside or out), I know that the socks and the breeze that carries them know more than I, have seen more than I, and will continue to carry socks and clouds and tides for longer than I ever could or would or will. I lavish in it, all grinning no bearing. Life gives and I make.

column25: ELISE, born NY 1999, lives and experiences with eyes first. She creates with a level of tactility that brings experience back to that which can be held. In her creative process, She is looking forward, referencing backward, but existing in the now. ELISE’s use of precarious forms and aberrant shapes makes a home of ephemeral oddities. having lived the vast majority of her life in urban landscapes her constructions of safety coexist, and eventually merge, with a present wilderness of something that can’t be described as anything less than femininity. repeatedly subverting the center and carving out space with a knife, hole, or brush creates a new form that is the foundation for her practice. situated on the line between representation, utilitarian, and abstraction her work is her practice is her understanding of the self and the object and the muddy intersection of the two. She owes her understanding of space and balance, as well as the importance of place to having grown up in her parents' architecture office based in her childhood home. by condensing vast regions of time, space, and thought her work aims to invite the viewer in, giving them as much as they ask.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My paintings trace the boundaries of my imagination. Patterned with gestural lines inspired by the abstract expressionists, each piece is a collection of choices that together are alive. My work breathes like a cobweb in the wind - faint, delicate, and ephemeral. No matter the material or discipline, my work is rooted in drawing. This process - drawing, examining, finding a flaw, and making changes - is the heart of my creative practice. Throughout my life, I have floundered with language. Art provided me with a visual vocabulary in which I no longer felt restricted. Drawing unbound my voice. With each line and every stroke, I document my existence. Layers of paper, paint, dust, and glitter are the scars left behind as I strive to express what language denied me. Each painting is a stolen moment of peace and beauty amid the never-ending battle between my dreams and desires, my regrets and pain. My work is a love letter to the girl I was and to everyone who yearns to express what cannot be said with words. Each piece is an imperfect sonnet to the power of imagination and joy of expression.

column25: Williams’s abstract spaces are an ode to an imperfect language. Through motion, she makes marks that translate thought - subconscious – a viscerally understood language. Williams works from her hometown of Chattanooga, Tennessee. In 2009, she received her Bachelor of Fine Arts from Tufts University and the School of the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston. Next, Williams received a full scholarship to complete her Master's in Printmaking in 2012 at SCAD-Atlanta. Williams worked at SCAD‘s Southeastern Center for Printmaking. There, she worked on an ambitious print collection with Kiki Smith and Valerie Hammond. Initially working as an assistant printer, Williams was indispensable to the project and was promoted to assistant project manager. On the heels of this project, Williams founded, managed, and co-owned Straw Hat Press, which specialized in fine art publishing and contract printing. As Straw Hat flourished, Williams realized that despite her success in printmaking, she would always be devoted to her love of drawing. Subsequently, she left Atlanta and returned to her hometown to pursue her career as a gallery artist, where her career soared. In 2022 and 2023, Williams exhibited with PXP Contemporary, Viridian Artists, Van Der Plas Gallery, and the New York Equity Association in NYC. She was the first exhibition and solo at Good Art Co. in South Carolina. She was an artist in residence with Carrie Able Gallery and asked to return for a solo exhibition in the Spring of 2024, when she will also be an artist in residence at Chateau d’Orquevaux in France.

As a kid and teenager, I felt things very strongly and pushed back on convention. Now, I get to focus on these feelings and thoughts, show them to an audience, and get feedback. It's like a dream. I also love creating memorable experiences for people and learning from them. I have realized that children are often my most active viewers, always asking more questions and trying to understand things fully. I hope to inspire and help create places where viewers can access a part of their minds that I feel is so often suppressed in daily life and the academic environment.

column25: My work attempts to place the viewer into a situation that allows them to confront the limits of what we, as humans, know or can understand. By creating experiences where senses are subverted, big ideas and the Western scientific method are questioned, and worlds are made, I attempt to bring the viewer to a part of their brain that is not resolute and is willing to suspend disbelief. By opening our minds to the unknown, not only can we better appreciate each other, the world we live in, and the universe, but also begin to reconsider the place we’ve given ourselves as the benchmark from which else should be measured. Inside the studio, my practice looks like experimenting with materials that are not always conventional. Material exploration and experimentation are crucial to developing conceptual themes, and I rarely shy away from a material that can communicate my ideas best. As I have a varied educational and vocational background, including baking and cooking professionally, I also have a vast tool belt outside the world of conventional sculpting and art materials. Due to the nature of the experiences I aim to create, I often use projection and audio in conjunction with the less-conventional materials to create a fully immersive experience. The research part of my practice is as varied as my material usage but generally involves marine biology, astronomy, other sciences, religious belief systems, philosophical thinking, and the current environmental and political climate. Many of the systems I study can be summarized as tools of human understanding and exploration. My interest in these subjects is tied to my upbringing in that I grew up at the intersection of multiple cultures and had to adapt daily from one culture to the next. I have also lived in and visited many cities and countries, each with its own structures and beliefs, and have discovered that something that feels innate in one culture can exist in its opposite form in another. As a result, as well as because of my natural tendency to question convention, I have opened my mind since I was a young child to simultaneously opposing truths. A fact that allowed me to better empathize with the views of not only other humans but also the other beings, entities, and matter. This unshapable way of thinking has propelled my art practice and my mission to look at, acknowledge, and accept the unknown.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist I am drawn to the power of black and white, and color marks on paper. My figurative work straddles the line between realism and impressionism presenting a unique style that is neither wholly one nor the other. As I work my pieces begin to take on a more expressive quality, with charcoal becoming an increasingly important medium in my art process. Shadows play a prominent role in my work, often surrounding isolated figures and barren landscapes. I am deeply fascinated by the way inanimate objects can evoke memories of times gone by, imbuing the present with a bittersweet sense of nostalgia. Through my work I seek to explore the complexities of the human experience, drafting pieces that are simultaneously beautiful and haunting. My art invites viewers to consider the interplay between light and shadow, the poignancy of memory, and the subtle undercurrents of emotion that shape our lives.

column25: Carmen Schaefer is an accomplished artist with an impressive educational background, holding both an MA in Art History and an MFA in Photography and Drawing. She studied under renowned artists Allan Kaprow at Stony Brook University, Henry Homes Smith at Indiana University and Arnold Gasson at Ohio University. Born into a career Navy family, Carmen lived a life of constant travel and had no “home town'. This peripatetic and isolated upbringing has had a profound impact on her work. Shadows, isolation, and barren landscapes became personal symbols. Memories of times past are evoked by inanimate objects, which have a bittersweet presence. The scene is punctuated with subtle undercurrents of emotions. Sunny imagery may paradoxically envelope a forlorn spirit, waiting for life to happen, as a poignant sense of ‘absence’ pervades the drawing belying the apparent simplicity of the subject. Carmen has exhibited her drawings at over 40 venues and galleries. including the Memorial Art Gallery in Rochester, NY, the Prince Street Gallery in NYC, and the First Street Gallery in NYC. She has won numerous awards for her drawings, including the 33rd Bradley Print and Drawing Exhibition, the 53rd National Drawing and Small Sculpture Show, the 29th International Drawing & Print Competitive Exhibition at Gormley Gallery Notre Dame of Maryland University, The Connecticut Academy of Fine Arts CAFA+ 2021and 2023, the 2020, 2023 American Art Awards and 2023 W0rld Art Awards competing with artists from 62 countries. In addition to her drawings, Carmen’s photography has been exhibited both nationally and internationally, including at the Everson Museum in Syracuse, Alex Ferrone Gallery, Plymouth Centre for the Arts, Rhode Island Center for Photographic Arts, and Texas Photographic Society International Competition to name a few. She has also shown her photographs at PH21 Gallery in Budapest, Hungry. Carmen’s artwork has been featured in Lake Affect magazine (cover), Along Magazine and Spotlight Contemporary Art Magazine. She is an elected member of the Connecticut Academy of Fine Art, a signature member of the Rochester Art Club, a member of Rochester Contemporary Art, a signature member of the the National Association of Women Artists, and Member Artist Circle Arts. Carmen’s unique perspective and upbringing have resulted in artwork that is deeply personal and thought-provoking. Her work can be found in many private collections.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Our lives are based on a concession of volts; the numerics of bars, power, and connection are our confidants and security. Mutilated by tiny cameras, filters, and split screens, and segmented by data, we have ironically become both posthuman and subhuman. My work is about reestablishing the virtue of the physical body. It is a call for algorithmic unfolding that demands return to a practice of presence. Consisting of kinetic sculptures, multi-media installations and video/performance, each piece intentionally creates physically instinctive gaps for viewers. From empty benches to installed reactionary sensors, machines appear to pathetically wait for humanity to return. I believe technology can be utilized to re-humanize by prompting an audience to begin asking not, “What is that thing doing?” but rather, “What am I doing that makes that thing do what it's doing?” This engagement is central to the work and acts as an alarum for societal reboot; where viewers are asked recognize the deep need for humanistic responsibility and presence within a technological and numerically driven culture.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The purpose of my artwork is to visually appreciate my Bolivian and Mexican indigenous roots and its people as revered subjects by reinventing master paintings. Due to how indigenous and latine people are being treated in this society my art contains cultural influences, political, and sociological themes while not directly portraying these issues. The origin of me wanting to portray my Bolivian heritage is to uncover what was lost to me. I don't have any connection to my Bolivian roots so this is my way of reconnecting with my dormant heritage. Not only are my subjects different from the original white american and european depictions, but I will be presenting sexuality beyond a heterosexual lens and exploring the human figure. I want to do my part in putting out into the world what I’d like to see represented. In the illustrative style of J.C. Leyendecker, I’ve depicted the indigenous women of Bolivia called Cholitas, who were once considered subhuman within their own country. It’s in these women that I find a kinship I’ve never explored before. I see myself in them despite never having interacted and I want to find myself in portraying them. With Leyendecker’s style, I enjoy working with oils because I find it more forgiving than acrylic and the application runs streaky. My audience is filled with people who can see themselves in my art. On the other hand, my audience is filled with people who don't care to think critically or perceive injustice in the world-- I want them to look at my work not only to admire and critique, but to think beyond traditional aesthetics.

Like a social observer, based on objectively observing the living environment and society, then I create artworks through subjective thinking. I collect information and clues from the outside world, such as abandoned objects in parks and seemingly unrelated events that happened in New York City in the same month, and associate them with my thoughts on culture, society, politics, and my personal experiences about race and my cultural background. Through intervening in specific situations, such as public spaces, to create artwork from my perspective, I enjoy creating unusual and humorous scenes that reveal hidden critical and political concepts and meanings. I strive for the moment when people become aware of the unexpected realities my project sparks.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
People often leap to a definition about things in terms of what they contain, rather than what they're constructed by. An image, for example, is often understood in terms of the colors it contains within it, rather than pigments and paints that are the real components of the image. Which makes sense, an image is specifically constructed that represent or express some element of life itself and most people wouldn't consider the life they experience as merely imagery; this is merely the real world defining the criteria for how we approach those that imitate it, but are not a part of it. But the supremacy afforded that world by its title holds questionable legitimacy at best and is a confining, hierarchical, and somewhat oppressive attempt to maximize societal cohesion at worst. Our experience is constructed at all times, understanding and engaging with the means of that constant construction in a human way is how we build a more human world.

But I also hope my art will have a social impact: fostering understanding and promoting questioning by depicting connections, divergences, hierarchies, and processes that may not be empirically observable. Generally, my work seeks to represent and potentially transform prevailing structures of power. As a fierce feminist, I want my work to interrogate gendered power and its impact on human agency, opportunity, and dignity. As a committed lover of our planet and all of the lifeforms that occupy it, I want my work to explore anthropogenic impact on the physical and biological world for both good and ill. Central to my work is the motif of transformation, both as a constraining force that can warp the intrinsic dignity of lives but also as progress toward new ways of thinking and doing that can replenish and amplify that dignity.

I’m “~.THE.DoT.”, an Emerging Fine Artist born in Europe, made in New York, & currently creating from the West Coast. My mixed-media art is vibrantly colored & accented with echoes of the Hip Hop culture, reflecting my background in music. I primarily feature dignified women & men from the African diaspora....a melanated fusion of the 'tribe' that raised me. All lovingly brought to life with acrylics & lush materials such as leather, fire glass, 24K Gold leaf, & Swarovski Crystals.

column25: Dorothy “~.THE.DoT.” Wilson is a self-taught, emerging contemporary artist. Born in Europe, made in New York, & currently honing her skills from California. Moved to capture the African Diaspora where they are, her compositions are reflections of our ancestors wildest dreams...a collection of images that are her personal contribution to #THECulture. Recognizing a need in the art world for upscale modern representation, ~.THE.DoT.'s regal images primarily consist of accomplished Black women...usually adorned with the iconic 'DoorKnocker' (Bamboo) earring, which has become a signature presence in her art. Overall, these visuals are a nod to ~.THE.DoT.'s youth....growing up in a never ending love affair with Hip Hop. This is in keeping with the Happy Vibes & Nostalgia that her images evoke! Her Mixed-Media paintings showcase her concentrated application of color, boasting at least 10 coats per hue. She then builds on each piece by utilizing acrylics, oils, leather, Swarovski crystals, glass, 24K gold leaf, & more, with some even taking up to three months to complete. ~.THE.DoT. has successfully completed art commissions for Tyler Perry Studios Executive Mark Swinton, & her original artwork was leased-for-feature in Gabrielle Union's 'Being Mary Jane' Movie (Series Finale), which aired on BET, as well as the STARZ Network's 'Survivor's Remorse', seasons 3 & 4, produced by Lebron James. Additionally, ~.THE.DoT. has been a featured artist in the 'Spectacular Black Girl Art Show' events in Atlanta & Los Angeles, with recent Private Solo Art Shows under her belt. She also serves as Creative Director & Junior Designer for her family run business DRKBLU Industries. On this International Women's Day (3-8-23), ~.THE.DoT. traveled to 'THE HIP HOP MUSEUM' (THHM) in the Bronx, N.Y., with deliveries of her most recent works. A collection of six paintings entitled 'THE HIP HOP FLOWERS, VOL. I', showcasing legendary women Hip Hop execs & artists who are pioneers, honoring their priceless gifts to the now - global genre. Volumes II & III are currently in the works for THHM'S 2025 Grand Opening!

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create fiber-based socially engaging figurative sculptures and installations to engage the viewer to explore the relationship between materials and form. I recontextualize the more feminized traditions and practices of fiber art inverted through the use of industrial materials to explore issues of gender identity, sense of place and purpose, and cultural identity and commonality. My recent focus is on repurposing and up-cycling materials using traditional fiber techniques to create installations of exaggerated clothing, and temporary public art displays. I manipulate texture and form through style combinations including hand knotting, knitting and manually altered machine knitting techniques with crochet. The more compliant fiber materials I use include cotton, wool, mohair, linen, lurex, acrylic, nylon yarns and cotton string and rope. Mastering traditional techniques with more cumbersome materials such as flagging and barricade tapes challenged me to create a new set of tools. I choose to work with all of these materials because of their tactility.

Her meticulous, labor-intensive processes result in fiber-based sculptures and installations. She engages a dialogue that crosses cultural understandings of identity, gender and place. I want to dissolve the conception of the singular self, peel back the rime of perception and catapult the viewer/participant into a state of wonder as I would equate to childhood. To illuminate the true self of the universe is to create art. My work [itself]: the embodied concept, communicates sensuously. You, the observer and participant, are a variation/ extension of the conceptual object. I would hesitate to categorize myself, but to do so I would as a contemporary, neo-expressionist. I find deep inspiration from quantum mechanics, philosophy, studies of consciousness, extraterrestrial beings, meditation, substances, and love.

column25: In the heart of Portland, Oregon, amidst the struggles of single parent/ substance use and the grit of poverty, Xavier Brown found their voice as a queer artist. Raised alongside two younger siblings, Xavier confronted the shadows of neglect and battled the weight of mental illness and abuse. Yet, from the crucible of adversity, a resilient artist emerged. In 2019, Xavier embarked on a transformative journey through the language of street art and digital expression. The concrete walls of the city became their canvas, and the pixels of a screen bore witness to the unspoken chapters of their challenging past. It was the paycheck from Xavier's first job that became the catalyst for change—a small but powerful brushstroke that allowed him to transition from pavement to canvas. With brushes and pigments in hand, Xavier began a new chapter, painting not only images but a story of triumph over wounds and a testament to the protocols he crafted to become a better human. Each stroke is a narrative of authenticity, an invitation to witness the extraordinary power of transformation through the lens of Xavier Brown's art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The goal of Adventuring Art is to reconnect people with nature. While there are many formats involved with maintaining a social media account, my physical art remains traditional and is inspired by real people and events in my life. As a lover of nature, the goal is to capture people's adventurous spirit, and help re-establish a more healthy relationship with nature. Each painting is meant to communicate something to someone, from an outsider's perspective, and create a sort of language that can hopefully be recognized by anyone. I hope by keeping my techniques traditional, I am able to capture an authentic style, as the majority of the visionary movement and surrealist movements have taken other directions. I believe that creating art from our brains is a skill like riding a bike, and we get better at studying real life and capturing it on canvas. I believe that digital assistance, and modern techniques have led the visionary artist movement away from exercising this part of the brain and learning these skills, thus limiting their creativity and often producing collage type cartoons and lifeless portraits in place of what I consider to be 'visionary' art. I hope that my visions will help others explore these parts of their mind, and remind them of their roots in nature.

column25: David Brothers is a traditional visionary surrealist. Living as a homeless person for over 10 years he travelled to most of the popular cities in the U.S. distributing his paintings on as many First Friday art walks as possible. He then began applying for artist residencies and has slowly created a portfolio of almost 100 traditional acrylic paintings among other personal projects and public murals and installations. He is now in southern Oregon caretaking a property for a non-profit community association and event center with a physical workshop for Adventuringart.com and home studio. At a time when traditional art techniques are becoming 'obsolete' in the commercial world with A.I. and other software and digital technologies; Dave is committed to making 'actual' traditional visionary art from brain to canvas without the use of digital assistance, photos, or any models or visual reference, and he believes this is a skill that shouldn't be forgotten.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is exactly the same as what 15 year old me thought it would be when I made the decision of pursuing a career in art. Freedom. When I was 15, I hated everything about my life and what I feared it would become if I continued down the path of what the people around me said were required. In an attempt to free myself of this fear, I started pursuing art as my only hope to live without the constant pressure of teachers and bosses. As I am now a fresh college graduate and full time artist, this thought remains the same. Freedom is what being an artist is all about. It is however a hard earned freedom. The reward is so great because the work is so hard. The structure of our society is not friendly to artists, this we know, but our responsibility is to continue telling young people to pursue their artistic dreams despite what their fears tell them. Your connection to art is forever, and it will grow with you.

column25: Days before his death, my father Dr. Robert L. Douglas—a brilliant painter and art history scholar—said, “Being an artist will break your heart.” As dire as the statement sounds, it landed as a moment of levity. “Now you tell me,” I responded, and we laughed. We both realized it was too late for both of us: he as the painter with a highly-anticipated career retrospective he would not live to see, and me, following in his footsteps in my own way as a visual artist, poet, and fellow professor. I am a firm believer in the idea that art enhances your quality of life. I am a better person for creating art and, by sharing my gifts for teaching and creating with others, I improve the quality of life for every community I encounter. Admittedly, it’s a lofty goal but the difficulty has never been a deterrent. Especially when you take the time to make the dreams in your head materialize and witness positive reactions to what your imagination reveals. In that sense, being an artist for me is both joy and duty. My intent is for my viewer to find my many interests and identities interacting in each collage. The onomatopoeia of comic books that sparked my creativity at a young age is also a nod to my work as a poet and the textual-based art of graffiti that was a significant part of my nurture in Hip-Hop culture. My subjects share my African-American ancestry and are often depicted in black and white against a backdrop of color. The color contrast draws more attention to my subjects and hints at a history that predates the backdrop they inhabit. It’s an aesthetic that speaks to the necessity of not only acknowledging history but appreciating what we find in its truths.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being born and raised in California, I've had the benefit a wide variety of landscapes all within a couple of hours drive. Quiet forests, chaparral scrub, deserts, and rugged coastlines filled my childhood. A world without social media, I instead spent time in the backyard flipping logs and rocks and trying to make vivariums out of milk jugs for the creepy crawly things I found. The majority of my work does, in some way, center around nature. Especially the ocean and the desert. The places I spent the most time in, and both some of the most fragile ecosystems there are. I want people to see the charm in some of the creatures I depict. Rockfish can be called ugly, but aren't they also kinda cute? And did you even know they lived right under your nose? Even the fantastical stuff, the aliens and the fantasy creatures have a foundation in my love of plants, animals, and the way things fit together in an ecosystem. I don't just want my aliens and beasts to look interesting, I want them to look like they live and continue living even when you're not watching anymore. I want to bridge the gap between fantastic and mundane. I don't try to or want to limit myself to one medium. I want to keep trying new things that make new aesthetics and require me to learn and try and fail. With that said, my favorite things to play with by far are in 2 dimensions. Drawing, painting, pyrography. There's something magical about making a whole universe that only exists on the canvas or panel or slab of wood. Like looking through a window. And I think that's what I want. When someone looks at something I made, I want it to be a window to a different place. Be that a different universe, or just a piece of this one that you didn't think about before. If I can evoke a small fraction of wonder or curiosity in a viewer, I have succeeded in my goal.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I grew up in Socialist Bulgaria. Which means I grew up an atheist and scientific materialist. For most of my life questions of meaning, truth, and purpose were to be answered by philosophy (which I pursued as an academic career early on). Art was also useful in my struggles in some ways: as a form of therapy, as a tool for imagining radical social change, and as a way to explore the limits of human experience and test what’s on the other side of those limits. Eventually I became disenchanted with academia and gave it up: I wanted to live life as an artwork, not a career. So I did. And along the way, I discovered that drugs and alcohol can be just as effective as a brush or a pen when one wants to find the hard truth about oneself. I also discovered that unlike a brush or a pen, drugs and alcohol tend to siphon one's life in an ever-tightening spiral of hangover-blastoff-blackout-hangover. At some point I realized had to change the direction of my efforts. What followed was not irrelevant but is also not pretty so I’ll fast forward to a time when, after having managed to clean up my act for quite a few years, I was thrown into a series of personal experiences that fundamentally changed my understanding of life, the world, and my place in it. I believe this is probably what people with mortgages refer to as a “midlife crisis”. To me it meant I discovered I had lived all my life in a world of two dimensions. And now suddenly spiritual realities were no longer a matter of belief or skepticism since I was experiencing them directly - a vibrant, illuminating, and at times maddeningly confusing life in 3D. I was lucky enough to stay out of mental institutions and channeled my experiences into cultivating an art practice. This time the purpose of art is completely different: a way of pointing to such realities with the hope that others will be guided to catch a glimpse of the Things that are not just things. Art deals with internal truth - the sphere of the subjective. And art can extend the spectacular achievements of science - the sphere of external objective truth - to enrich our collective human experience. In art there is no argument, no one asks to prove a poem. We don't need to discard reason to move forward - an equation is perfectly useful when one needs to calculate the bill. But life is not a spreadsheet and we can't stop there. We need to stop talking about “natural resources” and “the limits of growth” as if the Earth is a closed rational system. We know we are not. So we need to venture beyond. We need to meet Life or Earth or the Mystery with our hearts. And hopefully not lose our minds in the process. Even science has embraced complexity, uncertainty, chaos - all words for the Mystery. The outer limits of objective truth trace the fuzzy silhouettes of Things perceived when one feels called to look inside. My hope is that my works can serve as signposts along the way for those who feel the call.

column25: oro8oro is an artist born and raised in Bulgaria. After emigrating to New York, he dropped out of college to pursue an unhealthy lifestyle as a DJ in the early 2000s. His early interests in art all but vanished after witnessing the devastating effects of consumer capitalism on the integrity of art practice. Seeking subversiveness, he explored street art but had to confine it to stenciling posters for fear of getting deported (he was undocumented at the time). Another fear (that art is not a sustainable career) prevented him from ever calling himself an artist, despite collaborating on many occasions with people who had no such qualms. Eventually, in the early 2020s, now fully documented and feeling inspired by the unwavering support of his partner, he embraced the idea of being a professional artist and began developing his practice. He now works in a variety of media and no longer seeks subversiveness but transcendence. In retrospect, that may have been what he had been seeking all along. Or maybe not.

column25: I am Yimei(Emair) Zhu, an interdisciplinary artist weaving together the realms of Art, Disability, and the potential of Posthuman Bodies. My work challenges and resists stereotypes associated with disability, exploring deficiencies and imperfections from an unconventional perspective. I invite viewers to see disabilities not as limitations but as unique modes of experiencing the world. Art, in my view, is a powerful means to tackle complex challenges, creating connections across various disciplines. I delve into the sensory systems of plants and animals, exploring nature's intricate networks that extend beyond human perception. My endeavor is to expand the boundaries of human senses, opening up new possibilities for perception. Employing a broad spectrum of media, from traditional writing and imagery to the avant-garde realms of coding, interactive installations, game design, and bio-art, my goal is to upend conventional narratives about disability and use art as a catalyst for understanding and innovation. My vision propels art beyond simple expression. It's about forging new ways of sensing, feeling, and interacting with our world by integrating technology with our corporeal existence. I aim to cultivate a community where art and technology mutually enhance one another, providing a space for contribution and growth for all, irrespective of ability. I believe artists should envision a future in which technology is no longer regarded as lifeless tools separated from our minds and our bodies. I aspire to use art and technology to challenge people who are no longer satisfied with the world we have known. I want to provide a possibility—humanity with new bodies and senses—to further explore a new world we have never envisioned before or rediscover the senses we have forgotten.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work gets pulled from an unwavering unconscious area in my mind. I've been artistically inclined since I was a child and creating has been apart of my whole life. I started with the bare minimum. Crayon and paper than to pencil and paper. I was set at mimicking detail for a large period of my young adult life. From the ages of 18 to a little as much of 2 years ago I did nothing but realistic charcoal drawings. Then I was introduced to 3d digital medium. And now I funnel my energy through the expression of abstracting and in some ways destroying or disfigurement of the human anatomy which in return touches on the idea of our mortality and the human experience. I use 3d sculpting software as my blank canvas. Most of my figures are sculpted out of pure unconscious movements. I do not sketch out or map out before hand what the figure will look like. In some ways always creating new life. This new personality that will become abstracted or disfigured. Realism is still prevalent in my digital work. Skin texture and a clean render is definitely a set goal. The digital sculpture is then exported out to illustrator where a new layer of aesthetics can be applied. I then implement shapes and surrounding objects that bring some harmony and placement to the figure. Giving life to a more set composition. Photoshop is introduced in order to set tone and mood through editing and color grading techniques to overall polish my vision. The one element that I can say I actually think out and deliberately do is the ethnicity of my figures. I have chosen to always make my figures of a black decent. Growing up as a child you are exposed to a predominantly non-ethnic media. From my favorite superheroes to my Saturday cartoons. I grew up emulating and drawing people who do not look like me. So at a certain point I decided that it was my responsibility to make sure I create art that represents people who look like me and my family, allowing for us to be represented more within the art world. This in return will allow for the next generation of artists that look like me to feel more comfortable with exploring and creating art that represents people of color.

column25: Hello, I'm a 3D digital Artist/ Designer that works in a wide range of mediums from traditional to 2d digital and 3d digital mediums. I create animated videos fully produced by me from sound effects to the soundtrack and develop motion graphic content. I was born in New York City on April of 1989 but relocated here to Miami at the age of 5. I'm of Afro Latino decent and representation in my work is a beautiful priority that I take full responsibility of. For a long time I didn’t believe in being visually seen as an Artist, it was the thing I furthest wanted to stay away from. There was no need for the audience to know who I am or even what I looked/ or sounded like. For what? My Art speaks for itself. The viewer is the integral piece to breathing life into my work. But as time has gone by, as I get older and extend further into the man I will die as, I’ve found out that I have misunderstood myself greatly as an Artist. That my words carry value. I am my father’s son and it is important my loved ones and anyone new to experiencing my work sees him in me and possibly my work. So ultimately I don’t mind if the audience does not understand me, it’s way more valuable for me not to misunderstand or mischaracterize myself. Being more visual as an Artist and or showing my face/ expressing myself through words, will accompany in making that connection not only for others, but more importantly for myself.

column25: Ryan 'Revel' Nguyen is a 26 year-old muralist based out of Orange County, California with 10+ years of experience in street art. At a young age, Ryan was inspired by stylized letters his mother drew for one of her classes she taught and was later introduced to the Graffiti Scene by older friends. Since then, Ryan has transitioned over the years from graffiti to large-scale public murals. The transition has been a process of bringing beauty to something once considered to be destructive. At 13 years old, Ryan was unfortunately caught up in the chaos of graffiti and ended up being sent to a school for troubled teens. There, the Book of Revelations caught his attention. As he progressed with his craft, he decided to use the alias 'Revel' as a homage to this finding. Now, 13 years later, he has come to an understanding of his love for the art - a balance of emotion and creativity - a way to deal with our complex human emotions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist, I find that one of the most wonderful things is being able to be inspired to create from a deep love of nature I had as a young child. At that time, I liked to immerse myself in dreams, look up at the sky, feel the breeze, and observe the leaves swaying in the air. These wonderful experiences not only become my creative inspiration, but also inspire my unique understanding of the beauty of nature. My personal experiences also provide a strong foundation for my art. Born during the Chinese Cultural Revolution, this period of history profoundly affected my growth and way of thinking, providing motivation for me to pursue my artistic dreams and seek free expression in the future. At that time, I was not only a pursuer of the wonderful power of nature, but also a participant in the times. Throughout my artistic journey, I have experienced a natural transition from painter to performance artist. During this period, my creative medium turned to my mobile phone, and this small device became a tool for me to record my life. I began to use the method of 'making between' instead of the traditional 'drawing between' to record the subtle moments in life. This process not only allowed me to use my mobile phone to record subtle moments in life, but also made me realize that I have truly become an artist. My work becomes a work of art that documents the interconnectedness of different things, captures subtle contradictions, and transforms abstractions from reality into new possibilities. My dissatisfaction with reality does not prevent me from maintaining a soulful interest in reality, because I hope to discover new possibilities through abstraction in reality. My interest in things around me, like feathers on top, bank balance slips extended from cash machines, boards with staples, etc., is where my artistic inspiration comes from. Through my works, I try to capture and present the space represented by the “in-between” and the mysterious power contained therein. I use minimalist and accidental aesthetic principles to integrate the intertwined energy of 'between' into my living state. This is a manifestation of profound reflection on life. Through observation and creation, it guides viewers to experience the relationship between different things. Mysterious energy, and a new way of thinking about the world. My artistic journey is not only a personal creative process, but also an adventure exploring the gap between reality and fantasy, existence and nothingness. During this journey, I learned the art of appreciating the exquisite beauty in daily life, and also realized that the existence of 'between' and 'between' is implicit, permeating and surrounding everyone's daily life. Therefore, my work is not only a visual expression, but also a meditative resonance. By capturing the beauty of “in between,” I hope viewers can experience more connections between things in their daily lives, as well as those exquisite, poetic moments. This is the original intention of my creation and my unique response to art. In the days to come, I will continue to use art to perceive and express life, continue to pay attention to the existence of the 'in-between', and look for more possibilities in it. Through my works, I hope to guide the audience to think and feel that in the infinite 'in-between' of contemporary times, each of us is looking for a unique and beautiful existence. This is a journey of constant discovery in which I will continue to grow and improve.

column25: I carefully study the world around me while striving to understand something that may transcend it. I am passionate about wandering between different things, with a keen interest in light, shadow, and visual perceptions. I enjoy using them to capture the unquantifiable dimensions of the real world. My approach involves utilizing the material structure and the straightforward transformation of perception to achieve a surreal presence that can be sensed by the body, bridging the gap between what is known and what is felt. By contrasting different things, I seek to attain maximum tension and energy. I craft my works through meditation and tranquility, extending them beyond art into my life, transcending the boundaries between art and life. I delve into the construction of digital videos, focusing on elements that resonate with us. I concentrate on the paradoxical capacity of digital technology as a non-material medium and its contradiction with vivid materiality and the body. I attempt to explore possibilities within this contradiction, contemplating the impact of automation, digitization, and technological advancements on our personal lives, reality, consciousness, and self-concept. This paradox becomes one of the central themes in my creative endeavors. My body navigates between various social institutions, cultures, and technological realms, traversing boundaries across different domains. I envision my body as a vague container, akin to a shadow, which can be confirmed through digital means. I place this sense of existence behind working devices and cameras, connecting it with my consciousness through the energy of breath and movement. This ensures that even if the body disappears, the shadow persists, not destroyed but liberated from the flesh, allowing me to transcend temporal and spatial constraints. The shadow becomes critically significant in my creative process, symbolizing a profound exploration of existence, consciousness, and self-awareness. I traverse the realms of the real and the imaginary, the artificial and the natural, aiming to reveal their relationships and express the connections between them. Through such expressions, I hope to suggest the gradients, transformations, swings, grafts, crossovers, and overlays present in these relationships that may bring about new realms of existence. I use overlapping, moving images, detailed fragments, and repeated actions, combined with computer-generated animation, to create a surreal, minimalist, and incidental experience. I focus on the interweaving of images and experiences, avoiding any literal definitions, silently presenting scenes from overlapping aspects of life. It's a sense of makeshift composition formed by fragments and pieces, seeking vague poetics in ordinary objects and familiar movements, creating a space for pause, confirmed by the body between the distances of sensation and experience, establishing new relationships with everyday life. I believe in the materiality of shadows; they can break all boundaries. I attempt to develop my own way of measuring shadows, creating my view of history and life processes. Such 'shadows' can genuinely consist of playfulness, faith, and mechanical interaction, focusing on the creation itself as the purpose. Currently, I compress time through imagery, slowing down to experience life, connecting the subtle vibrations with contemplative thoughts. Understanding how time unfolds, transforming into a form or numerical structure. When immersed in time, much like in the natural realm of the digital world, it allows me to pause or reset my relationship with the environment. My works aim to blend the reality of survival with the universal experience. By transforming daily life into dynamic images, injecting vitality into them, and merging this vitality into my state of existence, I emphasize the intertwined relationship between life and art. Through the dynamics of various life scenes, I seek to infuse this interwoven energy into my creations, presenting the genuine expression of art within life. Through the contrasts and overlays of different dynamics, I experience things and spaces that have not occurred, along with the accompanying confusion and hope. My works are intended to evoke associations with the 'in-between' life in specific formalized forms, a reality that, though intangible, exists in our daily lives. This reality lacks clear contours and boundaries, continually expanding in an imperceptible manner. It is omnipresent, able to permeate and surround the lives of each individual. Unlike familiar things, the 'in-between' is not fragile; it exists in an intangible and unexpected manner. I aim to create a precarious relationship between objects and art. My goal is to touch upon the audience's sensitivity to the unconscious. I hope to slow down the passage of time, change the distance and position of observing things, focus on the relationship between things and their opposites, and re-examine our connections with the world and hidden possibilities. By comparing and recombining different elements, my works create an uncertain poetic existence, focusing on whether mysterious forces from distant horizons exist and if we can perceive signs of supernatural existence, challenging the audience's imagination. I aspire to break their established perceptions of the world and habitual thinking, allowing them to feel the infinite beauty of details and the joy of contemplation. I expect the audience to re-focus on our connections with the world and deeply contemplate the reasons and purposes of their own existence.

column25: Shingo Yamazaki’s work addresses the complexities of cultural hybridity, identity, and the meaning of “home.” He draws upon a mixture of cultural nuances, iconography, and personal history inspired by his upbringing in Honolulu, Hawaiʻi and more recently his migration to Los Angeles. By intertwining personal history with the familiar rhythms of daily life, Yamazaki transforms the concept of home into a vessel of collective identity, inviting viewers to partake in these dialogues of everyday existence. Yamazaki delves into a unique perspective, a journey defined by the lack of generational knowledge in intertwined with the intricacies of Hawaiʻi’s diverse culture. His discovery of his Korean heritage at a later age unraveled generational trauma within his family, shaped by their history as Koreans living in Japan. This profound revelation, goes beyond the surface exploration of cultural influences.. The work serves as acceptance of this lack of information and a realization that it's perfectly valid to occupy the in-between, where multiple versions of oneself coexist. Yamazaki is deeply intrigued by effects of untold stories that have been obscured across generations. These narratives, which older generations felt compelled to hide, have often manifested as generational trauma and anxieties, subtly woven into the broader narrative. Rather than exploring a history that has been unspoken and vague, his work becomes a journey of filling in the gaps, of mending the voids, through connecting with community delving into the rich, diverse culture of Hawaiʻi. Thin, veil-like, transparent layers of paint act as both a physical layer of history and a symbolic tool to merge figure and objects. Meticulous arrangements push and pull the viewer to unravel hidden meanings beneath the surface of the cherished and the mundane, while tracing their intricate connection to the complex mosaic of identity. The visual tensions between household objects and the occupants they inhabit reflect the subconscious struggle with selective memory, traumas, and the erasures of memory and history. These tensions, conveyed through varying opacities as interjections, craft a compelling visual narrative that both allows and denies access to the observer. In this interplay, Yamazaki constructs spaces that transcend the limitations of mere settings. Each scene becomes an embodiment of an entirely new reality, a plausible history reconstructed through his personal lens and internalized memories. These spaces, as they unfold, offer as much as they withhold from the viewer, leaving them suspended in the realm between memory and forgetfulness—a reflection of the ever-shifting landscape of personal and collective recollection. Through his evocative creations, Yamazaki beckons viewers to traverse the boundaries of their own memories, questioning the fragments that shape their understanding of self and place. This body of work acts as a conversation between the past and the present within a liminally psychological space.

column25: While I work on site and from life whenever possible, I also enjoy exploring the “what if” view of our world, alternative facts, based on imagination and dream world. Images often begin as dreams from random splotches or quizzical juxtapositions, emerging in layers of paint. These are then studied for themes and stories that wish to emerge. I am learning how to enlist bystanders - either friends and family or members of the public, to help me find the hidden images. Sometimes this pushes me into subjects or juxtapositions that are not of my choosing, which requires trust and surrender of full control. I try not to veto most ideas and to let them grow into their own meaning. The best ones have a story to tell, or better yet - more than one story. Similar in style and process to the image samples given with this application, I am presently beginning on my biggest version yet of this painting process that I have nicknamed “The Blobs”. I have built up several layers of random drips, smears and splotches on a 6’x12’ un-stretched canvas. I will then have 12 days this January in a local studio residency where I have use of a large wall for the canvas and where I can invite the public to come help me discover what the painting wants to be, using charcoal for visitors to sketch. The loosely-themed working title of “Dream Big” will hopefully encourage some positive thoughts and perhaps a few nightmares as well.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The only thing constant in life is change, and yet so many things in life are repetitious or even sometimes stagnant. When i look at the world around me i see humans as being stuck in a loop. When i explore my own loops, my thoughts, habits, actions that become repetitive, i notice that novelty, engrossment or flow are ways out of the trap. There are a few different ways that i use art to offer unstuckness. Drawing from my time making props for the circus, i fill my surroundings with the backdrop for a strange and magical story to unfold. I make things that transform into other things, i craft narrative illustrations, i invite collaboration, improvisation. On the more serious side, i’ve begun harvesting my own pigments and making paints from earth and non toxic things - we have to get unstuck from the patterns of pollution, and i have to be accountable too. In all of this it is my aim to grow through the work that i do, to evolve and become a better version of myself and thru that process grow in my capacity to generate more lightness of being around me. I still have a lot of growing to do, but i can imagine it, so if i can imagine being loving and kind, perhaps i can also create more loving kindness in the world.

column25: Maris Van Vlack is an interdisciplinary artist whose work bridges fiber art and painting practices. Using traditional textile techniques, she constructs tactile images that reference topography, geology, and generational memory. Her work is primarily hand-woven, consisting of panels of fabric that are slowly built up thread by thread, trapping memory and history in the sedimentary process. After hand-weaving the base tapestry, she layers the surface with drawn, painted, and stitched marks, and incorporates areas of industrial jacquard weaving and stoll knitting. Each piece is a balance between the materiality of the fibers and the atmosphere created within the deep pictorial space. The landscapes that appear in the work depict architectural spaces from her family’s history, referencing photographs of places where family members lived in Europe that were destroyed during World War II. Her work is a window through which to see layers of time and memory, depicting the space that exists between the past and the present.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Through immersive installations, photography, image-based work, and text, my work explores memory, identity, loss, melancholia, and recovery in the context of family history and surrounding community. Frequently employing family and community archival material for the basis of my work, I raise questions about the things we inherit: physical, mental, and emotional. How can we reinvent our lives, our pasts, our narratives? Focusing on ruptures, fragments, and historical residue, I show how various forms of loss—from the personal to communal—can be regenerative, serving as the connective tissue to the past. Ephemeral elements of light, movement, and temporality are ongoing themes in my work to engage in mindfulness, reflection, and the sublime. My work centers around the preservation of narratives, modes of healing, and uncovering obscure histories. I’m interested in preservation and healing—a sensitive, painful, laborious, and deliberate process. Through the physical and conceptual act of recovery, I recreate worlds by using interventions, disrupting linear readings of the past, and bridging the gaps. My work transforms archival artifacts and remnants from the past by re-presenting them to both reveal and reimagine the history it holds. I am processing and creating work about the stages of loss, as outlined by poet Kevin Young: reckoning, regret, remembrance, ritual, recovery, redemption. Through this process, I am finding alternate ways to move forward with my relationship to the past—one that is not consumed by it nor disavows it. My work is immersed in a state of melancholia, occupying liminal space. The inability to part with loss is the desire to retain it and keep it alive. This calls for a sustained and deep connection with the object of loss, whether it is physical or emotional, which in turn can bring fulfillment. Instead of perceiving loss as a burden, I choose to recognize it as the connection to the past. Currently, I’m contemplating how we can regain control over loss through digital manipulations, material explorations of layering, transparency/opaqueness, il/legibility, and transferal processes. How can we find redemption in the everyday?

By taking the monotony of daily life and her love for nature as subject matter, Cynthia often produces work using creative tactics. Her artworks/portraiture are characterised by the use of everyday objects in an atmosphere of middleclass mentality in which recognition plays an important role. By examining the ambiguity and origination via retakes and variations, she tries to increase the dynamic between audience and author by objectifying emotions and investigating the duality that develops through different interpretations. Her works sometimes omit the complete structure. This results in the fact that the artist can easily imagine an own interpretation without being hindered by the historical reality. By applying abstraction, she creates intense personal moments masterfully created by means of rules and omissions, acceptance and refusal, captivating her Audience. column25: Cynthia Blumberg, a New York Native, is a self-taught Photographer and Digital Artist, who is best known for turning mundane moments into expressive works of Art. As a child living in Florida, Cynthia utilized the gift of Art as a coping Mechanism, through difficult circumstances. Cynthia continued to examine works by well-known Artists, and use watercolor, pencil, and marker to practice working on shapes and mixing colors. When Cynthia moved back to New York in her early 20s, she became captivated by Photography. She loved the idea of being able to “pause” moments, and have a visual to look back on. She began taking photos with her phone everywhere she went, even stopping while crossing busy Streets in the middle of red lights, where it was safe to do so. Cynthia especially loved taking skewed photos of park benches and flowers after Therapy Sessions. In 2016, when Cynthia left Nursing School at the age of 27, Cynthia began to realize that her love for Photography could turn into her next Career Endeavor. Though she could not afford to purchase a Professional camera, she didn’t let that become a barrier. She took memorable shots with her phone throughout the City, posting them on Instagram, and gaining almost one thousand followers. Later on in 2018, Cynthia decided to focus her energy, and lens, on Portrait Photography. She began recruiting friends to be Models, and taking trips with them around the Tri-State Area to do photo shoots. Though Cynthia was still unable to purchase a camera, she was very determined to create stunning images with a phone, natural light, scenic backgrounds, incredible Models, and her self-taught skills as a Director. She was able to accomplish this, as one of her Models launched a Career as a Professional Model, using her photos to build his portfolio. Cynthia also provided her Models an outlet, allowing them to tell their stories. After learning the mood that they wanted to convey, Cynthia would collaborate with her Models on planning everything from wardrobe, to new locations, to how she posed her Subjects when she wasn’t taking candid shots. In 2019, Cynthia joined Fountain House, a Psychosocial Clubhouse in Manhattan, and also joined their Art Gallery. She sold her first piece in November of 2019, at her first show, “Mad About Art.”. When the COVID-19 Pandemic broke out in 2020, Cynthia experienced the loss of her beloved stepdad, and seldom took photos of her surroundings. She was able to sell Art on Artsy through shows held by the Fountain House Gallery online, but her interest in Photography was dwindling. In 2021, Cynthia began attending Meetings with the Fountain House Studio, via Zoom. There, she noticed one Artist in particular who was zealous about creating Digital Art, and his confidence caught her attention. Cynthia wanted to feel that spark again, so she started exploring different Art Apps on her phone, and then her iPad. Cynthia was able to showcase her new Digital Pieces on Zoom Meetings with her Studio audience, and garner feedback that helped her improve her skills over time. In the Summer of 2021, Cynthia sold her first Digital Piece. In October of 2021, Cynthia was awarded an Artist Grant through NYFA, and hosted and curated a Virtual Art Show, dedicated to Essential Workers. In 2022, Cynthia was accepted to the La Mama Galleria, with a Digital Piece titled: “And We Dance”. She was also accepted to showcase work at the Hudson Guild Gallery, with a curated selection of five photographs and Digital works. In June of 2022, Cynthia relocated back down to Florida, as she was losing her mobility, due to severe scoliosis. Due to limited mobility, and using a wheelchair, Cynthia cannot physically capture photos at angles and heights that she once did. She now takes photos for fun, taking shots of friends, outings, and sometimes helping her mother photograph homemade blankets. Cynthia has been networking with members of the Art Community in Florida, and continues to sharpen her skills as a Digital Artist. She is resolute on not giving up in telling her story through any Media that is available to her, because it’s not over yet!

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artistic journey, I've traversed various career paths, realizing that a life without a fulfilling passion was incomplete. Heartbreak, a universal human experience, has shaped my art, stemming from romantic relationships to the loss of my mother. Art became my language for expressing the hardships of protecting and reopening the heart. Symbolism plays a crucial role in my work, particularly through animal parts representing strength and protection. Antlers, regardless of gender associations, adorn my art, challenging traditional categorizations of masculinity and femininity. We all embody both aspects, transcending stereotypes. The heart symbolizes love and resilience. After enduring heartbreak, reopening ourselves to love becomes a challenging journey. My work explores how to protect while embracing vulnerability, navigating the balance between strength and softness. Malleable metals serve as my canvas, transformed into bold statement pieces that bridge sculpture and jewelry. Life-size bronze sculptures showcase my creations, providing an 'outer body' experience for observers. Through the fusion of human form and nature, my sculptures offer a transcendent experience, inviting viewers to appreciate the interconnected beauty of masculine and feminine qualities. My goal is to inspire individuals to appreciate the entirety of the human experience, embracing both masculine and feminine attributes. In wearable and standalone sculptures, I aim to convey empowerment, creating art that resonates with strength and beauty when admired or worn.

column25: Rebecca Wallace was born in Bellflower, California. She originally studied fashion design at Long Beach City College and then continued her education in San Francisco, California. After moving to Santa Cruz, California, she appreciated what that the fashion industry had to offer, but decided to study Early Childhood Education at Cabrillo College in Aptos, California. Feeling disconnected from her art, she delved into many jewelry and sculpture courses at Cabrillo College. Falling in love with craftsmanship, Rebecca decided to pursue higher education in the Arts. Rebecca’s counselor encouraged her to apply to San Jose State University’s Spatial Art Program. Rebecca was compelled to expand her knowledge of metalwork. Rebecca was accepted in the Fall of 2018 and graduated with her BFA with an emphasis in Metalsmithing in the Spring of 2021.

column25: I use high and low-quality art historical, archaeological, and personal references to understand the desire to connect ourselves to the ultimately unreachable past, and forge a livable path under climate change anxieties. Peripheral influences like ancient graffiti, extraterrestrial curiosities, universal collapse theories, and common mourning practices all begin to represent the allure of half-knowing something while feeling intimately close to it. I am interested in the impulses, nervousness, desires and guiding forces that cause us to seek out relationships to history, and the way these forces inform our present-day relationships. Membranes that divide different kinds of transition states appear frequently in the work, speaking about time, death, and the 'ushers' that move freely between states. Sea birds have become a visual motif in recent years, floating through dark and glistening spaces as one kind of “usher”, due to their Jurassic heritage. Layering, density, texture, and devices of obstruction all work to slow the act of viewing and induce cathartic sensations of digging, burying and magnifying. The work takes form in fiber, sculpture, painting, drawing, installation, and audio. My recent hoard of research ranges from Astrophysical theories of the ultimate end to the universe, to more intimately-scaled poetry by Ocean Vuong and Ellen Bass. My work tries to cope with apocalyptic anxieties and the effects of a future-focused mindset that snowballs into the unknowable future.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Is my body imagined? Uncanny marriages of material morph into sculptural bodies, video monsters, or drawn surrogates who perform for you as both self and other. Flapping over, tucking in, and spilling out; their voluptuous forms flex, sag, and flinch. I’m acutely aware of my body as it's imagined in the minds of others, and I generates my own imagined bodies to counteract this ‘othering’. These proxies are acts of self-imaging. A boundless contemporary framework of identity, or ‘self’, is defined as something which is both experiential and socially programmed—an ever-metastasizing contradiction which leaves little room for me. Me: the subject and the object. Me: the vibrating desire between. Whether upholstering velure folds into sculptures, inflating saggy pink balloons, or extruding colon-shaped clay tubes, I question how much of this body—my body—is fundamentally my own. Phasing through pixels, or clay, or latex, my work asks: is your body real?

column25: E. Elhoffer is an interdisciplinary artist who makes bizarre and visceral work. They earned their undergraduate degree from Kansas City Art Institute and are an MFA candidate at the Sam Fox School of Art & Design at Washington University. They live and work in St. Louis, Missouri, where they actively grapple with political adversity regarding their nonmale, nonbinary body. Elhoffer exhibits in galleries, museums and institutions nationally and internationally. Elhoffer's work deals with themes that affect their body, such as the contemporary gaze cast upon it and mind-body dysphoria. Their pieces examine the ways in which societal norms, virtual space, and power structures shape one’s understanding of their bodily identity. Through their art, Elhoffer invites viewers to question their own assumptions and consider alternative ways of living within one's own flesh.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice is activated through the lens of expressionism and the passing of time. In my assemblage work, the natural entropy of objects draws me in and it is from this pull that I am attracted to discover how things - made by humans - take on another life after their intended use falters. When working with the figure, I gravitate toward emotional expression to convey a concept usually summed up in a written slogan placed in juxtaposition to an active body in space. I am inspired by comic books, graphic novels, and bumper stickers in their ability to relay a quick message to be considered from many points of view. Recently my work has evolved to interweave the solitary practice of assemblage, deconstructing and reconstructing disparate objects, into the social practice of assembly of people into experiential situations with the intent to reveal how form informs our perceptions and how those biases can be remade into new narratives. The work aims to invite curiosity and reflection through sensory experiences to affect change and to seek alternatives to open doorways and invite exploration. I am motivated by curiosity and inquiry. Living in an experimental 'what if' with shapes, mediums, discarded objects, and expressing our relationship to them. My work reflects my love of the truth of shape and how form informs us. I am in a constant search to discover new life in the unseen liminal places.
