column25: Adrienne De Guevara (she/her/ella) De Guevara Art Studios located in Goleta, California is driven by the experimental inquiry with shapes, mediums, and discarded objects. De Guevara combines self-taught and formal training in visual and performing arts to achieve a unique expression outside of any specific pedagogy. De Guevara’s performance work has been presented in Santa Barbara, CA, San Francisco, CA, New York City, and Putney, VT. De Guevara co-founded Crux Productions, a theater and film production company in Brooklyn, NY, was co-creator of Mister Sister, an Avant-Vaudeville comedy duo performing in night clubs in Manhattan, front woman for Shakti Pops, an electronic-kirtan band, and has experience as a director and lighting technician on various projects in New York City and Vermont. For the last three years she has co-created Masq(p)arade!, an annual performance progressive in collaboration with Pianos on State in Santa Barbara, CA. She is also the co-creator of the Kinetic Cake Express slated to happen in April of 2024 and is a primary collaborator at MISC Workshop in Santa Barbara, CA. This past fall De Guevara was granted an Individual Fellowship Grant as an Emerging Artist from the California Arts Council. Currently, De Guevara works as the Programs and Fund Development Officer for the Santa Barbara Arts Collaborative, serves on the board of the Children’s Creative Project, and is an avid community organizer focused on the arts.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Joan Belmar was born in Santiago, Chile in 1970. He left Chile for Spain at the age of 24. He began painting professionally in Spain, using the Catalan name Joan for his first name John. He came to Washington, D.C. four years later in 1999, was granted permanent residency in the U.S. based on extraordinary artistic merit in 2003, and became a citizen in 2010. Joan Belmar is well known for his unique technique of 3-D paint. He combined his former painting and collage techniques with both painted and untreated Mylar/paper strips in circles and curvilinear shapes. This technique produces variations in transparency, as light and the viewer move in relation to the work. The sheer quality of these translucent materials captures and reflects light and encourages an up-close viewing of the work to reveal the different layers within. He was a Mayor's Art Award Finalist in 2007 as an outstanding emerging artist in Washington, DC. The DC Commission on the Arts & Humanities recognized him with an Artist Fellowship Program grant in 2009, and in 2011 he was awarded an Individual Artist Grant by the Arts and Humanities Council of Montgomery County, MD. He is a two-time recipient of the Maryland Arts Council Individual Artist Grant in Visual Arts: Painting, in 2010 and 2013.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My name is Savaree “Sav” Hazard-Chaney and I am 978 -raised, 401-based, self taught multimedia artists. I explore art through a personal framework of culture, community and design. Through a series of mediums I get to visit blackness, queerness and nostalgia. My current work is with fibers in the art of rug tufting. Through tufting I feel like I am challenging what it means to be and navigate as a creative person here in this city. I’m challenging what it looks like and means to “make a rug”. Creatively, tufting has allowed me to explore old-school nostalgic feels but with a new innovative approach. The idea of “making a rug” has been elevated in my mind since I first laid eyes on a tufting machine. Over the last year I made it my mission to create and grow a tufting community here in Providence, Rhode Island. I am the proud founder and owner of TuftxPVD, Rhode’s first Black, queer and woman-owned tufting studio. I focus heavily on providing access to this art form and also trailblaze young black entrepreneurship for creatives in PVD. I continue to work and have passion for public art and other community activating work. I hope to continue finding opportunities that allow me to continue exploring the intersectionality of creative and community work.

column25: My paintings and drawings act as found mementos that reference reminiscences of place or art historical memories. The images act as a somewhat askew anchor to familiarity. Using the tropes of the artifact, postcard, ruin, and souvenir, a sense of nostalgia, humor, and fragmentation of memory is expressed through abstracted narratives. Memories are recorded as stage sets, as fragments, as things that are movable and covered and rediscovered. Images surface as fragments, where the whole isn’t shown but can be imagined. The first reproduction of a painting that I remember seeing is a detail of Judas’ betrayal by Giotto. The aged physicality of the surface, the paint application, the magic of the detail defining the whole, the layering of color with time and the rather jarring image by itself has always played in my background. Surfaces of recycled styrofoam are used in the irregular shaped paintings. The idea of using the material of styrofoam, has to do with it’s artifact aspect as it takes at least 500 years to decompose.The pieces appear to be made of a heavier material and plays on the elusiveness of reality and plays with our preconceptions.The pieces have an aged physicality to the surface with layers of images or color. In using styrofoam, there’s a sense of humor, comment on the environment, as well as a sense of tactility felt in the concrete ethereal beauty of the crudeness of the pieces. The drawings are embellished and altered with stitching. There’s a sense of acquisition and nostalgia in the handheld, souvenir images. Referencing the process of sketches used in fresco design and childhood sewing cards, as well as cast aside postcards or receipts, they show a beauty in the cast off, along with a comfort of memory.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create paintings about mathematics and computer science, often incorporating landscapes and natural elements, such as shapes inspired by butterfly wings. Math is a language that humans use to describe the natural world. I imagine mathematical symbols, reminiscent of hieroglyphics, to represent numbers and logical operations. By constructing my own mathematical language and painting symbols by hand, I channel my reverence for nature and the beauty of mathematical patterns onto the canvas. My artistic approach is process-based and intuitive. I use the logical structure of algorithms to create a geometric scaffolding for my paintings. Within that structure, I follow the steps of the algorithm to paint visual representations of the mathematics. I paint my mathematical symbols intuitively. I listen to science lectures, observe natural patterns, and embrace a curious mindset. I keep mathematics in my ear and mind’s eye and let my hand translate, painting symbols spontaneously. I often paint the ancient algorithm Sieve of Eratosthenes, which finds all prime numbers up to some number N. I represent each prime number with a unique symbol, color, and texture. I depict composite numbers by using combinations of the colors and symbols of their prime factors. Changing the algorithm's parameters, such as the grid's geometric shape and the cells' size, highlights interesting patterns and symmetries in the prime numbers. My recent work visualizes the fast Fourier transform (FFT), an essential algorithm in science, communications, and image/audio processing. The FFT represents a signal as the sum of sinusoids of different frequencies. My painting Frequency Transformation depicts elevation data I recorded at Bridle Trails State Park, passing through the data flow diagram of the FFT and finally as a spectrum in the frequency domain. Frequency Transformation refers not only to transforming a signal into the frequency domain but also to the shift in mood and brainwave patterns that occur when people are immersed in nature. As a scientist, discovering how the natural world works brings me similar feelings of wonder and connection.

column25: Juliet Anne Fiss (b. 1986, Madison, WI) is an abstract painter and computer scientist. She has a B.S. in Imaging Science from the Rochester Institute of Technology and a Ph.D. in Computer Science & Engineering from the University of Washington. Her paintings are colorful visualizations of mathematics and computer science algorithms. While in graduate school, Juliet became severely ill, requiring a medical leave from 2016 to 2020. During that challenging time, she began creating colorful abstract paintings to lift her spirits. Her medical conditions made standing and sitting upright challenging, but by engaging her mind in meditative creativity, she could stand longer and strengthen her body. As she recovered, she created larger and more intricate paintings. In 2021, Juliet was well enough to return to graduate school. She also began incorporating her interests in mathematics and computer science into her paintings. She got involved with the Kirkland Arts Center and participated in several group shows. Art2Life, Artrepreneur, and Visionary Art Collective featured her art and story. In 2023, Juliet participated in Visual Impressions at Ryan James Fine Arts and won the Curator’s Award. Her painting Frequency Transformation was featured in the 4-person show Lush Computation at Seattle Central College and reviewed by the Seattle Collegian and the Capitol Hill Seattle Blog. After the show, Seattle University Sinegal Center for Science and Innovation acquired the painting. Fiss lives and works in Kirkland, WA.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For the past 15 years I have created photo and video-based artworks addressing themes of ecology, environmental conservation, and environmental sustainability. I believe that artists have a key role to play in inspiring societal changes, and this informs my creative and teaching practice. With the climate crisis accelerating, artists must be on the vanguard of imaging solutions to engage viewers in a new sustainable future. That is what I have done with my most recent photographic series, “Sustain.” This series is a photographic visual diary of self-portraits sharing my commitment to living a more sustainable lifestyle by growing produce in my urban organic fruit and vegetable garden. I express the sensuality of connecting with the Earth’s life-giving capacities. The styling references the Victory Garden movement when Americans grew 60% of all produce consumed in urban plots and community gardens. I would love for this movement to happen again except instead of service to war, instead to maintain a life-sustaining climate. Influenced by the mythological compositions in Neoclassical sculpture and Baroque painting styles, I use posing to express the sensuality, humor and joy of connecting with the Earth’s life-giving capacities. I use color, dramatic lighting, and framing to celebrate the beauty, variety, deliciousness and sustainability of the abundant food that can be grown in a small urban space.

column25: Ivana D. George is an interdisciplinary artist working with photography, video and mixed-media. Her artwork originates from a conviction that great art not only is visually compelling but that it should also have a subject matter, a meaning, and an inspirational purpose in addition to the aesthetic value. She is passionate about exploring the relationship between humanity and the natural world, informed by her ethics of environmental stewardship. Her artwork is the manifestation of George’s love of exploration and learning. She takes on various roles such as gardener, mountain climber, investigator, and environmental activist in order to explore and learn. In her art she shares her discoveries, insights and observations. Her early life experiences influence the artworks she creates today. Throughout her childhood she was exposed to rural and urban agriculture in her father’s backyard. This upbringing connected her to the seasons and an awareness of growing and eating seasonal healthy food. She developed a sense of enjoyment in nurturing life from small seeds to plants or baby chicks to adult chickens. This connectedness to the cycles of life contributed to her developing environmental ethics. George’s parents encouraged her involvement in civic life and she was active in several environmental issues. George raised money to create the Children's Forest preserve in Costa Rica, planted trees in a massive tree planting campaign on Mount Mitchell in western North Carolina, and she led a youth weekly letter writing campaign to state senators on environmental issues. She continues her civic involvement by volunteering for the BSU community garden which provides food free through the campus food bank. George’s creative influences are varied. Her educational background in technical theater, acting, video and performance art influences her approach to conceiving her photographs. As an artist who uses lens-based imaging, her aesthetic is one of carefully constructing an image rather than taking a picture. She manipulates lens-based media to construct a metaphor, idea or expression in her work. Visual influences from art styles as diverse as ancient Greek, Italian Baroque, Dutch still life, Surrealism, Pictorialism, and Dada can be seen in her work. She has exhibited her work in over 50 national juried and invitational exhibitions including Museum of Fine Arts Boston, Lens Culture Online, Flash Forward Photography Festival, The Griffin Museum, Newspace Center for Photography, Soho Photo Gallery, Dallas Video Festival, Junction Arts Festival and the Danforth Museum. Her work has been featured in solo and two person exhibitions at Panopticon Gallery, CAC in Las Vegas, Fort Point Gallery in Boston, Tower Hill Botanic Garden and Bridgewater State University. She has completed a fellowship residency at the Vermont Studio Residency Center and Oolite Arts in Miami. Her work has been written about in Orion Magazine, the Boston Globe, New England's Bay Windows, the Las Vegas Sun, Atlanta's Creative Loafing as well as several blogs. She holds a M.F.A. degree from the joint program of The School of the Museum of Fine Arts, Boston/Tufts University. She is a Professor of Art at Bridgewater State University (Massachusetts, USA), where she teaches black and white analog photography, historical photo processes, digital photography, audio, video, sustainable mixed media art, and environmental stewardship.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
How do you make a permanent mark in the world of impermanence? Ever since I was diagnosed with cancer, I always had a feeling of unease and impermanence. This feeling was always present in my life - as a woman, a mother - but the unease hit differently upon the phone call from my doctor. It gave me the desire to find a way to ground something in my life and make an indelible statement. My drawings are crafted with unwavering permanency, using tools like Pen and Ink, creating spaces of safety. The work comes from a place of searching, trying to understand the spaces we inhabit and explore. The roles we play in life and the places it takes us. I close my eyes and allow the lines to create these worlds that I won't be torn away from, and hope I can create a retreat for others.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is the freedom to define my discipline. The freedom to consider the relevance of ideas, materials and labor that is not tied to monetary gains or the requirements set by an institution, boss, client or manager. Rather than financial gains, I am driven by cultural relevance and the goal to share ideas in unexpected forms. I also enjoy the creative act of defamiliarization - that is of taking things from video games to mass produced products and reworking them to have new applications that are creative in nature. At a time when every element of our lives is being financialized and we are all asked to become stock brokers or our existence is financialized, art making works agains the status quo to envision an alternate reality.

2024 Grant Applicant - Round 1
column25: The issues that I confront through my work stem from personal history; born in the United States to Nicaraguan parents, as a child, I was aware of my parent's difficulties as immigrants struggling with a new language and culture. Growing up I spent my summers in Nicaragua and the school year in San Francisco. These two very different environments created considerable confusion in so far as my understanding of where I belong in the world and of the human condition in general - a sensibility that has extended into my artistic practice. The ways that inequality and power manifest themselves in our lives are consistent threads in my work. Themes such as immigration, discrimination, gentrification, political hegemony and the effects of monetization extend from highly subjective experiences and observations into works that tactically engage others through popular metaphors while maintaining critical perspectives. I employ illustration, animation, sculpture, sound, interactivity and public spectacle to draw an audience and ideally initiate or help cultivate a fruitful discussion. Currently, I have been collapsing documentary material with virtual space by developing virtual reality and computer applications. I also maintain a series of “web sketches” that are not research based, but rather playful visual ideas in which I combine animation and code. With each project, I approach art as a social practice that seeks to establish dialogue in public spaces (both physical and virtual) to broaden the work of art beyond what I may privately conceive. I view the urban environment as an incredibly rich arena for interactive works that employ illustration, animation, sound and interactivity to draw a participatory audience. Perhaps, most importantly, the street presents a space where the totally unexpected may occur through unmediated interactions; a space where I may engage with individuals that I would otherwise never interact with leading to unique exchanges.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being an artist is both great and terrible. Like all aspects of life, it contains duality. I am immersed in the universe, visually and experientially. I am lifted to delicious emotional heights and cast into the depths of hell, often by those self-same experiences and sensations. Everything I do is part and parcel of my world and work as an artist. I believe I was born an artist, and began my realization of that in 1954, in a stroller in MoMA. My mother took me there regularly as I had two favorite pieces—Pavel Tchelitchew's Hide and Seek and Henri Rousseau's Sleeping Gyps. While I feel that I never had a choice to be anything other than an artist, despite a range of talents across many fields of endeavor, I am thrilled to know to the depths of my being that I am absolutely an artist. I love my life and accept the extreme struggles.

column25: I create art driven by shamanism and its resultant entry into the spirit world. I paint to channel the Infinite. My studio is an alchemist's laboratory; my work comes forth through the four alchemical elements: fire, earth, water, and air. Fire and earth create pigments, through natural and human-made processes, from which I make my paints from scratch. I create paintings on canvas using a complex mixture of acrylic and urethane. I also create works on paper. My work ranges in size from 5 x 7 inches to 60 x 72 inches. I use aqueous mediums, acrylic and urethane, and then add polymers and other additives. I apply the paint with brushes or by flinging or pouring. I use air as a manipulation method and as a desiccant. I use air from an industrial air compressor to move the paint, and to mist and spray water. I also enlist gravity to move and disturb the paint. I intend this technique to add a unique, mysterious texture and depth to the paintings. I mix colors both on the painting and before application. Color is vibratory; our beings are vibratory; the universe is vibratory. My current paintings are abstract; I intend to remove the resonance of our day-to-day world. After 50 years of working with my materials, I no longer need to give conscious thought to the nature or process of creation. I just get out of the way. The Universe speaks, and I obey. I am as much on a voyage of discovery as any person who views the work I channel.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work plays with the conventions and boundaries of photography, material, and the expansion of photography's language into the white cube. I am inspired by the history of photography, which began with the camera obscura and the way painters used it to transfer landscapes onto paper. I seek to transform photography in today's society by challenging its traditional practices and exploring new possibilities. Photographs are dismantled (deconstructed?) and reassembled through collages, sculptures, installations, and site-specific projects. Much of what I do begins as a response to feelings of alienation from volatile environments. I question the meaning and value of personal identity and have developed a habit of intentionally changing my perspective to understand my surroundings. In photography, I found a medium that has allowed me to redefine and recreate what I see and hear in my day-to-day. I use the vocabulary of photography to communicate and explore the spatial relationships between images and materials, as well as light and shadow. My research explores the evolution of figurative photography into abstract geometric ideas. It is inspired by ancient Chinese figurative patterns that have undergone similar changes over time. One of the ways that I do this is to distill images of modern cities and re-present them as new abstract geometric installations and sculptures. In my recent works, I repeated the image of a particular building in a photograph I took to signify the memory of time. I also translated the building’s form into a complex, multi-layered rectangle in response to socio-political events happening around me then. The new ways that I engage with traditional photography give my practice a new visual language. I invite viewers to participate in these novel approaches to understanding images—from 2D to 3D. These 3D images, often presented as installations, are my way of transmuting memory fragments into physical forms, a way to document my daily life through materials. One of the materials that I am especially interested in is plastic. I love its translucency, which is like the glass in a camera lens. It was also contentious material when I was growing up. Its “status” has changed over the years, and it has become a controversial material with divided attitudes toward it. This has led me to consider things from different perspectives. By using plastic in my work, I challenge the boundaries of photography and material and extend the medium's language into new territories. Ultimately, what I have done through this body of work is to break the spatial constraints of photographic images by making them 3D. Furthermore, I wanted to go beyond the one-way relationship between images and the audience. And release the images into the viewer's space, allowing them to interact with traditionally flat images as dynamic sculptural forms.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my Pandemonium series, I combine performance art, drawing, painting, and photography. Exposure to other fields of study have always been an intriguing part of my artistic practice and a means of discovering new ways to articulate my ideas visually. In complete darkness, I outline myself with a flashlight and record my performances on X-ray film using a self-built 16x20” camera. By applying photo chemicals with a brush onto its surface, the film acquires its distinctive painterly look. My unorthodox processing technique often obscures the figures, so I use photographic bleach with a brush to reveal them. Experimentation and chance have become important tools in my research. My methodology has emphasized an abandonment of control, allowing the material to take over in some unexpected and unpredictable ways. The figures were drawn in response to photographic documentation of the January 6 insurrection. I consider the Pandemonium series to be a microcosm of our society, in which friction, antipathy, and ill feelings dominate our interactions with one another, ultimately dividing us. Themes of justice and injustice are prevalent in my art. My work is anchored in a moral sensibility that relates to my upbringing on a prison compound where my father was the warden. Daily interactions with prisoners were common. My father was a true believer in rehabilitation and the need to treat each person with respect and dignity, regardless of their crimes. These experiences nurtured a sense of empathy in me for the human condition that has been a guiding force in my life, as well as in my work, and has led me to depict a wide range of human emotions in my art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice is an active investigation of agency and desire. Informed by various feminisms, popular media, craft, and found objects, I examine language, the complexities of love, and the human search for truth and meaning. Material manipulation is a transformative tool for catharsis and confession in my work. I create evocative objects and installations that mimic our human experiences and emotions. I do this by carefully selecting familiar forms from everyday life. By reassembling their material composition, I can re-imagine moments of my desire, longing, uncertainty, and vulnerability. This process materializes as an attempt to unlearn my heteropatriarchal, capitalist conditioning. Inspired by public perspectives of gendered behaviors and the intimacies of private relationships, I work to expose my alternative pleasures by constructing familiar forms through material poetics and precarity. These forms become stand-ins for politicized bodies, mimicking our emotional vulnerabilities and uncertainties, offering a voyeuristic view of our failing relationships: romantic, platonic, familial, and institutional.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Isolation and marginalization have always influenced my work as a gay artist. These themes are magnified since my stroke. Through paint application and typographic media, I explore themes of neurodiversity and disability to build a dream world open to interpretation—things aren’t what they seem, but rather what we seem to think they are. My urban landscapes use metaphor and nocturnes to explore neurodiversity, the workings of the brain, feelings of isolation, and the vagaries of subjective and objective reality to create a sort of magical realism. I paint in part to chronicle the time and place that I live in, but interwoven are bits of the dream world engaging the subconscious and exploring how the brain interprets reality. By creating an emotional narrative that supersedes visual accuracy, I paint how things feel. How do we make sense of the world? How do we find meaning? Crucial to my work is the process of understanding how the brain interprets stimuli. Street signage and light poles transcend the purely representational because as we traverse the city streets we ignore such details too. Half-drawn sketches and translucent objects speak to memories and the future expectations of our internal narrative. Primed canvas between paint streaks expose and express a vision simultaneously coalescing and dispersing. Sketching in paint I soak my canvases with water creating rivers of pigment. I paint between these dripped lines, manipulating perspective and creating a jumble of flat and rendered imagery. Low and high contrasting values help tell the story of confusion and disorientation. Figures slowly emerge from shadow, while bright paint spatters recreate the jarring euphoria of synesthesia experienced with sensory processing disorders. The chaotic drippings are symbolic of the “static” of aphasia and the lack of muscle control of dysarthria and apraxia; while simultaneously creating sensations of vertigo, common to traumatic brain injury. Patterns within the drips are highlighted or darkened to resemble MRIs and angiograms. Bits of medical records are decoupaged into my paintings, often backward or upside down, referencing neurological language disorders and a metaphor for medical gaslighting. Hidden in signage or shadows, almost indiscernible, reflecting the experience of millions of people living with invisible disabilities. I strive for the familiar and uncanny. A world of winding roads, shadows in windows, and the echoes of forgotten promises. I leave the narrative open—for as your brain imparts meaning, so does the work exist.

column25: Ian Shearer is an artist based in Seattle, Washington. He is a graduate of The Academy of Art University of San Francisco. In 2018, Ian survived a massive stroke. Through intense therapy he was able to regain use of the right side of his body, however, his painting style and abilities were greatly changed. These changes have led him to “start over” as an artist. Currently, Ian’s work focuses on urban landscapes. Using the city as a lens to explore themes of isolation, subjective reality, sensory experience, and memory; often ﬁnding beauty in the everyday moments of urban life. These moments intrigue him, especially as ordinary moments become extraordinary in the wake of disability. Through his work, Ian is currently exploring the changes in his visual language and working on developing a dialogue of post-stroke experience. Adding another voice to a condition that aﬀects hundreds of thousands and documenting the recovery process through mark-making. He has shown throughout the country, done public art commissions, and recently received a prestigious 2023 Hope Corps Grant from The Seattle Office of Arts and Culture. Ian is passionate about disability access in the arts and the healing power of art. He has curated group shows for working disabled artists, exploring ways to make art exhibition more accessible and to foster dialogue and awareness around disability and inclusion. He also teaches painting classes at Path With Art, a Seattle creative center that fosters the restoration of individuals, groups, and society from the effects of trauma through arts engagement and community-building. The work submitted is from his 2023 series “Alone in a crowd”. The series explores access, isolation, and inclusivity, examining how personal narrative colors our experience and interactions with the world. After his stroke, he began a journal to help relearn writing and penmanship. He scanned these entries and printed them on acrylic fiber paste, which he then decoupaged onto canvas— painting and obscuring /revealing words until they become one. The entries document his fears and frustration around new limitations, the isolation and anxiety of neurological changes no one can see and that which few fully comprehend, and the sometimes painful and oft humorous process of acceptance and perseverance. For this series, he portrays, as central figures, those often marginalized in our communities or historically underrepresented in the story of Western art—instead, focusing on BIPOC, LGBTQ+, women, older folk, persons with disability, etc. Ian explores the edges of the invisible barriers in our communities and ask: Who has access here? Who is being seen and included? And where are the lines being drawn? Many of these pieces aim to capture moments of isolation in urban life, such as stolen minutes of reflection in crowded spaces and busy streets. Or including persons or moments urban denizens often hurry past with eyes averted. Even crowd scenes are from a distance as if a pane of glass separates the viewer from the action. Included are experimental pieces created to be more inclusive of low-vision individuals—through surface texture, the building of layers, decoupaged braille, and enlarged braille dots embedded into pieces. The reasoning is twofold. Firstly, to find ways to make the work more accessible and give some agency to low vision and blind individuals. Second, to bring to the attention of sighted viewers the profound need to think more about accessibility and accommodation. More of Ian’s work can be found at ianshearerstudio.com.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Gary LaPointe Jr.’s artistic practice grapples with the material constructs embedded in the shifting frameworks of our built and naturally produced environments and within facets of identity construction. He produces installations, sculptures, process-based drawings, collages, and prints that navigate and mine the material systems around us and question the relationships, and rituals within them. At the core of His work lies the ardent fascination with found material objects, which He collects and transforms based on their construction, utility, and the social and political foundations they represent. Through a range of interventions, LaPointe challenges the functionality and material properties of these objects to produce new forms and narratives. His artistic exploration does not adhere to a linear path but rather sheds light on diverse themes encompassing our changing environments, queerness and desire, and the construction and slippage of language, form, and materiality.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I use watercolor, ink, acrylic ink, and a mixture of gestural, contour, and figurative drawing to evoke the dreamlike, spiritual, deeply ambiguous, temporally fluid nature of black trans reality. My work both gestures towards black trans embodiment as a mode of meaning (re)making and knowledge production. Specifically, I interrogate what the speculative nature of black trans embodiment and futures teaches us about time, space, being, and colonial history. I draw and paint memory and personal photographs to stage and (re)imagine my black (trans) past, present, and future, and contend with the contradiction of existing as a free, multiplicity of beings housed, rather than at home, in a body that might never be free. My work is ultimately a manifestation of my radical black trans imagination, my black world, and my black psyche. This is, therefore, a portrayal of black life and black gender from the margins that many of my ancestors were never free enough to publicly embody and imagine. I evoke time, space, being, the body, and colonial history through visual imagery that alludes to spirit, ancestral pasts, trans reproduction, intergenerational trauma, disability, and images that 'trans' colonial images of the black female body. In this sense, I am also exploring how trans embodiment expands how we perceive the body as a temporal and spatial vessel. My work is in conversation with artists such as Kara Walker, Wangechi Mutu, Basquiat, and Wilfredo Lam. I use my personal experiences to tell marginal, 'unimaginable' stories about black trans life. I make art intending to heal, inspire, and advocate for my people on a global scale.

column25: Imani (they/he/we), 28, is a black, genderqueer/fluid, trans-masculine, neurodivergent writer, interdisciplinary artist, healer, and educator born and raised on the South Side of Chicago. They call Harlem home. Imani earned degrees in African-American Studies and Visual Arts (in Sculpture) from Princeton University in 2018, and an MA in English & Comparative Literature from Columbia University in 2020. In 2021, they began making music and were accepted to Berklee College of Music. That year, they also founded Black Body Productions, a platform for black, queer, and trans-complex trauma survivors. Their work is un-gendered, and therefore un-genred. They move between visual art, writing, movement, music, spiritual practice, and the healing arts to speculate upon the spectacular and seemingly impossible nature of black trans futures. Their visual art performs the gender ambiguity of black femininity and black masculinity by blurring the boundaries between abstraction and traditional figuration. Imani uses watercolor & ink — materials that suggest liquidity, hybridity, symbiosis, and transformation — to portray how black trans* embodiment in motion subtly gestures towards liberation from normative conceptions of time, space, and being. Their performance practice self-witnesses their black, trans, disabled body as a crossroads and conduit for ancestral, spiritual knowledge and intergenerational healing through rituals of adornment, self-care, and somatic experiencing. Embodied practices and somatic experiences such as yoga (Anapanasati, vipassana, and asana), Reiki, and calisthenics are central to Imani’s art practice and way of being with the world. They have published writing in Aperture, The Sole Adventurer, Black Minds Mag, and most recently Recess’s Critical Writing Program. They have had solo/group shows at Princeton University, Broodthaers Society of America, and the Arts Students League of New York.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I love many things about being an artist. I notice that the more I put into this community, the more connections I have to others and understanding I have of my deeper, truer self. I feel a strengthening within my network and my community. In the studio, I’m able to surrender myself to the impulsive, objectively crazy ideas whispered timidly by my inner self. My twin flame doesn't know the sting of rejection or the fear of judgment, and the feeling of being supported in my artist community helps me to take these chances. This enriches my human experience further and it emboldens me to take the next step, even if it feels insane or impossible. I am an artist just like thousands before me and thousands after. Because I can take bold steps to dream and imagine what life could be in the future and in the present, I become a less fearful, more courageous person in my art practice and in my everyday life as a caring, compassionate person. Being an artist awakens me and unlocks the ability to extrapolate feelings and experiences in a way which activates a jolt of energy into my blood, my bones, the entire essence of myself. Making art makes me feel alive, with a purpose and a project. It's the feeling of my chest filling with the freshest, sweetest air I've ever known, ballooning my heart to the warm sun, taking my soul and leaving my body behind. Being an artist helps me to see that there are no limitations on what I’m capable of as an individual and as a member of a family, a group, a community, and a global human who calls this planet home. There is always hope if only we can see past the limitations.

column25: I create artwork concerned with examining uncertainty of self through romanticized aesthetics. In my multi-layered, curious practice, I’m inspired by slow, sometimes painful moments of reckoning where concrete authenticity and truth, especially in terms of identity, become ambiguous and loose. I reference figures in my work while sometimes allowing perceived movement to influence and twist the shape of the body into proportions that feel slightly off. This abstraction symbolizes instability and creates space to call into question how far we can bend before collapsing. In my paintings, I balance a jarringly saturated color palette with private, solitary moments absorbed with loneliness, reverie, and an insatiable desire to feel whole. My process of making a painting meets somewhere in the middle between relying on reference photos snapped by using my iphone and listening to raw intuition and impulse. My subjects are commonly depicted with placid, dreamy yet awestruck expressions reflecting inner turmoil, desire, or utter bliss in the face of the void of existentialism. I think about voids, endless spaces of un-reality that go on and on, a lot in my work in friction with real things: bodies, thoughts, feelings. The color red is also important in my work, bringing emphasis to the dream-like spaces depicted or highlighting certain parts of the body like the hands and feet; our feet and feet are outlets which root us in present, unshakable reality. I am interested in exploring the journey of how people must make sense of who they are. In my daily life, I imagine myself as a side character in my interactions with others (the stars of their very own blockbuster movies). Referencing my own experience with identity dysphoria, painting allows me to investigate how a person’s physical and mental presence in the world is malleable and fragile. The artist in me experiences a squishiness and fluidity in my own personal identity. In my art, this shows up in subjects experiencing insecurity in their place in the world, juxtaposed by a romanticized visual depiction. I am interested in the moments we lose faith in what we believe to be true about ourselves, and how these moments can be the most authentic, human and unfathomably true moments of our lives. These moments can be beautiful.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Within my work, I articulate a joyful quirkiness within detailed compositions in illustration, painting, and printmaking. In my formative years of graphic design training, the influence of Frank Lloyd Wright propelled me toward a dedication to balance and fluidity in my creations. I inject a touch of levity into every piece, whether through an unconventional color palette or whimsical elements, such as anthropomorphizing inanimate objects. Being outdoors is the spark for much of my artistic inspiration. Elements like flowers, mushrooms, herbs, birds, bugs, and woodland creatures find their way into my pieces as a celebration of organic beauty. Living in Detroit for the past seven years has fueled my fascination with the intersection of civilization and nature. The city's juxtaposition of urban development and natural resilience serves as a captivating influence on my work. Detroit has been transformative for me as an artist. After growing up in a rural town in mid-Michigan, the new cultures, perspectives, and art that permeate the city have reshaped my work. I am currently experimenting with landscapes and portraits, using color to new depths and exploring different representations of the forms I usually draw. For more than five years, mural design has been integral to my artistic journey. The challenge of translating intricate compositions to a larger scale feels like assembling a puzzle—a puzzle I thoroughly enjoy. I’m also fascinated by how a mural becomes ingrained in its surroundings, as a mile marker on your work commute or part of what you picture when you think of that particular city or area. My ongoing commitment revolves around expanding my mural and illustration portfolio, paving the way for transitioning into a full-time role as an independent artist.

column25: Danielle fell in love with art at an early age, thanks in part to her family’s shared artistic talents. She studied at the College for Creative Studies and earned a degree in graphic design and a minor in illustration from Spring Arbor University. She’s balanced full-time work with freelance artistry throughout her career, practicing everything from murals to illustrations, printmaking to henna tattoos, and more. Danielle was quickly promoted from graphic designer to senior art director at Crain Communications, enabling her to lead the corporate brand's and its campaigns' creative direction. She also established a rotating art partnership with the College for Creative Studies, featuring local artists’ work at the company’s Detroit headquarters. Danielle and her family also worked the Michigan art fair circuit for nine years until 2020, the last three years of which Danielle sold her illustrations and prints. She was scheduled to be the featured artist for the Royal Oak Market Art Fair Edition in Spring 2020 before it was canceled due to the COVID-19 pandemic. Danielle also developed an interest in painting murals through participating in the Bright Walls Festival in Jackson, MI, from 2019 through 2022 and designing multiple murals for the Brewery Park campus in Detroit and one in Beulah, MI. In 2023, Danielle established Danyeller Design as her mural and illustration studio. She is currently taking on mural clients and illustrating a children’s book. Danielle and her husband, Eric, split their time between their home in Detroit with their cat, Grizz, and the Traverse City area. When she’s not painting, drawing, or crafting, Danielle enjoys antique shopping, listening to music on her record player or at live concerts and festivals, and exploring the great outdoors.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Alexandra N Sherman Artist Statement: Weight of the World – a reckoning “Weight of the World” is a series of collages that reflects the current state of our world; addressing the existential threats of climate change, war, partisanship, and our impact on nature. As humans, we have had an incredible effect upon our environment, from the clearing of natural habitat to introducing invasive species. We have taken so much water from the ground that we have even changed how the earth sits upon its axis! We have explored, mapped, and claimed almost every square mile of this planet. We face a future where getting along will become increasingly difficult as our precious resources dwindle. This series is a literal reckoning of our past and present but not without hope. Each collage, featuring the earth in various states of stability, is built upon antique ledgers which employ intricately cut paper from a multitude of sources and time periods to create surreal scenes. The placement of each symbolic element is carefully considered in terms of compositional balance and response to the existing marks upon the page. Several of the larger ledgers backgrounds are painted, creating context for the collages, much like a diorama one might see in a natural history museum. The ledgers are reckonings that serve as a historical snapshot of the mundane, such as food purchases and inventories. By working atop these ledgers, I am collaborating with people of the past and building another layer of human history, while pondering how we arrived at this point, our similarities and our differences in relation to the people that lived before us. The hands in both the collages reference work, action, discord, help, protection, and pleas for help, while the feet refer to movement, trouble, and even tantrums. Other characters include a simultaneously protective and vengeful mother Earth, her children, and birds and butterflies asking us to consider their existential plight. The earth represented by antique illustrations and vintage globes serves as a record of history, empires, colonialization and how things have changed over time.

column25: Alexandra N Sherman’s art explores the landscape of the mind through a myriad of subjects and mediums. She seeks to give the viewer an intimate experience through works that explore the uncertainty and mystery inherent in life, ourselves and our connections with the world around us. The environment and the need for conservation are underlying themes in her work. Alexandra’s work depicts the spaces in-between, the seen and unseen, through painterly watercolors, created from her subconscious using pareidolia. Her hand-cut collages and assemblages sourced from antique and vintage ephemera approach these themes from different angles. Each work is a vignette with an ambiguous narrative, which invites the viewer to complete the story by layering their own story on top. Her installations feature found and created objects that shift meaning through context and juxtaposition. Alexandra grew up and worked in the antiques business, which inspired her love of art and the decorative arts. She has an MFA from the University of Wisconsin, Madison, in painting and trained in ateliers L’Eclat De Verre and Gold Leaf Studios where she studied framing and the art of cartonnage. Her curatorial experience includes assistant curator for Mobil modern art collection, and the curator of The Dadian Gallery.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As much as there is a lot of exploitation of artists, and artistic labor is usually undercompensated and underrecognized, and the situation of being an artist is very precarious, the freedom that comes with being an artist makes up for all of that. I cherish the ability to be my own boss, invent my own projects, not conform to societal expectations, and above all to follow my curiosity. While it does not justify being underpaid, it does make it so that I would not choose to be anything other than an artist. It is this freedom that provides me with the autonomy to delve into personal as well as societal, philosophy, and different viewpoints, for the purpose of analyzing the structure of contemporary society from an outsider’s perspective, with the goal of nudging the trajectory of culture towards the just and egalitarian. I find this freedom in the experimentation with new techniques, styles, and mediums. It is engaging to mix and match traditional approaches with new media, thereby innovating and pushing boundaries in ways which challenge conventional thinking. But I also find that this freedom is of personal benefit to me. I find that the process of exploring, researching, and making is fulfilling in its own right, but also is good for my mental health. And while being an artist can oftentimes be hazardous to one’s health—dealing with toxic chemicals, in addition to dangerous tools and processes—the freedom that it provides to choose when and how to work with my body usually contributes greatly to my overall physical health. The freedom that comes with being an artist empowers me to work in ways that are authentic, innovative, and impactful, with the hope that the work I make will resonate with both individuals and society as a whole.

column25: My practice focuses heavily on developing tools for building environmental resilience, empowering individuals, as well as building relationships between humans and non-humans(fauna/flora/fungi). I do this through choice of materials and methodologies, development of concepts, as well as the incorporation of public events into my practice. I see my function as an artist to highlight the importance and interconnections between environmental and personal health, while promoting social and economic equality, through accessible public art and community events. I aim to engage with and bring together multifaceted communities, immersing them within diverse programming, creating unique shared experiences, as well as providing space for experimenting with tools, materials and processes. I encourage citizen participation in contemporary art, maker culture, and sustainable design practices. This practice of community engagement does not just provide for aesthetic engagement but aims to form bodily, sensory as well as intellectual relationships which manifest at the nexus of art, environment and community. In developing events and workshops, I strive to merge community knowledge and experiences, with my own knowledge of structural design, material fabrication, as well as academic theory, to develop multifaceted, interdisciplinary and often undefinable site-specific projects.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist and designer, Kelsey Ann Kasom redefines how space is valued sculpturally. Her shapes, although are an extension of the body, always remain a part of the body, the inner body. Philosophically, her work is grounded in thoughtful symbolism through attention to detail. The process of materializing the complexity that exists within her is a mirrored reflection within her work. Instinctively, she does not create without her memories. Kelsey was Detroit-born but raised in the countryside of Columbus, Michigan where she began anchoring on creativity as a form of expressionism. “I grew up surrounded by nature which has instinctively influenced my work today. Having been raised in a low-tech environment my hand intelligence became my primary source of understanding the world around me.” Kelsey explores the intimate relationship she shares with the metaphorical power of her material language. She gains endurance from exhausting material iterations by challenging the properties of what we know to then celebrate what something can become.

column25: Kelsey Ann Kasom began her creative journey at Columbia College Chicago on scholarship due to her award-winning fine art portfolio from the Scholastic Art & Writing Awards. Kelsey graduated with honors in 2016, earning a Bachelor of Fine Arts in Fashion Design with an award-winning thesis collection that was sponsored by Fashion Group International. Kelsey went on to showcase her work at the International Textile and Apparel Association Exhibition in Vancouver BC, Canada prior to working for Anna Sui and later Valentina Kova through back-to-back seasons in New York Fashion Week. In 2019, Kelsey moved to London to pursue a Graduate Diploma in Fashion at Central Saint Martins, and in 2020 she was accepted into the Royal College of Art for her Master of Art in Womenswear. Alongside her studies, she was selected to exhibit her work in Detroit Michigan’s Annual XLVIII Exhibition. During the past year, Kelsey has become a finalist for the British Library x British Fashion Council Competition, Westfield London’s Future Fashion Competition, and FASHIONCLASH Festival for New Fashion Narratives. Kelsey was featured in Grazia Magazine as part of Grazia X CSM: A New Age for Women in Fashion. She was introduced as part of the next generation of fearless designers who put the female experience at the heart of their work. Recently her work was published in WWD, S/ Magazine, System Magazine, 1 Granary: Reimagining Materiality, and The Dyslexian. Kelsey was identified as 1 of 5 designers and creators whose dyslexia gives them the ability to build, design, create and define the embodiment of the dyslexic aesthetic. Kelsey’s work was on display in Battersea South RCA 2022 Exhibition and featured in the British Fashion Council Graduate Preview. Kelsey’s work was exhibited during neurodiversity month in San Francisco for the Dyslexic Dictionary hosted by Dyslexic Design Thinking at the Arion Press Gallery. Surrounding this event her work was published in 48hills, San Francisco Examiner, and Texintel. She was also selected to exhibit during Frieze London 2022 at Zari Gallery. Kelsey concluded the year by being shortlisted as part of the Arts Thread Global Design Graduate Show in collaboration with Gucci. At the start of 2023, her work was selected to be exhibited at Vanner Gallery in Salisbury, England, and featured in Vogue Japan and Nasty Magazine. Kelsey was named one of the Top 50 Influential Neurodivergent Women of 2023 by Women Beyond The Box. Kelsey's work Fractal Thinking was exhibited at the Queens Botanic Gardens for the Creative Climate Awards hosted by the Human Impacts Institute where she was awarded for her creativity raising awareness and inspire climate action. Followed by being awarded a grant as an alumni of the Scholastic Awards, by the The Alliance for Young Artists & Writers to support her creative projects. Kelsey was most recently the guest on Blazon, for the podcast episode, Moulding Fashion: The Fusion of Art and Design. Following this Kelsey was commissioned as the wardrobe designer for Allen Ruiz x NAT AVEDA 2023 Congress Show. Concluding the year as part of the Fashion Film Program for FASHIONCLASH screening at Lumière Cinema in Maastricht, Netherlands.

column25: As an artist, my work is deeply rooted in my personal experiences and cultural background. My name is Kenneth Alexander, and my art reflects the kaleidoscopic nature of our world, where the fantastical meets the mundane and the abstract converges with the tangible. With a keen eye for detail and a passion for the creative process, I aim to challenge the boundaries between reality and fantasy, and to spark a conversation about the significance of cultural identity in contemporary art. My background and experiences as a Black American inspire much of my work. I believe that art has the power to shape the future, and through my art, I seek to inspire, educate, and celebrate the rich cultural heritage of Black Americans. By creating pieces that are both thought-provoking and visually stunning, I bring a new perspective to the conversation surrounding diversity and representation in the art world. My art is a multi-sensory experience that engages the viewer on many levels. I use color and form to create a visual representation of the internal, emotional and musical landscape of my imagination. I draw inspiration from my love for music, and my pieces are often an ode to the beauty and power of sound. My work invites the viewer to explore the hidden dimensions of their own imagination and to experience a journey of self-discovery. In my latest works, I continue to explore the theme of the Black experience and its connection to the future. I believe that the Black experience is a vital part of our cultural heritage, and my work celebrates the richness and diversity of this heritage. Through my art, I seek to create a dialogue that highlights the significance of cultural identity and promotes social and cultural awareness. In conclusion, my art is a powerful representation of the world as seen through the eyes of a creative and imaginative mind. My work pushes the boundaries of what is possible with traditional and digital art techniques, and my unique approach to color, form, and music creates a visual and emotional experience that is truly unparalleled. My aim is to inspire, educate, and celebrate, and I hope that my work can spark conversations and challenge perspectives in the art world and beyond.

Photography became my chosen medium of self-expression, enabling me to convey my thoughts and emotions through self-portraits and commercial work alike. My artistic focus lies in capturing the essence of my subjects, revealing the fun, the creative, and the authentic aspects of their being. With every photograph, I aim to bring out the real you, allowing your true self to shine through. Whether I am working on a fashion editorial or a portrait session, my goal is to encapsulate the beauty, depth, and unique qualities of each individual. Photography is more than a medium for me. it is a means of connecting with others, fostering understanding, and challenging societal norms. It allows me to create a visual dialogue that goes beyond words, speaking directly to the hearts and minds of viewers. I am constantly honing my craft, seeking new ways to push boundaries and capture moments that evoke emotions and provoke thought.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work considers the gendered gaze, the demands and joys of motherhood, sensuality, and the measures women take to protect themselves in a patriarchal world. European folk tales act as recognizable reference points within all of my projects. I examine how women are represented in these tales and how their narratives reflect contemporary feminist issues of bodily integrity. Motherhood has transformed my life entirely; my work is deeply influenced by postpartum anxiety and panic, rebuilding my body, and the joys and deliriums of caring for a baby. Out of this comes Batshit, a fairy-godmother/demon/alter ego. A chimeric blend of my home country’s Swiss folklore, childhood nostalgia, and tales of alluring monsters under your bed, Batshit is a guide and a hinderance, full of the indulgence and fearlessness that I crave and the excess and foul behavior that comes with giving into indulgences to avoid responsibility. Batshit allows me to break free from fears in my work, giving me access to themes of sensuality, sexuality, and the absurd. All of the work is intentionally a mixture of comfort and discomfort, fear and fun, and scary and sexy. My current experience is a constant alchemy of opposing forces and I am excited by the tensions these dualities hold. The work is also intentionally highly decorative: beauty gets people in the door and allows me to let my nonlinear narratives unfold over time. Furthermore, it lets me touch on visual inspirations such as Swiss folklore, turn-of-the-century craftsmanship and ornamentation, and fairy tale illustrations that also connect with print history.

column25: Annika Earley makes drawings, paintings, and prints about transformation, in-between states, and the experience of being a woman. She often uses German folktales as reference points in her work. Earley grew up in rural Switzerland but moved to mid-coast Maine in 2000. When she was very young, she used to play in the woods; there she found a small, shingled hut with tiny windows and a locked door. Raised on Grimm stories, Earley was very confident that this must be a witch’s house and proceeded to avoid it at all costs (she had no interest in being baked into a pie). When she returned to Switzerland for the first time after ten years, she discovered that this hut was, in fact, a storage shed belonging to the forestry department of her village. This experience continues to have a profound effect on her work. Earley holds an MFA from Maine College of Art and an M.Phil from College of the Atlantic. She has been a resident at the Ellis-Beauregard Foundation in Rockland, ME, Hewnoaks Artist Colony in Lovell, ME, Monson Arts in Monson, ME, Pace House Residency in Stonington, ME, and will be in residence at the Walkaway House in North Adams, MA in Summer 2023 and at the Burren College of Art in Ballyvaughan, Ireland in 2024. She has been supported by the St. Botolph Foundation in Boston, MA, the New York Foundation for the Arts, The Albert K, Murray Fund, and SPACE Gallery’s American Rescue Plan Grant. Earley’s work is in the collections of the University of Southern Maine and the College of the Atlantic. Her work has been most recently exhibited at Zero Station in Portland, Maine.

column25: “Every day that I wake up, I tally the number of days I have been alive. As of December 30th, 2023 I am 10,266 days old. Each day becomes a new address for my homes I embroider. The number fits well as an address, but more importantly becomes a placeholder for time and memory. These homes live in landscapes of fantastical and dreamy energy. But also exist in a space of subtle lostness and chaos. I collect icons from my every day, past experiences, and false memory. I’m shifting between the real and fake realities that intertwine in my mind. These works become my own versions of maps, or postcards. They hold no real navigational route or reference of specific place but my own. These are an archive of thought, routine, and familiarity.”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I approach art as an investigation, a way of understanding and as salvation. Everything that is happening in my life can serve as inspiration for my work and I paint to make sense of things. In our chaotic world where we are inundated with information and images, I find solace and meaning in the intimate and mundane moments of life. I see life in fragments: a snippet of a memory, the flash of a news story, a bird landing on the table, a coffee cup, a child in the yard, or a bride in a wedding photograph on the wall. Inside these small moments I find the potential for experiencing laughter, sadness, confusion, or heartbreak. Art has always been a way for me to put things in some kind of order. I begin with an idea but in the process of working, things change. I may start with a memory, but I like working without any anticipation of outcome. The process is what makes the work exciting and unpredictable to me, and In the end, there is an ambiguous and spiritual narrative. I enjoy the tension between formal elements and the imagery. Often scale is skewed, gravity is a defied, color is heightened, and the picture plane is covered with images, pattern, and color. What I am seeking is not realism or abstraction but the unconscious and intuitive mystery of being human. The paintings can be read like a diary containing not only the joy of remembered family events and rites of passage but also the sadness of trying moments.

I incorporate symbolic representations of the natural world, spirits, and mythological beings to convey contemporary thoughts, fears, and societal problems in a meaningful and authentic manner. Cultural Meaning: I feel a strong responsibility to preserve and convey the cultural traditions and beliefs of my community through my artistic expressions. It is essential for me to ensure that the Latin way of life is imbued with meaning through my role as a creator. The spiritual concepts and beliefs of my people are reflected in my work, contributing to the preservation of our cultural heritage. Ritual Practice: I actively engage in the rituals and ceremonies of my art practice, using my creative expression as a means to participate and contribute a unique voice that seeks to maintain harmony and balance in this unjust contemporary society. Through my art, I strive to play a role in upholding social and spiritual beliefs, making a contribution to history.

column25: 'When I paint in front of the canvas I try to demolish rational thought. I love to immerse myself fully into a world that I am exploring, to get obsessive about the formation of the pieces and their details. Even though I step into a world of fantasy, I stay grounded through my connection to the collective concerns present in my work, ranging from political violence, the American dream, and domestic abuse to surveillance and the rhetoric of belonging. Through art I try to reconcile our collective capacity for creating both ecstasy and suffering, and the fraught sexual and psychological forces which are hidden just beneath the surface of reality.” As an artist, I am deeply influenced by the feminist movement and its ongoing struggle for equality and empowerment. Through my art, I aim to contribute to this dialogue by portraying the strength, resilience, and complexity of women's experiences. My paintings serve as a visual narrative that sheds light on the multifaceted nature of womanhood, from the personal to the political, from the historical to the contemporary. The act of painting itself becomes a form of catharsis for me, a way to process and articulate my thoughts and emotions about the world around me. Through each brushstroke, I strive to capture the essence of the human experience, reflecting both the beauty and the struggles inherent in our existence. My use of color, texture, and form is deliberate, as I seek to imbue each piece with layers of meaning and symbolism that invite the viewer to engage in a deeper contemplation of the subject matter. For the past seven years, my focus has been on the following issues: the protest movement of women, the beginning of disassembling patriarchal systems in political and civic arenas, and my experience of motherhood and the inequalities of being an immigrant Latina in New York City. The protagonist of a woman keeps appearing in this series of paintings that I have been developing. After being invited to exhibit at the Museo Laboratorio di Arte Contemporanea-Università Sapienza, Rome, I began an investigation into cases of domestic violence and discovers the story of the Roman figure Beatrice Cenci. It is from this desire that I created and continue to produce paintings that look at and learn from women's portraiture throughout the history of art. I wanted to feature the strength that we as women have carried within ourselves from the dawn of time. The life of Beatrice Cenci, a young Roman noblewoman of the 16th century executed for her involvement in the murder of her parents, has been my most recent inspiration. Her tragic story and her struggle against oppression and abuse have led me to explore themes of justice, resistance, and the complexity of the human experience. Like Beatrice, many women throughout history have faced similar challenges and have fought for equality and freedom. My work is an attempt to honor their legacy and to share their stories through artistic expression. In the midst of the 21st century, at a time when feminist struggles have been fundamental in raising awareness about various forms of oppression and violence against women worldwide, I revisit Beatrice's tragic experience, as well as the fatal fate of her entire family, to communicate the traumatic experiences of the victims, highlighting how, in some cases, these experiences lead to extreme measures in the pursuit of justice and liberation. My work has focused in recent years on making visible aspects of the female experience that are often ignored in contemporary society, including domestic violence. Although Beatrice Cenci lived in a very different time, her experience stands as a valuable historical reference point. Through symbolic elements and other formal resolutions that characterize my work, emotional complexities such as anguish will be reflected in female figures. The use of solid black backgrounds and specific compositional tools will convey the atmosphere of despair that surrounds victims of sexual abuse and emotional mistreatment like Beatrice. In summary, the visual language will focus on expressing the emotional and psychological tension faced by these women. The paintings and mural will serve as an artistic homage to the memory of Beatrice and as a means to reflect on the persistence of oppression and the struggle for justice throughout history.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is the empowerment you get from seeing yourself create. Art gives us such valuable insight and perspective about ourselves, our past selves, and what we are capable of in the future. Our art is a record of our identity. Talking to a poet friend recently, she told me how she had found her old journals from childhood in her mom's house. She described them to me and told me about the comic book character on the cover, which totally fits her personality, her passions, and how she perceives herself in the world today. She said the experience of reading back her old poems was beautiful and moving. Through her poetry now, she sends love to her inner child, things like that are what I love about being an artist. The way being an artist also empowers me is seeing people change or think about something differently after experiencing art. Art can transport us to a place that pushes us to act and interact differently in the universe we do live in. Art-making is empowering because art shapes history and influences human behavior. I love that being an artist you can notice traces of your art on a micro level. Maybe it is a change in the language of how your parent speaks, or the confidence of a friend who is trying something new. Making art gives me the satisfaction of having an impact on a small part of the world. It's also empowering that when maybe we don't feel at our most creative, we can look back at worlds that have grown from inside our minds, and things we have created, and remember our personal power and potential.

column25: This selection of photographs I took in 2022 in Puerto Rico. I started using film as a medium a few years ago and although it is challenging I have grown to love it. I moved to Puerto Rico in 2017 before Hurricane Maria took all the leaves off the trees. In the years since, I have watched them grow back and am more grateful for the abundance and lushness of the island. The landscape is always changing, often by natural disaster or gentrification and these photos are a record of what I've seen. I love to capture colorful environments in the bright sunshine. The power grid in Puerto Rico is one of the most unreliable realities of living here and many of my photos feature power cables and electrical poles. Living here I am constantly reminded of the island's colonial status and fraught connection with the United States. Many infrastructural problems stem from the question of Puerto Rico's status. The natural beauty of the land juxtaposed with the mess of cables represents the entanglement of Puerto Rico's history with the United States. After Hurricane Maria wiped out the electrical grid and cell service , leaving the island in the dark (me personally for 42 days), instead of using renewable forms of energy to rebuild, the electrical utility provider was switched to a private company and prices were hiked. To this day blackouts are common. On an island where sun, wind, and rain are abundant, the power grid remains un-updated waiting for the next natural disaster to destroy it. Many of my photos are of places that no longer exist even though they were taken recently. These photos provide a record of how things looked before they were bought out, changed, renovated, or torn down.

column25: The urge to collect and store memory is a form of grief-work— I first came to the world of art and archives through collecting rocks, letters, textiles, faux-finish brushes; evidence of artists I’ve loved or worked for who have passed on. My studio work studies ancient and mostly ephemeral Judaic apocalyptic symbols along with Abrahamic ritual objects based in Christian material histories. They are collected from family-run factories in Italy near Veda, the gallery that I work with in Florence. I’ve used these defective religious icons in sculpture to consider alternative histories of individuals erased by the reductive monumentality of historic narrative. This laid the groundwork for my involvement with oral history, which seeks to capture those histories through the medium of voice. I have been a core collective member and the coordinator of the NYC Trans Oral History Project (NYC TOHP) for five years. The NYC TOHP is a community archive devoted to the preservation and sharing of trans histories, organized in collaboration with the New York Public Library. My interests in community-based healing through narrative, diasporic Judaism, and oral history emerged out of my longtime investment in the transformative power of storytelling. To date, I’ve had the honor of conducting dozens of interviews with trans New Yorkers from many walks of life, including trans people formerly incarcerated, houseless, immigrants, and elders of many backgrounds. Their stories were accessible to the public in my installation, Angelic Hosts, at the Park Ave Armory this last October. My oral history and sculpture work are vested in the traditions of both folk art and paying reverence to the indigenous technology of oral history keeping. I deeply believe my urge to do preservation work is drawn from my diasporic Jewish lineage.

This journey is driven by a relentless pursuit of truth, a quest that has continually reshaped both my perception and my artistry. Each piece I create is a testament to an evolving consciousness, a mirror reflecting the ongoing metamorphosis of both thought and technique. My recent foray into the realm of generative AI marks a significant phase in this evolution. This technology is not merely a tool in my creative arsenal; it is a dynamic collaborator. It challenges me to reimagine the boundaries of what is possible, bridging the expanse between the fantastical realms of imagination and the concrete reality of artistic execution. My ultimate aspiration is to invoke AI only when traditional methods fall short - be it due to safety concerns, practicality, budget constraints, or the inherent limitations of existing artistic mediums. In this collaborative dance with AI, my goal is to stretch the fabric of creativity, pushing each project to its maximum potential, exploring uncharted territories of artistic expression. Essentially my art is a dialogue between human creativity and artificial intelligence, a synergy that continually redefines the scope and scale of what can be envisioned and achieved. This alliance enables me to transform intangible thoughts and emotions into tangible artistic expressions, continually expanding the horizons of my creative journey.

column25: My artistic practice is focused on finding a personal understanding of how the search for a universal theory of everything jives with the idea of a many truths view of reality. Trained as a nuclear physicist, I make drawings as a kind of thought experiment in which I imagine creating a “universe” one step at a time according to a set of rules. What I seek is a complexity that seems to defy an algorithmic process. This program which I have engaged in for the past 12 years is designed to asymptotically achieve some kind of intuitive understanding. I start these drawings the same way, with a mark and a rule for how to repeat it. A rule usually describes how different the next mark can be from the previous one. I try to rigorously adhere to the rules. Once this process is set in motion, I let go and see where it takes me. Each mark is a small yet conscious decision, but I work quickly enough that it does not feel that way. In fact I often feel like I don’t know what I’m doing. If a drawing seems to stall under the weight of too much homogeneity I will loosen the rule or introduce another system to allow for greater diversity. I think of these smaller 11' x 10 drawings as mini physics calculations or simulations. Like trying to build my own universe from scratch, or as we say in physics from first principles. The development of each drawing mimics the process of growth with a built-in mechanism for mutation—the inability of my hand or my mind to remain free of error. I don’t use any tools beyond the pen itself. And I’ve come to see errors as acts of creativity. I experience two distinct sensations when I draw. One is the feeling that I’m busy at work making mark after mark. I enjoy having a strong sense of purpose. The other sensation is that of an observer. Surrendering to the process, I watch as if I have no idea what’s coming next. Every new move is a surprise. I even feel a sense of admiration for how the drawer seems to create this universe out of nothing but the imagination.

column25: I’m a self-taught photographer born and raised in Indianapolis, Indiana. Photography has always been a part of my life, my grandfather was a photojournalist for an African-American publication. It’s through my observation of my grandfather and how he worked that encouraged me to tap into my creativity. I enjoy shooting black and white portraits and street photography. The adrenaline rush of shooting strangers and the calmness studio portraits offer. In my opinion, both areas require a certain amount of discipline and creativity. I’m a bit daring in my studio sessions, so it’s important to hear the story of my clients and gain their trust before shooting. When a client isn’t afraid to be vulnerable, the picture becomes a work of art. I started Blended Images Photography, LLC, after the birth of my daughter. Capturing her every moment became my obsession, but it also developed me as a photographer. I currently work alone, but I’ve met some people I would love to collaborate with in the immediate future. I’m most proud of the risk I’ve taken to create my brand. I don’t place myself in a box and I don’t worry about what others are creating. I believe there’s space for everyone to build. I remember when I first started sharing my work on social media, people I’ve known for years had no idea I was serious about photography. I produced image after image because I have so many photos and I just wanted people to enjoy my work. Then a demand to print, exhibit or publish my work arose, and I had to reset. This reset allows me to be present in my life. I see the growth in my work and clientele. I have some huge goals for 2024, and I’m really excited to see where my persistence takes me.
