column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
A new twist on an ancient art — this selection of pieces explores the timeless archetypes of Tarot with bold, kaleidoscopic collage landscapes made up of a multitude of symbols, both new and old. Each hand-assembled collage is made up of dozens of tiny fragments, each element conscientiously selected from an ever-growing library of found materials, and then carefully and purposefully combined back together in a new form. Tarot is an artform that transcends time and place; an unending storyline of archetypes who have lived countless lives in countless forms. The characters and settings that illustrate each card are, at once, both unique and universal, constantly shapeshifting, but never losing their essential form. Their inherently archetypal nature has allowed artists to revisit and reinvent the symbolism of each card in thousands of ways, each interpretation following the same timeless format of the deck before it, but taking on the unique perspective and expression of their creator. My aim is the same as these artists before me; these pieces are meant to showcase both the immortal, unchanging nature of Tarot, as well as the aspect that shifts and transforms. Each piece is familiar, and yet, something you have never seen before. My use of collage intentionally echoes Tarot’s fundamental duality; it allows my pieces to be unique iterations, and yet, they are also recycled and reassembled, each made out of universal materials scattered in fragments throughout the world. When you look for it, Tarot is everywhere; it’s in our movies and music, our books and TV. Most importantly, Tarot exists inside of ourselves. This is what allows us to recognize it; the symbols in the cards are simply a reflection of an immaterial reality. Tarot comes from the very essence of our being, and the ways we all interact with the world. We are The Fool when we take a leap of faith, The Hermit when we are alone in introspection, The Tower when disaster strikes and our entire world is changed. It is this awareness, this intrinsic recognition inside of us, that allows me to find them and give them new form. I look for Tarot everywhere, in everything I can get my hands on. I find it in magazine racks and recycle bins, junk mail and children’s books. The archetypes call out to me, in pieces, scattered, and I bring them back together again. My hope is that my art helps others see it too.

column25: Hannah Freitag resides among pine trees and rain clouds in the Pacific Northwest with her partner and their chatty cat. The variety of landscapes that surround her - forests, beaches, mountains and plains – heavily inspire her art, as do all the plants and animals that call them home. She is a chaser of dreams, a multidisciplinary creative, and a lifelong student of the Divine. Hannah’s art is informed by a myriad of seemingly disparate personal interests; psychology, spirituality, sociology, physics, mythology, and nature, just to name a few. She also draws heavily on her life experience and hopes to inspire the same healing she has gone through in others; particularly within the realm of recovering from trauma and rediscovering the self through introspection. Following the same process as she does with her collages, Hannah practices breaking these interests and experiences apart into pieces, and then bringing them back together in a new form. Hannah’s faith in the guidance of the Universe is a huge factor in her art career and she incorporates a daily spiritual practice into her creative routines. Her art is based heavily around symbolic languages that have aided others’ spiritual journeys through centuries. Currently, she is working with the archetypes and format of the Tarot, a divinatory practice that she has personally used in her own spiritual evolution. Each of her art pieces uses both ancient and modern symbolism to represent phases of the spiritual journey, and are meant to resonate on a deep and intrinsic level.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
From tent cities to opulent palaces, I am alternately touched and outraged—yet all the while fascinated—by human invention. For all our attempts at sophistication and control, there is the inevitable random or abject element; the fly in the typewriter. My constructions, ranging from installations to wall works, examine this interaction through jarring juxtapositions of ordinary materials. In using recognizable (and surprising) detritus and surplus from everyday life, these junctures stand as metaphors for the contradictions we witness daily. Obstinate concrete molds itself into voluptuous curves formed by seams in thin organza. The refined shimmer of silk and brocade takes on the quality of innards as it issues forth from industrially stitched rubber. I often rely on raw materials and found objects to function as would text or imagery, allowing the history, function, metaphorical value and/or sensibility of each element to contribute to the work’s intent, along with my own investment of labor. My process of creation blends intent with accident. Through both humorously crude and painstakingly delicate handwork, I welcome the random element that exposes the absurdity, beauty, and vulnerability in our well-intended machinations.

column25: Victoria May is a first generation American, raised by resourceful German immigrant parents. She learned to sew at the age of 10 and has never stopped. After attending UCLA and receiving a BA in Design, one of her first real jobs was making custom wedding gowns. After switching to jobs in graphic design and publishing, she went on to earn an MFA from San Jose State University where she worked with Consuelo Underwood and figured out how to use textile construction methods to create mixed-media sculpture and installation that address the tenderness and absurdity in the human condition. Her work has since been exhibited at the Monterey Museum of Art, the Minnesota Street Project, the Headlands Center for the Arts, the San Jose Institute of Contemporary Art, the Maloof Foundation, the Craft Contemporary, and at Quotidian and other Los Angeles galleries. She has had residencies at Jentel Arts in Wyoming, Kala in Berkeley, California, the Lucid Art Foundation in Inverness, California and the Camera Obscura Art Lab in Santa Monica, California. She received a Santa Cruz County Rydell Fellowship and was named a Silicon Valley Artist Laureate. May exhibits nationally and internationally and has been represented by Conrad Wilde Gallery in Tucson, AZ and Don Soker in San Francisco. Most recently she and a collaborator were short-listed for the Australian Print Triennial.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is the inherent kindness that one must possess in order to be a good artist. You must be kind to your immediate surroundings in order to truly observe and appreciate them. You must share kindness with those in your community in order to be inspired by your fellow neighbor, become inspired by their routines, their dreams, their bedtime stories. As an artist, the practice of always learning, always asking questions, returning to what we do not yet understand, is the greatest kindness we can bestow upon ourself. I believe that art without using kindness as a muscle is simply a projection of our own egos out into the world, and there is enough of that existing already. I love that rigorous practice of my art has taught me how to be more kind to the nature that we are born from. The best part about being an artist is the knowledge that you cannot be everyone, cannot create everything, but that you must create something, and will create something, and by doing so, you create yourself.

column25: I see my artistic work as the air that exists between a kiss; there is a desire to bottle something, to hold on to every detail, yet there is also a desire to move forward into another realm that requires less description, and instead, more listening-- more abandon. I'm always slightly daunted by putting my artistic goal into words- so, instead, imagine that I am kissing dreams. My paintings exist as tokens of appreciation for when feelings of homesickness, loud joy, grief, and pure quiet all dance together on one two dimensional plane. My goal as an artist is to capture the tableaus and beings that present themselves to me in visions, combined with a desire to celebrate the steadfast iconography of southern gothic culture that I was raised by, juxtaposed with the metropolitan world in which I find myself now living. I share my art with the world in the hopes that it might furnish the desires of kindred hearts.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work plays at the intersection of gesture, ritual, and myth within the Black diasporic imagination. I rework, repurpose, and transform artifacts such as brown paper bags, Hispano cuaba soap, dominoes, and organic residues through actions like sewing, folding, submerging, cutting, burning, and layering. I use the subjective experiences of these artifacts or interactions to interrogate how they create social meaning and cultural norms. These explorations point to slippages of self and tensions around colonial relations to exoticization while expanding our capacity for pleasure, refusal, and liberation.

column25: A 24 year old multidisciplinary artist, environmental advocate, and owner of Fallon Loves Nature LLC, Fallon Rabin emphasizes sustainability within the fabrication process. Purposeful yet functional works of art highlight her intense spiritual bond with nature while spotlighting environmental and social issues through mixed media. She promotes art through mindfulness and strives to bring light and connection to all. At a young age, Fallon Rabin has been on 'One Simple Mission' to protect wildlife, nature, and the need for coexistence between humanity and nature. Born and raised in Southern California, Rabin is an intensely dedicated nature lover who paints/sculpts from her imagination, nature, and spiritual experiences. She creates art for a purpose, to foster her mission. Throughout her life, Rabin has practiced spirituality, reiki healing, and mindfulness. Since the age of eight, she has volunteered with non-profits including Heal the Bay and Bring the Elephant Home. In 2019, Rabin partnered with Wim Hof the Iceman and Bring the Elephant Home in Thailand, to organize a 5-day fundraiser/art installation to save elephants. Featuring 30 participants from 17 countries united to build the body of an elephant. Rabin’s environmental initiatives and community service efforts have earned her accolades from the mayors of Los Angeles, Santa Monica, and Thousand Oaks at the age of 16. In 2019, in response to her DAPL portfolio, Rabin was awarded a merit scholarship to study art at The School of the Art Institute of Chicago. She graduated with her BFA in May 2022. Her Universal Soul Connection Series was on view in the SAIC Spring 2022 Undergraduate Exhibition. Shortly after, in June 2022 Rabin was invited to showcase her Universal Soul Connection Series at Gary Spivey Spiritual Retreat and Gallery showing in Star, North Carolina. In September 2022, she displayed her new Soul Connection Series Part 2 at Gary Spivey Spiritual Retreat, having her second Solo Exhibition. In January 2023, she was invited to Las Vegas to showcase her recent Soul Connection work at Gary Spivey's one day seminar. In May 2023, Rabin displayed her Spiritual Connection To Nature series at Gary Spivey’s Spiritual Retreat and Gallery Showing. Shortly after, becoming a finalist for the International Boynes Young Emerging Artist Award in Australia. A few days later, some of her Spiritual Connection To Nature series was put on display in Guess McCall Art Gallery in New Orleans, LA. Rabin was then featured in an interview with Shoutout LA, and Spectrum TV Channel 1 News in September 2023. In November 2023 she helped host The Grateful Event: Holistic Retreat, Art-Walk Debut, and Fundraiser for Chumash Heritage National Marine Sanctuary, in Cambria CA. 2023. Showcasing her newest collection “Of My Heart” during the art-walk, emphasizing the importance of coexistence between humanity and nature.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In the intricate tapestry of my artistic journey, luminaries such as Ruth E. Carter, Gordon Parks, and George Lucas serve as guiding stars. Drawn to the profound narratives encapsulated within fabrics and the visual story, I, an artist of color, find my muse in the rich legacies of African and Native heritage. Like Carter, whose masterful costume designs breathe life into cinematic sagas, or Parks, who captured the essence of African American life through his lens, and Lucas, a visionary storyteller whose narratives resonate across galaxies, I aim to infuse my work with the spirit of cultural storytelling embeded in Afrofuturism. My work, often an array of painted textiles or canvas, becomes a realm where I paint with the vibrant hues of my ancestry. I am not merely an artist; I am a custodian of stories, weaving threads of tradition into contemporary expressions. My devotion to wearable art and painting becomes a vessel for heritage preservation—a testament to the resilience and beauty of communities often marginalized. Inspired by the radiant energy of the sun, I anchor many of my creations in the color orange. It becomes a symbol of vitality, warmth, and the life force inherent in my African and Native roots. This hue, carefully woven into my art, symbolizes the cyclical nature of life, growth, and the eternal connection between past, present, and future. When it comes to my wearable art, each piece is an offering—a wearable narrative that intertwines personal experiences with collective histories. Just as Carter crafted the iconic garments of Wakanda, I seek to encapsulate the essence of cultural pride and strength within every stitch. The canvas, whether fabric or linen, transforms into a visual chronicle, narrating stories of resilience, triumph, and the enduring legacy of my ancestors. As an artist of color, I navigate the intersection of heritage and modernity, channeling the wisdom of those who came before me. My work is a dialogue—a bridge between worlds that connects the ancestral vibrations with contemporary narratives. Through each stroke of color, and every meticulously chosen fabric, I aim to foster a visual language that speaks to the soul—an evocative tapestry that invites the viewer to not only witness but participate in the ongoing narrative of cultural richness and resilience.

column25: Yolande T Hunter-Johnson is an American artist from Queens, New York, celebrated for a diverse body of work that spans over two decades. A graduate of the prestigious High School of Art and Design, with further studies at the Fashion Institute of Technology (F.I.T) specializing in fashion art and design, Yolande has cultivated a unique artistic voice that transcends traditional boundaries. With a rich artistic journey that began over 20 years ago, Yolande’s exploration of different mediums reflects a commitment to pushing artistic boundaries. From acrylic on canvas, paper, and fabrics to dynamic art installations, each piece is a testament to an ever-evolving creative spirit. In 2004, Yolande had a breakthrough moment with the debut of her first art exhibit in London, becoming a notable member of the esteemed artist group, London Biennial. Since then, her work has been showcased in Paris, Germany, Rome, and, of course, her hometown of New York. Yolande’s art is a vibrant tapestry woven with influences from sci-fi, African and Native textiles, and the rich tradition of ancestral storytelling. The fusion of these diverse elements creates a visual narrative that transcends space and time. In the current year, Yolande is channeling her creative energy into recreating the magic of her inaugural exhibit, this time on home soil. The focus is on Afrofuturistic storytelling, exploring themes of space and time, and offering viewers a glimpse into a world where tradition meets the cosmos. Yolande invites audiences to engage with her art, to embark on a journey that transcends cultural and temporal boundaries. As a storyteller through color, texture, and form, she continues to weave narratives that celebrate the intersection of the ancient and the avant-garde, inviting viewers to explore the boundless possibilities of Afrofuturism.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I see my art as conceptual, or imaginative. I have a very free-form process. I focus on familiar patterns, such as those found in nature, as well as in the beauty of the human face. My art incorporates a love of spirituality, nature, mysticism, myth, world culture and science fiction themes. I create 2D and 3D digital Art using a variety of media and methods, some as outdated as they are unconventional. I mean, seriously, who uses PowerPoint for digital art? I use what I have and what I can easily access. For my current digital work, I’m using photo editing tools, effects apps and AI applications to enhance my art. I also use my own art (designs and animations, for example) to create new art. I do a lot of merging and double-exposure work with designs I’ve previously made. My intention through my art is to relay a message or share a visual story. Ultimately, I’m trying to reach a person’s inner child (mine included) — that core being who still has hope, a sense of magic, wonder, joy and humor.

column25: Veronica Martinez, or ArtLynette was born in Seattle. Her family background is military so she’s traveled a bit. Her grade school and HS years were spent in Germany, from where she was able to tour cities in Italy, and take an Art trip to Strasbourg, France. In the ensuing years, she and her family lived on a number of military bases across the U.S. She grew up shy: she began drawing at age 4 and wrote short stories to entertain herself around age 9. She was an avid reader growing up, and admits to being that person whose Fantasy novels often included a map. She also had a fascination for names, name etymology and world-building. She created otherworldly beings and sometimes even languages for her stories. Despite her creativity, she joined the U.S. Army at 21 to continue traveling, and to follow in her father’s footsteps. She wasn’t able to return to Germany (as she’d hoped), but was given the opportunity to serve in Sinai, Egypt instead. She believes this experience became a spiritual catalyst for her. During her time in the military, she married, had a child, and subsequently became a single parent. Following her military service, she returned to school in Washington state, then she and her son followed her parents to Alaska where she’s currently based.

column25: After receiving my BFA is December of 2014, which I initially wasn't planning to pursue, I took a 6 month break due to burnout. I was discouraged in school to do fantasy themed work at the time as it wasn't deemed 'modern art'. So I primarily focused on illustration during my education. I had joined in the Renaissance Faire community close to the end of my college education and found a thriving fantasy loving community there. So, at the end of my 3rd season there with my cast (2015), I decided to pursue what I wanted. I started my series and built my portfolio up and by May of that year I jumped into small Renaissance Festivals and built my art up to what it is today. Now, I have well over 400 personal works besides the client work I've done for children's books, book covers, and even have had the grand opportunity to do traditional work for Dungeons and Dragons. I continue to have the Renaissance Faire community be my main audience, but I want to keep telling my visual stories to all who might enjoy them.

With each piece of her art, she invites viewers into her cherished memories, hoping they may find familiar faces from their own lives. Her creative process begins with reflection, often centered around her family. She sifts through family photo albums retrieving treasured moments. Using these images as her foundation, she meticulously sketches out her compositions before breathing life into them with an array of tape varieties. Her choice of tape as a primary medium is rooted in its vast spectrum of colors and patterns. She blends these diverse elements, seeking out compelling contrasts in color or relationships between organic and inorganic shapes in each work. Cheryiah has recently begun incorporating the block printing technique into her work, adding depth and dimension to her practice. Cheryiah currently calls Waipahu, Hawaii, her home.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Clarisse Abelarde received a BFA in drawing and painting at CSU Long Beach. Some significant influences on her work as a Filipina immigrant are adaptation and the changes that occur when assimilating to a culture foreign to her upbringing. Displacement and disorientation are prominent themes in the work, attempting to pinpoint her place in society and dealing with issues of identity and representation. Her work was more carefully made and calculated while forming her education at Los Angeles City College, using subject matter dealing with daily life's monotony and the impact of social and digital media on identity. As she develops her more recent work, Abelarde’s paintings echo the visceral effect of the abstract expressionist movement, emphasizing paint physicality and raw emotion. Questioning our immersion into the digital realm and how the fusion of real and virtual might affect our psyche, the drawings and paintings investigate the in-between space of both worlds converging using an abstract-mimetic hybrid approach to determine imagery and content. I am interested in space fragmentation and the active use of imagination or invention to elevate the work, pushing its natural elements using graphic shapes or high color saturation. Digital devices and screens have been integrated into the human experience. Cutting-edge technology, social media, video games, and more are fundamental features of modern life. Still, their long-term psychological effects haven’t been explored nearly as much as how frequently we use and interact with artificial intelligence. We are running a large-scale experiment on ourselves as a society, with little to no inklings of its unintended consequences. As the statistics of depression, anxiety, and eating disorders rise and we continue expanding into the metaverse, it becomes increasingly crucial to question the technological effects on our spirit as physical beings existing in a computerized, programmed world. Expressing ideas through glitches, pixels, and shapes to fabricate a believable space corresponding to a figurative focal point, I use various paint application techniques ranging from thick globs of paint to significantly thinned underpainting. Andy Warhol and his obsessive, repetitive images of cultural iconography influenced the repetitive aspects and use of screenprinting. The repetition in my work also becomes an icon - a reference to the prevalence of emojis or other symbolic digital elements in social media. Taking inspiration from the ab-ex and impressionist art movements while including and disrupting components of realism allows me to find a teetering balance between the two worlds that is neither grounded nor completely illogical. Fragmentation is a metaphor for being in multiple places at once. Most commonly, my subject matter expresses outrageous behavior for attention on the internet, while the medium of using oil paint on canvas urges the audience to slow down and analyze the imagery. This contradiction is necessary to show the antithetical relationship between the fast-paced digital world and the human need to reflect, ponder, and slow down. Ultimately, I aim to express the emotion of overwhelming external influences, information overload, and intense internal retrospection concerning the mundane.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
We are inherently tied to the nature we try so often to control and separate ourselves from. My work centers on the call back, the ache, to return to a space where the bounds between 'me', the earth, humans, plants, and animals soften and intertwine as they all rejoin as echos of the same energy. I enjoy creating playful, colorful, and slightly abstracted renditions of harmony that invite the viewer to 'fill in' the spaces to complete the perception of the piece. I work on large canvases (often 24x36 in or larger), predominantly with acrylic paint due to it's ability to dry and layer quickly (yet feel the guilt of working with plastic based paint), as well as oil pastels. I am curious about queerness, authenticity, and the feminine gaze in my work: I want to honor and appreciate the subjects as they are and maintain sensitivity to subjects that were predominantly objectified in the past (ie. female bodies); I want to give femininity a space to be soft yet intense, independent and passionate, restful and curious; I want to find ways to portray masculinity that feels kind, giving, and grounding; I want to embody the constant exchange in which we engage in as we inhale and exhale, that we are just as much part of our environment as is a tree or a squirrel or a rock. Our streams of consciousness, awareness of the lessons we’ve learned and are learning, how we engage our senses and minds, can be directly impacted by what (and who) we surround ourselves with. Much of my inspiration is drawn from these interactions with others and with the environment I find myself in or called to, with the books I'm currently reading, and listening to the ongoing conversation of history and the present moment. I hope that this art finds people who are aligning with their own 'beingness' and inner sense of calmness that can be rather difficult to find these days.

column25: Most of my abstract paintings are filled with bright colors that make me happy. I look at my paints to decide on the main color. I have used many colors in the past, but I now try to limit myself to 3 colors. My favorite techniques are using sponges and feathers. I use broad strokes of acrylic paint on canvas, but sometimes I paint on small wooden crafts or recycled materials. I don't like the smell of oil paint, but I like the way that acrylic paint flows off my brushes and dries faster. My paintings are unplanned. I never know how they will end 'until the colors pop.” Use your imagination when you look at my paintings. I have autism spectrum disorder and don’t drive. I am inspired and motivated to paint because it makes me happy and gives me something to do when I am alone. My only sibling and best friend, Zach, was killed in a motorcycle accident in 2011. I was more isolated then and found myself painting more. The isolation of my studio gave me freedom to create. My paint and canvas do not judge me because I have a disability. Painting helped me get through a really challenging time just like it helps me deal with everyday problems. Painting is my profession and a big part of my therapy. Painting helped me get to a happier place in my life. I have always been isolated due to my disability, but it makes me happy to hear people say that they like my art. Art connects me to my community and to the larger art world. I am grateful for the opportunities that art gives me.

column25: Hedy Torres is an artist dedicated to shedding light on the experiences of immigrants and street vendors in the United States. Through her artistic practice, she endeavors to challenge prevailing prejudices, labels, and stereotypes that have historically marginalized these individuals, instead celebrating their unique and invaluable contributions to society, economy, and culture. Her ongoing series, Soy, De Aquí y Allá ( I am from here and from there), intimately intertwines her personal narrative as a Mexican immigrant with the stories of those she encounters on her artistic journey. In these portraits, Hedy showcases her subjects engaged in their work, skillfully incorporating personal details and narratives through a diary-style background. Presently, Hedy's artistic endeavors serve as a profound tribute to the lives of street vendors, materialized through captivating oil portraits on wood-free canvas and evocative charcoal sketches on paper. Employing a range of tools, including stencils, markers, and meticulously placed signs, she creates immersive backgrounds that lend depth and convey personal narratives. The choice of wood-free canvas symbolizes the fluid mobility characteristic of street stands and resonates with the essence of her art. column25: My name is Linda Jurkiewicz. A woman’s challenge of self-determination dominates my mind and work. I grew up on the cusp of the Second Wave of Feminism and I believed that I was on the tipping point of women getting their rights. I was sure that my generation was going to be the recipient of this new open-mindedness and fairness for women. Today, I continue to be reminded that the scale not only did not complete its tip but has reversed its course in many areas of women’s lives. My insights are transmitted through personal narratives, mine, and others. I want the viewer to be reminded of the daily conflicts experienced by women in our culture; unequal domestic expectations, unpaid roles as caretakers, sexualization and exploitation of women and girls, and generational struggles with body image. My recent work focused on historical female figures in religion and myth as spiritual guides for contemporary women. I want women to trust their ability to act on behalf of themselves, and to have the conviction that they have the right to do so. I explore these serious issues thoughtfully, with my own sense of humor. By using repetition in my work I symbolize the importance of continued planned action to create change in one’s life. I utilize found objects and repurposed cloth to stress that anyone can work with what they have. Cloth itself holds human history within its tactile experience. It is omnipresent in our past and present lives, our homes, and our cultural way of remembering. By using stitch as mark-making on cloth I can tell a story with gesture and imagery to a contemporary audience. My art form honors the collective work of women and seeks to call out an essential reminder of our importance in this world and the struggles that continue to be unheard.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My paintings are essentially about existential uncertainty, as seen through the merging contradictions of disparate elements of my life and the wider culture and history of which I am a part. The interpenetration occurs where histories of abstract mark-making, developing iconography, and sub-cultural motifs are placed in relationships on the picture plane. Colloquialisms and urban vernacular appear alongside abstract art history, references to Brutalist architecture, comix, sk8, 1% MC life, dance cultures - to explore an extended and negotiated dialogue between ideas of the political and class division, race, sub-cultural resistance and tribalism via the parallax viewpoints that extend the critique via issues of masculine identity, vanitas, memory and nostalgia, in the context of a transient experience. They are how I order the world, somewhere inside my mind, while maintaining an outward dialogue with the viewer. They are built through an intuitive initial phase that is then 'modified' by formal concerns and the addition of these elements. My approach is somewhat akin to curating - selecting separate, disparate motifs and approaches and assembling on to the picture plane to form a new web of ideas. These aspects remain isolated and existential, although in a relationship with each other, expanding their potential and giving rise to complex structures of understanding, questioning and possibility . I incorporate elements from real life, in the form of badges, patches and photo transfer images onto the surface as a reference to existing sub-cultures and realities. The function is of discursive formations between images, gestures, signs, symbols in a fluid, contingent and multi-layered network of meanings and possible relationships. They incorporate, on the same picture plane, a mixture of historical reference, auto-biography and sub-cultural content in a shifting soup of knowledge, conjecture, opinion, guesswork, and the irrational.

I use many different mediums to convey my messages such as large-scale installation, ceramic sculpture, various painting mediums, resin, photography, and henna. I find that each new exploration of mediums allows me to connect to my thoughts in different ways. While I embody many different styles and techniques, my work is unified by motifs of nature. I enjoy collecting natural materials such as flowers, stones, insects, wood, and even animal bones as they cross my path and incorporating them into my works. Using animals and plants in a figurative manner gives me a template to portray human emotions without the assumptions that accompany human identity. Portraying the human ego through scenes of nature rather than direct depictions of humans clarifies the connections between humans and nature in the purest of forms. My artwork explores and redefines the line between humans and nature through abstraction. I hope that viewers can connect to the human themes and emotions in my work while being inspired by the beautiful delivery I offer as well.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Nia’s creative practice explores the ways collage, writing, and traditional & digital painting methods can be used to tell stories through design. She believes that storytelling in any medium can function as an excellent way to combat ignorance, give a voice to the otherwise unheard, and bridge the divides wicked problems create. In both her visual and written work, Nia is passionate about inclusion and sharing the experiences of marginalized communities, making a point to depict more than just trauma narratives. As a designer, she develops experimental toys and games that act as catalysts for meaningful conversations about race, history, gender, and identity. These creations function both as sources of joyous play and as tools for profound exploration. At one end of the spectrum, they offer pure, unadulterated playfulness; at the other, they serve as springboards for thoughtful discussions. She aims to extend an invitation to dialogue rather than demand it; if individuals find solace and joy in her designs apart from the discourse, she is delighted to offer that respite. This malleability is paramount, as it allows individuals to engage at their own pace without feeling pressured to immediately digest complex topics and emotions. By imbuing her creations with this dual nature, she offers a bridge that encourages curiosity, reflection, and ultimately, connection.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I feel abundantly lucky to have discovered that I am an artist. Although I work every day of the week to make art, apply for grants and residencies, and manage ongoing projects, since I have devoted myself to the pursuit of this career I have been (exhausted but) extremely happy. It is true what they say about loving your work - doing what you are passionate about gives you this very deep well of energy and resilience, which makes the grind of actually being an artist possible. Artists have such a special role in society. They engage with the spiritual aspects of our existence, and they have the ability to sit with the reality of the world and create some physical object in response that is both apt and beyond words. What I love most about being an artist is the joy of constantly being a student - learning a new medium or technique, learning from a place, from our history, from a feeling or an object, or from the ever evolving now. I know that this agile and responsive stance means I will always be discovering a new version of myself, and that I will need to remain open to life surprising me.

column25: I make art that, like me, is often quite chipper and a little subversive. I paint enigmatic creatures from my forest home, put on shows of live animation with paper and light, and sculpt enormous animals from garbage and bamboo. I create art hoping that a surprising interaction with a street performance or piece of public art can restore the brilliance and immediacy of life for a passerby, even just for a moment. Most often my art reaches a conclusion of joy, but not in ignorance of the inequities in our society, or the pain and absurdity of being. Instead, through mirthful imagery and mythic themes, it gently asks us to remember why anything matters at all. I often use inexpensive materials like paper mache, cardboard, cardstock, photocopiers, trash, and acrylic paints. Not only do they lend themselves to experimentation, but they are also perfect for skill sharing, and empowering more people to make art. I am greatly influenced by my work with the giant puppet company Paperhand Puppet Intervention, where I learned art can be uproariously alive, get grubby from being touched, and fall apart from use. My work is similarly intended for a broad audience. Almost all of the art that has helped me understand something important, or made the world feel like a big and wonder filled place, was art created for people uninitiated into the fine art world. I am interested in creating captivating, entertaining art that can still be smart and convey complex themes. I have a very multidisciplinary practice, which sustains a freshness and energy in my work. Performance is one natural home for my explorations in physical theater, creative writing, music composition, and several forms of visual art. I have worked for many years building and performing giant puppets, devising nonverbal comedic scenes, and producing small puppet shows. I love the focus and responsive communication demanded by live performance, and using my entire body to bring stories to life. Above all, I am interested in exploring what it means to tell a great story, one which takes its audience on a memorable journey of developing emotions and perspectives, in any medium. My work frequently lives at the intersection of art and science. I often feel a paradoxical solace and unmooring when considering the proportions of our universe and existence. I feel elated in science museums, and a kinship with scientists, who believe that creative inquiry into the natural world can deepen our fervor. The charismatic megafauna I depict in my public art is intended to elicit a curiosity about creatures too small, slippery, or far away to see. Animals typically regarded with fear or indifference are instead reemployed as friendly ambassadors into the biosphere. I believe simple and compelling representations of scientific information can inspire advocacy, and also enhance the reverence and wonder we feel in the world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
One consistent belief that I've held during all of my experience as an art teacher, administrator, facilitator, and practitioner is that all humans are creative. Demystifying the creative process and bringing others into the mind of an artist encourages others to explore their own creative spirit, and that is part of the ethos of my practice. Everyone is touched by art in one way or another, and we all have the ability to access the joys of making. I believe that art and symbolism are powerful tools that help us find peace and meaning in our lives. They nurture our imagination, our capacity to love, and our openness to appreciating small joys in life. So, to answer this question simply, the best thing about being an artist is inspiring others to think more deeply about art and life.

column25: I transform everyday, seemingly unremarkable moments in my home into abstract, colorful imagery. While the real moment itself is simple and serene, my internal experience may be very different. Using a wide variety of color and collage material, I aim to create visually complex images that suggest a kind of psychological activity in a space that is otherwise quiet or still. I am a mixed media collage artist and a scrap enthusiast. Most of the collage materials I use are either found or repurposed from previous collages, drawings, sketches, and paintings. My work is not only an exploration of my internal experience of the mundane, but it is also a celebration of the materials I use. By breaking down and repurposing material, small remnants of old projects reappear in newer projects. I am so fascinated by the fact that we and most things in our diverse world are all made up of the same elements. Those elements are whittled down and reshaped to form new compounds, and sometimes we can visibly observe a long, multifaceted history within a single object. The formal aspects of my practice emulate this process, like cracking open a sedimentary rock and seeing billions of years of compounded material, or like peering into a junk drawer to find a lifetime's worth of quotidian objects. My mixed media collage practice points towards the cyclical nature of the universe, a process that I believe is similar to the routines, rituals, and thoughts that shape our day-to-day lives. In pointing towards this similarity, I aim to uplift what is often overlooked to a place of awe, curiosity, and reverence.

column25: I use vinyl, paper, and other media on a variety of surfaces to create mixed media collages and murals printed on vinyl, paper and other adhesive substrates. I traditionally have used found images to make these works, but I have transitioned to collaborating with photographers to source photos to create with. My process usually begins with creating my pieces digitally, transferring the layers of that composition to a physical medium, then assembling it. The digital process allows me to apply effects and resize my source images to create larger scale work. The softwares I use also allows me to develop unique assembly and fabrication strategies before going into production. I also design and create installations and interactive oracles from various found and fabricated materials. I draw inspiration from spiritual principles and esoteric concepts found across the African Diaspora to create Surrealist artworks that serve as portals into other worlds, and viewstations of Queer, Black people, culture, and identity in an alternate dimension. I use these materials and approaches to encourage the viewer to experience and seek the subtle and unseen worlds, while reflecting on their place in it. I use my work to challenge the status quo, and disrupt the conventions of what we know to be cisgendered, heteronormative, and patriarchal ideologies, while offering something beautiful and uplifting in its place. Through this creative process, I seek to make real a way of being in, thinking of, and viewing the universe that celebrates, preserves, and restores historically excluded communities.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Places form along edges, created by people as they interact with and experience a space. Spatial awareness is integral to both my two and three-dimensional work, expressed conceptually, symbolically and physically. Reflection on the complexities of urban ecologies and their relationship to the public explore the parts of the world and mind where places are made. Collections of memories and thoughts can illustrate how a place becomes and who shapes it. Logos, objects, colors, and icons hint at what defines spatial experience. Using sketching, collage, installation and social practice, I aim to examine what a place means to individuals or communities and how that meaning is created through interaction between space, time and people. My goal is to empower communities and individuals to exercise their power to shape their environments, to combat feelings of impotency and helplessness in the face of both climate change and hostile urban design. Recognizing that we actively construct the places we inhabit allows us to question our own and others power, taking control and accepting responsibility for our ability to create and destroy. The practice lends itself multidisciplinary lines of enquiry, resulting in work that exists at the crossroads of art, urban studies, psychology, cultural heritage, ethnobotany and more. Sustainability is of large importance in my practice, both thematically and in the media I use. My collage work is created almost entirely with found paper and handmade adhesives, on cardboard. Many traditional media in contemporary fine art use extractive materials and methods with large amounts of waste and/or harmful byproducts. My goal in experimenting with non-traditional finishing is to reframe the aesthetics of artmaking to include more sustainable ideas of what makes a “professional” or “finished” work of art. Projects include workshops designed to create community-sourced visions of local neighborhoods and cities, drawing installations tracking social connections, and a vibrant sketchbook practice illustrating my personal journey through and within urban environments.

column25: Nora Byrne is an artist and educator based between the U.S. and Turkey. They use their international experience to raise complex and unique perspectives on place, nature and artmaking through a diverse and multi-disciplinary approach to visual art. With a Master of Arts in Visual Art and Visual Communication Design from Sabancı University in Istanbul, Turkey; a Master of Arts in Museum & Gallery Practice from the University of London in Doha, Qatar and a Bachelor of Arts in Art History from The College of Wooster in Wooster, OH, Byrne’s work leans towards installation, drawing on years spent working and volunteering in many different areas of the art world. Throughout their career, Byrne has participated in numerous exhibitions that have showcased their artistic talent and unique vision. They are a regular contributor to the Mahalla Festival, an international arts festival focused on cities and their urban fabric, for which they contributed and installation in 2021 and a collage mural in 2023. Their collage and design work has been displayed in esteemed venues such as the Sakıp Sabancı Museum and the Spazju Kreattiv. In addition to exhibitions, Byrne has been involved in various residencies and projects that have expanded their artistic practice. They were an Artist in Residence at Endemia: Valletta in 2023, which involved leading workshops and hosting an open studio.They served as a curator for 'Far afield & from next door,' an online exhibition at Nadas’a Bırak Online Art Residency, and co-founded the Nadas’a Bırak Online Artist Residency. Byrne also curated the online art project/exhibition 'Face to Face // Yüz Yüze' as part of Slow Art Day in 2020. Dedicated to sharing their knowledge and skills with others, Byrne has been a guest artist for various events and interviews, including at ATÖLYE Bomantiada and for Aposto Europe. They have delivered workshops and classes on a range of topics, to students from a variety of backgrounds, in collaboration with projects like Vuċijiet Beltin in Malta and Kite Runner with Afghan political cartoonists. A lifelong teacher, Byrne hopes that her art inspires visitors to consider new perspectives and broaden their horizons.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a versatile creative, my profound drive lies in the active expression of self. I believe that the profound vulnerability inherent in my visual art has the power to unleash emotional freedom for those seeking their energetic voices. Raised in an African household, I cultivated an expansive imagination guided by my mother's constant reminder to 'express yourself.' Creating became my source of vitality and liberation. The journey into painting commenced in May 2020 as a response to global uncertainties, a therapeutic endeavor to alleviate anxiety. However, the true revelation unfolded a year later, following the loss of my mother to Covid. In the face of this profound personal loss, I discovered a newfound purpose — to employ this evolving art form as a conduit for healing and transformation. As an artist with synesthesia, my creations are profoundly influenced by the interplay of colors, textures, and the fluidity of paint. Delving into the myriad layers of grief, my art seeks to inspire authentic self-expression and catalyze healing from the depths of the soul.

column25: Embarking on her visual artistic journey in 2020, Lulu Fall, a self-taught abstract artist from Washington, DC, found solace and expression in the world of painting during the tumultuous global pandemic. With the unexpected loss of her mother in 2021, Lulu's approach to art underwent a profound metamorphosis, marked by intricate layering and blending techniques guided by her innate creative intuition. In February 2022, Lulu unveiled her inaugural solo exhibition, 'Lulu Fall: An Introduction to Hues,' at Café 1923 in Hamtramck, MI. This showcase not only introduced her unique play with color, fluidity, and balance but also revealed her synesthetic connection, associating colors with moods and sounds. A second solo exhibition, 'Grief: A Visual Diary,' unfolded at Congregation Detroit in January 2023, unveiling 20 deeply personal pieces that demonstrated Lulu's ability to channel grief and pain into poignant and relatable art. Beyond solo exhibitions, Lulu's art has graced the spaces of various group exhibitions in metro Detroit, including Irwin House Gallery, Detroit Artists Market (DAM), Nicolena Creates Gallery, Berkley Coffee,BasBlue, and the Torch of Wisdom Foundation's 2023 Silent Art Auction. In 2021, Lulu extended her artistic reach by launching her Etsy shop, LuluFallAbstracts. In addition to her visual artistry, Lulu Fall is a multifaceted talent, excelling as a singer, award-winning songwriter, and accomplished Broadway/TV actor. Her theatrical credits span notable productions such as HAIR (National tour/Broadway), Natasha, Pierre, and the Great Comet of 1812 (Broadway/off-Broadway), and Skittles: The Musical (one-night event, Broadway). On the small screen, Lulu has made memorable appearances in Random Acts of Flyness (HBO) and The Last O.G. (TBS). Lulu's musical prowess extends to her discography, featuring albums like 'The Unpaved Road,' 'Heal: Part 1,' and 'Between Two Worlds.' Recognized for her singing and songwriting prowess, she has garnered accolades from prestigious competitions such as the Great American Song Contest, Sarah Vaughan International Jazz Competition, and International Song Competition (ISC). Her latest musical venture, 'Transform,' released in September 2022, further solidifying her status as a dynamic and impactful artist across multiple creative realms.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Through art, I embrace the absurdity of being thrown into existence as conscious living creatures. We did not consent to come into existence, and any reason for life remains a mystery. Within the chaos of existential dread, I encountered consolation in creating my own purpose – continuous growth. We can choose to live intentionally so that we can die feeling grateful. The practice of painting allows me to keep learning every day. Human interactions, ranging from our relationships with our own subconscious to relationships with each other, fascinate me. The compositions in my paintings originate from an analysis of moments in my life that I’ve had the hardest time processing. Diving into cultural idioms, stories, and philosophical texts, I wrestle with the feelings that keep me up at night.

column25: My greatest passion is to see the public space used for artistic endeavors creating beauty or creating conversation that encourages us to be more human and less cogs in the cities subjected to advertisements designed to make us unhappy. I try to do my part in this idealistic goal by helping others see their spaces as places they can change and challenge. Much of my work is community created to help groups of people realize they can push the boundaries of what can be accomplished with community art. Refusing to settle for the mundane and ordinary, I want to show the world that allowing individuals to shape their narratives, creating a powerful reflection of their identity, and instilling a sense of pride and ownership can create extraordinary artistic expressions. Within my portfolio, you will find large-scale installations that pay tribute to victims of injustice, vibrant murals that celebrate resilience, and captivating pieces that shed light on marginalized communities. In each mural I deliberately choose to paint local people, sending a powerful message that the public space is not reserved solely for the famous or the conventionally beautiful. I want to boldly declare that everyone deserves representation in the public space. My hope for each mural and community mural is that they become a catalyst for change—a visual story that evokes emotions, builds empathy, challenges societal norms, and ignites meaningful conversations.

column25: HOW DID I GET HERE? I spent my career in the professional arts working for clients as a brand marketer and graphic designer. Despite my relative success as a commercial artist, something was missing. I am here to do something else and have a more profound personal mission. Though I didn't know it then, in 2005, I took the first steps on a journey that would change my life. Somewhere between Hurricane Katrina and Al Gore, it became clear that our world was in trouble. In the mid-2000s, climate change was an imminent threat that we were only starting to recognize. A disquiet within me and an urge to use my skills for something more positive drove me to learn as much as I could about sustainability and living a life of meaning. I poured myself into my studies - enough to earn a Master's Degree in Sustainable Design. Yet, it still didn't feel like this was enough. Fast forward to 2020 and the pandemic. This pause gave me time to reflect and finally allow the artist within to emerge. At last, I have regained my artistic voice through the joy I experience as a printmaker. Everything has led me here. Printmaking is both messy and precise. I could easily print my designs digitally but would lose the hands-on quality I crave. When my hands are inky, I feel alive and at my happiest. For inspiration, I draw on influences such as Corita Kent, The Guerilla Girls, Barbara Kruger, Andy Warhol, Shepard Fairey, Anthony Burrill, and Keith Haring to name a few. Printing allows me the opportunity to experiment and learn. It is all at once scary, thrilling, and liberating. I am deeply troubled by the inaction and political divides our world is experiencing. I feel a certain amount of powerlessness and know I am not alone. My art gives me back some sense of power and agency. I aspire to create work that engages the viewer and asks them to see things from a different perspective. I want people to recognize that they have the capacity for change. It is successful if my art can influence even one person to be a force for hope and positivity in the world. Hope + Action = Change

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My multimedia projects explore the significance of blurry vision as a metaphorical lens through which we perceive the world. I often combine permanent and impermanent media in order to present visual ambiguity and challenge conventional notions of clarity and definition. The transformation through obscuring imagery symbolizes the fluidity of human experience, where boundaries between reality and abstraction converge. Life, rarely lived in stark focus, is portrayed through my work, embracing the nuance and complexity inherent in the human condition. As viewers navigate my work, they are prompted to reconsider reliance on precision and certainty. The deliberate obscurity encourages a shift in perspective, where emotions, memories, and perceptions blend into a rich tapestry of interconnectedness. Blurry vision becomes a metaphor for the uncertainty inherent in our existence. In a world where truth and falsehood often blur, my work serves as a visual commentary on the subjectivity of reality. The deliberate introduction of uncertainty challenges observers to question preconceptions and embrace the beauty found in the indistinct and the unresolved. Reflecting the fragility of perceptions and the impermanence of certainties, my work serves as a mirror for the constant ebb and flow of understanding in the human journey. Simultaneously, my exploration extends into the realm of living in the gray — a celebration of the beauty inherent in life's uncertainties and complexities. Recognizing that life seldom adheres to absolutes, I navigate the nuanced spaces where contradictions coalesce and dichotomies blur into subtle gradations. In these ambiguous realms, inspiration transcends dualistic thinking.

column25: Kendra Dandy is a multidisciplinary artist known for creating colorful illustrations and cheeky, smile-inducing patterns featuring a variety of whimsical characters. Using familiar icons mixed with bold colors and patterns, she creates her own unique world that commands attention. Experimentation is at the forefront of Kendra’s creative practice and she fully believes in embracing imperfections, playing with odd combinations and using recurring themes in her work such as cheetahs, makeup, tropical foliage and cocktails. Rotating between painting, printmaking and digital illustration on the iPad allows her versatility and freedom with mixing colors, ideas and forms. The cheetahs present in Kendra’s works are representative of her moods and give insight into the life and mind of an introvert. At times they portray her hobbies such as dancing and seeking out the best craft cocktails worldwide, and other times they reflect her desire for leisure and the ability to exist as is, peacefully without further explanation. They are a blank canvas for human emotions that can range from angsty, joyful, sarcastic, or just plain neutral. With gazes that are either direct and confident, a strong side eye, or staring into space, the cheetahs engage with the viewer in different ways and establish a relatability that creates an individual connection with each piece. No matter what mood or activity the cheetahs portray, they are simply honest and unapologetically themselves, which is how Kendra always strives to be.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Christy finds beauty in ghastly horror and has spent her entire life trying to capture and share that love of the horrific with others through her paintings and films. She finds magic in the macabre and beauty in brutality. She embraces the fun of adventuring into the forbidden and she chases after monsters into the very realms of nightmares. She likes to explore the darkness and see what lies hidden in the shadows, often challenging conventional art norms and pushing the viewer to think and see more creatively while not turning away from the grotesque. She believes that every image should tell a larger story and prompt the viewer to think, to feel and to explore. She is profoundly inspired by the juxtaposition between classic beauty and extreme brutality, as found throughout history and in many works of classic literature and this is a reoccurring theme in her work. In addition to interpreting brutality in classic literature, common themes in Christy’s work include ridiculously bizarre accidents, tragic events in history, iconic moments in horror cinema, women in peril and the sad reality of murdered women being discarded like trash by callous madmen. She works primarily in watercolor and acrylic and her ideas are heavily inspired by the art of horror films, horror comics, pulp art illustrations, pin-up art and twentieth century true crime magazines. Her artistic influences include classic painters such as Goya and Carravaggio, comic artists such as Jack Davis, Graham Ingels and Berni Wrightson, pulp illustrators such as Norman Saunders, H.J Ward and Frank Frazetta, as well as cinematographers such as Dean Cundey and Mario Bava. These influences are highly visible in her artwork and in her cinematography. Christy looks forward to continuing her artistic journey into nightmarish worlds of fantasy and encouraging young artists to follow.

column25: Christy Savage is a Sacramento-based mid-career artist with over 30 years professional experience, working primarily in visual and media arts. She specializes in creating horrific watercolor and acrylic paintings and she produces independent exploitation horror films. She is an accomplished artist and filmmaker, as well as producer of a successful film festival and many community events. She has also spent time as an art teacher and has curated many arts festivals for students. Since a young age, Christy was driven to create fantastic images of terror and death. From the time she won her first art contest at age 4, she knew she was destined to pursue a career in the arts and she has worked hard to hone her skills. She sold her first painting at age 18 and has worked professionally in the arts since, exploring multiple disciplines. She has worked in the fields of commercial art, graphic design, portraits, murals and scenic design and she has participated in many exhibits and group shows as well as solo shows. Christy’s love of genre cinema has been a driving factor in her art and she has simultaneously pursued a career in the world of low budget independent filmmaking, making feature horror films with her husband and collaborator, Darin Wood. She is skilled in set design and construction, photography, film and video production, cinematography, theatre production, film festival coordination and exhibition, and video editing. Christy also has many years experience teaching art to children and working with the local community to bring arts programs to students. She firmly believes in the power of the arts to enhance and strengthen a community and works hard to promote, encourage and develop the arts in Sacramento. At the current stage of her career, Christy is focusing more on painting and less on collaborative projects such as arts events and teaching. While her main focus will continue to shift from time to time, she is now concentrating on fine art paintings for exhibits and direct sales to private collectors. Her work is held in collections throughout the United States.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being able to create physical objects with my own hands is one of the most satisfying things I am able to do. Materializing abstract ideas into tangible works has always felt magical, and that feeling has never diminished for me. Even more so is connecting with others through my art practice. I see being an artist as a public service role. How do I make my community feel seen? How do I bring joy, beauty, and meaning to others? How do I preserve traditional craft, and use my practice as means for public education on fiber craft? Although there is a temptation to appeal to a mass audience, I ultimately find the most purpose in serving my small audience of peers. I often think of conversations I’ve had with strangers interacting with my work, and how special it is, in our cynical, overly saturated, collapsing world, to form a genuine connection through art.

column25: Merging traditional hand embroidery with new media-based imagery, I create coded queer portraits defying time and borders. The embroidered portraits of “Send Nudz” are an ongoing series started in 2017, which contains over 30 works. Each one features a single figure and at least one hand. They are all based on nude/semi-nude photos sent between two queer lovers, my ex-partner and myself, over the course of three years. The practice started as an attempt at embodying long-distance relationships in material. The stretching of time over 6 different time zones and the desire to physically manifest the person who only exists in digital form. I work in contemporary Blackwork embroidery, a type of geometrical repeat pattern counted embroidery, popular in 16th century England. Embroidery is significant for its slowness, defying productivity, and grounding in material reality. It is also a highly gendered, feminized art form in the West, often looked down upon and considered a “decorative art”, a term often used in a derogatory manner. While the technique I use of traditional Blackwork, the color palette of red, black, and white is that of Slavic redwork. I am drawn to the red in the Slavic embroidery, symbolizing blood and land, as an anchor to my ancestors who were killed on that land or sought refuge from. In the portraits, the Hebrew language gets codified into patterns. The politics of gender in Hebrew are convoluted and volatile as grammatical gender is integral to the majority of words, and what is regarded as “neutral” is, in fact, male. This gendered reality is utilized by feminists and queers to signal amongst ourselves, as we further politicize female grammatical gender to covertly signal to each other our sexual and political inclinations. The words in the patterns are all in female grammatical gender, ranging from suggestive to sexually explicit. Codifying these words into the works falls within the lineage of queer visual coding: illegible to the majority of people, but highly legible to the intended few.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Here are some of the best things about being an artist: Self expression, there is no better way (in my opinion) to express oneself than with a brush and paint. My painting is my refuge, I go to it for the pleasure of creating, but also to relieve stress, find peace and calm. I am my best self when I'm painting. Painting is more effective than therapy. The telling of a good story without the need for words. 'A picture is worth a thousand words' in a painting I can convey complex and sometimes multiple ideas in a single still image, which conveys its meaning or essence more effectively than a mere verbal description. Painting has so much emotional value. When I paint a portrait for a client (whether a personal portrait, a pet portrait, or a family portrait) I have captured that moment, that memory forever, and this gives me a lot of fulfillment, especially when my commissioner is happy with the portrait. Art as social and political activism: art is powerful in conveying ideas and breaking them down. I painted in response to the epidemic of gun violence in America, and I painted in response to domestic and sexual violence. And I believe in the impact and power of my paintings on those who consume it.

My work embodies a unique strength expounded by the oppressed. I explore race, history, and intersectionality. My ideas are often expressed in a graphic style that incorporates realism, minimalism, and hard-edge painting. My mixed media works show individuals in and out of site-specific or abstracted black and white spaces, framed by or shaped like rectangles and squares, a metaphor for segregation, defiance, and freedom. The black space represents Black power, and the white space represents white supremacy. Currently, the blue can represent three things: the omnipresent police state, the sky, which represents unity and the creation of just futures, and water, which I use to convey rebirth.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For me, there are two equally meaningful aspects of being an artist. The first, more individual experience, is of being in the flow of a creative process. I love the feeling of improvising with an idea to see where it can go. This can happen for me in several different ways - either while drawing an idea by hand, or testing out new ways of knotting textile fibers, or modeling a structure in Rhino (3d modeling software). In any medium, the most pleasurable process is starting with an initial move (a shape, a knot, a line in space), and just seeing what's possible with that idea by testing all of its different permutations. The pure pleasure of improvisation feels infinite and I always wish I had more time to spend in that state of mind. The second, equally meaningful dimension, is a collective one. Since my projects exist in public spaces, the moment that they open is incredibly moving, because that's when I finally get to see people playing and exploring the project. Kids are usually the first ones to explore, and I usually get emotional seeing kids run towards a project and start climbing all over it. They immediately find ways of interacting with it that I never even imagined (hanging, swinging, jumping), and seeing the project bring them joy is the most meaningful thing about doing this work. Most of my projects have been temporary, and it's really fulfilling for me when people tell me that they wish they were permanent, especially when I hear that from children.

column25: I combine my experience in studio art, architecture, and community engagement to create large-scale public art projects that activate public spaces and historical sites around the world. For over ten years, I have been designing site-specific projects that bring deep engagement with communities and find expression through experimental material craft. My work is highly collaborative from start to finish. My projects emerge from conversations with local community organizations and advocates to respond to the cultural histories and social conditions of a place. Moving between research, interviews, workshops, and material testing, my practice creates projects rooted in specific communities. In addition, I see the future users of my projects as additional collaborators. I create immersive environments that are brought to life by people visiting the space. The projects are designed to invite interaction, offering playful exploration for people of all ages. Through tactile materials, changing light effects, and intriguing patterns, the projects offer sensory exploration and play as a medium for social connection and community building. In terms of materials, I work with historically gendered and marginalized techniques of space-making, such as textiles and decorative arts, to produce immersive sensory environments. Many of my projects explore how techniques of domestic craft, such as weaving and macrame, can be deployed at a monumental scale to transform public spaces. I use my training in both art and architecture to design large-scale structures that envelope people in sensory, textile wonderlands that encourage exploration and discovery. In bringing textures and techniques from domestic space into public space, the projects invite more tactile interaction and social connection than is typically available in public spaces. The attention to sensory qualities and interaction in my projects is intended to make projects inviting to a range of embodied experiences. Many of my projects emerge from collaborations with people who are marginalized in public spaces, such as those with developmental disabilities. Several of my projects have been created in collaboration with people that identify as neurodivergent and have sensory sensitivities to light, color, texture, and/or sound. My projects therefore offer a range of intensity, from playful social zones to quieter restorative zones, so that everyone can find a space that is comfortable for them. In supporting people who are neurodivergent, these projects also support anyone who might be seeking a greater range of sensory options within public space than is normally available.

column25: My artistic practice revolves around observation, reflection, and the art of noticing. These fundamental elements serve as the driving force behind each of my compositions. What captivates me the most are the lines and textures that I encounter in my everyday surroundings, serving as the inspiration for my collages and mixed-media landscapes. Whether it's the contours of a mountain, a crack in the pavement, or the intricate texture of a rock, I find myself compelled to capture these moments through photography, sketches, or direct rubbings on paper. These visual recordings become my reference points as I create larger compositions, using a combination of found papers, handmade papers, and existing artwork. In recent artwork, I have intentionally begun incorporating materials from my immediate environment through techniques such as papermaking, printing, and alternative photography. By incorporating foraged plant materials and photographs into cyanotypes, and using found papers, note paper, and older artwork to create handmade papers, I infuse my art with a deeper connection to the world around me. Each series I embark upon starts with a deliberately chosen limited color palette, which serves to unite the various shapes, textures, and lines within the artwork. As I carefully select and prepare my materials, I simultaneously work on multiple compositions, tearing or cutting shapes and arranging them intuitively. Through this process, I continue to add paper or make marks until a sense of balance emerges. The play of textures, colors, and edges becomes increasingly responsive, guiding the artwork's evolution. Ultimately, the final compositions draw attention to space, prompting viewers to contemplate whether they depict real or imagined landscapes. The subtle variations in edges, textures, and layers of paper lure the viewer into each piece, inviting them to explore and discover hidden nuances. My intention is to evoke moments of thoughtful observation within the viewers as they engage with my artwork. I strive to make my work accessible to a wide audience and actively seek opportunities to showcase it in non-traditional and community-oriented spaces. By doing so, I hope to foster a deeper connection between my art and its viewers, creating meaningful interactions that go beyond traditional exhibition settings.

column24:

My art celebrates the fun I have when creating my own worlds and the characters that inhabit them. I want to share that experience and passion with the viewer. As ideas pop into my head, I capture them using pencil and paper, and may also transfer the idea to other mediums such as oil paint and 3D models. The majority of my imagery is detailed superheroes, monsters, robots, hybrid designs, and ancient (as well as futuristic) warriors. I create intrigue, with honestly expressing ideas I find interesting, weird, fun, and awesome with a steadfast and energetic passion. I challenge myself and explore undiscovered paths and ideas. I also want to share my artwork to inspire a similar creativity in the viewer. Pushing the imagination can develop important skills such as improved creativity, problem solving, and flexibility. It is my hope that my work will make contact with the viewer in an intimate and inspirational way. I want to do something truly great with those connections. I hope to build new relationships with a community full of people who also pursue their goals.

column25: Terence Moronta grew up in the Bronx where he started drawing as a young child and moved to Poughquag where he eventually developed into an artist with a desire to share his work with the world. He has turned a childhood hobby to use his artwork as a way to connect with others as a career. Each of his main works contain a variety of characters that stand alone with their own distinct silhouette, personality, and poses. The repetitive nature of the work and characters is prevalent and shows a commitment to the development of each figure and idea. Sketchbook drawings are the main focus of Moronta’s art career and these drawings are meant to be shared in this intimate space with the viewer. Other works, such as paintings compliment the drawings as an exploration into other mediums. Moronta’s work always contains a consistent energy that the artist enjoys portraying to the viewer. This conversation becomes just as important to the work as the art itself. Terence Moronta graduated from State University of New York in New Paltz with honors in 2019, which has lead to his work being displayed in shows such as the Foundation Student Exhibition in 2017 and thesis show, Attendance is Mandatory, in 2019 held at New Paltz and the Contemporary Figures show in 2018. His work is also part of a sociology blog and an Airsoft team logo. His work can be applied to multiple art formats and mediums. While at New Paltz, his work has developed into other mediums including oil and watercolor painting as well as 3D printed forms. The connections he made at New Paltz have helped him develop his career.

column25: “If it is a human thing to do to put something you want, because it's useful, edible, or beautiful, into a bag, or a basket, or a bit of rolled bark or leaf, or a net woven of your own hair, or what have you, and then take it home with you, home being another, larger kind of pouch or bag, a container for people, and then later on you take it out and eat it or share it or store it up for winter in a solider container or put it in the medicine bundle or the shrine or the museum, the holy place, the area that contains what is sacred, and then next day you probably do much the same again--if to do that is human, if that's what it takes, then I am a human being after all. Fully, freely, gladly, for the first time.” - Ursula K. Le Guin “The Carrier Bag Theory of Fiction” I was never taught to weave. My great-grandmother was never inclined to show me how to make a vessel to haul our heavy fish from the sea to the hearth. No one showed me how to set up a loom, stitch a basket, or twine grass into an object meant to hold our food. I was taught to bury things; Shown how to hide roots in deep, dark, damp soil with the assumption that that’s what was necessary for them to grow. The greenery above the ground the only evidence of a healthy body below. I was instructed many times to dig holes for the squirrels and rabbits the family dog had killed. Each impact of the shovel to dirt reminding me that an innocent animal had been killed simply for the pleasure of the hunt. I was taught to bury my tears because wet eyes are a sign of weakness. Often scolded for being the kid who let their emotions run around on their sleeve, unprotected and wild. These experiences have led me to concentrate my work on understanding what gets buried, and what gets pushed aside. What feelings, roots, and memories are allowed to slip through the stitches of a familial basket, when others are held tightly? I wonder if they're the things that I can afford to miss or the feelings I allow to be free to nurture themselves back into the earth. Maybe the things that fall through are the lucky ones. Never made to feel like they are hidden or stored away, not held so tightly that they are no longer seen at all. My practice lives at the intersection of ritual and learned behavior. I use ancient ritual techniques found in weaving that traditionally provided necessity as a road map to explore the way learned coping behavior is passed along generationally. Each twist, tuck, and braid is different. Some hold each weft tightly, while others unintentionally let the piece sag, weakening it. The unintentionality of assumed weakness offers the opportunity for what’s being held to fall through. Maybe a small piece is meant to get away.
