My work is centered on my identity as a Kichwa woman. Through this artistic practice, I tap into personal, ancestral, and collective memory that allows me to strengthen my identity as an Indigenous woman outside my ancestral homelands. This practice allows me to enter into conversation with those who aren’t in this physical place, ancestors of the past, present, and future. My work is heavily influenced by what it means to be part of the diaspora as a Kichwa woman and the complexities of it. Through bright vibrant colors, I explore what it means for city and land to co-exist, stillness and chaos, belonging and feeling like an outsider. Something that is always present in my art is the feminine/matriarchal energy as a way to disrupt patriarchal spaces. To myself, it’s a reminder that I stand on the shoulders of my mother, my community, and my ancestors. Despite all the violence of colonialism, and its effects, our art has always been here. This work is rooted in love.

column25: I am an Appalachian scholar-activist, ethnographer, media artist, teacher, and cultural organizer with a critical interest in digital humanities and archives. I am also a Generation-X zinester interested in regional zine-related practices, productions, and communities. I am a first-generation college graduate in my family, and my interdisciplinary educational background includes a BA in women's studies, an MTS in world religions and women's studies, a graduate certificate in gender/women's studies, and an MA and PhD in cultural anthropology. As a theatre artist, I have participated in or supported several community productions in Kentucky. In 2016, I participated in a month-long Fall Youth Theater Lab that was a collaborative theater workshop facilitated by Appalshop’s Appalachian Media Institute and Roadside Theater. I also participated in the 2017 Hurricane Gap Community Theater Institute at Pine Mountain Settlement School. My media arts training includes participating in several digital storytelling and film production workshops and courses, and I have made more than 50 short videos over the past two decades. One of my collaborative documentary and storytelling projects is the production of a multi-media documentary on my partner’s grandparents, Bluegrass musicians Frances and John Reedy from Harlan County, Kentucky. I co-produced the local Clear Creek Film Festival from 2007-2011, and I was invited to serve as a jury member at the 2013 Festival Internacional de Cine de los Derechos Humanos (International Human Rights Film Festival) in Sucre, Bolivia. As a media teacher, I have mentored and assisted with the production of more than 100 digital storytelling projects by young people in the Appalachian region. In 2018, I was juried as a media artist into the Kentucky Arts Council Teaching Artist Directory and the Berea College Partners for Education (PFE) Promise Neighborhood Teaching Artist Directory. I have led or participated in media arts projects as a visiting teaching artist in several Kentucky public schools, including but not limited to those served by the PFE (now Partners for Rural Impact or PRI) through the “Our Creative Promise” and “Arts Connect Appalachian Youth” projects funded by the National Endowment for the Arts. I helped document lessons learned from the “Our Creative Promise” project and helped consult on a “Media Arts Toolkit” of K-12 resources for Kentucky Educational Television. I served as an artist mentor for PRI's 2020-2022 Appalachian Teaching Artist Fellowship (ATAF) program and am currently serving in this role again for the new 2024-2025 ATAF cohort. In 2023, I served as one of three alumni-advisors for the Kentucky Community Scholar revision process and as lead facilitator for a pilot hybrid in-person/virtual Community Scholar training at Appalachia-Science in the Public Interest (ASPI) in partnership with the Kentucky Arts Council and Kentucky Folklife Program. I started making zine in the late 1980s and early 1990s as a high school student, and I have continued engagement with this activist medium through artwork, research, and education. I included a section on zines in one of my dissertation chapters based on encounters and zines collected from my fieldwork in youth media education programs in West Virginia and East(ern) Kentucky. I wrote a book review on Slifer’s book about AMP that was published in a recent special issue of the Journal of Appalachian Studies. I presented a virtual workshop on “Place-Based Cultural Mapping Zines” in June 2023 as part of Sarabande Books’ Zine Lunch! series. I have also included zines in graphic novel workshops for teaching artist residencies in public schools. I recently initiated a regional “ZiNetwork” (or zine interest group) to connect maker-activists in Kentucky and Appalachia with support from a 2023 Kentucky Foundation for Women Art Meets Activism Grant and community partner Sarabande Books. I currently serve on the board of Kentuckians for the Arts, the Editorial Board for the digital Kentucky Folklife Magazine sponsored by the Kentucky Folklife Program, and the Steering Committee for the new Small Town America Institute for Rural Arts & Culture. I am also a member of the Waymakers Collective: Appalachian Arts & Culture Assembly, and I received the 2022 Appalachian Arts & Entertainment Award for Best Arts Educator.

column25: My art practice stems from a need to create beauty from that which has been discarded. Striving to lessen plastic pollution and overconsumption drives my creativity. Zero waste is the ultimate goal for my practice. Most of my materials come from daily life. I collect single-use plastic food packaging in my home, or clean up trash on my walks and bring it to my studio to transform it. Stitching, collaging and transforming these repurposed materials provides an additional level of uniqueness. Reclaiming and repurposing materials that often end up choking our environment or overflowing landfills has been my art practice for years. I use meditative hand stitching, alongside my industrial sewing machine, to stitch and collage layers of translucent single use plastics which would otherwise contribute to litter pollution. The transformation I subject them to makes these materials unrecognizable. Humans are instinctively drawn to beauty, thus I must entice viewers to look at something which they would otherwise avoid or not notice. Creating public art installations is an important part of my practice. I believe part of my job is to inspire people to take ownership of this planet and hopefully, to do what they can to help in our global fight to manage the climate crisis. Each installation endeavors to make the trash littering our forests, waterways and beaches visible, contributing to an awareness that drives change.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I'm very interested in the exploration of dreams and strive to immortalize moments of awe. My paintings are windows into the surreal landscapes of my soul, where reality and imagination coalesce into vibrant, otherworldly compositions. My search for the extraordinary invites viewers to share in the wonder and transcendence I experience. I aspire to transport others to the same realm of boundless creativity and exhilaration that I have always sought. My work serves as a bridge between the mundane and the extraordinary, a testament to the limitless possibilities of human imagination. My process begins from empathy, imagining myself in the shoes of protagonists from books and media that I admire and dream of, followed by sketching thumbnails and iterating on several layouts digitally to explore the best composition and color palettes before beginning to paint. My painting techniques investigates the history of European traditional painting from my favorite masters, as I experiment with different types of application, from grisaille, to glazes, scumble and velatura, creating depth with different treatments all around the canvas, to reveal the process and letting the audience complete the narrative through suggestions of unconventional, irrational subject matters, in hopes to ignite a suspension of disbelief.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work focuses on the subtle imperfections and absurdities that are inherent in our lives. It draws out fragments of memories, observations, feelings, and identities from ordinary objects rooted in personal stories and experiences. The feeling of endless searching, belonging, in-betweenness, unexpectedness, and inevitability arises from the repetitive irritations and unavoidable inconveniences in our lives—too small to avoid, yet too big to ignore. Instability is the inevitable element from which the viewer cannot escape; it is part of each moment. Balancing between recognition and mystery, I merge recognizable forms such as human shapes. I start by blending old memories with present surroundings, sourced from personal photos. Through a repetitive process involving wiping, washing, sanding, drawing, pouring, scraping, and repainting, I create multiple layers and ambiguous forms that blur the line between representation and abstraction. This process further enhances the disorienting effect within the work. Irregularly sewing the canvas itself structures a painting that creates a sense of confusion, amplifying the ambiguous feeling of the work and encouraging contemplation and reflection. The inevitable interruption is expressed through the tied sewing crumples of fabrics and hand stitches. Seemingly distracting, it creates confusion and provides a setting for the awkwardness, blending puzzlement with relatability, driving toward life's absurdities and imperfections. At first glance, viewers may struggle to identify what they are observing. However, with deeper examination, they may discover familiar shapes and forms open to various interpretations, triggering a slight sense of absurdity akin to seeking shapes in clouds. My process is informed by personal experiences and a desire to express the paradoxical nature of human beings and the contradictions that define our humanity. Recognizable forms create a sense of relatability, connecting viewers with the work and prompting reflection on their own experiences. The unexpected moment of recognizing familiar shapes within the artwork invites us to look beyond the surface, question our perceptions, embrace the unexpected, and consider the different layers of meaning within seemingly ordinary experiences. Through creating ambiguous and layered forms with warm and muted colors, my work invites viewers to question perceptions while embracing the unexpected.

column25: As my sculpture practice goes, material exploration is the name of the game. Necessarily. Since I am creating objects and not just images, the characteristics of the chosen material are directly in concert with the piece’s subject matter. Indeed if the piece were deformed and disarrayed to its essential stuff, the material and its materiality would be all that remains: it's the inevitable through-line for any sculptural work. The materiality of a chosen medium exists before an artwork’s creation, pointedly it remains extant during an artwork’s existence, and in some fashion must still exist once the work is dead or destroyed (even if that’s all the material does then…exist, without its other half). It’s an unavoidable interaction, material and subject, though the artist is afforded some luxury in governing its importance, varyingly — the ‘clayness’ of a clay vessel, for example, may be of such an obvious unimportance that it doesn’t merit discussion. Alternatively I have found that promoting material-as-the-point opens the work up to a myriad of artistic possibilities that I find exciting and fruitful, especially when coupled with narrative and subject matter, dialed up to an equal degree of importance. By heavily emphasizing this interaction between subject and material, I am hoping for a kind of symbiosis to ensue which activates the artwork — that the two halves of this whole inflict a constant, practical, vital influence on one another. And if the work at a glance only puzzles, then let it be a puzzlebox, and if the material which constitutes it is afforded as much consideration as its subject and form, then let it be the key to a lock. And how can I square my drawing practice with this philosophy? Beyond the simple fact that it is my first love, and my greatest strength, and to show you myself without it would be to show you myself naked — I do still have some feeling about it regarding material exploration. While I don’t quibble as much over, say, the ‘paperness’ of the paper — the objecthood of a framed drawing is, I think, self-evident — there is something to be said about my inclination to use charcoal almost exclusively. What resonates most with me is the sheer ancientness of the material, burnt wood — it must be as old as the berry-made paints and dyes which decorate caves. Though I cherish the antiquity of charcoal, I don’t venerate it — it’s an inherent characteristic of charcoal which I accept and find precious. But the reality is that it is a means to an end: with charcoal I am most able to put what’s in my head upon the page. The subjects I choose to draw run the gamut and shift frequently. Almost always, a narrative is attached to a drawing or its attendant body of work. Though I call it ‘narrative’ it is not as simple as that, or maybe narrative is the wrong word. As a general rule, if I am opting to tell a story in drawing and sculpture, then the story must necessitate drawing and sculpture as the most suitable medium to tell it with. As in, if an idea grows in my head and it is better off in writing or film (or music or dance), then I will write the story down or make a short film of it (or a ballad, or choreograph something for a stage). Meaning I am left with stories that are necessarily non-sequential, static, symbolic, obscured — sensory and emotive stories — stories which behave more like a vapor passing over you and less like a ticker tape dispensing news. I hope: the musical phrase, not the metronome. Lastly, if my sculptures are of a more experimental and exploratory pursuit, the drawings serve to render those explorations into clarity (not to say one comes before the other, though). The drawings are linchpins that keep the whole enterprise afloat. They define. They act often as direct injections of my current preoccupations, if not intentions — narrative or historical or formal and what have you — and I let them, in their way, rarefy the more threadbare pieces, those miniature eruptions of experimentation, which orbit them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Tiantian Ma's paintings are a celebration of the natural world and its inherent spirituality. Inspired by the Transcendentalist philosophy that sees divinity in nature and the interconnectedness of all living things, Tiantian's works are a meditation on the universal spirit that animates all creation. Through her use of color, texture, and form, Tiantian seeks to capture the shining moments of divinity and spirituality that are present in everyday life. Her paintings are a testament to the belief that nature is not just a physical entity, but a spiritual force that can nourish and inspire us. Under the guidance of instinct, Tiantian imagines a world where animism and empathy, as her favorite poem: “I am integral with you…. I too am of one phase and of all phases.” (By Walt Whitman, Song of Myself) Throughout her paintings, Tiantian’s use of acrylic resin adds a sense of depth, movement, and flow to her works. The choice of materials adds to the spiritual and nature-inspired themes of Tiantian’s paintings, as the use of texture and layers mimics the complexity of the natural world. By layering, building up, and altering the different effects of the material, her works depict an exploration of the energy, rhythms, and emotions that flow through the natural world. Taken as a whole, Tiantian’s paintings are a testament to the power of nature to inspire and uplift us. Tiantian's paintings are a celebration of the beauty, diversity, and spirituality of the natural world, and an invitation to the viewer to join her in exploring the mysteries and wonders of creation. As Tiantian herself puts in, “I believe that harmonizing and resonating with nature is my ‘bliss point’. Through my paintings, I hope to share that sense of harmony and resonance with others, and to inspire a deeper appreciation for the nature world that surrounds us all.”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
 My practice springs from my fascination with the text+image relationship and the freeing, yet limiting and inescapable, gaps in language. Humans must approach language as a closed system in order to achieve effective communication, but language has been and will always be an open system through which semiotics, context, and the subjective cause language to – however minorly or dramatically – shapeshift. Since the advent of computer-mediated communication, today's globalized expansion + contraction of language (think of Google Translate's accessibility and fallibility, ChatGPT, or the daily misinterpretations of text messages) posits a new iteration of an old set of issues: e.g. miscommunication, authorship, ownership. Society’s inundation of text+image and text-as-image is fruitful ground for the restructuring of systems of power. Considering the latter, and our ongoing political climate, I often use words spoken to me by men as my materials, defining materiality as separate from physicality, and use the grid as a semiotic stand-in for the oppressive white, heteronormative patriarchy. My work challenges the power dynamics present in our system of – and perception of – language through the ways I handle and manipulate the text + image / text-as-image relationship. Intersectional feminist values propel my work, as well as attempts at challenging the sweeping notion of an American psyche which strictly values that which is fiscally or numerically quantifiable – a problem that began in racist, capitalist, patriarchal “ideals” and continues across many channels in different forms. As an LGBTQ+ American woman, decoding power structures which underlie language use is dire – because few things are as powerful and political as language.

column25: Dawn Kramlich is a queer artist and writer who lives and works in Chicago, IL. Her artwork has always been text-based, and her artistic practice involves research on semiotics, poetry (especially ekphrasis), Post-Structuralism, Intersectional Feminism, and the history of text-based American art. Kramlich earned her BA degree magna cum laude from Muhlenberg College, producing the first cross-major creative honors thesis (paintings + poems) in the history of Muhlenberg’s English and Art departments. She then moved to Philadelphia and received her MFA summa cum laude in Studio Art from Moore College of Art & Design. Kramlich regularly lectures at institutions, including PAFA (Pennsylvania Academy of Fine Art), and has shown her work widely across the US and internationally in Ireland. Most notably, her work was included in a solo exhibition titled 'Mark My Words' at the Barbara Crawford Gallery in 2023, Maus Contemporary's 'Capitolism: The Normalization of Political Violence in the United States' exhibition in 2021, Rowan University Gallery's exhibition entitled 'Dialogic' alongside John Giorno, Jaume Plensa, Jenny Holzer, and Glenn Ligon in 2013, and in the 4-person installation-based exhibition entitled 'PaperScapes' at the Philadelphia Art Alliance in 2017. Kramlich was commissioned in 2022 by the PHL International Airport to create a painting which was scanned and printed at mural scale (7.5ft x 22ft) for the connector hallway just before Terminal F. Her artwork has been published in 'I Like Your Work' podcast's catalog, 'Muhlenberg Magazine,' 'CODAmagazine,' 'THE magazine,' and the online version of 'Creative Quarterly.' Kramlich moved from Philly to Chicago in 2023 when she began her position as the Assistant Professor of Painting and Drawing at Elmhurst University.

Waves of Creation My ongoing series of expressive abstract paintings draws inspiration from my personal journey in 2023. The magnificent waves and breathtaking Pacific coast sunrises, as well as the enchanting experience of wading through a Spring charmed with cherry blossoms, have left a profound impact on me. Witnessing the immense power and resilience of nature in solitude, I can't help but recognize its deep connection to the creative process artists undergo. Working primarily in seclusion, I strive to translate my thoughts and ideas into visual expressions. While the creative journey often involves moments of self-doubt and deconstruction, it also offers glimpses of hope and opportunities for healing. The captivating and harmonious interplay between these elements fascinates me and adds a profound beauty to my artistic exploration.

column25: Originally from Seoul, South Korea, YouBeen Kim immigrated to the United States alone at the age of 16 in pursuit of a better education leaving her single mother behind in Korea. YouBeen faced culture shock as she landed in Utah, then finishing her high school years in Illinois. Struggling to communicate with people due to the language barrier, she turned to art as a means of expression and began creating landscapes and portraits to connect with others and ease her homesickness and loneliness. Ultimately settling in California to further develop her artistic talents, she graduated from the Academy of Art University with a bachelor’s degree in Fine Art Painting in 2015. Her work has continued to evolve and reflect her life experiences, exploring her Korean heritage and how being in America has added dimension to further shape that. Whether they are American pop star portraits, nature inspired abstract expressionist paintings using bold colors or literally her own self-portrait, all of these speak to the multi-faceted aspects of her identity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For the longest time I felt like I couldn’t see myself, but the gift of being an artist is that my work allows me to see myself and those who stood before me in ways that a mirror or my unexpressed thoughts never could. My artistic and poetic aesthetic serve as an expansion of my internal world. I write and create from the seat of the soul. Whatever I am experiencing or have experienced informs my emotional, physical, spiritual and sensorial worlds. Creation becomes a gauge for my immediate internal state. When the jumble of thoughts in my head creates dissonance, the act of creation draws out not only my conscious, but subconscious and unconscious musings. That is the beauty and pain of commitment to an artistic practice. It continuously obliges us to be honest with ourselves; the truth always finds a way to manifest in the work. Being an artist is seeing beyond the veil.

column25: My installation projects inherently rebel against the classical methods of art presentation within traditional galleries. I believe art should be viewed and digested within intimate spaces that emit an aura of warmth. Hence, I prefer recreating my own intimate spaces (such as my studio) within galleries to offer an immersive and intentional experience. To do so, I utilize accouterments, such as movable walls, to create smaller spaces within a larger space and personal touches, such as my own furniture, rugs, clothing racks, and knick knacks, to offer my own humanity as part of the experience. Not only does this method of installation help retain the intimacy of the moments in which the work featured was created, but it also immerses the viewer in multiple worlds at once, from the microcosm of the poetry/art itself, zooming out into the room, to the gallery. I would also like to think that this installation room method makes art and galleries less intimidating to visitors that experience difficulty becoming immersed and connected with galleries/museums based on historical representations/presentations of museums and a kind of cultural segregation in which some demographics are made to feel less welcome in these spaces. A room with personal objects becomes an environment to which people can relate and in which they can see themselves. It humanizes not only the art but the entire space in a way that people can feel seen and accepted. It becomes a safe space. It is important to me that my work acts as a safe space both physically and emotionally. Ideally, my work is a vessel for people to sink into and reflect on their own experiences and emotions. I focus on appealing to the senses through texture, flow, symmetry, complementary colors, and vivid imagery. Poetry is the unifying thread that upholds the narrative within my installation spaces. I often tell myself that if I am not going to the page to write for a period of time, something I am hiding from myself or am fearful of acknowledging must be at the core. When I do write, I like to think of it as communing with the natural world to tell stories that emphasize the inherent strength conjured in states of vulnerability. In combination with poetry, my other mediums, such as collage and tie-dye, allow me to awaken my senses to be able to receive and deliver narratives that ask to occupy more space than a page; sometimes they necessitate a whole room/rooms.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
A spirit of exploration and experimentation resides at the core of my artistic approach. In 2014, I started experimenting with the effects of everyday materials such as sugar, coffee, and salt. The unique texture of salt lead me to incorporate solutions in varying saturation levels to my paints. Researching other artists using salt, I found inspiration in Juris Boiko's photographs, Sigalit Landau's sculptures, and particularly Christopher Cichocki's environmental art. Through my environmental study and research, I recognized the significance of salt in the realms of economics, politics, and trade. While the value of salt as a commodity has declined, its impact on freshwater systems has become a concern due to rising sea levels, climate change, and global droughts. 'Whispers of The Land' brings attention to the devastating consequences of salt intrusion in the land, highlighting its detrimental effects on the environment, agriculture, and food production.

Kipaipai Group Exhibition, Melissa Morgan Gallery, Palm Desert, CA (2022), and Artist Alliance Biennial at Oceanside Museum, Oceanside, CA (2022) and “A Century of California Women Artists,” Brand Library and Art Center, Glendale, CA (2022) are among her most recent group exhibitions. Previous installation projects include Stoning (2006) and Dare to Be a Woman (2004) created in response to the injustice and violence against women of Iran and exhibited at the Cal State University Northridge (CSUN) Art Gallery. She was the featured artist at the Museum of Art & History - MOAH:CEDAR, Lancaster, California (2015) and exhibited in MOAH’s Annual All-Media Arts exhibitions in 2021, 2020 and 2015. In 2020 she was awarded Third Place at “The Art of the Square” at bG Gallery, Los Angeles, CA, and received Best of Show at JF Gallery, Miami, in 2017. She was also awarded staff’s favorite at the 31stAnnual “Made in California” at the Brea City Art Gallery, Brea, CA in 2016. column25: Like artists of the past, I am fascinated with the human face and how the face can be recycled, changed, deconstructed/reconstructed, fragmented, fraught with meaning (or not), viewed prejudicially, seen as universal, or simply enjoyed for its unique beauty. “Recycled Identity” is a photo collage series of faces of woman of all ages, racial, sexual orientation, and ethnic backgrounds, from the past as well as the present. Though each woman comes with her own baggage, personality, memories, when combined with another’s image, they meld and take on a fused identity. The work shows the possibility of creating a new harmonious person, combining the attributes and features of one with another. It also points to the universality of people who are joined together…we are all the same, while still being different.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Cultural identity and the human ramifications of political and social events are the inspiration for my work. I utilize narrative and encourage public participation in order to create connections and empathy. As an Arab-Jewish-American, I am a minority of a minority; born in Indiana to parents from Baghdad. My work is an attempt to synthesize these often-conflicting identities and make peace in and with the world. My choice of media and scale varies and is dependent on the specific project. I gravitate towards showing my work in public spaces in order to reach the widest audience possible. Some of my projects span multiple years, growing and changing to adapt to the different situations they inhabit. I cherish collaboration, with other artists and with the larger community in the creation of my work. By constructing frameworks for sharing stories and experiences I seek to weave together a multiplicity of voices. This can be seen in some of my most recent work where I have begun literally weaving structures that are intended for people to use. Sited in public spaces where they interact with the light and the wind, they offer a place for rest and reflection or conversation and a picnic; they present an opportunity for people to share an experience together.

column25: Patriarchy has controlled the narrative for 10,000 years. My staged miniature photography series, RECLAIMING THE MUSE, reframes historic artworks and stories in contemporary terms. In centering women, historically cast as objects of beauty or scorn, I strive to revitalize the muse with agency, furthering the issues important to me as a contemporary female artist. Mythos, power dynamics, gender roles, liberation, empowerment, and self-preservation are explored in this series, all with a deceptively playful overlay. Although I never depict actual people in my photographs, the human psyche is undeniably at the center of my work. I am fascinated by the psychological landscape, our search for meaning and the contradictions of human existence. So many stories, myths and artworks throughout history address these same concerns. I have found much rich source material to inspire my own interpretations for this series. In my research, time and time again, the women in myths, folk tales, the Bible, and elsewhere are held responsible for causing both the world’s ills and the failings of men. This includes their own rapes, which are recounted in mythology with shocking frequency, and are typically deemed the woman’s fault, justifying her inevitable punishment. Of course, creating variations and reinterpretations of past tales and depictions is not a novel idea, but rather an age-old tradition, practiced throughout art history. My muses take back their power and tell their own stories. There is a rich well to draw upon from historical representations. We must remember, the old tales are fiction, and it is far past time for the retelling.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is that I feel as though I can communicate more genuinely through my images then I can do any words that I say or write. It is also an opportunity to escape. I'm at my calmest when I'm just making lines. The world can become so chaotic, but I find a gratifying pleasure in sitting at a plate and look at a natural form and try to imitate the complexity that I see. While it somewhat futile knowing that I can never make enough marks to capture the object in its entirety, I am grateful for the opportunity to try. I feel privileged to have the time and materials to sit in the presence of nature and interpret something so beautiful and wondrous.

column25: My work is a response to the intricacies and depth of natural forms. Lines are the building blocks of my imagery. The etching process in printmaking allows for a technical consideration of how these lines are distributed throughout the work, with the weight of each line relating directly to how much time it is kept in a saline solution. Lines develop into curves, from curves to circles, and then to fields. As each line extends outward, the form begins to resemble how it occurs in nature: preconditioned, though subject to the elements around it. Beyond bringing attention to the immense complexity of the natural world, one of my primary goals as an artist is to raise environmental consciousness. I print on handmade papers made from recycled materials such as old cotton shirts and linen sheets, as well as plant fibers, especially those of invasive plants. Conceptually, the union of process and subject embodies an important metaphor for my views. I hope that making sheets by hand not only can help the environment, but also can promote sustainability. Fundamentally, my work is about discovering, then understanding, and finally expressing an attitude towards nature.

column25: Ubiquitous … An Immersive Installation About Microplastics in Our Bodies Description: “Ubiquitous” will bring attention to the proliferation of microplastics in the environment and the human body. Microplastics are everywhere: in the air we breathe, in the food we eat, in the clothes we wear, in our homes, and in our bodies. When the viewer first enters the installation they will be drawn in by the organic shaped sculptures that are suspended from the ceiling, hung on walls, and grouped in piles on the floor. As they walk through the exhibit and read the straightforward signage, visitors will gain an understanding that the pieces they are viewing represent microplastics which are found in our bodies. The viewer will have the experience of being fully encompassed by the sculptures, to further raise their consciousness about the pervasiveness of microplastics. They will come to learn about the catastrophic effect that the oil and packaged goods industries have on the environment through single-use plastics. The installation will be an immersive experience, featuring 100 microplastics sculptures, filled with discarded plastic on the inside and textured with upcycled plastic on the outside. The installation will communicate how the plastic that we discard in our environment eventually end up inside our bodies. “Ubiquitous” poses the question, by the time scientists figure out the full impact of microplastics in the body, what damage will they have done? The goal of “Ubiquitous” is to inspire the viewer to be part of the solution to reduce the creation and consumption of single use plastic and the microplastics which are created when they break down. Rationale: · Global plastic production is more than 800 billion pounds yearly and is expected to double by 2034. · Americans create more plastic waste yearly than any other country; almost 500 pounds per person. · Only 5-6% of plastic is recycled. · Plastic waste continuously breaks down into particles the size of a pencil eraser or smaller. Over time, these microplastics become airborne. · Scientists have found microplastics everywhere, from the deepest trench in the ocean to the summit of Mount Everest. · In 2021, Japanese scientists estimated 24.4 trillion microplastics in the world’s upper oceans, the equivalent of 30 billion half-liter water bottles. · Humans absorb microplastics from air, food, and water. According to scientists, adults ingest up to 121,000 microplastics yearly. · Research has detected microplastics in blood, lungs, intestines, and placentas of living humans and newborns. · Plastics are made from the by-products of oil and gas refining and many of the chemicals used to create plastics are carcinogens. Also, many of the additives which give plastic strength and flexibility are also suspected carcinogens. · Tel Aviv University researchers found that in a marine environment, microplastics absorb and concentrate toxic organic substances and increase their toxicity by a factor of 10, which may lead to a severe impact on human health. · The full impact of microplastics on our health is as yet unknown. However, researchers are concerned as microplastics have been shown to cause damage to human cells in the laboratory.

The work is a meditation on how people interact with the world around us; how we manipulate space for our comforts, abuse our natural resources for personal gain and rely on our houses and communities stability, without necessarily considering how they came to be. I paint representations of surfaces like wood grain and concrete to allow the viewer entry to the work with universally recognizable textures. I use motifs of patterned fabric to create form and space while evoking a sense of comfort and domesticity. By living in the realm between abstraction and representation, the shapes and colors housed within each composition interact to elicit the illusion of space in an abstract reality.

column25: As a painter and textile artist, I attempt to navigate the complexities and tangibility of being Filipino American (FilAm) as a second-generation New Yorker who has never been to the Philippines. I combine timelines by referencing images and documents from early U.S. imperialism and contemplating their Western perspectives of the Philippines. I also cite ancestral stories and childhood memories while subverting national symbols in my own myth-making. By sewing knitted and woven textiles with painting, I equip myself with full autonomy to determine aspects of my identity. I challenge bicultural tangibility, and concepts around predetermined structures and perceptions around diasporic identity. Central to my work is the carabao (water-buffalo), symbolizing both my maternal lineage and the broader Filipino strength and resilience. It was introduced to me through my mother’s childhood stories of my grandfather's strong and gentle carabaos that plowed his fields in the Philippines. My current work explores themes of migration and cultural assimilation through both a carabao figure and depictions of family photographs. The carabao figure is a personification of FilAm Culture and is characterized by a carabao head on either my mother’s or my own body. She usually exists among objects related to my associations with the Filipinos I grew up with, such as my parents and neighbors who pace the house in tsinelas (sandals), cook milkfish for me, and collect various porcelain vases. A parallel body of work focuses on celebrating and normalizing Filipino presence in the US through family portraiture. The work shows snapshots of painted intimate intergenerational bonds stitched against woven and knitted backgrounds. Some painted figures wear textile garments with patterns of fruit my grandfather used to grow. These family relationships mimic my relationship to the Philippines and Filipino-ness, and my attempt to uphold their legacies. At the same time, through the seamless merging of mediums, I give myself the responsibility to pave a new pathway for other diasporic identities to pursue a place of belonging. Utilizing machine knitting and floor loom weaving, I engage with concepts of time, structure, and labor. These tactile processes prompt viewers to question the boundaries between reality and painted illusions within my pieces. The intricate process of building a textile and patiently joining it to a painted canvas with thread references journey-making and time-stamping. Both are critical elements in the hushtoned landscapes of which my characters exist. It magnifies my exploration of how identity is formed and influenced by the access to ancestral timelines and experiences of my lifetime. The journey of yarn looping within itself and passing through the warp documents layers of time within a textile. I aim to not only offer a new way of understanding the facets of diaspora but also to document oral narratives and recognize the global legacies established by a marginalized nation.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am writing this note from the studio in Port Townsend, Washington. Port Townsend is located on the NE corner of the Olympic Peninsula in Washington State, 30 miles north and west of Seattle. The peninsula is surrounded by the Salish Sea and home to the Olympic Mountains and the Olympic National Park. I have lived in Washington State since the early 1980's and before that l lived in Colorado where I grew up and in Utah where I spent most summers. This land is the ancestral home of the Chimakum People and then the home of S’Klallam (or Klallam) Nation. I acknowledge that I am present on indigenous land, the traditional lands of the S'Kallam people. I honor my ongoing connection to this community, past, present and future A painting practice is very personal and that work takes place privately in the studio. However, the creative process is finally completed when the pieces or work is shared and the viewer is able to engage and give the work its life force, incorporating or integrating the piece into their lives and making it then personal for them, this process making us very much interdependent. I work for the viewer. I really enjoy working in series. One image then suggesting another direction or idea. A series could number two or three or may include many paintings over a number of years. I use oil paint and work on canvas, paper & board. For the last several years I have been working with subjects related to maps, borders, families, immigration, extinctions and the climate emergency. My first goal is to make a strong image that is well crafted, original and interesting. Visual art is a powerful agent that can highlight fragility, injustices and document current affairs and subtle changes and bear witness to emerging conflicts. Our mutual humanity is the thread that I strive toward In my creative process. Often the work may move from representational/recognizable subjects to non objective/ abstract subjects, then moving back again or become a combination of the two. Being open and encouraging the subject matter to emerge and to become known is at the core of the process. Painting is my feeble human attempt to make some sense of the unknowable & to express the inexpressible.

column25: I have always dreamed of becoming an artist...a painter. This journey or path began bubbling while I was young and in middle school. I was born in Denver, Colorado and then grew up in the suburbs of The Mile High city near the foothills of the Rocky Mountains near Boulder. The indigenous history of the Denver region and Colorado more broadly begins with the Southern Ute Indian Tribe and Ute Mountain Tribe who are the first people nation. I am honored to acknowledge that this land that I have occupied is their ancestral home and land. Both sets of my grandparents homesteaded 160 acre dry-land ranches, one in Wyoming & the other in Oklahoma. Second generation immigrants from eastern & western Europe they participated in the colonizing of America at the expense of indigenous cultures. My folks were third generation Americans, they were solid, sturdy, hardworking ranch stock, who wanted something more than the rural farm life for themselves. My suburban landscape included views of the Flatirons, and an incredible postcard landscape. Looking out toward the East, were the great plains that stretched out for what seemed like forever. An intoxicating western landscape zipped past the car windows on the consistent road trips north into Wyoming and west into Utah. Summers were often spent in Torry Utah or in and the Capitol Reef National Monument. My dad was an independent prospector, he held and worked gold, silver and uranium claims in both Utah and Colorado. He also was a talented jack of all trades & an accomplished silversmith. My mom left Wyoming and moved to the big city of Denver, worked as a waitress, retail sales clerk and administrative assistant. My mother also was a painter. My older sister taught elementary school and my younger sister was a psychotherapist and artist. My entire family was forever engaged in making art and doing crafts of all sorts. We lived in the shadow of Rocky Flats Nuclear Weapons Plant (as downwinders) near Boulder. Witnessed the unfolding horrors of the Vietnam war and with that, the ongoing polarization at home. Endured assignations and enormous positive social upheaval. Great creative talents were destroyed by excesses. Buddhism came to the west and flowered in Boulder and in America. Artists became influencers and superstars. Museums and galleries prospered, collectors collected and art became a big business. I am grateful to my parents who enrolled me in private/group oil painting lessons with a local artist, Ruth Turner while I was still in middle school. I attended both public middle & senior high school. The art department was a safe refuge. Lucky for me, I studied for six years with an amazing mentor/art teacher; Warren Lundquist. Attended college at Fort Lewis in Durango, CO as a FA major on work scholarship and studied with Mick Reber, Gerald Wells and Stanton Englehart. Attended Rocky Mountain College of Art & Design in Denver, CO founded by Philip Steele. Studied with Shannon Kegan, James Valone and Phil Steele. I met my best friend and life partner, Sandy and we have a wonderful son. I have maintained a studio practice for a number of years and have lived in Washington State for awhile. Artists who have influenced me include: Michelangelo, Rembrandt, El Greco, Raphael, Chaim Soutine, Paul Cezanne, Arshile Gorky, Agnes Martin, Joan Mitchell, Georgia OKeefe, Mark Rothko, Willem De Kooning, Lee Krasner, Brian Rutenburg, Frank Auerbach, Kerry James Marshall, Gerhard Richter, Jacob Lawrence, Alice Neal, Jean-Michel Basquiat, Pat Passlof, Howard Hodkins, Cecily Brown & Woody Dennis. Group exhibitions: 13 Solo exhibitions: 6, Enjoyed teaching as an adjunct faculty instructor of adults and of young children in the following areas: painting, life drawing, basic & advanced color theory, linear perspective, rendering and art history. A journey takes a lifetime

column25: Find a wolf who won’t swallow you, and ask if you might climb into its mouth to make your trip. That way you at least you stand a chance. --Paul Fattaruso Travel in the Mouth of the Wolf I became fascinated with wolves while reading Of Wolves and Men by Barry Lopez about 20 years ago. A few years later I came across a photograph from the 1950's of a woman (with a gun) and four smiling children standing over the corpses of several wolves. As sickening as the image is I felt compelled to paint it. I painted a series of 6 paintings of people proudly displaying their fresh killed wolves. Those paintings, while not particularly good, left me with a bad feeling. A few months later I started painting postcard sized paintings that morphed into 'collaborative' Pop Up Art Galleries. These paintings are playful re-imaginings of famous paintings with a wolf stencil replacing characters in the original. The small size of the paintings was initially influenced by 2 things. I had a conversation with an artist friend about art books, about how no matter how amazing the book, one can never get a sense of the original work, and how much of that has to do with scale. So I wanted to create works that could be reproduced full size. The irony of this is that so much art, even these small scale pieces, are now seen on ones phone, further reducing it from the original. The second influence was a Mark Mothersbaugh show at the Museum of Contemporary Art in Denver. Included in the show were hundreds and hundreds of post cards he had painted. Painting one a day for years. But now the wolves are not wolves anymore. Now they are famous paintings, they are self portraits, representations of humans who conform to what we call the real world. But it’s not so easy…we are also wolves who howl at night for what we are missing. All stories are about wolves. All worth repeating, that is. Anything else is sentimental drivel…Think about it. There’s escaping from the wolves, fighting the wolves, capturing the wolves, taming the wolves. Being thrown to the wolves, or throwing others to the wolves so the wolves will eat them instead of you. Running with the wolf pack. Turning into a wolf. Best of all, turning into the head wolf. No other decent stories exist. --Margaret Atwood The Blind Assassin

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My art practice is about manipulating, layering, and recomposing information primarily found in advertising. Using paper as my primary material my focus is to go beyond traditional collage and create a surface of densely layered material that is rich in pattern, text, images, and color. Much like a painter adds layers of paint to build up their surface I collage layer upon layer of information and images to create striking abstract compositions. As an artist I embrace many other disciplines (Design/ Architecture) to help inform me how to create work with more depth and interest to look at collage like “the architecture of thin layers'' as I am always experimenting with ways I can explore ideas beyond the flat 2D surface. At a distance, the surfaces I create are formal abstractions, but as you move closer you start to see the information come apart as the layered vignettes start to reveal a hidden story and become a subject matter that was not apparent at first encounter. As I creat each piece a process of social anthropology come to mind and I access, study and recontectulize its many fragment searching for a new meaning to present. I like to think of my process of layering material and information as building or constructing a surface. As the surface becomes more and more layered it appears to reveal shadows and crevices revealing yet another dimension of information to consider. Upon building these surfaces the works can appear as high-relief. Over the years, the works I have created have become more and more 3D beyond the once flat surface of paper to be more sculptural and installation based. In the end I am challenging the viewer to align the act of looking with that of reading by using paper, images, and text to create a critical visual narrative about the meaning of information as it is reflected in our personal and social cultures.

column25: A skillful handmade image is reassuring and empathic, whether a Paleolithic equestrian cave painting or a landscape watercolor. Such images validate nature's realities, reinforcing our capacity to behold and understand. In working plein air, meaning outside, from life, no other reference, I am deeply connected to the land and water I paint and stand upon. Each plein air watercolor painting is a performance, in real time, a reflection of a collection of observations and a living document of my presence and attention in the field. My work is honest, and challenging to make. Through iteration, I attempt to retrieve the effects of natural light in watercolor at a large scale, over and over. I seldom make revisions or changes in the studio - the marks seen are the marks made. This process can communicate an experience to the audience, a specific time, place, and light, a gateway to memories and shared realities. I am drawn to scenes that are difficult to see, such as moving water, the glare of the sun, elusive morning and evening atmospheres, live wildlife. Always learning, I am practitioner and student.

Through my art, I materialize into physicality an urgent plea for us to recognize and appreciate the beauty of our collective cultural mosaic.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My artistic journey is deeply influenced by the pioneering spirit of the Bauhaus women weavers. These remarkable individuals were autodidacts, unburdened by preconceived notions, and embraced free experimentation in their craft. Their unwavering commitment to learning through trial and error resonates with my own path as a predominantly self-taught artist. My work revolves around the concept of discovering the subtle beauty concealed within our daily surroundings and transforming often-overlooked materials into expressions of art. In this process, I search to push the boundaries of the materials I am using which enables me to tell a story. My works are infused with traditional weaving patterns, adding texture to their surfaces and evoking a sense of familiarity and connection to our shared history. These patterns serve as a bridge between the past and the present, reminding us of the enduring threads that tie us together. The wellspring of my inspiration is vast and ever-evolving. It draws from my experiences while traveling, the dynamics of current events, and the intricate nuances of my immediate environment. I see my art as a way to capture a moment, to seize it and transform it through the medium of weaving and the power of human connection. Ultimately, my goal is to create art that resonates on a profound emotional level. I aspire to kindle a sense of shared humanity in those who encounter my work, reminding us that we have more in common than what sets us apart. Through my art, I aim to inspire belief in the enduring, connective threads that bind us together, even in our increasingly complex and divided world.

column25: She was born in 1962 in Blender, Germany where she earned a degree in social pedagogy. After a few years of traveling and living in Asia and England, she moved to Texas in 1986. Although she took several years of weaving classes at the Southwest School of Art in the early 1990s, she didn’t start weaving artwork until she returned from a visit to the Bauhaus Exhibit in Berlin in 2009. She found herself inspired by the women who entered the Bauhaus Community thinking they would produce art like the men, using glass, metal and all the media we associate with the Bauhaus period. Instead, having been persuaded into weaving and teaching themselves the craft, these women honed that skill, creating beautiful and unique works of art. Weber is a mostly self-taught artist who weaves with diverse, modern, quotidian materials. Searching for what quietly exists in our daily surroundings, she transforms those often overlooked materials into works of art which demand attention. Her pieces utilize traditional weaving patterns, which provide texture to conventionally flat surfaces and give an assurance of familiarity and connection to our history. Inspiration for her artwork comes from her travels, current events, and her environment. She strives to create something which seizes the moment and transforms it through medium and connection. Her work has been exhibited nationally and internationally and can be found in private as well as public collections.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My painting unites abstraction and figuration. Color elements create an abstract composition while simultaneously depicting a figurative scene. In my painting, I squirt fluid paint out of the bottle onto the canvas, letting gravity and fluid dynamics distort the intended figure. Random results transform the intended meaning. Chance opens the eye to surprise. Using flowing paint to approximate a figurative situation is a method that I have practiced for many years. This method can be applied to a landscape, figure, portrait or any imaginable situation. The juxtaposition of swirling paint with faces, figures and landscapes leads to unexpected possibilities of interpretation, opening a door to the unconscious. My goal is to express emotional energy with color and rhythm, using paint as material, but also as color, in an abstract composition that corresponds to a recognizable scene. I look for ways to reconcile the dynamic tendency of the paint with the requirements of representation.

column25: Brooke McGowen Bio Born in Chicago in 1953, her parents students of the University of Chicago, Brooke took to painting at a very young age. She studied painting at the Art Institute of Chicago before pursuing a science major at Bard College. After graduation she went to Vienna, Austria where she continued her painting studies at the Art Academy in the storied Baroque city. Here she delved into figure drawing, admiring the expressive lines of Schiele and Klimt. She became acquainted with the Actionism movement, where paint was poured or splashed onto models. The question arose, how can you release the material paint onto a figurative scene in a painting? Brooke explored new possibilities of applying paint while working in Austria and Germany, where she lived for five years. Her goal was a fusion of abstract and figurative painting. In 1996, Brooke moved to the South of Portugal, where she could apply her color experiments to glowing landscapes of olive and almond trees. In 2008, Brooke moved to New York City. In 2009, she founded Radical Art Initiative, a collaborative to create visuals, signs and street theater for social action. In 2011 she moved to Peekskill, a river town just north of Manhattan. There, in a spacious studio, Brooke developed her signature method of squirting the paint directly out of the bottle onto the canvas, allowing the paint to follow its own path and subjecting the intended figure to the ravages of chance. In 2017 was the publication of her art book, “The Waterdrinkers”, funded through Kickstarter. A book release was held at Quimby’s Bookstore, Brooklyn. New York group shows included “Me, My Selfie and I, Contemporary Self-portraits” in 2017, and “Transgression, Unsettling Irreverent Contemporary Art” in 2018, both at the Hudson Guild Gallery in Chelsea and “Frozen Gesture” in 2019 at the M. David Gallery in Brooklyn. A solo show on the Lower East Side called “Flowing Figures” in 2018 was curated by Fashion Moda. In 2018, seeking to live in nature, Brooke moved to a wilderness area of Northern California, where mountain views, tenacious vegetation and knarly pines present a daily inspiration. In 2021 she had a solo show at the Little Raven Gallery in San Francisco entitled “New Paintings”. In January, 2022 Brooke spent a one-month artist residency at the 18th St. Arts Center in Santa Monica, California. The Los Angeles SPRING/BREAK Art Show included a solo booth of her paintings and sculptures, curated by Fashion Moda West, in both 2022 and 2023. The New York SPRING/BREAK Art Show gave her a solo booth in 2022. Painting in a 10 x 12 ft. shed on the remote land, Brooke has found the peace needed to delve further into her exploration of applying flowing color to a figurative situation. When not working in her studio, she can be seen romping through the bushes with her dogs or cooking nice vegetables for her husband.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work has always been inspired by my ruminations on my immediate environment and the people with whom I cross paths. Caricature and cartoons are the visual languages that I connect with the most. Although seemingly different, the work has evolved over the years in a very linear path. The newest body of work has always been informed by the work that proceeded it. In the early 2000’s, I was living on a property with several chicken coops converted into human living spaces in Penngrove, CA., a rural area just outside of the suburbs. I was intrigued by the forms and textures of the land, outbuildings, grazing sheep and gleaning chickens. A narrative of masked and decoy animals parading around, battling and torturing each other for dominance over the farmland unfolded in a series of etchings and lithographs. In addition, headlines of the George W. Bush Administration mirrored the actions of these lost and misguided characters. The narrative of territoriality and isolationist fears evolved into an epic battle of inner conflict played out by a trinity of characters: death, the fool and, the madman. This series titled “One Across the Bow” was loosely based on Moby Dick. The characters fumbled about on a voyage without direction or an end. The prints and drawings from this series saw the printing process as another drawing tool rather than an end in itself. Many of the lithographs were hand-worked with monotype as well as watercolor and colored pencil. In 2011, I had grown tired of making “cartoon” imagery and reevaluated my interest in the genre as an expressive style. I abandoned the saturated colors for graphite pencil and contour lines, later switching to acrylic ink with brush and a broader range of mark-making. These mediums allowed me to emphasize the fluid line movement, animated shapes and bold graphics of cartoons. No longer was I exploring a narrative told through cartooning but rather exploring the elements of cartoons through characters in motion. As I explored these twisting, turning and morphing faces (sometimes full figures), I was drawn to the areas where lines intersected creating abstracted “bits and pieces” of anatomical oddities. In drawing these fragmented forms, a personal hieroglyphic language developed. The drawings became monotype prints. A process that allowed me to explore the evolution and deconstruction or decay of language. Recently, I have resumed working with the characters in motion. The images are carved in linoleum and printed to paper as well as t-shirts. The fall of 2023 saw the return of my “live” printing events where the public brings t-shirts for me and guest artists to print directly to the garment. They receive their printed shirts on the spot. We are currently planning the next “Fresh Prints with Wil Smith and Friends” for spring 2024.

The next “Fresh Prints” event will take place in the spring of 2024.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I’ve had the privilege of photographing over 650 trans folks in all 49 continental US states, as well as Hawai’i, Puerto Rico, and Washington D.C. Ides of Gender is a photo series celebrating everyone under the trans umbrella – binary and non-binary. As a queer immigrant, I set out to document the rich, diverse tapestry of this community that is so often overlooked. There is an obsession with trans bodies that society often feels entitled to – dissected and objectified to the point of physical harm. Being cisgender, there was a responsibility to photograph each person honestly and with utmost integrity. From the very beginning, the series has been an exercise in trust. Trusting that a complete stranger will show up and share their authentic self. Extracting their truth and facing my camera intimately, each portrait was a collaboration. I never had to take anyone’s photograph. It was simply given to me. Since moving to the US, my pursuit of community and self-expression has been constant. Being on the road working on Ides of Gender for the past six years ended up – unexpectedly – validating my own queerness and intersectionality. I see myself in every portrait I take. I hope you can see your own journey mirrored back within these lives. Visibility matters. Representation matters.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work focuses on the delicate and fleeting nature of our world by preserving floral elements in ice and photographing them. The act of freezing a flower is paradoxically both preservative and destructive. By suspending a flower in water ice, I take it out of time. It is changed, but its beauty is saved. It will not wilt and it will not drop petals, however it is still destroyed. The still living cells are pierced by ice crystals and killed. Its fragrance is drowned and sealed away. Eventually even its color will inevitably fade and leach into the water when the thawing process begins. Each flower’s death is only delayed. By photographing these moments I attempt to abstract and preserve them further. My work examines our futile instinct to hold on to the ephemeral both in ourselves and in the world around us. I’m asking the viewer to consider how we interact with the natural world both individually and as a whole. Could these drivers of our own behavior be leading us to blindly exploit and destroy the natural beauty of the world around and within us.

column25: XXX, a perpetual mover since childhood, developed adaptability through frequent relocations. Summers in her coastal Maine sanctuary exposed her to the tangible impacts of Climate Change, fueling her determination to capture these transformations. Armed with a camera from an early age, XXX passion led her to Lesley University in Boston, eventually transferring to Columbia College Chicago for a BA in Commercial Photography. Her artistic journey took flight with the 2018 solo exhibition 'Fleur et Lumen' at C33 Gallery, marking the genesis of her exploration with flowers and ice. After a pandemic-induced hiatus, XXX reignited her creative spark in 2023, keenly observing the escalating effects of melting ice caps on her cherished Maine haven. Leveraging her studio skills and determination, she embarked on a mission to spotlight the transient nature of human life and the global climate crisis through her work. XXX actively contributes to the art scene, participating in group exhibitions at Kunstraum studio and the Brooklyn Waterfront Coalition. Her creative momentum culminated in a residency at the School of Visual Arts, showcasing her commitment to bringing impactful art into the world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Influenced by folk art and macabre fairy tales, my artwork is spiritual and tells stories of magic and nature. The history, mythology, and mystery of nature holds a world of information that drives my work. I explore my emotions and experiences, and connect my condition to that of the natural world — my artwork is a visualization of these interactions. Using bold interactions of color, I encapsulate the aura of each piece, allowing it to emanate its own light. A sense of consciousness within the piece allows a conversation with the viewer. Through organic forms and familiar symbols, my work invites the viewer to draw their own conclusions and hold their own space with the artwork. My work shines when it allows the viewer to consider their own experiences, as it has for me. Expanding my knowledge of materials and techniques allows me to explore without boundaries. Each material I introduce adds to the ecosystem of the piece. Through cracking tiles, cutting hundreds of paper shapes, bending wire into loops, carefully smoothing clay and tirelessly entangling wool fibers, I immerse myself in the artwork. What some may find tedious, I see as an opportunity to connect with my intentions. The act of creating a larger form out of thousands of little actions is a direct reflection of my inner workings. I am piecing small ideas together to better understand my existential curiosities. I describe my process as playful prayer – collecting, experimenting, and discovering are driving forces in my work. By creating, I honor the connective consciousness of the natural world and the dormant power it holds. My artworks become shrines to those mysterious and magical forces that surround me, and reside within me. If I were to categorize my work into a particular style, I’d call it existential folk art. While my work is rooted in my own feelings and folk art is more community-centric, I believe these two intentions can coexist. All experiences are shared in some capacity, and by creating, I allow my self-consciousness to expand and connect with others. When we speak for ourselves from a place of authenticity and see where we connect with others, that is where the spirit of folk art emerges. Creative expression that is for and by the people is more important now than ever. I view my own work as a part of that larger story, where everyone is working in their own spheres, and it all connects into a fascinating new emergence of folk art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create simulations in painting, photography and short films where futuristic technology is a force to experience the sublime with. Through the work I create space where Latino myth and folklore are part of the visions of a futuristic world. This is an extension of my dual-cultural background because through the work I combine American dreams with Colombian heritage. Imagining the future is a strategy to question our reality. It expands on the thinking that people familiar with migration engage in- to imagine possible lives and alternate destinies. Individuals who have migrated embrace elements in their lives that over time can change their identity. These transformations can include changes in language, worldview and how we relate to our heritages over time. In the paintings, futuristic vehicles, the designs of these vehicles and their placement in rural landscapes are symbols for these internal changes. The paintings present these changes on a scale of time of multiple generations of a family. The works presents recurring scenes in the life of immigrants but in the future like traveling home for family reunions, funerals, burials in outer space, a Colombian restaurant as a rest stop on a space station. The works presents scenes generations into the future and reflecting the way our cultures meet and are shaped by technology today. The paintings are about noticing what changes and what stays the same over time. Sometimes this appears when someone returns to a hometown after many years and notices how the vehicles are transformed, but the town is the same. In a town in Colombia, in the future, the shadows of flying cars become part of the cultural landscape. The townsfolk watching these shadows represent what is essential to an identity and what is timeless. In these works, the futuristic vehicles and their designs represent hybrid identities. In the 1900s, Colombians imported buses and modified them for mountainous regions. Over the years, people embraced these vehicles decorating them in local emblems and imagery of their towns. Now these buses are icons of Colombia. Perhaps flying vehicles will be embraced similarly. This is about questioning the process of how something displays “authenticity” and how over time, new elements that a culture adopts become classic and traditional. In the “yo no soy marinero, soy capitán (I am not a sailor I am the captain)” painting, a man in a rural town observes a futuristic vertical vehicle hovering by. The vehicle is very colorful and has these traditional designs. It appears to be Colombian, but there is something different about it- just like people who are children of immigrants that return to their family’s place of origin. We return as a combination of identities. We look back at the culture at how it continues to transform, and the culture looks back at us too. The combination of elements in the paintings is meant to break with the expectation that rural communities are not part of stories of the future. The works question notions of progress and investigates what may be timeless across generations.

column25: Juan José Cielo (b. 1996, Medellin, Colombia) is a Colombian-American artist, based in New York working in painting, photography, and short films. In 2017, Cielo was selected as an artist-in-residence with scientists and researchers at the Mars Desert Research Station in Hanksville, Utah. In 2023, Cielo was invited to be an artist-in-residence at Fountainhead in Miami, Florida and with Piero Atchugarry Gallery in their Garzon, Uruguay Studio. His work was featured on Univision, RCN Internacional television (Colombia), and published in National Geographic Traveler magazine, El Heraldo Newspaper (Colombia), Hyperallergic and ARTnews. Cielo is the recipient of the 2022 YoungArts Jorge M. Pérez $25,000 award.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My sculptures are a product of my continual desire to understand and change the meaning of objects, especially those that were once valued, then discarded. I search for and alter mass-produced secondhand ceramic figurines, stuffed toys and furniture to create sculptures that embrace the unique aspects of the original objects while pushing them into the space of the uncanny. Through my collecting, recycling and reconstruction I reinvent the world, and bring attention, interest and love back to unwanted things. I grew up in the rural Midwest with a collection of ceramic animals made from mass-produced molds and hand-painted by my grandmother. I loved these things and as I grew to understand how they were perceived by others it confused me to see them relegated as lowly, laughably ridiculous kitsch, rather than accessible ornaments worthy of recognition. In working with these objects, I draw attention to their subtle uniqueness and give them the respect they deserve. In my newest works, I’ve been making super-organisms-- larger sculptures made with many figurines. These works, like my smaller ceramic reconstructions, often use the mask of comedy to make the tragedy of consumerist waste and lost love palatable. Referencing fairytale and folklore, the overlap of science and science-fiction, and the psychology of comfort objects and consumer culture, my work sentimentally and humorously offers considerations about the power of kitsch, the malleability of identity, and the 'truth' of objects as they pass from one owner to another.

column25: Debra Broz collects and deconstructs secondhand ceramic kitsch figurines, then combines them into reimagined versions of their former selves. Using ceramics restoration techniques, she effaces history by creating seamless reconstructions that are part humor, part mad science, and part tender sentimentality for the rural Midwest where she grew up. In her larger works, she creates domestic installations that combine her reconstructed figurines with sculpture made from disassembled secondhand stuffed toys and discarded furniture. Broz was born in 1981 in Springdale, Arkansas, and was raised in rural central Missouri. She received her BFA with honors from Maryville University - St. Louis in 2003. Shortly thereafter, she moved to Austin, Texas where she trained as a ceramics restorer and began using ceramic figurines in her art practice. Broz moved to Los Angeles in 2014, and then to Seattle, Washington in 2022. Broz shows with various galleries in the US, and has had exhibits at the American Museum of Ceramic Art, Austin Museum of Art, and Houston Center for Contemporary Craft. Her work has been featured in print in Ceramics Monthly, American Craft, and Frankie magazines; in a number of online publications; and in two international surveys of contemporary ceramics.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Rebecca M. Fullerton has a passion for the forests and mountains of New England. When not painting her region's wild scenery in her studio or en plein air, she is probably hiking, running on a trail, or out on a lake or river in a kayak. The grandeur of alpine summits, the intricacy of intertwining limbs in the deep woods, and sparkle of rushing water down a mountain stream inspire and inform her painting process. Within her practice, Fullerton specializes in landscape painting in the realist and impressionist traditions. She has been painting in oils and watercolors for over twenty-five years. Her focus is landscape painting in the mountains of New England, particularly in the White Mountains of New Hampshire and Maine. Each year she hikes into the backcountry, taking in the dramatic scenery of the wilderness, sketching, painting, and taking photographs for later use. Fullerton is always trying to capture the special atmosphere unique to this area with its changeable weather, steep trails, and limitless views as a way to promote the unique and fragile beauty of places that deserve conservation.

column25: Rebecca M. Fullerton resides in Bethlehem, New Hampshire, on the edge of the White Mountain National Forest. Originally from Vermont, she grew up in southern New Hampshire. After a long spell of living in Boston, she returned to the Granite State in 2018 to pursue her dural career as an Archivist and Artist, to live among the mountains she aspires to capture ever more authentically in paint. Fullerton hold a Bachelor's degree in Studio Art and Art History from Hartwick College, as well as a Master's degree in Museum Studies from the Harvard Extension School.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Like a dream, my works resemble reality, but with distortion around the aspects that may be difficult for the mind to confront. As with an ancient fairy-tale, a 'fantasy' story allows the viewer to process a difficult, yet relevant topic in a way that is both honest and entertaining. My works often analyzes the story threads shared throughout the myths and legends of several cultures. Some of these pieces explore character archetypes, while others are reinterpretations of particular tales. I am particularly interested in the way that our stories portray women and children. I often use my work to reframe these narratives, portraying children and women as the central agency of their stories. My process begins with a character. I imagine a clear image in my mind of a crucial moment in their story. I will sketch out this image and as I do so, I often more fully develop the narrative behind this snapshot. Drawing both from my love for Art history and Pop Culture, I invent symbols to illustrate the themes behind my work. I will then take photo references for my work, and use a combination of my own photography and other sources to create 'digital collages' as a jump-off for my visual storytelling. I then begin the process of creating my work on paper. I draw freehand, observing my 'collages'. As the “reality” of my work begins to emerge, I switch focus away from my references and put my imagination down on paper. As I build up the layers of my work, the fantastical elements become more colorful, often slightly textured or reflective, depending on what materials I have chosen. The end result is an image that draws from the tradition of representational art and morphs it into an exploration of the mysterious and intangible.
