column25: Grace Netanya is an artist and storyteller whose art combines contemporary and traditional ideas to create an art fusion that is both Avant-Garde and introspective. At a very young age, Grace taught herself to draw in order to communicate stories and characters from her imagination. A childhood of homeschooling in Florida provided her with the time and isolation to become engrossed in her imagination, and today she is interested in pulling the magical and surreal out of the ordinary and banal. Grace is interested in putting unique spins on existing styles and finding new ways to push a medium past the conceived limit. She prefers to use mixed-media techniques with ink, graphite, colored pencils and acrylics. However, she loves to discover new mediums and is constantly expanding her repertoire. Grace’s unique combination of colorful fantasy and realism has caught the eyes of the art world both at home and internationally. She had her first exhibit at the age of fourteen, at MOAS FL, and has exhibited with such prestigious organizations as The ARC Realism Salon and The Colored Pencil Society. She has won many awards, such as The Copic Award Grand Prize 2019, 2022 Martha Vineyard’s Drawing Prize 1st place, and most recently, Boyne’s Artist Prize 2022 Second Place. Her work has also been published with imaginative art publications such as Infected by Art and Supersonicart.com

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My abstract work evolves from repeated themes based on contradiction and movement, forces materializing in collage, drawing, painting, sculpture, and installation. Mixed media provides a fluid structure for the exploration of disparate ideas and offers a broad material vocabulary for unpredictable combinations. Artistic tension in my work arises from: still versus energetic, organic versus synthetic, fragility versus strength, bizarre versus logical, flat versus relief, wall versus room and chaos versus order. These opposites co-exist and agitate within physical and psychic space. My experiences with improvisational dance have created a body awareness that frequently inserts itself into my work. Copious markings and unnamable shapes reflect movement in space, a searching for direction and discovery, found on paths and within layers. I am intrigued with the relationship between chaos and order as a symbol for life processes as is stated in chaos theory; that within the randomness of chaotic systems (such as in nature), there are underlying moments of order and pattern. My works are often filled with random, energetic movements in paint, ink, wood, and collage. Found among markings and layered materials are moments of ordered patterns - visual stops of rhythm and repetition. These moments offer structured relief from meandering, seemingly aimless, dashing paths and drippings of paint. Because the majority of my work is of an abstract nature, I am fascinated by neuroscientist Eric Kandel’s research that explains how the brain processes abstract versus figurative images. He also explains how reductionism in art and brain science are quite similar and that these two fields are often interwoven.

column25: Karen Snouffer concentrates on creating abstract mixed-media objects, collage, painting, and installation, exploring tensions in movement and contradiction as forces to expand play and beauty. Her experience with improvisational dance influences her visual language. Materiality is a visual force in her work through the merging of non-traditional media with conventional materials and techniques. Having researched chaos theory, she explores randomness and order found in nature, works of art and the art making process. She is also intrigued by definitions of abstraction, how the brain processes it, and how creating it impacts her studio process. Snouffer has exhibited nationally at: Art Center South Florida, Miami; Aqua Art Fair, Miami; The Work Space, New York; Cleveland Center for Contemporary Art; Weston Art Gallery, Cincinnati; Hammond Harkins Gallery, Columbus, OH; The Ohio State University Center for the Humanities; Poor Dog Space, L.A.; Soo Visual Arts Center, Minneapolis; and La Médiathèque, Epernay, France. She is a former residency fellow at the Fine Arts Work Center, Provincetown, MA, and a Artist-in-Residence at Headlands Center for the Arts, Sausalito, CA; a recipient of six Ohio Arts Council individual artist fellowships; a GLCA New Directions Initiative; a McGregor Foundation Global Exchange Grant to France. She is represented by Hammond Harkins Gallery in Columbus, Ohio. Her work is in the Girls Club Collection, the Pizzuti Collection, Sun Life of Canada, The Greater Columbus Convention Center and numerous private collections. Snouffer has collaborated with poets, dancers, sculptors and new media artists. Her work has been published in three books: The Next Hedgerow; Love Life; Love Life: Memory and Nature at Play; and Somatic Principles and Dance. Snouffer received her BS in Education and her MFA at The Ohio State University. She is a Professor Emerita at Kenyon College and with a studio and residence in Granville, Ohio.

column25: My delicately rendered drawings manipulate perception, creating fantastical iterations in which artificial and natural imagery fuse to become newly impossible sites. Working in graphite pencil on paper and wood panels, I create obsessively detailed scenes with cross hatching and other techniques, the imagery inspired by both the urban environment and the wilderness. The works juxtapose the banal with the unbelievable in allegorical compositions that speak to environmental degradation and the complicated human relationship with nature. In my series 'Species in Danger,' each pencil drawing highlights an endangered animal native to Southern California along with the human-made causes for its decline, juxtaposed with a toxic element of human society that I argue more rightly needs to be 'endangered.' The goal is to create a piece for every one of the approximately dozens of federally endangered creatures in the varied ecosystems of Southern California. From invertebrates to mammals, this series presents an allegorical rather than didactic picture of the intertwined fates of humanity and other animal species. These graphite drawings on wood panels use pop culture references, old-fashioned decorative borders and clip art, emojis, and a variety of the iconic California palm trees to illustrate the circumstances threatening these animals. Palm trees are incorporated to add another layer of meaning, as they both personify the Southern California vibe yet are a non-native, water guzzling species. Symbolism and references to historical events hint at specific aspects of humanity’s predilections to destruction and polarization, in a personal expression of what I think needs to become extinct. These highly detailed works employ the dissonance of pleasing aestheticism with alarming imagery.

column25: Thoughts on “Women’s Work”, the emotional labor silently demanded across social, political and economic embodiments of life are woven together with the experiences of being a child of immigrants. The quiet cultural balancing acts that go unnoticed in predominantly white homogeneous landscapes like the Pacific Northwest. The kid in the cafeteria with the 'weird lunch'- the child of diaspora. My mother immigrated from Poland and my father from Iran. My work explores a person's relationship to heritage and destructive historical events. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an Irish diasporan figurative painter, my artistic practice is a dance between two worlds – California, the place of my birth, and Ireland, the land where I spent my formative years. This duality has been the crucible of my identity, a journey marked by a perpetual sense of not quite belonging, a continuous negotiation between two homes that exist both in reality and in the recesses of memory. The core of my current painting series is an exploration of this sense of displacement, a quest to unearth the elusive concept of 'home.' Growing up with the constant awareness of straddling two cultures, I've grappled with the ever-present dichotomy. Through my painting, I seek a resolution, a visual synthesis that incorporates both Irish and American influences, reconciling the differences that have marked my existence. Portraits and scenes from both cultures become my vessels of expression, capturing not only the external features but also the intangible essence of each. These paintings weave imaginary scenes that exist as loose representations from the tapestry of my memories. In these scenes, magpies, bare trees, hooded figures, and gloomy atmospheres emerge as recurring motifs, reflecting the impact of my Irish experience. Recently, my artistic inquiry has delved into the menacing nature of Irish youth subculture. Through narrative and formal investigations, I aim to unravel the complexities and nuances of this sociological phenomenon, adding an edgy sub-narrative to the broader dialogue within my work. By grounding my representational figures in atmospheric settings, I draw inspiration from classical Romanticism. This deliberate choice is a nod to the genre's ability to evoke emotion and contemplation. However, my compositions take on a contemporary openness, allowing for the exploration of edgy sub-narratives informed by the clash and confluence of Irish and Californian cultures. In essence, my art becomes a bridge—a visual and emotional confluence where the split narratives of my upbringing find a harmonious resolution. Through the blending of imagery, the negotiation of cultural differences, and the exploration of sociological phenomena, I strive to create a body of work that transcends borders, capturing the universal essence of the diasporan experience while offering a personal testament to the continuous search for a place called 'home.'

column25: Working between neon, blacklight, and holography, I'm an educator and experimentalist who deconstructs light to harness its affective powers. Leaning into deep space as a key to our collective subconscious and atomic origins, my vivid sculptures and material interventions overlap optical effects to source new visual encounters. Light is a language we experience through the body. Its palpable glow and high-visibility powers of attraction affect our environments as well as our minds. Yet light itself is massless—a non-medium—a visually vibrating absence of material substance our human forms use to perceive heat, space, distance, time, and motion. I find the exchange of electrons which generate light to be an allegory for connection and I create spaces where a glow can strive towards a tangible presence. In these exchanges we become linked, defragmenting our little place in the universe, one encounter at a time.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Clause Fischler, a French social scientist asks, “if we do not know what we eat, how can we know what we are?” Food contributes to an individual’s physical and psychological well-being and expresses cultural identity through cooking, communion, and consumption. My current art practice uses food and the narratives shared at the kitchen table to make sense of my hyphenated gastronomy as an American woman from Dominican and European heritage. To create my mixed-media paintings, I combine digital programs, like Adobe Photoshop, with traditional materials such as acrylic paint, paper, and markers. Family members, tableware, and food are all rendered semi-realistically with bold brushstrokes. I use colored markers to draw expressionistic contour lines overtop acrylic paint to emphasize different emotions in my figures or the intricacies of cultural foods and their ingredients.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I still remember how I looked forward to Thursday nights as a kid, when I could stay up with my family to watch a Hollywood film on our one-channel television in our home in Baghdad. America on the screen was fascinating; the landscape seemed not of the Earth to me. I never imagined that I would one day live in Southern California, in the strange landscape of those films. In Iraq, it was hard to think about art and even harder to create it. The constant danger and daily routine reality of being on the edge of death made the landscape of the big dusty city look like an aged poem. As a teenager, I observed war and I understood my life through that lens. After I escaped to the US, that lens also distorted the memoryscape of Baghdad. The routine of everyday violence and chaos made everything strange and faraway, and maybe that was the hardest part of living in a war zone. When I moved to Montana for college, the chaos and uncertainty I experienced growing up in Baghdad through wars, sanctions, and diaspora left me with many blurry memories that started to become clearer as time passed in the calm, empty state of Montana. Fragmented memories returned: how the people and the streets felt after every explosion, when dust filled our mouths and ears. My films lean towards the non-fiction, experimental and poetic form. Poetry in cinema slows time or magnifies detail. In tiny, vivid visual details there is something vast and universal, particularly when the object or gesture chosen illuminates the displacement of emotion onto a seemingly coincidental thing. My films aim to give the story and its emotion a sensory reality through creating similar displacement. I try to draw from my memory of growing up in Iraq, as part of the indigenous Chaldean/Assyrian minority, to find those kinds of harsh or romantic moments, and contrast them with the strangely calm and uncertain life I have in the US. I weave nonfiction, poetry, voice narration, found footage, and abstract image because this juxtaposition feels closest to embodying both the chaos and the irreconcilable beauty of my memories and my present. Although my subject matter often derives from my experience of war, I try to bridge the cultures of the East and the West in my films. I am very interested in abstract, nontraditional modes of visual representation. I rely on music in my films, and I believe a song can turn a simple image into something powerful. Juxtaposing sound and image from distinct cultures, such as heavy metal with an image of Iraq, can complicate the emotional impact of the image. In one of my films Bitter with a Shy Taste of Sweetness (included in portfolio), I take an experimental approach to navigate the meaning of fate in Baghdad and the concept of survival in Southern California. The film looks critically at California’s intense quality of a seemingly utopian, happy, clean, and wealthy landscape and explores what it means to carry trauma and nostalgia in a world of pink dreams (an Arabic phrase with the same meaning as the phrase “pipe dreams”). Visually, I wanted to represent California’s landscape as foreign yet familiar, disrupted by my childhood memories. The camera tilts or spins, making the beautiful Southern California beaches and mountains jarring while the sound distorts the bright colors of the landscape. Through second-person writing and shrilling visuals, the film aims to make the viewer feel off-balance, disoriented, and estranged from the landscape and content, offering them the dislocating feeling of survival and immigration.

My work explores the idea of a homesickness for a place you cannot return, a home that maybe never was. I create cinematic scenes of abandoned houses through photography and incorporate traditional figure drawings using colored pencils. These figures are drawn directly on printed photographs of the abandoned houses to discuss lingering memories within the spaces. I transform decayed homes into emotionally haunting scenes to give a sense of loss and nostalgia, and to challenge the stereotype of eerie abandoned houses. Documenting these spaces serves the purpose of preserving histories, as well as creating memoirs to the echoes of the past that still reside in the homes

McDaniel's cinematic scenes are windows to the past and the lingering presence of the people who once inhabited the spaces. She photographs abandoned houses, prints high quality images, and proceeds to draw figurative elements directly onto the image to create memories left behind. She is interested in the ideas of memory, loss, nostalgia, and homesickness. McDaniel is a graduate of Fine Arts and Art History at Purdue University in West Lafayette, Indiana. She received an Office of Undergraduate Research scholarship to assist in continuing the development of her photo drawing project, which explores memory and loss through haunting portraiture of the past in decayed spaces. McDaniel is currently working and living in Wisconsin and continues to research, photograph, and incorporate abandoned places in her work.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
When I was seven, I arrived in the US on the pretense of a vacation, only to be told I could no longer return to Korea. My parents, working artists back home, scrapped a living in the service industry and had to choose survival over art. In the wake of this experience, I have turned to art and all its expansiveness to integrate the pieces of my life that were forced apart. This inspired me to start video game titled 'ReverSeoul.' The game draws from my life as a formerly undocumented person and return to my home country after nineteen years of separation. It is a point-and-click adventure with a first person horror twist, featuring themes of deceit, displacement, and tearing through multiple fabrics of reality. I am primary illustrator and programmer of the game. My art practice is multimodal, and include freehand drawing, monoprint techniques, and digital pixel art. I am not classically trained and I rely on continuing education classes in anatomy, life sketching, and digital art to find mentorship and community.

column25: Much of our lives involve the tension between control and chance. We strive to determine outcomes, but life throws the unexpected at us. The clay monoprint medium mirrors this uncertainty. The art of the clay monoprint is based on a tradition where new works literally build on the prior; the artist designs each original print upon the remnants of the prints before. Clay monoprint artists know that random elements of past prints will appear randomly; the artist shapes the work but never has complete control, much as life itself. With this in mind, the medium and artist focus on process over product. Pennsylvanian Mitch Lyons, the clay monoprint medium and techniques creator, passed away in early 2018. I was fortunate to learn specialized techniques from him before his passing, and now I feel passionate about helping to share his unique art form both through creating works and teaching others.

column25: I draw, document and archive intimate scenes and histories in an effort to reframe the way we talk about Blackness, Queerness and the realities of Black Trans Parenthood. Culminating in several self-contained but interlocking installments 'Dear Beautiful Black Baby' is an ongoing drawing and installation based documentary practice traversing afro-diasporic narratives and dream stewardship of both genetic and chosen familial bonds impacted by socio-economic disenfranchisement, violence, and geographical displacement. column25: Music made my world go round. Art made it possible for me to travel the world. Though from an early age, I never dreamed it was possible. I had an affinity towards music, and surprisingly, I didn’t care much for art. I remember begging my mom to draw my assignments because I couldn’t figure out how to do it myself. Doodling caused me more angst than anything. It wasn’t until I started watching draw-with-me children’s television like Pappyland that I found inspiration and guidance in the craft. Once high school started, I took every art class I could take, though between art and sports, I chose sports to stay close to my dad. I thought I could be the athlete of the family. It made my dad proud to see me be the point guard on the basketball team. Sports was a way for me to feel accepted; being on a team made me feel like a part of something bigger than myself. After eight years of competitive sports, I lost my ability to play, and for the following year, I lost all of my friends, too. I was lonely for most of high school, feeling helpless for losing friends because of something I couldn’t control. It was painfully hard to bounce back and make lasting relationships. What used to be early-morning basketball practices and away games became late-night searches for music and travel destinations. Music became the only thing that made sense to me. Art became the only way to express my emotions, but I continued to shy away from the dark ones. I only made art for other people. In 2010, when I enrolled in AP Studio Art in my senior year of high school, I had to focus on what I wanted to do and what I wanted to conceptualize into tangible existence. I couldn’t figure it out. With limited guidance and no one to truly lean on (not even my family), I was lost. When my teacher said that my art would not hold up in the real world, it took away the last shred of hope I had to pursue the career. I let go of my passion for art and went to college for science, though admittedly, I continued to make art as tokens of appreciation to my family and friends. My journey as an artist has been a winding path for as long as I could remember, a narrative of self-discovery that led me from being a competitive sport-driven high school student to a science-focused college student to a mindful explorer and visual creator. Despite the initial discouragement I felt about pursuing art, I found solace in immersing myself in the intricate wonders of nature's connections. Before I found my place in nature, I found my footing through learning about the connections within the body. With each body being so intricate, complex, and unique, I knew generalizing the health of humans as a whole was a moot point. If we were fundamentally different on a molecular scale, it's safe to assume our interactions with our natural surroundings altered us on a visceral level. Learning ways to generalize health made me question why I was a health major in the first place. I enjoyed learning about the body, but something was still missing. The pivotal moment of transformation occurred in 2014 during my senior year studying abroad in Australia. Away from the familiar confines of my academic routine, I rediscovered my deep-seated love for art that had been buried beneath the demands of a different path. This rekindled passion became the catalyst for a significant shift in my life. Instead of continuing my medical studies, I embraced the unknown and moved back to Australia after my college graduation in 2016. There, I worked menial jobs, applied (and failed) at places that needed art for their businesses, and fixed up an old 1988 Mitsubishi Delica to live out of. Van life put things into perspective. From traveling with almost nothing to my name, I realized that the love and support of a significant other and the trust within a community have a massive effect on what dreams are worth pursuing. They saw the potential within me when I was too doubtful to wonder, 'What if?'. The idea that we have all of the answers within ourselves and only we can make our dreams come true turned out to be a lie for me. I couldn't have encouraged myself to pursue my passion without my partner by my side and friendly strangers commissioning me for art. I remember a special moment in Australia when I worked at a coffee shop. I sold my first commission pieces to a woman who was a frequent customer. The two drawings (one turtle and one snake) were for her sons. The second commission was from a construction worker who also came to the coffee shop regularly; he gave the owl drawing to his mother and paid me double for it because of how much he loved it. I knew I had something going, so I kept going with it. In 2017, I discovered a program that could combine my love for science and art. Since I just picked up the hobby again a few months back, I felt compelled to wait a few years so that I could build up my artistic skills. After traveling around for another year working as a backpacking camp counselor for teenagers, a waitress at a dive bar, and a snowboard instructor at a ski resort, creative pursuits still called to me as I settled in Colorado. In the dead of winter, though, I suffered a nasty concussion while participating in a snowboarding workshop, so I hung up my board and for the time being, called it quits. That concussion pulled my head down from the powdery snow-topped mountains, and I decided I wasn't going to be a ski bum anymore. I needed to be all in or out with my art because indecision made matters so much more difficult to navigate. I worked tirelessly to figure out how to create my first professional art portfolio. After less than two months of constant work, I submitted my application to the prestigious Science Illustration Graduate Program at California State University in Monterey Bay, CA. This educational endeavor marked a profound turning point for me. My focus expanded from the narrow confines of medical science to the broader realm of environmental conservation. Through my newfound artistic lens, I began to explore and appreciate the interconnectedness of the natural world. Living in the mountains was breathtakingly beautiful, but getting accepted into the program and moving to California for graduate school gave me purpose. Once I completed the program, I wanted to travel again. After agreeing to paint a mural for the Hawaiian Islands Humpback Whale National Marine Sanctuary in Maui, I had the chance to research with NOAA, scuba dive for reference pictures, and design my own 30 ft by 10 ft solo mural. For two months, I immersed myself completely into my craft, successfully finalizing six projects with them. Right after that, I moved to San Francisco to complete another internship, but this one was a 180-degree change from my previous experience. I worked with a lead artist and her partner as an artist assistant along with 11 other artists on a 50 ft by 100 ft mural. Days were long and arduous, and the toxic feelings from high school resurfaced at the forefront of my mind again. I finished up the 4 week internship to the best of my abilities then moved back home to lick my wounds and regroup my thoughts. I was catapulted into my favorite career yet but quickly put back into place once the pandemic swept the world. I resorted to working as a bartender and waitress after quarantine, finding odd art jobs here and there, and struggling to make ends meet. Things felt like they took a turn downwards before picking back up again in 2021 when I was invited to my first mural festival in Sand City, CA. Working alongside some of the most talented local artists gave me a glimpse of what my life could be like in this art community. After four years of graduate school, COVID-19, and hospitality jobs, I connected with new people. I had friends in the science illustration program, but after graduation, they all went their separate ways. I felt like I needed to prove myself within this community to be seen again. The mural festival was a beacon of light, navigating my artistic comeback after the pandemic. I've worked in art markets and music festivals since then, getting hired by a music festival production company at the start of 2022. After my interview with the co-founder, I screamed and sang loudly in my car. I thought I had finally found my people. Quickly though, I discovered that that giddy, naive, ecstatic feeling I felt after that first interview would be a distinctively rare feeling for the rest of 2022. I picked up this position with the hope I'd be able to develop my artistic skills, or so I was led to believe. Along with art, music helped me navigate life when I couldn't articulate my feelings, so I figured if I combined my love for music with my adoration for science and art, I could have everything. It felt possible for a split second. In a nutshell, 2022 was riddled with anxiety, depression, and walking on metaphorical eggshells. Instead of designing posters, I managed thirteen different social media accounts, provided customer service care, designed marketing flyers, and organized files. I was quick to show my desire for filmmaking and video editing, and they quickly saw my talent as a way to expand their profits. They encouraged me to make videos, but not at the expense of everything else I was expected to accomplish. Once again, I needed to prove myself, especially my self-worth. I soon learned that I flew too close to the sun and burned my heart. Soon after, I transitioned from employee to contractor, and after a year, nothing changed. Instead, it got worse. They contracted me as a muralist for Cali Roots, and a few days before the event, they had the artists paint over the existing murals from their previous festivals. One of those painted-over murals was mine. They intended to display them at each festival to act as barriers, and again, they betrayed my trust. To make up for it, they gave us VIP passes. I never received an apology. After that, I spent a few months contemplating my relationship with them. On one hand, they took a chance on me and provided a space to develop my passion for filmmaking, but on the other hand, it became apparent that they only wanted to use my talent for their financial gain with zero regard for me as a person. I decided to cut my contract with them and open myself up to new opportunities. It was the best decision I made in 2023 because I picked up a six thousand dollar filmmaking gig right after I let go of what was holding me back. In October 2022, one of the friends I gained at the mural festival in 2021 reached out and set up a meeting for us and the founders of the Youth Arts Collective. Built as a studio space for high school and college students to explore creativity without financial or societal limitations, this non-profit organization started in 2000 and has created significant strides in developing the artist community within the younger generation. Once I stepped into the studio, I felt a sense of calmness and peace. Maybe this was where my tribe resided. I've been working there ever since, and the opportunities I received as an art mentor lit up the dark tunnel carved at the start of 2020. The people I now surround myself with support me with all of my creative endeavors, whether it is filmmaking, painting, printmaking, or mentoring. For 2024, I have an exciting mural underway at the Monterey Beach Hotel. I'm working on a documentary about nature and art with the Arts Council for Monterey County, and I'll be hosting a printmaking workshop with the Monterey Museum of Art. Classes are starting soon for the Western Flyer Foundation, and I'll be teaching classes there while also mentoring at the Youth Arts Collective. With so much good coming out of those treacherous four years of instability, confusion, and anxiety, I have faith that I will continue to succeed. As long as I have support within the community, I know I can do anything. With my art, I know I can give back to those around me. It's the highest form of repayment I can provide. I'm slowly but surely building up my art business so I can start navigating art communities around the world. I hope to inspire more creative communities through our like-minded love for natural connections. The Monterey Community saw my potential, and now I get to work with people whose values align with mine and feel safe enough to express myself authentically. I have found my place in nature, and now I can work on what matters most to me: to inspire others to connect with what truly makes them feel alive, discover love in every living thing, and open hearts to embrace the beauty in unity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am able to explore unwelcome feelings and make peace with trauma through naive imagery. To me, a lighthearted wrapper opens doors to vulnerability as if protected by a shiny veneer. Likewise, I feel the viewer can digest uncomfortable truths they might have avoided. Clay’s tendency toward the naive continues to shape my artistic practice. My work is intentionally saccharine in order to mediate the bitterness of daily life with the joy (and potential shock) of self-exploration. In Trash Cowgirl a pair of confident legs wearing cowboy boots and a lumpy hand holding a lit cigarette languish over the edge of a metallic waste bin. The piece is finished with a cartoonish banana peel that hugs the base. While funny at first sight, the piece belies a darker implication. I find candy coated armor a preferable form of protection.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In our current political climate of amplified voices and vehement certainties of belief, I explore what happens when things aren’t so definite. I begin a painting with a specific reference, but soon cease using it. Instead, let the painting lead me to unknown places. Objects begin to change shape; planes become distorted, crashing into one another, melting. I often paint upside down in order to view the subject as an abstract composition, enabling me to focus on color and movement rather than representation. I throw myself into the work with abandon, covering up or breaking apart surfaces I have just created. In becoming willing to destroy my work, I allow for something entirely new to emerge. These processes occur multiple times during the creation of a piece, removing the image even further from reality. My use of thick, impasto paint, gouges, intense color, and expressive brushwork all contribute to obscuring the specific features of my subject while revealing previously hidden emotional qualities. For me, it is in these emotions where the truth lies.

column25: Project Overview: How to Break A Zebra How to Break A Zebra is the title of a full body of work, including mixed media paintings, drawings, sculptures, prints, and an accompanying book. All works despite differentiating media(s) and a spectrum of styles will use narrative and repeated imagery of the main character- “The Zebra” -to unify the body of work. How to Break A Zebra portrays anthropomorphic themes of individuality, spirit, and freedom. The name 'zebra' originates in the Latin equiferus, meaning 'wild horse'. Attempts to domesticate zebras have been historically unsuccessful. It’s thought that having evolved around large predators (including early humans) they became aggressive, thus making their domestication complicated. How to Break a Zebra first establishes methods used to tame, restrain (colonize), and “break” people, both past and present. “The Zebra” character personifies what cannot, and should not be broken in a person. “The Zebra” is the hero of the narrative, followed through all works. “The Cowboy” is the villain, used as a portrayal of American domestication. Ultimately, the tone of How To Break A Zebra is ruthless- yet humorous. The work is meant to reflect the audience’s fantasies of freedom and encourage the observation of one’s own clout, but to do it in an undeniably funny way. The best way to tell hard truths is in the form of a good joke.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The material in most of my work is the sound, uses and meaning of language. My interest in sound and language started after a year of being a selective mute. In several sound and video installations, the use of phonemes and the intonation of words form the basis of my sound compositions. As an early adopter of integrating a computer in the creation of my work, I characterize the building of computer-controlled devices as “instrument building” and the writing of software as the “method of composition”. One of my “instruments' is a sixteen speaker computer-controlled sound distribution system in which the computer controls any one or combination of speakers active at any time. The software is programmed to move sound in space in several unique patterns thus creating a sonic three dimensional sculpture.

column25: At a young age, I was diagnosed with dyslexia. Reading and writing was difficult, but there was a feeling inside me that needed a mode of creative expression. I was drawn to the visual mediums of the structural film and video movement of the 1960’s and 70’s. I apprenticed with filmmaker/science fiction illustrator Ed Emishweller for three years while in high school. Later I went on to SUNY Buffalo, Center for Media Studies where I apprenticed and studied with film artist Paul Sharits as well with photographer/filmmaker Hollis Frampton and video artists Woody and Steina Vasulka. As I matured as an artist, the main material in my work became the uses, meaning and sounds of language in the creation of sound/multimedia installations. My interest in sound and language intensified after a year of being a selective mute. In several sound and video installations, the use of phonemes and the intonation of words form the basis of my multimedia compositions. My artistic career was revitalized by the Maker Movement in the 2010’s. I taught several “Art and Technology” courses to middle and high school students and mentored high school students in Kingston’s Alternative High School. During this time I created several sculptural pieces while organizing and displaying in official Maker Faires in Poughkeepsie, NY in 2015-2017. Since my retirement, I have resumed my art career combining my video and sound experience with sculptural and electronic elements. column25: Bridge-building is central to my practice and trying to reconcile the mind and the body is the biggest bridge. Making work in physical space is—in part—an attempt to create moments of embodiment in an age of disembodying advertisements, interfaces, and social circumstances. The tension generated when opposing forces are introduced then begets conflict or challenge that invites viewers to deconstruct their assumptions about the content. Through this, the work hopefully urges a move towards resolution and reconstruction into a new, more empathetic and understanding way of thinking. Meditating on the space in-between is, from where I sit, one of the more honest and effective ways to ascend to loving understanding of people and things. That is to say, my work attempts to name things by what they are not rather than what they are. I do this in part to avoid assuming the role of advocate, advertiser, or expert. Instead, the role of my practice and my work is to create moments that foster inquiry and reflection for the sake of human connection. The work currently deals with notions of racial identity in America, namely the subtle and personal intersections found in my experience of being a Black man with an anaphylaxis inducing allergy. It also reflects on how mass media and individuals engage with the topic of racial equity and how this can at times be both well intentioned and counterproductive. The work also investigates the “attention-economy” and how the avenues through which we receive information impact the legitimacy of our collective sense of identity and values.

My work is simultaneously an exploration of the world around me and a reflection of my own experiences with it. The subject matter of my pieces primarily focuses on scenery and live performance. In capturing a space or live performance on canvas I focus on relaying the physical and emotional experience of being there and witnessing the subject. Works focusing on spaces tend to reflect more of my personal experiences--grief, wonder, and discovery to name a few. In contrast, my work capturing a performance reflects on both the content of the performance and the viewing experience. I achieve these effects by combining representative depictions of the subject with expressive color, mark making, and even distorting the imagery at times to convey the experience I want to share with the viewer.

column25: Contemporary Watercolor Artist BIOGRAPHY Kerry Hugins is a contemporary watercolour artist based in Austin, TX. Her work explores a variety of themes, including Panpsychism, expanded consciousness, complex emotions, solitude, growing up as a foreign kid, ADHD, motherhood, children, and breaking free from the constraints of the past and future. Hugins’ use of watercolour enables her to create a wide range of textures and shades, from bold and vivid to soft and subtle, as she delves deep into the realms of memory and color. Visually, Hugins’ art is influenced by her experiences living overseas, as well as by colour field painting and contemporary art. Her skillful use of color and form creates a sense of depth and movement within her pieces. Kerry has been involved in the arts her entire life. Her recent work centers around the pursuit of grasping and tracking time, preserving our dark skies, and observing rare wildflowers as they bloom and paint. Essentially, Hugins’ work delves into the realms of memory and color, primarily through the medium of watercolour. Hugins’ passion for exploration is reflected in her life’s journey as well. As the daughter of an American Diplomat, she spent her formative years living in different parts of the world, immersing herself in new cultures and experiences; resulting in her cultural disassociation. Hugins has exhibited in numerous group shows across the United States and completed a residency at El Sur in Mexico City. She earned her BFA from Virginia Commonwealth University in Richmond, Virginia. more links: https://www.instagram.com/kerry_hugins/ https://linktr.ee/karawaane_artist Kerry-Hugins-CV_2023__- Artist Statement My paintings are a portal of color, which remind you of the impermanence of life, a Memento Vivre; “remember to live.” I am Hyperphantasiac, rendering entire worlds in my imagination and have waking dreams. In all three of my series—the grids, the wildflowers, and the newer dark sky paintings—I explore intimacy of time, memory and light, as well as concerns for climate and motherhood. I am neurodivergent and also time blind. I was raised moving around the world, instilling in me a strong cultural disassociation. Because I grew up in different countries, I absorbed multiple cultural identities into my core identity; but as anyone who has ever had a foreign child in class knows, that child never fully assimilates; always the foreigner. I later lived for a year at an Ashram, for my integration of realities. The colors are collected from my life, for example in my painting Agents of Creation, the colors were present in the flower I watched grow and meditated with, and the colors reminded me of pairings I would see in Mexico City in the 80’s as a child. I am a mother in constant motion. Painting in the grid allows me to track time and create that delicate space in between this moment and the next event. Grids are so prevalent in our daily lives, in the apps that we use constantly, our shared information in grids, this format in contemporary. Paintings of wildflowers mostly grown by my hand are my addition to the tradition of portals for devotion, Panpsychism and the exploration fluid time. I always think of the line from Nabokov “flowers reflect the stars” when I sit with them and transmute their journey into a painting. A flower is giving a lesson about the fragility of life. These painting are exploring Critical Plant Studies- we live in the Earth’s atmosphere, which though 26 miles tall, we really live in the space of 6 miles up from the sea level. We are so connected, I want my garden to be useful as possible in becoming a pantry for the migratory and local pollinators and back yard animals. The darkness is important for our mutual sense of well being, the absence of light helps all living things thrive and have access to the magnificent expansive night sky. I have a theory that the expanding universe is the rapidly expanding consciousness on planets all over the universe. Events that happened years ago can be more on my mind than what is going to happen tomorrow. Exploring the existential thoughts around my role a a mother, person and artist using colors to evoke the sensory agregates. My meditations have shown me that my steps I take in making art is my contribution to the greater consciousness we share. I use an alchemy in my work, I use the activism, a proactive relaxation of the used, exploited joys of being alive, I want to celebrate the joy, the memento vivere.

column25: As a representational figurative oil painter marrying mythology with colloquial culture, my work is narrative, creating new stories from the old, life-sized, bold, colorful, and painterly, but also graphic and illustrative. My paintings take over-arching stories that echo through many cultures and fill archetypical roles with modern faces. Though a visual artist, my work is grounded in literature, from the early oral traditions of Homer, through the Greek Lyric poets, and Neoteric poets, to current authors such as A.S. Byatt and Elizabeth Hand. I also find storytelling inspiration in the visual art of the Egyptians, Minoans, and Greeks, Renaissance artists like Michaelangelo, Caravaggio, and Artemisia Gentileschi, later artists like Beardsley, the Secessionists, Frank Miller’s graphic novels, and the Pop Art movement. My work mixes atelia oil painting with graphic elements, low-relief sculptural elements, and literature-based installations. Though I approach oil painting with traditional atelier methods, with under-painting, glazing, and scumbling, my under-paintings are not composed of neutral earth tones but primary and complimentary colors. My final color palette varies from painting to painting as each painting is a new experiment with color and materials such as metal leaf and mica powders. I work both by hand and digitally. When painting, I have a photo session with models, then complete drawings on paper before moving to the canvas. For my sculptural text, I write the text by hand, scan it, and manipulate it in graphic programs before sending it to a fabricator to be cut from wood. I finish the text with spray paint and acrylic. I design the graphic background on the computer and have them printed on vinyl. I also create partial ‘frames’ for the paintings from wood board to physically connect the paintings and the graphics. The installation process is lengthy as all these pieces, painting, relief text, framing, and vinyl graphics must fit together seamlessly on the wall. The vinyl hung like wallpaper; the text affixed to the wall letter by letter. Each framing piece is also attached to the walls individually. My installations envelop the viewer in the experience of a room-sized graphic novel. Graphic and relief elements push the paintings beyond the usual rectangular shape by extending past canvas edges, with text extruding from the wall as 3D objects the whole echoing a graphic novel page but in low 3D relief. Each installation piece also has a QR code adding an audio element.

column25: Bobby W. Baker Born: 1958 - “When I was but a wee guppy, friends would come over and ask, “What do ya wanna do?” I always answered, “Hey, let’s make stuff!” I have never outgrown that. Growing up in the Sierra Nevada mountains of California gives one a profound respect for all the wonder and beauty of nature. I consider myself extremely privileged for the adventures and the freedom we experienced as children. Dad would look out the window, see the sun shining and ask, “What the hell are you kids doing in the house?!” We were set free to roam any part of the mountains we chose (at least until lunch time). Once fed, it was back to adventure in the wild forests that surrounded our modest home. It was the beauty of nature that pulled me into the world of art. From crayon rubbings of leaves in the first grade, to the stick and paper lamps I love to make now, there is always an undercurrent of honoring and paying homage to natural beauty. I want to bring as much wonder, joy, and inspiration to as many people as I can.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work explores the interfaces between photography, ecology, and landscape, both past and present. I seek to examine and complicate the implicit conceptual divide, inherent in Western histories of landscape photography, between the rational viewing subject and the natural world. I am interested in how these conventions have historically mediated understandings of nature and how photography might help reimagine anew a relationship with nature in the current context of ecological crisis. Using appropriated, archival and original photographs of natural environments as points of departure, I reactivate photographic imagery in relationship to other media such as sculptural and sound installation and video. In doing so, I aim to disrupt the traditional neutrality of landscape photography and instead invite viewers to encounter images of nature in terms of the technologies, media platforms, affects, and stories through which we render nature meaningful.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Throughout the history of Western aesthetics there has been a division among the elements of design: drawing, sculpture, and architecture were seen as masculine and the most important of artistic elements and mediums. These masculine elements in art were considered clean, holy, controlled, and rational, while color was seen as feminine and exotic. Color was considered to be dirty, emotional, uncontrollable, and irrational, thus giving the impression through a patriarchal lens, that “color” and elements associated with it must be subdued and controlled. Each painting included in CHROMOPHOBIA portrays a hyper-masculine and violent classical sculpture depicting a death, kidnapping, or rape of a feminine mythological figure. Combined with the sculptures are the color arrangement from classical paintings characterized by stories of women’s rage and retaliation. They depict women repossessing the power that was taken from them repetitively by the common visual narrative of the time. By removing content and context and minimizing them to nothing but the sum of their colors, I am redefining a problematic history of imbalance between the sexes. An imbalance that l continues to this day. Creating the illusion of paint dripping and reflecting over the white marble sculpture gives the effect of dirtying the narrative of the original sculpture in order to entangle the stories of both artworks. Recontextualizing the seemingly disparate works of art, creates new meanings and gives us a way to evaluate contemporary ideas of the sexes through a classical lens. In this way I ask the viewer, has anything truly changed?

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I interpose objects of the everyday to distort our sense of space, explore our ability to inhabit rooms, and merge the dreamlike with the rigid. I think of paintings as they exist in the home, decorating our lives, using us to give them purpose. And inversely, objects become paintings to question abstraction and reality. My work focuses on the acute and the polite, the domestic and the utilitarian. Overarching themes in my studio practice include issues of desire, consumption, and the absurdity of convenience. This takes form in immersive installations filled with both soft and hard wall pieces. Within the installations I abstract everyday objects utilizing materiality and hyper-cohesive color so that they may hover in a space of nostalgia and dystopia. My work combines imagination and points of memory to unpack the history of modernism and minimalism in the middle-class home throughout American history. This question of domesticity comes from a curiosity of the curated home space; how the imperfections of home life can be contrasted with the polish of interior design. I am interested in how domestic aesthetics has been linked to material sensibility and social trends. My research is tied heavily to suburban expansion and the merger of creativity with fabrication, as well as how the development of these aesthetics impacted immigration assimilation and consumer culture. This work aims to further understand domestic design and its relationship to color theory. Homes are meant to be a sanctuary, and yet we fill them with the latest trend. But that latest trend is beautiful and we want it in our lives. So let’s live with it. Let’s look at it and use it.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Art is the most honest thing I can do. I fear (and that is the correct word) that too many people (and peoples) have sacrificed the importance of community at the altar of individualism, rather than seeking out a balance between the two. The result is a social sickness that deteriorates not only society, but the very individuals who are desperately looking for meaning, value, and the safety of their individual proclivities. My goal with my art is to ask the viewer to reassess their worldviews in relation to this balance; to have them wonder what value community is; and to ask themselves not how they can use or exploit community, but how their skills and talents can be used to strengthen community. Concomitantly, my art attempts to reintegrate the Divine into the conversation of individualism and community. Spirituality, religion, and beliefs in the supernatural have been used to support hyperindividualism on the one side, and oppressive communitarianism on the other. This tendency is a reflection of the same social sickness I see around us. In both cases, the search for meaning is too terrestrial to be lasting. Thus my art insists that the viewer consider elements of the numinous, specifically through visual antinomies: time contra timelessness; empirical contra fantastical; organized contra entropy; or ancient contra modern. In short: I want my viewers to be honest with themselves about what's most important to them, and why, but to do so within a specific context: that of a community underpinned by the presence of the Divine. To achieve this, I work with old media (for example, woodcut prints) but use modern approaches (rather than woodcuts, I use acrylic sheets with etchings designed on a CAD program). I also employ a lot of collage elements from a variety of art historical time periods (especially the renaissance and the baroque) and architecture. I focus particularly on integrating 'The Epic of Gilgamesh' in each piece in some way shape or fashion. Doing so reminds the viewer that what I am asking of them is nothing new: the balance between the individual and the community has been discussed and thought on for, literally, ages.

column25: Jon Cooper is a 40-something academic who has worked in higher education for almost 20 years, both as a faculty member (social statistics) and administrator (dean's office). About three summers ago, Jon was sitting at his dining room table when he stopped what he was doing, looked up at his wife, and said, 'You know, I'm only happy when I'm making art.' This was the start of a radical career change that is still in process. Jon's introduction to making art was through table top miniature games, such as Warhammer 40,000, Warmachine, and Runewars. Always an 'army painter', he decided to 'up his game' by purchasing some books on color theory and taking more time and pride in his craft. As his miniature painting improved, he got more and more interested in other art media. This culminated one evening after watching a rerun of The Joy of Painting with Bob Ross (in an effort to assist his then two year old son to fall asleep). Watching what Ross was doing and how he was doing it, Jon quietly said to himself in a fashion worthy of any biopic: 'I could do that.' And he did: ordering a basic 'Bob Ross' brand starter kit, Cooper started recreating the same paintings that Ross was painting. He'd paint in his basement, his phone close to him, pausing between steps. His first efforts were ok, and then slowly became a lot better. Wanting to improve his landscape painting, Cooper purchased a book on oil landscape techniques. In the first paragraph of the introduction, the author noted that good painting required good drawing. A dubious assertion, perhaps, but one that Jon took seriously. Acquiring a copy of the famous 'Drawing on the left side of the brain', he endeavored to teach himself how to draw. Innumerable YouTube videos and workshops later, and Jon had his dining room table epiphany. He enrolled in the Art Studio program at Indiana University of Pennsylvania with a focus on 2D media, while also expanding his toolbox with other skills, most notably 3D fabrication. Additionally, he has found a deep love of art history, from ancient times to the 21st century, across all media and nations. While still maintaining employ as an academic administrator, Jon is full-steam ahead to complete is BA Art Studio degree. As a student, he has taken part in three university exhibitions, and has been selected to display at an invitation only university exhibition based on one of his final projects. He is also set for his first solo exhibition as a student in March, using some of the artworks from this grant's application, among others. Finally, Jon is an active member of the Indiana Arts Association, where he serves as the Membership chair, a board member, and regularly takes part in their exhibitions and fundraisers.

column25: As a Los Angeles-based artist from Seoul, South Korea, I am invested in exploring both the points of connection between disparate cultures, social classes, and histories and how these disparities also have a marginalizing effect on social groups shaped by Western hegemony. My work exploits residues, leftovers, byproducts, imprints, traces, and indentations born of labor, time, memories, and personal and public histories. Integrating these charged materials in a cohesive art piece means providing a physical, collective presence of all we have left behind in our labors, hinting toward our unseen potential and gesturing toward alternate histories and counterfactual realities. My previous work explored the countless social struggles seeping through Korean society, including gentrification, class inequities, and generational conflict. Through the deconstruction and reconstruction of meticulously selected found materials, I examined the sculptural occupation of gallery spaces as a means of commenting on political issues in local communities. In White Wall and White Cube, I created walls and flooring for art exhibitions using abandoned furniture from neighborhoods emptied of their original residents as a consequence of gentrification and massive redevelopment. As the titles ironically imply, the ostensibly “neutral” art spaces were blanketed in materials indelibly marked with the time and memories of the original residents. In doing so, the work questions the evolving role of art in local spaces as communities themselves evolve. My move to the diverse city of Los Angeles inspired an interest in the hierarchy and interconnectivity of race, culture, and class, as well as how these affect the individual experience. Informed by years-long work as a professional fabricator, as well as exhaustive historical research on Asian immigrant labor, the theme of my recent projects revolves around the exploitation of racialized physical labor for the benefit of modern Western society. In Town Square and a series of mask sculptures inspired by Korean traditional mask plays, each piece is made of wood scraps left over from products I created for others as a fabricator. As physical proof of my time and labor, these small and varied scrap pieces—which would otherwise have been discarded and lost to time—gather and hold each other to create something bigger than the sum of their parts. The incense elements of the sculptures are made of hardwood sawdust, also collected from my labors; these are burned with the intent of inviting visitors to inhale the plumes and contemplate the existence—and meaning—of labor in our collective past, and in everyday life. The most recent project, Before The Last Spike, a series of suspended fabric pieces, sheds light on the untold stories of invisible immigrant labor in America. My concern about how physical labor is treated in our society drives the proposed project to delve into two distinct histories: the railway construction that initially imported Asian immigrant laborers to North America; and the garment industry, where Asian immigrant workers currently comprise the majority of the workforce. I sew together scrap fabrics that I collected from Los Angeles garment factories to create an embodied collectivity while retaining their original shapes, textures, and colors. The scraps are rust-dyed with railway spikes, which alludes to the fact that most railway workers were Chinese immigrants and they had driven countless spikes for the Transcontinental Railroad. By drawing connections between historical exploitations spanning different eras and industries, this work challenges the colonial, nationalist narrative of history with the presence of historically and politically charged materials. My artistic goal is to challenge white male-dominant art history and spaces by deconstructing Western visual language and recontextualizing it in conversation with the histories of under-recognized laborers of color. My found materials occupy “neutral” art spaces and help connect myself as both a worker and artist to the larger notion of Asian labor in the context of globalism, colonialism, and capitalism. In doing so, I aim to challenge existing social hierarchies and offer a space for reflection and dialogue.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Interdisciplinary visual artist Reggie Davis Creative Wellness through Art Practice focuses on enhancing equitable environmental wellness through Constructing the Sacred in Space and Place through Art. Anchoring cultural, spiritual values and community-relationships to the land. Sacralization as process Art and a vehicle for spiritual transcendence to facilitate social bonding. Reggie Davis inherited an Artist activism mindset as a child of the 60’s Civil Rights Movement and coinciding Black Arts Movement in the San Francisco Bay Area. Growing up in a community of black activists, artist, and intellectuals. Among sit-ins, marches, pickets and parades. Swallowed up in the outpouring of creative activity of visual artists, writers, poets, musicians. And colored by the designs, hairstyles, fashion, themes, and rhythms of Pan Africanism. Reggie was growing as exponentially as the movements he was embodying. Emerging as a-living testimony of Blackness, and a voice to the many and varied expressions of black creativity. At the same time dealing with racism, bigotry and prejudice. The arts nor suburbia kept us inculcated. They would each leave an indelible mark. On a childhood of otherwise fond memories. Carefree but vigilant. Happy go-lucky, but knowing of de facto segregation.Reminded by generations of ancestry “we’re very American, considering the fact that our ethnicity was born on American soil. Indigenous to this country. We were “created” here and are truly of native soil Engaging in the arts freed Reggie from the constraints placed on his behavior and thinking due to methodical and systematic racism. A creativity and 'revolution' in black thinking started in the 60s evolving with the reality of civil rights. The black consciousness inside him stood up and started walking him boldly into youth and through adulthood. Along the west coast through the 60’s and 70’s. With progressive parents that allowed him to explore his talents. Within a community Incorporating elements of philosophy, sociopolitical activism, and creative integrity into conversations and processes of art-making while forging a black identity around social protest, artistic achievement, unity, freedom of expression. Fundamental artistic expression would center on the amplification of a multitude of voices, reinforced , contradicted, and expanding choreographed paradigms of black life through art. Embracing a wide array of formal approaches, revealing the inherent Blackness of the work. Soon his artistic expression will task social activism through an environmental art lens. That still focused on the Black body politic and wellness; finding home and providing revelation and activation through new media and video art instillations. As Du Bois articulated wellness could not be reconciled within the environment it was created. But needed a space for integrated wholeness. Collective, community and environmental wellness. Which meant confronting environmental racism. A lingering effect of exploitative racism. Reggie approaches through innovative sustainability solutions. By exploration into Afro-indigenous cultural, spiritual knowledge systems and life-ways that under-lie the adaptations and syncretism of these culture groups in creating and sustaining environments through the Idealization of Totemic Art. For Reggie the 70's 80's and 90's was a time of challenges and opportunity. Change and progress. Social problems and strengths. Art was put on hold for a mainstreamed American education. But returned during high school in dance, drama, music, and visual art.University led to majors and degrees in psychology and art history along with minors in studio art and classes in alternative, new media, industrial art and design. In graduate school social work joined more studies in new media arts, especially digital and video lead to an MSW and 17 year career in social work using the arts in social work interventions and community programming. Reggie continues to hone is artist skills through adult education and distant learning at The San Francisco Arts Institute, The College of the Arts in Berkeley, California. And the San Francisco School of Integral Studies. He continues to showcase his multidisciplinary work at various local, national and international exhibitions, galleries, film festivals and publications. Including site specific health and wellness centers.

column25: Through my photography project “Sasha Winchester,” I seek to visually articulate emotional landscapes informed by my lived experience of complex PTSD through the combination of self-portraiture and surreal generative AI. As a survivor of childhood trauma, I have found the camera to be a vital medium of self-exploration and healing. This project represents the convergence of my artistic practice with an inner need to creatively transmute pain into empowerment. I will create a series of diaristic self-portraits using photography which will then undergo generative AI processing to fracture, duplicate, and transform my image. The resulting hybrid works will combine photography and AI to construct dissociative, dreamlike visions of identity and psychology and visually communicate my emotional inner world. Vivid crimson tones and feminine motifs will recur throughout the work as symbolic representations of strength reclaimed, pairing surreal and mundane environments with flashes of legs sheathed in red tights. These bold feminine motifs represent my impulse toward unapologetic self-expression. By publicly sharing these intimate and imaginative self-portraits, my hope is to foster empathy and healing around the universality of trauma.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist, I am fascinated by the concept of psychogeographical mapping and its potential to reveal hidden layers of meaning and emotion in the urban landscape. I explore the connections between people and their surroundings and how our environment shapes our perceptions and experiences. My process typically involves walking and observing as I explore different neighborhoods. By documenting my observations through drawings, photos, and other mediums, I create disaggregated cartographies of the complex undercurrents of our intimate and collective interactions within these spaces. That also includes the nonhuman world: objects, plants, trees, or animals. My coded algorithms take up geographic and collective data based on links with the nonhuman world to reveal unconscious interconnections and build a broader mental architecture of ourselves. I am particularly interested in how psychogeographical mapping can help us to reimagine living spaces as a site of creative potential and transformation. We can begin to imagine new possibilities for the spaces we inhabit and explore new ways of relating to the world around us. Through my art, I hope to contribute to this ongoing exploration and inspire others to engage with the psychogeographical dimensions of their environment.

column25: Philippe Halaburda, born in 1972 in France, is a self-taught abstract painter currently residing and creating in Newburgh, NY, USA. While his formal education includes a degree in Graphic Design from the Academy of Graphic Design and Visual Arts, EDTA SORNAS in Paris, France, his artistic journey truly blossomed beyond the confines of traditional education. Philippe embarked on a transformative exploration of art across Europe, drawing inspiration from the Bauhaus teaching, integrating elements of Constructivist technique, and embracing the ethos of the Situationist movement. His art delves deep into the interplay between urban and natural landscapes, exploring their profound influence on human emotions and daily life, all expressed through vibrant and geometric map compositions. Since 2010, Philippe Halaburda's artwork has graced numerous exhibitions throughout Europe, steadily gaining recognition and becoming cherished additions to both private and public collections. In 2013, his pivotal solo representation by the Peyton Wright Gallery marked his foray into the American art scene, signaling his readiness to expand his creative footprint. During a four-year stint in New York starting in 2015, Philippe's artistic vision flourished, particularly inspired by the intricate grid of Manhattan, which he incorporated into his work. The LionHeart Gallery and Artmora Gallery showcased his solo exhibitions in 2016, further cementing his presence in the contemporary art scene. Since 2018, Philippe has collaborated with various European and American galleries and curators, participating in collective exhibitions that have enriched the discourse around his art. In 2021, he embarked on a new artistic venture by creating colorful art installations, shedding light on a different dimension of his work, one that delves into human psychology. These striking installations have graced prominent art spaces such as the Hudson Valley MOCA in NY, the Garner Art Center in NY, and Terrain Biennial Newburgh, providing viewers with an immersive experience of his evolving artistic exploration.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I visualize my mind as a maze of hallways filled with closed doors. Different thoughts trigger synapses to fire and the doors open as I run through them, trying to keep up. I love working with many different media: painting, sculpture, printing, found object, woodworking and collage. Each theme informs the medium. My painting style changes depending on the subject. I am fascinated by both the natural world and the grit of industry (mostly past but also present) and their different relationships. I have a heavy sense of nostalgia and find excitement in the themes and stories of objects from the past. I am also fascinated by the influence that death has on life. As a carpenter I love constructing things with my hands and I am inspired by the shadowboxes of Joseph Cornell and the assemblages of Robert Rauschenberg. I continue to experiment and learn. I work at improving my painting and drawing techniques and I am always exploring new materials to best express the idea. Just the act of creating helps hit on important themes or inspire new experimentation. I often need to stop overthinking and just Do.

column25: Linsey was born in 1984 in New Jersey. From birth her parents encouraged her to express herself creatively. She remembers going to a pottery class at a young age and how it sparked her love for three-dimensional media. At the age of 8, her family moved to Greensboro, North Carolina. In High School she had the opportunity to take lots of art-focused classes including photography, arts and crafts, and IB Art to help build her portfolio. She continued those studies for another few semesters at a local community college taking art history, illustration, design and drawing until she got into a bachelors program at the School of Visual Arts in New York. She graduated from there in 2007 with a Bachelors Degree in Fine Arts: Sculpture. After college she moved to Burlington, Vermont where she was working at a local silk screen company. She left there a year later to pursue a career in Carpentry. After relocating a number of times over the next 9 years, she finally settled back in Burlington in 2016. That same year she started working for herself as an independent contractor and got an art studio for the first time since leaving college. Since then she has worked to keep up her art practice and show her art in group shows. She regularly shows her work at the S.P.A.C.E. Gallery, where she is also a member. Her bicycle portrait “Blue Betsy” was accepted into the 2019 Art Hop Juried Show. She also had a solo installation at the Hive on Pine at ArtHop 2019 for her giant squid sculpture titled “Fritz”. “Fritz” later hung through the winter in the box office at ArtsRiot, a now-closed restaurant and event venue.

The best thing about being an artist is the calming effect that it has on the spirit of the artist herself which is translated into many things, but most importantly into healing from trauma. In the best selling book called The Body Keeps the Score author Bessel van der Kolk confirms that thousands, perhaps millions, of Americans suffer from the trauma of war, abuse, natural disasters, and more, and his research has proven that art is an important medium in healing from the same. I truly believe that art not only heals the artist, but the viewer as well. I also believe that every human being possesses artistic gifts of some sort, but some have to nurture their gifts more than others. I am convinced that the Divine gave us these gifts and enjoins us to discover them for our own survival.

column25: In THE ARTIST’S WAY, Julia Cameron reminds us that religions say we’re made in the Creator's image and likeness. If this is true, we are all creators and healers. Divine inspiration heals us in the creative process, and we heal others through our creations. People see my art with bold, bright, Afrocentric colors and fabrics and say: “Oh, it’s beautiful!” Some viewers feel a connection to their African heritage, which they were stripped of. Some gain a newfound respect for Afrocentric art, culture, and African descended people. Viewers feel a positive vibration, and thus the healing begins. But healing is not complete without justice. As an elder Black woman born in the Jim Crow South, I feel a strong responsibility to use my artistic voice to advocate for justice for all cultures, but especially for restorative justice for the Black African diaspora. Much visual art reflects the narrative of Black struggle against injustice, but it should also embrace a narrative of Black beauty, healing, resilience, and joy, because we are indeed more than our struggle.

I find the best part about being an artist lies in the sense of freedom it offers. In a world dominated by algorithms and predefined norms, the role of an artist becomes a powerful stance against conformity. It provides a unique opportunity to break free from the shackles of societal expectations and forge one's own path. Before becoming an artist, my life was governed by the rules and terms set by other people. However, at the age of 22, I took a leap of faith and decided to pursue art-making, realizing the immense potential to shape the world around me. This newfound autonomy became a catalyst for self-discovery and empowerment. Furthermore, the act of creating art is a transformative process that harmonizes every fiber of my being. It engages not just my skills, but my entire essence. As an artist, I find a profound unity of self, where my entire body and mind work in unison in the act of creation.

column25: What is the difference between making art and trying to start a revolution? I argue that they can be one and the same. In objection to historic and continued abuses toward Queer peoples, I gather Queer materials and organize them into a growing arsenal of assemblages and constructions, which I call Queer Revolutionary Objects (QROs). The Queerness of the objects and materials in my works comes from my own understanding of “Queerness”. I define Queerness as an attribute that extends past the realms of sexuality and gender identity. To be Queer is to be abandoned, broken, hidden, discarded, and/or confined. Using my experience-based definition, I source Queer materials from a diverse array of origins: plywood from scrap bins, steel from bed frames, spindles from staircases, and fragments of broken mirrors, to name a few. As I work with these materials, I utilize a diverse collection of craft and trade skills, including metalworking, woodworking, sewing, and more. This wide range of skills not only enables me to incorporate a broad spectrum of materials into my work but also empowers me, as a genderqueer individual, to challenge the gendered binaries entrenched within crafts and trades. During the Holocaust, the Nazis used a system of colored triangles to codify people they saw as inferior; Pink and Black were assigned to Queer people. Building off the work of Holocaust survivors and AIDS activists who symbolically reclaimed the Pink and Black triangles, I claim all triangles for Queer people. While using pyramids, tripods, and other triangular forms as the base structures for my QROs, I exclaim triangles to be a counterpoint to the binary. Triangles are easily recognizable despite their differences and are extremely strong, balanced, and withstanding. I see triangles as the shape that will bring forward a Queer utopia. In the vein of bringing forward a Queer utopia, all my QROs are nomadic. All my larger works are easily assembled and disassembled, often using only a handful of bolts. I use this design feature intentionally not only as it relates to the military-like mobilization needed for battlefield structures, but also as a reference to the Queer experience of relocating in search of acceptance, love, or to escape violence. The titling of my works often references Queer and revolutionary histories, cultures, and aesthetics. My work Make Love to Me, Tovarisch uses the Russian word for ‘comrade’ as a reference to the Russian Constructivists, a group of artists heavily involved in the Russian Revolution. Other works reference figures like Sappho of Lesbos and Disco hits like Boogie Wonderland.

I am inspired by my experiences as a Jewish, Indigenous (Blackfeet Nation), neurodivergent (ADHD), and queer woman as I examine dystopia, delight, and delusion in society. My artwork reflects the experiences of being othered. In my work, I critique the mundane, the morose, and the magnificently mad. Within my practice, I use a bit of dada, surrealism, and satire to engage my audience and play with color and form. I like to experiment with art. Painting, collage, and mixed media allow me to delve into a world where familiarity and the unknown merge. Art is more than a means of expression for me- I use my creations to heal, connect with others, and grow. My Indigenous roots help to ground me as I explore the elements through art. Overall, I hope to teach my audience to think about the world differently, challenge societal norms, and inspire a greater social consciousness.
