ARTIST STATEMENT:
The practice of mark making translating the remarkable unfolding of contemporary events grips me artistically, Even as I have dedicated over a decade making images of figurative monuments, I still love translating their current fate onto a blank sheet of paper. The other wonder is that art is so open that if a thread enters my awareness such as the remarkable vignettes of monuments that had stood for generations succumbing to the fury of people who before would have ignored them, I have been exploring monuments as tools for the erasure of public and private memory. Now monuments have been erased in large numbers. As the dust settles, the aftermath of monument removal has led to many places where monuments were raised to elevate some memories and efface many others, having the monuments that occupied those spaces suddenly having vanished leaves gaping holes in collective memory that as Derrida states where traces of erased past haunts the present.

column25: Shortly after my father’s death in 2011, I began creating plein-air drawings of figurative public monuments nearby my long-term home in Brooklyn, New York, principally at Green-Wood cemetery. Coming across a forgotten monument raised during NYC’s Gilded Age past and descending into ruin, I would draw it in constantly shifting light framed by Green-Wood’s lush environs. I named the series the Anna Pierrepont Series after a grand dame of Gilded Age Brooklyn whose gothic filigreed sarcophagus stands at the summit of a hillock in Green-Wood surrounded by the modest graves of familial minions. Anna’s ambition to remain relevant impeded by the inevitability of ruin. As I wandered Green-Wood’s pathways, I stumbled upon the site of a revolutionary war military disaster, its memory buried under a towering monument to civil war glory raised on its barely acknowledged killing fields. Memory erasure, both personal and collective, thus became the principal focus of the Anna Pierrepont Series incorporated into pictorial essays that were widely published. A radical shift in my practice towards studio works on paper corresponded with others recognizing monuments as elemental to the manipulation of memory and identity. In recent years, monuments have become palimpsests for popular discontent and removal. My studio works document in found gestures and color, formally pristine monumental surfaces embroidered by contemporary Luthers with outpowering of rages applied with paint, pen and hammers. Years spent translating the tracery of ruin on Green-Wood’s forgotten figures has led to studio works documenting this moment’s extraordinary reckoning. As the dust has been settling, my most recent works explore how the past is resisting erasure and is haunting the present.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My name is Rekha Krishnamurthi and I am a mixed media printmaker and fiber artist on a colorful journey of creative exploration. My artworks are a vivid tableau of texture, bold colors, and intricate patterns inspired by my South Asian heritage, my global wanderings, cherished childhood memories, and the beauty I observe in the ordinary. For me, art isn't merely about crafting artifacts; it's a mission to encourage creative thinking, stoke curiosity, and stimulate conversation. Having a background in Children's Book Illustration and Textile Design allows me to create artworks full of whimsy and eclectic compositions. I believe in constant experimentation and creative play in my work, which is evident in the diverse techniques and styles that I like to use. The process of crafting a new artwork feels like solving a jigsaw puzzle to me. I search for the ideal artistic elements and gather pieces that seamlessly fit together, allowing the magic of creation to take shape. I create a visual symphony of depth, texture, and detailed images using a variety of materials such as acrylic paints, watercolors, graphite, and numerous layering processes. Even when translating my original artwork into digital mediums, the enchantment persists, resulting in innovative digital artworks. Through my work, I aspire to kindle wonder and curiosity in others and I wish to urge them to embark on their unique journeys of creative exploration.

column25: When painting, I am committed to challenging myself. I have not been painting my whole life, so starting this way of expression was difficult in itself. I was nervous to take my first course in the matter, knowing that at some point I would be disappointed. I struggle with having confidence in my work and calling myself an artist because I view that as an earned title, unsure of whether I give it to myself or wait for someone else to. However, over the years, I’ve become accustomed to loving the feeling of not knowing what lies ahead. I do not know where I will be in 5 years, or where I will even be tomorrow. Art dares me to live uncomfortably, and it’s that unsettling feeling of the unknown that excites me. In my work, I strive to create other-wordly scenes based on human interactions. I have recently been intrigued by interpersonal relationships, whether that is between humans or animals. I take in emotions that I see shared between others, and do my best to recreate a sense of that through painting. While a lot of my work is inspired by strangers, several paintings come from my own familial life. My more personal work stems from how people who love each other permit themselves to be mistreated, evidently passing trauma to the next generation. I’ve grown up with a loving family, but they have never been the type to confront negative emotions or accept traumatic experiences. Rather than keeping past experiences inside and eventually mistreating others, I paint. Painting gives me a space to myself, somewhere that I can release anything from my mind that I choose. There are no rules to how or what I have to paint, so I’m given the freedom to say what I need to. I enjoy creating abstract areas on the canvas that play with figures and finding ways to mesh imagined areas with the real. Within these areas, I have started to relish the feeling of created surfaces. Putting materials like sand or soil into paint transforms the medium into a mixture of natural and unnatural, emphasizing the content often seen in my paintings. Exploring physically rough textures like this has helped me appeal to heaviness in areas that I see fit. In the future, I hope to teach studio art classes. My professors at Michigan State University have transformed me in ways I never would have imagined, and I hope to do the same for others. While teaching technical aspects of painting, I am also determined to open my arms to other artists and show them that they belong anywhere they wish.

column25: In my work I research material evidence of the power dynamics embedded in hierarchies of institutional oppression and the histories that coincide with their existence in a democratic republic system of governance. As a formerly incarcerated individual, I explore how my experiences are reflective of our society by creating meticulous sculptures created from wood and silicone that are a result of the exploration of how perception, materiality, and process may question and reflect upon the psychological structures inherent in the design of societal mechanisms and their consequences on the human body and psyche. I use skill and craft intensive exercises that evoke a physicality onto a material and close attention is paid to the nuances of the forms to evoke emotive responses to the aesthetic qualities to place the facticity of the object into crisis through processes of subtle abstractions and material transformation. I also employ processes of carving, laminating, steaming, bending, sanding, turning, and polishing wood as means to create a visual rendering of both labor economies and time.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Growing up in Australia, surrounded by a vast “bush landscape,” I witnessed how open spaces could be both freeing and confining, echoing the challenges found in bustling cities. As a sculptor deeply immersed in my artistic journey, I explore the intricate dance of reflection and change, anchored in the intimate connection with materials that give life to my sculptures. My art dives into the concept of landscapes — not just the obvious city or nature setting — but also as the subtle architecture that molds our emotions and perceptions. My sculptures act as visual stories, inviting viewers to explore diverse human experiences within these landscapes. While abstraction has long guided my artistic expression, my evolving work introduces figurative elements, forming a nuanced emotional discourse. From small personal works to large commissions, my sculptures vary in scale, employing materials like marble, cast metals, porcelain/ceramics, and wood. This distinct palette weaves simplicity and complexity, connecting viewers to the essence of how we visualize and experience the landscape.

column25: I have an insatiable drive to understand the cyclical relationship between human beings and the social constructs we build that in turn, govern our behavior. My process always begins with photography but often expands into mixed-media installations that include narration. The use of mixed-media in my work is a reflection of the way I see the contemporary human condition as an attempt to thrive within a human-constructed network of social systems that seem to be void of humanity. I employ storytelling with my visual art to process these sometimes-disparate materials. Through my photography-centered, mixed-media practice, I make visible the physical, psychogenic, and material interchanges between social institutions and personhood. Reflections on standardized time, a human invention, serves as a foundational component in my work because of its role in managing social institutions.

column25: Peñafiel's works unravel the grim tapestry of our world—a chaotic realm entrenched in the cyclical grip of history and politics. His immersive installations serve as a raw mirror reflecting the relentless struggles faced by the marginalized and downtrodden, revealing the insidious workings of prevailing power structures. Across a diverse array of mediums, from sculpture to animation, Peñafiel crafts visceral experiences that thrust viewers into a disconcerting confrontation with the unsettling paradoxes of contemporary existence. Through a fusion of projection and physicality, he blurs the line between reality and illusion, forcing audiences to grapple with the tangible consequences of our shared reality. Drawing from the disquieting aesthetics of German Expressionism and the omnipresent gaze of surveillance, Peñafiel's visual language captures the pervasive anxieties that haunt our society. Distorted lines and disorienting angles embody the oppressive forces of bureaucracy, the ruthless control over human movement, and the dehumanizing rituals of labor. Peñafiel's installations transcend passive observation, becoming instruments of disruption that amplify the silenced voices of the marginalized. They urge viewers to challenge established norms, embrace new perspectives, and engage in a visceral dialogue about the intricate web of human experiences ensnared in a perpetual loop of history and power.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am Ver IKESEH, a seasoned visual artist with a decade of immersive artistic practice, I hold a Bachelor of Art (BA) in Fine Arts from Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria, Nigeria, and a Research Master of Art (ResMA) from Leiden University, Netherlands, where excellence granted me the prestigious Leiden Excellence Scholarship (LExS). My ResMA journey provided invaluable insights into ethnographic and holistic research, shaping my artistic perspective. The genesis of the groundbreaking '920 Project' emerged from my desire to extend the impact of my art beyond canvas. Inspired by a transformative TED Talk featuring artists who, while documenting the Favelas in Brazil, found themselves painting murals, my project initially aimed at adorning and donating murals to 920 communities across Africa. The positive ripple effect witnessed in Brazil spurred me to extend this initiative to the United States, notably in Harrisonburg, Virginia. Venturing into the heart of social engagement on the James Madison University (JMU) campus, I now paint murals in student resident halls. My medium, a fusion of acrylics, brushes, print wax, and newsprint, breathes life into wall murals and collages. Drawing inspiration from my surroundings and personal experiences, my creations forge a profound connection to my identity, roots, and the contemporary environment, fostering relatability among viewers. Beyond the vibrant strokes of my art, my commitment to community impact surfaces in the '920 Project,' where mural painting sessions double as opportunities to tutor children, youths, and students in color theory and mural painting techniques. This dual-purpose initiative not only enhances artistic skills but also nurtures the next generation into becoming advocates for social change, instilling the ethos of paying it forward. At the nexus of my artistic evolution is my current MFA research at James Madison University, where he utilizes the metaphor of 'Family' to explore themes of 'Identity, Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion.' Recognizing the family as the foundational institution, I delve into its symbolic richness, encompassing diversity in gender, age, race, complexion, size, language, culture, and beliefs. Despite the inherent potential for inclusivity within families, I observe a stark contrast in many instances. The profound message embedded in my art resonates with the belief that a more inclusive space within diversity can engender unity. Through the lens of 'Identity, Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion,' my work becomes a continuous dialogue on a global scale. As an artist traversing international studies, residencies, local practices, and adventurous tourism, I find myself oscillating between the 'Native' and 'Alien' contexts. This dynamic interplay between familiar and unfamiliar terrains not only defines my artistic journey but reflects a universal reality, where what may seem 'Native' in one context becomes 'Alien' in another. My art transcends the canvas; it is a testament to the transformative power of creative expression and the potential for art to be a catalyst for social change. In every stroke, mural, and project, I weave a narrative that extends beyond aesthetics, inviting viewers to contemplate the intricacies of identity, diversity, equity, and inclusion in our shared human experience.

Ver's murals, funded by a blend of commissions, crowdfunding, angel investors, and seed capital, have breathed life and color into dilapidated walls, sparking inspiration in the hearts of both young and old. Through this project, he not only adds vibrancy to forgotten spaces but also instills a sense of creativity, encouraging individuals to reach out to humanity through their own unique expressions. Currently pursuing his MFA at James Madison University, Ver's research transcends traditional boundaries. He is on a mission to paint and donate murals across Harrisonburg and other parts of Virginia, fostering positive social engagement. Ver Ikeseh stands as a testament to the transformative power of art, proving that a single brushstroke can create ripples of change across communities, leaving an indelible mark on the canvas of humanity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I rely solely on the impact of the dance between light and shadows. Without their final display, my world, thus my art, would be flat. Taking note of how each light change can accentuate different body areas has become my favorite pastime because, with each change, a different story develops. Drawing inspiration from the very being that I am, the female form is an art form to be celebrated. As an artist, I am drawn to the profound beauty and infinite complexity of the female body. Through my work, I strive to celebrate and promote body positivity by challenging societal norms and embracing the diverse forms that women embody. The female body, in all its shapes and sizes, serves as a powerful canvas for expressing the richness of human experience and emotion. To see my art is to see the battle between feminine and masculine energies, light and dark. I am learning the differences between a submissive and dominant core and how they often can battle within a female, within me. I paint to invoke a feeling of familiarity, power, and freedom. Each piece I create uses symbolism, whether in color choice, object placement, or form development, to give a new voice to the battles within. All of my work is heavily influenced by my history with religion, overshadowed by my exploration of my sensuality and my overall love for taking a blank canvas and turning it into something worth talking about.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
For me, the best thing about being an artist comes from having the freedom to creative expression in order to heal, empower, and connect. Through exploring vulnerabilities and triumphs within myself and communities I belong to, then giving them shape and color, a sense of wholeness emerges within me as an artist. Having ended an 8 year long abusive relationship, I was able to release pent up emotions through art. What began as a personal journey to process trauma through creating art shifted into a calling to spark that same inner light for others. So now when I channel emotional experiences into symbolic portraits or abstracted scenes, it feels like divine energy moving through me. I bear witness to lived stories and paint visual stories that resonate on spiritual levels. That an artwork crafted from my once-broken pieces could resonate with a stranger mending their own...this is the restorative magic exclusive to the artistic practice.

My figurative work is a testament to the resiliency of the human spirit, an ode to the endurance that defines Black experiences across generations. Through rich hues, textures, and detailed linework, you'll find a celebration of my Afro-Indigenous heritage, interwoven with intricate aboriginal patterns and vibrant flora that holds the stories of my ancestors. This mesh of colors and textures mirror the interplay of emotions and experiences within the collective consciousness of the Black community. Through acrylic paint and natural minerals, I transcend the tangible, using continuous contour line and pointillism techniques to capture a healing journey. My artwork focuses on healing, empowering, and reconnecting Black communities to their roots and to each other. By highlighting the importance of nurturing Black psychological, spiritual, and emotional well-being, I invite viewers to connect to my work on a personal and spiritual level.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
John Gintoff Artist Statement These five images that I have submitted are part of an ongoing exploration that I have been conducting for the past two years. Collage has always been an important part of my work, and two years ago I decided to shake up these collages of found and original photographic images by drawing over them with colored marks using a iphoto tool. Originally the marks were randomly drawn over the collages employing different colors, widths, opacities and transparencies responding to the shapes beneath them. After dabbling with this procedure for a bit, I decided to do a series of images which were loosely based on A CLOCKWORK ORANGE. In these images I decided that the title of the image which had been been predetermined would be written over the image using the above technique. In this way the image’s title would become an actual part of the image. The only novelty to this technique was that each letter of the title was actually based on a Russian word using the Cyrillic Alphabet. So what I am presenting to you are photo collages with their titles emblazoned over them in different colors, transparencies, techniques, fonts,etc but in Russian. Not only are you seeing my photographs but also the title of the image as part of the image.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My large-scale expressive canvases portray the evolving environment of the woods and fields of my native rural Rhode Island, particularly in areas where the forests of New England meet the open Atlantic Ocean. I capture a moment of time, a memory, or the sensation of a particular place in Nature. I’m compelled to continue a body of work creating lyrical abstractions of this changing natural world. My fluid and spontaneous brush strokes form the organic shapes of my shifting surroundings. At the same time, words from New England poets and authors such as Emily Dickinson and Nathaniel Hawthorne often emerge from their enclosures between many delicate layers of paint, like palimpsests, as if rising from the mists of the rustic landscapes where they were once written. I have also created environmental sculptures using translucent materials and lights that are similar to my paintings in that I utilize Xeroxed images of faded photos of people and places from long ago that mysteriously appear as if they have fallen through time from an old photo album. Each painting begins with color and natural forms as the layers spontaneously evolve into a composition that becomes its own world. My color palette often has subdued soft blues, greys, and other pastel tones that convey my connection with woodlands, sea, and sky. My smaller-scale pastel works on paper depict elegiac images of ghostly wild flora that haunt coal-black backgrounds, while others offer the more vibrant and enchanting hues of Nature. My works often evoke remembrances with a subtly portentous feeling that echoes the temporality and impermanence of Nature.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
li rothrock’s work plumbs how human divisions of resources, such as water and land, favor human communities over – and often at the irreversible expense of – plant and animal communities. She gathers impressions of water, powerlines, plastics, plants, and earth to examine the collisions that occur when humans relentlessly take, and to ask if we really benefit when we make decisions that favor us alone. The cyanotype medium holds latent poetic meaning when turned to these explorations, as it has historically been used both to document the fauna of the natural world and for urban planning. The weavings express the entanglement of humans with more-than-humans. She makes neat squares to represent the neat stories humans tell ourselves about how we interact with the earth, and she pulls these weavings apart to remind us that our interactions with nature are inevitably more fraught than we anticipate or understand.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I begin with how a particular material or object engages me. For many years, I selected a heavy weave canvas to paint on as it’s materiality left no question that I was painting on an object rather than seeking out illusionism. I began to work with sea worn sails to push this idea further. Sails are designed to catch the wind and their primary purpose is to move a boat from one place to another. Their imbedded Kevlar, graphite and carbon fiber lines create a built in system for exploration. I received a 2023 Ora Schneider Regional Residency Grant for four weeks in the silkscreen studio at Women’s Studio Workshop in Rosendale, NY. I created a series of screen prints based on collages made from various types of sail material. The imagery developed organically as I experimented with transitioning to the screen printing medium from my abstract collages and paintings. The prints brought me in unexpected directions, creating imagery that references the cityscapes of my home, Poughkeepsie NY and a summer trip to Paris, with its streets filled with people. I feel like a magpie, empowered to use magical materials—paper ephemera, luminescent paint, boat sails; collecting, ordering, and imbuing materials with the content of WHO made them and with WHAT. Through the process of making, I succumb to that particular piece’s insistence of having its own architecture and story.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I enter my practice with a deep curiosity about craft, labor, and materiality. Working in the space between the provisional and the durational, I merge found utilitarian items, synthetic construction materials, and handwoven cloth as sincere homages to the vast spectrum of labor and making. I think about the plywood countertops, compound bucket basketball hoop, and other improvised solutions fabricated by my father in our home. I look to the lineages of processed wood, handwoven cloth, and other materials intertwined with manual labor as both functional assets and cultural anchors for understanding ingrained values of productivity, utility, and the middle-class American dream. I’m interested in the assumptions of function and authenticity within object categories, and how the expectations we place on objects directly reflect the expectations we place on ourselves and others. My work deliberately undermines and dismantles these expectations, attempting to reconcile shame and embrace the expansiveness of hybridity. Throughout my work, handwoven cloth emerges as a series of failed facsimiles, echoes of everyday objects and materials. I manipulate colonial overshot drafts by hand, weaving cloth that mimics the knots and curves of natural wood grain. A screen print of a ubiquitous low-brow box fan adorns natural fiber warps prior to weaving, leaving distorted acrylic imprints. Blue polyethylene tarps are intricately unwoven, row by row, only to be re-woven into a cotton warp, grommeted with the outline of a Lisa Frank butterfly, and suspended by livestock hardware. Absurd labor intensity alongside swift, provisional gestures in attempts at imitation reflect on lived experience and connect to larger themes of intersex girlhood, working-class aesthetics and speculative objects. Through creating hybrids that occupy the space between cloth and wood, material and object, I subvert expectations of authenticity and functionality and celebrate failed attempts at achieving the ‘real thing.’

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Qrcky is an artist who passionately believes that art is not just a luxury but an essential element of our existence. With a deep understanding of its power to chronicle history, enlighten minds, and preserve knowledge, Qrcky embraces art as a crucial component of our cultural heritage that should be accessible to all. Driven by a profound purpose, Qrcky's artistic creations aim to safeguard the historical narratives of generations often underrepresented in traditional art forms. By forging their identity and storytelling through creative expression, Qrcky aspires to inspire others to share their unique experiences and collaboratively shape a brighter future for humanity. At the core of Qrcky's art lies a fundamental theme: the unification of communities. Recognizing art's remarkable ability to foster connection and facilitate meaningful conversations about history and its enduring impact, Qrcky's creations serve as spaces where individuals can converge, exchange their stories, and discover common ground. Through diverse mediums and artistic techniques, Qrcky's works create a visual language that transcends barriers and invites viewers to engage in thought-provoking dialogue. Whether through vibrant brushstrokes, intricate sculptures, or immersive installations, Qrcky's art catalyzes introspection, encouraging viewers to reflect on their place in the world and connection to others. Qrcky's commitment to inclusivity and social progress extends beyond the studio. Collaborating with community organizations and art-based initiatives, they actively seek to use art for positive change. By engaging in conversations that challenge the status quo and promote understanding and unity, Qrcky strives to contribute to a more equitable and empathetic society. As an artist, Qrcky stands firmly rooted in their belief that art should be accessible to all, transcending boundaries and giving voice to those whose stories deserve to be heard. With each artistic creation, Qrcky invites viewers on a journey of exploration, empathy, and collective empowerment.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Theo began to paint as a means of mental coping and creative release during the COVID-19 pandemic while finishing his second year at Loyola Marymount University (LMU) over Zoom. The ensuing year of online school cemented his detachment from pursuing conventional corporate career paths, and instead left him determined to lend himself to more fulfilling, passion-driven work. During the quarantine period of the pandemic, Theo spent most days working at a local wholesale bakery alongside three older Salvadoran women, who were his primary friends in the lockdown bubble. They shared with him stories of life back home, the terrors of a decades-long civil war, and their treacherous journeys into the United States. Later on, after graduating from LMU in May of 2022, Theo worked as a farmhand at ALMA, a nonprofit urban farm in Compton focused on rehabilitating formerly incarcerated individuals by way of urban agriculture. Working with these individuals, he gained invaluable perspectives on how the criminal justice system takes its heaviest toll on LA’s marginalized communities. Shortly after, Theo embarked on a 3-month long solo backpacking trip through Mexico and El Salvador. The trip opened his eyes to the vast beauty and cultural richness of the region and Latino communities, as well as the false narratives Western media subjugates them to. While these experiences vastly differ from one another, they each represent the lived experiences that inform his artistic voice, and drive him to create.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Approaching my artistic practice as both a creative and a healthcare practitioner, I investigate the personal, cultural, and historical traumas that burden the mental wellness of Black communities today. Simultaneously, I explore the complex realities of one particular symbol, the pineapple. Immersed in a world of colorful patterns and imagery, I access a collective consciousness of cultural memory that seeks to acknowledge and heal the traumas of the Black psyche while assessing a less examined narrative that positions the pineapple as a global commodity that fueled colonialism and the movement of millions of African and Indigenous. My work becomes an investigation of race based traumatic stress induced by the state of racial disparities and unethical health practices. Art and symbolism become an approachable means for individuals to confront the experiences, memories, and histories that challenge their well-being. My works of art span across disciplines that spark new dialogues. Acrylic painting with mixed media and collage adds textures, patterns, and objects that enhance the underlying narratives of trauma, memory, and reconciliation. Packed with imagery, these works, like our own minds and memories, take time to dissect. Each facet of the work is meticulously incorporated to tell a story of the past, reimagine our present, or even share a speculative vision of the future. As my practice evolves, I engage with new mediums, including found object alters and plaster sculptures, as well as community activation that borders on the realms of social practice to encourage individuals to discover avenues of healing. The Pineapple Metaphor takes viewers on a journey from pain into purpose, and the pineapples are a healing device appearing where you least expect them. A symbol of hope: it presents viewers with pain and its root causes, but also with the antidote. While aimed to provide an outlet for the Black community to find peace, acceptance, and solace in the face of history repeating itself, these messages can speak to broader diverse audiences. In instigating these difficult discussions, I aspire to change antiquated perspectives, make meaningful changes within our communities, and change our trajectory as a society for a better future.

column25: April Fitzpatrick, a licensed Mental Health Therapist and Board-Certified Art Therapist, centers her work at the intersection of mental health, racial healing, and community engagement. Born in Jackson, MS, April's journey through the realm of art is a testament to her passion for promoting health equity and sparking transformative conversations within communities. April earned her Master of Science in Art Therapy from Florida State University in 2017, following her Bachelor of Arts in Psychology from Tougaloo College in 2012. These academic foundations laid the groundwork for her distinctive approach to blending psychology, art, and community engagement. Her art is a central tool as she works to expand Pineapples with Purpose, a mobile mental health service that introduces all things art to communities impacted by race-based traumatic stress. As an Artist and Culture of Health Leadership Institute for Racial Healing Fellow, she has been at the forefront of developing and implementing innovative art-based workshops that foster healing and dialogue around racial healing and relationship building. Her collaborative efforts with fellow researchers have contributed to a nuanced discourse on RBTS, reflecting her dedication to advancing health equity through artistic means. Distinguished as a Global Arts in Medicine Fellow in 2021, April has showcased a commitment to the intersection of arts and healthcare on a global scale. Her artistic prowess has earned her prestigious awards such as the 2022 Baldwin for the Arts recognition in Brewster, NY, and first place in the 'Hindsight is 2020' competition in 2020. April's solo exhibitions, including 'Digital Grace' at the Ely Center of Contemporary Art, New Haven, CT (2021) and 'The Pineapple Metaphor' at the Tallahassee International Airport, Tallahassee, FL (2022) served as catalyst to examine the core of racial divisions impact on health. April's influence extends to public spaces through her impactful public art installations. Notable projects include 'Lamp to my Feet, Light to my Path' and 'Telescopic Vision,' part of the City of Tallahassee Artful Infrastructure Program in 2021. April continues to see her artistic practice as a process; she is currently working on a new series at the Ferrette House, an artist in resident program in South Carolina. Her journey has been marked by accolades, impactful projects, and a relentless commitment to the transformative power of art in mental health and community well-being, however; April is not merely an artist and therapist; she is a catalyst for change, leveraging her creative prowess to break barriers, foster healing, and create a more inclusive world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Inspired by scientific insights into the complex physical forces that shape landscapes, I explore the outdoors with my camera. I am attracted to uncommonly visited places, from remote Antarctic and Arctic wilderness to the overgrown banks of an urban stream, looking for the visual traces that reveal how natural and manmade forces have shaped the space. My previous major project, came out of a residency in Antarctica through the National Science Foundation Antarctic Artists and Writers Program, which resulted in the traveling solo exhibition Walking in Antarctica. 33 photographs, four sculptures and an audio tour are touring US museums from 2022 to 2027 under the auspices of the Mid-America Arts Alliance ExhibitsUSA exhibition rental service. I am currently working on a photo book project exploring the remote Arctic town of Kangerlussuaq, Greenland, a former indigenous hunting ground that has been reshaped by its highly unusual geopolitical history and the impact of climate change. This isolated town of 500 people at the end of a 118-mile-long fjord was founded as a US air base that operated 1941-92. Repurposed and abandoned buildings and obsolete machinery remain, as do thousands of musk oxen descended from 27 imported as a scientific experiment in 1962. In 1999, German automakers built a road to the ice cap covering 80% of Greenland for a short-lived failed project to test drive cars on ice. The effects of climate change are starkly visible at the road's end, formerly the ice cap edge, now a long gravel slope uncovered by melting ice. The book will explore the past 80 years of the impact of human interventions in the environment: military, corporate, scientific, and global climate change. Compelling visual images bring home the seriousness of the climate change problem and the urgency of the entire world confronting it now. My project also illuminates a little-known corner of American and Danish history related to their presence in the region and its lasting impact, as Greenlanders aspires to a postcolonial future independent of the Danish governing authority. I strive to convey the wonder and complexity of the natural world to others, hoping to motivate viewers to explore their environment and protect wild places. My study of earth science over the past several years heightened my awareness of the multiple factors shaping the land over time. In recognizing that complex patterns in nature express the physical forces at work, I became more attuned to the interplay between geology, climate, life forms, and human activity in a particular location. That awareness informs how I select and compose my photographs. I search out surprising incidents that convey nature's inexhaustible variety, which I may underscore with vantage points that foster spatial ambiguities and focus attention on details that typically escape notice. Some of the photographs are produced as archival prints. I also make sculptures of landscape forms generated from a series of still photographs taken on site and reconstructed as 3D scans by photogrammetry software. After further editing in 3D modeling software, the resulting digital files become the basis for hand-painted sculptures made with digital fabrication technologies — CNC routers or 3D printers. They provide the viewer a visceral experience of form and texture that differs from the flat image and fixed point of view delivered by a photographic print.

column25: My work in photography and photogrammetry-based sculpture of geology is informed by insights from the sciences and humanities into the physical and cultural forces affecting ecosystems and shaping landscapes. My thinking has been significantly shaped by interacting with researchers as Baltimore Ecosystem Study artist-in-residence (2014-15) and as a grantee of the National Science Foundation Antarctic Artists and Writers Program in 2015. Even earlier there is an accumulation of experiences that inform my current project about Kangerlussuaq, Greenland. I graduated Yale University with honors; I also spent a summer studying at the Skowhegan School of Art. In the classes outside my art major, I focused on understanding the various dimensions of communication and perception — written, psychological, social, and cultural. That curiosity about different systems of knowledge and an interest in connecting art to other areas of human inquiry have remained with me and motivate my current project. Although I had taken black-and-white film photography courses at Yale and have been a photo-based artist for the past 15 years, in the interim I worked in other art media. Shortly after starting an MFA painting program at Maryland Institute College of Art in Baltimore, a painting of mine was chosen by artist Nancy Graves for the Maryland Biennial at the Baltimore Museum of Art. After graduating, I remained in Baltimore and have regularly exhibited my work ever since. Throughout my career I have supported myself with part-time and freelance jobs in the arts that both afforded me time to pursue my own artwork and offered opportunities to develop related interests and skills. Notably, the 12 years I spent in a half-time position as exhibitions director at Goucher College gave me curatorial and project management experience and the opportunity to interact with a wide range of regional artists and develop research, communication and presentation skills including writing an essay about the artists' processes and intentions to accompany each show. I also completed a Fellowship in Museum Studies at the Smithsonian Office of Museum Programs (1994-95). Under the mentorship of the Director of Institutional Research, I developed a prototype self-guided tour of nine Smithsonian museums on and near the National Mall on the theme of mythology and tested it with visitors. After receiving my MFA in 1978, I produced paintings and pastel drawings for the next 18 years. I also completed two public art commissions exploring how different cultures have projected recognizable forms onto the abstraction that is the nighttime sky. Two Bears and Variations (1993), is a 28' x 60' exterior mural that I painted in Southwest Baltimore, commissioned by the Mayor's Commission on Art and Culture. Touch the Stars (1997) is a 9' x 24' x 8' mural of 10 painted relief panels commissioned by the Baltimore City Department of Education. It was my first venture into relief sculpture, designed to be accessible to the 10% of the student body in a program for blind children. Continuing to explore the possibilities of painted relief sculpture, I produced a series of biomorphic painted sculptural reliefs based on my drawings and photographs of plant and aquatic forms. Two such reliefs were exhibited at the American Embassy in Peru through the State Department Art-in-Embassies Program in 2010-13. My curiosity about the natural processes that give rise to such forms led me to read about the scientific principles underlying patterns of growth in nature, including chaos and complexity theories. These theories influenced me to look at landscape as a dynamic system, and to think about any given form in nature as representing a temporary condition on a continuum between order and disorder, structure and disintegration. While such systems proceed according to general rules, they are never entirely predictable owing to a myriad of subtle influences. Given this trend in my thinking, I became increasingly drawn to the three-dimensional forms created by the movements of wind and water. I studied sourcebooks of photographs of experiments in fluid dynamics, which taught me to recognize similar structures in nature. I started to understand natural formations differently, recognizing rhythms and patterns — albeit complex ones — in what at first appears random. These scientific concepts so enriched my perception of the natural world that they have had a profound impact on my art. Photography re-emerged as my primary medium, to capture and communicate the subtleties of complex natural forms that are impractical to model or draw by hand. I saw expressive possibilities in digital photography and began exploring how editing in Photoshop could bring out the latent sensor information embedded in RAW files. I produced a large series of photographic prints of cloud formations exploring the complex forms that interested me, adding subtle hand coloring with pastel pencils as a way of emphasizing the patterns and spatial dimensions that science had taught me to recognize. My hand-colored photographs of clouds have been exhibited in a solo show at the Nailya Alexander Gallery, New York City (2012), and in other solo and group shows, including the Delaware Art Museum and New York Hall of Science. I received an Individual Artist Award in photography from the Maryland State Arts Council for this work in 2012. By 2013, the field of digitally aided 3D design had evolved to the point where a new technology called photogrammetry enabled me to translate a series of still photographs of an object directly into 3D files. After much perseverance in working out technical issues I developed a workflow to translate photographs of large and complex landscape forms into sculptures, even massive Antarctic glaciers. Some of these are in Walking in Antarctica traveling exhibition. Two projects within the past nine years paved the way for my current project in Greenland. As the 2014-15 Baltimore Ecosystem Study (BES) artist-in-residence I embedded with a long-term ecological research study that has facilitated pioneering research on urban ecology, which considers the interaction between the natural world and human activity and infrastructure. Accompanying scientists into the field opened my eyes to obscure sites along urban streams and back alleys that few Baltimoreans ever see. Viewing these sites through the researchers' eyes I attended to their spatial organization and learned the significance of details I would have otherwise overlooked or not understood, especially those modifications to the environment that manage water. The culmination was Watershed Moments, a large solo exhibition of photographs and two sculptures derived from photogrammetry scans organized by the BES and presented at Baltimore's Space Camp Gallery with wall labels I wrote. People who had lived in Baltimore for years found the images revelatory, both the natural beauty and the environmental degradation. In late 2015, I spent seven weeks in Antarctica as a National Science Foundation Antarctic Artists and Writers Program (AAWP) grantee, photographing ice and rock formations and wildlife. I produced a large solo exhibition of photographic prints and sculpture from that project called Walking in Antarctica with the assistance of a Robert W. Deutsch Foundation Rubys Award and a grant from the Puffin Foundation. The exhibition premiered at the Rosenberg Gallery at Goucher College, Baltimore, in 2017 and includes 33 photographs, 4 sculptures and a first-person narrative audio tour I produced, accessed by visitors on their mobile phones via free WiFi in the gallery. In 2022, Walking in Antarctica embarked on a five-year tour of museums and art galleries nationwide under the auspices of ExhibitsUSA, a traveling exhibition rental service operated by the Mid-America Arts Alliance. The confirmed schedule to date includes six venues in Texas, California, Connecticut, West Virginia, and New York. Individual works from the Antarctica project have appeared in several other exhibitions, from 2016 to the present, notably two photographs enlarged to 7 x 10 feet that have been displayed as part of a rotating display at Baltimore-Washington International Marshall Airport since 2017, and one enlarged to billboard size and displayed in a temporary outdoor exhibition of international photographers for a year (2023-24) at the Palacio de las Aguas Corrientes in Buenos Aires, Argentina. I have been interviewed about the Antarctica project for features by a variety of print, public radio, and online media outlets. My Antarctica archive and a sculpture are in the collection of the Nevada Art Museum's Center for Art + Environment and were the subject of a year-long display in its research library (2020-21). All these experiences inform my current project about Kangerlussuaq. In 2019, I won a second Rubys Award from the Robert W. Deutsch Foundation to fund the initial stage of the Kangerlussuaq project. I reached out to director of the Sisimiut and Kangerlussuaq Museums to see if the museum would provide historical and logistical advice; in exchange I offered the use of prints license free to update exhibits at the museum. COVID-related travel restrictions forced me to postpone the first trip until September 2021. In early 2023, I wrote a successful grant proposal for the museum to submit to the US Embassy in Copenhagen Small Grants Program, which funds cultural exchange between American artists and Danish and Greenlandic institutions. In collaboration with the museum's director, who is gradually replacing exhibits that have not substantially changed in 25 years, we planned a new two-room permanent exhibit that focuses on Kangerlussuaq's history and surrounding environment. I designed, produced, wrote the wall text, and installed it. The exhibit opened with a public program on September 7, 2023, and includes 22 of my photographs, some paired with one of 12 vintage photographs of similar sites that I sourced from Danish and US archives and a local resident. It also includes my hand-painted sculpture of the ice cap in summer, based on a 3D scan of a small section that I created using photogrammetry. A photograph from that project is also included in the book 'The New Geologic Epoch' (ecoartspace, 2023). My images from Antarctica have captured viewers' imaginations, connecting them to awe-inspiring nature in remote locales, and, for those concerned about the health of our planet, provide inspiration and a sense of solidarity. Next, I want to bring the story of Kangerlussuaq to a wider international audience as a photo book and am currently working on a book proposal to present to potential publishers.

2024 Grant Applicant - Round 1
column25: My work explores the destruction and reconstruction of the grid, and the fight for self in late stage capitalism. The ultimate goal of all my work is to crack the lenses society handed people to view the world with – not to replace that lens with my own dogma but to inspire people to seek understandings of their own outside of the influence of high-control organizations such as the cult of christianity I was raised in . My work is deeply connected to my internal world, and shows viewers one alternative way to process sensory input, unlearn dogma, and find delight in questioning the world around them. It can be easy to be lost in the power these societal grids hold - but we are not defined by them. Drawing from a personal taxonomy of drawings, I create a 2D visual surface to project on top of my movement, then capture these moments in film. Through an iterative process of projecting and re-projecting my own movement, I hope to create dialogues about nonlinear time, neuro-complexity, and non-singular senses of self. By interrupting the flow of an intercrate black and white pattern with my body, I celebrate the power in each of us to create our own narratives. The multi-framed moments are combined to create a new experience out of constructs of linear time. My process is constantly iterating on new and old images, just as we all are a series of imperfect versions of ourselves improving each time but never fully finished. I see my work as furthering the lineage of feminist artists using their body as a medium. I have often danced in the shadows – walking home on a dark financial district street on the way home from another 14 hour day in a graduate program not designed for me to succeed in. As a masked Autistic, presenting my stimming as dance has helped me move more safely through the world. By presenting my movement for the public to see, I hope to reclaim my neuro-complexity and begin to remove the shackles of the neurotypical dominance that harms us all. In the infamous words of Jiddu Krishnamurti, “It is no measure of health to be well adjusted to a profoundly sick society.”1 I do not know what is authentic underneath all the layers of internalized ableism, capitalism and heteronormativity, but I hope this piece can be a step to finding that. Who am I when getting my basic needs met are not dependent on playing the role capitalism assigned me? Who are we underneath the grids? 1 https://www.brainyquote.com/quotes/jiddu_krishnamurti_107856

Rashad Ali Muhammad is a multidisciplinary artist who works in various mediums, including analog and digital collage, assemblage, design, painting, and non-narrative video. As a queer, gender-nonconforming person of the African diaspora, he resonates with the ability to deconstruct and reform reality — a method of thinking and creation that can counter society’s hierarchies and binaries. Muhammad uses unexpected found objects, organic shapes, patterns, and symbols as cultural signifiers to highlight the vast intersections shaping the human experience while creating space for healing and rejuvenation. No matter the medium, Muhammad's touchstone is honoring the inner child.

column25: Rashad Ali Muhammad is a multidisciplinary collage artist known for creating vivid and captivating works reflecting the vast complexities of our human existence. Muhammad's ongoing journey to expand his emotional intelligence ignited his desire to explore the intricacies of the human experience — the expansive intersections that shape our lives and how we can relate to each other beyond the surface. His experiences as a queer, gender-nonconforming person of the African diaspora reflect his fondness for utilizing art as a catalyst to liberate minds from the binary confines of society. Through his art, he cultivates open space for healing and rejuvenation from our chaotic world. Muhammad's emphasis on connecting to his inner child and love for experimentation fuels his whimsical and enchanting artistic sensibilities. For him, collage combines intention, invention, and investigation, dissecting established references and reassembling them to create new compelling visions. With a formal graphic design and photography education, Muhammad blends his acquired skills to create art that fascinates and expands the mind. Muhammad is a resident artist at the Torpedo Factory Art Center in Alexandria, VA. His art has been exhibited extensively throughout the Washington Metropolitan region (DC, Maryland, Virginia), with other national and international exposure. He is a 2023 Art and Peacebuilding Fellow with the Carter School for Peace and Conflict Resolution at GMU. His artwork has appeared broadly in print and digital media, including the Washington Post, Kolaj Magazine, Create! Magazine, and British GQ.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The underlying theme of my artwork has consistently been semi-autobiographical in nature, reflecting my own background and experience. I primarily use photography and video to address the ways in which lens-based media can connect deeply with experience and memory. While my work is photo based, I have never felt a need to stay in my lane and will use other media (e.g. video, drawing, painting, mono prints) when the need arises. Recently, I’ve begun to shift away from evidence-based work (e.g. straight photography) towards a more experimental approach. The straight image is still of great interest to me, yet I feel other ways of seeing may add the nuance and a sense of joy and wonder that I seek. Current Artistic Exploration With my varied experiences in the outdoors, as artist, tourist, and backpacker, where I have witnessed both the positive and negative ways that humans impact our national, regional and local parks, I’ve increasingly become interested in our relationships with nature and how my work might contribute to the conversation. As with all my work, research plays a significant role. Because of this, I seek out the expertise of others to teach me how to be a good steward. This, in turn, informs my work. Broadly speaking, what interests me is the marks we humans make, those which 1) are temporary (footsteps in the sand), 2) are designed to be useful (conservation efforts, scientific inquiry) or 3) destroy (vandalism, extensive logging). I am exploring both desert and forested areas as a way to reflect on our relationship with the land.

column25: Nancy Floyd has been a visual artist for over 40 years. Her interests include the aging female body, the passage of time, barren landscapes, and trees. She uses photography, video, and mixed-media to address the ways in which lens-based media can connect deeply with experience and memory. Floyd has received numerous grants and awards including a 2022 Guggenheim Fellowship, the 2019 International Center of Photography / GOST Books First Photo Book Award, a 2018 Aaron Siskind Photography Fellowship and a 2014 John Gutmann Photography Fellowship. Floyd’s artwork is in the collection of the Center for Creative Photography (Tucson, AZ), the High Museum of Art (Atlanta, GA), Museum of Contemporary Photography (Chicago, IL), Lightwork (Syracuse, NY), and in numerous private collections. Floyd holds a BFA from the University of Texas at Austin, an MA from Columbia College Chicago, and an MFA from the California Institute of the Arts. She is Emerita Professor in the Ernest G. Welch School of Art and Design at Georgia State University in Atlanta and lives in Bend, Oregon.

One of the best things about being an artist is the boundless freedom of expression. It's a journey where creativity knows no limits, and your imagination serves as both compass and destination. As an artist, you have the incredible ability to turn emotions, ideas, and perspectives into tangible, impactful creations that resonate with others. There's an unparalleled joy in the process of bringing something new into existence, whether it's a painting, a sculpture, a piece of music, or any other form of art. Plus, art allows you to connect with people on a profound level, transcending language and cultural barriers. The sense of fulfillment that comes from sharing your creations and sparking emotions or thoughts in others is truly extraordinary. Being an artist grants you the privilege of continually exploring, evolving, and leaving a lasting impression through your unique artistic voice.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I use photography, language and mark-making to explore unseen aftermath of traumatic experiences. My work is a visual and psychological inquiry into experiences and surfaces, ubiquitous in everyday life, yet largely unaddressed. Inspired by the elusive nature of such experiences, I lean towards a minimalist abstract aesthetic. I am interested in the way power structures force their narrative of truth upon us, and how photography plays a role in that false objectivity. I think about the perception of safety, comfort, trauma, objectivity and knowledge. Repetition is an important part of my work as a formal way of insisting on looking at a thing. I am invested in the friction of exchange between body and surrounding, between photograph and printed surface, and between artwork and spectator. I use material metaphors to lead the potential meaning of the work outside of the descriptive nature of the thing that is pictured and into abstracted personal and political implications. Within the body of work entitled ‘under standings’ I recreate an introspective investigation of an unnamed event and the facts surrounding its meaning. It is a landscape of psychological processing. A thread of reactions and actions builds tension as a fragmented narrative is slowly revealed through the examination of found texts: hand traced bits and pieces from a psychology book, web engine answers and an apology letter are intertwined with photographs. The photographs portray a text based expressive material play. Touch and affect is explored through mark making, traces and residues. Words are traced to paper, embossed on napkins, erased and rubbed with bare hands, glued together and covered with ink and water spills. I use “material failures” to point to both emotional instability and resilience.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
About “Wander Around” series – by Olivia Miller Using a combination of materials, artist Xinyu Zhang explores the ever-increasing roles that technology plays in humans’ daily lives. She paints the figures with acrylic paint, the texture of the paint referencing the physical nature of the human body. The digitally created environment in which the figures wander symbolizes the virtual worlds that people now inhabit. In some of Zhang’s works, digital elements create barriers between people—they wander alone, oblivious of one another. In other pieces, the human figures interact despite of and perhaps even because of the digital elements. These two modes of representation serve as a metaphor for the duality of technology itself. Technology keeps us connected and informed about our world. People have found family, rekindled friendships, and networked in ways that were never possible before. Yet there are fundamental concerns with these forms of communication. Are our virtual relationships physically isolating? Is technology inhibiting our ability to form deep connections with one another?

I'm drawn to the set of rules that must be followed to complete a piece, and the guidelines that can be flexed and sometimes breached to find new ways of making and seeing beauty in the material. When I'm out of the hot shop, I use my space as a laboratory to explore optics and light through experimentation with self-fashioned instruments. Beyond this practice, I'm drawn to the disruption of perception through the optical qualities of glass itself and in this view, how concepts can be shifted with different perspectives.

Throughout his career, Evan has had the privilege of working with world-renowned glass artists, whose influence continues to shape and inspire his current artistic endeavors. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My most recent paintings address issues about the environment that point to the too often unknowing consequences of convenience, technology, and consumerism. The paintings continue in the tradition of using art as a civilizing instrument for social and cultural reform. By re-contextualizing and re-presenting works of Renaissance Old Masters through the use of historical visual references, titles, frames, techniques, and materials, I want to draw parallels between early Christian religious iconographic themes and present day environmental concerns in hopes of raising questions about mankind's current relationship with the natural world. In doing so I want challenge society's blind faith in current institutions, both historically religious and corporate (capitalist religion), and their imposed myths about the value of conveniences offered by technology and ideally set the stage for a potentially new perception(s) and changes(s) in behavior.

column25: Narrative Bio I was born and raised near Charlotte, NC in a family of modest means and ambitions. I discovered painting while studying product design and architecture at N.C. State University. After being laid off due to the 1973 recession from a D.C. architectural firm I enrolled at the California Institute of the Arts and received an MFA in graphic design. I later worked for Charles and Ray Eames as a graphic designer and animator. In 1980 I returned to N.C. State and taught design fundamentals, graphic design, and painting for 6 years. After a financially unsuccessful year of trying to sustain myself as a painter I designed a visitors tour center for Philip Morris's Manufacturing facility in Richmond, Virginia. Shortly after that project and several similar others I decided to stop helping corporations sell questionable (and sometimes lethal) products and began helping my wife restore antique carousels, mechanical band organ facades, and paint finishes in historic houses, which I continue to do to this day in Saxapahaw, NC where my wife and I live a green rural life. I have been an exhibiting artist for 40+ years. My work has been exhibited in numerous venues including the North Carolina Museum of Art, the Mint Museum, the Greenville (SC) County Museum of Art and other museums and non-profits across North Carolina and the Southeast. I have received 2 North Carolina Arts Council Fellowships and a Pollock-Krasner Grant.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I deconstruct and build landscapes from photographs taken while traveling around the country and walking down city streets. My work is about imagined places, and the reconstruction of memory through landscapes. Much of my work in printmaking exists within an imagined horizon, or lack of ground, with hand-built models creating an imagined and anonymous place. It is through these abstracted and fabricated landscapes that I can create and show how I see the world around me, through work that is deeply rooted in the life and experiences I’ve had (and still have) living and traveling in unconventional ways. I began documenting my life as a female in the NYC punk/street scene while in high school and continued as I rode freight trains across this country. My experiences living on the road and traveling around this country in unconventional ways are what inspires my work and continues to be the source and subject matter in my sculptures and printed works. Photography is at the core of all my work, with printmaking through photographic print processes allowing me to use my imagery to collage and mix media, building landscapes that tell a story as to how I view the world around me.

column25: I was born, raised and still live in the East Village of New York City I am an interdisciplinary artist, working in multiple areas of the visual arts, with ideas from one medium crossing over and informing my work in other media. My work is based on my experiences growing up in the NYC street scene, and later traveling around this country riding freight trains and hitchhiking for almost 10 years. column25: My approach is one of inquiry and problem solving. I push materials out of normative terrain and integrate intention to germinate hybrid imaginations of form. In the works submitted for this grant, I explore light and color in the materiality of fabric and thread. Translucent overlaps create a visual effect that exposes permeable boundaries of perception, depth, and substance: this is the terrain in which the porous relationship between truth, beauty, and identity vibrates. The sculptural forms are Soul Umbra – icons materialized from a space unseen. They are precise projections of my soul’s intentions that verbal language cannot articulate, exteriorized into corporeal form. The Soul Umbra embody a shadow space in the seam between painting and sculpture, yet are made of fabric. They intersect sculptural forms and minimalist painting in an inceptive framework, while referencing traditional fiber arts . It combines softness and transparency with the hardness of shaped and solid form. The complex shape reaches for an alchemical body where the kinetic and unseen collapse with the materiality of physical form. Like me, the work is female bodied, with a masculine structure and spirit. The work challenges the viewer to reconsider the artistic products associated with traditional feminine craft while referencing a masculine heritage. It exists in a state of duality that is familiar yet displaced and evokes conversations that examine perception and process. I am a craftsperson and problem solver in equal measure as an explorer of color and liminality.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Histories on many spectrums—ancestral, artistic, material, personal, and cultural—fuel my practice. My studio is a flux of mediums and objects in constant conversation with each other: A small original gouache painting gets printed on custom fabric, which goes into a mixed media collage, that informs a piece of wearable art, or adds to the composition of a new gouache painting. Nothing is off-limits to being repurposed and reimagined. As a member of the Cherokee Nation, this process is a personal narrative of the Native American Diaspora. Indigenous peoples had to be inventive and purposeful with elements and resources as ancestors were removed from native homelands to new environments. This is not only instilled in my mind from native ancestry, but also from a rural Kansas upbringing. As a product of Cherokee, Scottish, Swiss-German, and other diaspora, I am, in the very makeup of my DNA, a collage of cultures, values, histories, and personal aesthetics. I react by collaging materials from my ancestors, contemporary community, and personal life experiences. Graphic design, fine art, and fiber are all woven into my ancestral tapestry the same way I weave in and out of mediums in my studio. Sometimes, literally weaving materials. I illustrate personal narratives by inventing compositions driven by a theme or experience, translating them through the lens of abstracted patterns I’ve built out of my cultural research of Cherokee and other native arts. These personal illustrations are a hybrid of being culturally grounded, while also being far from native upbringing; a life influenced by various sources to the point of abstraction. There is a feel of beadwork or sewing, but the artwork may contain neither. I view each piece I create as its own individual entity, yet all contributing to a visual language I am inventing. An aesthetic world that people of many cultural backgrounds can relate to or appreciate in some form. My fiber weavings were born out of a time when I was starting to research my Cherokee Nation Heritage. We all knew my great-grandma Hattie was very proud of being Cherokee and being from Oklahoma, but over the generations most of the stories and values, both family and cultural, got lost. This was due to her moving away after marriage as well as, I’m sure, the general outlook and cultural emphasis in the States about being Native. As I studied Cherokee basketry patterns and read about the traditional ways they are made, I began to look around my studio for ideas and saw my ever-growing pile of bias tape. It accumulates from sewing notion lots I pick up at garage sales or thrift stores, and I also had a lot from my grandma’s. I started making weaving out of it, affixed to stretcher bars, and using Cherokee patterns. I see them as a visual reference of my standing in the Cherokee Diaspora- of being proud of my heritage and wanting to connect and grow deeper in it, but also still being influenced by the other communities I’m placed in or made up of, and working with the influences and materials that surround my daily life. Some of my weavings actually have skips in the patterns, and I leave them there as a visual representation of how I have not been able to learn my heritage and art forms from elders in person yet- but I’m trying to rebuild and relearn our heritage in order to hopefully continue our legacy more deeply for future generations. The weavings have all been different- sometimes I use patterns that are directly from Cherokee basketry, sometimes I draft my own composition to weave. I’ve made my own bias tape out of vintage fabrics and I’ve used antique frames from my Grandma. Each one takes on a story of it’s own with the pattern or color I choose, as well at the materials that go into it. They are also like fiber paintings, as I am working with only the fiber I have on hand at any given time, and I choose colors and weave as I go and as the piece takes shape. I love repurposing materials and stewarding the little big of objects and fibers that I can. My great-grandmother also saved and used very little bit she could, so I feel like I”m honoring our family heritage in that way as well. I desire my art to open conversations and be a starting point to discussing tribes, Native Americans past and present, and the many nuances of being descended from Native cultures in a world growing increasingly diverse in numerous ways with every generation. For myself, this also includes my time in Southeast Asia and friendships I’ve made globally. Cultivating and presenting a joy of cultural differences, influences, and histories allows hope to exist for where we might go in regards to tribes, countries, and personal communities in the future.

column25: Hattie Lee Mendoza is a multi-disciplinary artist who grew up in Fowler, Kansas, and now lives in Peoria, Illinois. She has an MFA from Bradley University in Peoria, Illinois, and a BA in graphic design from Tabor College. Lee has been published in the #155 Midwest Issue of New American Paintings, as well as Excellence in Fibers VII & VIII by Fiber Art Now. She has been in exhibitions in in ten states. Awards include an Honorable Mention at D’Art Gallery’s “Spot On #3”, Denver, CO, Emerging Artist Award for the juried 51st Trail of Tears Art Show as well as First Place in Contemporary Basketry and Merit Awards in Diverse Arts at the 27th & 28th Annual Cherokee Homecoming Art Show in Tahlequah, Oklahoma, the Cherokee Nation capitol. Mendoza will be having solo exhibitions in 2024 at Oklahoma State University in Stillwater, OK, as well as Cameron University, Lawton, OK. Lee is influenced by her Great Grandmother’s Cherokee heritage and stories, desiring to revive and continue that legacy within her family. She spent three years living in Thailand, as well as traveling to various countries, while working with a non-profit organization on their fine art and media team. The experience gave her opportunities to interact and learn from many people groups and tribes in Asia and the Middle East. Her maternal grandmother’s frugal values, stemming from a depression era childhood, are also reflected in Lee’s practice by including repurposed and recycled personal and family items, as well as thrifted and found objects. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is that I get to be creative and do what I love, which is making all forms of art, across multiple mediums such as sculpture, film, jewelry, digital arts, costume design, music, cultural practices, while advocating for social and environmental justice with a focus on Indigenous rights. I love sharing my Quechua culture from the Andes of Peru and uplifting the Indigenous voice. Artist talks, both in person and virtual, cultural exchanges, and social media are ways I enjoy to engaging with a larger audience. We have so much to bring as stewards of the land and water, with an important worldview that carries traditional ecological knowledge applicable for climate change mitigation and adaptation. Art can open our minds to a decolonized perception and help embrace our diverse ontologies. Reconnection and reconstruction of Native identity can be achieved, with art being the heart of genuine expression of the Indigenous cultural resilience, connected with the roots and based on the cultural process of resistance and decolonization. My artistic expressions contribute to the liberation and self-determination of Indigenous Nations with the message that Indigenous peoples have the knowledge and wisdom to live in harmony with their natural environment without destroying it. Native approach of life is of great importance because it is grounded in the understanding that life is an interrelated and interconnected web of diverse entities, all of which are alive.

column25: Transformation, regeneration and healing are interconnected concepts in my work, with messages of environmental and social justice. My expressions encompass film, jewelry, digital art, illustration, and primarily large sculpture. I work with diverse materials such as wood, bronze, and stone. I bridge contemporary and ancient aesthetics with figurative mythological beings, stylized symbolism, interweaving magical and spiritual realms. My inspiration flows from pre-Columbian Quechua ancestry and Andean cosmovision honoring Indigenous peoples and land, celebrating the beauty and spiritual depth of my culture. My work awakens my generation to achieve balance between living immersed in this world of modernity and maintaining our cultural roots. By healing the division of colonialism aftermaths and historical traumas, Indigenous peoples can emerge with dignity to build our collective destiny, reuniting our nations.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work engages the Black quotidian, its relationship to digitally-aided methods of making, and the various lenses through which Black daily life is warped, stretched, chopped, and screwed. I have a transdisciplinary practice that resists medium specificity. I note this because I aim to embody the malleability of Black culture when I make. I'm concerned with the extent of Black identity and how dominant modes of cultural exploitation decentralize and exploit it. In turn, I subvert and critique these exploitative systems to return Blackness from the margins of society. The Internet and my Afro-Caribbean identity are central to my practice: Black Twitter, WorldStarHiphop, WhatsApp, and #BlackLivesMatter. These sites complicate my existence not only as Black-body-connected but as a maker concerned with the dangers of over-exposure. In my work, I invoke the problematics, powers, and perils intrinsic to these spaces to render conceptually striking impressions and inversions of material to propose new possibilities for contemporary Black ontology.

column25: Njari Anderson(b. 2001, Clarendon, Jamaica) is an artist whose work engages the Black quotidian and its relationship to the digital. Anderson's transdisciplinary practice traverses sculpture, new media, performance, and writing. Anderson draws heavily from the internet and his Afro-Caribbean identity to implicate himself and viewers in morally complex allegories. Often invoking the extended metaphor in his labor-intensive practice, he thematizes loss, visibility, politics, peril, and the pleasures of ambiguity to narrativize the nuances intrinsic to Blackness. Most recently, his work has explored what it means to be Black and “chronically online.” Borrowing from poor images, online subspaces, #hashtags, and meme aesthetics, he investigates the extents of digital, Black agency.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
A body is an index of passing time. Skin protects us as it shows freckles and wrinkles forming, bones shifting, bruises appear, and muscles loosening and tightening. I think of this as a transient fashion of skin, including the revealing way a blush decorates one’s cheek, freckles form constellations on an arm, or hair creates sheen on skin’s matte surface. I am a visual artist with a skin condition called dermatographia. It means ‘skin writing’ and with a gentle, painless stroke, I can draw welts on my skin. The welts last about 30 minutes and are caused by an excessive amount of histamine coming to the surface. I draw on and press items to my skin to make pictures, marks, designs, and echos of the objects that make contact. In Bloom Back, I share a moment with plants by pressing them to my skin. I bloom back at the flora pressed against me, offering some reflection and appreciation, seeing how bodies can echo their surroundings. Photography is the basis for all my work. Along with photographs of dermatographia, I also make wallpaper, collage and temporary tattoos. I’ve been making art inspired by skin for 20 years, and am always excited by the possibilities skin has to offer. My work has been shown all over the world, in both fine and medical art contexts. I like that there’s a connection between the medical/health and fine art worlds because well-being is very important to me. The skin photographs can appeal to anyone who has struggled with bodily ‘flaws.’ Skin is such a visible and important part of us: it makes us vulnerable to judgement from the outside, yet also keeps us safe from harmful microorganisms on the outside. It’s our largest organ and our greatest protector. And it can reveal things about our internal happenings, like a blush betraying our innermost desires, or dermatographia showing the world how sensitive we are. My work is helping to spread the word about dermatographia so folks find ways to heal and appreciate their skin just like I have.
