column25: Haoyu Wang is an architect and artistic researcher based in California, USA and Helsinki, Finland. He studied architecture at the University of Miami between 2012 and 2017, received his Master's degree in Urbanism from MIT in 2020, and practiced design in Japan, China, and across the USA before joining Aalto University with his artistic research project 'Beyond the Closing Future: Decoding Popular Architectural Imagination through AI Creativity'. His research involves developing an art genre of architectural fiction that incorporates speculative design with visual storytelling to visualize radical urban futures. His works have been granted art residencies, production supports, and exhibition opportunities across the US and Europe. His current experiments involve AI-augmented speculative design and VR immersive storytelling, with projects on display including 'Wake Up From 2020' supported by Moskosel Creative Lab and Swedish Art Councils, and 'The Canceled Future of Plovdiv' supported by the Context AIR program of Plovdiv Municipality in Bulgaria.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Over the past 20 years I have focused on creating large scale installations that emphasis a variety of media including painting, video and sculptural elements to involve the physical, social and aesthetic possibilities of traditional exhibition spaces, public spaces, found situations, architecture, and the natural sense of dérive we feel as we move through the world. Most recently my practice has been paired down to drawings and paintings that examine language and writing as well as abstraction. I think one of my favourites, which I won’t show you, comes from an idea couched in Russian Suprematism that idea that. “Abstract planes of color are vividly anti-materialistic and powerfully unstable” My idea of painting exists in relation to a host other materials and larger signifying framework. This can encompass site specific murals and a primary goal is to let the word outside of an artwork leek or worm itself in for the viewer. Often the materials I use to make works are culled from ephemera of my life. I figure these experiences form my world and the language I assign to the world and how I feel about it. These snippets of phone conversations found sounds, and a generally eclectic assortment of memories, sketches and photographs of places, situations, and people with whom I've had direct experience. are as good as any source material I might find or dig up. I particularly like the idea that the work might act as a type of rupture or marker in my own and a viewers' understanding of the world.

My proposed project is to complete a series of 12 original collage works of 'imaginary friends' that will be scanned and published into an all ages book alongside original poetry. I am currently working on the first series of 4 astronaut friends. The book and original works will launch at the same time. The book is inspired by the work of Eric Carle and is meant to bring a sense of imagination, joy, happiness, and curiosity. The poems will be optimistic, insightful, with a tinge of nostalgia. column25: I use the human body to convey and explore human emotions. The body has the capacity to hold memories that can manifest in emotional trauma and/or physical issues. I hope to capture the rawness and vulnerability of human experiences through these large scale nude figures. Working from and through personal experiences, these bodies become a channel for storytelling and a means to communicate themes of trauma, degradation and mental health. I begin my work by allowing expressive washes of acrylic paint to take control of the surfaces I’m working on. The textures that result from this emphasize themes of emotional and physical degradation. I then use charcoal powder as paint to sculpt knotted and overlapping human figures into the painting. This process of full control versus no control of medium adds to the emotional struggle within each work. The monochrome palettes serve to highlight the stark reality of the human condition by allowing for a greater focus on the intricate interplay between the figures, as well as light and shadow. This speaks to the simplicity and universality of human emotion. The paintings become more relatable, as emotional experiences transcend race, gender, and culture. The washes of paint and powdered charcoal add depth and dimension, creating a subtle but impactful texture that contributes to the overall emotional resonance of each piece. The intention for this exhibition is to evoke a visceral response from viewers, encouraging them to reflect on their own experiences and the ways in which emotions can shape our bodies.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an international student pursuing my Master of Fine Arts (MFA) degree in graphic design in the United States, my artistic journey is deeply rooted in personal experiences and driven by a passion for creative expression. Having worked as a graphic designer in various fields, mainly publishing in Iran, I harbored a persistent desire to address critical issues through the language of art. My decision to study in a country where freedom of thought and action prevailed was a pivotal moment in realizing this aspiration. As a woman, I could not ignore the discrimination and inequality faced by women in my homeland. These observations ignited my determination to utilize design as a medium for expressing the complex challenges confronting women in Iran and the Middle East. Their struggles, resilience, and pursuit of equality form the core of my artistic journey, amplifying their voices through my designs. However, my perspective underwent a profound transformation upon arriving in the United States. Here, I encountered a society where women enjoyed greater autonomy and diversity of roles, compelling me to reevaluate gender discrimination from a fresh angle. My artistic exploration delves into how women can challenge societal constraints, traditions, and gender norms. My design projects now revolve around empowering women by fostering self-awareness. This involves recognizing and accepting their individuality, roles, experiences, characteristics, and affiliations and understanding how these facets impact their lives. This self-awareness equips women to confront and navigate multifaceted challenges. In my artistic journey, I aim to blend personal experiences and social commentary with typography and motion to provoke thought, inspire action, and encourage viewers to perceive the world through a different lens.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I, J. Valencia as a photographer, realizes that life can be harsh, fragile, and beautiful from my experiences of hardships, childhood trauma along with positive parts of life such as achievements, family, solid friendships and other relationships. My main focus is capturing memories that can be easily forgotten and sharing it with the world for that reason. My approach to capturing candids of people in their natural state and environment is mostly through a 85mm camera lens to keep both distance and trust all while making it easier to be themselves when shooting. In some cases, I also use film cameras to create a nostalgic effect that can remain timeless. I work with both digital and film cameras depending on the mood I wish to create. Then the magic and creativeness is followed by my photos being edited through Adobe Lightroom and Photoshop. The last step is always the most important part, posting and printing my photography through a professional printer and sharing the beautifully captured moments for those with an open heart and mind. My goal in the photography world is capturing the human experience and I will continue to do so.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is an exploration of the imaginary worlds through intuitions or sensations that I experience while painting. The first approach of the series Magic was the small sketches in 'Spirit of the Forests'. These small paintings in walnut ink and watercolor represented mystical creatures developing and evolving in the mangrove forest. But for these creatures who are sometimes evil, but often beneficent, the forests were not enough: I had the feeling that, to invoke the spirits and sing their incantatory melodies, they needed much larger spaces. This is how 'Magic' was born, a series of large abstract watercolors on YUPO, in which an exuberant tropical nature covers an endless mountainous landscape. Add to these landscapes the fire and the sparks of incantations and spells - and you get the colors, black, pink, orange, green that appear in the paintings. Genie Davis article about this series on https://www.diversionsla.com/ “Voluptuously vivid swirls of color soar through Annie Clavel‘s vibrant watercolor works. Her images are like liquid captured motion, as svelte as they are visually kinetic. They form clouds and winds, waves, sunsets and flora, wings in flight, and always evoke a sense of profound mystery and beauty, like flowers half-formed but still bursting into bloom'

column25: BIOGRAPHY Annie Clavel is a mixed media abstract painter who grew up in Paris, France. She studied mathematics and computer science and taught mathematics in France and in Tunisia. Annie moved to Long Beach in 2006, where she opened the gallery “Les Jolis Trésors” and began her career as a professional artist. She takes her inspiration from her background in science, thinking of the perpetual motion in space since the Big Bang and of the concept of infinity. Cosmic landscapes, magical nature, emotions, and stories might be seen in her paintings. Her art is in private collections in France, Tunisia, Germany, Canada, Japan and USA. Annie is a board member of Women Painters West (PR committee, Facebook and Instagram). She is an active member of Los Angeles Art Association (Gallery 825) where she moderates a critic group. She has been treasurer and chair of the public relations committee at Orange County Center for Contemporary Art in Santa Ana, from 2015 to November 2023.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am salvaging a burned down cabin in the Sierra Nevada mountains of California using its beams, tin roofing to create classical figurative/ refugee sculpture. The cabin was a victim of the Caldor wildfire that evacuated 20,000 residents from Lake Tahoe, California. The pedestal Big Oil graphic bring attention to the personal greed of politicians and big corporations, discrediting scientific evidence for their own personal power and greed, resulting in environmental, climate and human disasters. In addition, but not shown here, I have been salvaging a shipwrecked Haitian refugee sloop run aground in the Florida Keys at Islamorada, Florida. I have been using the hand hewed planks from the ship hull for the found objects to also create classical figurative/ refugee sculpture.

column25: Folklore, reverence, refinement of nature, observance of daily life, are the general concepts behind my work. Stories and myths of a culture convey a sense of place and a sense of the people who inhabit that place. In this way, visual art that interacts with the story enters a narrative sphere where it must encounter the local. I use a balance of abstract and representational forms in order to sever the connection between shape and meaning, connecting the viewer instead to the cut of the knife, so that they become complicit in the art. I draw inspiration from my heritage of traditional Jewish paper cuts. Jewish paper cutting has been around since the middle ages but as an art form it was lost during WWII. Jewish art reveals universal elements in its visual representation of animals. Cut paper is a folk craft, one which you can find in every culture over the world. Cut paper, like storytelling, is a human art, shared by everyone and crossing borders and cultures. This work contains universal elements in its visual representation of animals, Stories are one of the ways we create a sense of community. Artists have often turned to stories for subject matter, especially stories whose roots reach deep into their culture’s collective memory. These stories are why I create for a living, they feed the context and content of my work. I do not expect my work to discover “new” aspects of these stories but only to add the dialog of what is and to create a new way of looking at them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Artist Statement - My name is Tahia Farhin Haque, and I am a photographer and an artist from Bangladesh. My artistic journey has led me to delve into the poignant narratives left behind in the aftermath of the Partition of India in 1947 and 1971, the birth of my country Bangladesh. This cataclysmic event in South Asian history left deep scars and indelible imprints on the lives of countless individuals. In my photographic work, I aim to trace these broken lives, to give them a voice, and to shed light on the enduring effects of history from my perspective as a woman artist.My photography is a journey into the forgotten corners of history, a tribute to the often marginalized and discounted stories that have shaped the lives of South Asia's post-partition citizens.My works revolves around a visual exploration of the past, the personal, and the political, and how these elements are interwoven into our lives. Through my often black and white images, I aim to bring to light the complex narrative of Bangla/Bengal belonging, a narrative that often remains hidden behind the dominant and selective stories of nationhood. The violent upheavals that marked the birth of independent nations like India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh and many other colonized nations have often sought to erase the intricate and often painful experiences of those who lived through those times. My work challenges this erasure and confronts the linear-modernist idea that the past is a distant, disconnected place. Instead, I draw attention to the past's embodied endurance, showing how it continues to shape and influence our present.My lens is often drawn to the veiled women, these images serve as a stark reminder of the turmoil and terror that my own grandmother endured more than seven decades ago when she fled her home in Kolkata during the partition with a child in her arms, the child was my father. The pain, the loss, and the resilience that she embodied are etched in my memory, motivating me to document and share the stories of those who have faced similar trials. In my art, there is a duality that exists. There's an unsettling beauty that can be found in the most unexpected places, a beauty that coexists with the chaos and oppression that are part of our lived experiences. Through self-portraits and photographs of others, I explore my personal journey through life, family, love, and adversity. The delicate flowers superimposed on a portrait of a young boy, for example, symbolize tenderness and innocence, but when you delve into the personal stories of the subjects, a different, more complex picture emerges. I refer to this duality as the 'duality in reality.' It is a reminder that life is a multifaceted tapestry of experiences, emotions, and histories. My work is an invitation to engage with these dualities, to look beyond the surface, and to recognize the profound beauty that can exist in the midst of hardship. Through my photographs, I hope to challenge perceptions, provoke contemplation, and, most importantly, to give voice to those whose stories have been discounted or forgotten in the dominant narratives of our history. Currently I am working on the different realities that exist in the USA, and delving into the complicated stories of segregation, abuse and resistance.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a fashion designer by training and have worked at the nexus of art, technology and fashion for more than 15 years. With a focus on textile development and wearable technology, my practice explores ways of supporting people with disabilities, those recovering from disease, and people who are socially isolated or marginalized. My collaborative approach often convenes physicians, scientists, designers, artists, and community members. A major focus of my work has been the development of touch-sensitive textiles that trigger sound recordings and other kinds of outputs. On numerous occasions, my work has been integrated with the medical field. Under the auspices of a DAAD Faculty Research Grant, I developed a new kind of touch-sensitive head covering (“cap”) with the capacity to record the brain waves (EEG) of patients with epilepsy, in collaboration with RWTH Aachen University and Berlin’s Fraunhofer Institute in Germany. As a result of that work, I subsequently became involved with the Los Angeles-based Not Impossible Foundation for the design of BrainWriter, a path-breaking technology that enables people to communicate using their brain waves. Our design and the process of training patients on how to use it was presented at Digital Revolution, an immersive exhibition of art, design, film, music, and video games at London’s Barbican Centre in 2014. These projects ultimately enriched my work, curriculum, and student experience, with the inclusion of a section on fashion for people with disabilities. In 2021 as part of a collaborative project called Re-FREAM | Embroidered Touch | Life Space, to explore the future of fashion and technology, I collaborated with the Fraunhofer IZM in Berlin to develop touch-sensitive embroidered sweaters. When touched, circuits embroidered on the sweaters’ surface trigger sound palettes recorded from communities in Berlin and Chicago. The results were exhibited in Berlin, Madrid, Bilbao, and in Chicago (Spring 2022). An earlier iteration of the project I am proposing to the International Museum of Surgical Science features embroidered touch-sensitive tablecloths, called Table Talk, A Sense of Place and Play. Touching the tablecloths triggers sounds assembled from field recordings of nature and urban spaces. The work was exhibited at FACILITY Chicago in Fall 2022, and will be shown in Montreal in Fall 2023 for the exhibition: Textile Design Now at Centre de Design de l’UQAM. My long-term research objective is to expand and discover new ways for technology to impact, as well as alleviate, feelings of isolation among marginalized communities. This approach is informed by the pandemic, as well as social unrest in the US. It also is a response to a need for change throughout the fashion industry to foster more sustainable practices for creating, producing, and consuming garments. In addition to serving a functional purpose, clothing acts as our second skin, conveying who we are to the world. Creating responsibly made textiles that communicate with diverse environments informs my current practice.

column25: Contact prints of the body have a tradition in sacred arts, often functioning as evidence of the physical existence of divine or holy persons. We know the Shroud of Turin, the footprints of Buddha and Mohammed, prehistoric cave hand prints, and the thumb print signature. The message of the hand print, foot print, or body print is “I was here”. There is also a sense of a straightforward image not interpreted by the artist’s imagination: a direct impression. The process of making the work involves one or more actual contact body prints. I put paint on the model’s body, and press it onto a surface. Then I paint color around it and in layers over it, working further with brushes, my fingers, airbrush, and splattered painting techniques. The layers, adding both physical and metaphorical substance, enhance the original print, as well as building up a glow, so that the aura becomes luminous. The body is a site of life and death struggle. It is through the body that we experience pain and ecstasy, immanence and transcendence. I draw upon those spiritual traditions that honor living in the body. My art evokes complex and intense emotions both joyful and troubling, and intensely spiritual. I seek to invoke in the viewer a sense of awe for our existence. My art connects emotion, spirit, and body. Nudity in my art is not simply sexual display. It expresses honesty, openness, and freedom. I work so that my paintings would bring a shift in consciousness – a spiritual experience – in the viewer. I seek to invoke a sense of awe for our physical and psychic existence.

column25: My artistic practice provides a framework to hold space for the pain and power of places. This dichotomy of pain and power is central to our creation of reality, as we interpret and incorporate our experiences of life. In the face of ubiquitous violence, loss and disconnection, my practice explores how we can reclaim our power and imagine new realities into being. After working in more than 20 countries as a development economist–most extensively in Palestine and Jordan–and undergoing my own spiritual evolution, I became curious about how our relationship to what “is” impacts our ability to make change. Through my series “Imagination of Salvation”, I have addressed the potential to return water to the former oasis town of Al Azraq, Jordan. I hand-knotted a fishing net and documented its use to catch rocks in the desert and created collages and mixed media works incorporating the stories of local villagers. In Greece, I investigated the paradox of ancient wisdom and the 2008 financial crisis. Using plaster casts of my own body parts I created digital collages overlaying the faces and bodies of statues of gods and goddesses and he furious graffiti found across Athens. Through mixed-media collage, video, and interviews with three generations of refugees, I examine the meaning of home and belonging in Palestine, where it is far more complex than it should be. My current project “I join the Visitors at the Gates” uses my own poetry, photographs, handmade coffins, and 40 carefully chosen artifacts to create and document mourning rituals. Beginning at the inception of the war in Gaza that began on October 7, 2023, the Sufi mystic Shams Al Tabriz’s 40 Rules of Love provide the framework for meditations on how to live from the heart and remain soft in the face of grief.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Throughout my artistic journey, I tend to navigate a space where comfort and isolation converge, exemplified in my 'bedroom pieces.' These scenes explore the intricate relationship between individuals and their possessions, with a particular emphasis on technology. My work is a testament to the human connection with external entities, delving into how possessions, shape our identity and emotions. From a female perspective, I highlight the societal pressure to incessantly consume—clothes, makeup, and technology—as a metric of value. I create my renders using the 3D software Blender. From scene inception, modeling, sculpting, lighting design, to texture application and final rendering, each step is a deliberate journey. For animations, I render a minimum of 240 frames, followed by post-processing in Photoshop or Premiere to enhance color and texture. Childhood books, particularly those by Dr. Seuss, Shel Silverstein, and classics like Goodnight Moon, form the foundation of my inspiration. The simplicity, matter-of-factness, and timeless themes resonate deeply. Whimsical contemporary artists such as Jiayi Li, Nathalie Nguyen, Hilma af Klint, and Nam June Paik further influence my work as well. Symbols like old tech, post-modern furniture aesthetics, pastel colors, and delicate hands punctuate my work. Within these symbols, I want viewers to feel nostalgia for the present, appreciating the beauty of now amidst a perpetual yearning for the past or future. I invite them to recognize that our essence isn't defined by possessions or online personas but by what brings us joy, what we create, and our multifaceted selves. In a world consumed by trends, I urge viewers to pause, reflect on their genuine preferences, and appreciate what truly matters.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
In an increasingly globalized and globalizing reality, returning to identity has been central to my painting. Portraiture has become a search for the imperfect beauty of everyday life. My works are a celebration of that in-between space between the public and the private: human relationships, social norms, emotions, self-awareness imperfection, incompleteness and the ability to find a place in the world that resembles who we are. From the use of photographs of popular culture and my surroundings, my paintings become imaginary of what my own experience is. Halfway between the private and the collective, patterns, abstractions and figuration are part of the amalgam of my voice, in constant mutation. Rather than an in-depth examination of the visual language of painting within contemporary visual culture, my work explores the simple trace of timeless everydayness in which different ways of being in the world converge

ARTIST STATEMENT:
The majority of my paintings are monochrome, where the energy from saturated color brings a living room or a lawn into a world of emotional urgency. Settings that naturally repeat in the work, are the bedroom, the car and the public park. I watch a lot of teen dramas, where these locations are regularly the backdrop for paradigm-shifting events. The figures I paint age with me. Settings for the paintings are moving into domestic spaces, representative of young adulthood. As new milestones emerge, I am interested in continuing to mythologize them. Imagery is sourced from film stills, social media, and photographs of my friends and family. I gravitate towards scenes that repeat in the public consciousness such as birthday parties, and tattooing. The work is pseudo- autobiographical, centering on intimate moments that reference my own experiences, but push into fiction beyond that. The scale of the figures is one to one, inviting the viewer to enter the work.

column25: Eli Kauffman grew up in Salt Lake City, Utah, and is currently based out of Providence, Rhode Island. They graduated from Rhode Island School of Design with a BFA in 2022. Though primarily interested in painting, Kauffman is also known to explore printmaking, textiles, and ceramics. The work mythologizes interpersonal relationships, sourced through a combination of photos and film stills. The reference imagery highlights the disparity between personal experiences of friends and family, and the sensationalized storytelling of social media and television. The paintings are pseudo-autobiographical, reflecting a possible, but not entirely truthful narrative. Sourced from lived experience, television, and instagram, the paintings reflect milestones that repeatedly prove to be important in the public consciousness, such as birthday parties, and tattooing. In some of the works, figures return the gaze of the audience, as if they are waiting to speak, or to have their photo taken. Kauffman aims to convince the audience that they could step into the work and be a part of the events depicted on the canvas.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I work with richly colored textiles, luminous beads and sequins, and fill my pieces with the mystery and splendor of life. I create powerful women from decorative media. My pantheon of female deities, spirits and mortal women are filled with joy, anger, self-determination, and sometimes sorrow. They are of different races, ethnicities and ages, which reflects my world. Most images emerge from photo shoots of artist and dancer friends and colleagues, while others are pulled from imagination, giving voice to emotional experiences. My art is an expression of the ‘abundance theory’; more is more… more color, more complexity, and more like life. I am also working like a poet to pare down my expression, so that every single thing in a painting is essential, no extra; and simultaneously aspiring to be like a dancer, whose work looks magical and free, while underneath, there are many hours of labor and painstaking discipline.

column25: Artist’s Statement Anonymous X paintings argue: “I.” or self, is a tidy fiction necessary to distinguish what is proper to you and that is proper me. It creates the illusion that ego is a singular and complete entity. As the story goes; when a child perceives its likeness in a mirror, it recognizes itself as distinct from others. So begins the process of individuation: I, me, self, and the world. A second order of cognition arises from mirrored illusions. The image appears to be what it is not, real, not an actual subject or person. Negation within “I.' The subject never sees their actual face, but a reflection of it. Only other subjects can see their actual face. I am, I am not, are not simple, not static. Individuals live many selves: lover, custodian, teacher, politician, friend, banker, police, dreamer, plumber, care-giver, parent etc. Identity is plural. Can a person be truly authentic, truly be themselves, when there are so many selves? Lives are porous. Anonymous _X paintings undermine political-economic use of identity/authenticity which monetize and divide social relations. Under each painting is a fully completed portrait masked by black strip negating visual cues of class, race, gender, celebrity, orientation, aspects of, not the whole of being human.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In the quiet embrace of solitude within the natural world, my artistic journey unfolds. Whether fishing, hiking, or scuba diving, these moments become the notes in a symphony, guiding my brush to capture the energy and essence of the world around me. Originally from Ohio, my path led me to Michigan in 2015 and then to a seven-year exploration of Northern California. Under the guidance of elder impressionistic artists, who became both mentors and family, I immersed myself in the rich tapestry of plein-air painting. Now back in the Midwest, Michigan's landscapes beckon me, and I eagerly anticipate translating the beauty of this new home state onto canvas. My art is a celebration of nature's beauty, a vibrant tapestry that includes the teeming life of a creek, the expansive grandeur of mountains, and the breathtaking sunsets over Lake Michigan's dunes. While my primary medium is acrylics, I find joy in exploring the science of tactile paint mediums such as encaustic. Currently immersed in material studies, I combine paint and resin to create the effect of looking through a painting, inviting the viewer into a multi-dimensional experience. Each piece is a unique synthesis of mediums—encaustics, acrylics, resins, and watercolors—a dance between tradition and experimentation. After graduating from Ohio State University in 2013 with a major in Fine Art and a focus on Sculpture, my journey has led me to an expressive approach to plein air. Beyond the canvas, I share my passion through teaching painting classes and have extended my artistry into fashion with a clothing line inspired by my creations. My art is an invitation to connect with nature's rhythm, its cycles, patterns, and textures. Inspired by the landscapes of Western Michigan and memories from winter adventures in Belize and Mexico, I aim to inspire a deeper appreciation for the natural world and convey the importance of preserving its beauty. Each brushstroke is an exploration, each painting a living testament to the dynamic connection between artistic expression and the world that surrounds us.

As a sculptor working primarily with metal flat bar and engaging in the art of assemblage, my creative journey is a fusion of raw materials and conceptual exploration. Each piece I craft is a testament to the dynamic interplay between form and function, where the rigid lines of flat bar transform into fluid expressions of emotion and thought. In my assemblage work, disparate elements find harmony, breathing life into discarded fragments and forging connections that evoke both nostalgia and anticipation. The process is a dance with the inherent tension of the materials, coaxing them into a delicate balance between structure and spontaneity. Through my metal sculptures, I seek to transcend the physical constraints of the medium, inviting viewers to navigate the intricate dialogue between the intentional and the incidental. The interwoven narratives in my creations are an invitation to contemplate the beauty found in the overlooked, transforming the mundane into a celebration of texture, form, and the boundless possibilities of imagination. Ultimately, my art is an exploration of the transformative power inherent in the act of creation, where the malleability of metal becomes a metaphor for the endless potential within ourselves and the world around us.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As the main medium of his paintings—pigmented crushed volcanic rock—is derived from the core of our planet, Bob Landström’s body of work is also an attempt to tap into the core of human experience. Landström believes we have more similarities than differences. Surveying human history, specifically through art, it becomes clear that all cultures share a collective understanding of meaning and symbols, what might even be called a collective soul. By using the iconography of ancient languages, religions, science, and mysticism, Landstrom hopes to not only reconsider our relationship with so-called “primitive” but also our relationship with the universal. Landström’s work is fixed in this central principle, in the reservoir that all experiences and realities drink from, and from which his paintings flow in a stream of consciousness. In many ways, these paintings are visualizations of his thoughts, transceivers for the esoteric knowledge that he’s exploring. This work is also about slowing down, offering dreamlike spaces where one is free to interpret, experience, and hopefully even break down the boundaries between familiarity and possibility. If anything, the work asks one to consider where we come from, where we are now, and how what we think can alter our own perceptions.

His abstract paintings, with their highly granulated texture and color combinations only achieved through such a medium, reconsider our relationship with “primitive” art by elevating the iconography of ancient languages, science, religions, and mysticism. Born in McKeesport, Pennsylvania, Landström studied fine art by invitation at Carnegie-Mellon University. He later continued his fine art education at the School of the Museum of Fine Arts at Tufts University in Boston, Massachusetts. Landström also earned a Bachelor and Master of Science Degree in Electrical Engineering. Landström’s work has exhibited extensively and can be found in public, private, and corporate collections around the world. When not in the studio, Landström spends considerable time abroad and has visited archeological sites across the world, which inform his studies and artistic practice. Landström currently lives and works in Atlanta, Georgia, United States of America.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Exploring my family’s story then and now is a journey I started some 5 years ago that I tell through my paintings. Our conversations and research give me a glimpse of their past. My observations reveal a present that, while it still holds traditions, reveals a people that have persevered and transformed despite changes. Loss and rebirth have been a part of our culture. Slavery, colonialism, or the displacement by the US Navy of my family from the island of Vieques, Puerto Rico, have only served as catalysts for the evolution of who we are today. The decision to choose a specific portrait is never truly planned. I spend my days, camera ready, waiting for that moment when my subject is engrossed in a task or looking away. What are they thinking? It is as if they are looking at an old reel of all that was and how it has all come full circle as in “The Fisherman”. The moments are intimate as they carry on tasks that have been learned for generations as in “Making Johnny Cakes”. In “Grief”, a woman had just experienced the loss of her son. I captured this moment as she moved towards the door and turned to look at the empty table, lost in thought. The soft light coming from the open entrance seemed to highlight her mood as she turned from light into darkness. In the end, I aim to capture a mood in the moment, and I am always seeking the light as in “A Day At The Creek” where the sun is reflected in the child’s face revealing a quiet yet playful moment as the child explores the movement of water with her fingers. In every painting, I aim to create an atmosphere that the viewer can connect with through placement, color, and light. A study sketch is important to establish placement and to make a final decision to see if it is paint worthy. Afterwards, a value wash to establish light then light layers, before committing to a full painting. It is always a journey of learning and discovery and connection. The painting experience becomes personal when I connect with the emotions on the subject. With every brush stroke, I smile when they smile; I grieve when they grieve. Then I know, I am on the right track.

column25: I am a self-taught artist and native of St. Croix, US Virgin Islands currently residing in Louisville, Kentucky. I developed an interest in the arts while observing my father who was a self-taught artist and sign maker. After 6 years in the US Army and 25 years as an autoworker, I decided to retire and pursue the arts. In 2021, I had the opportunity to do my first residency and exhibit “From St. Croix to the Southend” which explored my culture and my exploration of nature during my residency. In 2023 I was part of the group “The Black Experience” with the Kentucky Center for African American Heritage, the “Emancipation 175” Exhibit at Cane Roots Art Gallery in St. Croix, USVI which explored our evolution after emancipation, and “Afloramos ““We Flourish” , expressions of our souls, at The Speed Art Museum. All of these exhibits explore the everyday lives of my people through portrait art. Most recently, I was accepted as part of the Harrison County Arts Annual Exhibit, celebrating the unique culture of the Ohio Valley. I work out of my home studio in Louisville, Kentucky and in St. Croix during my extended visits. Oil is my medium of choice with an exploration of watercolors. Many of my portrait works are inspired from the everyday life of my family and friends who are in St. Croix, through research, conversations and observations.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
“Painting is just another way of keeping a diary.”- Picasso For most of my life, I battled with my mental health and did not know how to identify what I was experiencing. As a child, I was often criticized and discouraged from expressing raw emotions, suppressing my fear and depression. Embarrassingly, I did not understand what the word “anxiety” meant until college; and that is also when I discovered I had social anxiety disorder which became the conceptual grounding for my work. Painting allows me to authentically reflect on my painful story while simultaneously building self-empowerment and triumph. As a woman of color, I feel an obligation to dismantle the stigma of mental health and to help start honest dialogues about the childhood contributions that are normalized in the black community through my artwork.

column25: My work is built on a lifetime of observations driven by love, pride, and a desire to write my own story into history through my works. In addition to my culture, my desire to communicate remains at the heart of my artistic practice. I am constantly looking for ways to express ideas, reflections, and messages. In using installation and videopoetry I began to push the boundaries of traditional poetry, as it seemed natural to experiment with the interplay of imagery and text since both mediums are pivotal within communication. I am interested in the interchangeability of imagery and text to fill the gaps of communication. In using videopoetry as a medium, the novelty of the genre leaves space for experimentation with poetic devices and cinematic technique. In my project “Close Spaces” I develop visual vernacular for African-American vernacular English (AAVE)- which I was raised hearing, speaking, and still use to this day in my work. AAVE is a dialect with its own grammatical and syntactical rules that emerged from enslaved Africans’ attempts to fit the structure of their native languages into English. In “Close Spaces” I focus on two hallmarks from AAVE- the perpetual use of “be” and the continued rapport-building use of AAVE among members in the African American community. The use of “be” within AAVE to indicate the presence of something throughout time is represented in the film by vignetted clips overlaid on a larger scene. Just as the past, present, and future make up the collective entity of time; the hands, eyes, and mouth contribute to the larger entity of the subject. The poetry spoken in the piece reaffirms “we be close” to say that we have, are, and will be connected with one another. Additionally, I filmed as much of the footage as possible with tight lenses to get very intimate, personal shots translating the intentional communal use of AAVE within the Black community. I experimented with ‘trimming the fat’ of scene establishment in traditional filmmaking to get to what I really wanted to communicate- the relationship between closeness and wellbeing. AAVE is also highlighted in the refrain used in the 'Royaltee' series. The first iteration of the project included three stylized tall tees, or oversized t-shirts common in Black hip hop culture in the 2000s, of varying materials (as seen in the videopoem). The shirts feature the first three words of the rap lyric 'they don't know who we be' as a commentary on the separation and appropriation of Black culture in the fashion industry. The second iteration of the project featured a photo series and videopoem reconnecting the tall tee with the Black bodies that were often viewed with bias while wearing them; using the natural landscape to emphasize their beauty, belonging, and humanity. In the videopoem we see figures among the flora of the Pacific Northwest, reflecting the gentle flow of the elements around them in attire birthed of their culture and journey. Just as the writings of Zora Neale Hurston introduced the wider public to the vernacular of African-American culture, I am inspired to establish a visual lexicon composed similarly to Black dialect that can expand the realm of videopoetry.

column25: Years ago, I began making work using imagery from demolition sites. At first, I was interested in the complexity and variety of the forms found there- twisted metal, piles of concrete, wire fencing... I began to think about the life of these objects and how they existed at the end of a timeline (demolition is ultimately their end), and I wondered what the other points along the timeline might look like. Through this work, I envision a world that is constantly constructed and then broken down. Construction and destruction may happen simultaneously, and sometimes natural forms take over and are the “winners” of the unfolding drama. Each drawing or sculpture represents a different season or moment within the world. I also make installations, and because I don’t always have the resources to work large scale, I make “dimensional drawings,” which I think of as small installations. These works are meant to feel like immersive spaces for the viewer, despite their diminutive size. My visual style is rooted in eighties pop culture, referring to brightly colored cartoons and digital collage techniques that were hallmarks of music videos from that decade. These things shaped my inner bank of imagery, and they are evident in the world I build within my work. I use nonrealistic spatial perspective, intricate patterns, and layers of media to create an intentional sense of intensity, mystery, and controlled chaos.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work as a figurative sculptor is an exploration of self-portraiture and the subversion of domestic objects, rooted in the concept of queering. Through the medium of clay, I seek to challenge conventional notions of identity, gender, and the domestic space, inviting viewers to question and expand their understanding of these concepts. Self-portraiture serves as a central element in my practice, allowing me to excavate and interpret the layers of my own identity. I employ the malleability of clay to mold and shape forms that mirror my own body, but these sculptures are not mere reflections; they are complex, multifaceted representations that reflect the fluidity of identity. Each piece is a self-portrait in flux, capturing moments of vulnerability, strength, and transformation. In my exploration of queering domestic objects, I dismantle and reimagine everyday household items, imbuing them with new narratives and possibilities. These objects, traditionally associated with heteronormative roles and expectations, become the canvas for my artistic reinterpretation. By queering these objects, I challenge societal norms and invite viewers to reconsider the constraints placed upon our identities and domestic lives. My work blurs the boundaries between art and craft, as I embrace the tactile and intimate nature of ceramics. The process of working with clay is inherently physical and sensual, mirroring the embodied experiences that shape our identities. Each piece carries the imprint of my hands and the stories they tell, inviting viewers to connect with the materiality of the sculptures. Through my figurative ceramic sculptures, I aim to foster a dialogue about the fluidity and diversity of human identity, as well as the potential for transformation within the domestic realm. By queering these objects and engaging with self-portraiture, I hope to open up new avenues for exploration and understanding, ultimately challenging the norms that shape our lives and homes. My art invites you to question, to reflect, and to celebrate the beautiful complexities of being human.

column25: I have a responsibility to convey pain, freedom, joy, confusion, and healing through my art. My work is driven broadly by my own mental health, and more specifically by a desire to expose the masks I've worn, connection with people and nature, and the complex existence that is life. My process typically begins with a feeling that I’m unable to convey with words. Art allows me to put that feeling on canvas in order to unwrap and untangle the complicated and intertwined basic emotions from their underlying sophisticated counterparts. As my drawings and paintings begin to take form, it is there I begin to develop and create meaning and a new understanding of my initial emotion. My goal as an artist is to inspire others to create as a means toward healing. Speaking loudly and unapologetically about my struggles with mental health is of paramount importance to me. I create pieces that not only show my own healing process, but that also show the intention I have for my future self.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my creative practice, I weave together my identity as a non-binary artist and my deep-rooted connection to the natural world. My art extends from my sense of wonder and awe for the intricacies of nature. Through painting and ceramics, I convey the beauty of natural phenomena, the complexity of ecosystems, and the brilliance of the many creatures that inhabit our planet. I portray the travels of a self-representing, genderless character through my own natural universe. The absence of a specific gender emphasizes the universality of the human connection to the natural world, transcending societal norms and boundaries. Through a digital lens, I examine the existence of specific animals and the landscapes they inhabit, fostering a unique connection even when physical proximity is unattainable. When the opportunity for in-person interaction is limited, I turn to my vivid imagination. I depict vibrant, invented worlds that allow me to engage with these creatures beyond the limitations of reality. I foster a sense of kinship between humanity and the natural world, illuminating the profound beauty that can be found in the intimate connection between ourselves and our imaginations.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I’m a visual artist, born and raised in Argentina of Indigenous-Italian ancestry. I work across multiple disciplines such as textiles, installation and social practice. Using strategies drawn from the fields of education, research and community activism, I invite participation and collective imagining. My featured project explores labor, memory and migration via textile works, while portraying immigrants working as food vendors and domestic workers. I asked my subjects to send me photos of themselves at their place of work. I created embroidery works based on those images. Community members are not just involved in the project, but they are co-designers and co-creators of the project. Due to their legal status, participating workers in this project decided not to reveal their identities. The medium of embroidery allows me to acknowledge the contributions of the immigrant workers I portray while at the same time conceal and protect their identities. My embroidery works are made on fabric used for the Dept of Homeland Security Employee uniforms. The same people who try to deport these immigrant workers. Embroidery also allows me to include the concept of repetition often found in manual labor, addressing notions of visibility related to immigrant labor in the U.S. In this time of exclusion and displacement, I seek to bring attention to histories at risk of erasure while envisioning a more tolerant, connected and interdependent future.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
New York City has been my home since 2008. Upon first navigating the city, I found my calibration through music, art, activism, and the communities that surround them. I volunteered at the EFA Robert Blackburn Printmaking Workshop, lived and organized in communal, activist punk spaces in Brooklyn, and volunteered with neighborhood mutual aid organizations such as Ridgewood Tenants Union and Club A Food Bank. In all of these spaces, I tried to create overlap aiming for a larger, more diverse, flexible community. For example, in 2015 I began to use my living space to host benefit events for mutual aid groups, such as Make the Road New York. As part of the hosting effort, I used my access to the print shop to create unique posters for the gatherings. This ongoing effort and overlapping sensibility worked to not only hold space for collective meetings but also use visual art as a tool beyond a market-driven system. My favorite thing about being an artist is that I am unable to separate it from anything else in my life. It changes the way I interact with the world. I am always eager to talk about ideas and perspectives with people I am meeting for the first time. I want to learn and share without limits. The most important tool I carry with me as an artist is insatiable curiosity. I am always looking for ways to build a non-hierarchal community across all demographics and art is a way to do that.

column25: In my practice, I challenge and explore hierarchies of matter- deploying collection, sorting, and performance to build ephemeral installations. By making site-responsive assemblage sculptures I aim to investigate connections between people, public space, time, and value. I transform and integrate found objects into precarious new systems that explore a larger synthesis of New York City’s on-the-ground ecology which invites viewers to consider capitalism and its byproducts, as well as the creation of identity in public space on the individual, neighborhood, and city level. When crafting installations, I'm considering not only how public space informs scales of identity but also how resilience and play are methods to connect those identities with local history and a site's material realities. I use vernacular art forms inspired by the energy and urgency of activism to provide a lens to ponder fragility, displacement, transformation, and cycles of renewal. Assemblage can challenge the unspoken subjectivity of a traditional archive. Plastics and natural materials degrade and accumulate at different rates in different places- I aim for my sculptures to show the layered temporalities that happen at street level.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is autobiographical in many aspects and merges dreamscapes with lived experience through painting, drawing, and printmaking. I encapsulate several details or cryptic messages suggestive of a narrative outside the frame with representational allegories. My process is the extension of exploring realms outside of trauma and darkness which I often perceive as blackness stretched out to infinity in all directions. Darkness is my zero point in a vast coordinate system where I start out to investigate new compositions and possibilities through whimsical and morbid imagery that absorbs the immersive nature of trauma. It’s the tension between being submerged and setting out from a place of darkness to find the lexicon of my consciousness that keeps my creative urges in motion. This push and pull often draws from my Chinese-Turkish heritage, which is the root of my interest in depicting hybridity and in-betweenness while projecting my inner world onto the vessels of objects and animals. Using this imagery, I depict stories that are grounded in reality but evolve into other realms. Deep down, I sense that my experience of reality is ambiguously layered, with past lives, dissociation, and surreality, which prompts me to investigate how I can distort reality or explore something beyond reality in my art.

column25: Jennifer Ifil-Ryan is a New York based multidisciplinary artist, arts educator, and systems disruptor. Grounded in community, Jen’s two decades of socially engaged work is activated through the transference of creative agency through artistic expression. Working largely within schools and cultural spaces, Jen has produced large scale participatory arts initiatives that have touched over 200,000 students. Further broadening the dissemination of creative power, Jen has produced social interventions such as the #INEED Project, engaging over 3,000 intergenerational participants in a visual dialogue highlighting collective care, compassion and generosity; Love Letters to the People Series featuring over 1,000 paintings that affirm belonging and acceptance. These projects have been aptly positioned in communal spaces such as Montefiore and Washington Square Park, Columbus Circle, Harvard school of the Arts, and the NYC MTA subway system. Driven by the pursuit of collective well being, Jen has led transformative structural changes through her work at The High Line, The Sugar Hill Children's Museum of Art & Storytelling, the Queens Museum, and Development Without Limits. In tandem with her resolution for social-engagement and cultural connectivity, Jen’s own body of work cerebrates intersectional identities as in relation to race and queerness. The materiality of her work challenges notions of socially imposed standards of worth. Jen’s work is an inquiry and practice of unlearning and reconstruction of self as a form of healing.

These scenes are imbued with a profound narrative, prompting viewers to ponder the passage of time and the indomitable force of the natural world. A melancholy song repeats perfectly as we navigate these liminal spaces, we are confronted with the poignant question; What will remain when humanity is but a memory?

column25: My body of work encompasses large-scale drawing to explore the intricacies of our experiences and perceptions of the human body. In my current artistic exploration, I am delving into themes of body dysmorphia, identity, and the concept of self. These profound topics are intended to serve as a catalyst for meaningful discussions and connections through shared experiences. Within my portfolio, you will find expansive charcoal drawings of nudes on paper, ranging from 7 to 10 feet in size. My drawing technique is centered on instilling a sense of movement and energy in each piece, achieved through a fusion of surrealism and realism. I am committed to creating art that invites new modes of engagement, often encouraging interaction and providing a pathway for the audience to navigate through the artistic narrative. My ultimate goal is to prompt the audience to contemplate their own bodies within the context of their life's narrative, considering how this self-awareness influences their interactions with others and contributes to their overall quality of life. Through my work, I aim to illuminate the human form as both a prison and a vessel of creation, the origin from which all experiences emanate.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Inspired by dark matter, this series exists as an inspired interpretation of the interaction between dark matter and 'normal' baryonic matter as viewed from a bulk in which both are visible. Through exposing stacks of ceramic magnets to iron filings and manipulating the resulting structure into unique shapes to be photographed with a high resolution camera system, I imagine dark matter clumping and weighing down the visible, structured matter we normally see, giving it greater mass than what is observed. Now in its third iteration, Dark Matter is beginning to transcend its original purpose. The sculpture’s ambiguous scale sometimes illustrates itself as massive celestial space stations, suspended in nebulae. When viewed at their intended size, I recognize a similarity to images created by electron microscope and imagine the structures as odd, microscopic life forms having evolved from a completely separate evolutionary tree . Whatever thoughts come to mind from viewing these images, for me they represent a celebration of the knowable and unknowable forms of nature and their ultimate ability to pluck at the strings of human curiosity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
There are so many wonderful, fulfilling aspects of being an artist. One of the best things about being an artist happens when I take my work out of the studio and into the world. My art practice is very solitary - I have a private art studio, and I make art for myself. My studio time is a peaceful, weekly solace where I can make, reflect, experiment, and play. Because the work I make is personal and semi-autobiographic, it always takes me by pleasant surprise when others connect with me at openings or on social media and share that my work speaks to them. I’ve had conversations with people at openings about the soft tummies and natural-looking breasts in my paintings and long discussions about growing up, reflection, and memory based on my recent risographs. There’s always a bit of trepidation with releasing my work into the world. Questions like “will anyone else understand what I’m trying to say?” or “will viewers make unfair assumptions about me based on my work?” always simmer under the surface before openings. The trust that a viewer will see something made intimately and honestly and recognize that always prevails. It’s a privilege that things I’ve created and brought into the world spark conversation about our common human experience. At the end of the day, I think so much of being a person is tied to a need to connect and belong. Being able to explore connection and make community with my work is the gift of a lifetime.

column25: My paintings and risograph prints, while not necessarily self-portraits, are semi-autobiographic visual examinations of and reflections on my experiences. My current body of work explores memory, reflection, self-forgiveness, and self-compassion. The figures in my work, representing my past and present self, touch and interact, melting in and out of each other. The figures can be amorphous, and it can be hard to discern where one figure starts and the other ends. Sometimes, the figures are akin to children hiding in a blanket fort, telling stories about the past and sharing their hopes for the future. I utilize repeating figures to represent memory, the different versions of oneself, and the potential for growth and change. Each of my pieces exist as moments in time that are part of a larger narrative, like panels in a comic. I seek to imbue still images with a sense of action, as if they are moments in an overarching story. The softness of the human body is critical to my work; in addition to making art, I am also a competitive powerlifter. The love and care I show to my body in the gym and the representation of the human body in my work is not dissimilar. In my risographs, the repeating arch shaped backgrounds echo stained glass church windows, furthering the veneration and respect I aim to pay the body in my work. My work also embraces the visual language and bright color palette of graphic novels and vintage commercial illustration. I have an inherent interest in bridging the gap between traditional painting techniques and the colors and styles seen in popular forms of visual media. Risograph printing, with its combined digital and analogue process and inherently retro spectrum of inks, has allowed me to further explore this visual language and pay homage to forms of art that can be considered “less than” or “low brow.”

column25: Artist Statement My work is about retelling history, bringing back phenomenal people and places that have been misplaced, forgotten or unknown. These paintings are portals that create metaphysical realms where every thought and action is balanced by it’s counterpart. Within this infinite expanse I've created a space where directed energy and the immeasurable meet analytical thinking. Each artwork is brought to life through bold flowing lines with luminous color. Becoming one with the fractal allows me to transcend the 2D space where I can expand, retract and exist. Once connected with thought and energy I begin to harness the feeling. My actions of applying acrylic paint create energy around me as I shift and circle the canvas rhythmically. I bring to visual life the internal fight and harmony of Yin and Yang. I am interested in awakening cognitive processes, harnessing creative energy and expanding the limits of universal thinking.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I took my first dance class when I was three years old, then spent the next 15 years in that studio, working towards my dream of being a dancer. However, as soon as I started puberty, the biggest feedback I’d get from any dance teacher was on my looks, not my technique. I was told I was too fat, too curvy, too short, too masculine, had too large of features, etc.. At auditions, I would be released by my headshot alone, before they’d even see me dance. This all got me wondering, who gets to decide what makes someone a dancer? I’ve been exploring this question since 2020, which has lead me to my current proposed project. The paintings I've submitted are part of a series entitled Proof of Dance. This is a multidisciplinary performance and research project. I plan to use liberatory pedagogy with a group of ‘non-dancers’ to create both a new dance work and a piece of visual art. Since the earliest stages of my career, there has been an evolution of my work which has led to this project. This work began with a short play I wrote during the pandemic, Touch, exploring the idea and limitations of physical touch. As a sexual health educator, this theme is one that has always been of deep interest and importance to me. The play used dance to explore the theme. In 2021, I built on this play, turning it into a workshop for ‘non-dancers’ to explore how all humans are inherently dancers. I gathered participants in a public park to explore how touch lived in the world at that time. Participants completed a series of activities to connect with their bodies & the theme, and then devised a short dance exploring how physical touch changed in relationships over the course of the pandemic. I used this workshop as a case study for my research around a liberatory dance pedagogy by writing an essay analyzing pre-existing liberatory dance pedagogies. Inspired by that workshop, I’m currently seeking to use a combination of visual art and dance to advance the perception that all dance is art, and all people are inherently dancers. I’ve gathered a group of dancers, trained to various degrees in multiple styles, to complete paintings achieved by moving their bodies on canvases. The dancers all consider themselves outside of what the professional world defines as ‘dancer,’ whether this be because of body type, race, professional training, or years/types of experience. The paintings are a way to translate dance to visual art so that we can reconsider the perception of the dancer - by moving in the paint on the canvas, they use movement as a tangible modality of art. With this grant, I am now hoping to take this project to its fullest potential. The proposed project will recruit people who do not consider themselves to be dancers, but are interested in dance. Reincorporating the devising process, we will work together to devise a dance to be shared with the public. The dance will take place on a large canvas upon which the dancers will paint as they perform, so that by the end of the piece we have created both a premiere dance work and a piece of visual art representing it. The event will include a gallery show of the current dance paintings I am working on, as a culmination of the work I have been doing. The event, including the gallery show, the dance piece, and the live painting, all seek to answer the question, what makes movement ‘dance?’ The goal of the project is not to teach the participants how to dance, but rather, we will explore the inherent movement that exists within their bodies already, and put this together into a dance. The money would be used to provide all dancers with a stipend, as well as technicians, the project producer, and myself. Money would also go towards space rental, materials purchasing including things like paint, costumes, light & sound equipment, and multiple canvases. We also would have an advertising and outreach budget.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My indoor and outdoor sculpture installations start with making the paper by hand using local waste plant materials (leaves and bark) and plant fibers (from old blue jeans and sheet pulps). I also use seeds for wildflowers or to feed the birds in the pulp to produce continually evolving artworks that transform with nature as a partner. In my recent “Living Quilts” outdoor installations, the handmade paper dissolves over time becoming compost to nourish the earth, and the seeds sprout and grow to produce food and habitat for wildlife as well as aesthetic experiences for humans bringing us closer to the wonders of nature. The structures I create, such as the nests or the beds with a headboard and footboard on which the “living quilt” is placed, are made of wood and branches and last well for many years, until they too dissolve as compost to nourish the earth. These works comment on our vain hope for permanency in art and our desire to conquer nature rather than exist in harmony with the earth. With my latest projects focused on water and climate change, I hope to raise awareness about our dire circumstances, with too little water and too much water in different places at different times, and project optimism for a better future with seeds that depend on water like all life to sprout and grow into new life. I also focus on rivers and other waterways that connect and conduct life like the veins and arteries in our bodies. Mapping the lines of rivers and waterways with paper and threads is a way of understanding the path of water through our lives.

column25: Jane Ingram Allen is a visual artist who creates environmental and public art projects around the world using natural materials and sustainable processes to raise public awareness about environmental and social issues and increase our understanding and appreciation of nature and the environment. Jane was born and raised in Alabama (Delta, Clay County, 1940), and is now based in Santa Rosa, California (since 2012). After graduating with a BA degree in art (1961) and Master’s degree in art education (1970), she began her art career doing mixed-media paintings, later shifting to fiber arts and then to hand papermaking art. Her present work is a unique combination reflecting her prior experiences in all of these media with a focus on environmental art and mixed media sculpture installations. Jane has lived and worked in many communities in the USA as well as internationally during her long career as a professional artist, art teacher/professor, curator and art writer. Moving to New York State in 1989 greatly expanded her art career opportunities, and she had her first solo show in NYC in 1990 (C & A Gallery) and a public art installation in a NYC subway station for the Creative Stations program in 1992. In 1996 she began doing international art residencies and art installations in other countries as well as continuing her career in the US. Jane lived and worked in Taiwan from 2004- 2012, for two years as a Fulbright Scholar Artist in 2003-2004 and 2004-2005 and then staying in Taiwan for six more years working as an independent artist at various universities and organizations throughout Taiwan to make her art with handmade paper from local materials and create site-specific installations having an environmental focus using handmade paper and other natural materials. As well as producing and exhibiting her own artworks in Taiwan, Jane founded and curates international environmental art projects bringing artists from all countries together with local communities for new perspectives on local/global environmental problems and the creation of environmental art installations. For example, Jane initiated the Guandu International Outdoor Sculpture Festival in 2006, the Cheng Long Wetlands International Environmental Art Project in 2010, and the Keelung International Marine Environment Art Project in 2015. Jane continues to do art projects in Taiwan and returns there usually for 2 or 3 months each year. As well as the Fulbright grants to Taiwan in 2003-04 and 2004-05, Jane received a Fulbright Specialist grant in November-December 2015 for an art project and exhibition in Turkey at Ege University’s Museum of Book and Paper Arts, Izmir, Turkey. Jane also received an invitation in 2023 for an artist in residency project in Costa Rica and an invitation for an exhibition at Live Forever Foundation, Taichung, Taiwan as well as a solo exhibition in Mountain View, CA, at Mountain View Center for the Performing Arts Gallery. Jane has also received several awards for artist-in-residency projects in the USA (Sitka Center for Art & Ecology, Otis, Oregon, 10/2014-01/2015 and Scholar in Residence, Laurel School, Shaker Heights, Ohio, 9/2006) among others. She has also received many grants for international artist-in residency projects such as for the Philippines (Duntog Foundation, 1/1996), Japan (Mino Paper Art Village Project 09/1999 – 01/2000), Nepal (Nepal Crafts Collection, 1/2002), Brazil (Sacatar Foundation Fellow, 10/2001 – 1/2002), Tanzania (Warm Heart Art Tanzania with the Lighton International Artist Exchange Grant 1/2009), Indonesia (Bali Purnati Center for the Arts 1/2011 and 5/2016), and China (Liajiang Project 1/2010). Jane was a full-time art teacher at the secondary level when first starting her art career, and she continued to teach art part-time at the university level for many years as well as working part-time as an art museum curator and educator at institutions in Florida, Georgia, California, Louisiana, Iowa and New York. Most recently Jane works as an independent artist and occasionally teaches short-term workshops and classes for various institutions around the world. She has focused her art career on environmental or eco-art installations that use local natural materials and change over time to raise public awareness about such environmental issues as global warming, pollution of water, air and soil and biodiversity loss.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artwork, the images chosen for context express a specific themed story. These stories are shaped with humor, sarcasm, and a positive representation of the strength women hold in society. Through my praxis, I am learning that feelings, circumstances, and histories, personal and collective, real or imagined - shape each painting. From these organic origins, the painting is visually a descriptive narrative of my thoughts and expressions on society. I use my work to sort out my feelings and thoughts about the complexities and enduring mark of structural sexism in the United States and its impact on my life. Currently, my collage work focuses on the history of how sexualization of women has been used in advertising. Women are primarily the main subject I use in a right and left purpose communicating the duality of private and public personas. The intent is to recapture, recognize, and recreate the strength found from being a woman. The background color is collected from magazine clips and acrylic paints, communicate through color, raw and irregular mark-making, symbology (real and imagined), and embellishments, what these spaces feel like. Using materials sourced from historical archives, vintage and contemporary magazines and newspapers, I cut and compose mostly intimate-sized narrative collages. The resulting work ranges in scale from intimate 4”x6”to a larger scale of 36”x48.

column25: Cristina Sayers is a visually inspired intuitive Collage artist. She holds an authentic eye and distinct vision in her selected medium. Her collage art origins is from handmade, old-fashioned cut and paste collage. She makes this differentiation because so much collage work, on the internet, is composed digitally. Influenced surf culture, historical images,humor, racial equality, recycling, elements of nature, sea, and satire are common elements throughout her work. Subjects and images are selected by a visceral attraction and are what Cristina finds appealing in a visual perspective. The existence of these collages are found and carefully curated photographs taken in her home town of Santa Cruz, California and reimagined photographs from vintage publications. Born and raised “among the waves”in Santa Cruz, Ca. Cristina is a child of the 70's. Growing up in what is known historically as the Salad Days of High Stoke Surf Cultural. Cristina gave birth and has raised her daughter Jessica, in 1984, since the age of 14. She takes pride in how she was able to successfully raise and college educate her daughter. Cristina and Jessica spent quality time together through the art projects they created to enhance their home. Although difficult, they have lived independently and have been able to support themselves through hard work. Along the way Cristina discovered and used art to escape from life’s hardships and unhealthy childhood. Finding comfort with her daughter in coloring books, home projects, sketching, or pen & ink. Art allowed her to escape and center herself emotionally. Cristina has been joyously married to Johnny since 1996. They have added 3 more children in 1999-2002 extending her children count from 1 to 4. She spent time during her children’s daily 3 hour naps doing laundry and art. Over the last 15 years Cristina has accomplished minor success in her hometown. Her work is distinctively recognized and sought out by collectors at events. She has participated and selected by jury, Santa Cruz Open Studios Art Tour 2013-2023, local Gallery Exhibits, and Art Market festivals such as California premier annual Capitola Art & Wine Festival, 2016-2023. Cristina is encouraged and supported by her immediate family to pursue her desire to and love of art. She is a Mother of 4 children ages 20-40, Grandmother of 3, avid animal lover, dog walker, sand & sea admirer, and supporter/volunteer at Santa Cruz Animal Shelter. Her days are filled with family, walks with her service dog Lenny, Uno, shits-n-giggles, satire, & of course making art.

column25: I am drawn to storytelling in all its forms because I believe in the ability for art to be a force for good - to challenge mainstream narratives, and provide a new sense of freedom. I believe that art has the ability to change lives for the better. I am passionate about social justice and storytelling and have spent the last ten years working to intertwine the two in an artful and impactful way; My experience in documentary making has been spent supporting projects that accomplish just that, be it from the stories about formerly incarcerated people (LIFE & LIFE) to those suffering in silence (END GAME). Representation in media is foundational to how we as humans see ourselves and others around us, and how we perceive the world. With PARASTOO, I aim to create impact by shifting harmful paradigms about refugees into more positive and truthful ones. I seek to reach refugee audiences who might never have seen themselves in a positive and relatable light on screen and to push back on the wider societal narrative where refugees are solely seen for their refugee status. I also seek to disrupt the homogeneity of the stories we tell and who tells them. As a Black woman I have a unique perspective that has been systematically ignored. I have developed a personal mission to uplift and share stories that shine a light on those most marginalized, those voices most unheard, and to stand in power alongside them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist I want to provoke thought, educate, and create change. Although many might consider me to be a multi-disciplinary artist my roots are in printmaking which currently takes the majority of my creative energy. I am drawn to woodblock printing the majority of the time because I am drawn to the natural surface quality and characteristics of the wood. The work I create tells the story about some of the aspects of black identity and how we are perceived and treated socially both presently and historically. I have always had a great sensitivity toward social and cultural issues, even as a very young artist. Within the broad terms of social and cultural issue my work is focused on iconoclasm, identity and activism from the viewpoint of a black American female. As I seek to complete work that deconstructs existing stereotypes relative to black culture and representation of the black experience from my own perspective and experiences, I often use the self-portrait as a representation of the synonymous experience or reaction to what is happening in society. I am the artist acting as observer and documentarian. My overarching goal in addition to educating or being thought provoking is reclamation or reasserting the right to our history and culture that has been systematically undermined. I enjoy using the tools allegory and symbolism to aid me in achieving these goals in my artwork.
