column25: This series has been a delightful departure from my usual camera work. It began as response to a group challenge to begin a morning studio practice. A fascination with interior design magazines combined with a set of cherished Audubon illustrations by Athos Menaboni were my initial sources. Each collage started with formal concerns (colors and shapes) and in the process of filling the frame, a thematic concept would emerge and become the focus. Themes of a post pandemic world and the ongoing horrors of climate change are present. Thoughtfully placed models from fashion magazines made their way into the subject matter. Some in the forefront and some hidden. Camouflaged by nature (organized and wild) people and birds share habitats. The brief descriptions of the bird species informed the subject matter at times. Some of the fabricated worlds are utopian, others dystopian. Most of the works were created in early morning with coffee and the news of the day. July and August were particularly brutal months with reports of fires burning in Canada and elsewhere. My psyche filled with fear and anxiety, and appearing in “The Sun is a Memory”, where burning embers are tucked in structures. Some days coffee and classical music filled my studio when I needed a break from the real world and my collage would take on a hopeful tone. “Tomorrow and Tomorrow and Tomorrow” depicts an abundant supply of fruit and vegetables with plastic homes floating above the crops. Similar to one’s own limited capacity for constant dread, I found the collages allowed me the chance to solve problems in a small and intimate space, giving me a fresh perspective, one image at a time.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I experiment with the traditional craft of quilting by using clothes, linens, and other found material given to me by family and friends. The history and experiences of the three generations of my family who once immigrated from southern China to the Mississippi Delta are worn in these unique, threadbare fabrics. In addition to incorporating and reforming memory and history, using leftover materials is a direct response to the disparities in access vs excess in today's world. It also involves learning to work with what I have rather than choose what I want. Memphis, Tennessee is where I was born and currently reside. I received my degree from Memphis College of Art in 1994. I have been continuously creating art at home while raising a family and having various odd jobs. In 2004 I decided to become a nurse. I am an ICU RN and have worked at Regional One, Landstuhl Regional Medical Center in Germany, and currently at the VA. My art has been profoundly affected by the knowledge and expertise I've received from working as a nurse and spending five years in Europe. My flat wall hanging quilts have evolved into organic sculptures inspired by the biological nature of texture, shape, and form. My intent is to honor the precious material and meticulous process of the handcrafted while engaging the audience in themes of the past and present, permanence and temporary, growth and decay, life and death.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
IT COULD HAVE HAD SOMETHING TO DO WITH MY GRANDFATHER BEING AN AVID COLLECTOR OF THE ECCENTRIC, BUT I HAVE ALWAYS BEEN DRAWN TO FOUND MATERIALS AND THEIR ABILITY TO HAVE A CONVERSATION IN THE REALM OF THE ABSTRACT. I AM SIMULTANEOUSLY INFLUENCED BY THE POINT WHERE ABSURDISM AND NIHILISM INTERSECT. TO ME, THERE IS NO SINGULAR MEANING TO LIFE. THE FRUSTRATIONS THIS CONVENTIONALLY PRODUCES INSTEAD SERVE AS ENCOURAGEMENT TO EMBRACE THE UNCERTAIN, THE UNFAMILIAR, AND THE INCOMPREHENSIBLE. I AM DRAWN TO CHAOS, UNCONTROLLABILITY, CONTRADICTION, AND THE NEVER-STAGNANT STATE OF MEANING-MAKING. I BELIEVE THAT WE MAKE MEANING FLUIDLY AND THAT THE WAY WE ASCRIBE SIGNIFICANCE AND VALUE TO AN OBJECT DEPENDS ON OUR RELATIONSHIP WITH WHAT WE PERCEIVE AS REALITY; THIS RELATIONSHIP IS TRANSIENT AND, AT BEST, CONSTANTLY IN A STATE OF TRANSFORMATION. I SEE OBJECTS AS PLACEHOLDERS IN THE SEARCH FOR MEANING. THEY ARE SOMETHING CONCRETE THAT WE CAN CONFIDE SENTIMENTALITY INTO FOR A BRIEF MOMENT. MY WORK QUESTIONS WHAT HAPPENS WHEN THESE OBJECTS BECOME UNFAMILIAR, WHEN THEY TRANSFORM OR VANISH MORE DRASTICALLY THAN WOULD OTHERWISE BE ANTICIPATED. I AM SIMULTANEOUSLY VISUALIZING THE WAY OUR MEMORIES DETERIORATE AND BRINGING THIS SENSE OF TRANSIENCE INTO THE OTHERWISE SACRED SPACE OF SENTIMENTALITY. MY WORK IS AN ACTIVE CONVERSATION WITH THESE OBJECTS AND THE INFLUENCE THAT THEIR TRANSITION INTO AN UNKNOWN/UNKNOWABLE STATE HAS ON THE SENTIMENTS WE ATTACH TO THEM, AND BY EXTENSION, OUR UNDERSTANDING OF OURSELVES AND WHAT WE CLAIM TO REMEMBER. I QUESTION WHAT HAPPENS WHEN THE OBJECT IS INHERENTLY UNFAMILIAR TO THE VIEWER OR WHEN IT IS COMPLETELY LOST IN THE PROCESS. CAST-METAL FUNCTIONS AS A DISFIGURING AND RE-IMAGINATIVE CLOAK FOR THESE OBJECTS. THE PROCESS OF CASTING REMOVES SOME OF THE CONTROL I WOULD OTHERWISE HAVE WITH THE MATERIALS IN USE, ECHOING THE UNCERTAINTY AND CHAOS OF LIFE. MUCH OF MY RECENT WORK RELATES TO MY IDENTITY AS A NONBINARY TRANS-MASCULINE INDIVIDUAL AND CARRIES UNDERTONES OF RESISTANCE, LIBERATION, AND PROTEST. THIS OCCUPIES A CONTEMPORARY CONTEXT OF RESISTANCE THROUGH VISIBILITY, SELF ACTUALIZATION AND SELF DEFINITION. IN THIS CONVERSATION, MY STARTING MATERIALS ARE ARTIFACTS FROM MY TRANSITION.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My artwork, primarily prints, artists’ books, and mixed media pieces is grounded in personal and cultural narrative, seeking to illuminate contemporary issues, cultural traditions, and the stories of people affected by them. I take inspiration from my family history and my mentor teachers. I coined the term, artstories, to describe the process of combining written and visual narratives into artworks representative of a multiplicity of generational voices on issues related to coming of age within a multicultural society. My fascination with narrative forms of expression is one reason artists’ books and printmaking are my preferred mediums of creative expression. My process is a mix of relief printmaking, letterpress, image and text transfers, gelatin prints, collage, found objects, and decorative paper techniques.

column25: The natural world, our relationship to and in it, the variety of shapes and colors give me inspiration, understanding, and awe.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Colonization is a severing. The first severing was between humans and Earth, thus creating the concept of nature as separate, not simply us--one ecosystem. The second was stripping the unconscious or the feminine and embracing death as kin. These violences vibrate out and become our climate crisis, manipulating/butchering people and land for capital gain and the weaponized fear of death by nations and corporations. How did this start? Stories. I am a storyteller obsessed with the threads that make us and asking those threads questions. My energy work as a death doula aids in the passing and afterlife rituals. Death is an ecological cycle. My Blackness and transness call me to contemplate death. Our deaths have become unnatural--our lives gutted by state violence, institutional harm, neglect, and destruction. Our deaths do not belong to the police, economy, or the state. I believe we can take our deaths back and, in doing so, learn to reorient ourselves to Mother Earth. We are not beings on a planet; we are a planet, an interconnected ecosystem. Now is the time for art that supports personal growth, guides us through grief, and connects us to Earth. Grief has felt particularly potent as we collectively navigate mass death through illness, gun violence, poverty, and climate change. Our relationship to death is inevitable, so why not look it directly in the eye and ask it its name? My work embodies the ancestral memory that depolarization of nature/human/spirit is essential for our liberation. I call imagination rooted in the evolution of ecology/consciousness. This manifests as speculative fiction rich in the hybridity of biology and folklore and textural paintings that mix techniques/genres. The mediums are loose to combat my compulsive perfectionism and create a messy, timeless quality, not ignorant of time but twisted outside of linear time. I choose mediums as they call to me. Listening and divination are crucial to my practice. My energy work is also divination through various tools to summon written and illustrated stories of queer love/cosmic horror that decompose colonial fears such as Blackness, the wild, and femininity. The genre of horror, even Black horror, responds to whiteness/white fear. I want to listen to Earth instead. Explore horror--the fear of death/monsters--as transformation. Early humans drew themselves merged with animals, like “The Sorcerer” cave art. Merging “man and nature” was demonized by colonization, and monsters became synonymous with what is liminal–in between. I exist liminally in gender, neurodivergence, racial and cultural experience, and artistic hybridity. This experience is not more holy; it just is, but it is mine, and so I honor it. My work practices this ancient hybridization as shapeshifting body horror, deities outside of time, and speculative biology.

column25: I am an international contemporary artist collected and exhibited in the US from coast to coast and internationally from China to Paris, Chile to London, Israel to Argentina. I come from a long lineage of immigrants and refugees and am a citizen of four countries across three continents. I have experienced the magnificent differences in many cultures, and I have seen how profoundly similar we all are; therefore, I embrace all cultures and traditions and the highest level of spirituality. My series InBodied Light is about holding the possibility that the division of humanity into ‘sub-races’ is a man-made fabrication, we can look at ourselves with new eyes embracing the notion that we are One Race. While based on the human form the Light Beings in this body of work are purposely faceless, ageless, and gender neutral, resonating with all of humanity. Each sculpture is both no one and everyone exemplifying my message that we are, in fact, All One. The technique and the materials I use result in luminous and numinous Beings. The complete installation consists of 50 sculptures ranging from 2-7 feet tall. I speak Spanish, Hebrew, English.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best part of being an artist is when someone tells me they were inspired by something I made. If it has moved them enough to want to own the piece, even better! But when someone tells me that my art excited them, made them happy, offered them a new perspective, or gave them the courage to do something brave creatively, it makes me very happy. I realized that the goal of my artistic practice is to be in a constant state of inspiration. In this state, an ecstatic energy propels me forward and from here ideas come, art becomes inevitable, and other people begin to follow along. If I can transfer some of this energy through my work into another person, and make them feel the way that I feel, that’s incredibly rewarding. That’s what makes life worth living.

column25: As a mixed-race queer woman in Los Angeles, with its rich diversity and clear gaps in economic wealth, I am interested in exploring the in-between spaces that I inhabit day to day. I want my work to create a place between dark and light, vulnerability and safety, violence and beauty, freedom and fear, capturing the complexity of the changing modern world around us. My work borrows old master painting techniques, drawing inspiration from medieval art, internet culture, amateur photography and contemporary film. I aim to directly address feelings of powerlessness in a precarious and destabilized world. Creating compositions where the subjects are tightly cropped or partially obscured, my paintings spur a sense of anxiety where the full context remains unknown. Similar to the way we consume imagery through news and social media outlets, we are only allowed to view a small window into a larger more complex reality which we will never be able to fully understand. Leaving interpretation up to each of us. Strongly influenced by medieval Catholic artwork, I appreciate the way that it illustrates a world which is both grotesque and deeply beautiful, yet allows both of these qualities to exist simultaneously without being in conflict with one another. I’m told that the work is shocking, scary, subversive, enigmatic, divine, highly sexual, or beautiful. They are all of these things and they are none of these things. Real life encompasses everything, both sweetness and violence. Yet we exist in a space where these things are not divided. I hope that my work offers an invitation to this point of view.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
One of the greatest things about being an artist is the endless possibilities for self-expression. It's a unique platform to bring your innermost thoughts, emotions, and perspectives to life through tangible creations. But beyond personal fulfilment, there is an unexplainable joy in bringing happiness to others with your work. Witnessing people connect with, appreciate, and find meaning in your art is incredibly fulfilling. Moreover, being an artist often leads to an unconventional lifestyle. It's a path that is not often taken, full of unexpected twists and constant evolution, making it an exhilarating adventure. This fusion of self-expression, evoking emotions in others, and embracing nonconformity is not one - but three - of the best things about being an artist.

column25: Hi, I'm Stella. I paint because it's magic. I can transport myself to another world with a few brush strokes – or create one from scratch. I paint because it's a way to express myself and share my thoughts and feelings with others. And I paint because it makes me happy. Every time I sit down with my inks, I enter a state of flow where all worries vanish, and I become one with my art. For me, painting is a form of meditation, a way to connect with my higher self. When I paint, I feel at peace and know everything is possible. For me, art is all about channelling emotion and creating a sense of connection. By working with inks, I want to create magic that everyone can enjoy. I find myself inspired by the most unlikely of things - Celtic mythology and Californian sunshine. The ancient tales of gods and heroes have always captivated my imagination, and there is something about the sun-drenched coastline of California that makes me feel happy. Perhaps it is the contrast between the two - the endless blue skies and the green fields of Ireland or the wild sea and the gentle waves of the Pacific. Whatever it is, I am grateful for both sources of inspiration in my life.

column24:

Sometimes I have no idea what the best thing about being an artist is, sometimes I have no idea what being an artist even means. There is a lot of things I don't know, and I think that is where being an 'artist' comes in for me. Your ability to create is not based on whether or not you have a traditonal education, it has no biases on trauma, background, experiences, if you've fallen in love or not. Anyone can call themselves an artist if they have enough desire. There is no real 'success' about being an artist. You can find happiness within yourself by having work on display for others just as much as you could find it by never sharing with anyone else.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
For over a decade, I have been using a very specific process to gather images that I later use as references for paintings. I make short movies of myself interacting and communing with nature. Then I collect stills from the footage and paint from these images. This process lends me an immense amount of freedom and agency. When alone in nature, this is when I feel the most receptive and open to the creative process. As an Animist, I believe magic lives in the small and quiet moments nature provides. During these moments, one can tap into the vibration and frequency in and of the land. My favorite way to display my work is within the context of an installation. I find using the raw outside environment as the “walls” of the gallery liberating and in line with how I approach art making. In 2019 I had an installation in the desert of Southern California. Shelters from Earth was an on-site painting, drawing, and sculpture installation on a remote property in Yucca Valley, California. The exhibition was made with the intention to give home, respite, and house communication with the alien and spirit world. The paintings, drawings, and sculptures were of natural objects that I found in the area that looked like they were from another planet, alien-like. The dappled light from an ancient pine tree overhead danced across the paintings, clay sculptures, and hanging mobiles I made from raw materials, and made them come alive and seem as though they were part of the environment . This approach opens up a collaboration with nature and brings forward the physicality of sun, wind, and natures silence as they play on the paintings themselves. My concerns with deep time, space, and earthly sensuality are made physical. My life's work is documenting my deep respect, gratitude and curiosity of the natural world.

column25: Sara Long was born and raised deep in the Redwood Forest of Northern California. As a child growing up remotely and off grid, she lived a fairly secluded life, both figuratively and literally. She learned to commune with nature, making the birds, ferns, ancient trees, light, and fog her friends. Those relationships have stayed with her and she finds herself, decades later, painting those sacred bonds. Sara graduated from the University of Washington with a BFA in Painting and Drawing in 2008. In 2012, she co-owned Blindfold Gallery in Seattle, Washington. Blindfold won Best New Gallery in 2012 by Seattle Magazine. How Sara displays her work is as important as the work itself, for she believes in creating warm and inviting spaces that all people feel comfortable in. In 2017, Sara had a guerrilla style painting installation at the neighborhood park centered around an ancient Cedar that had to be partially cut down due to disease. Daughters of Summer brought love and life back to the tree, musing her with paintings, flowers, and pink cloth streamers. Currently Sara lives on the rocky coast of Trinidad, California, where she spends her days painting and tidepooling with her rescue Doberman, Billie.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Many people find comfort in ignorance; “What you don’t know won’t hurt you”, goes the old saying, but being uninformed is a detriment to society. A lack of exposure to opposing opinions is often self-imposed or assisted by the media. Social media usage has made it easier for alternative personal, political, and moral opinions to be ignored. As a result people misunderstand each other because of their refusal to acknowledge different opinions, which in turn fractures society. My previous work takes on a critical, yet metaphorical view on the concept of self-inflicted and media-assisted censorship in relation to all social, political, and cultural issues. Bright-colored squares from an iconic censored screen image juxtaposed with the anonymous two-faced portraits express the idea of the different forms of censorship. Altering the images into a pixelated form is a way of questioning whether censorship can actually hush internal or external conflict completely, or does it just bring more attention to it? By stepping back from the image completely the answer then becomes clear.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Sanctuaries are reverential in nature, and often comprise both art and object. I construct these experiences referencing the visual language of my former Anglican faith, as well as my burgeoning understanding of the spiritual practices of my ancestral heritage from West Africa, and philosophies from around the world. Lately, this has included the journey of reclamation of lost ancestry due to colonialism and slavery. My art practice is interdisciplinary, involving painting, sculpture, assemblage, installation, digital art and video, with creative reuse being a major part of my practice. This goes for both objects, and previously completed work, resulting in a constant evolution of my materials. Using this visual language, I often create large scale installations that the public are invited to touch, sit, and occasionally languish within, to really engage with and embrace the varied themes presented in the sacred spaces I create.

column25: Dario Mohr is a first generation Grenadian, U.S. Citizen with West African and Italian heritage, born in 1988. Based in New York City, Mohr is an interdisciplinary artist, educator and non profit leader. He received a BFA in Painting from Buffalo State College, an MFA in Studio Art from The City College of New York and an Advanced Certificate in At Education from Queens College. His work involves the creation of “sacred spaces” referencing his heritage, and expressing commentary on the cultural zeitgeist through immersive sanctuary experiences. His practice is interdisciplinary, converging painting, sculpture, installation, digital art and film. In addition to his individual art practice, he is also the Founder and Director of AnkhLave Arts Alliance, Inc. which is a non-profit arts organization for the recognition and representation of BIPOC artists in contemporary art.

column25: based in detroit, michigan, lisa waud creates multi-sensory, site-specific botanical public art installations offering exploration of the brief space between living and decay, and nature as a cleansing reset. built on her background in professional garden and floral design, lisa’s work intervenes within existing architecture mimicking natural armatures as a rare pause button in the context of perishability and impermanence. heavily influenced by land art and large-scale installation artists, lisa is drawn to create ephemeral works with plants and flowers that immerse and engage the hosting community throughout the project—in the planning process, throughout the exhibition, and in the project’s afterlife. collaboration is foundational to lisa’s work. incorporating others’ voices, abilities, histories, and perspectives expand concepts, tools, and techniques within installations into sensory and cultural experiences she could not create alone, as is her preference. lisa structures collaboration purposefully—mindfully partnering early in the design process, and welcoming steering from communities hosting installations to produce immersive art events that are built on a foundation of inclusivity and accessibility. striving for zero waste throughout her practice, lisa sources from local growers and vendors, utilizes found and foraged natural items, buys used and reusable materials, composts organic matter, and recycles what remains.

column25: Throughout my life, I’ve always turned towards a singular way of self-expression to communicate my happiness and my distress. That way being visual arts. It’s comforted me in moments that I didn’t even understand what was wrong. That is a short way to describe my art, an appearance of an emotion with a confusing or less obvious back story presented. Always digestible, though it would require an inquisitive look at the title, or description (if provided) that inquires the subliminal question of “What happened?” But that’s been the majority of my life’s art, and now moving into the comic book and strip realm, I’ve been able to expand my reach beyond that of my own stories, and actually inquire with the audience about their own thoughts and emotions, though nothing of a grand scale as of yet. Planned for the future and present, is a collection of crafty and visual expressive stories to be told to the masses. Stories similarly provocative as the likes of my inspirations in the realm of anime and manga, with the addition of the black experience, and the experience of Waladi Ali.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Since I come from a live-action film production background, my favorite part of doing my artistic work has always been the ability to collaborate with my friends and co-creators. I have gotten chills on set when seeing a beautiful shot come together due to the work of dozens of creative individuals, each bringing a different element and soulfulness to the image. I love the zone state my friends and I can get into and create something meaningful with each other. Most recently, In September, I was the Director of Photography for my good friend Arya Singh's short film about a grieving father. My friends were there to help me create the look of the lighting, compose the shots, hold the weight of the camera, and guide me while shooting in temperamental circumstances like underwater and in heavy rain. This feeling of connection, creativity and support is something I strive to experience throughout the rest of my life. Connecting with others also applies to what I like about my visual art and animation work. Since this type of visual artwork is more solitary, I find the connection aspect to be most gratifying after completing the work. Last week, I screened 'Fireflies' to my animation production class, and one of my classmates whom I hadn't spoken to much over the semester raised her hand during the Q&A. In tears, she discussed how much this film meant to her. She said it made her recognize her own queer experiences that she hadn't been able to talk about with many other people. It meant so much to me that she connected with the film, especially because I was scared to bring it into the world for the same reasons she felt fearful talking about her own experiences. I was happy that we could get to know each other a little bit more through something I created. This small moment represents the best thing about being an artist to me.

column25: The inspiration for this film was based on my experiences attending a summer camp in North Carolina with my cousin. At 16, It was my first time away from my home in Colorado for longer than a few days, and it was also where I discovered I was queer. I started to have a crush on one of the girls there. We texted every day and sent letters to each other until we could meet again at camp the next year, and when it finally arrived, she didn’t act how I expected. At the time, it felt insurmountable and soul-crushing, not only because it was my first time having a true crush, but it also made me question my identity and the entire world I had lived in previously. The symbol of “fireflies” is representative of my time there. Their existence is fleeting; they only come out at night, only in certain parts of America, and only in the summer. These bugs felt like a natural parallel to the idea of new and “first experiences,” we go through as teenagers, which are especially heightened for queer people. They represent the type of first love that can never truly be something real, as much as I was convinced it could be. Even at the time I knew what was happening to me would come quickly and disappear quickly. It stays with me years later, and shaped me for the rest of my life. At the time, I was very interested in watercolor painting, journaling and collaging. There are dozens of journal entries, scribbles, painted sketches, and memorabilia I saved from camp activities sitting in a box in my dad’s house. I wanted my past self to have a voice in the film, and create the animation as an homage to the kind of art that I was making during that time. I scanned more than 40 pages of my diaries at my local library while I was visiting back home, and luckily many of them were able to make it into the film. The idea of the film as sort of a living journal entry speaks to my motivation as a filmmaker to try and crystalize the past. I have always been interested in the concept of memory, and fearful of how emotions and images we once felt so strongly can just disappear into nothing. I hope that by watching the film, my memory can live on through others, and those who also have felt alone in their fleeting experiences can hold onto them a little bit longer.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Surrealism is my art. It's the language I speak and the way I communicate my deepest thoughts and emotions. To me, art is not just about making pretty pictures or creating something that looks nice on a wall. It's about expressing something that can't be put into words. For me, surrealism is the perfect way to do that. It's like a secret code that only those who are truly willing to look can decipher. My art is filled with symbols and metaphor, each one carefully chosen to convey a specific idea or feeling. Sometimes I draw inspiration from my dreams, where anything is possible and the rules of reality don't apply. Other times, I'll look at a mundane object like a chair or a shoe, and try to see it in a new way. By playing with perspective, scale, and context, I can transform the familiar into something strange and otherworldly.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Jordan Holms is a multidisciplinary artist working in painting and textiles. Her practice considers how aesthetic ‘tastes’ are materialized, organized, and made to mean. Mining source materials from the built environment (both physical and online) Holms’s work references commodity culture, folk art, antiquing, reality television, boutique concept stores, and so-called aspirational design accounts on social media. Filtered through the lens of abstraction, her work interprets the things we find both in our homes and out in the built environment that signal something about how taste produces meaning. Translating this ontology of online and everyday imagery, Holms paints irreverent and errant spaces that index their own meanings in an attempt to make sense of what ‘having taste’ might look like and mean in a moment wherein cultural trends are dictated by algorithms as well as elites.

column25: In the realm of my sewn abstract art, I embark on a creative exploration that intertwines unconventional techniques with a rich tapestry of materials. Based in the captivating San Francisco Bay Area, my work is a fusion of non-traditional approaches and a commitment to sustainability. At its core, my artistic expression departs from the conventional painter's path. Utilizing a foundation of acrylic and watercolor on un-stretched canvas and cold-pressed paper, I introduce a diverse array of materials that elevates each piece. Hand-painted and handmade papers, delicately adorned with touches of gold and silver leaf contribute to the intricate texture and visual depth of my compositions. What sets my art apart is the seamless integration of sewing machine stitches into every creation. This distinctive touch imparts a sense of fluidity and three-dimensionality, transforming each piece into a dynamic, tactile experience. The interplay between various materials and stitching techniques is a deliberate choice, carefully considered to evoke emotions and transport viewers to a tranquil state of mind. Through my sewn abstract art, I aim to engage and captivate observers, inviting them into a world where each brushstroke, paper selection, and stitch contributes to a harmonious visual narrative. It is my endeavor to create not just art but an immersive experience that resonates with those who encounter it. Join me in this exploration of materiality, as I seek to push the boundaries of traditional artistic expression and evoke a profound connection between the observer and the art itself.

column25: I draw, animate and paint to build a world with beings and botanicals that do not exist. My work is inspired by fantasy-based literature and nature. With much of my childhood spent in nature, it has become a key element in my artwork. Charcoal and graphite were once main mediums but as my art developed over the years, oil paint has become the medium I use most. I strive to achieve realism in my paintings and in my drawings. Focusing in on smaller details eases my mind and brings a sense of calmness to me. That sense of calmness is something I work to bring to everyone who views my art. I use a cooler color palette and specific botanicals to invoke certain emotions and to provide that calm feeling. Creating that tranquil feeling is important to me because as someone with anxiety, a sense of peace and tranquility is sometimes difficult to feel, and I want others to be able to get that calming sense that I get in my art. Using references is important in perfecting the realistic style. Taking these references images, myself rather than finding images online is an important part of my art making process because it allows me to connect with the piece I’m creating. I am able to express myself in these images I’m taking which helps me put a piece of myself in each work I create. Connecting with my art allows others to connect with it and with me subconsciously. Forming a connection and seeing the genuine emotions from the viewer helps makes the piece more meaningful to me. I create art that I enjoy and that I know others can enjoy and relate to. I develop a relationship with my artwork by taking a more impromptu take on its creation. Sketching and planning a piece has left my artwork feeling stiff and disconnected from myself in the past. The pieces that I spend a lot of time planning and sketching tend to be the pieces I am least happy with and the most stressful. My process has developed into a more fluid and carefree approach. This more carefree approach has allowed my art to obtain a more realistic form. Losing myself in this carefree approach allows me to grow as an artist and as a person.

I befriended Angus a year before these portraits were taken. We met in the lobby of a theater before the preview of his new play 'Dirt.' He was antsy. He exclaimed to me, 'I don't care if they love it, I don't care if they hate it, I just care that they feel something,' as he kept pulling at the bottom of his shorts, eyeing the crowd that was soon to be the audience. After meeting him a few more times, seeing another one of his plays, 'Cavemxn,' and reading his play, 'Australia Open,' I knew Angus was one of those playwrights; one of those playwrights who are saying something important that people are responding to, so I invited him to the studio. 7-Queens: Aubrey Haive 7-Queens asks the viewer to look at each photograph like a window into another world and write a storyline within their own mind. column25: My work is rooted in an environmental consciousness that derives from my concern for the Earth’s rapidly declining health. I use it to highlight the misuse and abuse that we (humans from Western industrialization through present day) inflict on local and global natural ecologies. My work is influenced by my everyday observations of landscapes (both naturally occurring, and human altered), environmental catastrophes, and how present-day society interacts with the natural world. I am passionate about green health because I grew up in a rural location where I learned from an early age to respect the natural world through acts of gardening; food preservation; and livestock care. As an adult, reflecting on these experiences that influenced my values and heightened my sensitivity to local environments, I am especially perceptive of our hazardous climate. My materials and my process show my concern for natural ecologies through meticulous attention to turning earthen and upcycled materials into strong sculptural form. My monumental sculptures are made out of earthenware clay, handmade paper pulp, and upcycled waste materials (plastic bags, fishing line, electrical wire, and old clothing and bedding). I marry my earthen objects with industrial surplus that is re-contextualized through repetitive textile processes and the contrasting media charges my work with tension. The union of materials also serves as a metaphor for the complex relationship that humanity has with the natural world. In addition to conscious material usage and impactful placement, I rely on strong formal devices and sensual form to create work that is symbolically charged. My forms are influenced by domestic and industrial objects that I experience in my daily life that are indicative of warning symbols and possible solutions for living more resourcefully. I use exaggerated scale, assembled mass, form isolation, and tension to emphasize my concern for the declining health of our planet. My large-scale work offers my viewers a space to reflect on our hazardous environmental situation. I hope that my passion for making, my love for the earth, and my delight in observing the world around me in combination with my work will encourage people to join me in reconsidering our daily routines.

column25: In 2010, I started making 3-dimensional flowers of my own design out of magazine paper. I had an abundance of National Geographic magazines given to me. The flowers are composed of small triangles of magazine paper that are glued in a circle that gradually gets smaller as I work my way into the center. After waiting about a day for the glue to dry, I use my thumb and index finger to curl the paper inward. Each flower takes approximately four hours to complete. When finished, I mat each flower and place in an eight by ten frame. During the Artist Inc. program in the spring of 2019, I was asked to create a goal that would help to further my art career. The goal was to take my paper flowers in a new direction. I decided to purchase hangable glass globes that I could place a paper flower inside. The first flower I made did not quite fit. My four-year-old daughter saw it and asked to hang it in her room on the wall without a globe. This sparked my idea to create a paper installation using not only hundreds of paper flowers, but “paper grass” as well. This piece has been an important part of my life because I thoroughly enjoy every part of the project from deconstructing the magazines to cutting out each piece by hand. I have grown an appreciation for the repetitive process. I am also a realist painter so by contrast this piece is much less stressful. This project continuously reminds me that all things take time to evolve. Additionally, I like the idea of repurposing the materials I am using. Every canvas was previously used. I was able to turn them into something new and completely different.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I make collages, prints, banners, and carved and painted sculptures that record a journey of ideas and memories around the unseen and the unknown, reflecting on time and mortality through facets of my personal history. I juxtapose real tree limbs and vines with arboreal imagery—including renderings of witch hazel and rhubarb leaves—using them as stand-ins for my own presence, and as expressions of the dogged persistence of life. Processes of pressing and rubbing, cutting and pasting, scraping and gouging are evident throughout the finished images and objects. I often balance instinctive marks against more considered decisions, arriving at a dynamic interplay between the deliberate manipulation of materials and the operation of chance. The heraldic banners, which suggest clothing or birds’ plumage, can be exhibited indoors or outdoors, where they are continually reshaped by the elements. In my recent “Crow and Sandal” series of works on paper, the recurring motif of the crow acts as a symbolic messenger and metaphorical self-portrait, while the sandal (which in Hinduism represents a realization of the spiritual) functions as a symbol of the infinite. Here, as in other works, the images’ repetition suggests an attempt to examine and suspend their power in time and space.

column25: Nancy Azara is an artist and feminist educator best known for her carved wood sculpture and mixed media collages. Azara records a journey of ideas and memories around the unseen and the unknown in her carvings, wood assemblages, prints and mixed media collage through the use of texture and symbols. She draws inspiration from nature and mystic spiritual traditions. Each piece of Azara’s artwork offers a reflection on the passage of time, spirituality and facets of her own biography. Born in Brooklyn, to a family of Southern Italian origin, Nancy expressed an early interest in gardens and art. Her earliest artistic training took place at Finch College, a women’s college noted for its mix of vocational and liberal arts curriculum, where she studied costume design. A keen attention to the scale and labor of the body remains a strong thread in Azara’s work. After several years working in theater as a costume designer, Azara craved a more personalized mode of expression and a greater artistic challenge. In the mid 1960s, she studied at the Art Students League of New York with expressionist painter Edwin Dickinson and sculptor John Hovannes. Although inspired by this tutelage, Azara’s work is explicitly feminist in subject and entirely unique in imagery and technique. In the 1960s and 70s an interest in spirituality and eastern philosophy interested Azara. She engaged actively with the mystic aspects of Yogic tradition and Zen Buddhism themes and symbols from these traditions can be found throughout her work. As her career developed Azara’s sculptures and vision resisted the labels and categorization of her peers. Azara rejected many of the lessons of the predominantly male art establishment. She turned to an automatic form of personal journal drawing to access the unconscious (Visual Diaries). She formalized this approach into a popular feminist consciousness-raising technique which was embraced in the nascent feminist art community in New York and groups like Redstockings. These Visual Diaries, were part record keeping, part spiritual practice and part social emotional learning though art. In 1979, Azara co-founded a feminist art school, the New York Feminist Art Institute (1979 to 1990) with Miriam Schapiro, Carol Stronghilos, Irene Peslikis, Lucille Lessane and Selena Whitefeather. Until 1990 NYFAI was an important educational space for female artists and art professionals to examine gender, self, and identity. In 1984 Azara was an advisor to the 1 founding of Ceres Gallery, another feminist non-profit gallery space in New York. Azara is an alumna member of A.I.R Gallery, founded in 1972, as the first all female artists’ cooperative gallery in the United States. She will be showing at A.I.R. in April/May, 2021. Azara developed, and continues to work in, a distinct style of rich large scale sculpture and mixed media collages of found wood, carved, ornamented and mounted. Instinctive chip carving peels off an outer layer of wood, reaching for an essentialized raw experience of the in body of the limbs, exposing flesh and blood. This work explores life cycles, utilizing the metaphor of tree for personhood. Egg tempera, often in reds and pinks, and gilding recover these exposed layers, exploring folkloric stories of women’s roles, goddess imagery, and affirmation of female self. Nancy continues to make and exhibit work from her studios in Tribeca and Woodstock. She is constantly challenging herself and her community in quarterly intergenerational feminist dialogues, (RE)PRESENT. She also holds an annual workshop, teaches, and mentors other feminist artists, sustaining a unique visual, experiential and pedagogical artistic practice which remains informed by the body, nature, spirituality and her experience as a woman. Written by Maeve M. Hogan With special thanks to Katie Cercone and her article 'The Healing Art of Nancy Azara,' Women's Art Journal, Vol. 31, No. 1 (Spring/Summer, 2010) pp. 35-40. Publ. Old City Publishing, Inc.

column25: In my work, I explore vibrant, layered florals through bold colors and a child-like sense of playfulness. I create intuitively with acrylic paint, allowing my emotions and memories to guide each painting. I’m drawn to the friendly confidence flowers possess in the way they spring from the earth, just when we crave their presence most. Their pops of color provide us with endless inspiration while adding a lighthearted energy to the landscape. In my creative practice, I view florals as the lively companions amongst their botanical friends. They inspire me to integrate deep internal reflection into my art practice with tenderness. Through the use of vibrant colors and expressive brush strokes, my paintings provide the viewer with an opportunity for inward reflection and outward awe. Acrylics grant me the flexibility to paint quickly and intuitively, creating complex layers and unique moments on the canvas. I view my work as a way to process my personal experiences and reconnect with my authentic self. I encourage the audience to appreciate the magical moments of everyday life and see through the lens of their own inner child.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist, for me, transcends the realms of mere passion or profession – it is a lifeline, a sanctuary that keeps me from succumbing to the shadows of depression. Creating art is not just a choice; it's my imperative. In the act of self-medication through the crafting of joyful pieces, I find solace and a profound sense of purpose. These creations, born from my struggles, carry the potential to resonate positively with others, extending a shared experience of joy and wonder. From an early age, art became my coping mechanism, a powerful tool to navigate the challenges posed by learning disabilities. It serves as a gateway to the childlike wonder of creation, allowing me to be fully present in the process. It's not solely about the end result; rather, it's the journey itself that becomes therapeutic. The act of diving into the creative process is a return to innocence, a rediscovery of the pure joy found in making something out of nothing. Being an artist isn't just a career path; it's an integral part of my identity, a lifeline that anchors me in moments of turbulence. It's a testament to the transformative power of art – not only as a personal refuge but as a beacon of hope and positivity that I aspire to share with others. In this lifelong commitment to creation, I don't see another viable option; being an artist is not just what I do; it's who I am.'

column25: My artistic journey revolves around harnessing the transformative potential of recycled materials. By repurposing items such as boxes, bottles, cans, and food containers, my creations stand as a testament to the power of upcycling, sustaining, and avoiding unnecessary contributions to landfills. At the core of my practice is a fusion of papier-mâché, gouache, acrylic markers, and wood, which imbues my pieces with a distinctive and recognizable style. This eclectic combination serves as a canvas for my artistic expression, offering viewers a visual experience that is both unique and engaging. My early diagnosis of dyslexia has fostered adaptability and a creative engineering mindset, qualities that seamlessly translate into my visual work. Rooted in contemporary American art, my creations strive to captivate audiences and contribute to the dialogue within esteemed institutions. My ultimate aspiration is to see my work proudly displayed in recognized cultural spaces. Breathing life into materials deemed as garbage, I embark on a journey to challenge conventional notions of beauty, unearthing the extraordinary in the ordinary. Through my art, I extend an invitation for others to reconsider the concept of waste, encouraging them to recognize the inherent beauty in what society might otherwise discard thoughtlessly. In essence, my work embodies the principles of sustainability, serving as a medium for creative expression while underscoring the profound impact of art on our collective history. Each piece becomes a testament to the interconnectedness of creativity, environmental consciousness, and the enduring legacy of artistic contributions to our cultural narrative.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
“In March 2020, I relocated to Charleston from the Blue Ridge Mountains to start a new life chapter, bringing my sense of wonder and discovery to the lowcountry, with its rich, complicated history and fascinating landscape. Exploration leads to inspiration, so I wander and draw from direct observation; downtown I sketch wrought iron gates, 300-year-old tombstones, and Civil War era daguerreotypes. Living near Folly Beach, I am fascinated by the ever-changing tidal marshlands and ethereal Morris Island Lighthouse always present on the distant horizon during my morning beach runs. I arrange this collected imagery into evocative, though-provoking new compositions by layering ink illustrations with intricate papercut patterns. This series began in 2018 with collapsible carousel books, named for their ability to configure in the round, and it has recently evolved into large-scale tyvek snowflake papercuts, and sculptures set into castaway suitcases and mirrors, each with its own mystery in the patina. I continue to explore and create new histories in this magical world.”

column25: Jennifer York (née Jen Swearington) grew up in rural Indiana, drawing since she was little. Mentored by her artist aunt Kelly Hoernig, she knew by age 12 she would become an artist. She studied fine art and art history, earning a BFA in Sculpture from Pratt Institute in 1997 and MFA in Fibers from the Savannah College of Art and Design in 2000, spent a year as Artist in Residence at Arrowmont School of Arts and Crafts, and then settled in Asheville, NC. In 2003, she started sewing original apparel pieces and printing her illustrations on them, founding Jennythreads Studio, by which she made an independent, creative living for 20 years. In 2013, her comprehensive how-to manual, “Printing on Fabric: Techniques with Screens, Stencils, Inks and Dyes” was published by Lark Crafts. Beginning spring of 2018, York traveled abroad for several month-long art residencies in Bulgaria, Mexico, Finland, and Greece. On these adventures abroad, she filled sketchbooks with drawings and created collapsible carousel pop-up books that could travel back in her carry-on suitcase. In March 2020, York relocated to Charleston, SC to continue her creative journey on the coast, where she has retired from sewing Jennythreads to focus on her fine art full-time. She developed her papercut illustrations from books to intricate dioramas built in vintage suitcases and mirrors, as well as large-scale papercuts from tyvek house wrap, with impressive solo exhibitions “New Histories” at the Gibbes Museum of Art in 2021, “Estuary Blues” at the Burroughs Chapin Art Museum in Myrtle Beach, SC and “Constructed Realities” at the Museum of Art-Deland (Florida) in 2023. Outside the studio, York is an avid trail runner and SC journeyman beekeeper whose small apiary on James Island, SC produces Jennybees honey.

column25: I share with you my art series, 'Dead Artist.' Beyond being a mere collection of self-portraits, it stands as a raw and cathartic expression, delving into the intricate tapestry of grief, loss, and transformation—themes that have profoundly shaped my personal journey. 'Dead Artist' is not just a visual exploration; it is a poignant journey through the emotional landscapes left in the wake of shattered dreams, ruptured relationships, and evolving identities. Drawing inspiration from Japan's 'Dondoyaki' tradition, a ritual of ceremonial burning symbolizing cleansing, my artistic exploration introduces a cynical twist, providing viewers with a sardonic lens to reflect on their own transformative odysseys. Grief, a central theme in this series, is laid bare as it represents the aftermath of ruptured dreams, relationships, and identities. The self-portraits, grappling with topics like infertility and divorce, unravel a psychological exploration of brokenness. In these moments, I depict myself as a resigned pyre, destined to be set ablaze and subsequently forgotten—a poignant metaphor for the impermanence of art and life. The very name of the series, 'Dead Artist,' serves as a bold challenge to traditional reverence, offering a bitter perspective on self-identity and renewal that is unburdened by toxic positivity. The collection beckons viewers to embark on their own transformative journeys, inviting reflection with a sardonic edge, all while emphasizing the indifferent and, at times, disheartening cycle of personal growth. At its core, 'Dead Artists' is a cynical expedition into the ephemeral and transformational dimensions of the feminine psyche. With a reluctant acknowledgment of the inevitability of change, my self-portraits serve as mirrors, inviting viewers to engage in a sarcastic contemplation of their own transformational narratives. It's a cynical questioning of the reverence associated with the past and an unapologetic acknowledgment of the indifferent yet powerful cycle of personal growth.

column25: My current focus is an endeavor to develop mark making and gesture through the act of movement made by the human body. I am interested in activating the body through its physicality as a tool or production mechanism. I have begun the process of documenting the body as artifact within the conceptual context of a bodiless future. I am driven to explore new ways of feeling, seeing, feeling, making, feeling, thinking about our relationship with the built environment. Even if we do not physically interact with a constructed space, how does it affect our ability to think creatively? As an initial approach to this field of exploration I have taken steps to map the human body as a mark-making object of form and mass. Artworks are restricted to the surface area of their application. My approach to these artworks considers the constructed space as an influence on my body, as my body is informed by that space. My practice continues to ask the viewer to consider the visual remnant of our flesh as a static object that aspires to the act of movement as well as an event of movement that has been inactivated by the built environment

My mission is this: to educate, start conversations, and have dialogue. Appreciation and understanding lead to a more unified, harmonious, compassionate, beautiful world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a youth sitting in grass absorbing the warmth of the sun, feeling the wholemovement of life that I knew nothing about. My earliest memories are playing in dirt, making things with sticks and rocks, looking at leaves, and discovering the world. As a child l drew and painted, making sculpture in my early teens. I delighted and puzzled with how things are. Early on I poured over a visual history of western art and what was then called primitive art. It stimulated me to make things like those people in the books. Receiving a fellowship from the Chicago Art Institute in sculpture allowed me a year living in Florence Italy where I began cast a few bronzes that got me thinking more about other methods people used for casting sculpture. Returning to the San Francisco Bay area, I built a foundry to cast my own work. Ten years later I moved to Santa Fe NM, continuing to cast bronze, weld steel, now with cast and fabricated rubber, and fiberglass. My work focused mainly on fear, anger, and distrust that I directed to issues of man’s inhumanity to man and human abuse through technology. My art had become firmly a refuge from, and a reservoir for childhood trauma. Needing to change what I was doing, with a desire to find out more about how things really are in space, my life took an abrupt turn. I began to explore geometry stimulated by the work of Buckminster Fuller. Ten years into this exploration I realized what I was looking for was in the circle, a process of folding circles that I call wholemovement. During this time I developed a new geometric classification for a transformational movement system, started a company, and bottomed out from the business world. After years drawing pictures of circles I could see the unexplored richness in folding them. I went to schools sharing what I was finding by working with students, teachers, and parents. I made a living folding circles and selling books I had written about it. This occupied twenty-five years in Chicago, and now continues in Grand Forks ND. Folding circles allows me to observe and ask questions that otherwise do not get asked about the unquestioned fundamental in math, art, science, and in philosophical musings. We do not fold circles and have no idea of the possibilities within circle unity; all we know is what we do with the unit circle. I have publishing 8 books that document my exploration. I have traveled here and abroad giving workshops and selling books until covid hit. Working with others has been an important part of my exploration into the circle. Drawing, modeling, and writing are ways for me to connect senses of head, hand, and heart to my experience in what I do and how I live my life, which continues to delight me. I still puzzled over how all of this happens. As a kid I thought whoever made this world must have loved doing it. I still think that, because I love making things too. Many people say what I do with circles is art. What I do is no longer about expressing myself with finished product. I make models using a unique process that is different than the traditional adding to and taking away used in making art. I want to give demonstration of the potential in folding circles that we have missed by drawing pictures of them. This process is about the transformational changes we see in beauty found in nature, and in the best of art that elevates the human soul. Nothing is added or taken from the circle, it is self-generative and a self-organizing process of movement that does nothing without a folder, a folder without the circle knows nothing of the circle. It would behoove us to fold circles at the same time we learn to draw pictures of them.

column25: As a child Bradford drew, painted, and made objects that interested him. He wanted to make art like other people do. Through his early years he did cartooning, designing, anything that challenged his interest and would further his skills. After completing two years at the Art Institute of Chicago, he received a Fellowship in sculpture enabling him to work for a year in Florence Italy. He saw the art that was familiar to him through images from his youth. While there he had some pieces cast in bronze that raised his interest in Italian and Benin bronze casting methods. Upon returning to the San Francisco bay area he set up a foundry casting his own bronzes. Ten years later moving to Santa Fe NM. He continued casting bronze, welding steel, incorporating cast and fabricated rubber, combining them in various ways to create his own visual language. Needing to get away from the toxic materials he was using, and to distance himself from the art world, enabled him to question what he was doing and get a perspective on why and how things actually worked together in space. After ten years exploring geometry stimulated by the work of Buckminster Fuller, he realized all geometry and math goes back to compass and straight edge and the flat abstracted logic of linear thinking that conceptually flattens our perception. He wanted more. As an artist he understood the circle we draw is an image. He started to fold circles to see what the image represented and why nobody was folding circles. A new world of transformational geometry opened for him to explore. He was now well removed from the art world of self-expression, finding beauty in a process by observing and making choices about information generated through the circle. A move to Chicago took him deeper into folding circles and working in schools with students, teachers, and parents. He wrote books to document what he found, sharing with others his discovers about the circle. He sold his books at math and art teacher conferences, both here and abroad. This supported his exploration in folding circles, something more beyond how we draw them and use them as metaphor. Since covid he has stopped doing workshops and is focusing his attention on going deeper into what he has done with folding circles. He does not call the models he makes art, although he is amazed at the beauty revealed in the process. He feels everything folded in the circle is proportionally beautiful because nothing is added to it or taken way. He says the art he brings to the circle are the connections and choices he makes with the information the circle generates. This process of folding, reforming, and joining circles is the same for everyone, yet seems uniquely different for each individual folder.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Being an artist is akin to being an alchemist of the soul, adept at the sacred transmutation of pain into beauty. The artist harnesses the raw and often tumultuous emotions that life bestows, crafting them into works of profound beauty. It is a transformative process that turns tragedy into triumph, despair into resilience, and chaos into harmony. At the heart of artistic expression lies the ability to transfigure personal struggles into powerful narratives. Each stroke of the brush, every lyric penned, and every note composed becomes a testament to the artist's ability to confront pain and, in the crucible of creation, birth something beautiful. It's a cathartic dance with one's demons, where the artist doesn't shy away from the darker corners of existence but, instead, illuminates them with the light of artistic creation. Artistic expression becomes a beacon, guiding both the creator and the audience through the labyrinth of human experience. The artist becomes a storyteller, sharing tales of adversity and turning them into anthems of triumph. In the gallery of creativity, the canvases tell stories of resilience, the melodies sing of overcoming, and the words narrate sagas of personal evolution. Yet, the magic of being an artist extends beyond personal catharsis. Art is a universal language that speaks to the collective human experience. In sharing the transformed narratives of pain and turning them into aesthetically resonant creations, artists inspire others to embark on their own journeys of transformation. It becomes a ripple effect, where the artist's triumph over adversity serves as a catalyst for others to transmute their struggles into something beautiful. In the grand tapestry of human existence, artists play a unique role as conduits of inspiration. They prove that from the crucible of pain, beauty can emerge; from the depths of tragedy, triumph can be born. It's a testament to the resilience of the human spirit and the transformative power of creative expression—a perpetual cycle of inspiration where one artist's metamorphosis becomes the catalyst for another's. In this symphony of creation, the pain becomes the raw material, and the beauty becomes the masterpiece, echoing the profound truth that, indeed, art has the power to turn the darkest moments into the brightest stars.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Josh Cabello's work focuses on creating queer sanctuaries. He paints lush, imagined gardens, conjuring a world where anyone who enters can be still, commune with oneself, and be engulfed by nature. Under a proscenium of ebullient blooming flowers, nymph-like figures will occasionally settle in each vignette, often engulfed by the flora surrounding them. Each piece is a portal for a viewer to step into the human interior – a space often uncharted, incomprehensible, and sometimes filled with tumult, but a fragile space that must be navigated with patience and care. Gardens can bloom into a mass of untamed overgrowth, but ultimately, the petals of a flower are easily knocked to the floor if we don’t watch where our hand sways. Josh’s works draw on a variety of histories. Inspired by Greco-Roman frescoes to Italian Renaissance works to queer art haus films, Josh connects both real and implied queer lineages from antiquity to the present. These works draw on queer traditions of play and excess, mingle with themes of intimacy and delicacy, and work to decenter hypersexuality and masculinity within the modern queer experience. By aesthetically and thematically linking art history and queer theory, Josh ultimately asserts queerness’ endurance and belonging in history, even if it has not always been visible to the naked eye.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I need art. Challenged with high functioning anxiety, major depressive disorder, ptsd, and suicidal ideation, I’m continuously grateful to experience first hand the ways in which art heals. When I can’t find the words, which is often, I draw figurative self-portraits of heightened moments, pulled directly from my journals, utilizing music to validate feelings and harness energy. Keeping most of my series monochromatic eliminates distraction for a therapeutic process. Using wood as my canvas not only serves as a mirror reflecting individual grains with each piece, but also keeps me connected to nature that provides a healing equal to art. While the confined aspects of my own creative process have proven its benefits mentally and creatively, it has been in exhibiting and sharing these vulnerabilities and tools that has attributed to the progression of my art career and healing journey. I have also seen the advantageousness first hand in leading journaling and self-portrait based workshops and providing basic creating instruments. Through my own art and public outreach, I hope to continue to advocate for more available and engaging art opportunities, education, and outlets.

column25: Michelle Lubin (MDFerrera Fine Art) is a Southern California based figurative artist, mural painter, and advocate for mental health and accessible art, actively volunteering with her local Arts Bureau and elementary schools. From her garage studio, Lubin finds healing in the process of drawing self-portraits on custom built wood panels. Each piece, heavily inspired and validated by music, encompasses the balancing act of navigating todays world with invisible mental health challenges, fast changing world events, and the responsibilities of motherhood. Lubin’s success is attributed to her ability to evoke empathy through the use of simple materials and a monochromatic style that captures heightened expressions and gestures. While awards and accolades have elevated her exposure and sales since pursuing art full-time in 2010, it continues to be her efforts participating in public art projects and outreach that fulfill the artists’ mission and passion.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I combine natural light landscape, macro, and portrait photography, timelapse, and stop motion animation to interpret natural and built landscapes across scales. Separately and together, these forms help us explore a sense of place for each of us within these spaces, within a single moment or across different time scales. My hope is that we think beyond ourselves, our immediate surroundings, and shallow time horizons, expanding into something deeper and broader than what is an increasingly distracted frenetic existence (collectively and individually) on this planet we share with all species. My still and motion compositions are driven by a desire to understand the world and discuss this understanding with others in our communities. I hope these works inherently spark discussions amongst others, even not in my presence. By using various media modalities to explore the juxtaposition of space, time, and light from both internal and external perspectives of human (and non-human) relationships with water and watersheds, I can create the opportunity for awareness, understanding, and appreciation of the complexities of the world in which we live, including a sense of place.

column25: Blending documentary and studio art practices, my work explores themes of illness, anatomy, grief, and mortality. I am largely interested in the body and all the ways it can mutate, deviate, and, ultimately, fail. I typically begin with a piece of documentary audio, whether that be an interview, a performance of nonfiction writing, or myself speaking in voiceover. Then, using combinations of animation, handwriting, 16mm footage, digital footage, and archival materials, I build up visuals which supplement and guide the interpretation of the audio. The visual choices are driven by emotion; I am less interested in a documentary rooted in Fact than one which most accurately portrays an experience from the perspective of the person living it. From cancer to menstruation to neurological disorders, I often use film as an attempt to recreate the subjective experience of physical suffering, or to grapple with the emotions that come along with sickness and loss. These films can serve not only as a site of healing for those sharing their experiences but as a way for myself and audiences to wrap their minds around the possibility of their own body’s demise. In order to address power imbalances often present in traditional documentary modes, my work often lands at the intersection of personal and co-created film, and is rooted in feminist, queer, and disability studies. I am inspired by women who explore personal stories in relation to larger philosophical questions- Bell Hooks, Barbara Hammer, Agnes Varda, Jeanette Winterson, Lynne Sachs- and am influenced by sketchbook and scrapbook aesthetics. In order to offset the emotional intensity of this work, I take on lighter projects involving 16mm and stop motion in interim periods. These projects serve as containers to learn and play, helping me expand the tools available to me as I continue to build a body of work that attempts over and over again to approach and understand death.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work explores the histories, myths, and images and that constitute the American psyche. Using humor as a tool to excavate repressed thoughts and feelings, my work blends traditional forms of visual art including painting, sculpture and collage with writing, sound/music, performance, and social practice. Pivoting from the oft-exclaimed artistic -ism that 'the personal is political,' my life and work insists that the inter-personal is political. That is, the (emotional, professional, familial, academic, social, etc.) relationships that we develop with other people are just as politically potent as any subject matter that we may take up as cultural producers, as people. As such, in my practice, I often work to unite my personal and familial experiences with what I recognize as the political stakes of the American project. The glitter paintings of president-less presidential portraits were inspired by a craft project that I remember doing in elementary school, and by the fact that at that time I was encouraged to sing 'The Pledge of Allegiance' and other patriotic anthems daily. To me, this period represents a moment when I was being taught what both 'Art' and 'America' were, ideologically. Similarly, the projects Mountains of America and Phases were attempts to blend my personal experiences with ideas about the United States, and the uses and abuses of landscape photography.

column25: I have lived more in my mind than my body for as long as I can remember, though, I could not tell you where exactly that is. I feel tangled in humanity, pushed and pulled from mind to matter and back again. It's an uncomfortable way to experience all the ins and outs of being a human, a woman. Etching copper and carving wood is like making a map for myself back to myself and following it, all at the same time, but true to form, I don't know where that is. Printmaking has been the connection between my personal life, my professional life and my dreamlife for the last ten years, and I use it to unpack all the discomfort and all the comfort I experience. The process demands a sort of surrender to the unknown and I am addicted to the moment of painful anticipation of the first reveal. In addition to print, I use collage to disassemble the current world and reassemble into one that tells a store more accurate to the inside of the human experience. Regardless of medium, I am interested in creating an environment of detail, color, and pattern in order to invite others into my surreal corner of humanity. I strive to abandon each piece at the most perfect time and lean in to the discomfort of expanding horizons.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
ROLE OF NATURE “There is something infinitely healing in the repeated refrains of nature” Rachel Carson My studio paintings, mixed media, photography, and public art commissions have always been about or included elements of nature. For example, when I received the Rome Prize Fellowship in Painting, I traveled throughout Western Europe photographing formal gardens. My travels to Japan years later, also took me throughout the country photographing gardens. I was captivated by the extraordinary array of the plant textures, and the richness and variety of the greens. Each garden was a world unto itself. I currently have a rural residence, in Monson, Massachusetts. I was born and raised in Brooklyn, NY and the differences between the two locations are extreme and dramatic which fit my needs for what both have to offer. The quiet rural setting is perfect for painting and photographing the birds, sky, and landscape. It is away from the pace of Brooklyn and affords me the quiet which I love. I am very productive in this environment where I feel refreshed and peaceful. I thrive on the sequence, saturation and intensity of the seasons in Western Massachusetts. My studio here is surrounded by windows and I feel as though I am inside and outside. My method of creating art is in a series format, adding and subtracting ideas and images, believing that all work generated on one subject, over an extended period of time, unites and intensifies the imagery and concepts. Acrylic paint is my chosen material because it allows me to create flat areas of color and sharp edges which I think is a clear method of expressing the paintings’ concepts. A complimentary color palette and asymmetrical shapes, results in a visual tension. Making art for me rests on one question: how can I organize all of the art elements to fit together to result in a cohesive whole? My approach to painting human images explores the emotions of vulnerability, strength, endurance while the natural world inserts itself. A variety of animals describe sensuality, determination, freedom and playfulness. When I choose to work with collage elements, this technique adds a degree of mystery, tension and depth. My public art commissioned murals can be seen in NYC, Massachusetts, Florida, Georgia and Mexico. Among her awards are an Art in Action Grant, The Women Waging Peace Award, and the MA Council for the Arts Grant. I was recently awarded a grant from The Community Foundation of Western MA to create Action: A History of Florence, MA. For one year, 2023, I supervised this community mural, exterior, 6’ X 20’ on Florence Paint and Decorating Center, Florence, MA.

The paintings feature characters from my life. I believe we are defined by the company we keep, and my sense of self is very much wrapped up in the communities, people, places, and things I interact with. I strike a balance between extremely personal narratives around each figure and the universal sense of familial bonds that painting activates. I use drawing to add a contorted aspect to the figure that makes it both indicative of personality as well as create a sense of familiarity that feels as though they are known people. The figures are aware, and in most cases strike a pose in response to compositional situations. It’s kind of like responding when someone tells you to “smile” or is staring at you. It is an invitation to interact with the painting. They almost force you to play a role in their narrative through the figure’s reactive nature. Inciting you, inviting you to have a good time.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Artist statement This is a sensual-themed body of work that grew as I recoiled from our acrimonious social climate. I felt the need for a figurative, collective, and supportive hug. The work speaks from a sensual female voice. One that confident in her value and worth both to herself and the society at large. The backstory to this series is one of female sensuality as a powerful force. A viewpoint that challenges the patriarchal hierarchy. Females and female identifiers are too often shamed for their power. Thus undermining their vitality and beauty. Sensuality can lead to sexuality, yet sensuality is a power in its own right. Female sensuality can be perceive as a threat in a patriarchal society. Allowing for disrespect and dismissive behavior. Female and female identifiers respond by policing themselves in regards to their body. This series celebrates feminine/female energy. Process I establish the shape of each character as a line drawing before transferring the design to silk. The color palette is created using a staining process. The embroidery layer finishes the personality of the artwork. I work in a conversational mode as the personality takes shape. Each mark adds depth, dimension and emotion. I am creating a personality that I am meeting for the first time. My choice of using sewing and embroidery as a medium is intentional. I am using a medium that is considered women’s craft not fine art. Usurping the male dominated accepted forms of fine art creation. My Asian influence: I grew up with my mother’s collection of Asian art and artifacts. The graceful movements of asian calligraphy spoke volumes to me. The narrative quality of Japanese woodblock prints brought me into a magical world. Not able to read the languages, my reactions were visceral; a response to the rhythmic line and shapes. These works still hold a power over me. Material choice: Working with dupioni silk supports and complements the sensual narrative. The initial drawing template is on paper and then transferred to the white silk. A staining process establishes the color palette, before I begin embroidering . The finished piece hangs as a silk scroll secured with magnetic bars top and bottom. They can also suspended in a frame.
