column25: Carol Scavotto is a visual and performing artist based in Rhode Island. Her decades-long artistic practice has included sculpture, painting, needlework, fine jewelry design, and performance pieces. Her work is both highly autobiographical and deeply relatable. Carol earned a BFA in sculpture from the University of Massachusetts Amherst in 1972 and also completed training in the early 1980’s at the Jewelry Institute of Rhode Island. For several years her artistic energy was funneled into building various art-related businesses, including founding The Springfield Arts Center in The mid 1970’s in Massachusetts, and Casanna Design in Rhode Island, which featured her own line of Jewelry. Her drive to create space for showing her and others’ work included opening and running an Art Cafe, and establishing an art gallery housed within a high-end furniture store. The focus of Carol’s career shifted after a diagnosis and successful treatment of cancer in 2009. She became absorbed in creating work that was more introspective and socially relevant. She intensified her artistic practice and increased her presence in art galleries and art shows, establishing a recognizable voice in her work. Since 2010 Carol’s work has been exhibited yearly in group shows nationally and internationally. As a solo artist she has participated in several art fairs, including The Fountain Art Fair, Clio Art Fair and Superfine Art Fair (all in New YorkCity). Carol has works in permanent collections in the Maritime Museum and Presidio of Ushuasa (Argentina), and the Haegeumgang Theme Museum (Korea). She has also exhibited with galleries showing at The Affordable Art Fair (Amsterdam), CICA Museum (Korea), Asia Contemporary Art Fair (HongKong), and Art Busan (Busan, Korea). Carol’s work has been featured in numerous publications and press releases, including Clio Art Fair, Art511, Recap, an Art Uncovered interview, Boston Voyager, Superfine Art Fair 2018, Studio Magazine, Newport Mercury, Artfuse, Boston Globe, and the Fall River Herald, and many more.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Ghost of a Dream's work embodies the essence of opulence while being constructed of materials that typically end up in the trash. We mine popular culture searching for discarded materials that people use trying to reach their goals. Whether it is a Hollywood film that transports the viewer into a dream reality, a travel poster promising a luxurious vacation, or a lottery ticket that gives the possibility of a future full of rich decadence; we use these remnants to both re-create people's dreams, and portray the dreamer. Our process leads us on a constant quest to find the hopes and dreams of a culture or sub-group. Quite often we will build the actual thing people are dreaming about, using the resources and materials we have collected. When we began working with lottery tickets we built a trilogy of the three things people dream about, and then bought if they won the lottery, a dream car, vacation, and home. As we moved on to other cultural desires we began creating abstractions of the emotions one feels when gambling, or watching Hollywood films. Lately we have been working with both the representation of the dream object, and emotive abstractions in the same work. In “When the Smoke Clears; The Fair Housing Project” we have used the representation of a Iconic American house and within that shape is a series of abstract paintings. “Fair Housing Project” in made entirely of the detritus we have collected from art fairs to examine the wastefulness, over saturation, and the economic drive that keeps these fairs going. Along side our collection of materials, we also collect words; words we hear, words we see in advertising, and words that represent what is happening in a given time and culture. Much like with the objects we collect, we take these words and use them in repeat as our pallet to create line in drawings, and lately, to create large site-specific wall drawings. The diversity of our subject and material we collect leads us to varied techniques and methods of making. Our work spans the gamut; utilizing drawing, collage, video, installation, sculpture, photography, and painting to produce our projects. Having this multivalent way of working gives the viewer multiple entry points into the work, and an opportunity to open the range of what can be learned, experienced, and discussed.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In my artistic journey, I grapple with the profound quest for purpose in a world saturated with routine. The absence of meaning renders us mechanical, devoid of the essence that defines our humanity. I confront the imbalance between our physical presence and the neglect of our spiritual nourishment in a society fixated on ceaseless labor. My work speaks to the neglect of our inner selves, the emaciation of our souls amidst the abundance of our bodies. Through my art, I merge the human form with the natural world, employing mirrors as potent symbols revealing the obscured truths of our frantic lives. Nature, an innate source of our emotions, mirrors our deepest desires and serves as a conduit between our inner and outer realms. I seek to provoke introspection, urging us to halt the relentless pursuit of daily existence and reconnect with our intrinsic essence. By embracing moments of awareness and introspection, we unearth the profound meanings woven into our lives. These revelations serve as beacons, illuminating a path toward a more meaningful existence, not only for ourselves but for the broader world. My project embodies this struggle, depicting the human figure among rocks, clutching a mirror—an allegory for the truth eclipsed by our chaotic lifestyles. The mirror, a conduit between our inner truths and the external world, symbolizes the invaluable connection between our inner selves and the world around us. Through my art, I aim to prompt a collective journey toward self-discovery and the revitalization of our neglected souls in an ever-demanding world.

Her art, often blending nature with depictions of the human body, delves into the intricate reality of female identity through personal experiences. Shafieikadkani frequently omits her subjects' faces, prioritizing body language over conforming to expected feminine ideals. Her photographs have been showcased internationally, and she participated in the Documentary Lumix Photo Festival. She collaborates with the Middle East Images Agency, showcasing her surrealist photo series. Azarakhsh obtained her MA in Graphic Design from Ferdows University in Mashhad, Iran, and is presently pursuing her MFA in Studio Art at Florida Atlantic University. She has received several awards, including WITVA (Women in the Visual Arts), Friedland, NSAL (National Society of Arts & Letters), and Rothenberger in 2023. Her recent project involves merging mirror-work with photographs, creating interactive art through mirror reflections.'

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
One of the main reasons I started building my artistic career from the ground up is because I felt a calling to share my artistic vision and energetic gifts with the world. It brings me a lot of joy seeing who each of my pieces get paired with. But as a queer Black artist from a humble background it can be hard to find the resources and the extra time to reach people beyond my home state and I think that's a shame because there's a lot about my background that makes my work stand out. I had to start out using supplies I could find or get on sale or for cheap which has influenced the way I layer colors and my tendency to default to using mixed mediums. All of my jewelry is crafted with their energetic properties in mind and when I display them at vending events , they've called to people on multiple occasions. My art pieces are usually worked on using auspicious timing to match the celestial energy I want to evoke, and a great number of my fine art and jewelry is inspired by my experience as a diviner (much of it Tarot inspired). The reason I do this varies, but in general I try to put good into the world. Whether the intent is to provoke a reaction, teach a lesson, or share an experience with my audience, I always reflect the subject with the divine timing I'm moved to work with. At times items I find in nature find their way on a necklace or a canvas (such as the coyote skull on the painting I submitted). There's no one particular reason I like to include them other than finding the natural world beautiful. Connecticut is densely populated, but there's also a lot of woods between the urban areas and suburbs and the dense mixed forest dominate our rural areas, so there is a lot of natural local flora and fauna to draw inspiration from. Earth science has always been a soft interest of mine, and as a pagan I believe that Nature is the best artist and everyone else just attempts to replicate Her beauty.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work questions objectivity, subjectivity, and individuality within the context of environment. Incorporating materials discarded and overlooked, I examine whether these have intrinsic value, or prescribed value based on their use. By pulverizing these objects they often lose their natural form, and become hidden in the depths of a painting, becoming a part of the larger whole, an element within a larger context. For Me, these materials become an allegory for the value of an individual person, examining if somebody is more than merely the sum of their parts, or perhaps; are all these parts the same. Using postindustrial materials in contrast with raw materials, I explore how these materials function within society, and for those using them. Wondering constantly whether he is a big part of something small, or a small part of something big, or perhaps nothing at all. The paintings reference phenomena which are ambiguously similar despite being drastically different scales. I create these paintings with no end in mind beyond a basic color, a vague shape. relying heavily on intuition of how the viscosity of the medium will be effected by physics, mimicking universal patterns. I make them with spit, acrylic, oil, and alcohol as mediums for pigment and pulverized materials I find from bone to stone to metal, ground up with an angle grinder, mortar & pestle, or the old hammer. These paintings were, for years, a meditation on my extreme anxiety and existential dread. A means of channeling chaos, and the unknown, from being such a small insignificant and ignorant speck, eventually providing something to reflect on within that maelstrom of discontent and confusion. Over the years, particularly during covid lockdowns, my focus shifted to embracing the idea of infinity, overcoming my fears, and appreciating the unknown, enjoying the feeling of two questions arising for every answer I find.

Vania Evangelique, formally known as Zeal Eva, works within the visual realms of painting, photography and curation. Being a visual storyteller her work is deeply rooted in personal history and is heavily influenced by the communities in which she navigates. Her practice, in its rawest form, focuses its foundation on the idea of looking away to look back. She creates works that move her towards the future, while also stepping outside of the present, and in so doing, being able to create and draw parallels from her past that tell of a more dynamic and vulnerable story. She looks beyond the mere creation of art and centers her work into the personhood of it, that is often found in the mundane, transitional, and sometimes stretching processes of life. In her pieces, she utilizes themes of nostalgia, longing, the complicated realities of comfort, all while exploring the intersections of the natural world and the built environments. Through layered processes, and integrated mediums, she anchors herself to the present in this way, allowing her to draw from the imagined, to reality and back again.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The NYC Subway Project consists of a series of 360^ Virtual Reality Illustrations depicting NYC subway stations in random future scenarios. Each of these illustrations can be accessed via a QR code thus bringing in an augmented reality experience to the user. These QR codes have been printed on to stickers and placed in the live locations so that these illustrations of the future are placed accurately in the 3D world. I started working on this project during my time at Parsons School of Design and wanted it to be a way through which I can immerse myself into the urban landscape of New York. I chose subway stations as the locations for my artwork because of its association to movement and change and how these spaces are constantly suspended in movement: everybody is Going Somewhere. I wanted to use this directional energy in contemplation of the future and in creating an immersive experience that empowers people of their own agency of choice in the future. For example, if someone experience the illustration of a future where the place is flooded in and destroyed due to floods because of global warming, the reality of this future would impact their conscience in a more meaningful way if it was a lived, immersive experience. The reactions of fear toward certain futures or of awe and excitement towards others will be an indicator itself of where the audience stands in regard to the factors in our present life that lead to those potential future outcomes and can initiate dialog at small levels. Essentially I am trying to make a commentary of the present through depictions of the future and to explore beauty in the face of destruction, apocalypse or dystopia. The themes or the future scenarios that I will be making in my illustration will be made in an attempt to initiate dialogue and thoughts about the choices we make as a society and where these choices could lead us. I want this project to feel like we are living in a rapidly changing futuristic world where the metaverse already exists and empower the audience to feel like they are part of this ever changing world and can choose their future scenario just the same way that I am visualizing the different scenarios and countless artists have probably done before me. By making these illustrations as accessible as possible, I feel like my work enters a new genre of street art that extends into the metaverse and opens up the canvas of reality in new ways. Not only are we able to experience our reality as it is but also simultaneously experience a future vision... By making this accessible I want these new and growing fields of technology to feel within reach to the general public. I want to ground the virtual world in the physical one and interconnect them both conceptually and literally. I have so far, made four illustrations. One depicting a future with cyborgs, a flooded apocalypse, or where nature has taken over; even then there are countless directions our futures can go and I want to explore how this will change as I change the locations of the drawings. I am currently applying for grants or funding to continue this endeavour into the future.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice is 'anti-focus' meaning that instead of looking through a microscope to define meaning, my work centers on emphases at will. This approach adds an impactful and moment-relevant take to different dialogs and allows me to sustain my work in visual storytelling. I question what it is that we experience from first impressions, brief glances, and fleeting moments. Guests on average spend 15-30 seconds looking at art. As a curator, exhibitions are a way for me to explore the use of visual impact through flow, combinations, emphasis, and space. This method unifies artworks with the experience of looking at them in a story as opposed to singled out. Supported by other 'unfocused' aspects such as ADHD and other neurodivergent experiences, the internet and short-form experiences, and unrelated research, exhibitions can be more accessible and interesting to people outside of traditional arts audiences.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I understand my work as an ongoing investigation of cultural continuities from Africa to America and I am particularly interested in how these ancient cultural forms find their way into mainstream culture. Thus, elements of Nigerian royal regalia, sports equipment with significant ties to African American history, Southern voodoo/hoodoo traditions, and quotes from hip-hop artists are seamlessly blended with beaded patchwork employing the same techniques used by the New Orleans Mardi Gras Indians. My most recent body of work is comprised of boxing gloves hand-embroidered with beads, using a backstitch, an adaptation of Yoruba beading traditions and Native American beading techniques. I also weave in objects of power and protection, such as High John the Conqueror root, a staple in American Hoodoo traditions and other hidden talismans. My work connects contemporary hip-hop artists to the tradition of the Oba (Yoruba king), where lyrical quotes and personas embody superhuman power and even some, like hip hop artist Pusha T, who call themselves gods. In the Yoruba and New Orleans tradition, men are the creators of beaded regalia; however, this is not the case in mainstream American culture where beading and weaving techniques are often seen as women’s work. My mash up of bead weaving, which quotes hypermasculine phrases from hip-hop songs, creates an intentional tension and contrast between delicate and powerful, beautiful and dark, masculine and feminine. The use of cowrie shells adds an additional layer of complexity to the underlying ideas in my work. Cowrie shell sculptures in the Yoruba tradition are called Ilé Ori or House of the Head Shrines. Ilé Ori are shrines to a person’s spiritual essence; protected by a shield of cowrie shells. During the height of the Transatlantic Slave Trade, human beings were purchased with cowrie shells brought by Portuguese slave ships. In some cases, owning an Ilé Ori could protect a wealthy Yoruba person from being sold into slavery. This juxtaposition of beauty, pain, power, and influence can be seen throughout my series; as the shells in my artwork represent the violence and wealth of the slave trading economy – a heritage that continues in sports and hip-hop lyrics.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist, I’m interested in ruin. I draw and sculpt as a way to process my fears around the body’s vulnerabilities and inevitable failings. My work from the last two years deals specifically with my anxieties around ruin and motherhood - the transformation of flesh, the violence of birth, and the demands of the newborn. I’m influenced by classical Italian depictions of motherhood: the grotesque elongation of limbs in Parmigianino’s Madonna with the Long Neck, the bleeding breasts in Artemisia Gentileschi’s Mother and Child among others. My work is also influenced by the natural world - how wolves, snakes, and other animals transform as they become mothers. I find something exciting in the idea that mothering brings forth the monstrous within each of us. I’m interested in the unusual power that emerges when we become our most grotesque, animate selves. It’s through copying and re-imagining various depictions of motherhood that I begin to find my own definitions. I come to terms with the complexity of such an experience - the violence and tenderness, the sensuality and fierceness, and the generative and destructive nature of making and mothering another.

column25: In my artistic practice, I explore the human experience by utilizing corrugated cardboard and repurposed wood frames. These materials, often overlooked and considered waste, become the foundation for my expressive artworks. By repurposing these materials, I aim to challenge the traditional boundaries of figurative art and create a dialogue between the subject matter and the medium itself. The use of corrugated cardboard as a primary medium adds a unique texture and depth to my sculptures. The ridges and layers of the cardboard create a sense of dimensionality, allowing me to capture the intricate details and contours of the human form. The juxtaposition of the raw and humble nature of the cardboard with the elegance and complexity of the human face creates a captivating visual contrast that invites viewers to explore the interplay between materiality and subject. Each sculpture in the series I titled 'Frames of Mind' represents a specific facial emotion commonly depicted as an emoji. The juxtaposition of the transient nature of digital communication and the more concrete, but very malleable nature of cardboard, invites viewers to consider the fragility and impermanence of our emotional states. The frames used in this series are old. Each frame carries its own history and character, adding a layer of storytelling to the overall composition. I refinished each frame by painting it with a color that match the intended expression. Through facial expressions and repurposed materials, I hope to inspire viewers to reconsider the potential of discarded materials and the beauty that can be found in unconventional mediums. By breathing new life into these materials, I aim to challenge preconceived notions of art and encourage a more sustainable approach to creativity. I seek to celebrate the human form, its resilience, and the inherent beauty that can be found in the most unexpected places.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create immersive, multidimensional artworks that challenge the boundaries and semantics of traditional painting. By blurring the lines between painting and sculpture, I expand the spatial presence of my pieces, reimagining the place and space of this traditional art form. My artworks transcend traditional wall displays and incorporate three-dimensional elements that shift on, beneath, or behind the canvas. They can be showcased on walls, stands, pedestals, turntables, and as floor-standing artworks that invite viewers to walk around or rotate them, enabling exploration from various vantage points. Additionally, I utilize the backsides of the canvas, offering the audience a different and more interactive experience that departs from the traditional static frontal viewing typically associated with painting. I create hybrid entities, constructing and deconstructing, revealing a pseudo-animation that exposes the intricate process, layered meanings, and evolving forms within. Through this approach, I showcase the artist's brushstrokes, planning tools, and techniques, creating a visual narrative that evolves over time. Drawing inspiration from diverse contexts, cultures, and disciplines, my themes are influenced by current events, personal and social issues, pop culture, cultural trends, and art about art. This exploration of uniting different dimensions and creating paintings that extend beyond traditional wall displays stems from my experiences as a transplant in different cultures. My artworks visually convey the concept of moving entities and their transformations, challenging preconceived notions of how a painting can be viewed and experienced, both within and beyond the confines of the wall. My approach addresses the two pivotal components of a painting: the application techniques of colors and forms by an artist and the frontal viewing of the outcome by a spectator. Not much has changed in this system for ages, yet one partner in this equation has moved on—the viewers and their habits. Viewing habits have developed; the duration and method of observation have shifted. The modern-day partner in this system is exposed to overstimulation that has dampened their attention span, and their expectations as spectators have changed. Virtual reality, immersive technologies, moving images, and electronic scrolling have turned art viewing into a faster-paced endeavor. Viewing art has mostly stopped being a contemplative action that engrosses and takes time. I create paintings that change the system slightly for both parties. I try to shift the onlooker’s attention and the viewing habits of the digital age spectator for a few more seconds by creating paintings that require a revised and longer look, one that goes beyond a passive, frontal viewing and questions preconceived notions of how we can view a painting, what a painting is, and can be, both within and beyond the confines of the wall.

column25: An Israeli-born, New York-based multidisciplinary artist, I hold a BFA in Sculpture and Painting from the Bezalel Academy of Art and Design in Jerusalem. At Bezalel, I received the Helena Rubinstein - American-Israeli Culture Foundation grant and was chosen by Philip Lieder, the curator, critic, and founding editor of ARTFORUM, as one of the ten most promising artists of the 80s in a publication commemorating the inauguration of Bezalel's new campus. In 1993, holding dual citizenship in the United States and Israel, I moved to New York to expand my artistic practice. Over the years, I've established myself as an educator, lecturer, and artist. I've lectured on Israeli art history and my own artworks through lectures at universities, institutions like the Scholar in Residence Series at the San Francisco Library and as a guest lecturer elsewhere. Alongside my artistic pursuits, I've held diverse positions, including working at the Tate Americas Foundation (American Patrons of Tate), and instructing art privately as well as in various schools, community centers, and institutions in New York City, Brooklyn, and Jerusalem. Throughout my career, I have had over 50 group shows and 5 solo exhibitions in the United States, Israel, and Germany, and my artworks are held in private collections. Currently, I devote myself full-time to my artistic practice.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Living within a universe of my own self-reflection makes me intensely curious about the inner lives of others. While it is a pursuit frustrated by the impossibility of knowing something without experiencing it myself, I’m driven to embody my imaginings so that I can bring them forward for conscious consideration. Introspective energies and private moments interest me. I access my intuitive knowledge of humanness and the natural world via materials that respond to my touch and atmospheric firing processes that respond to material and form. Working with human figures surfaces my empathic connection to human experiences. In my studio practice, the desire to ease anxieties about my own physical and mental states manifests in the choice to create figures that appear to have resiliency and quietude. I make choices that stand in for emotional states, figuratively — through gestures like an unfocused inward or upward gaze, and materially — via marks from atmospheric firings, surfaces that reflect organic qualities, and textures that reference natural processes. My intent is for one to imagine the surface of the skin or the atmosphere around the figure as a reflection of what is happening inside the body and mind.

column25: I am a Visual Artist from the United States working primarily with mixed-media collage, painting, drawing and digital art. My materials often include Sumi e Ink, Calligraphy brushes, charcoal, pastel, watercolor, acrylic, and a variety of papers including Japanese rice paper and canvas. My work involves exploration and experimentation through brushwork, markings and layered, open passages that lead the viewer on a journey of discovery, associations and insights. Through observing the relationships of applied materials to a surface, layers of seemingly random associations can merge into realization, recognition and awareness. These perceptions can then transform into a meaningful and unified whole. I think of creating this work as though I am sifting through an archeological dig of the subconscious mind, moving as if though layers upon layer of earth. Barely recognizable forms emerge from the surface of the paintings into a conscious gestalt where meanings are meant to galvanize through the viewer’s own personal references and connections. Surrealism in the early 20th Century used Psychic Automatism combined with acts of automatic writing based on the works of Sigmund Freud to seek revelations of the Subconscious mind in order to break through the mundane. My collages combine automatic writing as well as asemic writing with a similar purpose. Surrealism drew inspiration from other non-western primal cultures which provided an alternative set of aesthetic and social values. I also incorporate Nano images that capture the microscopic scale with my collages, combining these size references with a more cosmic orientation. I am honored to have my collages with Nano images included in the mission of MoonArk. The nine-ounce MoonArk -- a tiny time capsule-esque artifact of humanity -- will be attached to a small lunar rover with the Moon as its planned destination. The landing of MoonArk will be in Spring of 2023. The hope of this project is that one day The MoonArc may be picked up by lunar explorers -- hundreds or thousands of years in the future. Originally known as the Moon Arts Project, MoonArk was designed in response to the 2007 Google Lunar XPRIZE competition.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Creating dynamic gateways to alternate realms, with vibrant paintings, collages, and wall hangings. Infused with a riot of colors, my creations beckon viewers to challenge the conventional solidity of reality. The work is a harmonious blend of soft subtlety and vivid intensity, that skillfully weaves together saturated precision and spontaneous openness, encapsulating the ebb and flow of movement, time, and emotion. In the realm of wall hangings and canvases, I immerse layers of gouache, dye and watercolor into the velvet, creating layers of intricate details. My collage process unfolds with the transformation of ink-drenched yupo sheets, meticulously hand-cut and realigned into symmetrical formations. These formations echo the patterns found in nature, the cosmos, and anthropological history. My exploration delves into the very essence of equilibrium, intertwining symbols, symmetry and organic formations. my intentional and intuitive process oscillates between fully formed ideas and those discovered in the act of creation. Creating this work allows me to connect with my spirituality and express the truest version of myself, I aim to share this deep connection with you, the viewer. As you experience my pieces, I hope they unravel the limitless possibilities of the present moment and evoke a sense of mystery that resonates with your own journey.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a multi-media artist who has a passion for making. For me making art is not an option, it's a need. While I do have longer-term projects that I plan out and spend extended time on, I also create a variety of smaller, abstract, or expressionistic works. These are made out of my need to create, and they tend to be a snapshot that I need to get out before I lose it. My use of different mediums really depends on how much time I expect to devote to each piece. Works that take longer are typically oil paintings, woodcuts, and sculptures that are made of wire. These are works that I have ongoing in my studio, and have a focus on nature and specific glimpses of humanity. Works that I make spontaneously are made of markers, magazine collages, watercolor, and acrylic paint. These mediums allow me to work quickly, translating the moment directly onto the canvas. Art has always been a complex language to me that has taken me years to begin understanding. I have always been drawing, but through expanding my mediums and art education, I have been able to understand what I love about making art. I want to learn many more mediums and explore new combinations of subjects and media to deepen my understanding of art and artists, as well as my self and my own passions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Papier-mache is a material used historically in Iran and the surrounding regions (dating back as early as the 16th-century Safavid) to make lacquered artifacts. I make sculptures out of papier-mache inspired by Esimi and Khataei motifs that were widely used in the paintings on lacquered papier-mache objects as well as manuscript paintings. My sculptures evoke a range of femme bodily forms with their sensual curves and skin-like character of my papier-maché. Through these bodies I speak of personal and collective traumas, memories, and dichotomies. My use of papier-maché carries connotations of challenging gender dynamics and reclaiming artistic expression. Men primarily dominated this art form. Now, I am an Iranian woman who makes papier-maché artwork in a large scale. Moreover, bringing this historical context to the contemporary, I reverse the surface to rough textures, the paintings to abstract, and the two-dimensional tradition into a three-dimensional space. My sculptures symbolize standing up for one’s values, identity, gender, and femininity. Their scale and dimensions, alongside the skin likeness of papier-maché, hold symbols of suffering, protest, and persistence. When integrated with video or sound, my sculptures go beyond the traditional borders and create an immersive experience.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I started customizing sneakers in late 2002. It was during a time where only a really small handful of people were customizing and the industry was still in its infancy. Being a person who was big into sneakers, art, and fashion, I became inspired to try my hand at sneaker customization as I saw it as a great opportunity to make use of my art background. My goal was to create my favorite shoes in colorways that weren't available in stores and to stand out from the crowd. Through tons of research and trial and error I would develop my skills over time and even did some simple colorway themes for friends in the neighborhood but for the most part still did it only as a hobby. Fast forward to 2007-2008. During that period, I had gotten laid off from a full time day job that I worked for years. It was a tough period where I went through a lot of trials and tribulations in my personal life and I was able to take solace in my art. It was during that time where I officially came up with Ecentrik (formerly Ecentrik Artistry) and begin to take my craft more seriously. Continuing to hone the craft and with the internet continuing to become a regular part of everyday life, I saw it as an opportunity to expand on my gift and began to use social media as a platform to promote my work as I continued to build my brand. A self taught artist, the name Ecentrik (pronounced as 'Eccentric') represents my personal style as I am a big fan of abstract art and graffiti and I use those elements in my art style. The vision for my brand is to create original work with a signature style and vibe while presenting each pair of custom footwear as a piece of art that happens to be wearable. Over the last couple years, the brand has attracted clientele both domestic and abroad as well as some well known personalities in different fields of entertainment. My mission is to continue to build the brand as well as inspire future generations of artists to come.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Lena Regina I am an interdisciplinary and mixed media artist. My work explores the joining of color, texture and shapes on canvas. I create paintings of things I personally experience from my other senses. Having a visual impairment has heightened my other senses such as sound, touch and smell. I rely on my other senses to help me create art. I paint outdoors and when I smell flowers, it inspires me to paint what I am smelling. Sometimes I will hear the hummingbirds or bees and I paint them. I use my fingers, hands and unconventional objects to paint onto canvas. I do not have control over my eyesight and so I have allowed myself to lose control while painting and not to rely on traditional paint brushes. I create art for art’s sake. I make art because I love creating and I love the process. I spent 20 years of my life creating art (wedding photographs) for couples and their families and now I want to continue the art process but with other mediums. My work is colorful, whimsical and expressive. I use a combination of acrylics, watercolor, ink, glitter, markers, pencils and any medium that is in my art closet. I like to push the boundaries by trying things that may or may not work. I believe in the power of creation and will not allow a limited sense to stand in my way. I am finding different ways to express and communicate through my art.

It is the feeling of being swallowed in a new landscape, we feel the scale of ourselves and the awakening of our senses to the new tactile environment.

column25: In my practice, ceramics is a means of sharing and archiving queer Cantonese diasporic histories and futurisms. My practice reaches beyond the pedestal, moving from PhD studies in historiography through a visual medium involving performance, collaboration, kinship, ceremony; honoring and uncovering a queer Cantonese past absent from hegemonic archives, and bringing community together to imagine a future in which those who have been objectified in the margins of hegemonic U.S. histories can lovingly thrive. “cocoon” sprang from research on the Piedmont Silk Experiment in Oakland, in which Mrs. Hittle purchased land via federal funding and published a call to “awake to action” via appropriating silkworm farming, or U.S. domesticity would be “hopeless” against Chinese immigration (18th Century Press). In 2020, I read Hittle’s writings in the Bancroft Archives and raised silkworms bred on perhaps the mulberry trees Hittle had planted – a quiet rebuttal. “cocoon” was a portal of death, resurrection, transformation, and futurity. A performance below it showed the silkworm embodied as conduit of the Cantonese diaspora amidst anti-Chinese sentiment. About 700 PPE masks make up 'cocoon,' holding the complexities of the 2019-21 attempted Hong Kong revolution reframed as devices of racial objectification, violence, and protection. “Celestial Beings, Celestial Bodies” installation for the Berkeley Art Museum was a yearning expression of erased histories. Porcelain lanterns millimeters thin with patterns of silkworm cocoons cast shadows on an archival photographic collage highlighting the sinophobic term “celestial bodies” used in the U.S. during early Cantonese immigration. The takeaway of mulberry paper cites each photo, including community historian and leader David Lei, archival and personal evidence. “Celestials” were used along with “creatures,” “threats” and “orientals” as racialized terms used to further separate Cantonese people as “others” - non-human beings with non-normative bodies, eyes, and eating habits (Journal of Historical Archaeology 2012). I was raised by an immigrant single parent and taught from a young age the importance of cultural inheritance and ancestral lineage. My series 'These I will never lose' pays homage to my traumatic familial history of running from racialized domestic violence, losing home and belongings; in which I place childhood memories and dreams atop temple pedestals. In this sense, domestic, mundane, or quotidian objects become relics. “To See and Be Seen, To Love and Be Loved” and “May our family be safe and peaceful” are part of my current body of pieces informed by work with a local transgender Chinese astrologer, trained by Masters Meng Yu and Zhongxian Wu. I am learning Chinese astrology “BaZi,” an ancient wisdom not yet translated into English. Using my BaZi chart, I am creating the mythological creatures of ancient Chinese astrology, embedding each with personal histories and symbolic queer futurisms.

column25: My work explores the intricate push and pull between heritage, identity, and the complex dynamics of acceptance and rejection, belonging and estrangement, and the emotions associated with fitting in or standing out within the tapestry of cultures and communities. At its heart lies a deep examination of the Filipino American experience, a rich and multifaceted narrative that resonates with individuals of all different backgrounds who are navigating the complex terrain of cultural integration. My creative process mirrors the synthesis of cultures and the negotiation of identities. Much like the communities that embrace or reject us, I draw from both the tactile textures of my Filipino heritage and the contemporary patterns of American life. Art, like cultural identity, is a perpetual negotiation, weaving between the past and the present, an evolving dialogue between tradition and innovation. I seek to express the nuanced stories of those who find themselves caught between cultures. My work explores the tension between the yearning to feel connected with one's community and the struggle to adapt and thrive within a disparate environment. It delves into the paradox of belonging, the pain of rejection, the desire to fit in, and the courage to be authentic. Each artwork is a visual essay, a thread in the larger tapestry of the Filipino diaspora and the broader human experience. Through the use of indigenous materials such as piña fabric and capiz shells, I aim to create pieces that resonate with the universal challenge of navigating one’s sense of identity, acceptance, and community. My art invites the viewer to explore the ever-shifting boundaries of culture and the profound impact of acceptance and rejection on one's sense of self. It is a reminder that while cultural clashes may challenge our identity and belonging, they also give rise to new forms of expression, resilience, and empowerment. Through my work, I hope to inspire dialogues about the complexities of identity and self-determination, and the enduring power of cultural narratives in shaping our lives and our sense of belonging within a diverse and evolving world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
and the disappearing has become questions what it means when one’s memories fade after the death of a loved one and how this forgetting affects a person. After witnessing my grandmother’s death in our house, it became clear how temporary life is and how quickly memories fade without one’s control. I’ve forgotten many aspects about my grandmother, and there is a fear one day it will be my family’s faces, their laugh, and how it felt to be next to them that will be forgotten. What will become of me when they pass, and I am the one left behind? and the disappearing has become uses photography, mono-printing techniques, and erasing with sandpaper to embody the process of forgetting. This process being one where memories overlap, become obscured, and buried. The gestural act of sanding and painting works to claim this unconscious process of forgetting as a necessary part of life rather than as something harmful. Although bodies and memories dissolve, the ones we love will forever remain etched into the fabric of our being. Form may change but it does not disappear. The work that I share is grief work, it is mourning, it is the healing process. I share this project as a gesture of seeing each other. By seeing how others go through similar things, one feels less alone in grief. Through sharing my own struggles to cope with my family’s death, the photographs open a door for conversation about death and healing.

Carney is an artist and educator that uses autobiographical photography to question how death and grief affect familial connections—the bonds that keep a family together and cause them to separate, and how grief shapes an individual. Much of Carney’s artistic practice deals with confronting and exposing grief surrounding death to create an avenue for not only himself but also for others to find healing and cope with the deaths in their lives. Carney is originally from Baltimore, Maryland, and currently teaches at the Herron School of Art + Design at Indiana University Indianapolis. He received his MFA in Photography at Indiana University Bloomington. He holds a BFA in Photography from Pennsylvania College of Art &. Design. He is the recipient of the Bloomington Arts Commission 2022 Emerging Artist Grant. He has been awarded the First Place Single Image Award from LensCulture Art Photography Awards 2023 and the Best in Show Award in the 2020 Emerging Vision, Colorado Photographic Arts Center Biennial Student Show. Carney’s work has been exhibited nationally and internationally.

column25: Fashion, makeup, sparkly jewelry, and cultural adornments and traditions from my African American upbringing are the main focus of my current series of works. Through my art, I seek to not only celebrate the unique cultural characteristics of the African diaspora, but also to exude sophistication, grace, poise, and glamour through playful and sultry compositions. Every stroke of my brush, every delicate detail, is imbued with a sense of whimsy that transports viewers to a different time and place. It is a homage to the vintage black editorial works of the 70s, 80s, 90s, and early 2000s, which continue to inspire me as I navigate the world of art. Yet at the heart of my work lies a deep appreciation and reverence for afrocentricity. I am constantly inspired by the rich cultural heritage of the African diaspora, and I aim to infuse my pieces with the vibrant rhythms, intricate patterns, and powerful symbolism that define this culture. My art becomes a visual expression of the stories, and the triumphs that have shaped my culture. Through my art, I hope to create a sense of connection and pride that celebrates the beauty and diversity of black culture, inviting viewers from all walks of life to appreciate and honor the rich tapestry that it represents. Glamour is also an important element in my work. With each brushstroke, I seek to capture the essence of these imaginary women, shining with confidence and radiance, demanding attention from the viewer. It is through their captivating gazes and impeccable style that I convey a celebration of femininity, beauty, and self-love. In a world that often tries to define and confine women, my art stands as a reminder that they beautiful and worthy of admiration. It is my intention to inspire and empower women of all ages and backgrounds through my art, allowing them to see their own beauty reflected in these bold and captivating images. Through the canvas of my art, I aspire to create a world that not only celebrates the beauty and power of black women, but also ignites conversations and fosters nostalgia. Art has a transformative power, capable of inspiring, healing, and bringing people together. With each stroke of the brush, I aim to create pieces that evoke fascination and invite viewers to engage in conversations about identity, culture, and the complexities of the human experience. My dedication to creating vibrant and connected worlds through my art is a testament to my belief in the profound impact that artistic expression can have on society. It is my hope that my pieces serve as catalysts, bridging gaps, breaking down barriers, and ultimately fostering a more inclusive and empathetic world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create mixed media abstractions that are process-driven, heavily patterned, colorful, tactile, and inspired by a deep appreciation and desire to protect the natural world. Made primarily from repurposed aluminum cans, that have been washed, cut, woven and individually painted on both sides. The paint consists of a mixture of primer, acrylic and mediums designed for non-porous surfaces. Each strip is unique, where the paint is color matched to existing labels, expands upon or obstructs any iconography or text that may pre-exist on the surface. The paint application is loose and painterly to provide a softness in contrast to the semi-exposed texts and barcodes that reveal the base materials commercial origins. I construct these pieces first by weaving cut open cans with wire, selecting them for the colors and patterns. The width and length of these strips of aluminum are created to mimic brushstrokes that add texture, diversity in surface and movement throughout the piece. Once the overall form is complete, each individual strip of aluminum is painted as if it is one unique small painting. My work breaks the traditional frame, with organic forms and a depth that pushes the work into a conversation with sculpture. The work remains painterly through composition and technique, where the paint plays a significant, but secondary role. The audience first observes the materials, finding recognition and familiarity in an otherwise abstract form. Upon closer inspection, one discovers the brush strokes and patterning is created not just by existing labels but through paint application. The aluminum I source for my work comes primarily from craft beer cans as a symbol of my economic class. This material is also selected because it is produced and consumed largely for a male audience, not dissimilar to the Art World itself. Unlike the Art World however, it is an industry that is environmentally conscious and uses this language of “craft” as a method to elevate its product rather than to devalue. As an industry that is attempting to create its own unique definition, it is the perfect material for pushing my own definitions of painting and fine art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is being able to play around with the ideas in my head and the various materials that bring these ideas to life. Being an artist feels like I get to hang onto the best part of my childhood - I continue to explore and work with my hands - I get to get dirty. As an artist I get to learn something every time I walk into my studio. I am continually learning about the materials I'm working with or learning something about myself. When my art connects to other people - that’s the best thing about being an artist. When someone comes into my studio and says, hey can I buy this print (painting, sculpture, ceramic mug)? I don’t have a lot of money but I really like this. Yes! Make me an offer! It’s yours! When someone wants to take one of my art babies home and put it in their home to interact with everyday... That is one of the best things about being an artist. Sharing my work with other people, knowing that they get joy out of something that came from my brain and my hands... I was never very good at the academic classes when I was in school - I was the kid who showed up for art, music and P.E. So, when people respond to the works I create it’s validating in a way that goes back to my childhood. The other great thing about being an artist is discovering other artists and learning about cultures other than my own. Community is so important and when I meet other artists and get to talk to them about their craft it fuels my energy. When I walk into a gallery or an art museum and see the creations that reflect the culture, history, and personal stories of artists from all walks of life and all periods of time; it is inspiring. When I took my first art history course in my undergrad program I finally understood world history. That was a monumental moment - the day I understood how the artworks my professor was showing me were actually a record of world history, and that I could learn about the world through art. What a discovery! There are many other things I love about being an artist but those are my top three. Playing with materials and ideas, connecting to other people, and learning about the world around me. I feel gratitude every time I walk into my studio that I have a space to try out new ideas. When I get frustrated with a painting or feel like the thing I’m working on looks like shit, I don’t really worry too much about it because I know it will change and eventually that thing I’m reaching for will come into focus. There is an element of trust that I have to give into. It’s a form of spirituality for me. It makes me feel like I’m right where I’m supposed to be.

column25: Living in the moment is how I have conducted my life, it is no surprise that this is how I create art. Sentient beings begin as doodles on the canvas. They take form as they wind around the composition and stretch out across the surface. When I start an abstract work I have no idea where it is going to take me. I sit with my paintings and allow them to guide the compositional choices I make and then stop painting when I recognize the work as complete. The anticipation of watching individual frames combine to create a moving narrative is what drives my interest in stop motion animation. I am interested in the interconnectedness of species; how all living beings are related by a spark that animates life. I imagine the spark continues and joins the energy of the universe after the biological body expires. Scratching into the paint, creating imperfections on the surface of the canvas, is a vulnerable action conveying pain and growth. I see threads that connect us, ethereal landscapes which could be real or imagined, sentient beings that survive against the odds, and molecules that float freely through the universe.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a young artist, it can be daunting to create work that speaks on political issues or has a lot of debate surrounding the topic. As a young Latina artist growing up in the states, I often had trouble accepting my culture and heritage. The one thing I always took note of, however, is how my parent's deportation at a young age affected my life. As I got older, I began to accept and take pride in my heritage and started to create more works surrounding the hardships children without parents due to deportation, must endure. 404 is just one of the many works I have created surrounding this topic. It is a digital projection speaking on the fact that once someone is defined as an immigrant, an alien, any other defining factors -- mom, dad, worker, husband, cousin, etc. -- are thrown out the window. It is intended to be installed or projected at a big scale, almost as if it were a billboard, allowing the viewers to place themselves within the picture, literally and figuratively.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The best thing about being an artist is finding community through art, and having a way to process the external world, to turn intangible feelings and experiences into physical works. My art community has been a solace for me, and when we live and breathe art, we attract like minded individuals who also have this drive to create. Although I have a community of like-minded artists surrounding me, that path manifests differently for each artist. For me, having diverse relationships to other artists with different interests continually shows the the expansive nature of what art has the ability to do. Through art we can question, process, and ultimately take action on the things we want to change in the world in a physical way. Being involved in that process in all different forms has been important to me. Although my primary work is oil painting, I have worked collectively many times, both in designing informative posters on community issues, mural painting, learning from established artists through assisting their work, and working with arts organizations to teach children art. I view all of these projects as part of the practice. Having the ability to be adaptable and meet the needs of a project or a community, I believe this is a strength of artists. I also feel incredibly grateful to have an outlet to process what is happening around me. Through art I can make the intangible real in a way, and build on history, literary sources, and visual motifs to speak about issues that feel relevant to me today. As a child I was highly sensitive, and could get overwhelmed very easily. Art became a way for me to feel through a healthy outlet, and develop a visual language to speak through when I feel overwhelmed.

column25: By challenging the boundaries of realism and figuration, I illustrate a concept of self that resists fixity, continually morphing within an ever-changing environment. Within my paintings, entities inhabit constructed realms of blurred airbrush and saturated colors. Multiplication of the figure serves as a means to navigate the diverse facets of 'selves,' exploring our bodily connections to sexuality, queerness, and the impact of external relationships and the digital realm on our internal landscape. The motif of skin, acting as both a physical container and symbolic boundary, assumes a central role, embodying the dichotomy of permanence and permeability, protection and wounds. In essence, my paintings serve as a dialogue between the corporeal and the ethereal. The figures, multiplied across canvases, undergo continual mutations, reflecting not only the somatic but also the psychological states of the portrayed individual. My practice is influenced by dream motifs and psychoanalytic theories of the self, as well as the surrealist practices of automatism, mirroring, and distortion, in which parts of the figure have been deconstructed and reconstructed through natural and technological interventions. The figure is always present in my work, yet as limbs and features are multiplied, and space is transformed to feel otherworldly, the moments captured are of a being stuck in a liminal state of flux, metamorphosing with one foot in reality and one out.

Most art I encountered was hidden behind a gallery door or a fee to get into a museum. A lot of visual art is not that accessible by the general public. There's a lot about living in America today that is difficult, unfair, and brutal. But adding some beauty, especially to underserved communities, gives me some peace, and makes me feel like I'm helping in some small way. That is something that I am extremely grateful for.

column25: I'm Liv Losee-Unger, a muralist originally from San Francisco and now based in Oakland. Since then, I've been fortunate to collaborate on vibrant murals with cities, organizations, communities, and businesses all over California. What drives me is creating beautiful, colorful, and lasting public artwork that celebrates environmentalism, nature, femininity, and community. I've teamed up with nonprofits like Habitat for Humanity of Greater Sacramento and embarked on a year-long mural workshop with inmates at California State Prison at Solano. Being a muralist to me means more than just painting walls; it's about storytelling, connecting with people, and infusing positive vibes into the spaces I work on.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
From a young age I have always doodled. Whether that was on printer paper my mom would give me, my notebooks during class, or with a pen on my skin. Throughout time these designs have evolved, some have changed, but there is a level of consistency with some that I have found fascinating.There is something about the repetition of shapes that has always calmed my mind. This type of repetition is the connection between my paintings and ceramic work. The same patterns I paint on a large scale paper appear carved into the surface of my vessels. I have researched art and the unconscious mind to figure out why drawing the same design serves as a therapeutic method of automatic drawing. Creating, time and time again, one would think you’d get bored but there is a level of comfortability with these shapes. They are familiar and controllable. They are a part of me as I am a part of them, staying with me all these years. With creativity guiding my way, my work is ever changing and always evolving. As I have explored different studio practices, I have found an intense drive to create abstract forms while incorporating these designs. The connection between my hands and the way I manipulate the material awakes something that feels bigger than me. My purpose is to create and to create by making forms, shapes, and patterns. These abstract nonrepresentational forms communicate in an indirect way. There is a power in letting a piece control and affect your urges. What makes you want to touch the carvings of a ceramic piece? What makes you look around the corner of a piece to see hidden parts that aren’t visible straight ahead? Imagination and curiosity and desire for understanding. We want to understand but why? Are we driven by connection and want to feel understood? Is this form making you feel seen a way words can not communicate. The balance between colors also plays a strong role throughout my work. Otherwise known as color theory which is the study of how colors work together and how they can affect our perceptions and emotions. My methodology with selecting the colors as always relied heavily on intuitively choosing ones I feel presently drawn to. Continuing my research, recently I started reading The Secret Lives of Color which tells stories about seventy five shades, dyes, and hues. This book has helped paved the way for more experimental and exploratory practices focusing on symbolic color usage.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The use of mixed media in their art allows James Mellor to channel the multifaceted nature of mental health. It reflects on the diverse and often fragmented elements of our psychological landscape. Just as the human psyche is an intricate interplay of thoughts, emotions, memories, and external influences, their artistic process incorporates an array of materials, textures, and techniques that seek to mirror the layering of emotions and experiences that shape our mental well-being. With mental health being the focus of their practice, Mellor believes that the subject is not linear or binary, it is a kaleidoscope of thoughts and struggles. In their work, you will find vibrant bursts of colour juxtaposed with deep shadows, reflecting the highs and lows of the human mind. By integrating contrasting elements, of mental health it emphasizes the notion that there is beauty in complexity and that the path to understanding our mental well-being is not always straightforward. The artistic process is marked by experimentation, adaptation, and a willingness to embrace the unexpected. Through layering, go through and reconstructing, mirroring the processes of self-discovery, and healing that individuals go through, on their own journeys. Aiming to evoke curiosity in viewers, encouraging them to explore the depth of their own emotions and experiences, the layers of their work often hide hidden messages, symbols, and meanings, inviting viewers to look beyond the surface and embark on their own journey of interpretation. Through the art, Mellor hopes to break down the stigma surrounding mental health and inspire self-reflection, which then provides a platform for individuals to share their stories, knowing they are not facing their struggles alone. In this ever-evolving exploration of the topic, the artist’s mixed media creations remind us that the beauty of the human experience lies not just in the successes, but in our vulnerability, resilience, and the journey toward understanding the intricacies of our own minds

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I sew soft sculptures that portray a tragic sense of humor, celebrating the hidden beauty I find in anxieties. I merge upcycled taxidermy with visceral imagery, which serves as a metaphor for emotional vulnerability. I also use glittery sheer fabrics and vivid faux fur to contrast difficult emotions against inviting textures - softening the rawness in order to make these complex sentiments more approachable. Iconic works from art history, like the Unicorn Tapestries, Laocoön Group, Lion Hunt of Ashurbanipal, The Arnolfini Portrait, among others, often serve as a starting point for my work. My process reinterprets these references, merging past narratives with contemporary themes. My aggressive hand-sewing, marked by bold red threads, is a deliberate choice similar to an expressive outline used in a drawing. Central to my process is the concept of repurposing materials from my old sculptures, cutting them up after an exhibition. Connecting remnants of past works to make new work, creates a layered history and narrative within the materials themselves. This is similar to pentimenti, the Italian word for “repentance,” which refers to the subtle evidence of corrections in a painting.

column25: As a muralist hailing from South Carolina, my artistic journey is deeply rooted in a commitment to making art a shared experience for all. My primary goal is to dissolve the traditional boundaries of art galleries and museums, reaching those who might not otherwise have access to these spaces. Growing up surrounded by the vibrant culture of Columbia, I discovered my passion for art at an early age. Now, as a muralist, I embark on a mission to bring the art gallery to the public, turning city walls into canvases that tell stories and evoke emotions. Each mural is a deliberate effort to make art an integral part of everyday life. My work extends beyond aesthetics; it's a conscious effort to engage communities and create connections. By adorning public spaces with vibrant colors and dynamic compositions, I aim to spark inspiration and bring a sense of wonder to diverse audiences. From the charming streets of Columbia to unexpected locations around the world, I adapt my artistic vision to weave narratives that resonate universally. I have a special place in my heart for children, and I started an initiative called “Walls Alive!” directly aimed at bringing public art to schools. My goal is to have the students engaged in the entire mural process: discussing themes, brain-storming, sketching, and even assisting in painting the mural. I feel that including them throughout the process creates a sense of ownership and community for the students and school. Being a muralist is not just about creating visually striking pieces; it's about fostering inclusivity and accessibility. I am particularly passionate about reaching those who might never step into a traditional art gallery. Art should be for everyone, and through my murals, I aspire to make it a part of the fabric of everyday life, enriching communities with creativity and storytelling.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I create sculptural assemblages constructed of various ceramic elements and found objects. The intersection of materials: found and handmade forms, with psychological and emotional connections are essential in making my artworks. My current work allows me to address loss and heartache by creating and connecting to the moments that have passed. Emotions and the psychology surrounding mortality are exposed and confronted through the action of making. Clay is a tactile material that offers a fragile result, reflecting life. Throughout our lives we come to the realization that our lives are precious and fleeting, we are not invincible, creating these precarious objects that are vulnerable reflect this. I have always been interested in ideas and rituals involving preservation, so dipping dead birds and crabs into clay slip, firing them, and preserving their physical presence makes sense. In a way, I am stopping the decaying process and paying reverence to these beings. I consider clay to be a metaphor for the body in which I am preserving, the clay takes on the form of these once living beings safeguarding their ashes inside. We have this vessel that is our body, without it we cease to exist, but it is the persons soul and personality that is what we cherish in our memories. The impermanence and fragility of life raises its head to look directly at us. My work navigates the raw responses to loss and allows the viewer to connect with their sensibilities. Absence, presence and the void between are continually on my mind,leaving an altered reality that is left behind for us to negotiate and navigate with a serious lack of control. Making is easier for me than having the conversation, the gravity of emotion is expressed through this coping mechanism.

column25: Biography Miami born, Charles Humes Jr. comes from a rich family heritage of the Grand Turks, Exuma and Eleuthera Islands of the Bahamas. A nationally acclaimed painter, printmaker, draftsman and educator, Humes Jr. has been a professional fine artist for over forty years. Influenced as a youth by the Civil Rights Movements of the 70’s and 80’s, in which social perception and status were based on the color of one’s skin or the place one calls home, Humes Jr. early expressions found a voice championing the plight of the homeless, urban conditions and stereotypes predicated on socio-political , educational, economic prejudices, and bigotry. Humes Jr. received many national and regional awards for his signature depictions of the African-American condition. His studies include Florida State University, Florida International University and Miami-Dade Community College, earning arts degrees in painting, printmaking and arts education. A recipient of the State of Florida Individual Arts Grant in painting, A Smithsonian Southern Arts Fellowship Printmaking Fellow, a Bakehouse Residency Award, a Visual Arts Scholar and Residency at The University of Miami’s Center for Global Black Studies and most recently an Ellies Creator Award Recipient and a Oolite Home And Away Mass MoCa Residency. Humes Jr. paintings and drawings have been exhibited in galleries, universities and museums throughout the United States and most recently completed a solo exhibition entitled MOTIC and acronym for ‘Matters of the Inner-city’ at the Miami International Airport Concourse D, March thru September 2023. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The desire for home greatly informs my practice. I was raised by a single mother in the military. Childhood was a succession of army bases and new schools. In search of locational and generational belonging, I collected old photos, crusty books, folktales, and anything that could provide me with a larger, human narrative in which I could situate my own story. These second-hand memories became my surrogate home. My practice is a form of cultural scraping. I use discarded, “rewanted” objects to honor the stories of underrecognized individuals, peoples, and cultures. Materials are the remnants of everyday life – tattered clothes, orphan socks, broken toys, kitchen utensils, candy wrappers, metal scraps, and the like. When I incorporate these castoffs into my work, I reassign them value. What was abandoned becomes precious. This transfer of worth reflects the empathetic potential for viewers to connect with the stories I present, to see themselves in the othered, and to create a home that encompasses difference. The female-centric, domestic realm prevails in my work. My processes ritualistically enact women’s work ad infinitum. I wrap, sew, knot, glue, arrange, and rearrange. I instill each piece with craft and care, challenging America’s esteem for efficiency, achievement and bloated consumerism. I offer a different value system that prizes time, touch, and quiet persistence. My work is a small point in a human lineage that extends long before and after me.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My art practice is alchemic – a harmonious blend of ideas, materials, and processes that give rise to new expressions and knowledge through the creation of art objects. Blown glass serves as my vessel, allowing me to explore my creativity and curiosity in intuitive ways. My passion lies in creating Plasma Light Sculptures (Plasma), an art form that combines the technical and artistic aspects of glassblowing with the scientific application of electrified inert gases, similar to neon signs. Through this process, I achieve a captivating glow that establishes a profound connection between the viewer and the art. The luminous display invites interaction, responding to the viewer’s proximity and touch, allowing for a playful engagement with the artwork. In this exploration, I have delved into finding a delicate balance between the form and surface of the vessel and the expressions of light produced within. My curiosity and commitment to creating sculptural plasma forms drive me to experiment with the interactivity between the objects and the viewer, pushing the boundaries of this form of illumination. I foresee a clear trajectory and boundless potential for the future pieces in this transformative series. PLASMA I’m passionate about Plasma or Plasma Light, a process requiring the technical and artistic expressions of glass with the alchemical and scientific application of excited gases and specialized equipment that you would also find used in neon. Plasma brings something unique to glass, and that is to invite touch, something very difficult. By producing light through the use of electrified gases it removes the barrier between the viewer and art with its animated glow, allowing an invitation for play as it responds to the viewer’s proximity and touch. Inspire Inspire is a series of blown glass lungs, which began as an exercise in drawing on glass using engraving and carving techniques. In creating the lungs, effortless fluidity and breath are required to give it shape and form. To balance these efforts, like its namesake, I let the process be my inspiration. Each drawing starts with a series of lines that wrap around the piece. These lines are then used as guides to create an evolving pattern when viewed from multiple perspectives. While Design is the focus of the work, the labor reveals something beautiful when we can peel away the outer layers and see what lies beneath.

column25: Percy Echols II is a Pittsburgh-based Artist, Alchemist, and Educator at the Pittsburgh Glass Center. Here, he is at the forefront of pioneering the development of a space dedicated to the emergence of plasma and neon light art. He began working in glass in 2011 at Illinois State University, after diving into his first Glassblowing Class. The spark for plasma came in 2014 at Pilchuck Glass School in Stanwood, WA. There, a furnace glassblowing class taught by Patrick Collentine introduced him to plasma light sculpture. This ignited both his passion and mentorship. Throughout his pursuit of a BFA in Studio Glass, plasma remained a constant. After graduating, a year-long apprenticeship at the Pittsburgh Glass Center became his new chapter. Here he was gifted with an opportunity, where donated neon equipment and a growing network of artists, planted the seed for the emergence of a new medium in the public space. This space not only allowed Percy to create his artwork but also facilitated workshops, classes, and collaborations focused on Plasma and Neon. In 2017, the seeds of a podcast called Taming Lightning were sown, becoming a beacon for others to explore plasma and neon. Percy’s personal quest, fortified by the guidance of mentors, and relentless self-study, paved the way for enriching conversations with fellow artists, makers, and institutions intertwined in this captivating field. His commitment was further cemented in 2019 as he joined the Plasma Art Alliance Committee. Subsequent to his active involvement, he was nominated for a leadership role in 2020, an honor that mirrored his mission-driven dedication to propel illuminated plasma within glass as a profound sculptural art medium. This objective harmonized seamlessly with the ethos of his podcast, Taming Lightning. In his ongoing quest, Percy is dedicated to crystallizing the methodologies and insights he’s honed, and to disseminating this knowledge across the landscape of aspiring artists, makers, and engineers. His endeavors reverberate with a drive to make plasma accessible through public art institutions, transforming curiosity into enlightenment and engaging with the transformative possibilities of plasma, glass, and light.

I draw inspiration from the natural world, 80s/90s graphics and nostalgia, house/techno music, and the concept of comfort. I use handcraft, hiking, and listening to techno as means of meditation. I enter a nurturing headspace when I engage in these repetitive acts, which translates to works that are infused with care and personality. Consistently vacillating between the realm of craft and fine art, my practice utilizes needlepoint, latch hook rug making, and macrame. These largely overlooked techniques are a map of my self-taught and mother-taught experiences. I take pride in the time-consuming nature of this work, and aim to create pieces that subvert conventional notions about handcraft. I make a diverse range of objects which includes needlepoint landscapes, fake plant sculptures, macrame furniture, handmade rugs, and upcycled textile collage. I exhibit my work in various forms, with a passion for immersive tactile installations of my soft sculpture, furniture, and wall works. The joy of my viewers is at the center of my practice.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
After searching for 20 years, I finally feel that I can express my vision clearly. In addition to my 2D works, I have recently started creating installation works for public display. These installation pieces are large-scale sculptures that I personally craft, and they take 1-2 years to complete. It is a long and labor-intensive process that requires patience and endurance. However, when I finish and showcase my work, all the effort is worth it. The best part of being an artist for me is the moment when I look at my installation piece after all this hard work. I am able to share my artistic voice and evoke emotions in the audience through my art. While some of my art has complex, academic expressions, I strive to make it visually understandable and relatable to everyone. In 2023, I attended a three-month summer residency at the Tempe Center for the Arts, where I exhibited my installation piece 'EXUVIA OF POSSESSION.' The exhibition was open to the public, and I had the opportunity to observe how the audience was amazed by this work. It brought back nostalgic memories for them, which varied but were all joyful and peaceful. I felt that this piece brought people together, regardless of their age, ethnicity, beliefs, and identity. I believe that this reflective healing is crucial in today's chaotic world. This experience helped me realize the kind of artist I aspire to be, as there are many artists with different goals. I feel grateful that I have the ability to trust my creation and communicate with the audience through art as a mutual language.
