column25: Artist Statement My artistic explorations are inspired by childhood nostalgia that sparks memories of 1970s post-war Tokyo, where Western influence suddenly inundated the rapid economic growth of an otherwise traditional culture. Traditional Japanese aesthetic emphasizes modesty and simplicity, which originates from the Shinto and Buddhist Zen teachings, philosophies based on the premise that every human experience is substantial despite entering the world from nothingness and returning to nothingness. The two contradictory ideologies began intermingling in the everyday mundane of life in Tokyo; the modest traditional immaterial belief from the past was deluged by an influx of materialism and consumerism that revitalized a collective sense of joy, hope, and optimism for the future. As I observed this voyage of cultural adaptation and growth of my country, it overlapped with my own coming-of-age, where I also adopted a sense of adoration for the Western motifs that sprinkled my childhood memories. Today, I explore these juxtapositions by infusing Western toyetic sentiments into a tactile artistic language that overlays conceptual reflections of Eastern values, such as the sublime aging process that provides perspective to the passage of time. My artistic process aims to reveal my intrinsic traditional belief formed through the life of a migrant Japanese American that was suppressed by an overbearing dominant culture. Ongoing Project: “Exuvia of Possession” This is an ongoing project that started in 2023. The project aims to capture the sentimental value of possessions that were once a part of people's lives. These possessions served a purpose, held meaning, or brought joy to individuals on a daily basis. As people and society change, these possessions are left behind and exist only in memories, like empty shells. The artist strives to depict the fading emotional attachment and relationship people have with these possessions. The items selected for this project are mainly from the artist's youth and are preserved in resin and packaged in blister clamshell packages, reminiscent of how they were once displayed in stores as commodities. This ongoing project explores the time when these commodities were first encountered and became a part of people's lives, creating memorable stories. The objects are displayed in a large wall installation, presented in a minimalistic store-display style, prompting viewers to reflect on their own 'consumption of memories' in their lives. “THIS IS HOW I AGED 108” Series My recent series, titled “ This Is How I Aged” expresses the sublime beauty of the aging process, which I believe is heavily influenced by my unconscious understanding of Japanese Zen Buddhism. I use my perspective to depict this transcendent process: how we begin and how we end and what this means to us. By encapsulating our thoughts and memories in each of the numbered boxes, I want to reveal the passage of times. Each box is lasered-cut and hand-constructed individually, numbers are either hand-stitched or engraved. In our Japanese culture, Zen Buddhism holds the belief that humans have 108 defilements, and we have a tradition that all the temples in Japan must ring their temple’s bell 108 times right before the new year to cleanse us of our sins. Because of this, the number 108 is meaningful for the Japanese people, regardless of their religious beliefs. Inspired by this belief, I conceived the installation pieces titled “Composing Time” and “Erasing Time” to convey that our lives create all the occurrences and phenomena, but they are born from nothingness and return to nothingness.

column25: With commonly found materials such as wood, paper, and cardboard, Olivia Guarnieri constructs visual “sentences” that incorporate grammatical structures and typographical symbols into non-word forms. She is interested in working on a spectrum that has words at one end and images at the other, and exploring questions of how aligned with or estranged from one another these two forms of expression can be. What situations make words and images feel like synonyms of each other, and in what ways do they exist as opposites? Often occupying both a linguistic and a visual space, her constructions use a sparse, narrow language as a strategy to achieve qualities of secrecy, specificity, and uncertainty—all attributes that she finds become intensified by a heightened degree of restrictions and a lack of given information.

column25: My artistic journey is a combination of resiliency, diversity, and unexplored creativity as a black woman, veteran from the Midwest. Being self-taught and having multiple hyphenates in my approach, I traverse the artistic spheres as a representation of the intersections that influence who I am. The rigor of military life mixed with the free-spirited freedom of self-expression is reflected in my work. I incorporate vivid colors from the Midwest and the tapestry of my heritage into my works to give them a distinct touch of regional sensibility and cultural storytelling. My artistic work, which combines intuitive investigation with unbridled curiosity, is a monument to the unconventionality of learning. I embrace the flexibility to sculpt my path and define my voice as I weave together the strands of my artistic spirit using a variety of media and forms.Through my art, I hope to highlight the voices that aren't typically heard and to shed light on the tales that get ignored. My community's tenacity serves as an inspiration to me, highlighting the depth of black culture, the power of women, and the beauty that can be discovered in the places that are often ignored. By bridging the gap between the known and the unknown, my art allows spectators to go with me and get fully immersed in the intricacies and victories of the human condition. Every stroke, every melody, every work of art is a monument to the strength of self-awareness and the unwavering spirit of creativity. By means of my artistic creations, I aim to encourage others to accept and celebrate the distinctive tales that make them unique.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
• My art is experimental in the sense of allowing established notions their place in the realm of mediocrity and anti-innovation while supplanting them with a vision that attempts to see beyond. • The purpose of art is to illuminate the artist's vision, not document the spectra of available light. • Though silent, visual art must resound. Though static, flat art must create movement in the observer's mind. Though constructed of kinesis, more than itself, a movie must move me, a realistic expectation. To the artificial mind or the intellect of mindful artifice, abstractions are more persuasive than the memories they mock. • Small things are worthy of grand effort and extended attention, and the common—when viewed creatively—can be exceptional. • When enlightened by a veil of shadows, pedestrian scenes become visionary. • Exploring multiple visual styles creates a validity equal to the depth (not the volume) of the explorations. • The art disciplines I pursue include writing, painting, photography, digital art, sound, and moving image art. The latter allows me to practice all the former simultaneously, in a type of aesthetic completeness.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work at its root is a meditation on the adaptive quality of the human body. Through painting and other 2D media I am mining how specific emotions, themes or phenomena express themselves as sensations within the body, and through which visual cues a similar sensation can be simulated in the body of the viewer. Each piece begins first with a physical practice, miming on my behalf of postures and poses that I feel resonate within a thematic prompt I am working under (pleasure, religion, play, boredom etc). This positionality is based in the subconscious and acts as an outline for the work's composition, the prominent posture isolated in this process is then made to fit the picture plane. At that moment typically undergoing several contortions as I navigate the limitations of the works ‘’edges”. While the introduction to each piece comes about through automatic drawing/painting, The development of the work is established through the employment of afro surrealist symbols and optic illusions as facilitated through color theory. A technical and conceptual pillar of my work are the effects of simultaneous contrast, the ways in which a singular color (in my pieces typically a tonal gray or chromatic black) can subtly or drastically change its characteristics due to proximity and context. The effect is a figure or landscape that moves, undulates ,and reflects in real time, adjusting to the viewer. The goal is that viewers ponder on that bodily navigation. While my personal navigations as a black trangender woman are the bedrock of this practice, viewers should allow for posture changes within their own body to inform how they view the work and the compositions serve also as instigation for that movement. The figure stands, or squats staring directly at you with one eye closed and if you close one of your eyes she will change for you, or she stares in the distance at a star and if you stare with her your shared surroundings will shift. Her head cocked to the side with tongue on cheek may seem uncomfortable but if you try it you may find both shoulders (yours and hers) are lifting and an unknown tension that exists between you has alleviated, if only a little.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
While beginning on this body of work, I referenced back to my collection of intuitive pen and ink drawings. To revisit these drawings with an evolved sense symbol, I found myself able to decipher meaning and communication to a greater degree. Understanding the core elements of each drawing I selected, I brought them into a 3D sculpting program (Nomad) to reinvent the image. With these new references, I built each canvas to size. I transferred a basic outline. And then painted each image as one would paint a portrait using a photograph. My use of color and form aims to evokes a sense of nostalgia, play and wonderment. Though, looking further, the messaging is deeply rooted within an interest of psychology and the nuances of social dynamics. I often depict or reference the idea of the “other,” in many of my works. The Affairs of Others is particularly varied in situation but united through a stylized imagery and persistence of the “other.”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I’m a 76 year old woman painter who has much to say about our world through my paintings. Approaching a blank canvas is like beginning an unknown journey, with pitfalls, challenges and unexpected surprises; some of them “happy surprises,” some not. I call the process “paintstaking.” Due to the architectural geometry, arial perspective and inclusion of portraits and figures, my “Roadways” and “Today’s World” series’ are especially complex and challenging. During the 2020 pandemic lockdown I studied portraiture for 9 hrs/week with a master portrait instructor (through Zoom) in order to accurately learn to paint the anatomy and facial expression. Besides painting locations that are meaningful to me and to others who may identify with these places, I aim to show human diversity and feelings through my paintings, especially universal emotion. My paintings pose questions for the viewer; asking where the roads may lead to next, and evoking a sense of recognition or deja vu, whether or not they have actually been to these places. My goal is to create a sense of community through our shared experiences; to speak to hearts and minds.

column25: I am interested in how human communities might form and thrive without centralized leadership and power privilege. I explore models for non-hierarchical human potential through eusociality, which is the highest level of organization unique to the social insect world, characterized by cooperative brood care and division of labor. I investigate biologically coded altruistic characteristics of social insects within female-dominated collectives, where a fertile Queen is dependent on sterile Workers for her existence while the colony depends on her for its reproduction. I create environments for provisional habitation, combined with sculptural garments/bags and masks that serve as my protective living gear. Interacting with these environments and objects, I invent ritualistic play that revives my childhood experiences in Japan and interprets and resists the influences of my culture, particularly the contradictions inherent in Japanese attitudes toward nature and the presumed natural order of society. Inheriting hyper-aestheticized sensibilities and rejecting the malicious patriarchal privilege under which I was raised, I harbor a deep-seated fear of the untamed environment, especially insects, while also paradoxically identifying with both the power and the vulnerability embodied in insect life. Dressed in my nature-proof garments, disguising myself as an insect, I surrender her/my powerless body to the habitat, where cultivated by others, we gain power through relationship. Embracing my fear of insects, I expose parallels between human societal organization and non-hierarchical social insect systems. I examine the concept of temporary community building, while questioning the privilege of human dominance.

My current work explores a desire to unveil the private spaces that lurk quietly around us. We are surrounded by an intimidating amount of information found in the lives of others that are sealed for privacy behind brick and mortar. While this exclusion from the public eye often carries assumptions of safety and comfort, there is always a possibility that the entities that dwell beyond our walls carry sinister intentions. I have a pervasive curiosity to cross the threshold of the unknown that these barriers create and peek at the limitless, unusual and even supernatural possibilities these spaces might hold. I aspire to build a footing for limitless questioning. This foundation will cultivate an underlying dread that ruptures the comfort of home by revealing the frightening possibilities of what may dwell in the next room over.

column25: I grew up in northern China. Since I was in primary school, I had already started learning drawing and painting. I attended an art school away from my hometown in preparation for the Art Academy, and I have always wanted to be an artist, a desire that never changed. For my bachelor's, I studied at China Central Academy of Fine Arts. I completed one year of oil painting and anatomy classes to learn the basic skills necessary for a visual artist. Then I chose to study in the experimental art department, where my practices switched from visual art to conceptual art. During my undergraduate studies, I had my first group exhibition in The Bunker, which is a non-profit experimental art space located in Beijing. And I received a national scholarship from DAAD, allowing me to do an exchange semester in Offenbach HFG, Germany. During the time I was living in Frankfurt am Main, I worked on installation works and performances, which shown in Basis Frankfurt, Rundgang exhibition in HFG Offenbach and art festival Kunstansichten 2019. My stay in Germany was essential to my artistic development; Over time, I was able to move to Berlin, where I was able to learn Butoh Dance. I started to find more interest in performing art, dance theater, and the convergence of visual art. While residing in Berlin, I started to develop my own artistic language. I went back to figure drawings, they aren't anything like bodies; they are disruptive and amputated, reflecting architectures and my dreams and mundane movements I observed in the city. I also started to collaborate and present my performance work; I am able to work with many great musicians and artists from Europe and China. In my performances, I talked about confined bodies, emotional traumas, and fear. After my stay in Europe, I went back and forth between Beijing, India, Istanbul, and Berlin. In 2021, I moved to New York City to start my MFA in Integrated Media Arts department at Hunter College, where I have the chance to experiment with my physical drawings and dance with technology in public space, since we don't have any studio space available. During my time in NYC, I worked with many experimental musicians for improvisational dance performances, I also start to make my own avant-garde performances. My vision was widened in NYC, I got to see many amazing experimental theaters. Those experiences really helped me think about the concept and message I want to convey through my art.

column25: Painting and drawing are tools with extraordinary revelatory power, picking up where words often fall short. They help to tackle the past and divine the future. My aim, as a painter, is to act as an archivist; merging the spheres of internal desire and external demands. Growing up: my childhood in South Louisiana was an amalgamation of bad TV, Bible verses and strong Southern women – a gulf between decadence and repression. I eventually left that milieu, stronger, more aware, and with a lifetime of characters and experiences in tow. But somehow, it has never left me. And though my childhood doesn’t define me, it and the residual emotional stains certainly inform my life as an artist. In practice, I select imagery for my drawings from an archive I have amassed that enables me to categorize my sources. I then work to deconstruct the imagery in a manner that fosters new content. I begin to draw, connecting threads, fragment to fragment. And, through this collapsing of original context, I create new dialog and a more clarified reality. By working from pre-existing images, I find the resulting paintings suggest a more compounded, complex narrative without being shackled to the original framing of the subject. My mode of re-contextualization empowers the viewer to interpret them more symbolically and metaphorically. This allows for greater charity within deviations of initial context and more variety of visual representation of the painted figure.The often charged nature of my source material hints at our innate need and capacity for catharsis. I use this dissolution of boundaries, as a conceptual tool, to remind my viewers that in order to restore humanity, we must first acknowledge and define objectification within the body/mind dichotomy. My current paintings are an attempt to resolve the confrontation of the artificial and the real. They help me explore intimacy in all its permutations: physical, psychological or mystical. And, I do this with the anticipation of mitigating some of the damage inflicted by learned shame and hindered exposure to a genuine, self-derived identity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am Alexander Reagan, and my artistic journey is defined by a passion for merging the worlds of contemporary and fine art through the medium of oil and acrylic. My work is a captivating exploration of characters and artifacts that transcend the boundaries of traditional two-dimensional canvases. What sets my artistic practice apart is my unwavering commitment to breathing life into my creations, transforming them into captivating animations, sculptures, and toys. My artistic process is a fusion of diverse materials, techniques, and technologies. Through the delicate interplay of oil and acrylic paints, I craft characters and artifacts that exude depth and emotion. Each stroke of the brush or palette knife is carefully considered, and every layer of paint tells a story. My goal is to create art that captivates the viewer, inviting them to explore the rich tapestry of colors, textures, and details that adorn my canvases. But my creative journey doesn't end with a brushstroke; it extends beyond the canvas and into the realm of three dimensions. I believe that art should be a multisensory experience, and I achieve this by bringing my characters and artifacts to life. Through a seamless integration of technology and traditional craftsmanship, I breathe life into my creations. These characters, once confined to the canvas, evolve into interactive sculptures and engaging toys, ready to be touched, played with, and experienced in a whole new way. I draw inspiration from the contemporary world around me, as well as from the time- honored traditions of fine art. The juxtaposition of old and new, of classical techniques and cutting-edge technology, is at the heart of my artistic identity. I seek to bridge the gap between these worlds, challenging the boundaries of what art can be and how it can be experienced. In essence, my work is a celebration of creativity and innovation. It is an invitation to step into a realm where characters and artifacts come to life, where the canvas becomes a stage, and where the boundaries of contemporary and fine art blur. Through the dynamic interplay of oil, acrylic, and technology, I aim to ignite the imagination of my viewers and leave them with a sense of wonder and curiosity about the possibilities of art in our ever-evolving world.

column25: Born in Santa Clara Cuba I came to the US in 1961 on what was called The Peter Pan Flights. Unaccompanied minors were brought to the US in the hopes of expediting their parent’s exit from Cuba. I was one of the lucky ones as my parents followed me within 4 months. I have always drawn and painted. As a child I made my own paper dolls and solicited input from the adults as to what they would like me to draw for them. I became very interested in illustrators like J. C. Leyendecker, Norman Rockwell, as well as the illustrative world of Toulouse-Lautrec and Maxfield Parrish. I hoped my career would follow in their footsteps. I studied at San Jose State and graduated with a BA in Graphic Design with a concentration in Illustration, minoring in Advertising. A few years later, was able to take some classes at the S. F. Academy of Art. But the illustration aspect never fully materialized. My career concentrated in the graphic design field. I worked as a graphic designer for many years. In 2014 the call to draw and paint came back with a vengeance so I picked up fine arts where I had left off. Or so I thought. It was shocking to realize how much ability you lose after not drawing consistently. Diligent practice became first order of business as I rediscovered the basics: drawing, color, composition before attempting to paint. Reacquainting myself with media and materials I hadn’t used in years were part of the process. Watercolors provided yet another huge learning curve. I asked myself...Did I really know how to do this before? While working on mastering watercolors again, I discovered Alcohol Inks. I was captivated by the vividness of the colors and the movement of the paints. They became an obsession, discovering different materials and techniques, all which ended up unlocking a looser style for my watercolors as well. I now go back and forth between the two mediums which I feel fuels my growth and complements my painting style. Recently I have begun to dabble with soft pastels as well. Love the journey of discovery! I am a member of the Santa Clara Valley Watercolor Society, Pacific Art League and, as a member, my work is seen regularly at Gallery 9, a co-op gallery in Los Altos, CA. • www.gallery9losaltos.com.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice interrogates the assumptions of power and power dynamics we hold in and put onto the body. Using mixed media oil paintings large, colorful, and bound feminine figures and hooded men against rich draped backgrounds, I create indeterminate worlds that play on explicit and implicit biases of identity or lack thereof. These scenes, that take place somewhere yet nowhere, come together through collage and embroidery to depict planes of existence where I grapple with the symbology of power through the use of the body and its adornments. How and what the form is covered in has long been a shorthand for status and how that form is to be regarded. For me, material fabrics not only exist as a clue to status but also act as a second skin to the body, folding and wrinkling, being judged by weight, texture, color. The fullness of oil paint has lent itself to my work to help bring that physicality to the fabrics and forms in these mixed media paintings. The sensationally and texturally rich pieces can be characterized by the paradoxically positioned alluring color palettes and eerily fetishistic body language. It has been my undertaking in this work to examine delicate intricacies of desire, the Madonna/whore complex, and indirect communications of control. My most recent work delves into religion, deviated sexualities, and the simultaneous influence of heteronormative power structures. Our bodies are but player pieces driven by internal and external forces, nefarious and divine, and I seek to shine the light on them.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is a continuous exploration of the natural world, expressing the abstract concepts of time and motion as physical forms and in a (hopefully) relatable way. Most of my sculptures evolve out of an experiment in transforming one thing into another. I often start with a simple idea like ‘visualize a sound’, then explore various ways it could be expressed — from welding freeform three dimensional ‘paintings’ to capturing digitally, manipulating, and transforming back into an object. For public works, my goal is to make beautiful things with a connection to the cosmos — ancient, universal truths we all share. Each sculpture is inspired by our environment; the movement of people, the stars in the night sky, the shadows cast by the sun over a single day or entire year. Like an alchemist, I use metals as a favorite medium. Metals can bend, scatter, refract and reflect light while also being physically formed, shaped, and textured. Metals interact with the environment physically and perceptually; expressing the ravages of time on a scale different than our bodies, revealing our own impermanence on this planet and the ultimate triumph of entropy.

column25: ARTIST STATEMENT FOR REQUIEM Requiem is a lament for the dead An elegy, a poem for the suffering of dead The idea of Requiem derives from my grief for the world around us. The outrageous amount of death from Covid, the wars, gun violence, the displacement of refuges, the loss of biodiversity, extinction of species and climate change. It is an elegy for the dead, suffering, and the passage of time, along with all its hardships for humanity and the natural world. I aim to create a body of work that encompasses the pains of our fleeting and evolutionary existence on the planet. The common denominator for all: death. I view trees as gentle beings that silently observing, vessel witnessing our failings. Therefore, it feels natural to use them as an extension or hybrid of ourselves. I use branches and tree trunks as a vessel to explore our cruelty through flesh, decay, natural science, and biology. I incorporate elements such as blood, wounds, scars, bones, fungi, mummies, fossils, botanical drawings, and insect pinnings in great detail to create an intimate connection with each piece. My signature dark backgrounds, present in all my works, are well suited for Requiem as they symbolize loneliness and the idea of looking into the abyss of infinity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I make colorful figure paintings, drawings, monoprints and sculptures. My work explores who we are and what lies behind our actions. This interest began with a personal desire to grow emotionally, socially, and psychologically and let go of those things that have kept me from moving forward. That journey has led to an understanding that we are all connected and share basic human emotions and desires even though they may manifest differently. Gaining understanding of myself has helped me to understand others. In my art, I work through what it is to be human – addressing fear and needing to be loved as they manifest in myself and in others. This includes subjects such as body image, loneliness, isolation, ego, etc. in an effort to find healing and acceptance of myself and others. I work between the primal/subconscious and the narrative/consciousness often putting the figure in unusual situations. My other worldly colors reflect and emphasize the abnormal normalcy of these questions. In the work, I am not looking for answers so much as I am posing questions to lead to understanding.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Artist's Statement: Unveiling Perspectives in the 'Cameo' Series by Joseph Abbati In the realm of portraiture, Joseph Abbati's 'Cameo' series emerges as an exploration of dual perspectives, unraveling the intricacies of identity through a unique fusion of classic craftsmanship and contemporary innovation. In this collection, Abbati masterfully employs the timeless format of the cameo, weaving a narrative that transcends traditional portraiture, inviting viewers into a realm where subject and surface seamlessly intertwine. At the heart of the 'Cameo' series lies a profound inquiry into the essence of portraiture itself — a genre that has, throughout history, sought to capture the nuances of individuality. Abbati, however, takes this exploration a step further by ingeniously intertwining two perspectives within a single frame. The result is a visual symphony that engages the viewer in a dynamic dance between the seen and the unseen, the surface and the depth. Abbati employs the classical cameo as a starting point, a historical nod that immediately establishes a connection with tradition. Yet, within this familiar framework, he introduces a mesmerizing twist — a painted portrait that seemingly peels away from the subject's profile, transcending the confines of the cameo's traditional boundaries. The figures appear as if in the midst of a metamorphosis, as if they have willingly liberated themselves from the constraints of two-dimensionality to inhabit a realm where the essence of their being is laid bare. The choice of subjects further elevates the series. Abbati turns to his fellow artists, individuals intimately acquainted with the nuances of creating and revealing. These artist friends become both muse and collaborator, their profiles capturing a moment in the creative process when the veil between artist and artwork is at its thinnest. By taking them from behind the canvas and placing them on the canvas itself, Abbati establishes a profound dialogue between creator and creation. The subjects, in a sense, become conduits for the artistic spirit, embodying the very act of bringing the hidden to light. The painted portraits within the profile evoke a sense of timeless beauty, rendered with a skillful hand that pays homage to classical techniques. Simultaneously, the glimpse of the linen surface beneath hints at the raw, unfiltered nature of the artistic process — the very materiality that forms the foundation of creative expression. This duality creates a tension that is both arresting and thought-provoking, inviting viewers to contemplate the layers of identity, both revealed and concealed. The 'Cameo' series stands as Abbati's ability to marry tradition with innovation, paying homage to the rich heritage of portraiture while pushing its boundaries. Through his lens, the portrait becomes a dynamic exploration of selfhood, where the act of creation and revelation intertwine in a mesmerizing dance. Abbati's work beckons viewers to engage in a contemplative journey, challenging preconceived notions of portraiture and prompting a deeper reflection on the multifaceted nature of identity. In essence, the 'Cameo' series is an ode to the complexity of the human spirit, an artistic endeavor that transcends the visual to become a profound meditation on the interplay of perspectives, the unveiling of hidden truths, and the timeless dance between artist and subject. Abbati invites us to peel back the layers, to witness the alchemy that occurs when the seen and the unseen converge in a symphony of form and expression.

column25: My work explores the interplay of natural and digital entities which define our virtual subconscious, or our collective digital awareness. Fluorescent lights, the crisp edges of cracked screens, and bright, inverted colors embedded in organic imagery represent the coalescence of two realities: the natural and the digital. Reluctant to choose one reality over the other, I involve both in my creative process. Inspired by digital collages, each piece meshes analogue and digital vernacular. To visualize and actualize the merge of these two realities, I use visceral applications of acrylic, oil, spray paints, and pigments. Each piece represents today’s nearly invisible, screen-filled reality and the overconsumption of visual culture which competes for our attention daily. They are windows into my perceived reality.

2024 Grant Applicant - Round 1
column25: I am fascinated with the concept of perception. Many experiences in my life have made me feel quite alone. In those moments, I feel unique in the way that I perceive things. And yet at the same time, I strongly believe that we as human beings share many experiences, many thoughts, feelings, and reactions. With my work, I approach the dissonance between perceived unique individual solitude in a crowded environment, and the commonness and sameness of such feelings. Using detailed line work and heavy colors, I illustrate moments of 'unique' isolation that we all share. The feeling of walking alone while viewing social excitement through nighttime windows, or working while others play to their contentment. With controlled ink lines, I capture shadows and lights. Familiar angles, familiar feelings brought on by time of day or night, placement and presence in one’s environment. I manipulate the openness and tightness of visual spaces and interiors to reflect the claustrophobia of society's open space and the endless emptiness of being crowded and crushed. With strong colors, I attract the viewer’s eyes to specific spaces within the composition, reminding them of their own perceptions of environment, and that I, like them, find myself in there too. By capturing these scenes, I present the commonality between them and me. Though they do not know me, we are united by the sameness of the humanity we all share. The art exhibitions that affect me most strongly are the ones that remind me of myself. Of what I love, of what I find beautiful, and of what I fear. We are always looking for a certain degree of reflections of ourselves. In images, in friends, in pets, in communities, in romantic partners. With my artwork, I attempt to bridge the gaps of solitude by harnessing that innate search for our reflections. With familiar scenes, bright colors, and fine details of everyday objects and structures, I create scenes that are familiar in their individuality.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Colin Knight’s (b. 1998) body of work represents a microcosm of tracings of wartime, visually encapsulating a broad range of historical, artistic, and mythical allusions - from investigative research, British design, and the lineage of transformative myth passed down through artists Joseph Beuys, Anselm Kiefer, and Matthew Barney. Focusing on the time during London’s Blitzkrieg of World War II, Knight reflects upon stories and the identity of his family's survival of the bombing raids, grappling with the romanticization of this time of human survival and its modern parallels. Fusing together reality and myth, design and sculpture, he engages the traumatic events of history and the re-envisioned epics of life, death, and rebirth. His immense repertoire of imagery and reference is paralleled by the breadth of craftsmanship and medium palpable in his work. Knight’s oeuvre encompasses furniture, objects of conflict, crash sites, architectural ruins and an array of self learned craft such as leather work, mold-making, and sewing. The psychical elements of his practice—from wood, leather and soap to textiles, plaster, and upholstery—are as symbolically resonant as they are vast-ranging. By integrating, dissecting, and reimagining imagery and techniques, he brings forward the importance of the traumatic and the triumphant, myth and memory.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Transcultural displacement: A longing for a sense of home in a foreign environment. Born and raised in the small country of Guatemala, and having lived throughout the United States since my twenties, I don’t seem to belong in the U.S. and am often perceived as other. To my American peers, I have to explain where Guatemala is; within the Latin-American community here, I don’t come from where the majority comes from. And even when traveling back to Guatemala, I don’t belong there either – my speech is slightly different, and I have to remind myself to use a Guatemalan accent; I don’t dress the same; my life experience is unlike theirs. Ever a foreigner, always neither here, nor there, I create art to build a home for myself; my art practice is my home. In exploring transcultural displacement, my installations often touch on closely related questions of foreignness, the impermanence of memory, and concepts of home, all of which range from introspective inquiry to public dialogue. To touch on foreignness, for example, In Rota tu Volubilis I purposely created a physical divide between two readers, so that even though they are viewing the piece together, they must do so from different perspectives. In contemplating concepts of home for Building a Childhood Home for all TCK, I am building a home for all third culture kids (TCK), including myself. In extending the concept of home to global warming, I use biodegradable materials to speak to this pervading concern. Questions of global warming also appear in Nostos. The choice of materials for my installations is generated by my memories. A memory triggers the material, and the material leads the way. The material shows me how it behaves and reacts; it tells me what it wants to do. I also work with multiples and series; often finding that the material in one piece informs the other(s), thereby becoming a continuum body of work. My work is also ephemeral; it is transient as my installations live for a finite set of time and eventually cease to exist, as humans do. With Bet Lehem, the use of toasted bread dictated that I work faster than usual, as the bread lost moisture with each day and gradually diminished. The bread became stale, broke, and eventually disappeared. For my audience, I simply want them to experience the work; to engage; to let the installations make them see the world slightly differently. And if they still have time, to think about what it means to belong.

column25: Based in the New York Metropolitan area, Josué Morales Urbina is an award-winning installation and sculpture artist, whose work primarily explores transcultural displacement. Among frequent themes that arise in his art are a pervading sense of foreignness and the impermanence of memory, which he examines via contemplative abstract installations. These works are composed of a broad range of materials, including ordinary household objects such as, drinking straws, coffee beans, toasted white bread, and rubber bands. The questions of foreignness in Morales Urbina’s work are rooted in his being born in Guatemala City, Guatemala, and having lived across the United States as a “third culture kid” (an individual raised in a culture other than their parents'). Yet, as much as he creates to engage audiences in such explorations, Urbina’s artmaking has also afforded him the self-discovery that he, ultimately, creates art “to build a home for myself; my art practice is my home.” Among Morales Urbina’s achievements as an artist, are solo and group exhibitions in New York, New Jersey, New Mexico, Oregon, and Texas. A Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture alum, he's undertaken artist residencies at the Vermont Studio Center, Centrum residencies in Washington state, and secured the 2023 New Jersey State Council on the Arts - Sculpture Finalist Award. He also holds a Bachelor of Fine Arts from the University of Texas at San Antonio, with a minor in Art History and Criticism.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work focuses on combining technology, craft, and 3D printing to create functional sculptures that re-imagine the way we use everyday objects. I am interested in the products that we use and adorn such as furniture, electronics, and jewelry and the cultural references or nostalgia that result from re-interpreting them. I like to create work that is both familiar and useful, while giving it a new spin of humor or curiosity. I’m also influenced by the biological sciences, and try to relate forms found in nature - both molecular and anatomical in the work I create (Spine Lamp, Angstrom Ring, Globular Chair) I’m inspired by the technological tools at our disposal to fabricate and transmute objects both in material or in context. With 3D printing, I can faithfully replicate a digital design or product into a plastic model, I can then cast into another material or design electronic components to turn an object into a lamp. With GainCube I create a functional bluetooth speaker handbag out of an old game console, and incorporate 3D printed game controller joysticks to serve as feet and a plastic strap to make the object wearable. With The Barbie, The Bitch, and The Wardrobe, I use 3D printing to re-imagine a wardrobe into a rhinestone handbag with a LED-lit vanity.

column25: Sebastian Bidegain is a Maker, Artist, Designer and Physician. He divides his time between the academic makerspace at Cornell Tech as a Lab Manager and as a digital fabricator with Designer and Sculptor Misha Kahn to fabricate and design fine art furniture. His role with Misha Kahn centers around 3D printing furniture and functional sculptures, along with various post processing techniques using 3D printed models such as kiln-cast glass, cast bronze, and electroplated goods. His knowledge of the latest trends in digital fabrication and materials are an asset to students he works with. Sebastian studied medicine at Weill Cornell Medical College before pivoting his career into Art & Design. Discovering 3D printing helped his career transition and unlocked a new world of making, from products to fine art furniture. In his final year of medical school, he created a neuroanatomy teaching model at the MakerLAB to demonstrate the complexity of visual pathway deficits using LED lights. His own art and design work is showcased under the moniker Voidfill Studio where he combines 3D printing with various other fabrication techniques. He has designed several lamps, unconventional furniture pieces, and wearable electronics that have been showcased during NYCxDESIGN week.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am fascinated by bodies. They are magical and glorious and gross and bewildering. I can actively influence the processes of my body but cannot control any of them fully. This body is mine but is also an organism with its own needs and demands that has no respect for my feelings and is not bothered by my shame. Coarse hair now grows in places that were once smooth. Lungs take in air, bones degrade. Once pregnant, the same body now hosts faltering hormones. Despite love, wounds ooze. I have to accept it all as both revelation and horror. It is fundamental to being human. My work is the outlet for my compulsion to explore this exquisite, repulsive organism that exists beyond my desires and authority. My work of the last several years has centered on themes of feminine being through the body, hybridity and contrast, scale and disruption. Through all these works, hand-stitching, irregular seams and nasty knots are part of my process. These are the visible representations of the making and mending, repairing and refinishing we are engaged in as human beings on a daily basis. Stitching, sewing, constructing, assembling – I have been doing this to make bodies and body parts for over twenty years. Piercing, pulling, closing; each stitch is both aggressive and restorative. Stitching is an act of accumulation – it is a collection of marks, moments and attachments that give shape to a form. Sewing these forms, I come to rest in my body more fully.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
ARTIST STATEMENT: My most recent paintings depict nature with an acknowledgment of the nostalgia tied to specific environments. The places I paint have been here before us, will remain after us, and they hold all sorts of secrets. The Pacific NW is special in that here the veil between the spiritual and physical world is thin. Mysterious as they are, the great outdoors have taken on an antidotal role during and post pandemic. The woods serve as a healing escape, as well as a mirror to reflect our very human emotions of fear, isolation, and bravery. The imagery I chose symbolizes different qualities of the landscape: power, healing, haunting, magic, awe, and fear, all of which gives perspective to our human experience. Themes explored in these paintings include the nourishing aspects of Beauty and Awe, healing qualities of plants, and spirituality. My paintings take a psychological approach, depicting landscapes rich with a history of folklore with a focus on making visible the fleeting qualities that add to our understanding of Place. Often my work delves into motifs found in the literary genre of Magical Realism. Magical Realism is defined as 'what happens when a highly detailed, realistic setting is invaded by something too strange to believe'. This strangeness, in turn, invites the viewer to expand their own perception of reality beyond rational thinking and open to wonder, beauty, and awe. Magical Realist writers like Jorge Luis Borges and Gabriel García Márquez, as early as 1925, saw how wonder and awe could give depth and insight into our everyday lives. Recent scientific studies such as those highlighted in the NY Times, US Today, and Psychology Today, show that experiencing awe improves physical and mental health. “The experience of awe is the feeling of being in the presence of something vast that exceeds our current understanding of the world. The research reveals that experiencing awe can reduce stress, quiet the inner critic, and inspire us to act more altruistically toward the people around us.” The paintings in Wilds Within celebrate how the experience of the natural world can evoke awe, connect us with the divine presence, and discover the wonder to be found in everyday life. The Ultimately, my work reignites childhood wonderment, sheds light on the sublime, and calls on the viewer to protect natural spaces.

column25: My art is grounded in a playful curiosity that reflects my deep regard for empirical investigation. I frequently use scientific concepts and methodology to organize visual material. This approach allows for aleatory events such as those often seen in the oeuvre of Marcel Duchamp and John Cage. Therefore, chance and random occurrences inform important details of my work. The 6th century philosopher Anacharsis had it right when he said, “Play so that you may be serious.” Drawing upon minimalist and formalist inclinations, my work reveals itself in a wide variety of formats: performance, video, installation, painting, drawing, photography as well as object sculpture. I make extensive use of gradations of color and form, repeated patterns, geometric structure and modulated sound.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My new series of paintings depict landscapes with an abundance of color and repetitive brushstrokes. Through a disruption of the conventional notion of a landscape, I explore both the beauty and the tumult of the natural world as a contemplation of identity and selfhood. As an African-American woman living in the United States, I grapple with the multifaceted impact of my identity and how I navigate and exist within American society. It is a persistent challenge to tolerate a world that fails to acknowledge the richness of experience and heritage. These works were initially sparked by a vintage family photograph from the 1950s, capturing my father as a young child in North Carolina. The image portrays him being held by an elder cousin, flanked by two other cousins. Southern thickets of bushes envelop them all. Despite the era's reality for Black children, particularly young Black boys, I find solace in this family photo because it conjures feelings of safety and of a sanctuary. My studio serves as a place of refuge from the outside world. These paintings pay homage to that feeling, envisioning a place of safety and liberation. In my art, color is not merely a visual element but a symbol of the social construct of identity in society. The vibrancy and juxtaposition of colors in my paintings represent the complexity of human identity, challenging traditional notions of hegemony. My brushstrokes are meditations on transcendence and reincarnation, inviting viewers to explore the power of art as a means to transcend societal boundaries and find rebirth in the act of creation. I strive to leave marks of movement, of struggle, and of history on the canvas. The gestural brushstrokes capture a rhythm of my thoughts and emotions, allowing viewers to feel a connection to each piece and the process that created it. My works thus become not just static canvases, but living records of my presence here and now.

column25: Shara Mays (b. Princeville, NC) received her BFA from George Washington University and her MFA from San Francisco Art Institute. Notable solo shows include Color Sanctuary at Marrow Gallery, San Francisco (2023); Overgrown at Mehari Sequar, Washington, DC (2023); and Everywhere at Chandran Gallery, San Francisco(2022). Select group shows include After Eden at LatchKey Gallery, New York (2023); Considering Female Abstractions at the Green Family Art Foundation, Dallas (2023); Creative Gatherings at the International African American Museum, Charleston (2023); and Bloodchild at Jenkins Johnson Gallery, San Francisco (2022). These are not permanent; slowly they begin to lose their color and structure. I am fascinated by our connection to creation’s cyclical nature and the promise of our eventual return to it. I use plant material and soil as a record of personal, cultural, and ecological history. Memories of a place are found in the soil—nurturing our environment—creating a foundation to thrive in. The attention we give to our past and culture in the present will be embedded into the earth for generations.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice focuses on the relationship between architectural spaces, objects and people. I am interested in the ways that spaces are built to house objects and people, and the ways that people, objects and spaces can intersect and create new meanings through photography. I often utilize long-exposures to emphasize the differences between stationary physical elements and the people that inhabit and interact with the spaces. In the past, I have explored domestic spaces as a form of portraiture (Dwelling and My Boat is So Small), photographs of people sleeping in the intimacy of their homes (Sleepless), and religious spaces that have been transformed from their original uses over time (Transnational). In my recent project The Visitors, I explore the interactions between people and art objects in museums and in public spaces. In contemporary culture, works of art, particularly those by artists deemed iconic, have become fetishized and our engagement with them ritualized. They are sought out for brief moments of contemplation and adoration, often captured with a cellphone image or selfie to document the experience. My work explores these encounters while exposing the artificial grandiosity of the spaces in which these moments occur.

column25: Stewart Wilson's art has always been about creativity, connections and community. His life’s work, the creation of his Persona sculptures, first as physical and now, digital as well, has driven his humanity with a desire to form intimacy with his audiences. For over 40 tears, Stewart has been producing and distributing a message a spirit and peace through his Persona sculptures which he called “wrapped representatives from another dimension here for peaceful purposes.” He exhibited these small, whimsical sculptures in Museums and galleries and sold them in their shops world-wide. Each sculpture was packaged with its portrait signed and numbered and included a message of peace and connectivity. Since 1979, he has produced over 31,000 individual pieces in human and animal form. With the money from the sales of Personas, Stewart was able to open his first non-profit arts organization, Public Image Gallery on Mott Street in New York City in 1981. Public Image provided much needed opportunities to young, emerging artists and performers to share their talents. Many of these artists' works now grace major galleries and museums like the Whitney and MOMA. Public Image received grants from National Endowment for the Arts (1983), New York Council for the Arts (1981-84) and was a regular feature of many television (CBS), magazines (Games Magazine) and newspapers (NY Times). His Persona art shows were a feature art article in the New York Times and various Japanese magazines. The Personas were showcased in the Talking Heads book, “What the Songs Look Like,” MTV music video awards catalogue and a T-Shirt for Williwear designer, Willie Smith. After leaving New York City for the woods of Northwest Connecticut, Stewart's skill at organizing art community resulted in founding and being Executive Director of his second non-profit arts organization, Artwell Gallery in Torrington (1995-2011). Hundreds of more visual artists and musicians, dancers, actors and puppeteers. His work as an art teacher in the public schools is still another example of Stewart's desire to promote community and creativity (twelve years at Rochambeau Middle and Pomperaug High School – Regional District #15, Southbury CT). He uses his art and his passion and humanity to bring people together in respectful environments to be creative. His newest venture – personaland.com – combines his passion for creative community with the vast opportunities of 21st century technology. His online global arts village has showcased over 600 artists from 57 countries with 23 group shows in a mix of relevant and inspiring themes such as “Art in the Time of Pandemic, “ “Social Justice,” “Democracy and the 2020 Elections,” “Ecospaces,” “Peace,” “Community,” “Journeys” and more. These shows include group forums and music videos. 45 Individual art galleries most with video artist profiles. In all his ventures in the arts, Stewart has demonstrated an ability to bring strangers together in a shared mission of building creative community and relationships of respect and friendship. Recently, Stewart was giving a prestigious “CT Arts Hero” Award for his contributions to culture in Connecticut. He was recently interviewed on WTIC-Fox61. But there is more to Stewart than his love and passion for community. His a true visionary and creative force. He not only created over 31,000 Persona sculptures but have made thousands of paintings, drawings prints and tableaus about them. He has drawn hundreds of cartoons which has organized in the nine books. His Art-in-Residency with School of Visual Arts RisoLab resulted in a series of large ground-breaking prints and New york City exhibition. With the collaboration of a digital modeler and animator, Stewart has made numerous animations culminating in a 22 minute animated movie - “Fugitive” - which is being offered at international animation festivals.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Futile action is everything and nothing; it produces useless results. In my current body of work, actions that have been deemed aimless become the driving force towards the work’s true purpose. The repeating action then becomes its purpose. Making art that revolves around relentlessly repeating laborious tasks like digging, scrubbing, and sweeping uncovers the differences in how the act itself changes with each interaction. In the perspective of mundane labor, people repetitively clean to remove impurities, scrubbing with the goal of removal. However, I will never be able to revert a painted canvas back to plain and raw; sweeping the streets will never completely remove all the debris and grime. I find myself scrubbing the pigment off until it rips and falls apart, altering it to be weakened and destroyed as I attempt to convert it back to its original form. I sweep the streets with the knowledge that people will continue to walk along the sidewalks, cars will continue to drive on the road, and nature will continue to blow dust throughout the city’s streets. It is a monotonous cycle where what is left behind becomes an artifact of the action itself, a memory of an event. To add to the work's futility, I intentionally created tools that would predictably fail. My shovels made from bubble wrap, cardboard, paper, etc. fall limp but they serve to heighten the frustration felt while carrying out the action of digging. The overwhelming lack of accomplishment in the final product is false; by accomplishing nothing, I accomplish everything. Documenting myself carrying out the physical actions of digging holes, scrubbing canvas, and sweeping the streets through video captures the precise repetitive moments and movements that build muscle memories. It allows the process to reflect the truth within that moment in time and physical environment as a form of destructive meditation. Within it all, there is a certain helplessness but it comes with the contingency to remain constant; an inherent need to execute, to witness, to repeat.

column25: Jay Lee is a nomadic artist born in Seoul, South Korea, who, through her art, opens the door to looking into herself. Her practice starts with exploring different materials and generating a body of work where concepts such as dreams and emotions are intertwined with nature, landscape, and the spontaneity of forms to narrate her journey of self-discovery. Her understanding of space and her use and choice of materials connect with her personal exploration. In art, she has found a space to reconnect with her emotions, transform new facets of her life, and redefine her memories of the past. After certain decisive events, such as becoming a mother at an early age, migrating from her original country, and breaking with a series of social conventions from which she was raised, the artist finds in art a place to free herself from those mandates and break with stereotypes that define her. As a nomad, her ideas of permanence and convention have been transformed. She finds herself in motion, constant changes, the possibility to reinvent herself day by day, to let herself be carried away by the uncertain flow of encounters and the opportunities that change brings with it when guilt and fear are left aside. This gesture is reflected in her constant experimentation with new materials, media, and ways of exhibiting her pieces. Currently, she is working with materials such as acrylic, natural pigments, ceramics, papers and fabrics, as well as objects found at the sites where she lives and what are available in her immediate context, to introduce them in her installations and paintings. For her, connecting and building meaningful connections with spaces and people are fundamental when producing her pieces, as they reflect increasingly broader perspectives on different ways of living; each place is an opportunity to generate new encounters that she translates into her artistic production. In the same way, she incorporates elements of nature, such as stones, flowers, and plants, as metaphors for connectivity and cycles in life. Her artistic processes are guided by imagination, playfulness, spontaneity, constant experimentation, and transformation.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an Asian immigrant artist collaborative, we strive to create artwork that can make a strong voice for the Asian immigrant community in the US. Our experience as immigrants who struggled at the bottom of American society has informed and shaped our art. Since immigrating to the States in 2000, we have observed endless stories of Asian immigrants, their tragedies, struggles, pain, or joy. These real-life stories of our friends, families, colleagues, and community members have forever engraved in our memories and hearts. After witnessing many unfair treatments suffered by my community, we researched Asian American history and the lasting societal effects of the Chinese Exclusion Acts. Yet, through the course studying Western contemporary art, we realized there have been very few artworks addressing the Asian immigrant experience and its chilling history, rendering them invisible in the American arts and cultural landscape. Bringing light to a blind spot in contemporary art, we are motivated to create art projects directly depicting the life and history of Asian immigrants. For the storytelling, we reflect upon our experience working as curbside artists at Times Square and as restaurant servers for years, the livelihoods and deaths of our colleagues that we have witnessed. To create visual metaphors unfolding the realities of Asian immigrants, we juxtaposed ancient characters in Chinese folktales with landscapes of Western metropolises such as New York City. This approach is built upon our realization that there are many similarities between these old stories and today’s human sufferings, including hardships in immigrants’ lives and being victimized by racial hatred. Through the painting series, we endeavor to examine the Asian immigrant experience and cultural identity in both contemporary and historical contexts. As demonstrated by the Work Samples, our artistic style uniquely combines realism with Eastern aesthetics, depicting the deep humanity of the marginal group. Overall, our artistic creativity has received influence from European Renaissance art and Eastern folk arts. Taking a bold approach integrating Eastern and Western visual languages, we aim to create a new style that can effectively reflect the multicultural society of the 21st century and connect different groups through the communicative power of visual arts.

column25: I often depict human figures, creatures, and animals in my paintings and create environments that are suitable atmospheres for them. I enjoy creating liminal, minimalistic spaces in my paintings that evoke a sense of nostalgia or eeriness. I find inspiration in meditation, music, film, and my life experiences. Frequently I will concentrate on specific artists and genres that relate to a mood I want to convey in a painting then listen and allow ideas to generate for my work. The composition is the most important part of a painting to me. I love to distort proportions and push the boundaries of what an appealing composition should be. I find it vital to have a strong narrative in my paintings, often incorporating instruments, weapons, fashion components, and botanical elements to further create that. I usually create a narrative for each piece as the concepts come together, but I don’t always fully explain them to others. I like to leave the meaning open to interpretation by my viewers. Leaving space for them to create their own idea of the narrative as well. My paintings are always developing and changing with time, just as do I as a person. Creating artwork is such a great passion of mine and having this outlet to express my truth and ideas means everything to me. I look forward to continuing to create paintings, connecting with the community through them, and always developing my experience as an artist.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My paintings and collage pieces meld together disparate fragments of materials, and a variety of mediums. Mixed materials might include hand crafted and monoprinted papers; string, yarn, plastic fragments, cloth, wood, and painted canvas, torn and cut. Mediums include acrylic paint, oil sticks, pen, ink, and various gel mediums. I use a layering process, which creates depth and some mystery……. intentionally inviting the viewer to look deeper. My paintings are process oriented. I find the process of painting and collage to be meditative, while allowing free range to explore form, texture, line, color and composition, in sometimes surprising ways. Although formally trained, with a bachelor’s degree in art while on a full ride scholarship, I allow intuition, which dives into the unconscious, to take over and lead the journey of exploration and experimentation. I rarely use paint brushes to create imagery, but employ various methods which include scraping, stamping, sponging, spraying, and dripping.

column25: Bio 2023 Artmaking has been an integral part of my life since a very young age. According to my mother, I began making scribbling motions on the floor as a toddler…...she supplied me with crayons and paper, and I began a lifelong endeavor. Making art has been my passion, my joy, my therapy, and my meditation. I completed a B.A. degree in Studio Art while on a full ride academic and art scholarship, with studies with art instructors at the Art Institute of Chicago: Don Baum, Alice Shadel. Extensive travel throughout eastern and western Europe, the U.S, Canada, Mexico, the Middle East and Southeast Asia have influenced my use of color and exploration of subject matter, which includes but is not limited to nature and urban settings. As an immigrant I combine influences from my Eastern European background with contemporary western processes. My art has been accepted and featured in numerous juried exhibits in Chicago, Los Angeles, Vancouver, Laguna Beach, Japan, and Portland, and has won multiple awards (including Best in Show portfolio, Still Point Arts Quarterly Journal, an international publication) and has appeared in various local, regional, and national publications and journals. My art and studio have been photographed and videoed by the Bend Bulletin, Eagle Crest Resort, Columbian, and Oregonian with paintings in private and corporate collections. I publish my hand created coloring books through self-publishing company, and sell these on Amazon, and Barnes and Nobles. My art can be seen on fineartamerica.com. I chose not to be a commercial artist and instead had a professional career for over 35 years as a Vocational Rehabilitation Counselor and Therapist, and as a Forensic Expert Witness at Federal hearings. Have a good income and career allowed me the freedom to create without financial constraints. Jasper Johns has written “Sometimes I see it and then I paint it, and sometimes I paint it and then I see it.” I have seen it written that artmaking is “playing very hard”. To me, Art is Spirit made Manifest.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Painting has become, for me, a meditative process. It teaches me patience, kindness, and insight, allowing imagination roam freely. My hands know long before I do, (how to place color, what it move the space, what inner light do those colors find), rather than projecting onto the canvas that which I already know, (e.g. past color combinations that worked, or, a former skill). I have a need to keep surpassing what has worked in the past, an incessant curiosity to 'say the thing better.' No internal discussion about anything, just present in the moment of 'seeing.' The process I go through is such a mystery to me, a search beyond what I even try to understand anymore, (but just accept), as though I am not the one painting: another force tells my hands what to do, how to move the paint on the canvas, and that alone is what I trust. I feel the right decisions right up from my feet through my body to how the brush finds the gesture. Bruce Chatwin's 'Song Lines' describes aboriginal people communicating at great geographical distances through the earth. My painting comes from the earth. A painting is really never ‘done,’ like the monks at the Accademia in Rome who worked on one painting all their lives in tiny cells looking through tiny windows with archways, from landscapes they painted daily. I could easily become that reclusive but I love people too much. Still, I don't let anyone come and I don't go out until the painting is well off the easel, thus maintaining the concentration the painting requires. It is my intention to continue this exploration. Each painting takes 5-6 weeks to evolve. That translates to appx. 8 paintings a year. One painting begets the one yet to come. What I am after in each painting changes throughout the course of the painting, but I get closer each painting. Thus, painting is a perfect mixture of a highly developed skill and an everlasting frustration. My paintings are directly informed by the Impressionists and the Abstract expressionists. Compositional concepts absorbed decades ago are continually evolving within me to this day. I best express my most passionate realities (the story beneath ordinary everyday life things), producing on canvas much of what people feel when they get religious. Beginning a painting is an act of faith. The more the source of life in an idea expands, the easier it becomes to explore new pathways within it. Solutions lead to more solutions, like windows opening through to more windows. I allow myself to tightrope my way in without a net underneath. I look for spatial relationships right away; the color finds the shape, creates the volume between colors. I work oil on canvas. Nothing scraped gets discarded: I reuse everything. When oil paint gets scraped, the mud color is warmed up or cooled down with another color in order to obtain the exact temperature range. I only use five colors; with those, I can get every color: cadmium yellow light, cadmium red deep, cobalt blue, thalo blue, and titanium zinc white. The picture plane in the paintings changes constantly through the volume that color creates, (in a constant state of relational movement). What one thinks is coming forward then shoots back when in relationship to something else coming forward. In the landscapes everything that exists between gravity and sky exists in relationship to everything else, (i.e., the sky pulls back further proportionately to the foreground coming forward). The general misunderstanding of a painting is due to missing it’s spiritual and technical qualities. If the observer’s eye is not trained to equate formal visual problems with them, (such as where things exists in space in relation to each other), he/she will search for things which have little to do with the aesthetic content of a painting, looking for representational things instead of music-like relationships. Each painting has immediacy to it, but takes time to complete; it’s my own personal paradox. The painting is the consequence of technique and skill brought about by the concept. That is how what ends up on my canvas gets said, and how my paintings come to be.

column25: I studied under the painter James Gahagan, (www.jamesgahagan.com), who was a student of Hans Hofmann, in the 60's at Pratt Institute. A recent film was done of Jim's legacy: http://www.paintuntildawn.com, and I am in the film. I found such dynamic expression in the concepts Jim exposed to me and just never looked back. I teach these same concepts, certainly not stylistically, but as a way of life, to this day: e.g., what makes up a composition, how does color harmony express different times of day, how to mix exact temperature range of colors, how to locate color combinations with seeing landscape, tree branches, sky, light and shadow, warms and cools. These are universal concepts without teaching abstraction, or how to paint, (neither of which can be taught), but rather, 'how to see.' I developed my own inner voice through being present in the moment of seeing. (Hofmann discussed this a lot also in his teachings, as did Jim, as do I). I would not continue painting had the work not evolved, continually compelling me to do better, to push forward more with even more specificity in terms of what feelings I am expressing. Abstract expressionism is the outcome of Impressionism in the 60's. I am a product of the 60's from when I was 20 until now, a product of war resistance, and interacting with my community through teaching, exhibiting and lecturing on painting and painters. I once attended my undergraduate painting professors' exhibitions at the premiere abstract expressionist gallery, and am now an exhibitor there, at the Anita Shapolsky Gallery. (( am her youngest artist). I have two kinds of work: outdoor pleine air landscape drawing done on site, and the abstract paintings that come from what I see 'out there' but compose indoors on canvas, creating a moveable, plastic space. I consider all my work landscape, although the space moves differently, (there is no sky/earth gravity in the paintings that you do see in the drawings). In the 60's I was a product of the political undercurrent of the Vietnam war, a member of the Peace and Freedom party. I came to Amherst to live for one year before traveling again, but have stayed 37 years. I am well known for my teachings and for my painting lectures and critique sessions, workshops and exhibitions. I remain a product of the times in which I live by expressing on canvas that leading a life of research in color is a better one than finding dissension with each other. War only ever made people angrier. I even like to believe that one of my paintings can stop a war, or, at least open possibilities to think and live with that awareness.

column25: Soteria Shepperson, known as “I AM SOTERIA”, is a first generation college graduate and woman of color who identifies as Espresso. She finds Identifies herself as as an artivist (art + activist), and a culture shifter and sustainer. Shepperson anticipated basketball to be her ticket out, but began sustaining physical injuries in high school that ended her career. Poetry became a therapeutic tool that she used to get out of hardships, connecting early on with the legacies of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. and Maya Angelou. Through her mother’s financial sacrifice, Shepperson graduated from George Mason University debt-free. Upon graduation, she sought fulfillment through connection. During performances, she saw how her work invited others into their own places of freedom. Her work centers purpose, not in what one is able to do, but in who one was born to become. She challenges stereotypes, colorism, sexism, racism, and stigmas of criminality that she witnessed as a child, inviting herself and others to be the heroes they’ve been waiting for. Her recent work encourages courage to move from a place of outraged witnessing to courageous participation in dismantling unjust systems. She uses spoken word, music and printed work to engage audiences. Shepperson has performed internationally, in schools, prisons, at conferences and notably the International Civil Rights Museum and the 2019 and 2020 Women’s March (Raleigh, NC).

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am an interdisciplinary artist working on new media, focusing on self-identity amidst the evolving realms of the internet and technology, with a further specific focus on personal data. My current work is a self-exploration in the field of the internet and technology, with the media of the web, coding/physical coding, video, virtual reality experience, and interactive sculpture/installation. Based on my personal data, using keywords from my memories and the numbers from my health data, I am reconstructing virtual avatars, a story of my growth, and the bodies that hold these stories and memories. It also reflects my identity as a newcomer from China, migrating to a blended analog and digital new world. We are now generating more and more virtual data, and virtual life is becoming more and more dominant. Personal identities are becoming interchangeable and indistinguishable between the virtual and real worlds. Through this work, I explore the inter-transformative relationship between the physical and the virtual, the tangible and the intangible, the material and the immaterial, and the thing and the non-thing. In our current era, technology is extending its tendrils into the most personal spheres of humans, commoditizing personal data as a lucrative venture. I aim to critically engage in the reclaiming of sovereignty over our personal data, creating a space where personal data transcends commercial value and morphs into a playground for introspection, artistic expression, creativity, and exploration.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
'Liberation' Holocaust Memorial… 'Liberation' Holocaust Memorial… …with World Trade Center, 1994 …without World Trade Center, 2004 …with One World Trade Center, 2023 Liberty State Park, Jersey City, NJ The World Trade Center was bombed killing six, on February 26, 1993. I often rode my bicycle to Liberty State Park in Jersey City, NJ from my home in Bayonne to read. I was a student at Jersey City State College from 1991 to 1994. An assignment to make a silhouette for a photography class in spring 1994 became the “Liberation” Holocaust Memorial with World Trade Center. Yitzhak Rabin had been negotiating with the Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat and signed a peace agreement with the Palestinians in 1994. In 1994 Rabin and Arafat along with Israel Foreign Minister Shimon Peres shared the Nobel Peace Prize. Shimon Peres succeeded Yitzhak Rabin after the assassination. Yitzhak Rabin the Prime Minister of Israel was assassinated on November 4, 1995. He was shot while attending a peace rally in Kings Square Tel Aviv, Israel. He was shot by a 27-year-old Jewish law student Yoga Amir who connected with the far-right Jewish group Eyal. Amir confessed the assassination claiming that Rabin wanted, 'to give our country to the Arabs.' Two planes crashed into the World Trade Center in New York City, a third plane crashed into a field near Shanksville, PA, and a fourth plane crashed into the Pentagon in Washington, DC killing 2,977 on September 11, 2001. I moved from NJ back to Cincinnati in spring 2000. I returned to NJ in 2004 to photograph 'Liberation' Holocaust Memorial without The World Trade Center post 911. On October 7, 2023, Hamas attacked Israel killing at least 1,400. I returned to NJ on October 16 to photograph “Liberation” Holocaust Memorial with One World Trade Center. The extermination of innocent people disguised as “collateral damage” during war is objectionable.

column25: Creating art has taught me how to see. When I was a student, I mistook my mistakes for failures not as opportunities to learn. Eventually, I did learn how to analyze the flaws of compositions and to resolve problems. By doing so, I began to accumulate the experience and determination that has enabled me to solve more challenging problems. I learned early that visual art communicates differently from the way in which the other fine arts communicate. Art has its own grammar, which is patterned after how the brain makes sense of the visual world. It stands to reason that artists who understand these patterns are more visually articulate than artists who do not. Understanding the fundamentals opens the door ever wider to creativity. My aim is to create art forms that are visual adventures, works that engage the sense of sight and, through sight, the emotions and intellect. When everything works, these are art forms that transit a perceptual barrier that separates the mundane from the extraordinary.
