column25: I am a mosaic artist in Mooresville, NC. I received at BFA from Miami University, with a concentration in textile design, and began my career in the design studio of the largest worldwide wallpaper manufacturer. After 5 years there as design director, and 10 years managing my own textile design business, I started playing around with mosaic, and was an instant convert. I have now been making mosaics for nearly two decades. I create functional mosaic pieces such as garden benches, architectural installations, and of course 2D art pieces. I exhibit my artwork regularly, both regionally and nationally. I have worked with elementary schools teaching mosaic and creating permanent mosaics for their facilities. My work has been featured in magazines and news stories, and has been included in public art programs in North Carolina and Texas. I have created custom mosaics for gardens in 15 states, and in Australia, Canada, England, and the Netherlands. I received a grant from Iredell Arts Council to create two large mosaic art pieces for an historic Green Book hotel in Greensboro, NC., and since receiving this grant, I have served as a panelist for multiple arts councils grant programs. In addition to my studio practice, I currently serve as the Board Chair of Mooresville Arts, a volunteer-supported community art gallery and art education organization in historic Downtown Mooresville, North Carolina. Recently, I have been collaborating with a projection designer to create immersive interactive art experiences based on my mosaic labyrinths, mazes and mandalas. My new company, Get Lost Mazes LLC concentrates on bringing the magic of animated mosaic artwork to the public on a large scale.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My jewelry is driven by a delight in the body-adorned, as well as the narrative implicit in each piece. I have worked to develop a style that is focused on the capacity of my jewels to speak with a voice that both reflects my inspiration, and also speaks clearly to those who will eventually wear my jewels. My jewels are intrinsically wearable art, small sculptural adornment in precious metal, enamels, and gemstones, made to be experienced and flaunted. My attraction to jewels began early, with an aunt’s enormous pink leather jewelry box, filled with many trays of costume jewelry. I was dazzled by these jewels. I knew only that I had entered a tale of opulence, a story about the transformative power of adornment, my first experience with the way that jewelry could transform. My own pieces are inspired by archeological antiquities, color, gemstones, other artists’ work (including, but not limited to William Harper, Gustave Klimpt, Mary Oliver’s poetry, Mahler and R. Strauss), nourished by friends, family, and my collectors. I am a colorist and vitreous enamels and gemstones satisfy that itch. My jewels are a collaboration with the gemstones, the precious metals, and the glow of enamels, altogether, generating the body of my work. The jewels explore the fusion of their elements with the stories they relate, each object speaking as a collection of connected, coherent imagery. Though the wearability of my jewels is central, I hope to bring to them the possibility of transformation, as, when worn, they become the outward expression of who we hope to become.

column25: BIO LINDA KAYE-MOSES Linda Kaye-Moses is an uppity, unruly octogenarian woman, living a life enriched by the magic inherent in the handcrafted art objet. Though she graduated from The University of Vermont ‘63 with unofficial majors in psychology, theatre, and ancient history, she has been a studio jeweler making her work for over four decades, and she continues to make her jewels into this, the eighth decade of her life. Kaye-Moses has exhibited her jewels at galleries and at major juried shows, including The Smithsonian Craft Show. She curated the exhibitions at The Bignell Gallery, CT: Millennial Metal; The Art of Precious Metal Clay; and Re-Collected/Re-Invented; the Narrative Craft Object. Her jewels have been published in the following volumes: 1991, Jewelry/Metalwork Survey No. 1; 1992 Who’s Who in American Crafts;1992,Jewelry/Metalwork Survey No. 2; 1993,Jewelry/Metalwork Survey No. 3;1996, In the Picture; Framing the Visual Arts, Oxford University Press, Australia; 2003, Art Jewelry Today, Schiffer Publishing; 2003, Creative Metal Clay Jewelry, Lark Books; 2003,Discovery; Fifty Years of Craft Experience at Haystack Mountain School of Crafts, jewelry & essay; University of Maine Press; 2005, Fabulous Jewelry from Found Objects, Lark Books; 2005, Teapot Makers and Collectors, Schiffer Publishing; 2006, Making Metal Beads, Lark Publishing; 2007, PMC Decade, Brynmorgen Press and PMC Annual and New Directions in Metal Clay: 25 Creative Jewelry Projects, Lark Publishing; and Making Connections (Author: Susan Lenart Kazmer); 2008 Art JewelryToday II, Schiffer Publishing and PMC Annual; 2009 Design and Make; Mixed Medial Jewelry:Methods and Techniques, A & C Black Publishers and Amulets and Talismans, (Author: Robert Dancik), Northlight Publishers and Metal Clay Beads: Techniques, Projects, Inspiration, (Author: Barbara Becker Simon), Lark Books; 2010, 2011, 2012, PMC Annual; 2012, Contemporary Metal Clay Rings, Brynmorgen Press; 2011, Art JewelryToday III, Schiffer Publishing; 2016, Art Jewelry Today IV, Schiffer Publishing; 2017, Narrative Jewelry; Tales From the Toolbox; Schiffer Publishing; 2019, The Art of Fine Enameling, Stackpole Books She was a final juror for the international jewelry competition, The Saul Bell Design Awards (2010). From 1994-2002 Kaye-Moses was the Metal Studio Department Head at Interlaken School of Art (now Berkshire Art Center, Stockbridge, MA), has taught classes nationally and internationally. She has received two Massachusetts Arts Lottery Council Grants, three Massachusetts Cultural Council Professional Development Grants; a Niche Award; and was twice a Saul Bell Design Award Finalist.

column25: My artwork poses questions about the mechanics of how we remember – the complexity that exists within those entangled systems of perception and reality. I visually introduce dreamy instances of slippage in our recollection of the past and the decay of memory towards nostalgia. Through my work, I gather and sift through a slurry of archives: dreams, nightmares, and memories , to find how these essences make statements about the importance of memory but also the futility and temperance of life. I use the systems of remembering hidden within the body to make statements about identity, fear, and desire but also to search for the morphology of nostalgia. With a combination of watercolor, ink, game design, and book arts I create tension between the factual and the remembered. This combination disrupts the recognizability of the archive and thus also disrupts the stability and the seductive nostalgic essence of the past. These techniques pose the past as questionable, memory as simulation, and evidence as incomplete. My work seeks to make visible our growing pains and to reject comfort in the notion of a perfect genesis. The subjects represented in this body of work appear from the black of the abyss. Dense, inky mist distorts and emphasizes their form. They are revealed and hidden simultaneously, and also pose more questions than they provide answers for. The works provide no closure, but instead snapshots of an imaginary realm that we each visit, but rarely remember. Each work is a morphing riddle, or a liquid puzzle.

column25: As an artist, my work is a reflection of my deep connection to identity, community, and the power of storytelling. Through vibrant and emotive creations, I delve into the human experience, seeking to connect with viewers on a profound level. Through a diverse range of mediums, including painting, sculpture, and mixed media, I strive to construct a visual language that effectively communicates the intricate complexities of the human condition. My artistic journey commenced as a means of self-expression, a conduit through which I could navigate the depths of my own identity and existence. Over time, it has organically evolved into a powerful platform for exploring broader themes of social, cultural, and environmental significance. I find immense inspiration in the world around me, drawing from the awe-inspiring beauty of nature, the profound wisdom of literature, and the captivating allure of the human form. Drawing inspiration from the diverse communities I collaborate with, my art serves as a visual diary, capturing personal narratives and collective histories. Portraiture, symbolism, and intricate patterns are woven together to invite viewers to explore the layers of meaning and interpretation. In my artistic practice, I frequently use vibrant hues, intricate textures, and dynamic compositions to evoke a palpable sense of energy and movement. I firmly believe that art possesses the extraordinary ability to transcend language barriers and forge profound connections between individuals. Through my creations, I aspire to provoke thought, ignite conversations, and challenge societal norms. One particular aspect that captivates me is the concept of duality, the delicate interplay between light and darkness, chaos and order, and beauty and decay. By juxtaposing contrasting elements, I endeavor to explore the inherent contradictions and complexities that permeate our existence. I firmly believe that art has the ability to heal, empower, and transform. Through my studio practice, public art projects, and art education classes/ workshops I strive to create inclusive spaces that foster a sense of belonging and unity. I believe in the power of art to bring communities together, inspire hope, and ignite a shared purpose. Ultimately, my artistic objective is to construct a visual narrative that resonates deeply with viewers, inviting them to question, reflect upon, and actively engage with the world around them. I invite viewers to engage with their own stories, reflect on their place in the world, and embrace the power of creativity as a means of connection and empowerment.

column25: Embarking on a profound exploration of my Vietnamese heritage, my artistic journey unfolds across the multifaceted realms of painting, photography, and filmmaking. In the realm of painting, I engage in a visual dialogue with the vibrant landscapes of Vietnam, employing bold strokes and a vivid palette to encapsulate the nation's dynamic spirit and the coexistence of tradition and modernity. Photography becomes my medium to freeze fleeting moments, documenting untold stories embedded in the streets, villages, and faces of Vietnam. Through the lens, I reveal the diverse spectrum of human experiences, offering viewers a glimpse into the soul of my homeland. Venturing into the dynamic realm of filmmaking, I extend my storytelling to motion pictures, weaving personal experiences and collective memories into cinematic narratives that transcend mere visuals. In these artistic endeavors, I seek to preserve the authenticity of Vietnamese identity while embracing the inevitable winds of change. Influenced by the rich cultural tapestry and inspired by both historical and contemporary Vietnamese artists, my work serves as a testament to the evolving narrative of Vietnam—a harmonious symphony that resonates beyond borders. This ongoing odyssey is not just a creative pursuit but a profound mission to contribute to the global dialogue on the transformative power of art. Through my paintings, photographs, and films, I invite viewers to join me on this journey, exploring the beauty and complexity that defines the soul of Vietnam.

column25: I began making art as a last attempt to save my life. After a lifetime of unusual struggle and hardship I found myself lost in depression and every day losing my will to live, then one day I thought to myself, if life is going to be hard, it may as well be hard chasing my dream of being an artist. I tinkered with a few mediums before finding neon. After years of traditional therapy I began seeking holistic means of healing through reiki, meditation, and sound and light therapy. I developed a strong love for light and frequency and found neon to be the perfect medium for bringing my experiences to life and transmuting them into physical matter. My work is inspired by the polarity of light and dark within the human experience, the desire to heal and live a life in harmony with earth and creation. I use my art as a means to connect with others which has always been a struggle for me due to being neurodivergent. Through my work I aim to address dark and hard subjects with light, opening loving space for topics and energies that can be difficult to discuss. I push myself to build a career that could pave roads for others of similar backgrounds and also make up for the many years I spent 'just existing' Creating my work for me is vital to my well being. It gives me will to continue to exist in a world that is highly demanding for the deeply sensitive & empathetic, while also allowing me to create unique pieces that allow viewers and collectors to connect to the world in new ways and address aspects of themselves previously veiled.

column25: Uniting unrelated areas of artistry continues to be the main catalyst behind my work. My artistic practice involves combining crochet, a fiber technique, with materials and processes associated with painting, sculpture, and jewelry. In the Memory series, I spray acrylic paint through and over antique and vintage doilies, as well as some I personally made. The vintage and antique doilies were acquired through family, friends, estate sales, and during a visit to Prague. The doilies I made and use in this series were reclaimed from an old grad school project. Paint is applied one color at a time, in layers, and is allowed to dry before spraying the next color. After painting, found fibers are then stitched and crocheted onto the paper substrate, breaching and altering its surface and edges. Like a lot of the doilies, the fibers I utilize in the Memory series came from other people, mostly other artists. Manipulated into new forms or altered by physical fiber stitches, the compositions re-create the doilies in a different medium. The doilies are distorted, inverted, manipulated, and altered through the creation process intentionally and unintentionally, and mimic how we all relate to the past: we forget, remember incorrectly, recall certain pieces clearer than others, and evolve the event as we remember and retell it. The inversion, distortion, and alteration of the doilies with paint and fiber illustrate how memories can be unreliable imprints that have been filtered and edited. The works in this series will never be actual doilies or even representations of one, just as our memories will never be the complete and entirely accurate version of events.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Architecture is supposed to keep us safe. To achieve this simple directive, we must navigate a complex system which effects both our personal and cultural psyches. We share experiences of a built environment colored by personal, political and practical realities that often feel simultaneously antagonistic and comforting. I make paintings and drawings that engage the visual tropes of contemporary spaces to reveal and revise the stories embedded in the environments we build. To do this, I draw upon my personal memories of growing up in a “mid-century Modernist house” and conflate those in the studio with more recent direct memories of interiors and design objects to construct engaging hypothetical interiors. Rather than working from direct source material, I construct my images through a process of remembering, drawing, cutting and improvising forms. I consider these elements at every stage, from ideation to execution of the final work. I use both darkness and lightness for dramatic effect as my invented interiors host a range of potential, related conversations. I also channel, through visual interpretation, narrative tensions informed by symbolism mined from the history of painting, existential philosophy, film, horror fiction, extreme music and politics. I am particularly invested in post war narratives that shape contemporary life. For the history of white flight and its effect on contemporary urban real estate practices directly impacts my work. The implications of these stories do not simply affect my ability to maintain a studio but they literally pervade just about every other aspect of life. Thus, when I render a mid-century modern chair from memory, I allude to specific narratives of economic success and privilege.

column25: Erik Richard Parra was born in and raised in the vibrant, international metroplex of El Paso, TX- Juárez, MX. His work is widely collected by private patrons and corporations in the US and abroad. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
When someone leaves us because they choose that our function in their lives holds no more utility or their time on the earth comes to an end, there comes with that loss a void. We try to fill it in many different ways, we reminisce, ruminate, cry, wail, or try to find usefulness and meaning for the pain. We’ve been told to think of mourning as stages that we must experience to get over what feels like a waking nightmare and come out on the other side better for having lived until the pain stops. For me and my experience of mourning, loss of loved ones, loss of romance and friendships, loss of expectations for the life you thought you would have. Grief isn’t something you get through, it’s a continuous cycle that you learn to live with. It is a way for the body to remember that love was once housed here and that there is still room for love to live here. Grief is not just about loss but celebration of loved ones no longer with us, a reminder to love again, and that there is no darkness without light. In my work I examine my constant struggles with grief through ceramics by sculptures and repurposing my own functional pottery pieces that did not turn out as imagined. Repurposing failed utilitarian objects gives meaning to the initial disappointment at losing time and materials, still making something broken purposeful. The process of molding clay, designating a purpose for it, having the clay redirect your expectations and having to adapt to what ends up in front of you is much like the process of grief. Loss, disappointment, tears, anguish, wanting to quit, revelation leading to redesigning how resolution looks.

column25: The Black Lives Matter Movement saw the destruction of several murals and sculptures which were dedicated to the lives of Black individuals who lost their lives to police Brutality in the United States, Most notably, a bust of Breonna Taylor and Murals of George Floyd in 2020. The “Are you Offended” series utilizes vintage racist Black Caricature found throughout media from the 1930s and onward to question ideas of disrespect, to ask simple question: “If I did this to your heroes’ images whom you deeply respect, would you be offended?” Hence the name of the series. The question is formulated through the use of mutilated paintings and drawings of revered paintings and photographs of the European renaissance/classical, neo-classical era. The characters found in the paintings are always smiling or laughing referencing the original satirical nature of the caricature in a position where it is reversed. The caricatures I create are knowingly and indirectly jeering and insulting the audience. They smile and laugh knowing they do not belong in the place they are now inhabiting. The work is offensive by nature, to call into perspective the way in which Black people have been made fun of in the past and to this day. The series is a form of solace, a way to dispel the anger felt towards those who seems to regard my life as a Black Person as lesser than theirs. The series pulls their “People” to the same level in which they viewed mine.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Artist Statement My art is the way I explore ideas concerning both day-to-day life as well as more profound ideas surrounding relationships, the environment and culture. It is a meditative practice of sorts that helps me stop and think about life. Over the years, I have developed a personal visual language that uses symbolism and surrealism to transform my subject matter into archetypal images. I have been told this gives my work a mythical quality making them both timely as well as timeless. I create my art in series so I can fully investigate my subject matter. The size of each series is determined by what I need to think through and what holds my interest. While all my art plays well together, most are complete works of art on their own. Bear Hugs, Evening & Morning Star, WoMan, and the YinYang Series, are exceptions in that they are bonded pairs that were created together and need each other to complete the visual statement. I am often asked where I find inspiration for my artwork. In answer to this, I would say life is full of inspiration and I find it wherever I happen to be. It may be when I am traveling in other countries, or when I lived in China, or here in the States. I find if I look to nature, ideas for new work are all around me. I have found inspiration looking at trees that were peeking through a window in a thoughtfully crafted room in Japan. I was inspired by a sweet-faced mud-covered cow I saw standing by a road in Thailand. A delightful, spotted creature running through the woods in the States, has sparked new ways of thinking. These are just a few examples, and their resulting artwork can be seen in my Landscape, Cosmic, and (e)Xtrasensory Series'. When I am thinking about what my mission as an artist is, I have to say that I strive to create work that is aesthetically, and emotionally, meaningful. The fact that I portray all living beings as equals to be considered, and valued, is very important to me both as an artist, and a person. The historically important artists I most admire are: Kathe Kollwitz, a German Expressionistic printmaker/sculptor; Remedios Varo, a Spanish/Mexican Surrealistic painter; Brancusi, a Romanian Minimalist sculptor; and Elizabeth Catlett, an American/Mexican Contemporary printmaker/sculptor. These very different artists all inspired me to create my own visual language, which is accessible to others while remaining intimately personal. Kathleen Zimmerman

column25: Kathleen Zimmerman's Biography What inspires Kathleen Zimmerman? 'Life. Creating artwork is a kind of meditative practice that helps me slow down and think about life. The act of creating seems to help me make some sense of the world that I find myself in.' The world Kathleen Zimmerman first found herself in was a beautiful one. She was born in Fort Collins, Colorado, which is nestled along the front range of the Rocky Mountains. Her father was a carpenter with a love for the outdoors and her mother was an educator with a love for the arts. At the early age of three, Zimmerman, along with her family, began spending their free time either exploring the mountains of Colorado, or in the horse pasture with their beloved Welsh pony. These were developmental experiences that instilled in Zimmerman, a love for the natural world and a strong bond with animals. When asked about the early stages of being an artist, Zimmerman says that as far back as she can remember she wanted to make things. This need to create and her special way of expressing what she saw and felt was noticed first by her family, but then in school. In school, Zimmerman excelled in her art classes and was eventually awarded artistic merit scholarships and academic grants from the University of Hartford's Art School, in West Hartford, Connecticut. There, she graduated with a Bachelor of Fine Arts degree. Before and after graduating from the art school, Zimmerman wanted to fully develop her artistic skills and raise a family. So, she took a number of art-related jobs to refine her skills while she raised her two sons with the help of her husband, Michael. Once her sons were grown, her husband and she took advantage of an opportunity to live in China for five years. There she worked full-time as an artist, became part of a group of international artists and explored China's culture. During these five years, she also traveled to countries like Mongolia, Japan and New Zealand, to name a few. These experiences gave Zimmerman a broad perspective on life and helped her develop a very strong body of mature work. The drawings from this period were exhibited in China as well as in the States, in both group and solo exhibitions, where she won numerous awards. When the Zimmermans moved back to the States, Kathleen Zimmerman founded Zimmerman Fine Art Studio. It was at this time that Zimmerman began creating hand-made prints. She had made lithographs, while she was in art school, but she was now looking for a printmaking method that fit with her current work. First, she created a number of intaglio prints but then found that serigraphic prints were a better match for her artwork. Now that Kathleen Zimmerman is creating collector worthy serigraphs, she has begun acquiring gallery representation, is being collected by private collectors, and has been included in international and national group, as well as solo, exhibitions. All these factors, along with her continued need to create keeps her busy in the studio and looking to nature for inspiration. On reflection of her life and art up to this point, Zimmerman says she needed to live a balanced life before she felt she had something worth sharing. This thoughtful way of looking at life and art is evident in both the subject matter she chooses and her refined vision. A quote from Zimmerman as she talked about her vision follows: 'I am not sure how my vision developed. I do know I have a strong bond to the natural world, an inherent way of seeing and a very active imaginary world. All those things seem to have come together in my art. As a young artist, all I thought about was creating unique works of art. Now that I am a mature artist, I also think about how these works of art can make a difference in the world. Maybe even help others make sense of the world they find themselves in.'

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Freedom and choice are the simplest and most fundamental rights of a human being, and taking away these rights causes mental and physical harm to an individual. Sometimes, we, as humans, become accustomed to not having basic rights in our lives. In my country, women are restricted, and they need permission from the government for almost anything, including the right to protest. They have no say, and all the rights are for men, with women having no rights at all. This makes it so that no one listens to them. After recent events in Iran, women in my country realized that they have rights to claim, and they began continuous protests in both online and offline spaces. However, these protests led to many innocent women being killed, some losing their jobs, and experiencing other terrible consequences. Apart from that, some women are forced to hear things that do not represent their character and are considered a form of insult. However, despite this these, they remain strong, resilient, and continue fighting for their rights. As a woman involved in the field of art, I feel obligated to speak up, challenge, and resist government politics and practices on behalf of women in Iran. In my own artworks, I have used various materials such as plaster, clay, mirrors, and resin to create my pieces. Likewise, the Persian alphabet plays a significant role in my artwork, reflecting my culture and nationality. These letters represented in my art pieces are all symbolic of the commands and restraints we, as women, are subjected to with each aiming to diminish us and remind us that we have no autonomy. Nevertheless, we will prove them wrong. Historically, Iranian women had freedom, yet today, the government’s oppressive practices impose heavy burdens on the women in my country. I have tried to illustrate this unbearable situation by creating art pieces that reflect the beauty and complexity of women and, most importantly, their strength, and resilience. The Persian alphabet serves as an ornament in my works, and the color palette I use is inspired by the traditional colors of Isfahan, which is the heart of Iranian tilework. In addition, one of the materials that adds special meaning to my work is mirrors. By using mirrors, my aim is to show that anyone can be in the position that the women in my country are in, as geography determines our destiny. Overall, our women’s issues are rooted in the place of our birth.

column25: My experience of queerness is miraculous, varied, connecting, and complicated. With recent bodies of work I am focusing on some of the tensions that arise as a consequence of living in an intersectional body: tensions between showing and hiding, material and representation, seeing and not seeing, feeling and perceiving, and indeed between vitality and death. These tensions for me are about survival, as we must both be legible to find each other, but also must be covert and sometimes hide to maintain safety. Survival is the key magnetic force that brings queer communities together. I wish to celebrate this and also memorialize the attempts at survival which have left many of our comrades as ancestors. The reason I first combined textile with its reference image was for a very practical reason: the model wanted to remain anonymous. The naked, sexually aroused image of a queer body posed certain consequences to the model. Not wanting to hide the utter beauty of a queer person or the intimacy of this image, I partially covered a Polaroid with a textile copy. I began to realize that this combination satisfied many of my objectives: showing and hiding at the same time, proposing an alternative visual language for queer survival. I make embroideries by scanning the source material and producing an embroidery pattern with the aid of an algorithm specifically designed to see colors and assemble them on a grid. When I hand-embroider the pattern, it transforms me into a sort of human printer, following the instructions determined by a computer. Though this work will undoubtedly convey the analogue and the hand made, I fundamentally see these works as digital or at least in cooperation with the digital.

column24:

The most exhilarating aspect of being an artist is the transformative journey of bringing the vivid visions from my mind to life on canvas. It's a profoundly liberating experience, where imagination meets tangible creation, allowing me to weave the intangible threads of thought into vibrant visual narratives. What makes this journey truly extraordinary is the opportunity to share these creations with others. My art becomes a portal, inviting viewers to step into new perspectives and engage in conversations that might have otherwise remained dormant. It's the joy of sparking dialogue, of opening windows to fresh ideas and diverse interpretations. Through my work, I aim to challenge preconceptions, prompt reflection, and foster connections by offering a different lens through which to perceive the world. In this exchange of perspectives, the magic happens – conversations emerge, ideas evolve, and a shared understanding begins to unfold. It's the symbiotic relationship between creation and interpretation, a dynamic interplay that transcends the boundaries of the canvas. Ultimately, the most rewarding part of being an artist lies in the ability to contribute to a collective exploration of the human experience, enriching lives through the power of visual storytelling and the endless potential for dialogue and discovery.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am attracted to the humor of the everyday bizarre, seeking to make myself laugh, cry, feel something through the imagery in my pieces. During the pandemic I was forced to quarantine fairly extensively in the Southern California desert where I live. At the time seeking refuge through my own humor and art, making pieces with twists on the sardonic and sometimes disturbing. The act of collaging for me can sometimes border on the impulsive. Sitting on my comfortable orange couch, I will often begin flipping through sourced magazines until something piques my interest and off I go with a pair of scissors, slicing the image haphazardly from the page. Then, with adamant ferocity I delves through the stacks of collected source materials to complete the image I have in mind. Finishing the whole thing off with a title that helps to narrate what I see in the collaged work. Sometimes my work can take hours and involve lots of rework and changes, other times can be finished in ten minutes with a laugh out loud or even 'that's perfect' and I know it's done. My inspiration for Eloise, Queen for a Day was from a story I read about a 61 year old homeless woman. I clipped out her beautiful face adding glasses and used a base of a golden statue for a crown. The scarf around her neck is a lovely crinoline skirt that once had legs attached turned upside down. I wanted to make her a queen with a beautiful home to live in surrounded by lovely things and a place to rest because everyone should have at least that. Since retirement four years ago I have created a lot of collage. I also sing folk/country music, play guitar and perform in the southern California area. I do have many creative outlets which shows in my home decor. I have created a hallway of collections; crop art, macrame, clocks, photos, collages, paintings, skeletons, metal sculptures, a museum of sorts. I've painted a large mural in the dining room of a blown up 70s wallpaper design I found. Outside in our yard is a labyrinth 50 foot across the path was created by collecting lots of desert rocks, and a window “door” with a stained glass entrance. Adding strange and beautiful objects along the path you can even see the pattern in the yard from google earth.

column25: The continuous conversation with all life around us is the ground I stand on most strongly, and the softness that I relax into. This is the conversation and language of my heritage--one that I carry in the body and on the tongue as a moving artist, storyteller, poet, and land steward. I am called to expand my work past written or oral poetry, incorporating contemporary and organic movement styles. I find truth in all that comes with being present in the senses, and am heavily informed by the natural world. Beginning October 2023 I am embarking on a period of nomadism within the Caribbean, deepening ancestral work through exploring my artistic practice in Antigua and collaborating with local artists. The latter half of this nomadism is focused on conducting a freshwater conservation initiative in Anguilla December 2023 funded by The National Geographic Society and The Nature Conservancy. A goal within my work is to marry movement and sound healing. You may note shapes of a curved back, a coiled hip, or looping a lyrical sequence so that there is no beginning or end. Movement is a return to self with many news way out, that only the body can find. Similarly, a curved path in nature may take you somewhere new, or spark perspective in a familiar place more than one that is direct. Curving into the body and creating space with its expansion informs us about the form, its flow, and how we may return to the source of our creativity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
This work is about making space for the voice of spirit. The language of form in these paintings connects the dots to the spiritual realm through the energy carried in all things physical. Light, particularly, calls our senses to the presence of that which is meta-physical. Energy is the bridge between the physical and the spiritual. The relationship of matter and spirit is at the heart of my work. The physicality of the earth is tangible and real. But another dimension is indelibly linked to that materiality, one that is experienced even though it cannot be measured or delineated. That elusive relationship between matter and spirit has been explored extensively in verbal form—poetry, philosophy, metaphysics—and those texts are essential touchstones in my work. Finding a way to translate that awareness into visual language is an essential commitment in my work, and has been present in the work from 1979 forward. During that time, I produced a suite of work that questioned where the edges of things stopped, where space started. Glowing light was an essential part of these descriptive explorations. The work consciously mixed the philosophical question of edge with physical perception. I was, and am still, keenly interested in the inbetween. The current work explores the roiling energies we live with. How does matter coalesce? What are the invisible energies shaping our world, ourselves? The imagination plays an essential role in my thought habits, and the paintings reflect that wonder. I am astonished at the magic present in our world. A metaphorical understanding of the between and edges has deepened across my years of painting. As a young artist, I learned about the language of paint through watercolor. Those early paintings presented glowing, color-soaked forms that were abstracted from simple everyday things such as paper bags and empty rooms. 1989 marks the beginning of my commitment to the tondo form; and it is the point where I was mid-stride in using runny, thinned oil paint. I continue to use fluid media—watercolor, thinned oil paint, mica. This allows me to build up layered surfaces that are luminous, subtle, and dynamic. This way of working demands that I step back from my intentionality and trust the process, giving full voice to the materials I am using. I also incorporate gilding with precious metals as a way of adding to the “other worldly” quality I am seeking within my work. The focus on light in the work reaches back to my life as a young artist. I grew up on a farm in Wisconsin. We lived close to the natural world, tuned to the earth and its ways. While I have lived most of my adult life in Queens, NYC, that Midwestern rootedness continues to ground my work. For over 30 years I have worked exclusively with the circle, or tondo. It is a form that presents the inter-connectedness of the physical world, a concept that is fundamental to the relationship between the physical and metaphysical.

column25: Karen Fitzgerald earned a B.F.A. from the University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee in 1979 and soon after debuted her first solo exhibition (1980). In that same year her work was exhibited at The Madison Art Museum, Milwaukee Art Museum and Rahr-West Museum. She went on to earn a M.F.A from Hunter College in 1985. In 1990, she earned a M.Ed. from Columbia University. Fitzgerald has had 23 solo, 11 two-person, and countless group exhibitions along with collaborations with contemporary composers. She has curated over 25 exhibitions in the NYC area. Fitzgerald’s artwork has been featured at The Queens Museum of Art (1995), the United Nations in NYC (1997), Islip Art Museum (2000), Hammond Museum (2017), Fort Wayne Museum of Art (2023). It is in the permanent collections of the Spencer collection of the New York Public Library, Montefiore Hospital collection, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center collection, NYU Langone Medical Center collection, the Rienhart collection of Germany and Haslla Art World Museum, South Korea. It is housed in the permanent collections of the Fort Wayne Museum of Art, the Special Collections Libraries of Princeton, Berkeley, UW-Milwaukee, and William & Mary, and many other public and private collections. Fitzgerald has completed commissioned work with CITYarts (2003 and 2023), Public Art for Public Schools (2005), The Related Group, Inc. (2013), and Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (2015). Throughout her career, Fitzgerald’s work has been featured in well-respected publications, including Interalia, Monk Magazine, Porter House Review, and The New York Times.

column25: I started drawing at a very young age. I remember my mother and sister sitting me in my high chair. They would tear brown paper bags in half for me to draw on. Having that freedom at an early age allowed me to grow as a young artist, fueling my creativity. Now, I work hard to develop paintings that speak to me and others about various topics, such as Art history, American history, civil rights, popular culture, current events, technology, mythology, and music. A few of my artistic inspirations are Charles White, Earnie Barnes, Picasso, Rothko, and Andy Warhol. My painting ritual is unique. I generally start a new painting by gathering my resource materials from different sources. For example, I might visit the local library to read as much information as possible about a specific subject. I may even cut out photographs or articles from magazines that I've purchased. Sometimes, I may even review microfilm. I recently began incorporating technology into my ritual by taking digital photos with an iPhone or iPad. Once my resource materials have been collected, I'll start sketching a rough drawing on paper or canvas. Then, depending on the subject matter, I will choose which medium best suits the painting project. Most often, I enjoy using acrylic paint and mixed media. Sometimes, I might use oil paint to capture a different mood. I try to tell a story with my art. I hope to provide insight into the mental images that inspire me. Ultimately, I want my vision to provoke a dialogue between oneself, friends, family, and colleagues.

column25: In the last 100 years, 90% of large fish have been removed from the sea. Over half of the planet’s habitable surface is now used to produce food, mostly to feed the animals we eat and the total number of animals has halved since the 1970s in what is termed Earth’s sixth mass extinction. We live in a world of cognitive dissonance, with eyes, ears and hearts sealed to the clear truths that animal agriculture has demonstrable catastrophic environmental implications to our earth. Livestock and their byproducts account for 51% of all worldwide greenhouse gas emissions and 2,500 gallons of water are needed to produce 1 pound of beef. The earth is becoming a dehydrated, decimated land where the exploitation of animals and our lands reign supreme. We have forgotten that we are animals, we are nature, and the earth is not just the environment but our home. For artists, there is nothing more currently pressing, than engaging in discourse about the earth and all its inhabitants. We must look to art, activism, our stories, and our knowledge, to help others in future generations survive this planet. exhibition explores environmental issues through eco-social engagement that is anchored in community-led development and uses art to draw the community into talking about and acting on social, political, or environmental issues. This exhibition creates an opportunity to support dialogue to bring about social change that goes beyond the exhibition space. Though my previous work was informed by my interests in issues surrounding femininity and set standards of normalcy and propriety, my current studio practice sits at the intersection of feminism and environmentalism and looks to the relationship between human animals and non-human animals. I am looking at the overlap of feminist and vegan critical theory that discusses consumptive culture and objectification in language and “the absent referent” in literature. My work is inspired by research from The Sexual Politics of Meat by Carol J Adams, which has become the classic articulation of the hidden connections between meat eating and patriarchy, and between vegetarianism and feminism. In the solo exhibition, Of Flesh and The Feminine, I exhibit ceramic figuration, installation, ceramic cast multiples, and sculpture that explore ideas about interspecies relationships, animal exploitation, and ecofeminism. Eight ageless ceramic life-size femmes, depicted through torsos, and inspired by the Rococo. These armless busts hold up heads with enormous baroque fantastical coiffures and operate as pedestals for the gigantic meat bouquet of animal fragments and accoutrement. Atop the head sits metal woven 'cages' or upside down French revolutionary era pocket skirts holding cornucopias of synthetic hair, tassels, horns, faux flora and ceramic cast drumsticks, wings and feet. These consumptive symbols of excess accessorize the figurative form and at the same time reference an absence of body. Their gaze is unapologetic, even through hair so big it hides the ability to see.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Every landscape tells a story. As an artist, I work to make the landscape story legible. When we see a place, we are looking at the results of natural processes acting through deep, geological time, sometimes with human processes involved. My background in geology (rock formation) and in geography (landform evolution) shows me landscapes in ways that are frequently hidden. Cartographic experience gives me tools to make landscape processes, past and current, available to the viewer. Cartography is art – a map, well constructed, allows someone to see the information the researcher has collected, and to draw conclusions. Better understanding of a place encourages people within it to take better care of it. I use fabric and thread to build cartography, integrating the science of the data with the tactile aspects and implied comfort of fabric. All my representational work is firmly rooted in specific locations, and explores the landforms that currently exist, and the ways they change through time. The places that speak loudest to me are those where water is acting on the landscape, especially through rivers and coastal processes. With new tools and experience, my work showing these effects has shifted from landscapes rendered in fabric and stitch, through experimental fiber cartography, to 3D hybrid constructs using a variety of media.

Her stitched textile works draw on a background in geology, geography and cartography to explore the landscape from the bones up. Using quilting materials and fine art sensibilities, she creates visual work in a tactile medium. Starting with layered fabric, details are drawn in thread and the finished work shows a familiar landscape in a new way. With work ranging from local landscapes to cartographic imagery, she has recently expanded into the third dimension with multi-layered work in fabric and plexiglass. The pieces use satellite and elevation data to build a three dimensional model, which is combined with fabric and thread from her usual process to make pieces with unusual depth and excitement. Thomson’s work has been shown in regional exhibits in New England, including group and solo shows, as well as local galleries.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is abstract yet auto-biographical. I am obsessed with color and I combine bright, highly saturated colors with neutrals in order to achieve harmony. In my work, symbolism is crucial, with shapes representing various aspects of my life. Lately I’ve created a series of paintings that are small/medium size (16inx20in) and portrait orientation. The compositions in my work are varied because I think of the paintings as anecdotes. I identify with my motif 'The Star' (formerly known as 'The Flower'). In Unreachable I am a black star, whose limbs are being pushed and pulled in many directions. The black star with many limbs could also mean that I am like a spider, in control of my web, or at the same time I could be prey, caught in the middle. I use soft edges and opacity to convey my interest in both containment and escape. My life has been painful, and I think a lot of my work attempts to provide solace and find out, “Where else can I be?” Another throughline in my work is the notion of 'knowability.' In my studio, I grapple with the question of how unique, yet universally relatable, an individual can be. I choose abstraction for this conversation because I think it leaves more room to connect to an audience. I am an art teacher and in my classroom, we use thinking routines when analyzing art, and often talk about how every interpretation is valid. My close proximity to young artists impacts my mindset and visual language. In art class, we consider everything an experiment, and I mirror this in my studio. In my recent paintings, when I sit down to work, I am like a child scribbling with a crayon; I fervently get the paint on the canvas. My studio is my sanctuary. I spend so many hours each week, preparing and guiding other people’s artmaking experiences, I revel when it’s my turn to play. Recently, I have synced up my menstrual phases with the type of work I do in the studio. During menstruation, I rest (avoid the studio, prioritize reading and spend time walking outdoors). During the follicular phase, I build canvases and gesso them or mount linen on wood boards. During the ovulation phase, I paint my heart out. And during the luteal phase, I wind down with drawing or I make painted-paper collage. I have experienced infertility and I wonder if painting is an alternate way for me to fulfill the female ability to create new life. Overall, my work is deeply personal. Many aspects of my life work to sustain my creative career, and I make artwork because it’s fun. I could keep going like this, but I want to go to grad school in order to experience exponential growth in my work.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I am a dance theatre artist and teacher who brings people together through storytelling, touch, and shared experiences. In a time where society has become hyper-polarized, I hope to plant, grow, and propagate seeds of empathy through art making that centers agency and care at its foundation. I begin by attuning to individual and shared experiences that are stored in the body through somatic practices, writing, and prop play. The stories we/I uncover refuse simple binaries. Chaos is structured, and fantasy blends with reality in order to build familiar yet strange worlds; opposing tones layer, leaving the audience amused one moment, then tearful with heavy hearts the next- a practice in holding more than one truth at the same time. In these worlds, I challenge expectations of performance by breaking the fourth wall, and prioritizing the experience of the person/s performing. This is necessary in supporting the intent of my work: to heal, to answer questions, to ask questions, to connect, and to call-to-action. We have the capacity to produce collective change, and I believe the body, as a source of power and control, is the genesis for what our future will be.

column25: I am a citizen of the Muscogee Nation and of Cherokee ancestry. My artwork focuses on abstract Native landscapes and our relationship to the land. I work in multimedia with a focus on oils and cold wax. My abstract landscapes explore the deep emotional and spiritual connection I feel to the land in Indian Country. I like to explore the landscapes to get to know them, then convey my experience through the medium of oil and cold wax. My artistic process includes multiple layers of wax and pigment on wood or canvas. This starts with mark making, then layering pigment and wax, creating texture, adding marks or drawings in the painting, scraping to reveal colors below the layers and using a variety of tools and media. My finished piece produces a colorful, textured abstract landscape with colors, shapes, textures, movement, and marks that represent physicality, spirituality, emotionality, thoughts, feelings, and concepts of the relationship between land, mind, body and spirit. Like much of Indian Country, when you look deeper, you can see more than what meets the eye in my landscapes. Each layer, line, color, shape, and texture has something to say about the subject being painted. My inspirations and influences include Native people from all walks of life. The stories of survival that each Native person is telling by just existing. The stories of our ancestors and of our future generations. The power behind Native art as a tool of storytelling.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Online I often use the tagline “I marshal the middle between Mathers and McLuhan,” which is of course intended to be cute and catchy, but it also sums up my research interests. On one side, I am interested in figurative language use, specifically allusions in media. I have found these especially prevalent in hip-hop lyrics. On the other side, I am interested in technology and media theory. While I investigate these two areas separately, I have found the space where they overlap especially interesting. My interest in media theory started several years before I started graduate school. I spent several years working first at magazines and newspapers and then online. Observing the differences in how these media were produced and consumed sparked an interest in studying their history and the underlying concepts thereof. This led inevitably to the theory and criticism of Marshall McLuhan, Harold Innis, and other media ecologists. My book, Dead Precedents: How Hip-Hop Defines the Future, looks at the many ways that hip-hop informs the cultural practices of the new millennium (from sampling and remix culture to graffiti art as branding, as well as the repurposing of technology and figurative language use), and how an understanding of hip-hop culture is also an understanding of 21st-century popular culture. Just recently released, a companion collection of essays I edited called Boogie Down Predictions: Hip-Hop, Time, and Afrofuturism explores these themes further. As the overlapping area between these two interests continues to grow, I expect my research to follow.

column25: My process involves journaling, painting, switching mediums, and an addition/subtraction method. Right now my favorite part is the addition/subtraction method. To create a sense of balance between my colors and illustrations, I add as much as I can then subtract once it’s too full. Not only am I creating layers of illustrations and paint but I’m learning new ways to break apart the composition. It is a longer part of the process than when I first started painting but I enjoy having an idea that is ever shifting alongside the artwork. Most commonly I merge the two techniques. I would start the painting on a traditional canvas, building it up until I felt as if the work was in a good place. The canvas would then be scanned to be uploaded onto my program of choice. Digital techniques will then be used to add more layers onto the artwork. Sometimes, I even have the digital and the traditional artwork end in different directions. I consider technology as another tool in my pocket as an artist, allowing my artistry to grow outside of my initial expectations.

column25: My work takes a dialectical behavioral approach to addressing culturally normalized racial experiences that perpetuate historical trauma in contemporary America. Using an evidenced-based, Dialectical Behavioral Therapeutic model (DBT), I create paint, sculptural and lens-based projects that explore negative archetypes and stark racial disparities still operating in the language and psychology of contemporary American culture. I audit contemporary language to create counter narratives, new definitions and safe spaces for conversations that lead to increased empathy, understanding, insight, public engagement and community impact. Simultaneously, my work gives an equitable share of the Black trauma experience to our white counterparts so that they can share the responsibility of doing the work that most of us have been doing alone for so long. Equity, Inclusion and Diversity conversations are a ubiquitous part of the American experience and I use my work to not only access white spaces but to pull my white counterparts into Black spaces. My images are generalized to provoke the viewer to project their own thoughts, feelings and bias onto the work creating the impetus for discussion. “Only through discussion can we find a middle ground that may lead to an amicable solution.”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I intend to lift the legacy of people like my grandmother, who uses clay to form pots to bring water to our homes for daily use in a village called Bekwai . Where I come from, men are known for hunting and many of the women make pots with clay for a living . Whenever we visited our grandparents on vacation since growing up, I stayed home with my grandmother while she made pots and ceramics grinding bowls. Due to this experience,I had the opportunity to hold clay and it has been a part of my life since I was a child. Being so familiar with the material and the people who use it as a daily resource and necessity, my passion was sparked to use ceramics to mold a new life for myself. My work encapsulates the ideas of captivity and daily attachment to a system based on one’s socioeconomic and cultural status. I make work about my lived experiences to enhance my understanding of what it means to be in a system that is driven by the labors of its people. Art is a cultural and emotional entity that I use to convey how I see the world. I make installations combining mixed media, ceramics, and found objects. Realizing that emotions lead me to more profound critical thought, I use the history of found objects, collaborate with other artists to expand perspectives, and form works with my hands to engage in therapeutic and community-based practice. I use the power of art to transform a space by utilizing the shared legacy of objects and human emotions. When I feel inspired, my work becomes more experimental and introspective. I intend to elevate themes of humanity and equal rights through my acts of service and collaboration, both inside and outside of the studio. I want to show the world that art can help create a happier, healthier life by contributing my creative solutions. I am sharing my imagination, creativity, and innovative ways of making and thinking to give attention to underrepresented voices and make an impact on my community in Kumasi, Ghana. Being a contemporary ceramist from Ghana has allowed me the opportunity to express how my daily life influences each piece. Kumasi is a competitive and challenging visual arts center. My daily experiences involve hard work and struggle to make a living, while maintaining art practice to ensure I will cultivate the possibility of changing my life through opportunities such as pursuing my MFA. My culture influences my outlook on life. While I am an artist, I am also a person who feels deeply and wants to learn and understand the world in all it’s variety. Meeting other artists, having a job, and being among my family and friends influence the work I make. Even the works others never see inspire the next topic, which makes me excited to keep progressing. It is about the continued effects of staying within a system that molds you. When I speak of socioeconomic and cultural status, I feel that my environment, mentality, and opportunities have shaped me. It is essential for me to share my heritage because I am here to expand the work I make based on the intentions and identity of myself and others in Ghana. Home is where the mind and body work, rest, and evolve. I also realize the importance of projecting outward, and look forward to adding my voice to the international ceramics conversation.

column25: As a Queer 2nd-Generation Thai-American Buddhist with a background in the sciences, I am constantly exploring my place in the world. Themes of rebirth, sustainability, reclamation, and the relationship between material and immaterial worlds interest me. I am intrigued by how globalization and widespread consumption are linked to the destruction of our environment and may lead to the potential extinction of humanity. Through my fictive art, I speculate what it is like for a Thai person to live in the distant future. I attempt to illustrate how Thai culture and way of life evolve with the pressures of living in a near uninhabitable environment. With this fictitious narrative in mind, I enjoy using a variety of materials in my art, including those that are new and those that have had a previous life. Acknowledging that my presence in this world acts as a waste generator, I try to find creative ways to bring back waste through art. By allowing objects to take on multiple reincarnations, I explore the potential for transformation and the possibility of new beginnings. Ultimately, I need to explore my heritage through the lens of past, present, and future, while being mindful of my impact on the natural world.

column25: My work takes inspiration from and uses materials found within the domestic environment such as flour, toilet paper, bedsheets, and discarded household furniture. These materials become signifiers in my work for a personal and intimate space where I explore the experience of the intimate body, especially the female body. Navigating any female experience, whether carving out one’s own identity beyond cultural traditions and expectations, breaking down gender stereotypes within both personal and professional relationships, or even as simple as presenting as female, requires labour. My practice sits within an interdisciplinary context from large-scale sculpture to durational live performance while considering and incorporating this labour. I delve into my experience of the labour it takes to be a woman in the world and translate it into a broader consideration of the female body’s physical, psychological, and social functions. The work results in an exploration of the labour involved in navigating those three states. My muse is bread dough and relates back to my immigrant Italian childhood, where dough was no foreign matter. My first memory and experience of femininity and the power of creation (and potentially female desire) was watching my mother knead this soft, white, voluptuous material on our kitchen table. Folding the dough over onto itself and pushing her whole body towards it, she transformed the ingredients of flour and water into a living organism, created for both our oral pleasure and life sustenance. As a material focus, I am interested in the life cycle of bread dough and its ephemeral qualities. Once activated by warm water and sugar, the cells of yeast split and divide, similarly to when an egg is fertilized by sperm. There is a peak moment when the dough is full and voluminous, just before it begins to ‘die’. While many similarities between the life cycle of dough and my body emerge throughout my work, the domestic and physical labour processes evident when working with this material are also of great consideration. In a contemporary culture where the personal has become political out of necessity in achieving bodily autonomy, especially for women, labour presents itself in many forms.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work is concerned with Blackness, Mexicanidad, womanhood, girlhood, family, ancestry, memory (and its failures), the historical (especially in regards to the oral and processes of myth-making) and archives (particularly those stored in the body and the Earth, the familial, and the disappeared/forgotten). I create collage with acrylic medium and found objects on canvas. I enjoy using pages from my chapbook as part of my collage, alongside other textural materials like cotton balls, yarn, and tissue paper. I believe that history and truth are socially constructed, and my poetry and art is concerned with directing attention towards the often neglected and undermined meaning-making which occurs in resonances other than the mental or rational. So much of Black history cannot be accessed through 'rationale' and 'reasoning' not just because of the erasure and absence of historical documentation, but also because the sheer scale of violence Black people have experienced completely defies logic. This inability to access 'history' and 'truth' through 'logical' means has resulted in a Black consciousness which is firmly situated in the liminal spaces between death and life, the mind and the body. This consciousness reveals the instability of time as a linear reality, and the volatility of the firmly held belief of separation between self/other and human/nonhuman. It is this consciousness which I both channel through the process of art-making, as well as convey through my collages.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I grew up watching and enjoying The Big Three: Cartoon Network, Disney Channel, and Nickelodeon. My God-fearing mom and my passive aggressive dad were not so thrilled by this... They did not enjoy seeing how attached I was to screens and these channels (it was expected that the attachments I would have are the Bible and church testimonies and newspapers and CNN). However, my parents tolerated it because I was 'a kid being a kid' and they assumed that I would outgrow my love for TV and movies, especially animation. My parents, like many other immigrant parents, grew up with another assumption that every kid in America would grow and would want to be one of three things: a doctor, a businessperson, or politician. I did not desire any of those professions but I tried in earnest to enjoy them on my parents' behalf. But no other industry has captured my attention more than the entertainment industry. I knew at 17 that I had to pursue being a storyteller as media holds just as much power as the medical field, working in finance on Wall Street, or being the next Congresswoman. The entertainment industry has, first and foremost, provided me happiness and I knew, at that age, that I could not forgo that feeling. While secretly pursuing my dream of entering the entertainment sphere, I learned even more about the significance this industry has on me: I learned that I hold onto the characters and stories that look like me, act like me, or introduce something new to me. And seeing more and more of these representation encouraged me to end my battle with being silenced. As a self-proclaimed weird/awkward black girl, these portrayals always provide me satisfaction and a newfound self-awareness and show me the parallels in my own life. TV, specifically cartoons, had invigorated my imagination as a child and allowed me to realize I want to center on narratives that continue to invoke happiness and peace in me and other viewers.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist with a deep appreciation for the pottery traditions of my adopted home in Taos, New Mexico, I find inspiration from both the rich cultural tapestry of this region and my ancestral heritage in ancient Greece and Mesopotamia. My artistic journey is driven by a profound fascination with the power of pottery to tell stories and connect us to our past. In Taos, the centuries-old art of Pueblo pottery has captivated my imagination. The intricate designs, earthy colors, and the deep cultural significance of Pueblo ceramics have left an indelible mark on my creative vision. I am drawn to the idea that each piece of pottery carries with it the narratives and traditions of the people who crafted it. Through my work, I aim to honor and celebrate the legacy of Pueblo pottery, paying homage to the artisans who have shaped this unique craft for generations. Simultaneously, I draw upon my ancestral roots in ancient Greece and Mesopotamia, where pottery played a vital role in daily life and storytelling. These ancient civilizations understood the power of clay to capture moments in time, convey myths, and document their societies. I am inspired by the timeless quality of their pottery and the narratives it preserves. My ceramics serve as a canvas for storytelling. Each piece I create is infused with a narrative, whether inspired by the vibrant culture of Taos, the timeless traditions of ancient Greece and Mesopotamia, or the intersection of these diverse influences. I believe that in the act of crafting pottery, we are not only shaping clay but also weaving stories that connect us across time and place. Through my art, I aim to bridge the past and the present, inviting viewers to embark on a journey through history and culture. My ceramics are vessels of storytelling, inviting reflection on the enduring power of this ancient craft to communicate, connect, and inspire.

column25: I'm a dexterous designer, artist by calling, and devout for detail. My first encounter with art was when I was little. With my small hands dipped in paint, I splashed to life my first piece of artwork. There was a feeling of joy that swelled through me as the color splattered on my paper danced vivid images through my mind. I was recognized for my creativity in school, and I envisioned my future full of paint, color, and lots of paper. Years later, I found myself in contemplation of becoming an artist. I took art classes in high school. The defining moment came during my junior year when I attended the California Institute of the Arts through a summer program. The exposure to a community of artists was an enlightening experience, and I found myself immersed in this creative environment. In 2009 I attended the Academy of Art University to pursue a graphic design degree. Since then I've been using concepts I learned as an artist to help with my graphic design. My skills as an artist and illustrator give me a competitive edge. For many years I’ve been a freelance graphic designer, but in 2016 I suffered my first mental health crisis. I lost my career, financial, and health stability. I was diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder, Generalized Anxiety Disorder, PTSD, and I felt like my life ended there. For several years I’ve battled these diseases in psychiatric wards and intensive outpatient programs. In 2021 I was mentally well enough to start rebuilding my career as a designer. Throughout this time my business as an artist and designer have been a salve on my wounds. They’ve allowed me to feel the world around me, and not breakdown. I practice art when I finish my day working on designs for my clients. Art and design are my daily companions. By continuing forward using art and design to provide professional services for my community, I fulfill my life and enrich the lives of others around me.

Being an artist is freedom. It is seeing the ways perception can be moved at will, seeing meaning and spirit in the mundane, and seeing one's responsibility. In the words of painter Agnes Martin: 'We all have the same inner life. The difference lies in the recognition. The artist has to recognize what it is.' Being an artist is a way of being in the world. It is deepening attention and teaching oneself, and others, how to love.

GRANT APPLICANT:
2024 Grant Applicant - Round 1
column25: My work draws from romanticism, religion, and philosophy in order to create narrative paintings that relate internal experiences to natural environments. I am interested in the relationship between inner and outer worlds and the myriad realities that lie beyond grasp. I use oil as my medium to create dramatic, mythologized landscapes that marry conficting or contradictory elements, such as beauty and terror. Methods of glazing, scratching, and dripping paint invite an intimacy with the surface of the work, and a curiosity for the ways attention fluctuates. I grew up as a bit of a paradox, being both gay and part of the Mormon/LDS church. Having since separated from the church, my art is a tool for building a larger language for myself, identifying nuance, and connecting to the more-than-human. My work is imbued with the emotions of navigating identity, love, and spirituality in a time when an apocalypse (be it real or imagined) is looming. Throughout my work I question what polarities lie within a being, delve into the wonder of the natural world, and illuminate a cyclical conversation between loss and renewal.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The limits of the two dimensional format of painting open an infinite opportunity for creative invention and experiments with form, space, light and color. I work on painting an idea in my mind that allows something I can’t image to emerge on the canvas. Something with power and presence and a life of its own can only feel real when it comes in part as a surprise and appears as something I have never seen before. I want my paintings to connect on an emotional level though the experience of a very physical presence both as an object and the story of its subject. Format offers the chance to create a personal concept and poetry of space; the paint, the developing image and a light coming from within the painting ideally create an image most often of a woman with a physicality, sexuality and an inner world of profound thought, emotion and intelligence. I want the painting itself to be recognized for the object it is and that way, the moment depicted in the painting, becomes permanent, and the painting can transform the experience of time. - Betsy

ARTIST STATEMENT:
In this series of photographs, I reflect on the intimacy of my relationship with my partner and the importance of her love in my life. I ponder on the domestic spaces I inhabit and the familial aspects of life. This body of work is about the thoughts I have each day of being a better partner, being a great father, and ultimately, being a better person. To live this life for myself is beginning to feel selfish and I instead attempt to navigate my path with empathy and with the reminder of these efforts being for my family: past, present, and future.

2024 Grant Applicant - Round 2
column25: Working between the northern Georgia landscape and Atlanta cityscape, Horacio Arias-Rodriguez is an artist, writer, curator, and mentor who seeks to explore his Mexican American identity and heritage through the domestic spaces inhabited and the interpersonal relationships shared. His work aims to offer a sense of reflection and analyzation while acknowledging the limitations that the medium of still imagery poses. His practice does not work toward giving concrete answers to the questions he is exploring, but rather to allow space for individuals to think critically of their engagements in the spaces within their own lives. Horacio Arias-Rodriguez (b. 1999) is based in Atlanta, GA and received his Bachelor of Fine Arts in Photography from Georgia State University. His work has appeared in galleries across the south including Atlanta and Greenville as well in publications such as Lenscratch. He is a firm believer in using the arts as a means of educating people on the ongoing social and political issues at hand.

M3th1ld3 Artist, dancer, writer, human. Sotheby's 1996 With two Art History master's degrees from the University of St Andrews and Sotheby's (London), specializing in Postwar and Contemporary Art, I've explored various creative paths. I'm a multi-passionate artist, dancer, and writer, deeply involved in digital realms since college. While my career path has focused on exploring digital spaces in a variety of ways, my heart has always belonged to fine art. Now, I'm called to dive back into art wholeheartedly. My aim is to express my diverse experiences through art, blending knowledge and curiosity into each artwork, dance, or written piece. I'm on a journey to reconnect with my true artistic self, combining my academic background with my love for creative expression. I am especially interested in the intersection of art history and digital/generative art, and how we can make sense of the new tools at our fingertips in the 21st century within the spectrum of a long, patriarchal history of art that has always grappled reluctantly with new media and new art forms.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
When someone sees a painting or picture of an animal that is generally thought of as mischievous, destructive, or scary, their initial reaction can be negative. My goal is to show people a different side of these members of the animal kingdom by pairing them with objects or environments that suddenly make them endearing. When that reaction goes from 'ew' to 'aw', I know that I have made someone see those creatures in a different light. Not only is this helpful for humankinds relationship with nature, but if we can change our perception of animals we don't understand, maybe there is also hope that we can also change our minds about our fellow humans.

column25: Viewers often overlook road signs, considering them mundane and dull, seeing as they fade into the landscape despite their fluorescent and reflective nature. For me, these signs serve as a unique tool, allowing me to blend modernity with the past by combining religious and mythological iconography with old signage. The amalgamation forces the observer to pause and reflect, mirroring the contemplative nature of the signs themselves. My goal is to permeate these signs with new meaning, a significance that varies from viewer to viewer, creating a new, more tender mythology for each individual. While I don't delve into each belief comprehensively, each story and deity I explore contributes to shaping my worldview and values. Despite the incorporation of imagery requiring a background in art history, religion/mythology, and my personal experiences, such knowledge isn’t a prerequisite for appreciating the signs in their evolving context. Painting on signs provides me with an opportunity to work with the materials at hand, presenting a challenge that imparts invaluable lessons. The use of oil paint, referencing an old form of iconography, when applied to newer signage, brings forth narratives previously unattainable. My creative process revolves around playing with words, reflections, and bold colors, establishing a dynamic dialogue between 'then and now.' The origin of my journey into painting on signs stems from a profound grief toward my late father, who, before his passing, assisted me in crafting a chair from a road sign. This chair stands as the final project we worked on together. Utilizing leftover signs from that time, I chose to channel my grief into art, a decision made during this challenging period marked not only by his passing, but by a move to a new university in a foreign country amid the pandemic. Despite the difficulties, the act of painting on road signs persisted as a means of self-inspection, exploring who I was and why I became that person. This cathartic process has become intrinsically tied to my identity. Since I lack a distinct religion, my exploration of others' beliefs has provided insight into the reasons people turn to higher beings in times of grief and need, and ultimately why I did too. I've discovered that it's simply a desire to feel less alone, to establish a connection with others who are also seeking solace in their own ways.

It is a different sensation than satisfaction or contentment from finishing a project or having a good meal (which are some of great things in life). The sense of connection can be from communicating with my own work and material process, or from collaborating with other artists and conversing with the audience. I feel most alive when I'm in involved in the making process, broadly defined (research, making work, fabricating, showcasing, etc).
