2024 Grant Applicant - Round 2
column25: In an attempt to materialize such ungraspable things as time and the human relationship to time, my work explores the idea of transience and ephemerality through physical materials. To achieve this process, I arrange functional and nonfunctional objects in absurd relationships. Tangible objects, such as glass orbs and metronomes, interact with ethereal materials like light and air to confuse and rearrange any pre-associations attached to the material. Imbuing the concrete objects with a sense of temporality and intangibility, the abstracted situations in my work invite new meanings through direct experience. Giving form to something that is constantly in flux through the use of light, membrane-like objects like blown glass orbs, and architectural elements, my work is scaled for participants to perceive the presence of change in the medium of time. I am interested not only in the change itself, but also how one experiences this change. I weave in varied forms of reflection to shift the focus to the way we perceive things, rather than the thing itself, questioning visual perception as an approximation of truth. By constructing abstracted situations in collapsing layers and dimensions, the work meditates on the constantly shifting human perception and spaces where meaning is generated. I consider my practice as a gradual process of developing a language to describe the puzzling notion of time in relation to the human and beyond. Through an iterative process of making, certain elements of my work, such as a video resembling a peep-hole and sine waves, migrate from one body of work to another. In parallel to this rhizomatic approach to making, I experience light not only as an essential material, but also an existing condition of life that one relies on to perceive, navigate, and communicate in the world. The work embodies its own processual quality and materiality through this use of light.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Jessica Bell, a contemporary sculptor and dedicated community artist, seamlessly blends traditional techniques with modern designs to explore themes of race, identity, and societal concerns. Her creations resonate deeply, employing an innovative approach that assembles smaller elements into expansive installations, conveying powerful messages. In her current artistic phase, Bell channels her passion for public art to reflect her personal journey into motherhood. Her works transcend mere visual appeal; they are narratives that engage viewers on multiple levels, urging them to explore the underlying meanings. Through playful iconography and a keen eye for everyday objects, often overlooked, Bell skillfully entices observers to scrutinize her art more closely, revealing intricate layers of significance. Bell's artistic expression is arresting, playful, and rich in its use of materials. Her creative prowess has garnered recognition, including the Red Bull Artist Grant, the Priscilla Art Award, and the Juanita Craft Artist Residency. Throughout her career, she has received numerous artist grants, underscoring the impact of her contributions to the artistic landscape and community engagement.

column25: As an Aniyunwiya (citizen of the Cherokee Nation) artist, poet, and scholar, I consider it my responsibility to use my platforms and work as a means for amplifying Native voices and realities. I create experiences for sparking discourse and highlighting the authenticities and histories prevalent to both NDN country and everyone across Turtle Island. I prioritize collaboration whenever I can, such as exclusively working with fellow Natives for book cover art or as models for my performance work. Such group work also extends to my family, with my daughters helping to create a poetics-based series, “Strong FoundNations,” recently on exhibit at the Maddox Building on the occupied [read: stolen] land typically referred to today as “Portland, Oregon.” I began my career as a poet decades ago, and to this date most of my books are poetry collections. Although poetry is my most natural form of communication, not everyone reads poetry books or attends so-called “traditional” readings. I began incorporating other mediums into my practice, such as 2-D work, installations, performance, and interactive technology (VR), because I also believe it is my responsibility to engage with audiences on myriad levels. Art and artists do not exist in a silo, and yet the inherent design of many mainstream platforms is niche and exclusive (think gallery openings or poetry readings). Every project I undertake, no matter the medium, addresses Native Truths. This ranges from disparities and issues like the history of “Indian” residential boarding 'schools' (of which my father survived, at least physically) to addressing how the current abundance of Native children in foster care is an extension of sorts to the assimilation and erasure goals of those “schools” from our not-so-distant past. My own children (5 and 6) are from the Oglala Lakota Nation and adopted via foster care, which further cements my commitment to radical decolonization. My work is inherently personal, stemming from my own life and those of my ancestors. Identity and reconnecting with the Self in a post-contact world is a recurring theme. One such example is the results from my residency at Radar Productions in conjunction with the San Francisco Public Library’s James C. Hormel LGBTQIA+ Center in 2021 where I accessed scholarly works on Two-Spirit histories, creating a series of poems based on this research. This progressed to a performance at Hugo House on the occupied land now commonly called “Seattle,” where I performed my poem “Her Name Was Rita” alongside Native guitarist Teiorhathe Esquival. This was an original song in homage to Beth Brant’s “Her Name is Helen.” As a pianist myself, integrating music into my work is organic as well, such as a work-in-progress that includes me playing my mother’s favorite song to a spinning hourglass I am carving, which will be filled with crushed opioids found in her bedroom after her last, fatal overdose. A poem will be burned on the wooden stand. Opioid addiction and fatalities are more prevalent in NDN country (per capita) than any other demographic. I anticipate this work, “Chamber Coda,” to be featured at an upcoming 2023 exhibition at Columbia City Gallery in 2023. I have shared with you just a couple examples of my work and process as an NDN, as an artist, and as a writer. My goal is to inform. To create conversations. And, in many cases, to educate. Art demands quiet. Listening. It's why we are naturally shushing ourselves and each other in cinemas and museums. Through art we have a window to connect, and it is in those slices of time that I have found my work—and, more importantly, my purpose—most powerful.

column25: My paintings incorporate mixed media elements, such as indigenous fabrics, duct tape, paper, acrylic paint, drawing, and wood. Pulling from my culture, history, and origins, I produce three-dimensional works that address the tattered relationship with my Guatemalan, Mexican, and American ancestry. Elements of the traditional piñata are deconstructed and incorporated into these works to transform the piñata’s original identity from one of gratuitous celebration into one of a cultural construct. My work focuses on engaging the Latinx community with contemporary art through public and guerilla installations. Through the utilization of text and characteristics of the piñata, I am able to comfortably engage underserved audiences and provoke discussion, self-reflection, and an examination of one’s circumstances. Spanglish idioms often fail English translation and are incorporated into the work. The subtext is lost through translation and becomes a larger metaphor for society’s misunderstanding of cultures deemed as foreign. Text is specifically selected to reach an audience not typically served nor comfortable with art. I consider my artwork a social practice that seeks inclusivity through art placement and language. While a sentimental offering to our Latinx communities, my practice challenges dominant power structures through the most frivolous means: tissue paper. Jose Vasconcelos, the Mexican writer, philosopher, and politician, has greatly influenced my practice. After the Mexican Revolution, Vasconcelos became the Rector of the National University and, later, Secretary of Education. During his tenure, he commissioned artists to surround themselves with the Mexican people, absorbing their passions and conflicts and allowing artists to discover the true landscape of Mexican life. They were given the walls of public buildings to create murals disseminating their knowledge, giving rise to the cultural rebirth: Mexican Muralism. Vasconcelos intentionally used the artistic talent of Rivera, Orozco, and Siqueiros to educate the masses through a visual vocabulary. By his recognition of the importance of reaching new audiences outside of the contemporary art milieu, I, too, must be able to communicate visually with my community outside traditional aesthetics. In short, if I want my work to resonate within my community, I must come to them, utilizing the visual and cultural vernacular embedded within my site-specific and guerrilla installations, to engage and conceptually challenge my Latinx community.

column25: Hi! My name is Shana Roulhac. I am a self-taught artist from Buffalo, NY. My artistic journey began with simple cartoon character drawings using pencil and colored pencils. column25: My name is Bryant Hyun, and I am a director, filmmaker, and oil painter based in Los Angeles. As a Korean-American, my artistic journey as a painter and visual artist is deeply rooted in an unyielding commitment to the representation of the Asian-American experience. I am 25 and seek to become an artist of a generation, one who proudly carries the experiences of my confusion, culture, curiosity, and the continual pursuit of growth into all of my art. My work is driven by a profound desire to contribute to this broader picture, and with each original piece, I aim to weave the essence of our shared - (but unshared) - journeys, delving into the intricacies of cultural fusion, heritage, and the quest for belonging. I often introspectively compare myself to a Ronin–a wandering samurai, without master. Oil painting and the brush to me, represent the katana and its wielder. As I began this collection, I found myself drawn to vibrant colors: blood red, aquatic blues, and gold. The visual worlds and images of fables and cyberpunk cities are all brought to life utilizing oil paint, spray techniques, pastels, and mixed media as my mediums. Together, they create a visual symphony of controlled chaos. The vivid characters and champions of many of these pieces are comprised of self-portraits, and manifestations of superheroes and comic book characters that I grew up with– but wished looked a bit more like me. My reflections on my sessions and completed pieces revealed with violent clarity that I have a rage that motivates my persistence to create and be seen for what I am. Any and all resentments of feeling like a misfit and outsider spilled themselves into these pieces. I had a singular mission: to be the representation in the arts that I wanted to see; to be someone who adds legacy to the currently infantile history of what it means to be Asian-American. We are what I describe as chameleons, always trying to fit in, never quite standing out. I cannot accept this, and thus refuse this archetype, focusing this collection (and my career in the arts), on expressing the vibrance of this fiery obsession. Being self-taught, I find the canvas is a space where I can articulate and learn simultaneously, it becomes a voice for the unspoken–a space filled with contemplation and connection for myself and many others. So much of my work outside of painting is founded on teamwork, problem-solving, and good communication. Working as a director is often a medley of chaos, and the canvas to me is a place of peace, a portal to my innermost reflections and thoughts. Representation matters, and as an artist, I have committed my entire career thus far to contributing to a visual landscape that authentically reflects the diversity, resilience, and beauty of our community, and am only hungrier to take it further. Here are 4 original works from the Genesis Collection. Seamlessly integrated into this body of work are my directorial abilities, as I bring not only paintings but my newest work as a director. I humbly present a short film entitled: “Where the Mountain Met the River.” This abstract visual piece complements the entire mission of the collection and is a beautiful addition to my work as a visual artist. The film dances through the intimate confusion of being both Korean and American, often feeling: “not Asian enough” or “too Asian” for Western culture. Exploring a mystical fable founded on themes of familial ties, heritage, and the beauty of youth, I found myself pouring my heart and soul into this 10-minute short film. With a perceptive eye for composition and the soul of an artist, I aim to take you on a compelling journey that transcends cultural boundaries. I have yet to have my first debut solo show as an artist, and this film has not been released yet. Enjoy my presentation of: “Project Genesis”, a proud showcase of original art pieces and an abstract short film, each bearing witness to my philosophy as an artist, and unwavering dedication to my craft.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I started making obituary portraits at the end of December 2022. My grandma was a coal miner’s daughter who rose up from poverty and received her cosmetology license. Later she opened a salon. But as I was growing up, my grandma as my mom says, “did hair and makeup” at a Slovenian funeral home in Cleveland called Zele. We would drop her off at work. When she was done, we picked her up, and sometimes we would even go to lunch or the grocery store. To my grandmother, death, and burial were very matter-of-fact but respected. She was held to a high standard when preparing the deceased for their funeral, for those who attended. Zele is also the funeral home she requested her body to be prepared at and her service be held. Years before her death, she had picked out her dress and matching casket. Most important was that my mom was to draw in her eyebrows. With most obituaries online, you never really know exactly how the person died. Was it a suicide? Were they killed? Was it cancer? When I draw these portraits, I can’t tell you from what place I leave or go. I pick pictures from online websites. I tend to pick pictures that seem beyond a portrait. Hard to explain really. There might be a hat, a fish, an earring, or glasses. I follow the picture only so far, sometimes not really at all. I know there are many reasons not to make this work. Most reasons to not do something are reasons to do something. I make things I want to see or feel. I have not drawn people of color. Not for an act of exclusion, but because I do not feel I have the right or currency to do so. I find that many are not comfortable with thinking of their death. When people die, our surroundings heavily dictate our passing moving forward. I want to speak about the obituary photo. Some obituaries have a generic image of a flower or a candle. However, there are numerous resources available that help grieving families choose an obituary photo. Some websites attempt to guide you in picking a lasting image of the deceased. What does an obituary photo look like? Decide whether you want to use a current or past photograph for the obituary photo. Consider the quality of the photo. Understand where the photograph may be used. I am starting by just drawing. I am just drawing. But drawing can speak and act deeply. Drawing is much more eloquent in providing validation. Drawing is much more convincing than any of my conceptual motives. I am a selfish maker, a selfish viewer. You can do the same. I’m not telling you how to look, so don’t blame me.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The entry point to my current project was glacial erratics: giant boulders that were once carried long distances by shifting glacial ice. They experience time differently than us, it is seemingly slower and more certain instead of the ever quickening and chaotic human temporal experience. As I reckon with the heartbreaking and mundane experience of the passage of time, I look towards glacial boulders for a sense of permanency. Coming out of the last three lost, pandemic years I am older and different, and there is a grief in that. I cannot touch or alter time as it passes, but I can interact with its indelible objects; I can feel rocks and photographic paper under my fingertips. I can use the photographic medium to measure time as it passes, or use my hair to mark the years that change my form. This work is my means of exploring human impermanence and local landscape, and their intersections with the photographic process. As I work with the rock collections of those close to me, they act as a bridge between geological and human timelines. In the darkroom I focus on experimental printing methods, such as combing traditional printing and photograms. I have started intentionally printing on expired silver gelatin paper that was gifted to me and then sat unused for more than a decade. This has been a process of combining my own artistic choices with the effects that time has had on my materials.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My parents always lived an artistic life, and when they had me, they had no doubts I would be an artist. By looking at them I had no doubts as well. They put me in an old suitcase, which became my first bed and my first art studio; there I found my first pencils. My parents moved from one place to another, and I drew, drew, drew. That was a happy time. I used to draw on any surface I could find - on paper, walls, and asphalt, everywhere. As a child, I loved reading books with paintings of great artists and anatomy books. My mother used to help me finish my first drawings and my father explained to me what composition means and how to make a drawing complete. When I turned four, I began to call my activity “work”. Most importantly, I knew what art was. Now, this world gives me information, and I put all that I see and feel on paper. ‍ I work in all mediums, but mainly ink and paper. These media are natural materials with live energy. Just like humans, each sheet of paper has personal differences. Before starting a new drawing, I put a sheet of paper in front of myself and start a dialogue with it. First, I touch the surface and check the structure to understand its capabilities. Then I choose the side on which I will draw. Daily practice has reassured me that ink and paper are my most suitable media. Each thing created in this world has its name and purpose, and if I have to choose a name that would unite all of the pieces of my work into one whole, I will name it Chronicle. The reason driven by my work is that I genuinely enjoy the process, the outcome, and the feedback I get. For many years I have drawn the world I live in, and I continue. I pull it as I see, feel, and understand it. The images in my works are born from a combination of these factors. In other words, I do not come up with pictures and plots; these images are a product of this world. To some, figures and objects from my drawings may be fantasy, but this is only a point in the extent to which many factors intersect and form a 'thoughtform.' The aesthetics of images and objects, their definite sequential arrangement, create a particular philosophical aspect of the drawing. My work is a friend and partner, it will 'talk' to you, and you will discover new facets in it with each new sight.

column25: As a person who absolutely rejects bland, titanium white, Kelly Eden has found some of her artistic purpose by reveling in the specifics of the color wheel and in color theory. Life, as she sees it, simply has no room for the fluorescent washout of “pure” white—or for the rough, impermanent “tradition” of canvas as a medium. Instead, she paints on aluminum composite panels over a base of N5 gray, with an eye for bringing out the complex relationships among, and the interesting purities within, the many colors she uses. She paints in a realistic, labor-intensive style that puts emphasis on lighting and context. Thematically, she has begun to focus on evocative portraits of imagined women who represent the “saints” and the “spirits” that have shaped her very interesting life. Kelly had felt disconnected from religious thought and feeling for most of her life… and so, when she found a calling within herself to paint figures called “Mother Lithium” or “Primordial Debt,” the process slowly has revealed to her a deep and unnamed link between her path in life and something beyond. These figures’ mixtures of the positive and the negative, the modern and the ancient, have allowed Kelly’s artistic vision—which is both very specific in its imagery and very open in its metaphors—to bring her strengths together into a mature solidity.

column25: I am an Asian American painter, installation, and animation artist based in Houston, Texas. My conceptual research and art practice developed around the colorful heritage of textiles and patterning and how they can act as a base to connect mundane moments of contemporary life with rich layers of thought inspired from magical realism, philosophy, history, and storytelling. In anthropology, clothing is one of the early indicators distinguishing humans in the animal kingdom. From prehistory to the present day, textile, to some degree, holds a language of universality that binds the human condition. Throughout time, different individuals, groups, and cultures have used pattern-filled fabrics to shield their bodies from the elements, express their identity, and give philosophical meaning to their lives. For me, painting and creating patterns that derive from the print-in-textile tradition can make visible intricate historical constructs that are embedded in contemporary life. Patterns can be reminders of how motifs carried on the body travel from place to place. Some of these motifs travel under human prosperity, while others travel under forced migration. Within my art practice, I study where patterns come from, what philosophers wrote about particular heritage based motifs, and how they travel from one part of the world to the next. I derive these historical rich patterns into new forms and shapes placing various motifs with the contextual understanding of history to redefine the “decorative” as an active participant in the storytelling process.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
We are surrounded by visually intriguing, recurring designs in nature, ranging from simple to intricate. My screen-printed textiles focus on exemplifying and magnifying the complexity of repeated forms. I am passionate about the time-honored tradition of screen-printing textiles, taking great pride in executing all aspects of the process by hand. I want the viewer to absorb and be engrossed in the original drawings, overlapping shapes and complex compositions. My patterns and motifs reflect the significance of personal achievements and monumental life moments. Through the process of repetition, I wish to instill an overpowering visual sensation when viewing my work – a menagerie of brilliant, saturation of color, layers of delicate linear patterns, embedded with a unique record of personal anecdotes.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Embarking on a creative journey since childhood, my artistic evolution has been a continuous exploration of self-expression. Initially drawn to the comforting embrace of colors, the meditative quality of lines, and the sanctuary within shapes, my artistic roots trace back to early attempts with a crayon on my parents' walls. After an 18-year hiatus from oil painting, I immersed myself in using this medium as a tool for self-reclamation—a powerful means of reconnecting with my authentic self. The rediscovery of oil painting marked a pivotal moment as I delved into the profound process of pushing and pulling paint across the canvas, symbolically releasing pent-up anger and the tumultuous emotions associated with the mistreatment of my body. The aggressive brushstrokes became a visual representation of my journey towards self-acceptance and empowerment. My artistic trajectory took a deliberate turn as I embraced feminist theory, a passionate interest that began influencing my artwork and my discourse surrounding artistic practice. Concurrently studying Art History exposed the gaps within a patriarchal art industry dominated by male narratives and self-proclaimed 'inherent genius.' This realization sparked a determination to be a catalyst for change, challenging the status quo in the art world. The central theme of my work shifted towards a profound exploration of feminist perspectives, weaving narratives that examine what it means to be a woman within the intersectionality of feminism, activism, and artistry. Rejecting societal expectations, my art became a tool for introspection and outward change, a process that fundamentally transformed my creative output and sense of self. I embarked on a mission to question societal norms and amplify the voices of marginalized women. My upcoming project is currently in the research phase, gathering resources for a compelling exploration into women as victims and villains in society. This endeavor seeks to unpack the societal discomfort surrounding strong, unconventional women, challenging perceptions and questioning why certain women are labeled 'unlikeable' for living outside patriarchal norms. The project delves into the narratives of women who have been historically ostracized, jailed, chastised, or even killed for existing on the fringes of societal expectations. By examining this complex phenomenology, I aim to foster understanding and provoke thoughtful conversations about the portrayal of women in art and society. My art transcends a search for definitive answers, instead creating a space for grace, forgiveness, compassion, tolerance, and a nuanced understanding of the vastness of the human experience. As a woman artist, I commit to defying expectations, challenging stereotypes, and contributing to the broader dialogue surrounding gender, activism, and self-discovery.

column25: Megan primarily works with two mediums: repurposed burlap and tusche washes. Burlap is a renewable resource with rich texture and unique characteristics for making abstract marks and forms. Megan uses burlap in her artworks to represent struggle, imperfection, and sustainability. Tusche wash has unpredictable behaviors, and she enjoys how it moves as if it has a mind of its own. When Megan is up for a formidable challenge, she draws on burlap with oil pastels to create richly textured art. Megan hand-stretches repurposed coffee bags over wooden frames. She loves drawing on the knobbly surfaces of the burlap — a strenuously time-consuming adventure. It takes hundreds of oil pastel layers to fill each nook and cranny. She’ll overload some areas while leaving other spaces untouched, intentionally exposing the burlap. Megan also uses burlap to create monoprints or sculptural works. She cuts, deconstructs, and reshapes burlap pieces and then presses them into inks on her custom-made gelatin plates. She also paints on burlap, sews, and creates burlap sculptures. When she feels emotional, energetic, or explosive, nothing excites her more than working with tusche wash — typically a printmaking medium. Watery tusche washes dry into capricious puddles. In contrast, dry tusche wash is like painting with confectioner’s sugar: its powdery and moody behaviors are almost too wild to tame.

In Southern California, where golden sunlight reflects on the ocean, I see it slip into moonlight where it becomes like a dream. Every night, memories of daylight flourish in the imagination of. I paint all through the night to capture those memories and cultural nuances, and the sunrise tells me if I've done my job well.

column25: I am a hyperrealist painter with a focus on detail, subject matter, symbolism, metaphor, process experience, and archival quality. With my paintings I aim to create layers of complex understanding and construction, with a focus to connect to what is real; to understand reality; to relate with respect to the past, present, and future. By combining traditional oil glaze painting methods with new interpretations of the creative experience, individual paintings represent the stillness of light, influenced by events of their own time, through the critical eyes of art and linguistic philosophy. Light is meant to seem transitory, like a story that takes a few moments to tell and then fades into the memory. Reality-optics concepts naturally refine and synthesize my knowledge of the mechanics of light into highlight, with waves of light-crossed paths greatly connected. All of my paintings are made traditionally, using oil layering and drying techniques passed on for generations. My work conceptually focuses on optics and moments in time, like a photograph, as well as using photos as static resources and references to translate what I see and paint free hand.

column25: After ten years away from painting, I am here, learning to accept my losses, love myself, and regrow. I walked away from painting defeated. I was “not good enough.” I hated myself, and everything I made. I walked away to save myself. Three years ago I became permanently disabled due to a cyst in my spinal cord. Disability made me reconsider everything about my life, including my relationship with painting. Suddenly perfection was no longer an option in any area of my life, which has led to a strange freedom. Today, I struggle to walk and hold things in my hands. I no longer have feeling in my arms or legs. Everything is harder. So much has been taken away from me and my family. My body betrayed me, but I am still here, learning to regrow. Each painting is a clumsy stumble through an attempt to capture beauty. Where I once relied on skill and finesse, I am now scratching and clawing through the muddy process. Instead of getting wrapped up in how my work could be better, each painting is a celebration of the ability to participate in the process. Some days I paint lying on the couch. Some days I can only cut paper. I keep trying all the same. I’m learning to paint despite my brokenness… a humbling lesson for a recovering perfectionist. I find beauty in the Florida swamp that surrounds me. A quiet ballad of life and death. Decay and regrowth. The swamp is very much alive, consuming its own death, and ever-changing—a perfect place to contemplate my own regrowth.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Art is the most important thing in my life. I view it as the medium in which one assesses the complexity of humanity. There’s a liberating power that comes with being able to conjure whatever reality you desire through your art. I create art under various facets, including photography, music, and film. It is deeply imperative for people to have a vessel for their creativity. I’m so grateful to be able to create art that promotes introspectiveness and communicates the connective tissue that binds every human experience to one another. There is an important value in African philosophy called “ubuntu”, which translates to “I am because we are”. I feel that the arts are deft in creating unity and alleviating bigotry, which is a perfect example of the ubuntu principle being practiced in Western society. As a supporter of the arts, I’m simply in awe to witness the furthest extents of what the human mind can achieve through expression. The unique methods in which all of us present ourselves and our ideas to the world makes this collective society so beautiful.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Perhaps I am painting the cruelty of contemporary life because it is in opposition to my naïve outlook. In contrast with the physical features of my paintings, I think I am visualizing my feeling of anxiety in contemporary life. Though I do not think I am fully aware of the relationship between this cruelty and my anxiety, I represent this unsure feeling as figuratively as possible, and I am trying to figure it out in my painting. Although there are some prominent aspects of our contemporary world, such as commercialism and materialism, I cannot say who my enemy is. Some of the threats might involve the art world, however to protest such issues overtly does not suit my personal outlook. We cannot live in a developed society unless depend on its complex social rule. Following the rules and social values provides us stability. However, each of us creates exceptions, unstable rules and changeable standards. These double phases never unite, and I do not think they should be. I am really interested in these dilemmas and focus on these realities actually. This ambiguity may be my starting place and the reason I engage cruelty in my artwork. There does not appear to be a universal justice or evil in the present world. We need a grayer position in order to stay peaceful. In other words, I believe the dilemma of today’s morality, which is different from that of yesterday am, surely has helped develop my insight. Thus, my message cannot be a negation or an affirmation. My attitude in my artwork is a reflection of what the contemporary world is to me; my painting is my introspective confession. I believe I am making right artwork.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
'One man's trash is another man's treasure.' In the journey of life, we encounter challenges and hardships. Relationships fade, opportunities are lost, addictions can take hold, and setbacks become part of our narrative. We may hurt ourselves, or external forces beyond our control attempt to shape our lives. My art is a representation of taking the 'trash' in our lives, both mental and physical, and transforming it into the fuel that propels us forward on the next upward spiral of our life's journey. Art has been my vehicle for personal transformation, a powerful force that has propelled me beyond the healing I once believed was beyond my strength and courage to achieve. Without art, I question whether I would be alive today, considering the challenges of past addictions. As I internally transformed, my yearning for love and serenity expanded to encompass not only myself but also the planet and its inhabitants. My hope is that, collectively, we can discover true love for one another and enjoy the planet in a healthy manner. Therefore, it is crucial for me to make a positive impact on keeping the planet clean, sharing messages through 'trash' art. For instance, consider a sea turtle mistaking a jellyfish for a plastic bag, resulting in suffocation after mistakenly consuming the plastic. These issues are not insurmountable; they require connection, empathy, and an understanding of the interconnectedness we share with the planet and all its inhabitants. Through my art, I aim to foster a connection not only with the artwork itself but also with the message of preserving our planet. By advocating for switches to single-use plastics and sharing a new message of hope, I hope to inspire others to join in the cause of maintaining a clean and sustainable environment. Looking forward, my aspirations extend beyond this project. I aim to grow and undertake larger expeditions for scuba diving, collecting ocean debris, and making a significant impact on a global scale. Through these future endeavors, I hope to continue inspiring positive change and environmental consciousness around the world.

column25: Embarking on a creative journey spanning over a decade, my commitment to art has evolved into a focused endeavor—shaping plastic pollution into artistic expressions for nearly six years now. The genesis of this unique approach can be traced back to a moment of inspiration in my home state of Florida. I conceived the idea of standing on a city street corner with a sign that read, 'Every 33 cents you donate to me, I will pick up a piece of trash.' In a single night, donations totaling around $100 fueled a day spent cleaning up the highway, collecting hundreds of discarded items. This creative spark continued to grow during my university years, where I delved into art, environmental education, and sustainability. As part of a class initiative, we organized a 'trash fashion show,' fashioning outfits entirely from discarded materials. This marked the inception of my journey into creating fashion from trash. The first piece I crafted was composed of 1,000 cigarette butts, serving as an educational commentary on their harmful impact. Post-graduation, I participated in another trash fashion show, this time fashioning an outfit from 300 plastic bottles. My artistic exploration extended to Central and South America, engaging in beach clean-ups, collaborating with local communities, and producing music videos that conveyed messages about plastic pollution. The lyrics aligned with the meticulously crafted outfits, all made from found plastic pollution. This creative journey led me to Costa Rica, where, over the span of a year, I crafted six large sculptures from beach trash and discarded items. Collaborating with resorts, non-profit organizations, and coffee shops, I found unique opportunities to showcase my art and raise awareness. This exhilarating trajectory has propelled me toward my next expedition, for which I am actively seeking grant funding. The upcoming project involves scuba diving for ocean debris and transforming the recovered materials into a sculpture. The ultimate goal is to donate this sculpture to an educational institution and establish a lasting monument for the community, fostering self-education on environmental issues. Beyond being artworks crafted from plastic pollution and junk, my creations carry profound missions and meanings that extend beyond my comprehension. Each piece of art becomes a mission in itself. Recently, as I ventured into creating my own fashion show and learned to host events, I seized my first real fundraising opportunity. The event served as a platform to shed light on significant issues such as homelessness, human and child trafficking, mental health, and addictions. Each outfit, made from trash, symbolized a different problem in the world, aiming to bring awareness and educate people about these issues. During the fashion show, I connected with a child trafficking organization in the city, allowing trafficked and abused children to participate, raising awareness for the cause. I am truly grateful for this opportunity and eagerly anticipate the day when I can contribute even more to what truly matters in the future.

column24:

I began painting stories in portraits as a way to process my emotions as I spent years in internal search for reasons to live. I had a few breaking points that changed my understanding of reality. Using a myriad of influences – emotions, thoughts, art history, mythical experiences and travel – I create symbolic, figurative art that explores the secrets and power of the Universe and our relationship to it. I made a promise to myself to paint the light world I want to see, feel and create, which is free of pain, judgement and fear. A collection of recent paintings and drawings is a meditation on the meaning of life. I want to inspire people to connect to their own soul and light to arrive at a deep state of peace, love and balance, forming a heart-centered world. Through the movement of figures, color, geometric symbols, and the natural world that express balance and harmony, I’d like to show the power of the Universe that exists inside us. All we need is to tap into that connection to see our own light and purpose. Infinite space, love, joy and creation.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
When I started this most recent series of paintings I had just finished work on a new studio building behind my house. My father and I had spent the early part of 2019 constructing the studio and I spent the majority of 2020 slowly finishing the interior by myself – insulation, walls, ceiling, etc. I moved all my art supplies into the building in November of 2020 and started making these. I had a full container of drywall joint compound left over from construction which I used to begin building low relief surfaces to paint. I've been working with this icon, : ) for over 10 years. It’s become a solid character or group of characters in my personal pantheon. The avatar – two dots and a line – is the lowest-common-denominator visual representation of the individual online. As remnants of our social media landscape, it's not hard to imagine these could exist outside communication technology as totems, future archaic emoji. In the past I have used weed names coined by amateur botanists, bad internet passwords, and homeland security’s list of words being tracked across the internet to title my paintings in large series. Lately titles come into the studio as I’m working. They’re song titles, or lyrics, earworms I attach to the supports of paintings, coincidental fragments I pick up and put here and there.

Today his inspiration comes from his lifelong love of comic books, science fiction and fantasy as well as the advancement of technology and innovation. Using these concepts as a starting point, he is now exploring the interactions and interconnectedness of humanity, nature and technology and forcing the world around him to make sense through a lens of bright colors and bold lines. Never defined by his past, he is diligent in constantly refining his own image and vision creating art and design through digital and traditional media.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
We transcend all limits, boundaries, and language, recognizing the entire globe of being—a bridge between levels of consciousness, aware of being itself. In my artistic journey, I embody the constant sifter, sorter, and gleaner, akin to a huntress prowling for overlooked materials. Drawing inspiration from the natural world, my work mirrors those mystical spaces, weaving an alternate cosmos and mythos from the raw material discovered within the pages of second-hand books and found materials. The ritual of collecting becomes a source of equilibrium in my practice, offering a psychological sanctuary where I assert control over a world that otherwise spins beyond my influence. It's a complete withdrawal into fantasy, a creation of spaces born from the elation found in discovering new worlds within a single drop of water through my microscope. These microscopic revelations, glimpses into a world unseen, propel me into a slowed time, where I witness the interconnectedness of our surroundings. The potential for sentient lifeforms elsewhere, the sacredness of ritual, and the existence of a cosmic consciousness awaiting discovery guide the evolution of my artistic narrative. In the act of cutting out each form with my scissors, I realize a formation concurrently takes shape somewhere in the vast universe. My process becomes a dance with the unseen, a harmonious collaboration between the observed and the observer, as I continue to create meaning and myths from the raw material of existence.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I believe the best thing about being an artist is having a mental state that’s consistently generative— where I can explore an inner horizon line of potential through material investigations of sights, sounds, and textures to create visual experiences. I enjoy the freedom of creating, which comprises excitement and realistic distress over what to do next to acquire the resources I need. When I believe I have reached the end of my ability to create more, I inevitably find ways to continue on: the ideas never end, nor does the ingenuity to create with materials on hand. I produced this recent body of work by shining light through a piece of reflective plastic trash. If the reflective plastic, which was usually flat, had not been crumbled up, the light interactions would not be visible. Most of my work over the last several years is made from found materials. During the pandemic, I sometimes felt like I had very little to give the world, yet I continued to produce art from light, perhaps as a metaphor for hope. I feel lucky for this reason to have a strange part of my head that continues to evolve and explore the visual world, sometimes despite myself. That small creative part of my mind operates on endless optimism for the future.

column25: My paintings, sculptures, and videos emphasize cognition and perception through the generation of physical and digital experiences. When viewed from oblique angles, my work reveals the interactions of projected light filtered through optical effects, resulting in a recreation of the Aurora Borealis. I produce physical-digital works where liminality, in-between spaces, and experiences outside a colonized world offer new possibilities. As a multi-heritage, Alaska Native (Koyukon Athabascan), I document natural light in the Alaskan and Californian terrain to reimagine perception as Indigenous, subliminal, and theoretical. The work insists that perception is as much a simple act as it is a subjective creation. “False light” permeates my pieces and may appear artificial and, at other times, genuine. Light itself is not objective; it bends and twists, invoking the unsolvable particle-wave paradox in quantum mechanics. This shifting and unresolved framework guides my physical and experiential image making. Using light as a material, I reclaim the romanticization of landscape imagery, otherwise intended for policies of manifest destiny that drive tourism, commerce, and profit, by providing expansive interpretations of Indigeneity, perception, and the environment. As a visual metaphor, projections of beadwork and Indigenous imagery become the light that moves through everything, embodying continuity in relation to past and present technologies. Digital, traditional, and upcycled materials form hybrid objects that represent an imagined and ancestral potential. If humans perceive and are therefore active participants in the optical viewing of phenomena, my work speculatively asks, “What is seen when imagining the future?”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The main idea idea of my work is confidence. I want to show confident women and promote the idea of living life loud and unapologetically. Creating works and having them around me reminds me of this. I spent the first half of my life living in Pakistan and the lifestyle I was made to conform to made me very aware of my body and how little power I had in terms of doing what I wanted. There was an expectation of how to act and dress and make myself smaller and that never felt right with me. I always tried to challenge these societal norms as much as I could but I found by creating powerful works of art I found a way to push those boundaries. When I paint I have the fully nude realistic figures standing in their power. By having this energy women firstly will see a body that they can connect to and then seeing it taking up space and commanding attention rather than hiding away I would like them to be able to be inspired and start the conversation of if she can do it so can I. As well as the fact that it is from the female gaze and painted in a way that is not meant to be looked AT but the figure is looking upon you. So it's as if the viewer can see the figure is fully comfortable and almost take a breath of relief when they don't have to live up to it.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
African spirituality in coniunction with the concept of the chakras are powerfully connected and are the underlying foundations on which my work is rooted in the fertile soil of this ever teaching garden of knowledge. My art is quite evidentially African at its core. My third eye is constantly vibrating on a high frequency as a result of ancestral DNA activating my creativity to manifest their messages. There are countless African spiritual systems that differ from country to country and village to village. As a diaspora African, many of the differing systems provide reference points for me. Namely, the Akan lineage of mainly Ghana and Ivory Coast. I incorporate the Andinkra symbols as a representation of a connection between 'here' and 'there' although we are all truly one. The universal connections found in the symbols and practices of diverse African spirituality correlated greatly to the concepts of the chakras. While historically esoteric, the chakras speak directly to the connectedness of all things on earth and throughout the stars no matter how difficult it may be for others to grasp. People, places, animals, things, celestial bodies etc. are existing in the now and therefore are one. My art pieces manifest those messages shared between the Andinkra symbols and the chakra system system. I manifest the messages born within me as a means to challenge and remind others of what is.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
Diana Rojas explores the hidden and invisible through interdisciplinary collaboration and conversation across fields such as Philosophy, Music Composition, Physics, Material Science and Visual Art. The tools and environments she creates - by utilizing the archaic and contemporary, the digital and sculptural, known and unknown, and the minute and immersive - culminate in experiential works that prompt viewers to slow down and inspire introspection. The slowing down that these works provoke raises questions about existence, reality and being. By combining materials and elements of theories and devices of exploration, she references their antecedents, but creates new opportunities for viewer investigation. This includes utilizing creative coding, video, 3D modeling, welding steel, kiln forming and soldering glass, and utilizing sand as projection screens. Additionally, she utilizes sound and light to create captivating installations, inspired by interest in the influence that immaterial forces have on human minds and decisions.

I am inspired by the longing to be gathered, to create meaning together and to dismantle the structures that keep us separate and disconnected. In all of my work, I am seeking to both find and cultivate a sense of intimacy with self, others and place. As an artist, I give myself full permission to work in any and all mediums to which I am called. I’m not quite sure how the art world views artists like this, but to me, it is liberating to fulfill my hunger to create with any and all materials. In all of my work, there are thematic through lines relating to translating spiritual resonances from other realms, cultivating a sense of home and belonging and experimenting with the boundaries of beauty. I want to create art that brings people into dialogue about challenging topics at the same time as making them feel a sense of peace and comfort.

column25: Lo Jones, a native of New York City, has always been immersed in the city's pulsating music scene. Growing up, her roots in music were seeded in local theater and the eclectic tastes of her native NYC parents. While transitioning towards a fervor for filmmaking at the specialized high school, Academy for Careers in TV and Film, her love for singing never waned, as she shared cover songs across social media during those years. Rekindling a deep connection to music, she began crafting her own lyrics at 17 before embarking on her journey at the University of Pittsburgh. In her freshman year, fateful meetings with local musicians propelled Jones into recording and releasing original music under the moniker 'Lauren Mose,' where tracks like “I Wanna Know” and “Ad Hominem” crystallized her ambitions of becoming a singer/songwriter. In 2020, amidst the pandemic challenges and her university graduation, Jones made a choice to pause her musical path, allowing space to redefine their sound and artistic identity. The subsequent year, 2022, unfolded as a tumultuous chapter. Marked by ruptured friendships and duplicity in romantic relationships, a profound sense of isolation and depleted self-esteem culminated. Opportunely, this turbulent period also sparked a surge of creativity, transforming heartbreak and mistrust into powerful themes of self-love, understanding, motivation, and a clear vision for the future—this emotional journey laid the foundation for their upcoming debut album, 'Torrent.' 'Torrent' encapsulates a deeply emotional and transformative phase in Jones' life, reflecting her personal evolution. Born from the emotional heaviness of that year, the album symbolizes a passage from the depths of heartbreak and betrayal to a newfound sense of self and purpose. 'Torrent' ventures into the realm of Cinematic Alternative Pop, drawing inspiration from life experiences and the emotional depth of her musical influences. The album weaves together a diverse array of artists—from childhood favorites like Missy Elliot, Ne-Yo, N.E.R.D, and The Gorillaz to genres like UK House, Synth Pop, and Drum and Bass. Scheduled for release in the spring of 2024, 'Torrent' promises to be a profoundly moving and resonant musical narrative, a reflection of Jones' personal journey.

With an aim to bring communities together by ﬁnding human-elements that link artistry to real-life, she ﬁnds the repetitive theme of movement impacting her work, providing a bridge of consciousness to connect. Jessie Pettit holds a BA in Moving Image from the University of West London. She also has studied at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts. Her work has been showcased throughout the city of Detroit along with the City of London.

column25: I mix the devoted noticing of Obsessive Compulsive Disorder, the hyper-fixation of ADHD, and the distorted desperation of trauma to create my art. Each element of my art was developed to keep my hands busy while my neurodiverse brain does gymnastics. I use watercolor to capture the patterns of gravity and to loosen control over my OCD. column25: My work uses the dynamic relationship between the arts and natural sciences, reflecting also my personal and educational background. Having grown up in a society always torn between the desire to preserve nature and the need to exploit it, I am driven to act on this problematic duality. My purpose is to create sculptural designs that blend traditional craftsmanship with contemporary aesthetics, conveying narratives on biodiversity preservation and environmental issues. I believe in the potential of eco-conscious artistic processes, predominantly using reclaimed materials and upcycled wood, in a practice where I personally deeply engage, physically and mentally. I built large public installations through performances in the late 1990s, and my practice is still evolving to engage a broader audience. By combining artistry and biology, by bridging figuration and abstraction, the goal is to generate interaction and personal connection with the viewer. I strive to make visually appealing three-dimensional artworks with the perspective of a naturalist-activist. More than simplistic representations of animals or plants, these sculptures are intended to ignite eco-awareness and foster respect for the biosphere in a kind, guilt-free manner.

column25: Taravat Talepasand is an artist, activist, and educator whose labor-intensive interdisciplinary painting practice questions normative cultural behaviors within contemporary power imbalances. As an Iranian-American woman, Talepasand explores the cultural taboos that reflect on gender and political authority. Her approach to representation and figuration reflects the cross-pollination, or lack thereof, in our Western Society. “Growing up Iranian within America had been arduous and awkward. As a whole, we, as Iranians, had little consciousness of assimilation because of a constant denial of our permanence in America. In Iran, I found myself to be transgressive, yet within American culture being Iranian is transgressive in that American individualism and Iranian deference to tradition were irreconcilable. Traveling down one of those paths meant turning your back on the other even if the defiance was temporal; this was the hidden catch of the formation of my identity. The contradictions caused my head to constantly bounce around the question of inherent identity–that which is exterior and self-defined versus inward and pre-determined. To create art, it is imperative for me to be vulnerable—extracting the personal truths driven by my Diaspora, history, nostalgia, self-awareness, and rebellion. Much of my work revolves around the struggles of womanhood and navigating personal identity as a hyphenated individual. However, I believe that art must possess an element of vulnerability in order to provoke change—socially, intellectually, and morally. In a world where ideas of culture, political and intellectual activities are evolving, what will this change mean for generations to come?”

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Using repurposed, vintage, and rescued textiles, I create works that challenge the traditional notion of quilting and invite viewers to interpret meaning based on their own emotional response to the works. Drawing inspiration from my Appalachian roots and the quilting traditions of my foremothers, my work is an evolution of the quilting craft. Departing from conventional patchwork, my textile compositions depict shapes, objects, and narratives, influenced by historical art movements such as Baroque, Art Deco, Bauhaus, and The Golden Age of Illustration. With textiles as my medium, I strive to contemporize these influences, infusing them with my unique voice. In my artistic journey I aim to shift the meaning and perception of what a quilt can be. Within each work, I try to create luscious, soft, and sumptuous moments that provoke emotional responses and allow for individual interpretation. Each work carries personal significance for me, stemming from my experiences with mental illness. Through art, I navigate my inner emotions and find solace, and I aspire to offer viewers a similar comforting and introspective experience. The softness and tactile nature of textiles, inherently associated with comfort, become a metaphorical embrace, inviting the viewer into a gentle and contemplative space. I aim to create art that could be achieved through other mediums but is intentionally conveyed through the transformative and comforting nature of textiles.

column25: The Deleted World is a tarot deck project begun in late 2020. For this submission, the borders and formatting have been removed to present the artwork as a stand-alone creation. Many influences went into this deck, from emerging technologies and internet cultures, to occult histories and tarot illustrations past. Admittedly, tarot and internet technologies don’t seem like the most intuitive pairing. Yet this deck explores a common thread: the promise of a better world that never takes material form. Like many of you, I was raised in a religious home and grew up believing in the utopian vision promised to me each Sunday. Yet l also knew that salvation could never be mine - I was too queer, too strange, an outcast. Likewise, the early days of the internet seemed to offer a similar utopian promise of unlimited connection and community. Yet these visions of better worlds have faded into our current dis-information era of cyber superstitions and data-driven oppression. In both of these failed utopian visions, something was lost along the way. A choice was made. A world was deleted. The Deleted World Tarot is a tool for healing the rift between the choices of the past and the consequential loss of alternative futures. A tool and a story told out of order and out of time, it's a hope that illuminates the worlds we lose by the choices we make every day, and a call to consider what is worth saving. What is not saved will be lost.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Benjamin Timpson’s luminous portraits are constructed of butterfly wings, each visage delineated through mysterious patterns and complex interplay of color and iridescence. The delicate biological relics are responsibly sourced by the artist, deconstructed according to their unique markings, then pieced over the artist’s rough sketch on a lightbox and photographed at various stages of illumination. Timpson, a photographer who has been experimenting with positive/negative images and transmitted light for over two decades, has become familiar with the wings’ singular physical qualities – the microscopic structures that emit light and color, and the differences in these structures from species to species. The resulting compositions are both enigmatic and uncannily specific; an organic delineation that shifts depending on the viewer’s position as well as the light in which they are seen. A descendant of Puebloan peoples, Timpson’s transcendent works portray Indigenous women who have been the victim of sexual assault and murder – a population that is four times more likely to experience such violence. Some of his subjects are found through historical research, while others are contemporaries who partner with Timpson to articulate the injury to a community when its daughters and matriarchs are attacked, as well as speak to the beauty of memory and survival. Timpson sees these portraits as a metaphor for the significance of individual lives impacted by cultural violence, and as a way of examining the horrors of centuries-long exploitation of Native lands and cultures. Yet Timpson considers his work an act of hope and catharsis. The artist remarks: “The butterfly is appropriate because there’s a metamorphosis that takes place with these portraits; my work is about giving voice to the voiceless, and bringing to light the lives of these women.”

column25: Hello, my name is Reina Ciccarone. Born in Connecticut but now a proud upstate New Yorker, art has woven through the fabric of my life since childhood. As a naturally creative soul, my artistic journey commenced with the tactile joy of collage-making. My room became a gallery of layered creations, each composed from magazine clippings. Over time, painting and poetry have emerged as my primary and cherished forms of artistic expression. Being a mother of four, my family has consistently been my top priority. I've incorporated art into countless family occasions—holidays, birthdays, and special projects; you name it. It's been my way of keeping the spirit of art alive in our home. Now that my children are growing older, I find myself with more time to delve into exploring my personal style of art. For me, art is more than a pastime; it is a deeply personal form of self-expression—an avenue for escape and liberation of mind, body, and soul. Until this year, 2023, it remained a source of pure enjoyment and recreation. However, I've recently embraced the idea of extending my creations beyond my home to share them with the world. The act of creating with my hands brings me unparalleled joy, my own magic. This marks my initial venture into sharing my creations, and I am currently in the process of establishing a dedicated digital art store where my paintings will find a home.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
All my life, I have looked for peace. The first time I thought I was going to die, I was 5 years old. My Dad got in a car accident driving with me while intoxicated. I hit my head on the dashboard and there was blood everywhere. As a child in a tumultuous and often scary home, the only times I felt peace were in art-making and learning about animals. Art has always been a place where I could control my environment and manifest safety. As an adult, a lot of my present work is about creating perfect little worlds, full of happy animal families. They exist in a verdant atmosphere, full of abundance even while death is recognized via various bones and skulls. All terrariums are isolated, floating worlds in the sky, giving them a peaceful dream-like quality and indicating their severance from a harsh reality. I find myself thinking of death often, and I truly believe that the promise of death is what makes life meaningful and beautiful, not just for humans, but animal life as well. We exist together, inexorably linked in life on a planet headed towards destruction. Often, my work features endangered species and serves not only as a child-like reclamation of safety, but also speaks to habitat loss and a disappearing world. The animals in my worlds are at peace, but the threat of scarcity and disappearance looms.

column25: I attended Worcester Polytechnic Institute for my Bachelor’s in mechanical engineering, and UC Berkeley for my Master’s in nuclear engineering. Throughout these degrees, I frequently had to use machinery to make engineering parts. While learning these techniques, I found that I much preferred using the technology to create art rather than gears. While I should have been studying thermodynamics and physics, I spent as much of my time as I could making art for friends and family. After graduate school, I moved to the DC-area to start my corporate career. This began during COVID, and I quickly found myself lonely and struggling to feel like myself. Because of this, I joined a maker space as a way of meeting new people and making art. While there, I found that making art was always my favorite part of the week, and decided to make it a larger part of my life. I very recently switched to part-time in my corporate job to give me more time to work on my art, and have truly never been happier. I’ve since opened up an art studio—J Studio LLC— in Washington D.C. to both make my own art and help others do so as well. The studio is a space for me to inspire and help others to create art, and hopefully find as much reprieve from their daily lives from creating as I do.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My practice has developed through a yearning desire to understand one's cultural history. As a second generation Chinese American, my parents immigrated to the states in the 90's shortly after the Tiananmen Square Massacre, and I was born and raised in the Bay Area California. My upbringing like many individuals existing in a diaspora consisted of a fragmented perspective of cultural identity, creating a third culture of hybridity imbued with both western and eastern human conditions. At times creating contradicting visual and ideological languages that inevitably created the back bone of my artistic practice. My material process layers drawings, paint, and at times photo transfers, embedding found cultural symbols throughout the surface. Through this process, I confront my own cultural amnesia and grapple with the memories and inherited experiences lost through assimilation. Highlighting the process of the return. My work often juxtaposes loaded political protest imagery, such as photos from the Tiananmen Square Massacre where my parents experienced first hand, with bright auspicious symbols from my childhood. These symbols become a filter to experience and interact with generational trauma. Hoping to piece together fragments of my personal history, illustrating an essence of the Chinese Diaspora in the modern world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My drawings offer intimate glimpses into my life and personal experiences. Using only pens and pencils, I create intricate, spontaneous sketches on various surfaces, from sketchbooks and large sheets of Bristol Board to rolls of butcher's paper and even post-it notes. What sets my work apart is the element of serendipity—I never set out with a clear destination in mind. A drawing might begin with a simple idea, like recreating a figure from an advertisement, only to evolve into a nostalgic portrayal of my fourth-grade teacher. These drawings often become dynamic collages, incorporating a wide array of elements, such as notes, quotes, faces, hands, geometrical shapes, furniture, and fragmented body parts. I also infuse my drawings with words, capturing overheard conversations, personal thoughts, and vivid memories. Everything finds its place in this kaleidoscope of imagery, from innocent depictions of babies to the intriguing allure of mugshots and the melancholy of obituaries.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My artistic practice is primarily based in jewelry and metalsmithing. As a jeweler, my work is inspired by the decorative aesthetics and vast history of classical Indian jewelry seen through the lens of a first-generation American. My cultural background influences the subject matter of my pieces as I consider the role of my heritage in understanding beauty. I am fascinated by the relation of one’s past with their present and how they inform one another. By using fine metalsmithing techniques alongside modern methods of making, such as laser engraving and UV printing, I aim to question whether the past and present exist on a linear timeline, or if time (and beauty) are cyclical and collapsible. I create wearable art objects, including earrings, pendants, and brooches, which weave personal narratives to engage not only myself as the artist, but also the wearer. When making brooches, for instance, I show the back of the pieces the same attention and care as the front. Though not everyone may notice these intricacies, or even be privy to their existence, the secret messages and designs hold significant meaning for the wearer. In crafting these intimate connections through my jewelry, I aim to offer a different perspective on beauty, inviting viewers to explore the intricate layers of their own stories within my work.

column25: “SideStreets”— The series was inspired by the nearly 20 years the artist spent in New York City, where every street showcased artwork by hyperlocal artists— whether graffiti, large murals, wheat paste characters, yarn bombs, galleries— art is on every surface in the city. The large paintings in this series are loosely based on the building blocks of graffiti, which is simply: words. Hidden in each of the pieces are letter structures, stretched, deconstructed, repurposed into what the artist enjoys most: abstraction. “Organism.” — The series is a philosophical embrace of life and the most characteristic trait in life— uncertainty. As viewers and participants, we see the pieces and we recognize forms or textures, even in abstraction. We interpret them as possible forms of life, or beginnings as embryos; as cells or as complex systems. I’ve chosen to work in graphite, pencil, marker and pen & ink. My work sets out to encourage this blurring of interpretation and enable thoughts about what makes up life, as well as what life can be. Through the work, we receive permission to embrace chaos and uncertainty.

column25: Idling Conversations is a series of my attempts in liberating art from its fate as a communicative medium through a painting practice. Art, admittedly, panders to the penchant of a capitalist society, which demands professionalism and efficiency. Subsumed under the social context and its expectations for art; to effectively deliver rich information, it is constantly exposed to the dangers of becoming mere replications of existing propaganda. To create a collective nothing, leaving just the vacant, yet testimonial activations of paintings, is the only autonomous act I can dare to achieve. Idling Conversations encounters inevitable failures throughout its chronicled endeavors to dismantle the already established visual languages in paintings. However, a newly found constraint always births the next endeavor. Idling Conversations is the pitiful fruit of our constraints. It is only the constraint that facilitates the ever-failing, yet ever-shifting, Idling Conversations.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
In “what it’s like to feel”, I confront the struggle of dealing with major depression and articulating the depths of my emotions. Words, though powerful, often fall short, unable to encapsulate the vast expanse of assumptions and relentless ruminations that permeate my mind. As a queer, Black man, the challenges of existing in a world that others and stigmatizes individuals like me only add to the difficulty of this journey. It is amidst this dissonance that I continue to seek meaning and purpose, forging connections that remind me of the profound relationships that anchor my life. Photography and art making have become my conduits for understanding these feelings, allowing me to better navigate the serpentine pathways of my thoughts. Photography, in its paradoxical nature, becomes both a solace and a labyrinth. It offers a unique means of expression, sometimes possessing the ability to transcend the limitations of verbal language, yet simultaneously presents its own set of challenges. Through various photographic techniques and genres–portraits of myself and loved ones, constructed still-lifes, physical collage and manipulation, urban landscapes, and digital construction–I futilely attempt to decipher the complexities of my mental health and the world around me. Ultimately, the images created in this project are meant to serve not only as a personal catharsis, but also as a quasi-journalistic endeavor; inviting others to bear witness to the profound struggles and triumphs that we, the lesser valued brothers, sisters, and enbies of this community, face in grappling with our own existence.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Andy Demczuk's recent work embraces an interdisciplinary approach to both visual and sound art, delving into the techniques and theories that shape them. His creations reflect the influence of technology and the clock-based pace of modernity—aiming to unveil the coded systems of logic, like musical notation, that structure our temporal experiences, which ultimately manifest as orchestral visions. In his art opera, 'Amoeba Variations,' the musical pieces bear straightforward numerical titles such as 'One' or 'Twelve,' evoking personified hours, reminiscent of Edward Elgar's famous sketches of his friends in the symphonic piece 'Enigma Variations,' which served as inspiration for Demczuk. Within his 2D work, one encounters digitized humanoid assemblages inhabiting intricate cityscapes of buildings, doorways, and staircases. These visuals are intertwined with the sonic attributes of Andy's musical realms, which he often refers to as 'MIDI-Land,' where everything appears vivid yet is resonate of the uncanny. Within this compressed and speculative landscape, sound and form unite in an unending cycle of descent (catabasis) and ascent (anabasis), mirroring the metaphysical journey traversed by humans throughout any given day, akin to the undulating waveform of life's highs and lows.

column25: Artist Statement: Aimee Edgeworth My art is a reflection of the interplay between history, memory, and creativity. It's a celebration of the stories that linger in the corners of old photographs and the emotions that echo through generations. Drawing inspiration from my family's rich history, I explore the power of visual storytelling. The worn edges of old photographs, the sepia tones of time, and the faces of ancestors long gone all serve as my muse. Through my work, I aim to capture not just the visual elements but also the essence of these moments—those fleeting emotions and unspoken narratives that connect us to our past. My creative process is an exploration of the past and a reimagining of the future. It's a dance between tradition and innovation, where I incorporate techniques from various artistic disciplines to breathe life into historical tales. Whether it's through painting, sculpture, or mixed-media installations, I seek to transport viewers into a world where history meets imagination. Community plays a pivotal role in my art. I view my work as a catalyst for connections, conversations, and shared experiences. Teaching art to local students, collaborating on community-driven projects, and spearheading initiatives like the 'Murrieta Train Project' are all ways in which I contribute to the cultural fabric of my hometown. The 'Murrieta Train Project' is a testament to my belief in the transformative power of art. It's an endeavor that transcends the ordinary, turning an 85-foot dining car into Murrieta's first cultural arts center—a space where the past, present, and future converge. Through this project, I aim to offer a platform for our community to explore its roots, express its creativity, and envision a brighter future. In essence, my art invites viewers to step into the evocative world of storytelling through visuals. It's a journey through time, memory, and imagination, where every stroke of the brush and every sculpted form reveals the layers of history waiting to be uncovered. It's an ode to the enduring power of creativity, an exploration of our shared humanity, and an homage to the stories that shape us. Welcome to my artistic universe, where the past and present merge, where history becomes a canvas, and where every viewer becomes a part of the narrative. Join me in this quest to rediscover our roots, celebrate our creativity, and embark on a timeless journey through art.

column25: Holding space for the misaligned moment is how I see my work. It is about a disrupted urban childhood, the climate crisis, the ravages of war, and the parallels between them. My encounters with nature as a young adult were of awe. I saw the revitalization within the fecundity of nature. From this lens, I find my empathetic relationship with local and current events across the globe. Urban architecture, functional objects, and decorative heirlooms have served as the background for my most challenging situations. Because of this, I notice the trash on the ground, floating in the air, the evolution of our structures, and the people thought of as inconsequential and riff-raff. What is left behind tells a story. I repurpose the same glass I saw smashed in the streets and abandoned buildings. Glass is a dualistic medium. Its strength contains and protects, but if broken, it fails its contents and becomes dangerous to those around it. I cut, fuse, bend, blow, cast, and crush glass. Once manipulated, it is reborn, losing the stigma of being refuse, unwanted, and broken. Likewise, people and places can mend when left un-assaulted, though forever altered, loved anew, and hopefully, wiser.
