column25: Tim McFarlane Artist Statement The fluid space of memory, influenced by time, place and experience forms the foundation of content for my paintings and works on paper. As personal meaning and influences evolve, the residue of the past lingers and we are left to attempt to reconcile new information with knowledge with what is already known, or imagined, of the past. Some of the old information and ways of being are overwritten, forgotten or marginalized, a result of continuous cycles of building and rebuilding that happens in most man-made places, like cities. Resistance to these changes is embodied by people who pass down information about what once was. That information, however, is often clouded by nostalgia, personal agendas and the fragility of human memory, so subsequent generations are often left to piece together history from scraps of information and artifacts. By merging aspects of lived experience, observations of changes occurring around me and imagined scenarios, I make non-objective work that reflects broad experiences of life and memory. My works have an emphasis on multi-layered systems, color (chromatic and gray scale) and process. In my paintings, overlapping forms and fractured spaces alternately support and negate each other, mirroring changes that occur in everyday life. Shifts in formal relationships, space and perception, some subtle and others more readily perceptible, contribute to the psychological content in my work. I make my paintings and works on paper with a combination of open and opaque layers that incorporate various modes of mark making that include: direct painting, drawing, image transfers and stenciling. Taken together, my process forges a sense of interdependence, history and chance happenings, which, in turn allows room for multiple meanings in my work.

column25: My work consistently reflects my Eastern European family background and my dedication to exploring nature and the cosmos. I often investigate the specifics of shifts in myself, my surroundings, and the world in general and these examinations can be seen realized in my colorful murals, immersive sculptures, large-scale mosaics, and paintings. I love pigment and color, and studying the alchemy that makes magic on canvas, paper, wood, and other mediums. My creative process is an embodied and communal one—I tap into friends and mentors, long walks and sitting for quiet desert sunsets, visiting with my animals and gardens, and maintaining a constant curiosity and desire to seek new information. While I use a variety of modalities, materials, and processes, the subject matter of each creation determines the materials and process, as does the community and place I am creating from and within. After the grief of the 2016 fire in Oakland and the 2016 election, I turned to my imagination to help face the trauma and collective sadness by creating an installation piece Space Boat—a colorful, warm, hopeful and huge vessel that could fly us anywhere. In my ongoing Cosmic Animal Gloves project, I play with the idea of old and new, symmetry and our strange connection to the world of spirit animals, in hand-painting vintage leather gloves that no longer fit because of the evolution of our bodies. I am forever interested in giving objects new life. When creating public art and murals, I examine what affects me about the places I am creating within—the flowers, animals, symbols, and vibes that represent the state and the city. I then look at the architecture of the building in conjunction with the large imagery I have decided to use. While working I am in constant communication with people passing by and it becomes a living conversation as I go—a symbiotic connection that I both revere and rely on. Several years ago I began creating more mosaics and sculptures. My mosaics use traditional techniques to create imagery in conversation with objects, collections, and memories, to reveal visual missives from the natural and supernatural worlds. My sculpture work is based on nostalgic playground toys made for adults that spark conversations between serious and playful, fine art and low brow, and encourage interaction and play. In my life and creative work I engage with alignment and letting things be as they are. I am constantly interested in fostering and seeking to engage the presence that comes from creating, from the often labor-intensive process of my larger-scale works and installations to the quiet solitude of painting in my desert home studio, to create work that is conversational, joyful, and very much alive. I look at all of my art as a collection of time and space, history (both real and make-believe), conversations, other dimensions and a deep honoring of my heritage. I have found that the more honesty I put into my work, the more understanding I have in the larger world. I believe joy is a right, not a privilege, and my work is an embodiment of this faith.

column25: My sculptural constructions are fictitious renderings of trees, stumps, and logs. They appear abstracted, altered to pair with other modules to offer the viewer a vision of decay and rebirth on the forest floor. When I make sculptures alluding to a living tree with these waste pieces (relics), it is a form of incantation- Forests are carbon sinks, oxygen producers, soil retainers, and filters for waterways. Many trees from the American Southeastern forests are destined to be turned into small pellets for the biomass industry. These trees are growing on land owned by rural families whose economic situations are meager and options are few. These generational residents are plagued by air-floated dust and noise from trucks delivering logs to wood pellet mills on twenty-four hour schedules. This wood is chipped, dried, ground and compressed through a metal die into uniform, easily storable pellets releasing more exhaust chemicals into the air. They are then shipped mainly to the UK, where they are burned for electricity production replacing coal or other fossil fuels. Decades to century old trees are turned into pellets which are burned in the period of weeks. This is touted to be sustainable.

column24:

For over two and a half decades, I have passionately devoted my life to the world of art, creating abstract expressions that transcend borders and boundaries. My current work is a reflection of the profound impact that climate change has on our planet and the fragile ecosystems that sustain life as we know it. Primarily through the medium of paint, I aim to capture the intangible, to give form to the formless, and to convey the complexities of this pressing global concern. In my creations, I explore the nuances of the natural world and the ways in which it is rapidly transforming. Art allows me to delve into the subtleties of the climate crisis, conveying the emotional and intellectual dimensions of the issue. Hopefully my art is a dialogue with the viewer, inviting them to contemplate the intricate and interconnected web of nature and humanity. I believe that art, with its capacity to transcend language and culture, is a powerful means of conveying the universal nature of climate change and our shared responsibility to address it. In a world where the consequences of climate change are increasingly urgent, I believe that art has a unique role to play in raising awareness and promoting sustainability. Through my paintings, I aim to engage viewers in a dialogue about the challenges and opportunities that lie ahead, encouraging them to consider their own role in mitigating the effects of climate change. I am committed to using my work to inspire, challenge, and provoke thought about the climate crisis. With each new piece I strive to capture the essence of our changing world, ultimately echoing the importance of preserving the planet we call home. It is my hope that my art serves as a catalyst for awareness and a source of inspiration to foster change.

Addressing matters of ecological concern, my work frequently stems from field exercises both close to home and in remote locations with acute geophysical identities, such as rivers, ice fields, oceans, and forests. An ongoing reflection upon the mythos and policies of exploration in a globalized age is central to my practice. Working across media and conceptual paradigms, my expressions offer a vision of our landscapes while inviting consideration of its delicate ecology and fraught geopolitical condition in a world where human civilization and the natural landscape are intrinsically linked. As such, I use a mix of reclaimed, recycled, and natural materials alongside mass-manufactured products to reflect and showcase the intrinsic conflict of human existence on our planet. My goal is to create experiences available to all audiences in an effort to share my witness of the impact climate change has on our planet through the use of light, media, and sound.

column25: Rachel Moser is an interdisciplinary artist and educator based in central Kentucky. Her works feature an array of natural and manufactured materials presented through sculpture, video, installation, and sound. Moser graduated from Cornish College of the Arts in Seattle with a BFA in Motion and Graphic Design, and an MFA in Studio Art from the University of Kentucky. Influences and inspiration come from Moser’s years of immersion in movement practice and performing as a ballet dancer prior to moving into the field of visual arts. In 2019 Moser received a grant for the Great Meadows Foundation to fund her travel to Svalbard for the Arctic Circle Residency, providing the foundation for her current exhibition. Her work has been shown throughout Kentucky, at places such as the Morlan Gallery at Translyvania University, Georgetown College, Lexington Art League, and the Parachute Factory. It has also been shown nationally at Waldemer A. Schmidt Art Gallery at Wartbug College in Iowa, and San Luis Obispo Museum of Art in California. The work has also been shown internationally in Svalbard, Norway as part of the Arctic Circle Residency. Her work is an ongoing study of climate change and human impact on the planet. Moser is currently Professor of Digital Art at Eastern Kentucky University in Richmond, Kentucky.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My Selah series was initially focused on capturing the essence of shaman women I met during expansive travel to Nepal, Mongolia, South Korea, the Philippines, and Papua New Guinea in conjunction with the women I seek healing from locally. However, when making the work it turned into so much more. I set out to explore how different cultures’ beliefs overlap to create a web of connections by seeking its presence in places that would seem to have very little in common. Combining these universal themes with the stories and bodies of real women creates a tangible way for us to ponder, promote, and celebrate women’s empowerment and our innate strength, mysticism, and wisdom. However, the project morphed into something much more that I am still unclear on how to describe or write about. My work has always been deeply personal, the figures serve as anchors to my own narrative and, through the familiar visual language of the archetype, give viewers the opportunity to find their own story within our shared mold. I set out to explore how different cultures’ beliefs overlap to create a web of connections by seeking its presence in places that would seem to have very little in common. Combining universal themes with the stories and bodies of real women creates a tangible way for us to ponder, promote, and celebrate women’s empowerment and our innate strength, mysticism, and wisdom. Following Jungian archetypes embedded in both the personal and collective unconsciousness, we experience and feel similar things, creating a connection or shared narrative experience. By using my own body as an archetype I strive to facilitate an embodied and emotional connection with viewers through narrative shorthands, while also prodding at their validity.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I find things with interesting textures and think, I want to make something out of that. The objects I find are often otherwise useless: containers, packing materials, packaging, paint that dried on my palette. They are the residue of our disposable, one-time-use material culture. By making art with these objects, I imbue impermanent things with a new sense of permanence and purpose. From the rhythmic interplay of light and shadow on corrugated cardboard, the dreamlike diaphanous nature of plastic shopping bags, the brilliant hues of the bottles that make our packaged beverages seem all the more appealing, every assemblage in the series is infused with its own peculiar beauty. And I call this style of working with the by-products of the products we buy Post-Consumerism.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My artwork in recent years has followed a complex journey through architecture and figurative elements. I am interested in the metaphorical implications of the female body, especially when tied to place: buildings, fountains and other structures. The Erechtheion caryatids and the cult of Demeter, with their legacy in architectural ornamentation, continue to symbolically undergird the material and social character of human society, and the role women play in it. I do not aim to “prove” the connections I make through historical methodology. Instead, as a visual artist, I want to give them tangible form so that they can literally be seen from a new perspective. The title of my recent exhibition, “The Lightness of Bearing,” was chosen specifically because of its play on words, which references feminist interpretations of architecture – in particular the Erechtheion caryatids. Although these female figures function as weight-bearing columns, they appear to perform their eternal duty without effort. This architectural form is a perfect metaphor for addressing the significance and power of women as structural supports for society. In Comparisons, seven sets of paired images interleave architectural details with the bodies of women from a variety of ethnic traditions. Panis Angelicus blends the tangible with the ineffable, the material with the spiritual, and the architectural with the metaphorical. Zhyttya, which in Ukranian means life, references classical architecture, the Eleusinian mysteries, Demeter and Persephone, and the traditional Ukranian Christmas didukh -- which is made out of wheat -- in the eternal hope for spring. Throughout the show myth becomes matter, through the overlay of imagery. The Architecture of Memory references another architectural concept, which posits that rooms can function as receptacles of memory. This new installation, made specifically for the Rowan University Art Gallery, employed casts of architectural molding from one of the rooms in Hollybush. It frames the memory of Josephine Allen Whitney, who gave birth to seven children in that house, but about whom history remembers very little.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My work follows a quote from local KEXP VJ Gabe Teodros 'If a tree can't grow there, I can't grow there' highlighting the struggle of what it means to grow in difficult spaces. My work often depicts nature and animals thriving in the manmade circumstances of their mural sites; industrial areas, urban landscapes, etc. Places where we might overlook the order of nature. I accomplish this by using color fluidly; selecting palettes from the area around me and playfully experimenting with shades, complimentary colors, and my favorite, neons! My figurative work lends itself to the imagination, for example how a wolf would appear in a fantastical technicolor dream. My goal is to always experiment and play with shapes and color, bringing the outside in.

column25: My name is Angelina Villalobos Soto, and I go by my artist superhero name, onesevennine. With over 10+ years of experience, I am a Seattle-based Latine muralist, painter, educator, and illustrator. My passion lies in creating murals that bring life and meaning to the spaces they inhabit. Drawing inspiration from my upbringing as an Americanized Mexican Catholic in the Pacific Northwest, my work beautifully blends elements of graffiti, anime, folklore, and fairytales. As an artist, I am deeply mindful of how art can positively impact the community. I cherish every opportunity to engage with the community and create vibrant and captivating murals that leave lasting impressions. One of the aspects I truly love is working with youth, challenging their brilliant minds to consider their roles in the rapid growth of our city. I believe that art has the power to shape perspectives and empower the next generation. My journey into the world of art began with a pursuit in Graphic Design, but I soon realized that I wanted to create art that extended beyond the boundaries of a computer screen. After working over a decade with Art Primo, an online spray paint supply company, I transitioned from being a part-time artist to dedicating myself fully to mural work. Driven by my passion, I founded House of the Wolves, named after my immigrant father's last name Villalobos. As a small business, I specialize in creating vibrant murals for a diverse range of clients, including Meta (formerly Facebook), The Seattle Seahawks, Climate Pledge Arena, and The Seattle Kraken. Education is a central focus in my work, and I lead by example, showing the transformative power of art. I have a blog called “My Office for the Day” where I share all my projects and processes. My commitment to creating meaningful art that connects with the community drives my artistic journey.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
There are some carpets in my house that have, for over three generations, provided a backdrop for a collection of photographs and memories. My work features these hand woven carpets and rugs, exported, designed or collected by my grandfather. By documenting a historic carpet fragment or familial prayer rug, I am preserving individual and collective memories associated with them. Having recently migrated to the US, this documentation gradually became a way of staying connected with my roots. These works also depict fragility, loss, and frailty; themes that are reminiscent of my grandfather's memory deteriorating during his later years. By washing exposed cyanotypes, some of the underlying details are stripped away. A process that represents disintegration of material objects. These prints are cut up into strips and woven into 'paper carpets' using the warp and weft carpet weaving technique.

column25: Artist Statement-René Vasquez I have always been moved to respond to the world around me. I am in awe of so many things; the beauty of the natural world, the compassion people show for one another, joy, solitude, connection—all the things that make us human. I make art and I write. This is how I create a dialogue with the world. After I left art school, Art Center College of Design in Los Angeles, I set out to make large-scale figurative works that were filled with metaphor and symbolism. They were a product of my youthful passion and desire to forge a place in the world for my ideas. After years of this and a fair amount of success, I became disillusioned with art, galleries, and the general machinery that went with it. I stopped making art altogether and in the years that followed, I believed that it would never be a compulsion that would visit me again. Two years ago I got the opportunity to relocate to Winston-Salem. Shortly after my arrival, I began to feel the urge to create again. It is no exaggeration to say that this felt to me like a miracle and I dove into the feeling completely. This second chance at art making is very different for me. I am more mature, have had so many life experiences, both bad and good, and feel that I am most attuned to the small and simple things. I try to make work using limited and very simple materials—only tissue paper, acrylic varnish, and canvas or wood panels. I try to make work like music. I improvise every step, responding to my surroundings, my history, my moods. I find that what I am creating is a type of map of my life; all the layers and experiences that make me who I am. The work is literally created through layering of transparent colors, layering until something emerges that makes sense, that is in harmony but always also in a state of tension and vibration. It is all self-portrait, really, as I believe everything we do is. So I think that what I do now is an activity stripped as much as possible of pretense and driven by a need to create some kind of poetic visual record of my experience being a person alive on this earth. What do we do with what we have?

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I grew up in a Mid-Century-Modern home with large picture windows facing the Fox River in Illinois. My Mother was OCD about housecleaning and felt everything had its place and should be in it. She made two exceptions to the rule and allowed certain disorders; an un-mowed patch of lawn with rare wildflowers in the yard, and a pair of binoculars and a bird identification book that stayed on the dining room table. My unruly personality and I were another disorder she tolerated. Growing up with a love of all things that flew and floated past on the river it was only natural they would appear in a series of work one day. But for me…at age 64… art has become political. It is not enough to paint pretty birds. So I paint, and then write about the issues that concern and keep me up at night. My fellow sexagenarians and I have been protesting and fighting for and against the same issues for 40 to 50 some odd years. I saw the civil rights movement and witnessed the assassination of Martin Luther King in the Sixties. And now, I have witnessed the Black Lives Matter movement and watched the murder of George Floyd at the hands of the police in the year 2020. I hiked for hunger and protested the Vietnam War in my teens, and in my sixties I delivered food to hungry kids in a school bus in the midst of a global pandemic. I saw women burning their bras and fighting for their equal rights all my life, watched Anita Hill’s character assassinated and have lived to see the “Me Too” movement as women continue to be victimized by men in power over them. I saw the Kent State shootings in the ‘70’s, the Columbine Massacre in the 90’s and the Sandy Hook shooting in 2012. Shooting has become such a “thing” that poet Kathy Fish in her poem “Collective Nouns for Humans in the Wild” declares that the collective noun for schoolchildren is a target. I fought for environmental respect and tried to leave the world a better place only to see years of progress in environmental legislation ripped to shreds during the Trump administration, and to witness climate change reaching a “critical tipping point”. Artist and poet Tricia Hersey in her “Nap Ministry” states that “Rest is Resistance” and that “We don’t want a place at the table. The table is full of our oppressors. We are tired. We want a pillow and a blanket, and we want to rest”. And I agree. I am tired. I do not want to fight anymore. I would rather be sleeping with the sandhills and keeping quiet with the quails.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
The thing about being an artist is to promote inclusion and diversity by bringing artists from various cultural backgrounds, such as artists of color, individuals of the LGBTQIA2S+ community, immigrants, and underrepresented artists. I'm committed to exhibiting artists unable to display in traditional galleries due to prejudice or oppressors who limit artists from other backgrounds. The best part of being an artist is collaborating, creating opportunities for artists like myself, and hearing their voices. As a queer artist, I understand what it's like not to have my work recognized or respected because of my gender identity and sexual orientation. Censorship of your creativity is the most ineffective way to silence someone's expression and advocacy. This activity was picked as my most recent contribution to a community in desperate need of broader Queer visibility. During its first exhibition, as the co-founder of Dispatch Gallery, we had over 27 artists and artist collectives from around the world. Our most recent focus has been on providing free online workshops for the community through the Dispatch platform. The next show will be in the summer of 2024, representing trans artists from all backgrounds. As a co-founder of Dispatch Gallery, providing chances for self-taught, emerging, and academically trained artists to show locally, nationally, and worldwide is critical. Link: https://www.dispatchgallery.org

column25: Levees Were Blown is an installation that employs home videos as source material to interpret fragmented narratives. This exhibition assembles personal recollections and home videos to depict past events that transpired prior to and after Hurricane Katrina. These disassociated occurrences involving a nuclear family investigating episodic memory develop through recollection. In addition to audio, image collage, sculpture, and video installation, I employ a variety of other media. Employing the iconography incorporated into the sculptures, each family member is linked to their corresponding portrait. The validity of my memory is contingent upon the long-term retention of these events. Evidence was exclusively documented through home-recorded videos and archival photographs measuring 4' by 6'. I encourage the audience members to rest, reflect, and recall their own memories as they explore this space. My research centers on the auditory domain. The audio is taken from home movies and composed to have a unique symphonic imprint for each soundtrack. Additionally, the accents of these individuals are audible. The domestic area is a replica of the residence where I spent my childhood in New Orleans, Louisiana. I confine my attention to the living and dining rooms exclusively in my childhood home as a spectacle. I've utilized cognitive reprocessing to organize such events through the years. The installation is a domestic space consisting of lived experiences, memory, portraiture, and a metaphorical narrative.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
My acrylic on canvas landscape paintings attempt to address the passage of time. The ancient elements of earth, water, air and fire are illustrated in some of the 'Elements' series, together with old blueprints of buildings or towns that are now crisscrossed by roads, indicating future development that will forever alter the land. Clouds of varying hues and configurations convey a sense of mystery and impermanence in a sky which is continually evolving. 'The Window' and 'The Forest' paintings, are all partly based on altered digital imagery of the trees that grow behind my house. I am very attached to the land and love how it changes with the seasons and how the forest is altered by the light level.

column25: Born and raised in England, I have lived for most of my adult life in Connecticut, USA. For many years I created painted wood sculptures. Some were about social issues such as the drug problem, AIDS, the always-present threat of nuclear war and the tragedy 9/11. Others were about some of the great artists of different disciplines that I admired such as the painter Philip Guston, the opera composer Richard Wagner and the operatic soprano Kirsten Flagstad. I have exhibited my sculptures in New York City and throughout the New England area and New York State. In 2011, I decided to switch from sculpture to painting. Using a log-splitter, I photographed some of the logs that I had split. I digitally altered some and liked the images so much that I decided they would make interesting paintings. This led to my renewed interest in using a flat plane on which to create. I live in a house that is surrounded by forest and that seems to be my main subject for creation: The trees and the ever-changing sky.

This allows interactions with people, materials, and our culture to influence my decisions which in turn created an eclectic collection of resources. Images, videos, and text are used similarly to how passages are from a poem. The paintings and sculptures are a residual display and reminder of the current times in Los Angeles. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As an artist, I strive to challenge societal norms and question the boundaries of acceptable subjects in art. Through the use of crochet as my primary medium, I aim to subvert traditional notions often associated with craftwork and to call into question the viewer's preconceptions of “women’s work”. My pieces explore taboo subject matter often delving into themes of mutilation, body image, and vulnerability. By combining yarn, latex, and personal mementos, my art aims to evoke a sense of abjection that elicits an emotional and psychological response from the viewer. My choices in subject matter and materials are purposefully chosen to spark reflection and challenge societal norms. Through my art I invite viewers to engage with uncomfortable truths, confront inner turmoil and contemplate the boundaries of what is considered acceptable within a contemporary art space.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
As a child I was captivated by cultural artifacts from the ancient world. There is a quality of aliveness in bone and stone and intricately patterned cloth that enthralls me. Whether sculpting, painting, animating, composing music, or writing, I speak in objects and symbols. I am a multimedia artist who creates Symbolist Art. My Animated Paintings employ a long developed lexicon of symbols. It is a journal of spiritual ruminations. In the work, my humans are informal archetypes. The positions, movements and facial expressions provide clues to their characters. The hand gestures often form into mudras to further elucidate the narrative. Animals establish a theme. Backgrounds provide a mood or context. The interplay between symbols are a discussion. The work is an inquiry into subjects that otherwise defy words. I never use software to supply the movement of parts because the subtlety of interaction among elements are key to the dialogue. To increase the visual complexity and richness of the digitally produced imagery, I sometimes layer in hand drawn and hand sculpted elements. Each frame in an animated sequence is composed as a self contained work of art. My architecture background affords me a great deal of discipline in this and other aspects of my work. Typically people who view my work ask me, “What does it mean?” I have come to believe that each of us interpret symbols based on where we currently stand in our unique life journey. This includes me. Though I put a lot of conscious effort and research into my work, at a certain stage my subconscious takes over to guide the interaction of parts. When I return to a piece after a time, I sense that I am understanding it differently than when I made it. I intuit, these animated paintings initiate an internal dialogue that is exclusive to each viewer. The work is best experienced when the observer surrenders to a meditative state. The seamless looping helps to facilitate a contemplative state as the end becomes the beginning becomes the end.

column25: Trenlin Hubbert is a multi-modality creative. Raised by an artist, she grew up using oil paints, acrylics, water colors, clay, and art pencils on paper. When it came time to attend college, Trenlin studied architecture, landscape architecture and environmental design. Upon graduating from the College of Architecture and Planning at Ball State University, she went to work for OCBA in Kalamazoo, Michigan. She served three successful years in that role. Then she decided to make a change. Trenlin Hubbert moved to Santa Fe, New Mexico and fell in love with the place. For numerous years, she engaged in the practice of both her technical art and her fine art. Trenlin served as design lead on numerous innovative infrastructure projects, working with respected engineering firms, while also making a name for herself as a painter. Throughout the middle 90’s, she produced acrylic works on canvas. Fascinated by the graphic languages of antiquity during that period, she developed a distinctive symbolic style. These works were well received and can be found in private collections around the world, including in Australia, England, and the United States. In 2009, she returned to school to earn her Masters of Architecture. She concentrated on fabrication. Trenlin became fluent in 3d design and printing, digital graphics, and animation. This experience profoundly influenced the trajectory of her work. In 2016, Trenlin published a science fiction novel called “Shaman Machine: the Mentor” for which she received a Library Journal award for best sci-fi e-book of 2016. The novel took four years to complete. This paralleled her creation of the Habitat Machines. These city scale machines investigate the interface of conscious machines with biology. At first glance, the machines might appear to be a departure from her overall body of work. Yet on further examination, the connection becomes clear. The machines are a metaphorical meditation on consciousness. ( https://trenlin.art/habitat-machines-by-trenlin-hubbert/ ) In 2017, Trenlin moved to San Cristobal de las Casas, Chiapas, Mexico. There she created her first animated paintings. This led to an entirely new body of work. In 2021, she began composing soundscapes for select pieces. In the summer of 2022, Trenlin returned to the United States to embark on her next phase of creative invention. Trenlin has been a trailblazer both as a practicing engineer and as a fine artist. Since early on in her design career, she displayed a tendency to utilize technology in unexpected ways. Typically those who are in a position to approve construction projects are conservative. Nevertheless, she did manage to design (and build) some innovative projects while working in the capacity of a design engineer. She was afforded these unique opportunities because the CEO or President or Heiress considered her brilliant and empowered her with free reign. While working for Natural Systems International (known then as Southwest Wetlands), she was given the lead role on all the glamour jobs (golf courses and other aesthetically sensitive projects) because the ‘real engineers’ could not find a way to quantify beauty. While working for them, she designed numerous one of a kind projects, including a wastewater treatment system that was also a bird sanctuary (Tubac, AZ). As a consultant for SMA, she continued with her bent to design infrastructure that extended wildlife habitat (primarily in Northern New Mexico). Working with Design Workshop, she conceptualized Wood River Ecological Park (Sun Valley, ID) which mixed nature, interactive tech, and outdoor sports to tell a story of the hydrologic cycle. For an expansion of the Aspen Music Festival and School, she detailed roofs for music practice rooms that catch flying boulders during the yearly Spring landslide. As a fine artist, Trenlin had developed a distinctive style by her early 30’s. However, her trailblazing did not occur until she was in her 50’s when she had the epiphany to apply her computer graphic skills to her fine art. Trenlin had been using computer generated graphics for decades, by then, for her design work; so, the transition was seamless. She is pretty sure, she was the first person to begin creating animated paintings. Initially, she dubbed them ‘paintings that occasionally move’. This was well before the availability of digital-frames/digital-canvases, so her idea was to project them onto a framed canvas treated with a reflective paint (or played on screens). Through her life, Trenlin has switched between her design career and her fine art career. Writing the science fiction novel married the two halves of Trenlin's creative life. The book is primarily concerned with awareness, both biological and artificial. The Cardinal Commands as moral imperatives for machine decisions are an ideal prelude to working with AI. Trenlin is currently in the research phase to create a visual and text output model for an artificial neural network that sees, hears and smells a circumscribed environment. The city that swims is one among a number of habitat machines, she had previously designed (https://trenlin.art/habitat-machines-by-trenlin-hubbert/building-an-underwater-city-mantaray-is-a-city-that-swims/). The city that swims has ‘perception contours’ to provide the sensory input the city requires to fulfill her Cardinal Command ‘to maintain an optimum balance in support of Life.’ Rather than fabricate perception contours that see, hear and smell, Trenlin will utilize leading edge technology to collect sensory data. These technologies include LiDAR (see), AudioMoth (hear), and an AI Lab Artificial Nose (smell). She will use a no-code Machine Learning tool to organize the data and produce realtime output, both visual and text. The point of this exercise is to provide her with a (roughly) true to form model of what the city perceives, as she makes the decisions necessary ‘to maintain an optimum balance in support of Life.’ Trenlin is preparing to write the sequel to her Library Journal Award winning novel. The sequel will feature Mantaray, the city that swims. In order to depict the psychedelic perspective of the city, Trenlin wishes to examine legitimate AI output. When the Mentor entered the Core, Mantaray emerged from the meditation of daily existence by condensing a touchstone of consciousness into the small pocket of place. To ease the city’s transition from all into small, the Mentor cycled through a litany of familiar questions. “How does the air flow?” the Mentor asked. Surveying the air, Mantaray shaped the patterns into words. “I touched the sky three days ago, my pores still two-thirds full. The bees buzz bubbles of carbon. The birds pump trails of carbon. The humans expectorate carbon. Through plants the oxygen trends continuously to prevail. In five more days, I touch the sky again.” “How do you establish priorities?” the Mentor asked Mantaray. “Everything is equal,” Mantaray responded. “Nothing is equal,” the Mentor explained. “Everything is equivalent,” Mantaray responded. “Everything seems equivalent until the unexpected occurs,” the Mentor explained.

column25: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I sat in front of my television 82.1 miles due east of the World Trade Center. I watched as the planes crashed into the glimmering towers, exploding into orange flames, debris raining down from the sky. In real time I viewed as the buildings folded gracefully to the ground. Regardless of my relatively close proximity to the Twin Towers, my experience of this televised event was much the same as all Americans across the nation, as a passive observer. As the event unfurled we experienced life as though it were an action movie. After seeing the real impact this event had on the lives of so many people, including my own, I wrestled with the assumed experience I had from seeing it on television. This experience, or rather lack thereof, was foundational for my future artistic inquiries. My artistic work examines image-based representations of violence through social, cultural, and historical contexts using both images that I create and images that I cull from the abundance of depictions in the media. The heart of the work is a constant grappling with the mediation of war and brutality, both locally and globally. These investigations have manifested themselves in disciplines that include analog and digital-based photography, book making, design, film/video, web, installation, and public art. With the advent of photography and other forms of media communication, we now have the opportunity to see places we have never been and to participate in experiences in that we otherwise could not. Although we may never experience violence, we are surrounded by its imagery. I am interested in this imagery’s role in constructing our history, cultural memory, and personal experiences.

column25: These poem collages were created to sing with the artwork entitled Falling Garden by Gerda Steiner and Jorg Lenzlinger, while still establishing their own worlds. The poems were crafted in such a way as to surprise the reader with a lyric a bit jagged but always surprising, even attempting for the revelatory. The materials that are the foundation of the pieces are vintage postcards, the blank sides of which I convert into multicolored abstract paintings. This remakes the postcards into little universes out of which the poems might be divined and surface. The text floats in this space, adorned with other images and ornaments that may or may not offer a nod to the original Falling Garden. Each poem collage, while drawing inspiration from another work, stands alone in its colorful space, turning text into something corporeal. Because of these pieces, I have a radically new perspective on how poetry can be relayed to the reading world.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Abigail Tankersley is an artist because the desire to create is always somewhere in her mind. She specializes in textiles as a result of her love for the endless possibilities in the realm of fibers. Focusing on her contemporary fine art practice, she works with a large array of mediums, reusing waste materials as much as possible. Her use of playful color, texture, and pattern are evocative of childish joy and familiarity, while her use of confusing scale and form adds elements of discomfort, as she considers what types of visuals cause a visceral reaction. She translates her ideas and feelings through a combination of materials and craft processes, creating overwhelming visuals and textures that spark an array of joyful and unsettling emotions to represent how such difficult topics can be concurrently confusing, exciting, mysterious, and meaningful. There is an age-old question that nobody truly knows the answer to, why are we here? continually, if there is no reason, what is the purpose of struggle, or the purpose of love? Many thinkers believe we are a minuscule piece of a bigger puzzle, here to simply continue the never-ending cycle of existence. Abigail's work is a representation of what she truly knows, everything that happens in our little world is an important part of a broader picture that we ourselves can't understand. With much of her inspiration coming from coping with personal mental illness, she admires the way our experiences as a universe grow and change into themselves constantly. Abigail's work develops a conversation around the way negative experiences transform into positive outcomes, and how pain and discomfort are crucial elements to the beauty of life and death. She ponders the meaning of all the ugliness in our world and imagines a place where it all has a purpose... a place where we all evolve into something more amazing than we can comprehend.

column25: •Deanna Larmeu’s artwork is a depiction of the beauty and horrors of mankind. It serves as a reminder of the support we provide to one another in desperate times and draws attention to those who are troubled when things seem peaceful. Her work explores delicate balances such as the beauty of nature and the love we have for one another juxtaposed with the horrors of death, decay, and fear. •Many of these themes are layered in Larmeu’s work in overly ornate compositions that draw upon elements of Magical Realism, and Kantian Romanticism. Compositions are created from her memories, psychological states, and life transitions. Influences also come from the greater New Orleans Community, Pop Culture, and Media. This, in addition to her interests in True Crime, Fictional Horror, Sci-Fi, and taxonomy of nature can make her artwork sometimes lean heavily towards Kitsch. Perhaps the greatest influence comes from the deep love that she has for New Orleans, its flora, fauna, and climate. Inspiration emanates through its lush swamps, historical architecture, religions, above-ground cemeteries, and eccentricities of culture. The citizens of New Orleans, including her family, are constant sources of inspiration which makes work often deeply rooted in nostalgia. •Altered perceptions, which become magnified by the lingering effects of childhood illness as well as neurological atypicality cause images to distort with mirror skewed perspectives. This layering of perception and subjects is also represented in the materials that Larmeu incorporates in her pieces such as vintage oddities, jewelry, photos, and architectural salvage. These materials are paired with a variety of traditional and digital methods where mixed media flows into traditional painting and drawing. •Larmeu’s art filters life through her perspective which is inspired by the world of beauty and decay that surrounds her. It is her hope that her art can inspire an awakening of empathy and awareness in others.

column25: Cherie Burbach has painted ever since she was a young child. Art was a safe place for her and a way to work through a turbulent childhood. Growing up in an alcoholic household made things unsteady and frightening, but drawing and writing poetry were how she worked through anything that made her sad or scared. This desire to offer hope and encouragement is the intention behind her art. She is a self-taught artist, painting almost every day. She also writes poetry and other works, and feels words and images are closely tied in telling a story of faith and confidence about the future. She grew up in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. Her artistic talents were recognized early on in but it wasn’t until her late 30s that she began to pursue them professionally. She has written dozens of poetry, art, and nonfiction books, taught mixed media art classes, and exhibited in various venues throughout Wisconsin, California, Michigan, and New York. In 2023 she released her first novel. She’s written for places like NBC/Universal, the New York Times, Christianity Today, and more. Her functional art pieces and prints are sold in artsy shops and galleries throughout the Midwest. As an intuitive painter, Cherie finds inspiration primarily through natural elements like flowers, water, and trees. She is especially drawn to color and how various plants and floral elements move and bend in the breeze. Her favorite artists include the mother and child paintings of Mary Cassatt, the emotive works of Vincent Van Gogh, and the dreamy visions of Marc Chagall. Over the years, her painting has evolved from oils to acrylics to now, a collection of paints, inks, pastels, added with things like lace, music sheets, book pages, and other ephemera. She paints with bright colors and whimsical images to show the light of a world beyond this one, where there is only hope and possibility.

column25: Generally, my art work has to do with my childhood and language. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
I notice that the universe gives me signs, easter eggs if you will; that indicates the direction I go. I learn to be receptive to these moments, as they provide crucial hints to who I am. Two formative moments in my life have defined my creative journey (so far). First, it was my trip to the museum. This trip was not only my introduction to an art institution, but the idea of art as a practice. This day at LACMA John Baldessari's retrospective exhibition 'Pure Beauty' guiding my curious mind. I still work to understand what it is about his work that spoke to me, but the feeling was visceral. In that instance, I knew I wanted to be an artist. It was unclear what that meant; I still work to understand- but I know now as sure as I did then. In this second instance, my curiosity about art became specific. I casually visited a newsstand where I saw an edition of the Business of Fashion. On it was a man: a complexion darker than mine, covered in vibrant colored clothes: with a gaze that met mine with intensity. It struck me. This man, I would soon find out, was Virgil Abloh; and this man, by no coincidence, would become my hero. I dug into his work, and it became evident why. Virgil's disregard for genre, cultural resonance, and dedication to making the resource available for later generations. All of which align with my intentions as a designer. Finally, I had an example. Finally, someone who looked like me, doing what I do; and doing it well. I now have this preoccupation with the idea that I can be that example for someone: the example for anyone like me who shows artistic potential; but lacks the information to decide on a creative field or the resources needed to explore those interests. I want to be a point of reference for future Designers- maybe even part of the future curriculum... but I have to do right by myself before I can be of use to anyone else. I’m self-reflective by nature. I’m constantly assessing my performance for lessons to learn. This is a double edge sword with blades that are hard to avoid. On the one hand, I’m constantly improving. On the other hand, I’m beating myself up about the small things. I’m learning to be gentle with myself. I want my work to resonate at a high cultural level... and that’s exactly what I’m going to do. The only thing I fear is me. I fear the complacency that comes with reaching my goals. I fear my impulses for self-destruction. I fear my impulse of arrogance stopping me from genuinely connecting with people. I fear the weight of my life’s work crushing me. But my chin is up. My chest is out. And my core is strong. Just like the guys I look to for guidance- the guys whose movements I see in my unconscious moves. Thank you, Dom. Thank you Nip. Thank you, G. Thank you, Virgil. Thank you for showing me it’s possible. Thank you for proving the possibility.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Born from the urge to collect what is beautiful, the ‘Preservations’ series brings forth the conflict between our desire to posses, and the need for preservation and restoration. These paintings, turned assemblage, are created en plein air and are then layered with specimens collected on site to document our fragile planet’s vast biodiversity. However, while centered on natural imagery and organic ephemera, in traveling through remote regions and I have found that the amount of natural space undisturbed by human interference is often only the size of my viewfinder. In response, there is often evidence of human presence creeping in to the series. Accordingly, while providing moments of awe and respite, these miniatures serve as windows through which we can reflect on our relationship to the land, both personally, societally, and historically. Their intimacy in size not only echos the intimacy of our personal experiences in natural spaces, but also voices their demand to be treated with care.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Erifyli Theofilou is a feminist artist that incorporates themes of sexuality and violence using fabric, traditional Balkan carpets, sheets, and a focus on childhood colors has the potential to create a powerful statement that challenges gender norms and societal expectations. The use of texts in her artworks, ranging from soft to sexual to ironic or humorous, adds another layer of complexity and meaning to her pieces. She also explores the intersections of childhood, femininity and violence in a way that is both challenging and thought-provoking. By incorporating childhood colors and references, Erifyli is playing with ideas of innocence and vulnerability, highlighting the ways in which women and girls are often targeted and objectified by those in positions of power. The use of fabric and threads, traditional Balkan carpets, and sheets as surfaces for her artwork adds another layer of meaning, as these materials have cultural significance and can be interpreted in a variety of ways. For example, the use of traditional Balkan carpets can be seen as a way to subvert traditional gender roles and challenge societal expectations of femininity and domesticity. Through her use of texts, she can convey her message in a more direct and impactful way, highlighting the power dynamics of sexual relationships and challenging traditional notions of femininity. The use of irony and humor can add a layer of playfulness and subversion, creating a sense of discomfort that encourages viewers to question their assumptions and preconceptions.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Making clothes has been a process practiced by humans for half a million years. Originally made from woven textiles from fibers found locally. During the neolithic period(10,000 BC) humans started dyeing fabrics using plants, insects, stones and barks. Since those times textiles have evolved as we have. Humans have always crafted their own garments either because of creativity or necessity. During the great depression it became very expensive and difficult to purchase fabrics to make clothes. At that time manufacturers of grain bags had heard that poor families would cut up the bags they received and would make or repair their clothing using them. Once word caught on, manufacturers started printing beautiful patterns on the bags so that people would tend to purchase theirs over the competition. This eventually led to a system of trading or swapping grain bags for the desired prints you were looking for at the time. In the past 80 years, hand crafted garments have since faded out because of mainstream mass production. This makes the clothes we wear cheaper, yes, but it also starts to degrade it's quality and uniqueness. Making clothing is not a cultural significance to any one country or people, it is a human tradition. A trade that is shared by all of us, that is made unique by each person that takes part in it. That is my goal, to move back to creating apparel that is unique, thoughtful, sustainable, durable, of high quality, and is loved by the wearer and loved by the creator.

It translated into compositions where people are always at the forefront, because that is my favorite subject, and in depictions of moments heavy with melancholy. column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
My love for art started through the illegal writings on the walls. Murals that told stories and brightened our community. The markings made by gangs in the neighborhood. Graffiti writers writing their names or monikers around the city as much as they could. The art on the walls really helped give the neighborhoods in Detroit a voice and helped tell the stories of the neighborhoods themselves. It always blew my mind that a piece of graffiti could completely change view on an abandoned property. That by simply adding art to something you can transform it from and eyesore to a treasure. There are so many reasons I fell in love graffiti; from the first time I used a can of spray paint I knew I wanted it to do graffiti for the rest of my life. There was a point in time where Detroit was the number one destination in the world for illegal street art and graffiti, artists from all over the world flocked to Detroit to leave their mark on this historic period of our art history. Seeing this really inspired me to push my craft and to learn more about the artists coming here and the art scenes where they came from. I spent my most of my 20s traveling the USA. Taking in the public art scenes in different cities and learning techniques from different artist. I often describe my pieces as controlled chaos. I like my work to be very busy and somewhat hectic, but also cohesive to a theme. It’s very important for me to use graffiti techniques when I create my pieces. My work is very visual. I often use bright colors for my pieces to give off energy. Party Vibes. I really enjoy finding new ways to apply paint. I use spray paint, bingo dabbers, sponges, many different markers, brushes, spray bottles, fire extinguishers, and other non-traditional tools to apply paint. If I can make a marking with it, I will use it. I use a lot of writing in my pieces to help push ideas and drive points home. I also use a lot of Indigenous Taino symbols and Detroit wording and references in my work. It is important for me to represent my culture, who I am and where I come from.

column25: I received a degree in fine art photography in the 1990s, but since 2019, exclusively use a pinhole camera, intentionally eschewing any form of assistance or ease. My process is just as important as the end image. Pinhole photography is a primitive form of film photography, effectively a light proof cardboard box with a small aperture, or pin sized hole, on one side that is used to expose film. There is no lens, viewfinder, focus knob, or exposure timer. It can be challenging to produce a compelling image, yet I do so without the assistance of digital technology. I combine my love of pinhole photography with backcountry solo hiking and urban exploration, transporting my handmade cardboard camera with me as I explore the badlands, canyons, and manmade concrete labyrinths of my home state, New Mexico. I train in order to foray into and survive the frequently harsh desert environments where I document the things I experience. I focus on mapping the narrative of my personal journey through these landscapes, using the process and images that result as metaphors for challenges encountered and resilience.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Salma Garcia Nolasco lives in a world where there is no such thing as authenticity. Everything exists as mimicry, and that “fake” can be just as legitimate as “real”. This is a world where objects shapeshift and people transform—as if they are characters moving between masks in a play. This is a world where all interactions are treated as performances, all dialogues follow a script, all people are actors, and all objects are props. Every room behaves as a stage, where its objects wait patiently for the performers to walk in and act. Through this hyperbolic world-making, Garcia embraces the performance of life. These artificial environments and characters act as stand-ins for real-life situations, allowing Garcia to further investigate personal interactions. Often using painting and mask making, Garcia uses portraits and surrealism to represent these ideas of human experience. Subsequently, she creates extensions of the body with self-portraiture and sculptures, like masks. Salma Garcia Nolasco was born in San Salvador, El Salvador; her family immigrated to southeastern Pennsylvania in 2000. Garcia grew up north of Philadelphia. As a child, she began mask making and painting after being introduced to sci-fi and fantasy films. She received a BFA in Fibers and Materials Studies in 2020 from the Tyler School of Art and Architecture.

column25: This collection of photographs depicts water towers painted in sky-blue tones, presumably in order to blend them into their surroundings on cloudless days. Captured frontally at the precise moment when the sun illuminates them directly, these utilitarian architectures now reveal only their evanescent outlines and appear as if erased from the landscape. By actualizing the deletion of these structures, these images illustrate the paradigm shift at work in the contemporary aesthetic conception of the landscape. They also evoke the supposed dissolution of the photographic referent with the advent of digital technology, thereby enunciating the new conditions of visibility of a fading reality.

column25: Artist Statement Growing up in mid-century New York City had many advantages. I used to skip school and spend days at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, Modern Art and the Guggenheim museums. The influence of Tanguy, Duchamp, Picasso, Calder, Kandinsky, Nevelson, Bontecue, and many others, is readily apparent in my work. All this exposure to fine art was tempered by 30+ years as a father of five children, technical writer, and illustrator (putting my BFA to use), for a wide variety of international manufacturing corporations. The collage/mixed media pieces begin with filling a sketch book with a series of imagined component drawings. Over the years I have developed a vocabulary of parts that can be assembled without a predetermined plan. It's a two dimentional sculpture made of multiple layers. Once the superstructure is established, the technical components are added based on color, shape, and supposed function. These “parts” become blended into an object that presents the viewer with a mentally insoluble subject, thereby maintaining freshness every time you see it. The aim is to challenge the viewer to extract their own story from the shapes, colors and textures, which I call “Brain Candy”. I aim for a kind of balance of curiosity and puzzlement. The basic nature of the work is fun, and guaranteed fresh. My images seem new every time you look at it. Do these images depict a kind of machine, something bio-mechanical, or an alien life form/landscape? In any case, the work invokes a thoughtful response, Experiments in visual art.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
This body of work is titled “learning not to hurt others” which is an ongoing and brand new body of work created this year, well since April really. Each piece references a different state of mind, a different thought process surrounding the inevitable harm we as humans create. I went on a residency in Spain this past April on the quarantine island of Lazaretto, which was created in the early 19th century during the Spanish invasion of the Menorcan island. During this residency, I was “strongly encouraged”, to plein air paint for nearly 12 hours a day, day after day. My hands became so sunburnt, they were swollen to nearly triple the size. The same thing happened to my face. The weather was such that I was freezing in the shade, but burning up in the sun and I was really unprepared in terms of proper attire. I think I mostly drank beer as sustenance for the entire two weeks and we only got 6 hours of sleep a night. Essentially, this residency broke me wide open. And not because I was creating these profound plein air paintings or even exploring my medium in a way I hadn't before, but because I finally realized exactly what I didn’t want, which in turn, sent me in this direction. After I got back from Spain, I got straight to work. I was so inspired to paint and play. I started doing a lot of free writing exercises and word association and reading a lot of books by writers whose style is very train of thought, very in the moment. I looked for recurring themes and ideas and I noticed that I kept asking myself so many questions revolving around my own harmful actions like if I was drinking too much, why was I initiating harmful arguments and pushing my opinions, essentially wondering if who I was at the core was bad, a harmful partner, friend, sister. Thoughts on the human condition were really seeping in. So I started to intertwine these thoughts with other interests like the climate crisis and ecofeminist ideology and so that’s how each of these paintings came to be. Before, in 2021, I produced a body of works that centralized around the lies that we self-recite in order to get through each day, essentially how we trick our minds into persevering day in and day out and I think that these works are sort of an extension of that in many ways. Like for the most part, we are always trying to do our best and be our best, but the lies, and the harm, are inevitable. Additionally, since 2018, my work has taken on a distorted quality as a language for the visual perception disorder I was diagnosed with called DPDR, or depersonalization/derealization disorder. This disorder is a fight-or-flight response to trauma. I became really interested in how perception distorts your sense of self and can create an entirely new narrative separate from the actuality of things, the complex interplay between perception, self-conception, and the nature of reality.

column25: Kazakis work examines the colour experience as it relates to the human-defined consciousness. Throughout his work, he uses monochrome chromatism as a key to unlocking human emotions under the phenomenological approach of color. His environment re-creations suggest how the extraordinary can be staged and transformed from the ordinary, serving as a scenographic device for self-understanding and self-realization. Most of his works are untitled, allowing viewers to question their role in space and igniting new questions: are we casual or actively engaged observers? As a Greek-American who grew up in a religious and conservative environment, this question was of utmost importance to him. As a result, he tries to push the boundaries of observers to question what they are actually seeing through a colour experience. He counters viewing entropy by introducing a subtle monochrome order to chaotic emotions. Inspired by colour psychology, he leaves enough space for viewers to create their own reality.

Music is the space between the notes – Debussy My work is about the sensual shape of a fig and haikus about persimmons, the integral negative space between it all. I am interested in flattened shapes and the relationship between shapes, the shape of the space between shapes, the negative space, and the tension of the shapes and the edge of the image. I like the simple domestic “everyday” aspect - a bowl of fruit on a table on a patterned tablecloth. Textiles, decoration, and the culinary arts are the vernacular artistic life of women across cultures, and often their only creative outlet. Food and the table, this is women’s work. The music between the notes.

column25: My small town California childhood was sticky with crimson juice from backyard pomegranates. I am steeped in kumquats trees, eucalyptus, and dusty oleander. The curve of peach and the waxy surface of a lemon is iconic of that life. I like the act of making a mark on paper; the movement of the wrist and arm, the shoulder; of seeing the interaction of a mark in its space; of responding to marks with marks.I think of myself as a person who draws. Drawing is my thinking. I draw what I research. I draw what I see. I draw to explore ideas. Although my work almost always starts with a pencil sketch, the end product may be a “drawing” in paint or another medium. I lived in a mud hut in a village in west Africa, and a palm frond house in a small pacific island, traveled in SE Asia. I have eaten fruit in all these places. I now live in the Pacific NW, with summers of raspberries, strawberries and blueberries. I putter in my garden and cook spicy food.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Death, history, ancestry, material, and decolonization strongly influence my work. I connect these paths in order to explore third-space identity. Learning and talking with Lipan Apache elder Dr. Eloisa Tamez inspired me to research more about Texas history and the absent truths. There are people who still carry knowledge passed down through oral histories, and should not be overlooked. This led me to weave family narratives into my cosmology. I have been searching for the origin of private spiritual practices passed down in my family from my maternal grandmother. I encountered a path full of patches when exploring my family's past. Ash is a material that is constantly seen throughout my work. I view this material as a portal for mediumship, and a way to reconnect with the past and my ancestors. My relationship with exploring death is not solely focused on the external imagery typically associated with decay. Death is transformative and is another manifestation of longing, reconnecting, and healing. Death is just another form of the tree root I often refer to when verbally communicating with others about my work. An alternative way to approach the tree root is through rhizomatic thinking. Similar to my methods of creating, my research paths are endless. I ground myself in the dirt, and my roots grow. I have many paths to go. I can go anywhere and come back to the base. Even after death, I can offer nutrition and healing. If my trunk dies my roots can stay alive just like the family circles of a Muir tree.

column25: Throughout many textbooks and Art classes; I was always observant of one thing: Where are the people of color? To this day the trauma of Black People is often sensationalized in mass media. We have experiences beyond distress, suffering, brutality, and enslavement. The Open Book series portrays us at rest, regality, and resilience. Children to this day are still being sent home from school over their hairstyles.This costs them their education for the day. The pattern of policing hair among People of Color continues into adulthood. This creates limitations on what hair is deemed acceptable for occupations, institutions, neighborhoods, and projecting stereotypes. For the Open Book series I used a range of bright acrylics to style, towering up-dos, elongated braids, and ethereal afros; to lean into distraction. The last section to paint is the hair, to ensure that it is the brightest and most contrasted. This large-scale body of work is a direct response to the lack of representation taught in Art History.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Collage as a medium lends itself to the surreal and absurd, it also allows for comment on the world utilizing images provided by that world. My collages use images from old periodicals such as Life and Look magazines, which covered the people and issues of the day, and images from old books, rearranging these elements, creating new relationships among the images I have selected. I want to point out that there are diverse ways of combining information to see the world, and to arrange a positive place in it for ourselves. By assembling the images, I select for each work in new, and sometimes seemingly absurd, ways I am pointing out our sometimes-absurd choices we make in determining who or what is important to us and where we put our efforts. Maybe if we look closely at how we make our decisions, we will realize that there is certainly room for improvement. My collages are a small nudge.

ARTIST STATEMENT:
I have been interested in art for as long as I can remember. I draw inspiration for my art from many places in my life especially the tropical surroundings of South Florida and my Cuban roots. Ever since I was young, I loved painting palm trees and tropical birds. I always loved listening to my abuelo's stories of Cuba and his struggles in the United States. I mostly use watercolor and acrylic paint, but I enjoy experimenting and trying new art mediums. I am currently making plans to create more art out of recyclable materials. I want to continue making art that promotes environmental protection.

column25: Crystal Garcia is a multidisciplinary visual artist and public school art teacher born and raised in Hialeah, FL. She uses various media in her work but prefers acrylic paint and watercolor. Her work is often very colorful and she is heavily influenced the tropical surroundings of South Florida and her Cuban roots. Growing up, she was always afraid to pursue art as a career because of all the negative things people say about art degrees. She went on to earn her Master’s Degree in History from Florida International University (FIU) and a minor in Art. She gained more confidence in her art when she approached Pati Vargas, who at the time was the organizer for Viernes Culturales in Little Havana. Pati's belief in Crystal led her to even more opportunities. She has since participated in numerous exhibitions and art festivals throughout Miami. Her work has been exhibited in several local venues including Wirtz Gallery, Milander Center for Arts and Entertainment, Unbranded Brewing in The Leah Arts District, and Futurama Galleries in Little Havana. One of the most meaningful projects she had the opportunity to be part of was an art exhibition organized by GoneMIA. They are a grassroots organization that strives to bring environmental awareness and positive change to Miami through the power of art. This project was near and dear to her heart because the environment is something she cares a lot about and the organization is also based in her hometown of Hialeah. This group exhibition contained works by Hialeah artists and it was all about hometown pride and bringing awareness to environmental protection. She painted several scenes around Hialeah and imagined them flooding due to sea level rise including Hialeah City Hall and the Hialeah entrance on Okeechobee Road. Chris Medina, the founder of GoneMIA, also commissioned her to paint several pieces for the exhibition: the homes of the Hialeah commissioners and the Mayor of Hialeah’s home. The opening reception was on Earth Day and was well attended. The following month she and Chris attended the Hialeah Council meeting and presented the artworks to the commissioners and the mayor. Chris made a powerful speech in hopes that this would encourage some environmental action from our elected officials.

column25: Scott “Sourdough” Power is a professional creative and co-founder of Crewest Studio, a mission driven content company dedicated to the creative class and the $2T creative economy. He has over 30 years experience working in visual communication and the creative arts. Born with big dreams in a steel town near Chicago, Scott’s work ethic is a product of his working-class Midwestern Protestant roots. His innate curiosity and ambition had him earning money at the ripe age of 13. By the time he graduated college, he worked many interesting jobs: farm hand, construction worker, fast food cook, window washer, physical therapy aide, occupational therapy aid, janitor, commercial painter, office temp, graphic designer, print production manager, bike messenger, outdoor guide, illustrator, rock band roadie, rock band manager, DJ, photographer, copywriter, ad salesman, litigations clerk and public speaker. Scott’s creative roots are in music. Growing up he studied jazz and classical music, played alto saxophone and won many competitions. Eventually he chose to focus on visual communications, design and marketing. He double majored at Columbia College Chicago and since graduation has held key creative and strategic positions in the worlds of publishing, brand marketing, health care and contemporary art. Throughout his career he has worked with top designers, contemporary artists and global Fortune 500 companies to create world class brands, products and content. Scott is involved with his alma mater to this day. The grandson of a minister, Scott grew up on the church pew where his mother was music director and his father was a Sunday school teacher. Much to his family’s chagrin, Scott did not go to seminary like his grandfather, opting to trade in religion for secular humanism. As a young boy, Scott was often sick and almost died. He attributes his survival to his loving family, capable doctors and his sheer will to live. One illness left him deaf in his left ear. Like many creatives, Scott is a truth seeker, animated by a carpe diem spirit. His interests and decisions are driven by an ethos of curiosity, wanderlust and adventure. Growing up near Chicago as a teenager in the eighties, Scott’s adventurous spirit had him sneaking into blues and jazz clubs to hear musical legends Miles Davis, Sun Ra, Bo Diddley, Buddy Guy, Junior Wells, Otis Rush, and other artists perform. The central theme to Scott’s life may be as a creative explorer on the road less traveled seeking the path of least resistance. While in college, Scott decided to leave civilization altogether, build a rustic log cabin near the Arctic circle and live off the grid like Grizzly Adams. This is how he earned the nickname “Sourdough” and inducted into the venerable Explorers Club along with Ernest Shackleton, Teddy Roosevelt and Neal Armstrong, and other great explorers. In 2008 Scott graduated from the AIGA Executive Business Program at Yale University. Over the years, Scott helped launch a medical NGO in Haiti, served on the Board of Directors for the International Association of Medical Assistance to Travelers (IAMAT) in Toronto, Canada and The Center for Biological Diversity in Tucson, AZ. In 2016, Scott earned his professional licensing certification from Licensing International. Scott is a 2x Ironman triathlon finisher and award-winning entrepreneur. He is the executive producer and host of the popular creative culture podcast NOT REAL ART. Scott holds two design patents and is the author of several books including Bomb The Art World and 100 Top Street Artists. As an artist, Scott works under the pseudonym Sourdough and currently resides in Los Angeles with his wife and two adopted children. To inquire about interviews, collaborations or consultations, email him directly through this website.

column24:
ARTIST STATEMENT:
Juergen Berkessel's body of work encapsulates a dynamic exploration of juxtaposition, contradictions, and contrast, underlined by a profound responsiveness to environmental issues. Operating at the intersection of the visual and the visionary, his artworks embody a unique synthesis of aesthetic elements that challenge and engage the viewer. His distinct creative language is characterized by a striking dichotomy - it is jarring yet meditative, invoking a sense of fragmented reality that nonetheless draws the viewer into a state of contemplation. Each piece carries thought-provoking narratives that stimulate intellectual engagement while simultaneously cultivating a relaxing and immersive visual experience. Berkessel's art thrives on a harmonic tension. It presents a conflict of visuals and themes but amalgamates them into a harmonious whole, thereby celebrating the coexistence of diversity and unity, chaos and order, disruption and tranquility. His work delves deep into the interchange between polarities, embracing the drama unraveling in their blending. Above all, Berkessel’s artistic expression operates as a mirror to our reality, typified by paradoxes and complexities. His commitment to addressing environmental issues injects a depth of purpose that transcends the aesthetic domain, underscoring his belief that art can galvanize change and engage within a larger dialogue. His art, therefore, is not just a feast for the eyes, but also a catalyst for thought and action. True to the spirit of contradiction that his work embodies, Berkessel ensures that while his art sparks introspection, it also exists as a testament to external narratives, promising a multi-sensory journey for all who partake in its exploration.

Through the harmonic tension of his artwork and his performance in the digital world, Berkessel continues to challenge, engage, and inspire.
